[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define Visual Perception as applied to Non-Destructive Testing. List the minimum vision requirements prescribed by ASNT CP-189 for a VT Level II inspector.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Visual Perception in NDT is the trained process by which an inspector's eye–brain system detects, identifies, and interprets surface and sub-surface discontinuities using visible light, under defined illumination and environmental conditions (1 mark). Per **ASNT CP-189**, the minimum requirements are (2 marks): - **Near vision:** Jaeger **J-2 at 305 mm (12 in.)** in at least one eye, with or without corrective lenses. - **Far vision:** Snellen **20/20 (6/6)** or better. - **Color vision:** Pass **Ishihara 24-plate test** (or equivalent). - Re-examination **annually**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** *State the formula for the minimum angular resolution of the human eye. With daylight pupil diameter of 3 mm and λ = 555 nm, compute the limiting angle.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Formula (1 mark): $$ \theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{D_{\text{pupil}}} $$ Substitution (1 mark): $$ \theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22 \times 555 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 2.26 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{rad} \approx 0.78 \,\text{arc-min} $$ Conclusion (1 mark): This ≈ 1 arc-minute theoretical limit corresponds to the Snellen 20/20 standard, confirming the consistency of human-eye optics with engineering inspection standards. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse Snellen with Jaeger** — they measure *different* distances and use *different* optotypes. Examiners deduct full marks if you interchange them. > - **Do NOT state "the inspector must have 6/6 vision" without mentioning corrective lenses** — CP-189 *explicitly permits* approved spectacles. > - **Do NOT skip units** in derivations (lux, cd/m², arc-min). Unitless numbers lose 1–2 marks. > - **Do NOT claim rods enable color vision** — rods are *achromatic*. Cones handle color. > - In illumination problems, **forget the efficiency factor** at your peril — KTU frequently adds a 0.5 utilization factor to test engineering realism. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **human visual perception chain** as applied to visual testing of a welded plate. Label the four key stages and identify two failure modes at each stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A VT booth is being designed for inspection of circumferential weld seams on 6-inch diameter stainless-steel pipes. The booth measures **5 m × 4 m**. Compute the **minimum number of 30,000-lumen LED floodlights** required to achieve **1000 lux** on the weld surface, assuming a **utilization factor of 0.55** and **maintenance factor of 0.80**. Comment on the inspection-distance choice for weld-cap observation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Q(a):** 1. **Block Diagram** (drawn in answer sheet, 3 marks): `Light Source → Object → Eye Optics (Cornea-Lens-Retina) → Optic Nerve → Visual Cortex → Inspector Decision`. 2. **Stage-by-stage explanation** (2 marks): - **Illumination stage:** Source must provide uniform, glare-free light ≥ 1000 lux per ASME V. - **Detection stage:** Light reflects off weld; defects alter specular/diffuse pattern. - **Perception stage:** Eye optics focus image on retina; rods and cones transduce to neural signal. - **Cognition stage:** Brain, trained per procedure, decides Accept/Reject. 3. **Two failure modes per stage** (2 marks): - Illumination: *under-lit*, *glare shadow*. - Detection: *low contrast*, *surface contamination*. - Perception: *presbyopia*, *color blindness*. - Cognition: *fatigue*, *procedural non-compliance*. --- **Model Solution — Q(b):** **Step 1 — Required illuminance $E$ on the booth floor:** $$ E = 1000 \,\text{lux} $$ **Step 2 — Booth area:** $$ A = 5 \times 4 = 20 \,\text{m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Total luminous flux required (with utilization + maintenance factors):** $$ \Phi_{\text{req}} = \frac{E \times A}{U \times M} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{0.55 \times 0.80} $$ **Step 4 — Numerator:** $$ 1000 \times 20 = 20{,}000 \,\text{lm} $$ **Step 5 — Denominator:** $$ 0.55 \times 0.80 = 0.44 $$ **Step 6 — Division:** $$ \Phi_{\text{req}} = \frac{20{,}000}{0.44} \approx 45{,}455 \,\text{lm} $$ **Step 7 — Number of fixtures $N$:** $$ N = \left\lceil \frac{45{,}455}{30{,}000} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1.515 \right\rceil = 2 \,\text{fixtures} $$ **Step 8 — Engineering comment** (2 marks): For weld-cap inspection, the inspector should maintain an inspection distance of approximately **300–400 mm** with **near-vision Jaeger J-2 compliance** and a viewing angle of **30°–45° to the surface** to minimize specular glare from the polished cap. A **second fixture at oblique incidence** (rather than direct overhead) eliminates root-pass shadowing. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **factors affecting visual perception** during NDT inspection. Categorize them into inspector-related, equipment-related, and environment-related factors, with at least three examples each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **minimum angular resolution** of the human eye for a pupil diameter of **4 mm** under photopic conditions (peak wavelength **555 nm**). Convert the result to **arc-minutes** and explain its significance with respect to the **Snellen 20/20 standard**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Q(a):** **Inspector-related factors** (2.5 marks): 1. **Visual acuity** — measured by Snellen / Jaeger; degraded by presbyopia. 2. **Color vision** — Ishihara screening; critical for heat-tint / oxide identification. 3. **Fatigue and shift length** — detection probability falls sharply after 2 h continuous VT. 4. **Training and experience** — pattern recognition is skill-based. **Equipment-related factors** (2.5 marks): 1. **Illumination level and uniformity** — minimum 1000 lux per ASTM E2707. 2. **Type of light source** — white LED vs UV (for fluorescent PT) vs polarized (for surface inspection). 3. **Viewing aids** — borescopes, mirrors, magnifiers, CCTV cameras with proper resolution. **Environment-related factors** (2 marks): 1. **Ambient light contamination** — critical in dark-booth PT inspection. 2. **Glare, dust, fumes, vibration** — degrade both inspector comfort and optical clarity. 3. **Surface temperature** — affects inspector stamina and material emissivity (relevant to IR overlay). --- **Model Solution — Q(b):** **Step 1 —** Formula: $$ \theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{D_{\text{pupil}}} $$ **Step 2 —** Substitute $\lambda = 555 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$, $D_{\text{pupil}} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$: $$ \theta_{\min} = \frac{1.22 \times 555 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} $$ **Step 3 —** Numerator: $$ 1.22 \times 555 = 677.1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 677.1 \times 10^{-9} = 6.771 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} $$ **Step 4 —** Division: $$ \theta_{\min} = \frac{6.771 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.693 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{rad} $$ **Step 5 —** Convert to arc-minutes ($1\,\text{rad} = 3437.75\,\text{arc-min}$): $$ \theta_{\min} = 1.693 \times 10^{-4} \times 3437.75 \approx 0.582 \,\text{arc-min} $$ **Step 6 —** Significance (2 marks): Snellen 20/20 is defined as the ability to resolve a letter whose critical stroke subtends **1 arc-minute** at the eye. Our computed $\theta_{\min} \approx 0.58\,\text{arc-min}$ is **better than 1 arc-minute**, meaning a young, healthy, photopically-adapted eye theoretically outperforms the 20/20 benchmark — this excess resolution is what allows trained NDT inspectors to detect sub-Snellen cracks under optimum illumination and contrast. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Visual Perception" Module 1** - **Definition:** Trained eye–brain interpretation of visible signals for NDT. - **Oldest NDT method** — predates UT, RT, MT, PT, ET. - **Snellen acuity** measures **far vision**; **Jaeger** measures **near vision** at 305 mm. - **ASNT CP-189 minimum:** Snellen **20/20**, Jaeger **J-2**, Ishihara pass — all **annual**. - **Photopic peak:** 555 nm (cones); **Scotopic peak:** 507 nm (rods). - **Rods** → low-light, achromatic. **Cones** → bright-light, color (S, M, L types). - **Presbyopia** is the chief age-related deficit; mitigated by approved spectacles. - **Standard illuminance** — **500 lux** (general), **1000 lux** (detailed VT, weld inspection). - **Fluorescent PT dark zone** — ambient ≤ **20 lux**; UV intensity **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** at 365 nm. - **Diffraction-limited resolution:** $\theta_{\min} = 1.22\lambda/D_{\text{pupil}}$ ≈ **1 arc-min** for daylight eye. - **Illumination formula:** $E = \Phi / A$ in lux = lm/m². - **Luminous efficacy peak:** **683 lm/W** at 555 nm. - **Inspection viewing angle:** typically **30°–45°** to surface (avoid specular glare). - **Continuous VT duration:** recommended **≤ 2 h** per ASNT without rest. - **Corrective lenses are permitted**, but **must be worn during the eye test** to be valid. - **Color blindness** (deuteranopia/protanopia) → disqualifies from PT/heat-tint-based VT. - **Engineering aids:** borescopes, mirrors, magnifiers, polarizers, fiber-optic illuminators. - **Standards triad:** **ASNT CP-189 / ASTM E2707 / ISO 9712** — all three expect at next KTU paper. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Memory Anchor — "1-2-3-5-7-1000":** > **1** arc-min resolution · **2** h max VT · **3** cone types (S/M/L) · **5** arc-min Snellen letter · **7.5 m** equivalent for 6/7.5 (J-2) · **1000** lux for detailed weld VT. Master this sequence and you will recall every KTU numerical on this topic.

topics.title: Direct and Indirect Methods of Visual Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Direct and Indirect Methods of Visual Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Testing (VT)** — formally known as **Visual Examination (VE)** or **Visual Inspection (VI)** — is the oldest, most fundamental, and most widely employed Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method. It is defined by **ASME Section V, Article 9** and **ASTM E270** as the process of examining a component's surface for discontinuities, geometric deviations, corrosion, surface damage, and workmanship defects using the human eye, either directly (unaided) or assisted by optical instruments.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET636)**, Visual Testing is broadly classified into two primary operational philosophies:

| Classification | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Direct Visual Testing (DVT)** | Inspection performed by the naked eye, typically aided only by simple, non-optical instruments such as flashlights, mirrors, gauges, and straightedges. The inspector maintains a direct, unobstructed line of sight. |
| **Indirect Visual Testing (IVT)** | Inspection performed using optical instruments (boroscopes, fiberscopes, videoscopes, cameras, microscopes) to redirect, magnify, or relay the image when the area of interest is **inaccessible** to direct viewing. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this:** Imagine you are a doctor checking a patient.
> - **Direct Visual Testing** is like a family doctor looking at a wound on your arm with a torchlight and saying, "I can see the cut clearly with my own eyes."
> - **Indirect Visual Testing** is like an ENT specialist using a tiny camera on a flexible tube to look inside your ear or throat — areas the human eye simply cannot reach without technological aid.
>
> In manufacturing, the "patient" is the welded joint, the turbine blade, the engine cylinder, or the pipeline, and the "doctor" is the certified Level I/II/III NDT inspector.

## 1.3 Why Visual Testing is the "First and Last" NDT Method

VT is unique among the eight primary NDT methods (VT, PT, MT, RT, UT, ET, LT, HT) because:

- It is the **baseline method** — all other NDT methods are typically preceded and followed by visual examination.
- It requires the **lowest capital investment** in the industry.
- It contributes approximately **80% of all NDT inspections** performed globally in manufacturing and in-service inspections.
- It is governed by codified standards: **ASME V, AWS D1.1, ISO 9712, API 1104**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly separates VT into Direct and Indirect methods to emphasize that the **physics of the human eye** (resolution ~0.1 mm at 250 mm viewing distance) sets the hard limit for Direct VT, while Indirect VT uses **optical engineering** to overcome this limit.

---

## 1.4 The Human Eye as a Measurement Instrument — Key Physiological Metrics

For the KTU 2024 board exam, students **must memorize** the following standard viewing parameters used in Direct VT:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum Viewing Distance** | **250 mm** (10 inches) | Near-point of distinct human vision |
| **Maximum Viewing Distance** | **600 mm** (24 inches) | Far-point for acceptable detail |
| **Illumination Level** | **≥ 500 lux** (general); **≥ 1000 lux** (critical) | Per ASME V Article 9 |
| **Angle of Incidence for Reflective Surfaces** | **≤ 30°** | To minimize glare and maximize defect visibility |
| **Normal Visual Acuity** | **Snellen 1.0 (6/6) or Jaeger J1** | Mandatory pre-employment eye test for Level II/III inspectors |
| **Color Vision** | **Ishihara Test (minimum 16/24 plates)** | Mandatory for inspectors; per **IS 5762 / ISO 9712** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown — Direct Methods of Visual Testing (DVT)

Direct Visual Testing is performed when the surface to be inspected is **fully accessible**, well-illuminated, and within the inspector's unaided line of sight. The operational logic proceeds in the following structured steps:

### Step 1: Pre-Inspection Preparation
- Review the **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)**, drawings, WPS/PQR (for welds), and acceptance criteria.
- Verify the inspector's **medical fitness, visual acuity certification**, and **calibration of lighting equipment**.
- Ensure the **working environment** is safe (scaffolding, lighting, ventilation).

### Step 2: Surface Preparation
- Clean the surface of oil, grease, paint, scale, rust, and weld spatter using wire brush, solvent, or grinding.
- **Why?** Because surface contaminants can mask discontinuities like cracks, undercuts, and porosity.

### Step 3: Illumination Setup
- Position a **white-light flashlight (LED preferred, ≥ 500 lux)** at an oblique angle (typically **15° to 30°** from the surface).
- **Why oblique?** Because shallow surface defects (cracks, laps) cast **shadows** when illuminated at low angles, dramatically increasing visibility — this is the "shadowgraph effect."

### Step 4: Systematic Scanning
- Scan the surface in a **lapping or overlapping pattern** to ensure **100% coverage**.
- Maintain a **viewing distance between 250 mm and 600 mm**.
- Rotate the viewing angle to inspect for **three-dimensional features** (e.g., weld crown height).

### Step 5: Measurement and Recording
- Use mechanical gauges such as:
  - **Fillet weld gauge** — measures leg length and throat thickness.
  - **Bridge-cam gauge** — measures weld undercut and reinforcement.
  - **Hi-Lo gauge** — measures weld cap-to-rail mismatch in rails.
  - **Straightedge and feeler gauge** — measures flatness and misalignment.
  - **Tape measure, vernier caliper, micrometer** — linear measurements.
- Record findings with **digital cameras** for archival.

### Step 6: Evaluation Against Acceptance Criteria
- Compare observations against standards: **AWS D1.1, ASME IX, API 1104, ISO 5817**.
- Mark defects with **low-stress chalk, paint pen, or weld map**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common defects detected by DVT** in welds: undercut, overlap, crater cracks, surface porosity, spatter, root concavity, excessive reinforcement, misalignment, and dimensional non-conformance.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown — Indirect Methods of Visual Testing (IVT)

Indirect VT is employed when the inspection area is **inaccessible** due to geometry (internal cavities, bores, tanks, pipelines) or when **magnification** is required (micro-cracks, fine features).

### 2.2.1 Boroscopes (Rigid)
- A **rigid, hollow tube** with an objective lens at the far end and an eyepiece at the near end.
- Image transfer is via a series of **relay lenses** (Hopkins rod system in modern versions).
- **Length range:** 50 mm to 1500 mm; **Diameter:** 1.7 mm to 12 mm.
- **Applications:** Engine cylinder bores, gun barrels, turbine blade cooling holes, hydraulic valve bodies.

### 2.2.2 Fiberscopes (Flexible)
- A **flexible bundle of optical fibers** (typically 10,000 to 30,000 fibers, each 8–10 µm in diameter).
- **Two fiber bundles:**
  - **Coherent (image) bundle** — preserves spatial arrangement for image transmission.
  - **Non-coherent (light) bundle** — transmits illumination from an external light source.
- **Articulation:** 2-way or 4-way remote tip articulation (typically ±120° to ±160°).
- **Applications:** Aircraft engine combustion chambers, chemical process piping, automotive transmission housings.

### 2.2.3 Videoscopes (Modern Digital)
- A **flexible probe with a Charge-Coupled Device (CCD) or CMOS image sensor** at the distal tip.
- Image is **digitized and displayed on an LCD/LED monitor** in real-time.
- **Advantages over fiberscopes:** higher resolution, no fiber breakage (mosaicking artifacts), image recording, measurement overlay, wireless transmission.
- **Applications:** Power generation turbines, aerospace engine bores, nuclear reactor internals.

### 2.2.4 Specialized Indirect VT Tools

| Tool | Function | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mirrors (periscopic)** | Redirect line of sight around corners | Welds in T-joints, behind obstructions |
| **Magnifying glass / Loupe** | Magnification from 2× to 20× | Fine surface cracks, micro-pitting |
| **Stereo Microscope (10×–100×)** | 3D magnified view | Lab failure analysis, metallography |
| **Digital Camera / DSLR** | High-resolution archival | Bridge inspection, building façade, pipeline outer surface |
| **Drone-Mounted Camera** | Aerial VT of large structures | Chimneys, transmission towers, ship hulls, wind turbines |
| **CCTV / Pan-Tilt-Zoom (PTZ) Camera** | Remote real-time inspection | Storage tank interiors, sewer pipelines |
| **Endoscopic Camera (Wireless)** | Portable, battery-operated | Field service inspections |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Optical Principles

Although VT is largely a qualitative method, certain quantitative optical relationships govern Indirect VT instruments and the human eye. These are **high-yield** for KTU numerical/derivation questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table** — it is a favorite topic for KTU board Part-B (14-mark) questions.

### 2.3.1 Key Optical Formulas for VT

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visual Angle (θ)** | $\theta = \dfrac{h}{d}$ | Where $h$ is the size of the object (defect) and $d$ is the viewing distance | radians |
| **Minimum Detectable Angle** | $\theta_{min} = 1' = \dfrac{1}{60}° \approx 2.91 \times 10^{-4}$ rad | Resolution limit of the normal human eye (1 arc-minute) | rad |
| **Magnification (M)** | $M = \dfrac{\theta_i}{\theta_o} = \dfrac{d_o}{d_i}$ | Angular magnification: ratio of image angle to object angle | dimensionless |
| **Lens Magnifier Power** | $P = \dfrac{1}{f}$ (dioptre) ; $M = \dfrac{250}{f \text{ (in mm)}}$ | Standard magnifier formula (reference viewing distance = 250 mm) | dioptre / dimensionless |
| **Numerical Aperture (NA)** | $NA = n \sin \alpha$ | Light-gathering ability of an optical fiber (n = refractive index of core, α = acceptance angle) | dimensionless |
| **Fiber Optic Resolution** | $R_{fiber} \approx \dfrac{D}{2d_f}$ | Where $D$ is bundle diameter and $d_f$ is individual fiber diameter | line-pairs/mm |
| **Illuminance (E)** | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{I \cos \phi}{r^{2}$ | Illuminance on a surface from a point source (inverse square law) | lux (lx) |
| **Snell's Law (fiber optic)** | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}$ | Where $n_1$ is core refractive index and $n_2$ is cladding refractive index | dimensionless |

### 2.3.2 The Inverse-Square Law of Illumination (Critical for Indirect VT)

When a flashlight or fiber-optic light source illuminates an internal cavity, the illuminance on the surface **falls off rapidly with distance**. This is the fundamental reason fiberscopes have dedicated light guides.

$$E = \frac{I}{r^{2}} \cos \phi$$

Where:
- $E$ = Illuminance on the target surface, in **lux (lx)**
- $I$ = Luminous intensity of the source, in **candela (cd)**
- $r$ = Distance from the source to the surface, in **meters (m)**
- $\phi$ = Angle of incidence between the light ray and the surface normal, in **degrees or radians**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Doubling the distance from a borescope tip to the inspection surface reduces the illuminance by a factor of **4 (one-fourth)**. This is why inspectors position fiberscope tips as close as physically possible to the area of interest.

### 2.3.3 The Boroscope Image-Relation Equation

For a simple thin-lens boroscope, the magnification and image distance follow the standard lens equation:

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i}$$

$$M = -\frac{d_i}{d_o}$$

Where:
- $f$ = focal length of the objective lens (m)
- $d_o$ = object distance from the lens (m)
- $d_i$ = image distance on the eyepiece side (m)
- $M$ = linear magnification (negative sign indicates inverted real image)

### 2.3.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of VT |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | 100% visual inspection of engine bores, fan blades, landing gear |
| **Oil & Gas** | Storage tank interior inspection, pipeline girth weld inspection |
| **Power Generation** | Steam turbine blade inspection, boiler tube internal examination |
| **Civil** | Bridge cable inspection, building façade survey, tunnel lining |
| **Automotive** | Cylinder bore wear measurement, transmission gear inspection |
| **Nuclear** | Reactor pressure vessel internals, fuel rod inspection |
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull plate inspection, propeller blade examination |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Detectable Defect Size by Direct VT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup (Typical KTU Part-A / 3-mark):** An inspector's eye has a minimum resolution angle of **1 arc-minute** (1′). If the inspector views a surface at the standard near-point of distinct vision (**250 mm**), what is the **smallest defect** that can theoretically be detected by Direct VT?

### Step 1: Convert the angular resolution to radians

The human eye can resolve two points as separate only if the angle subtended at the eye is at least **1 arc-minute**.

$$\theta_{min} = 1' = \frac{1°}{60} = \frac{1}{60} \times \frac{\pi}{180} \text{ rad}$$

Performing the arithmetic:

$$\theta_{min} = \frac{\pi}{60 \times 180} = \frac{\pi}{10{,}800} \text{ rad}$$

$$\theta_{min} = \frac{3.14159}{10{,}800} \approx 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

### Step 2: Relate the angle to object size and viewing distance

For a small object, the visual angle is:

$$\theta = \frac{h}{d}$$

Where:
- $h$ = height (size) of the defect (m)
- $d$ = viewing distance = **250 mm = 0.250 m**
- $\theta$ = visual angle subtended at the eye (rad)

### Step 3: Solve for the minimum defect size

Setting $\theta = \theta_{min}$ and rearranging for $h$:

$$h_{min} = d \cdot \theta_{min}$$

$$h_{min} = 0.250 \text{ m} \times 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

$$\boxed{h_{min} \approx 7.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 0.0727 \text{ mm} \approx 72.7 \text{ µm}}$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

This explains why the unaided human eye can detect:
- Surface cracks wider than **~75 µm** (about the thickness of a human hair = 70 µm).
- Surface discontinuities such as undercut **≥ 0.5 mm** as per AWS D1.1 (because of contrast and lighting factors, the practical limit is coarser than the theoretical 72 µm).

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **physical justification** for needing Indirect VT (boroscopes, microscopes) when defects are **smaller than ~75 µm** or located in **inaccessible areas**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Magnification of a Simple Lens Magnifier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup (KTU Part-B / 14-mark favorite):** A watchmaker uses a magnifying lens of focal length **+50 mm**. Calculate (a) the magnifying power, and (b) the dioptric power. Assume the near-point of distinct vision is **250 mm**.

### Part (a): Magnifying Power (M)

For a simple magnifier held close to the eye, the angular magnification is:

$$M = \frac{d_n}{f} = \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{f \text{ (in mm)}}$$

Substituting $f = 50$ mm:

$$M = \frac{250}{50} = 5\times$$

### Part (b): Dioptric Power (P)

The dioptric power of a lens is the reciprocal of the focal length (in meters):

$$P = \frac{1}{f \text{ (in m)}}$$

Converting: $f = 50$ mm = $0.050$ m

$$P = \frac{1}{0.050} = 20 \text{ dioptres (D)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb (KTU memory aid):** $M \approx P \times 0.25$, i.e., the magnifying power equals the dioptric power divided by **4**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Illuminance on the Wall of a Pipe During Borescope Inspection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup (Advanced KTU Part-B):** A fiberscope with a tip luminous intensity of **$I = 200$ cd** is inserted into a cylindrical pipe of diameter **$D = 100$ mm**. The tip is positioned **$r = 50$ mm** from the pipe wall. Assuming the light strikes the wall at **$\phi = 0°$ (perpendicular)**, calculate the illuminance on the wall.

### Step 1: Identify the parameters
- $I = 200$ cd
- $r = 50$ mm = $0.050$ m
- $\phi = 0°$ → $\cos \phi = 1$

### Step 2: Apply the inverse-square law

$$E = \frac{I \cos \phi}{r^{2}}$$

$$E = \frac{200 \times 1}{(0.050)^{2}}$$

$$E = \frac{200}{0.0025}$$

$$\boxed{E = 80{,}000 \text{ lux}}$$

### Step 3: Practical check

The ASME V Article 9 minimum illumination requirement of **500 lux** is satisfied by a factor of **160×**, confirming that even at 50 mm, a 200 cd fiberscope light source is more than adequate for visual inspection.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Direct vs. Indirect VT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most important revision tool for KTU board exams.** It is directly tested in Part-A and as a Part-B 7-mark sub-question.

| Parameter | **Direct Visual Testing (DVT)** | **Indirect Visual Testing (IVT)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Aid Used** | Naked eye + flashlight, mirror, gauge | Boroscope, fiberscope, videoscope, microscope, camera |
| **Accessibility Required** | Direct line of sight needed | No direct line of sight required |
| **Minimum Defect Size** | ~75 µm (theoretical); ~0.5 mm (practical) | ~5 µm (microscope) to ~100 µm (boroscope) |
| **Cost** | **Very low** (₹ 2,000 – 10,000 for flashlights/gauges) | **High** (₹ 50,000 – 20,00,000 for videoscopes) |
| **Portability** | Highly portable | Moderate; probe + light source + monitor |
| **Skill Level** | Trained inspector (Level I/II) | Trained inspector (Level II) + instrument operation |
| **Image Recording** | Manual photography | Built-in digital recording, annotation, measurement |
| **Hazardous Environments** | Inspector must enter the area (confined space risk) | Inspector can be remote; **ATEX-rated probes** for explosive zones |
| **Speed** | Fast for accessible areas | Slower due to setup, articulation, image interpretation |
| **Standards** | ASME V Art. 9, AWS D1.1, ISO 17637 | ASME V Art. 9, ASTM E1252, ISO 17637 |
| **Typical Applications** | Welds, castings, forgings — external surfaces | Engine bores, turbine blades, tanks, pipelines — internal surfaces |
| **Limitation** | Cannot see inside cavities | Image distortion, limited field of view, lighting falloff |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Visual Testing Methods (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VISUAL TESTING VT] --> B[Direct VT DVT]
    A --> C[Indirect VT IVT]
    B --> B1[Naked Eye Inspection]
    B --> B2[Aided DVT]
    B1 --> B1A[Snellen Acuity Test]
    B1 --> B1B[Daylight or Ambient Light]
    B2 --> B2A[Flashlight LED]
    B2 --> B2B[Mirror Periscopic]
    B2 --> B2C[Fillet Weld Gauge]
    B2 --> B2D[Bridge Cam Gauge]
    B2 --> B2E[Hi-Lo Gauge]
    B2 --> B2F[Feeler Gauge and Straightedge]
    C --> C1[Optical Instruments]
    C --> C2[Imaging Instruments]
    C1 --> C1A[Boroscope Rigid]
    C1 --> C1B[Fiberscope Flexible]
    C2 --> C2A[Videoscope Digital CCD]
    C2 --> C2B[Microscope Stereo]
    C2 --> C2C[Digital Camera DSLR]
    C2 --> C2D[Drone Mounted Camera]
    C2 --> C2E[CCTV Pan Tilt Zoom]
    C2 --> C2F[Wireless Endoscopic Camera]
    C1A --> APP1[Aerospace Engine Cylinder Bores]
    C1B --> APP2[Aircraft Combustion Chambers]
    C2A --> APP3[Power Plant Turbine Blades]
    C2C --> APP4[Bridge and Building Facade Survey]
    C2D --> APP5[Wind Turbine Tower Inspection]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Optical Architecture of a Fiberscope

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LIGHT_SRC [External Light Source]
        L1[Halogen or LED Lamp]
    end
    subgraph CTRL [Eyepiece and Controls]

        AR[Articulation Knobs]
        LC[Light Guide Connector]
    end

        SUR[Internal Surface]
        DEF[Defect Crack Corrosion Pitting]
    end
    L1 --> LC
    LC --> LB
    LB --> DT
    DT --> ILL[Illumination Beam]
    ILL --> SUR
    SUR --> REF[Reflected Light from Defect]
    REF --> DT
    DT --> IB
    IB --> E1
    AR --> DT
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Standard VT Inspection Procedure (KTU Board Diagram Favorite)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Receive Inspection Request and Drawings] --> S2[Step 2 Review ITP Acceptance Criteria Standards]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Verify Inspector Certification and Visual Acuity]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Prepare Surface Clean and Degrease]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5 Accessibility Decision}
    S5 -->|Accessible| S6[Step 6a Select DVT Method Naked Eye or Aided]
    S5 -->|Inaccessible| S7[Step 6b Select IVT Method Boroscope Fiberscope Videoscope]
    S6 --> S8[Step 7 Illuminate Surface 500 to 1000 lux at 15 to 30 deg]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Step 8 Systematic 100 percent Visual Scan]

    S10 --> S11[Step 10 Compare Against Acceptance Criteria]

    S13 --> S15[Step 13 Archive Photographs and Final Report]
```

```mermaid
    end

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between Direct and Indirect methods of Visual Testing. Give two examples of each." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Concise – word limit ~80 words):**

> **Direct Visual Testing (DVT)** is performed using the **naked eye**, with direct line of sight to the inspection surface. Aided by simple tools like flashlights, mirrors, and mechanical gauges.
> *Examples:* (i) Visual inspection of a welded plate surface for undercut; (ii) Measurement of weld leg length with a fillet weld gauge.
>
> **Indirect Visual Testing (IVT)** uses **optical or imaging instruments** to view inaccessible internal surfaces.
> *Examples:* (i) Inspection of an aircraft engine cylinder bore using a **boroscope**; (ii) Examination of a chemical storage tank using a **videoscope**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"State any four instruments used in Indirect Visual Testing. Mention one specific industrial application for each." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular format for easy valuation):**

| S.No. | Instrument | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rigid Boroscope** | Inspection of gun barrel bore for erosion |
| 2 | **Flexible Fiberscope** | Internal inspection of aircraft engine combustion liner |
| 3 | **Videoscope (CCD/CMOS)** | Real-time inspection of steam turbine blade cooling passages |
| 4 | **Stereo Microscope** | Laboratory fractographic analysis of a failed bolt |

---

## 5.2 PART-B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank PEMET636]**

> **Q. A (a)** Explain in detail the **Direct methods of Visual Testing** with neat sketches of the procedure and list the various **mechanical gauges** used during weld inspection. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. A (b)** Discuss the construction and working of a **flexible fiberscope** with a labelled diagram. Compare it with a **rigid borescope**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. A (a)** *(7 Marks)*

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Direct Visual Testing (DVT) is the inspection of a surface using the **naked human eye**, aided only by simple, non-electronic tools such as flashlights, mirrors, and mechanical gauges. The surface must be **fully accessible** with a direct line of sight.

**2. Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Pre-Inspection:** Review ITP, drawings, WPS/PQR, and acceptance criteria (ASME V, AWS D1.1).
2. **Surface Preparation:** Remove oil, paint, rust, spatter using a wire brush or solvent.
3. **Illumination:** Position a **white-light LED flashlight (≥ 500 lux)** at an oblique angle of **15°–30°** to maximize the shadowgraph effect.
4. **Systematic Scanning:** Cover **100% of the surface** in overlapping parallel passes, maintaining a viewing distance of **250–600 mm**.
5. **Measurement & Recording:** Use gauges to quantify defect dimensions; record with photography and weld maps.
6. **Evaluation:** Compare against acceptance criteria; mark **accept/repair/reject**.

**3. Mechanical Gauges (2 marks):**
- **Fillet weld gauge** – measures leg length and throat thickness of fillet welds.
- **Bridge-cam gauge** – measures undercut depth and reinforcement height.
- **Hi-Lo gauge** – measures misalignment of rail welds.
- **Straightedge and feeler gauge** – checks flatness and plate misalignment.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. A (b)** *(7 Marks)*

**1. Fiberscope Construction (3 marks):**

A fiberscope consists of:
- **Control body (eyepiece + articulation knobs + light guide inlet)**
- **Flexible insertion tube** (1–8 m long) containing two bundles:
  - **Coherent image bundle** (10,000–30,000 glass fibers, each ~8 µm, arranged identically at both ends) for image transmission.
  - **Non-coherent light guide bundle** for transmitting illumination.
- **Distal tip** with **objective lens, illumination lens, and 2-way or 4-way articulation mechanism (±120°)**.
- **External light source** (halogen or LED, typically 150 W).

**2. Working Principle (2 marks):**
- Light from the source travels through the **non-coherent bundle** and exits at the distal tip, illuminating the target.
- Reflected light from the target enters the **objective lens**, is focused on the **coherent bundle**, and is transmitted to the eyepiece.
- The inspector views the image through the eyepiece, and **articulates the tip** to scan the cavity.

**3. Comparison with Rigid Borescope (2 marks):**

| Feature | **Rigid Borescope** | **Flexible Fiberscope** |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Hollow rigid tube with relay lenses | Flexible tube with optical fiber bundles |
| Length | Limited to ~1.5 m | 1–8 m (longer possible) |
| Articulation | None | 2-way or 4-way remote tip |
| Image Quality | Higher (relay lens) | Moderate (fiber pixelation) |
| Application | Straight bores (engine cylinders) | Curved cavities (turbines, tanks) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank PEMET636]**

> **Q. B (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **human eye as an optical instrument** for visual testing. State the standard values of **minimum viewing distance, illumination, and visual acuity** for an NDT inspector. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. B (b)** An inspector uses a magnifying lens of focal length **40 mm**. Calculate (i) the **magnifying power** and (ii) the **dioptric power**. If the defect is viewed at a distance of **100 mm**, what is the **minimum defect size** the inspector can resolve? *(Assume minimum angular resolution of the eye = 1 arc-minute.)* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. B (a)** *(7 Marks)*

**1. Human Eye as an Optical Instrument (3 marks):**

The human eye consists of:
- **Cornea** – transparent front surface; provides ~2/3 of total refractive power (~43 D).
- **Aqueous humor** – clear fluid between cornea and lens.
- **Iris + Pupil** – variable aperture (2–8 mm diameter) controlling light entry.
- **Lens (crystalline)** – variable focal length (15–20 D) for accommodation.
- **Retina** – light-sensitive layer containing **rods** (low-light, ~120 million) and **cones** (color, ~6 million).
- **Optic nerve** – transmits signals to the visual cortex.

The eye focuses light from an object onto the retina, forming a real, inverted image. The brain corrects the inversion.

**2. Standard Values for NDT Inspector (2 marks):**
- **Minimum viewing distance:** 250 mm
- **Maximum viewing distance:** 600 mm
- **Illumination:** ≥ 500 lux (general), ≥ 1000 lux (critical)
- **Visual acuity:** Snellen 6/6 (or Jaeger J1)
- **Color vision:** Ishihara test ≥ 16/24 plates correct

**3. Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Student should draw and label the cornea → pupil → lens → retina → optic nerve path.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Q. B (b)** *(7 Marks)*

**Given:**
- Focal length of magnifier, $f = 40$ mm
- Object distance (viewing distance), $d = 100$ mm
- Eye resolution, $\theta_{min} = 1' = 2.909 \times 10^{-4}$ rad

**(i) Magnifying Power (2 marks):**
$$M = \frac{d_n}{f} = \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{40 \text{ mm}} = 6.25\times$$

**(ii) Dioptric Power (2 marks):**
$$P = \frac{1}{f \text{ (in m)}} = \frac{1}{0.040 \text{ m}} = 25 \text{ D}$$

**(iii) Minimum Defect Size (3 marks):**
$$h_{min} = d \cdot \theta_{min} = 0.100 \text{ m} \times 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$
$$\boxed{h_{min} = 2.909 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 29.1 \text{ µm}}$$

> **Conclusion:** With a 40 mm magnifier held at 100 mm, the inspector can resolve defects as small as **~29 µm** — about 2.5× finer than the unaided eye (72 µm).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Students**
> 1. **Unit Errors in Dioptre Calculation:** Many students write $P = 1/f$ without converting mm → m. A 50 mm lens = **+20 D**, not +0.020 D. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Confusing Visual Angle and Object Size:** $h = d \cdot \theta$ is correct only for **small angles** (θ in radians). Do not use $\tan \theta$ unless specified. (Lose 0.5–1 mark.)
> 3. **Forgetting Cos φ Term:** In illuminance problems, students often omit the $\cos \phi$ factor. Always check if the light strikes perpendicular or oblique. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 4. **Mixing Up Boroscope and Fiberscope:** A **boroscope is rigid** with **relay lenses**; a **fiberscope is flexible** with **optical fibers**. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 5. **Omitting Numerical Conversions:** Always show $1' = \pi/10{,}800$ rad derivation — partial marks are awarded for steps.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1, Direct & Indirect VT**

### 🔹 Foundational Facts
- VT is the **oldest and most-used NDT method** (~80% of all inspections).
- Governed by **ASME V Article 9, AWS D1.1, ISO 17637, ASTM E270**.

### 🔹 Direct VT — Core Points
- Inspection is by the **naked eye**, with or without simple aids (flashlight, mirror, gauges).
- Requires **direct line of sight** and **fully accessible surface**.
- Standard viewing distance: **250–600 mm**.
- Standard illumination: **≥ 500 lux (general), ≥ 1000 lux (critical)**.
- Inspectors must have **Snellen 6/6 acuity** and **Ishihara color vision** certification.
- Detects: undercut, overlap, porosity, spatter, crater cracks, dimensional errors.

### 🔹 Indirect VT — Core Points
- Uses **optical/imaging instruments** to view **inaccessible** surfaces.
- **Rigid Borescope:** hollow tube + relay lenses; used for straight bores.
- **Flexible Fiberscope:** coherent + non-coherent fiber bundles; articulated tip.
- **Videoscope:** CCD/CMOS digital sensor; real-time monitor display.
- Specialized tools: mirrors, loupes, stereo microscopes, DSLR cameras, drones, CCTV.

### 🔹 Key Optical Formulas
- Visual angle: $\theta = h/d$
- Eye resolution limit: $\theta_{min} = 1' \approx 2.909 \times 10^{-4}$ rad
- Min defect size (DVT, 250 mm): $h_{min} \approx 72.7$ µm
- Magnification: $M = 250/f \text{ (mm)}$
- Dioptre: $P = 1/f \text{ (m)}$
- Illuminance (inverse square): $E = I \cos \phi / r^{2}$
- Snell's law (fiber NA): $NA = \sqrt{n_1^{2} - n_2^{2}}$

### 🔹 Comparison DVT vs. IVT
- DVT → **cheap, fast, accessible only**; IVT → **expensive, slower, accesses internals**.
- Both must be performed by a **certified Level I/II/III NDT inspector** (per **ISO 9712 / ASNT SNT-TC-1A**).

### 🔹 Industrial Applications
- **DVT:** weld inspection, casting/forging surface, structural steel.
- **IVT:** engine bores, turbine blades, tanks, pipelines, nuclear internals, bridges, chimneys.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Direct and Indirect Methods of Visual Testing**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Testing Aids: Mirrors, Magnifiers, Boroscopes, Fibroscopes, and Closed Circuit Television
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visual Testing Aids: Mirrors, Magnifiers, Boroscopes, Fibroscopes, and Closed Circuit Television

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Visual Testing Aids?

**Visual Testing (VT) Aids** are a class of optical, mechanical, and electro-optical instruments used in **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** to extend the natural capability of the human eye. They allow inspectors to perform visual examination of components and structures that are **inaccessible, distant, poorly lit, or too small** to be examined directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Visual Testing Aids are auxiliary optical, illumination, and imaging devices employed in direct or remote visual inspection to enhance the inspector's perception of surface discontinuities, geometric features, and service conditions of a component without altering its functional state.*

These aids form the **oldest, simplest, and most widely used** NDT technique, categorized under **ASME Section V, Article 9** and **ASTM E2702** for direct and remote visual testing.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Visual Testing Aids

| Category | Examples | Primary Function |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Reflection-based** | Mirrors, Periscopes | Redirect line of sight around obstructions |
| **Magnification-based** | Hand magnifiers, Stereo microscopes | Enlarge small surface features |
| **Illumination-based** | Flashlights, Borescope light guides | Illuminate dark cavities |
| **Tube-based rigid** | Boroscopes | Inspect straight, narrow bores |
| **Fiber-optic flexible** | Fibroscopes, Video borescopes | Inspect curved/long cavities |
| **Electronic imaging** | CCTV, Digital borescopes | Record and view remotely |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Submarine Inspector" Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **submarine inspector** who must examine the inside of a **boiler tube** running 6 meters deep inside a steam generator. The inspector cannot fit inside, cannot see inside (it's pitch black), and cannot dismantle the equipment. So he uses:
> - A **flashlight + mirror** (to peek in from outside)
> - A **rigid borescope** (a long tube with lenses and light)
> - A **flexible fibroscope** (a snaking fiber-optic cable with an eyepiece)
> - A **CCTV camera** (to record the inspection on screen for the entire team to analyze later)
>
> **Each tool is a "Visual Testing Aid"** — extending the human eye to places it cannot naturally reach.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Speed of light in vacuum (c) = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Refractive index of optical glass (n) ≈ 1.5**
- **Critical angle for glass-air interface (θc) ≈ 41.8°**
- **Numerical Aperture (NA) of typical borescope fibers = 0.35 to 0.55**
- **Standard human eye resolution ≈ 0.1 mm** (1 arc-minute acuity)
- **Minimum illumination for inspection ≈ 500 lux** (per ASME Section V)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 1️⃣ MIRRORS — The Simplest Visual Aid

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A mirror uses the **Law of Reflection**:

$$\theta_i = \theta_r$$

where **θᵢ** is the angle of incidence and **θᵣ** is the angle of reflection, both measured from the surface **normal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **A flat mirror produces a virtual image that is laterally inverted but the same size as the object, located as far behind the mirror as the object is in front.**

### 2.1.2 Types of Mirrors Used in VT

| Mirror Type | Geometry | Application in NDT |
|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Flat plane mirror** | Planar surface | Peek behind piping, weld toes |
| **Concave mirror** | Curved inward | Light concentration + magnified virtual image |
| **Convex mirror** | Curved outward | Wide-angle overview of cavities |
| **Periscope (two parallel mirrors)** | Folded optics | Look over/around obstructions in tanks |
| **First-surface mirror** | Coating on front surface | Eliminates ghost image, ideal for precision VT |

### 2.1.3 Mirror Equation (Essential for Magnification)

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{v} + \frac{1}{u}$$

$$m = -\frac{v}{u}$$

where:
- **f** = focal length
- **u** = object distance (negative by sign convention)
- **v** = image distance
- **m** = linear magnification

---

## 2.2 2️⃣ MAGNIFIERS — Enlarging the View

### 2.2.1 Principle
A magnifier is a single **convex (converging) lens** that produces a **virtual, erect, and enlarged image** when the object is placed **within the focal length**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For relaxed eye viewing, the object should be placed at the focal point of the magnifier, producing an image at infinity with angular magnification M = D/f, where D = 25 cm (least distance of distinct vision).**

### 2.2.2 Magnification Equations

**Angular Magnification (M):**

$$M = \frac{D}{f} + 1 \approx \frac{25 \text{ cm}}{f} \quad \text{(for normal use)}$$

$$M = \frac{D}{f} \quad \text{(when image is at infinity — relaxed eye)}$$

**Combined magnification of a compound microscope:**

$$M_{total} = M_{objective} \times M_{eyepiece} = -\frac{L}{f_{obj}} \times \frac{D}{f_{eye}}$$

where **L** is the optical tube length (≈ 16 cm standard).

### 2.2.3 Types Used in NDT

- **Handheld magnifier** (2× to 10×)
- **Illuminated magnifier** (with built-in lamp)
- **Pocket comparator / Measuring magnifier** (with graticule for crack length)
- **Stereo microscope** (3D inspection of fracture surfaces)
- **CCTV-coupled microscope** (for team viewing)

---

## 2.3 3️⃣ BOROSCOPES — The Rigid Optical Tube

### 2.3.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **A borescope is a rigid optical instrument consisting of a tube containing a series of relay lenses (or modern gradient-index (GRIN) rods), an objective lens at the distal tip, an eyepiece at the proximal end, and a light guide for illumination. It allows direct visual inspection of the interior of bores, cavities, and hollow components.**

### 2.3.2 Optical Construction (Distal → Proximal)

1. **Illumination channel** — fiber optic bundle delivering cold light
2. **Objective lens** — forms real inverted image at internal focal plane
3. **Relay lens system** — transfers image down the tube (Hoplins, Roesch, or GRIN variants)
4. **Eyepiece** — magnifies intermediate image for the eye or camera

### 2.3.3 Key Optical Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------------|---------|
| **Field of View** | FOV | 30° – 100° | Angular cone visible at the tip |
| **Direction of View** | DOV | 0°, 30°, 70°, 90° | Angle between scope axis & line of sight |
| **Depth of Field** | DOF | 3 mm – ∞ | Range of in-focus distances |
| **Magnification** | M | 1× – 50× | Image size enlargement |
| **Resolution** | R | 50 – 100 µm | Smallest distinguishable feature |
| **Probe Diameter** | d | 1.7 – 25 mm | For bore access |
| **Probe Length** | L | 50 – 1500 mm | Reach capability |

### 2.3.4 Field of View Formula

$$\text{FOV} = 2 \cdot d_{obj} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta_{FOV}}{2}\right)$$

where **d_obj** is the distance from objective lens to the object and **θ_FOV** is the angular field of view.

---

## 2.4 4️⃣ FIBERSCOPES — The Flexible Borescope

### 2.4.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A fiberscope is a flexible, image-transmitting borescope that uses a coherent bundle of optical fibers. Each fiber carries one pixel of the image, and the bundle flexes around curves, allowing inspection of complex internal geometries where a rigid borescope cannot reach.**

### 2.4.2 Operating Principle — Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

Light is guided inside an optical fiber by repeated **Total Internal Reflection** at the core-cladding interface. This requires:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$

**For typical glass fiber:** n₁ (core) = 1.62, n₂ (cladding) = 1.52, so **θc ≈ 69.8°**.

### 2.4.3 Numerical Aperture (NA) — Critical Fiber-Optic Parameter

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1 \sin\theta_a$$

where **θₐ** is the maximum acceptance angle at the fiber input face.

> [!NOTE]
> **The NA determines the light-gathering power of the fiber. Higher NA = more light collected = brighter image. Typical fiberscope NA ranges from 0.35 to 0.55.**

### 2.4.4 Image Resolution in a Coherent Bundle

$$R = \frac{1}{d_f} \quad \text{lines/mm (limited by fiber diameter)}$$

where **d_f** is the diameter of an individual fiber (typically 5–25 µm).

### 2.4.5 Components of a Fiberscope

1. **Light source** (typically 150 W halogen or LED cold light)
2. **Illumination bundle** (incoherent fibers)
3. **Imaging bundle** (coherent, aligned fibers — ~10,000 to 30,000 fibers)
4. **Objective lens** at tip
5. **Articulation section** (wires deflect the tip 4-way, ±120° typical)
6. **Eyepiece / Camera adapter**
7. **Insertion tube** (stainless steel armored, length 1.5 m to 12 m)

---

## 2.5 5️⃣ CLOSED CIRCUIT TELEVISION (CCTV) — Remote Electronic Inspection

### 2.5.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **CCTV in NDT refers to a closed-loop video inspection system that captures, transmits, displays, and records images of internal or external features of a test object using a camera, cable, monitor, and recording device. It is the electronic successor to the optical borescope, enabling real-time remote inspection, recording, and team analysis.**

### 2.5.2 CCTV System Block Diagram (Conceptual)

A typical NDT CCTV chain:

$$\text{Test Object} \rightarrow \text{Camera (CCD/CMOS)} \rightarrow \text{Processor} \rightarrow \text{Monitor} \rightarrow \text{Recorder}$$

### 2.5.3 Modern Variants (Replacing Pure CCTV)

| Variant | Description | Key Advantage |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| **Video borescope** | Miniature camera at tip of flexible probe | No fiber bundle; higher resolution |
| **Pan-Tilt-Zoom (PTZ) camera** | Remote-controlled multi-axis camera | Inspects tanks/vessels from outside |
| **Crawler-mounted CCTV** | Wheeled vehicle with camera in pipes | Long-distance pipeline inspection |
| **Drone-based CCTV** | UAV with stabilized camera | Confined-space vessel inspection |
| **Wireless endoscope** | Wi-Fi/Bluetooth tip camera | No cable, smartphone display |

### 2.5.4 CCTV Performance Parameters

- **Horizontal Resolution**: ≥ 480 TV lines (standard) / 1000+ TVL (HD)
- **Sensitivity**: < 0.1 lux (low-light NDT)
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**: ≥ 50 dB
- **Frame Rate**: 25/30 fps (real-time inspection)
- **Zoom Ratio**: 10× optical + 4× digital (typical)

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Law of Reflection | $\theta_i = \theta_r$ | degrees |
| 2 | Mirror Equation | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v} + \dfrac{1}{u}$ | m |
| 3 | Linear Magnification | $m = -\dfrac{v}{u}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Magnifier Power (relaxed eye) | $M = \dfrac{D}{f}$ | D = 25 cm |
| 5 | Magnifier Power (normal) | $M = 1 + \dfrac{D}{f}$ | – |
| 6 | Compound Microscope | $M_{tot} = -\dfrac{L}{f_o} \cdot \dfrac{D}{f_e}$ | L ≈ 16 cm |
| 7 | Critical Angle | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | degrees |
| 8 | Numerical Aperture | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Acceptance Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{NA}}{n_0}\right)$ | degrees |
| 10 | Fiber Resolution | $R = 1/d_f$ | lines/mm |
| 11 | Field of View | $\text{FOV} = 2 d_{obj} \tan(\theta_{FOV}/2)$ | mm |
| 12 | Borescope Resolution | $R = 1.22 \lambda / \text{NA}$ | Rayleigh criterion (µm) |
| 13 | Fiber Light Transmission | $I_{out} = I_{in}\, e^{-\alpha L}$ | α = attenuation (dB/m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For exam calculations, remember: D (least distance of distinct vision) = 25 cm. Numerical Aperture has NO units.**

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Borescope inspection of **aircraft engine turbine blades**, combustor liners, and HPT/LPT stages without disassembly.
- **Power Generation**: Fibroscope inspection of **boiler tubes, steam generator tubes, and condensers** in thermal and nuclear plants.
- **Oil & Gas**: CCTV crawler inspection of **pipelines, risers, and subsea structures** for corrosion, cracking, and weld defects.
- **Automotive**: Visual inspection of **engine cylinders, gearbox housings, and weld seams** for porosity and inclusions.
- **Civil/Structural**: Mirror-based inspection of **bridges, building facades, and confined spaces** for cracks and corrosion.
- **Manufacturing**: Magnifier-based final inspection of **precision machined parts, weld toes, and surface finish**.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All derivations below are fully expanded. No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is justified with a written explanation.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mirror Equation from Geometry

**Problem**: Derive the mirror equation $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}$ for a concave mirror using similar triangles.

**Step 1 — Setup**
Consider a concave mirror with center of curvature **C**, focus **F**, and pole **P**. An object of height **h** is placed at point **O** at distance **u** from the pole, and its real image of height **h'** is formed at point **I** at distance **v** from the pole.

**Step 2 — Identify Similar Triangles**
The ray from the top of the object passing through **C** reflects back on itself, and the ray to **P** reflects with equal angles. Two similar triangles are formed:

Triangle formed by object and pole: $\triangle OAP$ (where A is the point where the object ray hits the mirror)
Triangle formed by image and pole: $\triangle I'A'P$

**Step 3 — Write Similarity Ratio**

$$\frac{h'}{h} = -\frac{v}{u} = m$$

(The negative sign indicates the image is inverted, so m is negative for a real image.)

**Step 4 — Apply the Focus Condition**
When the object is at infinity, the image is formed at the focal point **F**, giving **u → ∞** and **v = f**.

**Step 5 — Use the Relation for Ray Through Focus**
For a ray from the object tip through the focus reflecting parallel to the axis, and a ray through C, geometry yields:

$$\frac{h'}{h} = -\frac{f}{v - f}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the Two Expressions for Magnification**

$$-\frac{v}{u} = -\frac{f}{v - f}$$

**Step 7 — Cross-Multiply**

$$v(v - f) = f \cdot u$$

**Step 8 — Expand**

$$v^2 - vf = fu$$

**Step 9 — Divide Both Sides by uvf**

$$\frac{v^2}{uvf} - \frac{vf}{uvf} = \frac{fu}{uvf}$$

$$\frac{v}{uf} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{v}$$

**Step 10 — Rearrange to Standard Form**

$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law

**Problem**: Derive the expression $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ for a step-index optical fiber.

**Step 1 — Incident Ray Geometry**
A ray enters the fiber face from air (n₀ = 1) at external angle **θₐ** (acceptance angle), refracts to internal angle **θ₁**, and travels through the core (n₁) to strike the core-cladding interface at angle **θ₂**.

**Step 2 — Apply Snell's Law at the Input Face**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 3 — Geometric Relationship at the Interface**
The ray inside the core strikes the cladding interface. The angle of incidence at this interface is **θ₂**, and by the geometry of the triangle (sum of angles = 180°):

$$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 90° - \theta_1$$

**Step 4 — Apply Total Internal Reflection Condition**
For TIR to occur, **θ₂ ≥ θc**, where $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$. At the **critical limit**:

$$\sin\theta_2 = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 5 — Express sin θ₂ in Terms of sin θ₁**

$$\sin\theta_2 = \sin(90° - \theta_1) = \cos\theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_1}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the Two Expressions for sin θ₂**

$$\sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_1} = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 7 — Square Both Sides**

$$1 - \sin^2\theta_1 = \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for sin²θ₁**

$$\sin^2\theta_1 = 1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2} = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

**Step 9 — Take the Square Root**

$$\sin\theta_1 = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute Back into the Input-Face Snell's Law**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 11 — Define NA**

$$\boxed{\text{NA} = n_0 \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Magnification of a Simple Magnifier

**Problem**: Show that angular magnification of a simple magnifier is $M = D/f$ when the image is at infinity.

**Step 1 — Definition of Angular Magnification**
Angular magnification **M** is the ratio of the angle subtended at the eye by the image (using the magnifier) to the angle subtended by the object placed at the near point (D = 25 cm) without the magnifier.

$$M = \frac{\tan\alpha}{\tan\beta}$$

where **α** is the angle with the magnifier and **β** is the angle without the magnifier (at D = 25 cm).

**Step 2 — Object Placed at D (No Magnifier)**

$$\tan\beta = \frac{h}{D} \approx \beta \quad \text{(small angle)}$$

**Step 3 — Object Placed at Focus f (With Magnifier, Image at ∞)**
The ray leaving the magnifier is parallel, and the angle it makes with the axis at the eye is:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{h}{f} \approx \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio**

$$M = \frac{h/f}{h/D} = \frac{D}{f} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Numerical Example**:
For **f = 5 cm** magnifier:

$$M = \frac{25}{5} = 5\times$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Image Resolution of a Coherent Fiber Bundle

**Problem**: A fiberscope has a coherent bundle of 10,000 fibers arranged in a 1 cm × 1 cm square. The image is transmitted to a 5 cm × 5 cm monitor. Calculate the system magnification and the limiting resolution.

**Step 1 — Determine the Fiber Diameter**
The total area of the bundle = 1 cm² = 100 mm². With 10,000 fibers:

$$A_{fiber} = \frac{100 \text{ mm}^2}{10{,}000} = 0.01 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Fiber Diameter (assuming circular cross-section)**

$$d_f = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{fiber}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.01}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.01273} = 0.1128 \text{ mm} \approx 113 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Image Magnification (Bundle to Monitor)**

$$M_{display} = \frac{\text{Monitor size}}{\text{Bundle size}} = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{10 \text{ mm}} = 5\times$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Limiting Resolution**
The smallest resolvable feature is limited by one fiber (one "pixel"):

$$R_{obj} = \frac{d_f}{M_{obj}} \quad \text{(assume objective magnification M_obj = 1 for thin bundle face)}$$

If the objective lens magnifies the object 0.5× onto the bundle face:

$$R_{obj} = \frac{0.113 \text{ mm}}{0.5} = 0.226 \text{ mm} \approx 226 \text{ µm}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent Display Resolution**

$$R_{display} = R_{obj} \times M_{display} = 0.226 \times 5 = 1.13 \text{ mm on monitor}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Visual Testing Aid

| Inspection Scenario | Distance/Size | Curvature | Best Aid | Reason |
|---------------------|---------------|-----------|----------|--------|
| Surface crack on weld, ~2 mm long | 25 cm | Flat | Hand magnifier (10×) | Direct view, magnification sufficient |
| Inside of a fuel tank vent hole | 30 cm deep, 6 mm dia | Straight, narrow | Rigid borescope (5 mm) | Straight access, optical clarity |
| Inside a turbine engine, beyond 1 m, curved | 2 m, multiple bends | Complex | Articulating fiberscope | Flexibility + articulation |
| Long pipeline 500 m, 300 mm dia | 500 m long, straight | Mild bends | CCTV crawler | Long reach, motorized |
| Inaccessible boiler header weld | 5 m deep, dark | Curved | Video borescope | Real-time + recording + light |
| Confined space inspection of a vessel | 10 m down vertical | Ladder access | PTZ CCTV on pole | Distance, viewing multiple points |
| Micro-crack at root of gear tooth | 0.5 mm | Flat (gear) | Stereo microscope | High mag + 3D perception |
| Peek behind a pipe weld | Behind obstruction | Any | First-surface mirror | Quickest, cheapest |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Magnifier Power

**Q**: A watchmaker uses a magnifier of focal length **f = 4 cm**. Find (a) magnification for relaxed eye, (b) magnification for image at near point (25 cm).

**Solution**:
(a) Relaxed eye (image at ∞):

$$M = \frac{D}{f} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25\times$$

(b) Image at D:

$$M = 1 + \frac{D}{f} = 1 + \frac{25}{4} = 1 + 6.25 = 7.25\times$$

---

### Example 2: Fiber NA

**Q**: A step-index fiber has core refractive index **n₁ = 1.50** and cladding **n₂ = 1.45**. Find (a) NA, (b) acceptance angle in air.

**Solution**:
(a) $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{1.50^2 - 1.45^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} = 0.384$

(b) $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.384/1) = 22.6°$

---

### Example 3: Borescope FOV

**Q**: A borescope with angular FOV = **60°** is positioned **15 mm** from an internal surface. Calculate the linear field of view.

**Solution**:
$$\text{FOV}_{linear} = 2 \cdot d_{obj} \cdot \tan(\theta_{FOV}/2) = 2 \cdot 15 \cdot \tan(30°) = 30 \cdot 0.5774 = 17.32 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Example 4: CCTV Resolution

**Q**: A CCTV system has **580 TV lines** horizontal resolution and a sensor with **6.4 µm pixel pitch**. Calculate the equivalent limiting resolution in mm on a 1 m × 1 m field of view at 5 m distance.

**Solution**:
Total TV lines = 580 (limiting resolution = 1/(2×580) lp per picture height). The system resolves a feature size:

$$R = \frac{6.4 \text{ µm} \times 5 \text{ m}}{50 \text{ mm (lens focal length)}} = \text{object-side resolution (approx.)}$$

(For 1 m field of view at 5 m, the angular resolution gives R ≈ 1.7 mm at object.)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q:** Define **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber. State its significance in fiberscope design.

**Model Answer:**

> **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is a dimensionless quantity that defines the light-gathering ability of an optical fiber, given by:
> $$\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_0 \sin\theta_a$$
> where **n₁** is the core refractive index, **n₂** is the cladding refractive index, **n₀** is the refractive index of the surrounding medium (≈1 for air), and **θₐ** is the maximum acceptance angle.
>
> **Significance in fiberscope design:**
> 1. **Higher NA** → larger acceptance angle → brighter image (more light collected from the test object).
> 2. NA determines the **resolving power** of the fiber (via Rayleigh criterion: R = 1.22 λ/NA).
> 3. For NDT in poorly lit cavities (e.g., turbine engines), a **high-NA fiberscope (NA ≈ 0.55)** is preferred for adequate illumination.
> 4. NA also affects the **flexibility** of the bundle: a higher NA fiber allows a smaller bending radius without light loss.

**Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Definition with formula → **1.5 marks**
- 2–3 points on significance → **1.5 marks**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between a **boroscope** and a **fiberscope** in NDT.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Borescope | Fiberscope |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| **Construction** | Rigid tube with relay lenses | Flexible tube with coherent fiber bundle |
| **Image transmission** | Lenses (Hoplins/GRIN) | Optical fibers (TIR) |
| **Length** | Limited (~1.5 m max practical) | Long (up to 12 m or more) |
| **Curved access** | Cannot negotiate bends | Articulating tip (4-way, ±120°) |
| **Resolution** | Higher (no fiber pixelation) | Limited by fiber diameter (~5–25 µm) |
| **Cost** | Lower (simple) | Higher (complex bundle) |
| **Application** | Engine cylinder bores, gun barrels | Gas turbine HPT blades, curved piping |
| **Illumination** | External light via fiber guide | Built-in coherent + incoherent bundle |

**Marks Distribution:**
- At least **4 correct points of difference** → **3 marks**
- Tabular comparison format preferred by KTU examiners.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern**: Two alternatives offered, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer **ONE choice**.

---

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Q: (a)** Describe in detail the **construction and working principle of a fiberscope**, with a neat sketch showing the optical path. Explain the role of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** in guiding light through the fiber. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A borescope has an objective lens of focal length **5 mm** and an eyepiece of focal length **25 mm**. The separation between them is **180 mm**. The object is placed **6 mm** from the objective. Calculate: (i) the magnification of the objective, (ii) the magnification of the eyepiece (assume final image at infinity), (iii) the overall magnification, and (iv) the length of the borescope tube. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q (a):

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A fiberscope consists of the following main components:

1. **Light Source**: A high-intensity halogen or LED source (150 W typical) provides cool illumination via a fiber-optic light guide.
2. **Illumination Bundle**: Thousands of randomly arranged (incoherent) optical fibers carry light from the source to the distal tip.
3. **Imaging Bundle**: A coherent bundle of **10,000 to 30,000 aligned optical fibers** carries the image back. Each fiber acts as one pixel.
4. **Objective Lens**: A miniature lens at the tip focuses the object image onto the entrance face of the imaging bundle.
5. **Articulation Section**: A set of control wires deflects the distal tip in 2 or 4 directions (typically up to ±120°).
6. **Insertion Tube**: A stainless-steel braided sheath protecting the fiber bundles; length 1.5 m to 12 m.
7. **Eyepiece / Camera Adapter**: A lens at the proximal end that magnifies the image for the eye or couples to a camera/CCTV.

**Working Principle (3.5 marks):**
- Light from the source enters the illumination bundle, travels by **TIR** to the distal tip, and illuminates the internal surface.
- The objective lens collects the reflected light and forms a real image on the **entrance face** of the imaging bundle.
- Each fiber in the coherent bundle transmits **one pixel** of this image to the proximal end via repeated TIR at the core-cladding interface.
- The eyepiece magnifies this transmitted image for the inspector's eye or for a CCTV camera.

**Role of TIR (must explicitly state):**
- TIR occurs when light travels from a denser medium (core, n₁) to a rarer medium (cladding, n₂) at an angle greater than the **critical angle θc = sin⁻¹(n₂/n₁)**.
- For typical glass fiber (n₁ = 1.62, n₂ = 1.52), θc ≈ 69.8°.
- TIR ensures **100 % internal reflection** with **no light loss** (theoretically), allowing efficient image transmission over long distances and around bends.

**Sketch (KTU expects a labelled diagram):**
A fibrescope with light source, illumination bundle, objective lens, coherent imaging bundle, articulation wires, eyepiece, and the internal surface being inspected — labelled clearly.

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q (b):

**Given:**
- f_o = 5 mm, f_e = 25 mm, L (tube length) = 180 mm, u_o = 6 mm

**(i) Magnification of the objective lens:**
Using the thin-lens formula:

$$\frac{1}{v_o} = \frac{1}{f_o} - \frac{1}{u_o} = \frac{1}{5} - \frac{1}{6} = \frac{6 - 5}{30} = \frac{1}{30}$$

So **v_o = 30 mm** (image distance from objective).

$$m_o = -\frac{v_o}{u_o} = -\frac{30}{6} = -5\times$$

**Magnification of objective = −5× (real, inverted image)**

**(ii) Magnification of the eyepiece (image at infinity):**

$$m_e = \frac{D}{f_e} = \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm}} = 10\times$$

**Magnification of eyepiece = +10× (virtual, erect)**

**(iii) Overall magnification:**

$$M_{total} = m_o \times m_e = (-5) \times (+10) = -50\times$$

**Overall = −50× (final image is inverted and 50× enlarged)**

**(iv) Length of the borescope tube:**
The tube length = v_o + u_e, where u_e is the object distance for the eyepiece. For the final image at infinity, u_e = f_e = 25 mm.

$$L = v_o + u_e = 30 + 25 = 55 \text{ mm}$$

(If the given L = 180 mm, then the discrepancy suggests the formula used by KTU is:

$$L = v_o + f_e \quad \text{or} \quad L = v_o + u_e \text{ (eyepiece object distance)}$$

where u_e is calculated from the given 180 mm:

$$u_e = 180 - 30 = 150 \text{ mm}$$

Then $m_e = 1 + D/f_e \cdot$ adjustment = $1 + 250/25$ — but if the image is at infinity, the question expects the relaxed eye condition: $m_e = D/f_e$:

$$m_e = 250/25 = 10\times \quad \text{(matches part ii)}$$

**Final Tube Length = v_o + u_e = 30 + 150 = 180 mm ✓** — consistent with the data given.)

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a rigid borescope**. List **any four** important performance parameters of a borescope with their typical values. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A magnifier of focal length **6.25 cm** is used by a quality inspector. Calculate: (i) the angular magnification when the image is formed at the **least distance of distinct vision**, (ii) the angular magnification when the image is formed at **infinity**, and (iii) the **power** of the lens in dioptres. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q (a):

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
A rigid borescope consists of:
1. **Outer stainless-steel tube** (rigid, length 50–1500 mm, diameter 1.7–25 mm).
2. **Objective lens** at the distal tip (forms real inverted intermediate image).
3. **Relay lens system** (Hoplins, Roesch, or GRIN rod) — transfers the image along the tube.
4. **Eyepiece** at the proximal end (magnifies for eye or camera).
5. **Illumination channel** — fiber-optic light guide carrying cold light from an external source.
6. **Focusing mechanism** (manual or fixed focus).
7. **Direction-of-view (DOV) prism** at the tip (for 30°, 70°, or 90° viewing).

**Working (3.5 marks):**
- The borescope is inserted into the cavity (e.g., engine bore, pipe).
- Cold light from the source enters the illumination fiber bundle and exits at the tip, lighting up the internal surface.
- The objective lens forms a real, inverted, reduced image at its focal plane inside the tube.
- The relay lens system transfers this image, image-by-image, down the length of the tube without significant loss.
- The eyepiece at the other end magnifies the final image for the inspector's eye (or couples it to a CCTV camera).

**Four Performance Parameters (any 4):**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Field of View (FOV) | 30° – 100° |
| Direction of View (DOV) | 0°, 30°, 70°, 90° |
| Depth of Field (DOF) | 3 mm – ∞ |
| Probe Diameter | 1.7 – 25 mm |
| Probe Length | 50 – 1500 mm |
| Magnification | 1× – 50× |
| Resolution | 50 – 100 µm |

**Marks:** Construction (2) + Working (2) + 4 parameters (1.5) + Sketch labels (1.5) = 7 marks

---

#### 🔑 Model Solution for Q (b):

**Given:** f = 6.25 cm, D = 25 cm (standard)

**(i) Image at least distance of distinct vision (D = 25 cm):**

$$M_1 = 1 + \frac{D}{f} = 1 + \frac{25}{6.25} = 1 + 4 = 5\times$$

**(ii) Image at infinity:**

$$M_2 = \frac{D}{f} = \frac{25}{6.25} = 4\times$$

**(iii) Power of the lens:**

$$P = \frac{1}{f \text{ (in metres)}} = \frac{1}{0.0625 \text{ m}} = 16 \text{ dioptres (D)}$$

**Marks:**
- (i) Formula + substitution + answer → 2 marks
- (ii) Formula + substitution + answer → 2 marks
- (iii) Formula + unit conversion + answer → 3 marks
= **7 marks total**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors**: Students frequently forget that for a mirror/lens, image distance and magnification carry signs. KTU deducts **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing the negative sign on real/inverted image magnification.
> 2. **D = 25 cm Mistake**: Using **D = 25 cm** without explicit mention loses marks. Always **write "least distance of distinct vision D = 25 cm"** first.
> 3. **NA Without Units**: Numerical Aperture is **dimensionless**. Writing "NA = 0.5 mm" is a **0.5-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Mixing Boroscope & Fiberscope**: In definitions, students often confuse the two. KTU expects clear distinction: **rigid + relay lens** (borescope) vs. **flexible + fiber bundle** (fiberscope).
> 5. **Forgetting TIR Condition**: When explaining fiberscope, students mention "fiber" but **omit the critical-angle condition (θ > θc)**. This costs **1 full mark** in long answers.
> 6. **Missing Labelled Diagram**: In 7-mark questions, a **neat labelled sketch is mandatory** (typically 1–1.5 marks allocated). Skipping it is a direct loss.
> 7. **Unit Conversion for Power of Lens**: Forgetting to convert f from cm to m before computing Power (D) is a classic error — loses 1 mark.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> 📋 **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Visual Testing Aids** extend the eye to inaccessible areas — they are the **simplest, oldest, and most-used** NDT method.
- ✅ **Mirrors** obey $\theta_i = \theta_r$; **periscopes** use two parallel mirrors to redirect line of sight.
- ✅ **Magnifier** is a convex lens with object inside focal length: $M = D/f$ (at ∞) or $M = 1 + D/f$ (at D).
- ✅ **D = 25 cm** is the standard least distance of distinct vision — write it explicitly in answers.
- ✅ **Borescope = rigid + relay lens system**; lengths 50 mm to 1.5 m; cannot bend.
- ✅ **Fiberscope = flexible + coherent fiber bundle**; lengths up to 12 m; 4-way articulation.
- ✅ **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** is the principle of fiber-optic image transmission; condition: $\theta > \theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- ✅ **Numerical Aperture (NA)** = $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ — dimensionless, indicates light-gathering ability.
- ✅ **Borescope Resolution** (Rayleigh): $R = 1.22 \lambda / \text{NA}$ — higher NA → better resolution.
- ✅ **Coherent bundle** = aligned fibers (one pixel each); **Incoherent bundle** = random (for light only).
- ✅ **FOV formula**: $\text{FOV} = 2 d_{obj} \tan(\theta_{FOV}/2)$.
- ✅ **CCTV** = electronic successor to optical borescope; CCD/CMOS camera + cable + monitor + recorder.
- ✅ **Modern trends**: video borescopes, wireless endoscopes, drone-mounted CCTV, and crawler cameras are replacing traditional CCTV.
- ✅ **Power of lens (P)** = $1/f(\text{m})$ in dioptres — important for magnifier specification.
- ✅ **ASME Section V Article 9** governs VT; **ASTM E2702** specifies standard practice.
- ✅ **Minimum inspection illumination ≈ 500 lux** as per KTU/ASME recommendation.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — VISUAL TESTING AIDS**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Sources and Special Lighting Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Light Sources and Special Lighting Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Light Sources in NDT

**Light sources** are the foundational illumination tools in **Visual Testing (VT)** — the most fundamental and widely used Non-Destructive Testing method. In KTU's 2024 scheme for PEMET636, Module 1 establishes that the quality of visual inspection is directly governed by the **quality, intensity, directionality, and spectral content** of the light used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Light Sources and Special Lighting Systems form the precursor to understanding **Visual Testing (VT)**. Without proper illumination, even the most trained inspector cannot reliably detect surface defects like cracks, porosity, laps, or corrosion.

### Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

> **Light Source (KTU Definition):** A light source is any device, natural or artificial, that emits electromagnetic radiation in the **visible spectrum (≈ 380 nm to 750 nm)**, and in special NDT applications extends to the **ultraviolet (UV, < 380 nm)** and **infrared (IR, > 750 nm)** regions, used to illuminate the test surface to enable visual detection of discontinuities.

### Key Photometric Quantities (Foundation Concepts)

For KTU examinations, four photometric terms form the bedrock of light source characterization:

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|------|------------------|
| **Luminous Intensity** | I | **Candela (cd)** | Light emitted per unit solid angle |
| **Luminous Flux** | Φ | **Lumen (lm)** | Total visible light energy emitted per second |
| **Illuminance** | E | **Lux (lx) = lm/m²** | Light incident on a surface |
| **Luminance** | L | **cd/m²** | Light emitted/reflected from a surface per unit area |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Detective's Flashlight":**
> Imagine a detective searching for fingerprints in a dimly lit room. The quality of the flashlight determines the success of the investigation. A weak, diffused light (like a candle) hides evidence. A focused, intense beam (like a forensic torch) reveals hidden clues. Similarly, in NDT:
> - **Daylight = white light inspection** (general surface defects)
> - **UV lamp = fluorescent penetrant inspection** (subsurface cracks)
> - **Fiber optic illuminator = borescope inspection** (inside engine cylinders)
> - **Polarized light = stress pattern analysis** (transparent materials)
>
