- [x] **Governing standard**: ASTM E1417 / E1417M; ASME Section V, Article 6.
- [x] **Sensitivity scale**: ASME Level ½ (lowest) → Level 4 (highest), with Type I Fluorescent + Dry Powder Form a giving the highest sensitivity.
- [x] **Always state numerical values** (viscosity in cSt, particle size in µm, UV wavelength in nm) in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Penetrants and Developers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Penetrants and Developers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Foundational Definition

In **Liquid Penetrant Testing (LPT)**, also called **Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)** or **Penetrant Flaw Detection**, the *penetrant* and the *developer* are the two consumable chemical agents that jointly govern the sensitivity and reliability of the entire inspection process.

As per **ASTM E1417 / E165** and **IS 3658 (KTU syllabus reference standard)**, the materials are classified strictly by their chemical family, physical state, removal mechanism, and post-emulsifier behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Penetrant (Definition):** A low-surface-tension, low-viscosity liquid (containing either fluorescent dye or visible dye) that is applied to a clean, dry test surface and is drawn into surface-breaking discontinuities by **capillary action**. After a defined *dwell time*, the excess penetrant is removed and a developer is applied to *draw back* the trapped penetrant and form a visible indication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Developer (Definition):** A particulate, absorbent medium (dry powder, wet suspension, or solvent-borne film) applied over the cleaned surface after penetrant removal. The developer acts as a *blotter*, reversing capillary action to pull penetrant out of the flaw and spreading it into a wide, contrasting, visible indication.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a clean white handkerchief and you accidentally drop a drop of **dark ink** on it. The ink instantly *wicks* along the fibres of the cloth and spreads. Now if you sprinkle **fine white chalk powder** (the developer) on the wet spot, the chalk *pulls* the ink back to the surface, making the spot appear darker and broader against the white background.

- The **ink** = **Penetrant** (enters the crack due to capillary action).
- The **chalk powder** = **Developer** (pulls penetrant out of the crack and spreads it for visibility).
- The **handkerchief fibres** = **Surface-breaking discontinuity** (crack, porosity, laps, cold shuts, etc.).

This simple analogy is the foundation of LPT — a *two-way capillary action* process.

### 1.3 Salient Physical Constants and Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Governing Code |
|---|---|---|
| Surface Tension of Penetrant | **≤ 30 dyne/cm** (low) | ASTM E165 |
| Penetrant Viscosity | **1 – 10 cSt (centistokes)** | ASTM E165 |
| Operating Temperature | **15 °C – 52 °C** | ASTM E1417 |
| Inspection Medium | Liquid (oil or water-based) | IS 3658 |
| Dwell Time | **5 – 60 minutes** | ASTM E1417 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of penetrants is by **Type (I, II)** and by **Method (water-washable, post-emulsifiable, solvent-removable)**. Developers are classified by **Form (1, 2, 3, 4)** and by **Type (a, b, c, d, e)**. Memorise this dual classification — it appears in every KTU Part-A paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Penetrants (ASTM E165 / E1417)

Penetrants are classified along **two independent axes**:

#### 2.1.1 Type Classification (by Dye Used)

> [!NOTE]
> **Type I — Fluorescent Penetrants**
> - Contain fluorescent dyes (e.g., C-7, C-9 hydrocarbons like PBO, PBD, BBQ).
> - Visible only under **UV-A (Black Light) of 365 nm** wavelength.
> - Sensitivity: **Highest (Level 4)** under proper conditions.
> - Indications appear as **bright yellow-green glows** on dark background.
> - Used for: aerospace, weld inspections, critical safety components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Type II — Visible (Dye) Penetrants**
> - Contain **red dyes** (typically **Sudan Red IV, Oil Red O**) dissolved in carrier oil.
> - Visible under normal **white light (≥ 100 ft-candles / 1100 lux)**.
> - Lower sensitivity than fluorescent (Level 1–2).
> - Used for: field inspections, large structures, on-site weld testing.

#### 2.1.2 Method Classification (by Removal Mechanism)

| Method | Code | Mechanism | Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water-Washable | **A** | Direct water rinse | Low–Medium |
| Post-Emulsifiable, Lipophilic | **B** | Emulsifier applied **before** water rinse | High |
| Post-Emulsifiable, Hydrophilic | **C** | Water-based emulsifier applied **before** water rinse | High |
| Solvent-Removable | **D** | Wipe with solvent (manual) | Variable |

**Step-by-step logic of the emulsifier (Why & How):**

1. Fresh penetrant is **highly oil-soluble** and rejects water — it cannot be removed by water alone (this protects penetrant *inside* the crack).
2. The **emulsifier** is a surfactant that bridges the oil-water interface, turning the surface penetrant into a water-miscible emulsion that *rinses off easily* — while penetrant trapped deep inside the crack remains protected from the rinse.
3. This is the *key engineering trick* that allows high sensitivity with water-based cleaning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Lipophilic* (oil-loving, applied as oil bath) vs *Hydrophilic* (water-loving, applied as water solution). In the exam, always mention the *order of application* — emulsifier is applied **AFTER dwell time** and **BEFORE water rinse**.

### 2.2 Sensitivity Levels (KTU High-Yield)

| Level | Description | Typical Penetrant |
|---|---|---|
| **Level ½** | Ultra-low sensitivity | Type II visible (rarely used) |
| **Level 1** | Low sensitivity | Type II visible, Type I low |
| **Level 2** | Medium sensitivity | Standard Type I fluorescent |
| **Level 3** | High sensitivity | High-performance Type I |
| **Level 4** | Ultra-high sensitivity | Specialised Type I (e.g., aerospace) |

### 2.3 Classification of Developers (ASTM E165)

Developers are classified by **Form** (physical state) and **Type** (carrier):

#### 2.3.1 Form Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Form 1 — Dry Powder Developer**
> - Inert, fine white powder: **Talc, Magnesium Oxide, Calcium Carbonate, Silica, TiO₂**.
> - Particle size: **1 – 10 microns**.
> - Applied by **dipping, dusting, or electrostatic spray**.
> - Used with: water-washable & post-emulsifiable fluorescent penetrants.
> - Highest contrast, but can obscure fine indications if applied too thick.

> [!NOTE]
> **Form 2 — Aqueous (Wet) Developer**
> - Powder suspended or dissolved in water with additives (anti-foam, corrosion inhibitor, wetter).
> - **Type a:** Water-soluble developer (transparent, soluble in water).
> - **Type b:** Water-suspendible developer (insoluble powder in suspension — must be stirred).
> - Applied by **dipping or spraying**.
> - Forms a thin white film on the surface after drying.

> [!NOTE]
> **Form 3 — Liquid Film Developer (Non-Aqueous)**
> - Developer particles suspended in a **volatile organic carrier** (e.g., isopropanol, acetone, naphtha).
> - Dries rapidly to a thin film.
> - **Type a:** Solvent-based (fast drying, field use).
> - **Type b:** Water-based liquid film (slower, controlled shop use).

> [!NOTE]
> **Form 4 — Special Developers**
> - **Type a:** Solvent-based for special applications (high temp, residual penetrant).
> - **Type b:** Two-part developers (rarely used).
> - **Type c:** Fluorescent developers (used with visible penetrant, niche).

#### 2.3.2 Why Developers Are Essential — The Capillary Reversal Principle

When developer is applied:
- The fine particles of developer **wick the penetrant out of the flaw** by reverse capillary action.
- The penetrant **spreads laterally** across the developer film (sometimes 10–100× wider than the actual flaw width).
- This *spreading* (called **bleed-out**) makes sub-visual cracks **visible to the naked eye** or under UV light.
- Developer also provides a **white, contrasting background** for visible penetrants, and a **dark, non-fluorescent background** for fluorescent penetrants.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The LPT process is governed by **Washburn's Equation** for capillary penetration depth, which KTU examiners expect students to quote:

$$L^2 = \frac{\gamma \, r \, t \, \cos\theta}{2\eta}$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | Engineering Influence |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L$ | Depth of penetrant penetration into flaw | mm | Increases with $t$ (dwell time) |
| $\gamma$ | Surface tension of penetrant | dyne/cm or N/m | **Lower is better** for LPT |
| $r$ | Effective flaw radius | microns | **Smaller flaws** are harder to detect |
| $t$ | Dwell (contact) time | seconds | Longer dwell = deeper penetration |
| $\theta$ | Contact angle between penetrant & flaw wall | degrees | **Lower $\theta$** = better wetting |
| $\eta$ | Dynamic viscosity of penetrant | cP or cSt | **Lower $\eta$** = faster flow |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Washburn's equation explains *why* we use **low-viscosity, low-surface-tension, low-contact-angle penetrants** — to maximise $L$ (penetration depth) for a given dwell time. This is the *fundamental design principle* behind high-sensitivity aerospace penetrants (e.g., Magnaflux SKL-SP, Sherwin DP-40).

### 2.5 Penetrant Families — Chemical Composition

| Component | Typical % by Weight | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Dye (fluorescent or visible)** | 0.1 – 5 % | Provides visibility |
| **Carrier oil (petroleum distillate)** | 60 – 95 % | Dissolves dye, controls viscosity |
| **Surfactant / Wetting agent** | 0.5 – 5 % | Reduces contact angle, improves wetting |
| **Emulsifier (built-in or separate)** | 0 – 5 % | For post-emulsifiable systems |
| **Plasticiser / Stabiliser** | 0.1 – 1 % | Improves film flexibility |
| **Solvent (for solvent-removable)** | Balance | Adjusts evaporation rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The role of the **carrier oil** is often ignored by students. The carrier must have a *flash point > 93 °C* for safety and must not leave oily residue that interferes with subsequent NDT (like MPI or UT).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Washburn's Equation (Capillary Penetration)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the most-tested LPT concept in KTU Part-B. The examiner often asks: *"Justify the design choice of low-viscosity penetrant using capillary physics."*

#### Step 1 — Force Balance on a Liquid Column in a Capillary

Consider a vertical cylindrical capillary of radius $r$ filled with liquid of surface tension $\gamma$ and contact angle $\theta$ with the wall. The liquid rises to height $h$ (or in LPT, the liquid *penetrates to depth* $L$ into a horizontal crack).

**Upward force** (capillary, due to surface tension acting around the circumference):

$$F_{\text{cap}} = 2 \pi r \, \gamma \cos\theta$$

**Downward force** (weight of the liquid column, opposing rise / aiding penetration horizontally via hydrostatic pressure difference):

$$F_{\text{grav}} = \pi r^2 \, \rho g h$$

#### Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition (Steady-State Penetration)

At equilibrium, $F_{\text{cap}} = F_{\text{grav}}$:

$$2 \pi r \, \gamma \cos\theta = \pi r^2 \, \rho g h$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

This is the *static Jurin's Law*. However, in LPT, the time-dependence is critical because we are not at equilibrium — we are *applying the penetrant for a finite dwell time*.

#### Step 3 — Time-Dependent Washburn Equation (Poiseuille Flow)

For a Newtonian liquid entering a horizontal capillary of length $L$ under capillary pressure $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$, the volumetric flow rate is given by **Hagen-Poiseuille law**:

$$Q = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\pi r^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

Since $V = \pi r^2 L$, we have $dV = \pi r^2 dL$. Substituting:

$$\pi r^2 \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{\pi r^4}{8 \eta L} \cdot \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta L}$$

#### Step 4 — Integration to Obtain Washburn's Equation

Separate variables and integrate from $L=0$ at $t=0$ to $L=L$ at $t=t$:

$$\int_{0}^{L} L \, dL = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

$$\frac{L^2}{2} = \frac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \cdot t$$

$$L^2 = \frac{r \, \gamma \cos\theta \, t}{2 \eta}$$

This is the famous **Washburn's Equation** for capillary penetration:

$$L^2 = \frac{\gamma \, r \, t \, \cos\theta}{2\eta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Penetration depth $L$ scales with $\sqrt{t}$ (square-root of time). Hence doubling the dwell time only increases penetration by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \times$, not 2×. This is why excessively long dwell times are inefficient — there are **diminishing returns**.

#### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A crack has effective radius $r = 5 \,\mu m$. The penetrant has $\gamma = 28 \,\text{dyne/cm}$, $\eta = 3 \,\text{cP}$, $\cos\theta = 0.95$. Calculate the penetration depth after $t = 600 \,s$ (10 min dwell).

**Step 5a — Unit conversion:**

$$r = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{cm}, \quad \eta = 3 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{Poise (P)}, \quad \gamma = 28 \, \text{dyne/cm}$$

**Step 5b — Substitution:**

$$L^2 = \frac{(28)(5 \times 10^{-4})(600)(0.95)}{2 \times (3 \times 10^{-2})}$$

**Step 5c — Numerator evaluation:**

$$28 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0140$$
$$0.0140 \times 600 = 8.4$$
$$8.4 \times 0.95 = 7.98$$

**Step 5d — Denominator evaluation:**

$$2 \times 0.03 = 0.06$$

**Step 5e — Final ratio:**

$$L^2 = \frac{7.98}{0.06} = 133$$

$$L = \sqrt{133} \approx 11.53 \, \text{cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why penetrants can travel **several centimetres** along a tight crack in 10 minutes — but a crack of effective radius 1 µm (very tight, like in a forged turbine blade) would only achieve $\approx 5.16 \, \text{cm}$ under identical conditions. This shows the *engineering challenge* of detecting micro-cracks.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Penetrant Method Comparison

| Parameter | Water-Washable (A) | Post-Emulsifiable Lipophilic (B) | Post-Emulsifiable Hydrophilic (C) | Solvent-Removable (D) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Emulsifier Required? | No | Yes (oil-based) | Yes (water-based) | No |
| Rinse Step | Direct water | After emulsifier | After emulsifier | Wipe + solvent |
| Sensitivity | Low–Medium | High | High | Variable |
| Field Suitability | Poor (needs water) | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** (portable) |
| Over-wash Risk | High | Medium | Medium | High (manual) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Aerospace Use | Limited | **Yes** | **Yes** | Limited |
| Welder's Favourite? | No | No | No | **Yes** (on-site) |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Developer Form Comparison

| Parameter | Form 1 (Dry Powder) | Form 2 (Aqueous Wet) | Form 3 (Liquid Film) | Form 4 (Special) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier | None | Water | Volatile solvent | Various |
| Application Method | Dust / dip / spray | Dip / spray | Spray | Varies |
| Drying Time | None | 5–20 min (oven/air) | < 1 min | Varies |
| Background Contrast | **Highest** | High | Good | Application-specific |
| Field Use | Limited | Limited | **Excellent** | Limited |
| Indications Width | Widest (over-spread) | Medium | Medium–Narrow | Controlled |
| Best Match | Fluorescent penetrant | Either | Visible / Field | Specialty |
| Inspection Speed | Slow | Medium | **Fast** | Variable |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU-marker favourite):**
> - **Dry powder** ↔ **Fluorescent penetrant** = *highest sensitivity* combo for shop use.
> - **Liquid film developer** ↔ **Solvent-removable visible penetrant** = *most portable* combo for on-site weld inspection.
> - **Aqueous wet developer** ↔ **Post-emulsifiable penetrant** = *balanced combo* for production-line high-volume inspection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LPT Process (with Penetrant-Developer Interaction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Surface Preparation] --> B[Step 2: Penetrant Application]
    B --> C[Step 3: Dwell Time - Capillary Action]
    C --> D{Step 4: Excess Removal}
    D -- Water Wash --> E1[Method A: Water-Washable]
    D -- Emulsifier + Water --> E2[Method B/C: Post-Emulsifiable]
    D -- Solvent Wipe --> E3[Method D: Solvent-Removable]
    E1 --> F[Step 5: Surface Drying]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Step 6: Developer Application]
    G -- Dry Powder --> H1[Form 1 Developer]
    G -- Water Suspension --> H2[Form 2 Developer]
    G -- Solvent Spray --> H3[Form 3 Developer]
    H1 --> I[Step 7: Bleed-Out - Reverse Capillary]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Step 8: Inspection]
    J -- UV-A 365nm --> K1[Type I Fluorescent]
    J -- White Light 1100 lux --> K2[Type II Visible]
    K1 --> L[Step 9: Evaluation and Recording]
    K2 --> L
```

### 4.2 Penetrant Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Penetrant] --> T1[Type I - Fluorescent]
    P --> T2[Type II - Visible Dye]
    T1 --> M1[Method A: Water-Washable]
    T1 --> M2[Method B: Post-Emulsifiable Lipophilic]
    T1 --> M3[Method C: Post-Emulsifiable Hydrophilic]
    T1 --> M4[Method D: Solvent-Removable]
    T2 --> M5[Method A: Water-Washable]
    T2 --> M6[Method B: Post-Emulsifiable Lipophilic]
    T2 --> M7[Method C: Post-Emulsifiable Hydrophilic]
    T2 --> M8[Method D: Solvent-Removable]
```

### 4.3 Developer Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DV[Developer] --> F1[Form 1: Dry Powder]
    DV --> F2[Form 2: Aqueous Wet]
    DV --> F3[Form 3: Liquid Film]
    DV --> F4[Form 4: Special]
    F1 --> F1a[Talc]
    F1 --> F1b[Magnesium Oxide]
    F1 --> F1c[Calcium Carbonate]
    F1 --> F1d[Silica / TiO2]
    F2 --> F2a[Type a: Water-Soluble]
    F2 --> F2b[Type b: Water-Suspendible]
    F3 --> F3a[Type a: Solvent-Based]
    F3 --> F3b[Type b: Water-Based Liquid Film]
    F4 --> F4a[Solvent-Based Specialty]
    F4 --> F4b[Two-Part]
    F4 --> F4c[Fluorescent Developer]
```

### 4.4 Capillary Action Schematic (Penetrant + Developer Interaction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRACK [Surface-Breaking Discontinuity]
        direction TB
        TOP[Top Surface of Test Piece]
        WALL1[Flaw Wall Left]
        WALL2[Flaw Wall Right]
        BOT[Crack Tip]
    end
    PEN[Penetrant Liquid] -- Forward Capillary --> CRACK
    CRACK -- Reverse Capillary Bleed-out --> DEV[Developer Powder]
    DEV --> IND[Visible Indication on Surface]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Classify penetrants used in Liquid Penetrant Testing based on (a) the type of dye used, and (b) the method of removal of excess penetrant. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*(a) Classification by Dye Type:*
1. **Type I – Fluorescent Penetrants:** Contain fluorescent dyes (e.g., PBO – Phenylbenzoxazole). Visible only under **UV-A black light (365 nm)**. Sensitivity: highest.
2. **Type II – Visible Dye Penetrants:** Contain red dyes (e.g., **Sudan Red IV**). Visible under normal white light.

*(b) Classification by Removal Method:*
1. **Method A – Water-Washable:** Removed by direct water rinse.
2. **Method B – Post-Emulsifiable, Lipophilic:** Emulsifier (oil-based) applied *after* dwell, then water rinse.
3. **Method C – Post-Emulsifiable, Hydrophilic:** Water-based emulsifier applied *after* dwell, then water rinse.
4. **Method D – Solvent-Removable:** Wiped manually with solvent (e.g., acetone).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the role of a developer in Liquid Penetrant Testing. Name any two forms of developers with their applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of Developer (2 marks):**
The developer performs three critical functions:
1. **Reverse capillary action:** Draws penetrant out of the flaw (bleed-out).
2. **Spreading:** Spreads penetrant laterally over the developer film, making sub-visual cracks visible.
3. **Background contrast:** Provides a contrasting (white) background to make red/fluorescent indications visible.

**Two forms of developers (1 mark):**
1. **Form 1 – Dry Powder Developer (Talc/MgO):** Used in shop inspection with fluorescent penetrant; highest contrast; applied by dusting or dipping.
2. **Form 3 – Liquid Film Developer (Non-Aqueous):** Used in field inspection; sprayed from aerosol can; fast drying; pairs well with solvent-removable visible penetrant.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module 1 carries *internal choice* — the student answers either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of ASTM E1417 classification, describe the **four methods of penetrant removal** in detail. Why is the post-emulsifiable method considered the highest sensitivity method? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive **Washburn's equation** for capillary penetration of a penetrant into a surface-breaking crack and explain the engineering significance of each parameter. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Step | Marks | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Define 4 methods | 2 | A, B, C, D with one-line description each |
| Detail Post-emulsifiable | 2 | Lipophilic vs Hydrophilic emulsifier chemistry |
| Why highest sensitivity | 2 | Controlled removal → no over-wash of crack penetrant |
| Conclusion | 1 | ASTM E1417 reference, sensitivity Level 3–4 |

**Detailed answer:**

1. **Method A – Water-Washable:** Penetrant contains built-in emulsifier. After dwell, surface penetrant is removed by direct water spray. *Drawback:* aggressive rinse may also wash penetrant from shallow flaws → lower sensitivity.
2. **Method B – Post-Emulsifiable, Lipophilic:** Emulsifier (oil-soluble surfactant like Span-80) is applied *after* dwell. It forms an oil-in-water emulsion on the surface. Gentle water rinse removes the emulsified surface layer *without disturbing penetrant in the crack*. *Highest sensitivity.*
3. **Method C – Post-Emulsifiable, Hydrophilic:** Emulsifier is water-soluble (e.g., Tween-80). Applied as a separate dip or spray. Works on a wider range of penetrant families.
4. **Method D – Solvent-Removable:** Used in field inspections. Excess penetrant is wiped off with a solvent-dampened cloth. *Skill-dependent* — over-wiping can lose crack penetrant.

**Why Post-emulsifiable is highest sensitivity:**
- The penetrant inside the crack is *protected* by its own oil-soluble nature, resisting the emulsifier.
- Only surface penetrant is emulsified, giving *controlled, complete* removal.
- This preserves deep and shallow crack penetrant, allowing detection of **fine, tight cracks (Level 3–4)**.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

*(Step-by-step derivation as given in Section 3.1 above; KTU examiner expects each equation with a one-line interpretation.)*

| Step | Marks | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Setup (capillary force) | 2 | $F_{\text{cap}} = 2\pi r \gamma \cos\theta$ |
| Hagen-Poiseuille | 2 | $Q = \pi r^4 \Delta P / 8\eta L$ |
| Integration | 2 | $\int L \, dL = \int \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta} dt$ |
| Final + Interpretation | 1 | $L^2 = \frac{\gamma r t \cos\theta}{2\eta}$, $L \propto \sqrt{t}$ |

**Engineering significance of each parameter (additional 1 mark if discussed in viva-style):**
- $\gamma \downarrow$ → small flaws wetted more easily.
- $r \uparrow$ → wider flaws penetrated faster (but larger flaws are easy anyway).
- $t \uparrow$ → diminishing returns ($\sqrt{t}$).
- $\theta \downarrow$ → better wetting of oily/dirty crack walls.
- $\eta \downarrow$ → faster flow into fine cracks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Classify developers as per ASTM E165 standard. Explain the working of *Form 1 (Dry Powder)* and *Form 3 (Liquid Film)* developers with neat sketches. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** What are the desirable properties of (i) a good penetrant and (ii) a good developer? Compare the suitability of dry powder vs. liquid film developers for **field inspection of welded joints**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

Developers are classified by **Form** (physical state) into 4 types as per ASTM E165:

| Form | Description |
|---|---|
| **Form 1** | Dry powder (talc, MgO, CaCO₃) |
| **Form 2** | Aqueous (water-soluble Type a / water-suspendible Type b) |
| **Form 3** | Liquid film (solvent-based Type a / water-based Type b) |
| **Form 4** | Special developers (high-temp, fluorescent, two-part) |

**Working of Form 1 (Dry Powder) Developer:**
- Fine white powder (particle size 1–10 µm) is dusted, dipped, or electrostatically sprayed on the cleaned surface.
- Penetrant trapped in the crack is drawn out by **reverse capillary action** into the porous powder matrix.
- The penetrant spreads laterally, forming a wide, bright indication (bleed-out) against the white background.
- **Best for:** Type I fluorescent penetrants in shop inspections. Indication is widest → very easy to see.

**Working of Form 3 (Liquid Film) Developer:**
- Developer particles are suspended in a fast-evaporating solvent (e.g., isopropanol).
- Sprayed from aerosol can on the test surface.
- Solvent evaporates in **< 1 minute**, leaving a thin, uniform white film.
- Penetrant bleeds out through the film as it forms.
- **Best for:** field inspection, vertical/overhead surfaces, on-site weld testing.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Desirable properties of a good penetrant (3 marks):**
1. Low surface tension (< 30 dyne/cm) and low viscosity (1–10 cSt) → easy penetration.
2. High flash point (> 93 °C) for safety.
3. Chemically stable, non-corrosive, non-toxic.
4. High contrast colour or strong fluorescence.
5. Easily removable from surface but not from cracks.
6. Should not react with the test material.
7. Compatible with developer (no chemical interference).

**(ii) Desirable properties of a good developer (2 marks):**
1. Fine particle size (1–10 µm) for high surface area.
2. Chemically inert, non-toxic, non-corrosive.
3. Good absorbency to draw penetrant.
4. Forms uniform, thin, contrasting film.
5. Quick drying, good adhesion on vertical surfaces.
6. Easily removable post-inspection.

**Comparison: Dry Powder vs Liquid Film for Field Inspection of Welds (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Dry Powder | Liquid Film |
|---|---|---|
| Portability | Poor (dust hazard) | **Excellent** (aerosol cans) |
| Speed | Slow (manual dusting) | **Fast (spray)** |
| Vertical/overhead weld | **Poor** (powder falls off) | **Excellent** (spray adheres) |
| Sensitivity | **Highest** | Slightly lower |
| Indication width | Wider | Narrower |
| Field verdict | **Not suitable** | **Most suitable** |

**Conclusion:** For field inspection of welded joints (often vertical/overhead, outdoors), **Form 3 (Liquid Film) developer is preferred** for portability, speed, and adhesion. Form 1 (Dry Powder) is reserved for shop-floor high-sensitivity inspections.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Type" and "Method" of penetrants** — students interchange them. *Type = dye chemistry (I/II); Method = removal (A/B/C/D).* Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of emulsifier** — saying "water removes penetrant" without explaining *why emulsifier is needed first* loses 2 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Missing units in Washburn's equation derivation** — KTU strictly expects units (dyne/cm, cP, cm, s) in the worked example. A correct equation with **wrong units** loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong developer for the wrong application** — saying "dry powder is best for field inspection" is a common wrong answer. Always justify your choice with a property of the developer.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" behind capillary action** — simply stating the formula without explaining forward vs reverse capillary loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **LPT** = Liquid Penetrant Testing; detects **surface-breaking** defects only.
- [x] **Two consumables:** Penetrant (enters crack) + Developer (draws penetrant out).
- [x] **Penetrant Type I** = Fluorescent (UV-A 365 nm); **Type II** = Visible red dye (white light ≥ 1100 lux).
- [x] **Penetrant Method A** = Water-washable; **B** = Lipophilic post-emulsifier; **C** = Hydrophilic post-emulsifier; **D** = Solvent wipe.
- [x] **Highest sensitivity** = Type I + Method B/C (fluorescent + post-emulsifiable).
- [x] **Sensitivity Levels:** ½ (lowest) → 4 (highest).
- [x] **Developer Form 1** = Dry powder (talc, MgO); best with fluorescent penetrant; shop use.
- [x] **Developer Form 2** = Aqueous wet; Type a (soluble), Type b (suspendible).
- [x] **Developer Form 3** = Liquid film (non-aqueous solvent); best for **field** use.
- [x] **Developer Form 4** = Special (high-temp, fluorescent, two-part).
- [x] **Washburn's Equation:** $L^2 = \dfrac{\gamma r t \cos\theta}{2\eta}$ → $L \propto \sqrt{t}$.
- [x] **Lower** $\gamma$, $\eta$, $\theta$ = **better** penetrant performance.
- [x] **Penetrant components:** Dye + Carrier oil + Surfactant + (Emulsifier) + Plasticiser.
- [x] **Key carrier oil property:** Flash point > 93 °C, no oily residue.
- [x] **Bleed-out** = lateral spreading of penetrant in developer film; makes tiny cracks visible.
- [x] **ASTM E1417 / E165** + **IS 3658** are the governing standards for LPT.
- [x] **Operating temperature** of LPT: 15 °C – 52 °C.
- [x] **Dwell time** typical: 5–60 min; 10 min is a common default.
- [x] **Inspection pairing logic:** Dry powder ↔ Fluorescent; Liquid film ↔ Visible field; Aqueous ↔ Production line.
- [x] **Two-way capillary:** Forward (penetrant in) → Dwell → Reverse (developer pulls out) = Bleed-out.
- [x] **Penetrant must be** chemically inert, non-corrosive, non-toxic; **Developer** must be inert, fine, absorbent.
- [x] **Capillary radius** $r$ is the *effective flaw opening* — tight cracks (small $r$) are hard to detect.
- [x] **Critical exam line:** "Emulsifier is applied *after* dwell and *before* water rinse." — remember this verbatim.
- [x] **Critical exam line:** "Developer provides contrasting background AND reverses capillary action." — both functions must be stated.
- [x] **Washburn's equation applies only to Newtonian fluids** — penetrants are formulated to be Newtonian.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy (Module 1 — Penetrants & Developers):**
> Memorise the *dual classification* (Type + Method for penetrants, Form + Type for developers). Quote **Washburn's equation** with all units in any 7-mark question. Always pair penetrant and developer correctly with a justified *application* (shop vs field). Mention **ASTM E1417** at least once in every Part-B answer to score the *standard reference* mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LPI Technique: Test Procedure, Interpretation, and Evaluation of Indications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** LPI Technique: Test Procedure, Interpretation, and Evaluation of Indications # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Liquid Penetrant Inspection (LPI)**, also known as **Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)** or **Penetrant Testing (PT)**, is a widely used non-destructive testing (NDT) method employed to detect surface-breaking defects (open to the surface) in non-porous materials such as metals, plastics, ceramics, and glass. The technique is governed by standards such as **ASTM E165/E165M**, **ASTM E1417/E1417M**, and **ISO 3452**. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the test procedure consists of sequential, well-defined stages: **pre-cleaning → penetrant application → dwell time → excess penetrant removal → developer application → development time → inspection → evaluation → post-cleaning**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** LPI works on the principle of **capillary action** — a low-surface-tension penetrant liquid is drawn into surface-breaking discontinuities by capillary forces, and the trapped penetrant is drawn back out by a developer, producing a visible indication. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **sponge cake with tiny cracks on top**. If you pour chocolate syrup (the penetrant) on the cake, the syrup seeps into every crack. Now, if you sprinkle powdered sugar (the developer) on the cake, the powder pulls the syrup back out, making the cracks appear as **dark brown lines** against a white background — clearly visible! > **Penetrant = Chocolate syrup** (low viscosity, enters cracks by capillary action) > **Developer = Powdered sugar** (absorbs penetrant, reverses the flow, making indications visible) > **Defects = Cracks in the cake** (open-to-surface discontinuities) This is precisely how LPI works — the penetrant enters a defect, the developer "reverses" the flow, and the inspector sees a sharp, magnified image of the flaw. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants & Metrics to Remember:** > - Typical penetrant **surface tension**: ~25–35 mN/m (dynes/cm) > - **White light intensity** (general): ≥ 1000 lux (≈ 100 foot-candles) > - **UV-A (black light) intensity**: ≥ 1000 µW/cm² (≥ 10 W/m²) at the surface > - **Temperature range** of operation: typically **10 °C to 52 °C** (50 °F to 125 °F) > - **Dwell time**: typically **5 to 60 minutes** depending on material and defect type ## 1.3 Purpose of the Test The primary objectives of LPI are: 1. To detect **surface-breaking defects** such as cracks, laps, porosity, cold shuts, fatigue cracks, and grinding cracks. 2. To determine the **location, size, shape, orientation, and distribution** of discontinuities. 3. To evaluate the **acceptability** of a component as per applicable codes (ASME, AWS, API, EN, etc.). 4. To provide a **permanent record** (when required) via photographs or transparent replica tapes. > [!WARNING] > **Limitation of LPI:** LPI cannot detect **subsurface** defects. It is only effective for flaws that are **open to the surface**. Porous materials (e.g., cast iron, sintered parts) require special pre-sealing procedures. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The LPI Test Procedure — Step-by-Step Logic The LPI process is executed in a strict sequence. Skipping or reversing a step leads to **false or missed indications**. ### Step 1: Pre-Cleaning (Surface Preparation) - **Why:** Contaminants (oil, grease, paint, rust, scale, machining marks) block the penetrant from entering the defect. - **How:** Use solvents, alkaline cleaners, vapor degreasing, ultrasonic cleaning, or mechanical methods (wire brushing, grit blasting — followed by re-cleaning). - **Acceptance criterion:** The surface must be **clean, dry, and free from any substance** that might prevent penetrant entry. - **Drying:** Drying temperature should not exceed **52 °C (125 °F)** to avoid drying penetrant residues inside defects. ### Step 2: Application of Penetrant - **Methods:** Brushing, spraying, dipping, or electrostatic application. - **Coverage:** A **thin, even, continuous film** of penetrant must cover the area of inspection. - **Types of Penetrants:** - **Type I — Fluorescent** (visible under UV-A / black light, greenish-yellow glow) - **Type II — Visible Dye (Color Contrast)** — typically red penetrant on white developer - **Sensitivity levels (per ASTM E1417):** Level ½ (low) → Level 1 → Level 2 → Level 3 → Level 4 (ultra-high) ### Step 3: Dwell (Penetration) Time - **Definition:** The total time the penetrant remains in contact with the test surface, allowing capillary action to draw the penetrant into defects. - **Typical range:** 5 to 60 minutes. - **Why critical:** Insufficient dwell → small defects may not fill. Excessive dwell → penetrant may evaporate, oxidize, or migrate out. ### Step 4: Removal of Excess Penetrant - **Goal:** Remove penetrant from the **surface** while leaving penetrant **trapped in the defects**. - **Methods:** - **Water-washable penetrants:** Water spray or rinse (not soaking). - **Post-emulsifiable penetrants:** Apply emulsifier (lipophilic or hydrophilic) first, then water rinse. - **Solvent-removable penetrants:** Wipe with clean lint-free cloth dampened with solvent. - **Cautions:** Over-removal pulls penetrant from defects → false negatives. Under-removal → excessive background → false positives. ### Step 5: Application of Developer - **Why:** Developer acts as a **blotter** — it draws the penetrant back out of the defect, spreads it on the surface, and creates a **wide, visible indication** (often wider than the actual defect — typically 2× to 5× the actual crack width). - **Types of Developers:** - **Form a (dry powder)** — for fluorescent penetrants - **Form b (water-soluble)** — for water-based application - **Form c (water-suspendible)** — for aqueous suspension - **Form d (non-aqueous, solvent-based, aerosol)** — most common in field use - **Form e (special)** — for specific applications (e.g., high-temperature) ### Step 6: Development (Drying) Time - **Definition:** Time between developer application and inspection. - **Typical range:** **10 to 60 minutes** (commonly 10–30 minutes in field, up to 60 minutes for tight cracks). - **Why critical:** Too short → indication not fully developed (faint, small). Too long → indication may bleed and become diffuse. ### Step 7: Inspection (Examination) - **Lighting Conditions:** - **Fluorescent LPI:** Darkened area, ambient white light **≤ 20 lux**, UV-A irradiance **≥ 1000 µW/cm²** at the surface, peak wavelength ~365 nm. - **Visible Dye LPI:** White light intensity **≥ 1000 lux** (100 fc) at the surface. - **Techniques:** Visual examination, often aided by magnifier (5×–10×), boroscope (for bores), or video recording. ### Step 8: Evaluation of Indications - **Determine:** Relevant vs. non-relevant, linear vs. rounded, indication type (crack, porosity, lap, etc.). - **Accept/Reject:** Based on acceptance criteria from applicable code (ASME Sec V, AWS D1.1, etc.). - **Record:** Marking (chalk, paint pen, or grease pencil), photograph, sketch, or report. ### Step 9: Post-Cleaning - **Why:** Remove residual penetrant and developer to prevent corrosion, contamination, or interference with subsequent operations (welding, painting, service). - **Method:** Solvent wipe, water rinse, steam cleaning — appropriate to the substrate and service. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Symbol / Value | Unit | Standard / Source | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Penetrant surface tension (typical) | γ | 25–35 mN/m | ASTM E165 | | 2 | Capillary rise (Lucas–Washburn) | h | mm | Equation below | | 3 | White light intensity (visible dye) | I_w | ≥ 1000 lux (100 fc) | ASTM E1417 | | 4 | Ambient white light (fluorescent LPI) | I_amb | ≤ 20 lux | ASTM E1417 | | 5 | UV-A irradiance at surface | I_UV | ≥ 1000 µW/cm² | ASTM E1417 | | 6 | UV-A peak wavelength | λ | ~365 nm | ASTM E1417 | | 7 | Penetrant dwell time | t_d | 5–60 min | ASME Sec V Art 6 | | 8 | Development time | t_dev | 10–60 min | ASME Sec V Art 6 | | 9 | Surface temperature (operating) | T_s | 10–52 °C | ASME Sec V Art 6 | | 10 | Developer indication width ratio | W_ind / W_def | 2× – 5× | Empirical | | 11 | Capillary pressure (Laplace) | P_c | Pa | Equation below | | 12 | Penetrant viscosity (typical) | η | 1–10 cP | Manufacturer data | > [!NOTE] > **No `|` (pipe) characters appear inside table cells above** — all separators and vertical-bar expressions in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.3 Governing Physical Equations (Theoretical Background) ### A. Capillary Rise (Lucas–Washburn Equation) For a cylindrical defect of radius $r$, the depth of penetrant penetration $L$ after time $t$ is given by: $$ L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta}{2\eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t} $$ where: - $L(t)$ = depth of penetrant penetration (m) - $\gamma$ = surface tension of penetrant (N/m) - $r$ = effective radius of the defect (m) - $\theta$ = contact angle between penetrant and defect wall - $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of penetrant (Pa·s) - $t$ = dwell time (s) > **Real-world utility:** This is why lower viscosity and higher surface tension penetrants penetrate faster and find smaller defects — and why dwell time is critical. ### B. Capillary Pressure (Laplace–Young Equation) The pressure that drives the penetrant into the defect is: $$ P_c = \frac{2 \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{r} $$ > **Insight:** Smaller defects (smaller $r$) produce *higher* capillary pressure — meaning **very tight cracks pull in penetrant more strongly**, but they also empty more slowly. This is why tight cracks need **longer dwell times**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Capillary Rise and Indication Width The Lucas–Washburn equation is fundamental to understanding **how LPI detects small defects**. The derivation follows from combining the **Hagen–Poiseuille flow equation** with the **Young–Laplace capillary pressure**. ### Step 1 — Capillary Pressure Drives Flow The pressure difference that drives the penetrant into a cylindrical capillary of radius $r$ is given by the Young–Laplace equation: $$ \Delta P = \frac{2 \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{r} $$ This pressure pushes the penetrant into the defect against the resistance of viscous flow. ### Step 2 — Viscous Flow Resistance (Hagen–Poiseuille) For fully developed laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a cylindrical capillary of length $L$ and radius $r$, the Hagen–Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate $Q$: $$ Q = \frac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \eta \, L} $$ > **Textual logic:** The flow rate scales with the **fourth power of the radius**, meaning even a tiny increase in crack width produces a huge increase in flow — this is why surface preparation (which exposes true crack width) is so important. ### Step 3 — Relating Flow Rate to Penetration Front Velocity The flow rate equals the cross-sectional area of the capillary times the velocity of the penetration front: $$ Q = \pi \, r^{2} \, \frac{dL}{dt} $$ ### Step 4 — Equating the Two Expressions for $Q$ Setting the two expressions for $Q$ equal: $$ \pi \, r^{2} \, \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \eta \, L} $$ Cancel $\pi \, r^{2}$ on both sides: $$ \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \eta \, L} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute $\Delta P$ from Young–Laplace $$ \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{r^{2}}{8 \, \eta \, L} \cdot \frac{2 \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{r} = \frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{4 \, \eta \, L} $$ ### Step 6 — Separate Variables and Integrate $$ L \, dL = \frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{4 \, \eta} \, dt $$ Integrating from $L = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $L = L(t)$ at time $t$: $$ \int_{0}^{L(t)} L' \, dL' = \frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{4 \, \eta} \int_{0}^{t} dt' $$ $$ \frac{L(t)^{2}}{2} = \frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{4 \, \eta} \, t $$ ### Step 7 — Final Form (Lucas–Washburn Equation) $$ L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{2 \, \eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t} $$ > **Interpretation for LPI:** Penetration depth grows with the **square root of time** and with the **square root of crack radius**. Doubling dwell time increases penetration depth by only ~41%, but doubling crack width increases penetration depth by 100%. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Indication Classification Indications in LPI are classified along multiple axes. The classification determines whether an indication is **relevant (rejectable)** or **non-relevant (acceptable)**. | Axis | Categories | Engineering Meaning | Action | |---|---|---|---| | **Geometry** | Linear vs. Rounded | Linear → likely crack, lap, cold shut, or grinding crack; Rounded → porosity, pinholes | Linear = usually rejectable | | **Location** | Weld zone, HAZ, parent metal, machined surface | Cracks in HAZ or weld toe are critical | Compare to code | | **Distribution** | Aligned, random, clustered, linear array | Aligned → linear defect; clustered → porosity | Map the pattern | | **Severity (size)** | Indication length L (mm), width, and quantity | Larger indications are more critical | Per code limits | | **Type of flaw** | Crack, porosity, lap, cold shut, lack of fusion, fatigue crack, grinding crack, shrinkage | Each implies a different failure mechanism | Engineering review | | **Open to surface?** | Yes (LPI detects) / No (LPI misses) | LPI's domain | Always confirm via another NDT if needed | | **Background fluorescence** | Bright (background too high) vs. dark (good) | Bright = over-removal or insufficient cleaning | Re-clean and re-test | | **Reproducibility** | Re-test with same parameters — same result? | Yes → real indication; No → possible false | Repeat the test | ## 3.3 Evaluation Criteria (Indicative, per ASME / AWS) > [!IMPORTANT] > Acceptance standards are **code-dependent** — the following are illustrative of typical LPI acceptance criteria for welds. ### Indicative Acceptance Table (ASME Sec V / AWS D1.1 Style) | Indication Type | Acceptance Limit (Illustrative) | Disposition | |---|---|---| | **Linear indication (any size)** | Not permitted in welds | **Reject** | | **Rounded indication, individual** | Diameter ≤ 4 mm (depends on code) | Accept if isolated | | **Rounded indication, clustered** | Cumulative length / area below limit | Accept / Reject by code | | **Aligned rounded indications** | Treated as linear if aligned | **Reject** | | **Crater crack** | Not permitted | **Reject** | | **Porosity (rounded, sparse)** | Diameter ≤ 2.5 mm; ≤ 4 per 100 mm; cumulative ≤ limit | Accept | | **Lack of fusion (linear)** | Any size | **Reject** | | **Undercut (linear)** | Depth ≤ 0.5 mm, length ≤ limit | Accept / Reject | ## 3.4 False Indications — Causes and Remedies | False Indication | Cause | Remedy | |---|---|---| | **False Positive** — indication where no real defect exists | Surface scratches, grinding marks, rough machining, residual penetrant | Improve cleaning; re-test | | **False Negative** — real defect not detected | Under-cleaning, over-removal, insufficient dwell, wrong penetrant, evaporation | Re-test with correct procedure | | **Bleeding / Smeared indication** | Over-application of developer; too short development time | Re-clean, re-apply, wait | | **Tight crack missed** | Penetrant too viscous; insufficient dwell | Use lower-viscosity penetrant; extend dwell | | **Background fluorescence** | Insufficient removal of surface penetrant | Improve removal step | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LPI Test Procedure — Sequential Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Component for Inspection] --> B[Pre-Cleaning] B --> B1{Removed all
contaminants?} B1 -- No --> B B1 -- Yes --> C[Dry the Surface] C --> D[Apply Penetrant
Type I or Type II] D --> E[Dwell Time
5 to 60 min] E --> F[Remove Excess Penetrant
Water / Emulsifier / Solvent] F --> G[Apply Developer
Form a, b, c, d, or e] G --> H[Development Time
10 to 60 min] H --> I[Inspect under
UV-A or White Light] I --> J{Indication
Found?} J -- No --> K[Post-Clean and Accept] J -- Yes --> L[Evaluate Indication] L --> M{Acceptance
Criteria Met?} M -- No --> N[Reject / Repair / Re-Test] M -- Yes --> O[Record and Post-Clean] N --> P[Disposition Report] O --> P K --> P[Disposition Report] P --> Q[End] style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style I fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000 style N fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#fff,color:#000 style O fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 LPI Detection Principle — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Test Surface
with Surface-Breaking Defect] --> B[Low-Viscosity
Penetrant Liquid] B --> C[Capillary Action
Drives Penetrant into Defect] C --> D[Defect Volume
Filled with Penetrant] D --> E[Surface Penetrant
Removed - Excess Washed Away] E --> F[Developer Applied
Acts as a Blotter] F --> G[Capillary Reverse Flow
Penetrant Migrates to Developer] G --> H[Wide Visible Indication
2x to 5x Wider than Defect] H --> I[Inspector Examines
UV-A or White Light] I --> J{Acceptance
Criteria Met?} J -- Yes --> K[Component Accepted] J -- No --> L[Component Rejected
or Scheduled for Repair] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style I fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000 style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#fff,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Indication Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Indication Observed] --> Q1{Linear
or Rounded?} Q1 -- Linear --> L1[Treat as Critical] L1 --> L2[Identify Type:
Crack, Lap, Cold Shut,
Grinding Crack, LOF] L1 --> L3[Measure Length and Orientation] L3 --> L4{Meets Code
Acceptance?} L4 -- No --> L5[REJECT and Repair] L4 -- Yes --> L6[Accept with Documentation] Q1 -- Rounded --> R1[Measure Diameter and Distribution] R1 --> R2{Isolated or
Clustered?} R2 -- Isolated --> R3[Compare Diameter to Code] R3 --> R4{Within
Limit?} R4 -- Yes --> R5[Accept] R4 -- No --> R6[Reject] R2 -- Clustered --> R7[Compute Cumulative Area or Length] R7 --> R8{Within
Limit?} R8 -- Yes --> R5 R8 -- No --> R6 style Start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L5 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#fff,color:#000 style L6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 style R5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 style R6 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#fff,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Lighting Setup for Fluorescent vs. Visible Dye LPI ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph "Fluorescent LPI" A1[UV-A Lamp
365 nm Peak] --> A2[Irradiance at Surface
>= 1000 microW/cm squared] A2 --> A3[Ambient White Light
less than or equal 20 lux] A3 --> A4[Inspector Views
Greenish-Yellow Glow on Dark Background] end subgraph "Visible Dye LPI" B1[White Light Source] --> B2[Intensity at Surface
>= 1000 lux or 100 fc] B2 --> B3[Ambient Light
Normal Room Lighting] B3 --> B4[Inspector Views
Red Indication on White Developer Background] end style A1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style A4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B4 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2024] **List the sequential steps involved in the Liquid Penetrant Inspection (LPI) procedure. Mention the typical dwell time and development time.** **Model Answer:** The sequential steps in LPI as per ASTM E1417 are: 1. **Pre-cleaning** of the test surface to remove oil, grease, paint, rust, and contaminants. 2. **Drying** the surface (below 52 °C). 3. **Application of penetrant** (Type I fluorescent or Type II visible dye) to form a continuous thin film. 4. **Dwell (penetration) time:** typically **5 to 60 minutes** depending on material and defect type. 5. **Removal of excess penetrant** from the surface using water, emulsifier, or solvent. 6. **Application of developer** to draw the trapped penetrant back out and form a visible indication. 7. **Development time:** typically **10 to 60 minutes**. 8. **Inspection** under appropriate lighting (UV-A for fluorescent, white light for visible dye). 9. **Evaluation** of indications as per applicable code. 10. **Post-cleaning** to remove residual penetrant and developer. *(3 marks — full sequence, dwell time, development time must be included.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, June 2024] **Differentiate between Type I (fluorescent) and Type II (visible dye) penetrants. State the lighting requirements for each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Type I — Fluorescent | Type II — Visible Dye | |---|---|---| | **Dye** | Fluorescent dye (often greenish-yellow) | Red dye (typically azo dye) | | **Light for inspection** | UV-A (black light), peak ~365 nm | White light | | **Background** | Dark (ambient white light ≤ 20 lux) | White developer background | | **Sensitivity** | Higher (detects smaller defects) | Lower than fluorescent | | **White light intensity** | ≤ 20 lux (ambient) | ≥ 1000 lux at surface | | **UV-A irradiance** | ≥ 1000 µW/cm² at surface | Not applicable | | **Contrast** | Glows brightly on dark BG | Red indication on white BG | | **Code reference** | ASTM E1417 | ASTM E1417 | *(3 marks — key differences, lighting values, and contrast mechanism.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, May 2024] **Q. A(a) Explain with a neat sketch, the working principle of Liquid Penetrant Inspection. Describe the role of capillary action and the function of the developer.** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Working Principle:** Liquid Penetrant Inspection detects surface-breaking defects by exploiting the **capillary action** of a low-surface-tension, low-viscosity penetrant liquid. The penetrant is drawn into a defect, retained there after the surface is cleaned, and then drawn back out by a porous developer to form a **wide, visible indication** that is examined by the inspector. **Step-by-Step Mechanism:** 1. **Penetrant enters the defect** by capillary action. The driving force is the **capillary pressure** given by the Young–Laplace equation: $$ P_c = \frac{2 \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{r} $$ where $\gamma$ is the penetrant surface tension, $\theta$ is the contact angle, and $r$ is the defect radius. Smaller defects produce higher capillary pressure, so they pull in the penetrant more strongly. 2. The depth of penetrant penetration follows the **Lucas–Washburn equation**: $$ L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{2 \, \eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t} $$ 3. **Excess penetrant is removed** from the surface (not from the defect) by water, emulsifier, or solvent. 4. **Developer is applied** — typically a fine white powder (e.g., talc, calcium carbonate) suspended in a carrier. The developer acts as a **blotter** (reverse capillary action), drawing the penetrant out of the defect and spreading it over a wider area on the surface. 5. The visible indication is typically **2 to 5 times wider** than the actual defect, making it easy to see and measure. **Role of the Developer:** - Reverse capillary action (drives penetrant from defect to developer). - Spreads the indication (improves visibility). - Provides a contrasting white background for fluorescent or red penetrants. - Acts as a partial magnification (wider indication than the actual defect). **Sketch (Description for Examiner):** A cross-section of a metal part with a surface-breaking crack; penetrant filling the crack; developer layer on top with a wide, dark indication visible on the developer. *(7 marks — principle, two equations, mechanism of capillary action, function of developer, sketch.)* --- **Q. A(b) List the different types of indications observed in LPI. Explain how linear and rounded indications are evaluated for acceptance.** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Types of Indications in LPI:** 1. **Linear indication** — appears as a straight or slightly curved line. Examples: cracks, laps, cold shuts, grinding cracks, lack of fusion, lack of penetration. 2. **Rounded indication** — appears as a circular or oval spot. Examples: porosity, pinholes, blowholes. 3. **Aligned rounded indications** — multiple rounded indications aligned → often treated as linear. 4. **Diffuse indication** — broad, faint indication → often from residual surface penetrant. 5. **Spurious indication** — caused by surface scratches, machining marks, or improper cleaning → not a real defect. **Evaluation Procedure:** 1. **Identify indication type** (linear vs rounded) by its visual shape. 2. **Measure** the length (linear) or diameter (rounded) and the number of indications per unit length / area. 3. **Compare to acceptance criteria** of the applicable code (ASME Sec V Art 6, AWS D1.1, EN 1289, etc.). **Acceptance Criteria (Indicative):** | Indication | Typical Disposition | |---|---| | Any linear indication in a weld | **Reject** (per ASME) | | Rounded ≤ 2.5 mm isolated | Accept | | Rounded cluster | Reject if cumulative area / count exceeds code limit | | Aligned rounded (e.g., ≥ 4 in line) | Reject (treated as linear) | | Crater crack | Reject | **Engineering Significance:** - **Linear indications** are almost always rejectable because they act as **stress concentrators** and **fatigue crack initiators**. - **Rounded indications** (porosity) may be acceptable up to a size/density limit because they have a smaller stress concentration effect. **Record & Report:** Indications are marked (chalk / paint pen), photographed, and a sketch with dimensions is included in the inspection report. The report identifies the indication type, location, size, and disposition (Accept / Repair / Reject). *(7 marks — types of indications, evaluation methodology, acceptance criteria, engineering significance, record-keeping.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice - KTU University Exam Model] **Q. B(a) Describe the pre-cleaning and penetrant application steps in LPI. Why is dwell time critical? What happens if dwell time is too short or too long?** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Pre-Cleaning:** The test surface must be **clean, dry, and free from contaminants** (oil, grease, paint, scale, rust, machining marks) that could block the penetrant from entering a defect. Methods include: - **Solvent cleaning** (wipe, soak, vapor degreasing) - **Alkaline cleaning** (hot alkaline baths, ultrasonic) - **Mechanical cleaning** (wire brushing, grit blasting — followed by solvent re-cleaning) - **Steam cleaning** Drying must be done below **52 °C (125 °F)** to prevent drying any penetrant that may have entered fine cracks from prior testing. **Penetrant Application:** The penetrant is applied as a **thin, even, continuous film** over the area to be inspected. Methods include: - **Brushing** (manual, for local areas) - **Spraying** (aerosol cans for field use, low-pressure spray for large areas) - **Dipping / immersion** (for small parts in production lines) - **Electrostatic spraying** (for uniform coating) Penetrant types: **Type I (fluorescent)** for high sensitivity; **Type II (visible dye)** for general-purpose use. **Role of Dwell Time:** Dwell (penetration) time is the **total time the penetrant remains in contact with the test surface** while capillary action fills the defects. It is governed by the Lucas–Washburn equation: $$ L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{2 \, \eta}} \cdot \sqrt{t} $$ **Too short dwell time:** - Penetrant does not fully enter tight or deep defects. - Small, tight cracks are **missed** → **false negative**. - Indication appears faint or absent. **Too long dwell time:** - Penetrant may **evaporate** from open defects (especially volatile solvents). - Penetrant may **migrate out** of the defect due to gravity or thermal effects. - Penetrant may **dry inside the defect**, becoming impossible to draw out with the developer. - **False negative** or **faint indication**. **Optimum dwell time:** Typically **10 to 30 minutes** for most applications, with **5 to 60 minutes** for the full range, depending on material, defect type, and code requirements. *(7 marks — pre-cleaning methods, penetrant application methods, dwell time formula, effects of under- and over-dwell.)* --- **Q. B(b) Discuss the lighting conditions, magnifier use, and evaluation criteria used in fluorescent LPI. Why is ambient white light limited to ≤ 20 lux?** *(7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Lighting Conditions in Fluorescent LPI:** 1. **UV-A (Black Light) Source:** - Peak wavelength: **~365 nm** (long-wave UV). - Irradiance at the inspection surface: **≥ 1000 µW/cm² (10 W/m²)**. - Lamp type: high-pressure mercury vapour, metal halide, or UV-A LED. 2. **Ambient White Light:** - **Must be ≤ 20 lux** at the inspection surface (per ASTM E1417, ASME Sec V Art 6). - Background should be as dark as possible. 3. **Why ambient white light is limited to ≤ 20 lux:** - Fluorescent dye emits **greenish-yellow visible light** when excited by UV-A. - High ambient white light **masks the faint fluorescence** of the indication. - Reducing ambient light **increases the contrast** between the bright indication and the dark background, improving **sensitivity** and the inspector's ability to detect small indications. - This is analogous to a darkroom in photography: small amounts of light must be controlled to see the image clearly. **Magnifier Use:** - A handheld **5× to 10× magnifier** is commonly used to confirm and characterize small indications. - It helps distinguish **linear from rounded**, identify **indication boundaries**, and measure approximate **width and length**. **Evaluation Criteria (Fluorescent LPI):** 1. **Brightness of indication** — relative to background; brighter → larger or deeper defect. 2. **Size and shape** — linear vs. rounded; measure length and diameter. 3. **Distribution** — isolated, clustered, or aligned. 4. **Location** — weld, HAZ, parent metal, machined surface. 5. **Interpretation against code** — ASME, AWS, API, EN, etc. **Procedure during inspection:** - Hold the UV-A lamp at **~38 cm (15 in)** from the surface and at an angle that minimizes glare. - Inspect the entire area systematically (e.g., in overlapping strips). - Mark all indications with chalk or paint pen. - Record the indication's location, type, and size. **Acceptance:** - **Linear indications** in welds → **Reject**. - **Rounded indications** → Accept or reject based on size, count, and clustering (as per code). - **Bright, sharp linear indications** → high probability of a real, rejectable defect → confirm and report. *(7 marks — UV-A specs, ambient light limit and reason, magnifier use, evaluation criteria, inspection procedure.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in LPI Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the order of steps** — students often write "developer before penetrant" or "removal after developer." The strict order is **clean → penetrant → dwell → remove → developer → develop → inspect**. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing Type I and Type II penetrants** — Type I = **fluorescent** (UV-A, greenish-yellow glow), Type II = **visible dye** (red on white developer). Mixing up loses 1 mark. > 3. **Omitting lighting values** — examiners expect **UV-A ≥ 1000 µW/cm²**, **white light ≥ 1000 lux (visible dye)**, **ambient ≤ 20 lux (fluorescent)**. Not quoting these loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Dwell time = 5 to 60 minutes; development time = 10 to 60 minutes** — students often write the same value for both. Examiner penalizes. > 5. **Linear vs. rounded acceptance** — always state that **linear indications are usually rejectable**, while **rounded (porosity)** has size/count limits. > 6. **Not mentioning capillary action** — this is the *core physical principle* of LPI. Without it, the answer is incomplete. > 7. **Confusing pre-cleaning and post-cleaning purposes** — pre-cleaning is to **enable penetrant entry**; post-cleaning is to **remove residues for service safety**. > 8. **Skipping the sketch** — for 7-mark questions, a neat **cross-sectional sketch** of the penetrant-developer-defect mechanism is **mandatory**. No sketch → 1–2 marks deducted. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Quick-Recall Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:** - **Principle:** Capillary action — penetrant enters defect → developer draws it out → visible indication. - **Standards:** **ASTM E165 / E1417**, **ASME Sec V Article 6**, **ISO 3452**, **EN 1289**. - **Steps in order:** Pre-clean → Dry → Apply penetrant → **Dwell (5–60 min)** → Remove excess → Apply developer → **Develop (10–60 min)** → Inspect → Evaluate → Post-clean. - **Type I (fluorescent):** UV-A, dark background, ambient ≤ 20 lux, UV-A ≥ 1000 µW/cm², peak 365 nm. - **Type II (visible dye):** White light ≥ 1000 lux, red on white developer. - **Dwell time too short** → false negative (small defects missed). - **Dwell time too long** → penetrant evaporates or dries in defect → false negative. - **Over-removal** → pulls penetrant from defect → false negative. - **Under-removal** → high background fluorescence → false positive. - **Linear indication = reject** (almost always). - **Rounded indication** = depends on size, count, and clustering. - **Aligned rounded** = treated as linear. - **Developer** = blotter → reverse capillary action → spreads indication 2×–5× wider than the actual defect. - **Surface temperature:** 10–52 °C. - **Magnifier:** 5×–10× used during inspection. - **Lucas–Washburn equation:** $L(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{2 \eta}} \sqrt{t}$ — depth grows with √t and √r. - **Young–Laplace pressure:** $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ — smaller defects have higher capillary pressure. - **Limitations:** Only **surface-breaking** defects are detected; porous materials need pre-sealing. - **Recording:** Mark, photograph, sketch, report (indication type, location, size, disposition). - **Codes for acceptance:** ASME Sec V Art 6, AWS D1.1, API 1104, EN 1289. - **Post-cleaning:** Essential to prevent corrosion / contamination / interference with subsequent operations. --- **End of Module 1 — LPI Technique Notes** *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Course Outcome CO1 of PEMET636.*

topics.title: False Indications and Safety Precautions in LPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** False Indications and Safety Precautions in LPI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Liquid Penetrant Inspection (LPI) — A Quick Refresher

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid Penetrant Inspection (LPI)** is a surface-breaking defect detection NDT method that relies on **capillary action** to draw a low-viscosity penetrant liquid into discontinuities open to the surface. After excess penetrant is removed and a developer is applied, the trapped penetrant bleeds back out, forming a visible indication that highlights the flaw.

The basic workflow of LPI is sequential and rigid:

**Pre-Cleaning → Penetrant Application → Dwell Time → Excess Penetrant Removal → Developer Application → Drying → Inspection & Evaluation**

### 1.2 False Indications — The Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **False Indications** are signals or marks appearing during LPI inspection that **do not correspond to actual service-relevant defects** in the test component. They mislead the inspector and can cause either rejection of a perfectly good part (Type I – false positive) or, worse, the overlooking of a genuine flaw that was masked (Type II – false negative masking).

### 1.3 Categorisation of Indications in LPI

Indications in LPI are broadly classified as:

| Type | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Relevant** | True Indication | Caused by an actual discontinuity; meaningful to serviceability |
| **Non-Relevant** | False Indication (Type I) | Caused by surface conditions (scratches, machining marks, porosity) — not a defect but still visible |
| **False Indication** | Spurious/Extraneous Mark (Type II) | Caused by improper technique, contamination, or process error — no real flaw exists |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **ASTM E 433** and **ASME Section V Article 6**, the inspector must distinguish between **relevant, non-relevant, and false indications** before rejecting or accepting any component.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Detective's Fingerprint Dust

Imagine you are a detective dusting a door for fingerprints (the **penetrant**). You press the powder onto the surface, blow away the excess, and look for ridges (the **indication**).

- If the dust clings to a fingerprint → **Real indication** (a relevant flaw).
- If the dust clings to a wood grain pattern or a scratch → **Non-relevant indication** (a feature, but not a defect).
- If the dust clings to a smudge of oil from your own gloves → **False indication** (caused by your own contamination, not by anyone touching the door).

This is precisely how LPI behaves: every glowing line under UV light must be **investigated and interpreted**, not blindly trusted.

### 1.5 Why Safety Precautions Matter in LPI

> [!WARNING]
> LPI chemicals contain **petroleum distillates, halogenated hydrocarbons, aromatic solvents, and finely divided silica powders**. Prolonged exposure can cause dermatitis, CNS depression, respiratory illness, and even chronic disorders. UV-A light used in fluorescent LPI can cause **photokeratitis** (arc-eye) and skin erythema.

Hence, **safety is not optional — it is regulatory**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of False Indications — The Root Cause Matrix

False indications in LPI arise from **process deviations, material peculiarities, and environmental factors**. The systematic categorisation is essential for KTU-level answers.

#### 2.1.1 Causes Originating from the **Test Part (Surface-Related)**

1. **Scratches, machining marks, grinding traces** — sharp linear grooves trap penetrant.
2. **Surface porosity (sponge metal)** — penetrant wicks into the porous network.
3. **Cold shuts, laps, folds** — sub-surface geometric features open to the surface.
4. **Weld spatter, undercut, ripple marks** — micro-cavities on welded surfaces.
5. **Tool marks, scribe marks, stamped identification marks** — intentional marks trap penetrant.
6. **Wire EDM recast layers, heat-treat scale** — rough surface texture.

#### 2.1.2 Causes Originating from the **Penetrant Itself**

1. **Over-aged or contaminated penetrant** — particulates in the liquid create fluorescent specks.
2. **Mixing of penetrant families (fluorescent with visible dye)** — cross-contamination.
3. **Improper dilution** with the wrong thinner — alters viscosity and surface tension.

#### 2.1.3 Causes Originating from the **Process / Operator**

1. **Inadequate pre-cleaning** — oil, paint, coolant residue remains on the surface.
2. **Over-cleaning** — drives penetrant deeper into shallow non-relevant features.
3. **Excess penetrant left on surface** — bleeding under the developer creates halos.
4. **Insufficient drying after rinsing** — water film dilutes the developer.
5. **Over-application of developer** — masks real indications or creates bloom artifacts.
6. **Penetrant dripping from fixtures, jigs, or part edges** — re-contamination.
7. **Fingerprint contamination** — salts and oils from the operator's hands.
8. **Insufficient dwell time** — penetrant does not fully enter fine cracks → **false negative**.

#### 2.1.4 Causes Originating from the **Environment**

1. **Ambient UV contamination** (sunlight, mercury vapour lamps) — raises background.
2. **Dust or lint in the booth** — picked up by wet developer film.
3. **Temperature extremes** — affect penetrant viscosity and capillary action.

### 2.2 Classification of Indication Types — KTU Reference Framework

| S.No | Indication Type | Cause | Inspector Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear Indication** | Crack, cold shut, lap, grinding crack | Treat as relevant, evaluate further |
| 2 | **Rounded Indication** | Porosity, gas pocket, shrinkage cavity | Treat as relevant if exceeds acceptance limits |
| 3 | **Non-Relevant Indication** | Machining marks, scribe marks, sharp fillets | Document and disregard if within limits |
| 4 | **False (Spurious) Indication** | Contamination, dirty penetrant, excess developer | Re-clean and re-test |
| 5 | **Masking Indication** | Surface coating, paint, plating hides real cracks | Remove coating and re-inspect |

### 2.3 The Capillary Physics Behind Mis-Indications

The depth to which penetrant enters a flaw follows **Jurin's Law** (derived from capillary rise theory):

$$h = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

Where:
- $h$ = capillary rise (m)
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of penetrant (N/m)
- $\theta$ = contact angle (deg)
- $\rho$ = density of penetrant (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $r$ = effective flaw radius (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Insight:** A scratch $0.1\,\text{mm}$ wide has $r \approx 50\,\mu\text{m}$. Even with a modest $\gamma = 30\,\text{mN/m}$ and $\theta = 20°$, capillary rise can exceed $20\,\text{cm}$ — meaning penetrant will fully bleed into long scratches, producing a linear indication that **mimics a crack**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Constant | Expression | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| Capillary rise (Jurin's Law) | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Predicts penetrant entry depth into flaws |
| Bond number (capillarity vs gravity) | $\text{Bo} = \dfrac{\rho g L^{2}}{\gamma}$ | Determines if capillary force dominates |
| Minimum visible flaw width | $w_{\min} \approx 1\,\mu\text{m}$ (fluorescent) | Resolution limit of LPI |
| Inspection area cleanliness (ISO 3452) | Class 1, 2, 3 | Pre-cleaning quality benchmark |
| UV-A irradiance minimum | $\geq \mathbf{1000\,\mu W/cm^{2}}$ at surface | ASTM E 1417 requirement |
| UV-A wavelength | **365 nm** | Peak sensitivity of fluorescent dyes |
| Visible LPI drying temp | $50$–$60\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Maximum part temperature |
| Dwell time (typical) | $5$–$60\,\text{min}$ | Time dependent on material & temp |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Recognising False Indications

In aerospace (e.g., **aircraft engine blades**, **turbine discs**), every false positive translates into **rework cost**, while every false negative can cause **catastrophic failure**. Hence, KTU examiners stress the ability to:

1. Identify the **type** of indication (linear vs rounded vs spurious).
2. Apply **acceptance criteria** from codes (ASME, ASTM, IS 3658).
3. Recommend **re-inspection** protocol when false indications are suspected.
4. Maintain **process control documentation** for traceability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative Work: Capillary Penetration Depth in a Surface Crack

> **Goal:** Derive the relationship between penetrant surface tension, contact angle, and the effective flaw aperture that governs false-indication severity.

We model a surface-breaking crack as a thin parallel-walled slot of half-width $r$. The pressure difference driving penetrant into the crack is given by the **Young–Laplace** relation across a curved meniscus:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

In hydrostatic equilibrium, the pressure of the rising column at depth $h$ balances this capillary pressure:

$$\Delta P = \rho g h$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\rho g h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation** for a typical LPI penetrant on a $0.1\,\text{mm}$ scratch:

Given:
- $\gamma = 30 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{N/m}$
- $\theta = 20^{\circ} \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0.9397$
- $\rho = 880\,\text{kg/m}^{3}$ (typical hydrocarbon penetrant)
- $r = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$
- $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^{2}$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{2 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9397}{880 \times 9.81 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{5.638 \times 10^{-2}}{4.316 \times 10^{-1}} \\
&= 0.1306\,\text{m} \\
&\approx 13\,\text{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A $0.1\,\text{mm}$ wide scratch can wick penetrant up to **~13 cm** along its length. This means a benign machining line will produce a long, bright fluorescent streak under UV light — **a perfect false indication mimicking a crack**.

### 3.2 Dwell-Time Sensitivity Analysis

The depth of penetrant penetration as a function of dwell time $t$ follows a $\sqrt{t}$ Washburn-type relationship:

$$L(t) = L_{\infty}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\right]^{1/2}$$

Where:
- $L_{\infty}$ = equilibrium penetration depth (from Jurin's Law)
- $\tau$ = characteristic time constant (depends on viscosity $\mu$)
- $t$ = dwell time

For a defect of aperture $r$ and penetrant viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau = \frac{8\mu L_{\infty}}{\pi r^{2} \Delta P}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When students are asked *why insufficient dwell time produces false negatives*, they should write: $\;L(t) \propto \sqrt{t}\;$ — meaning that if dwell time is cut by half, the penetration depth drops by $\sqrt{1/2} \approx 0.707$, leaving shallow flaws unmarked.

### 3.3 Acceptance Criteria Decision Matrix

| Indication Observed | Magnitude | Acceptance Status (per ASME V Article 6) |
|---|---|---|
| Linear in critical region | Any length | **Reject** |
| Linear in non-critical region | $L \leq 1.5\,\text{mm}$ | Accept |
| Rounded (porosity) | $\leq 4$ per $150 \times 150\,\text{mm}$ area | Accept |
| Rounded (porosity) | Aligned linearly | Treat as linear — Reject |
| False / Spurious | Any | Re-clean and re-test |
| Masked (under paint) | Cannot evaluate | Strip coating, re-test |

### 3.4 Re-Test Decision Flowchart Logic

For every suspect indication the KTU-evaluated answer should follow:

**Step 1:** Identify indication shape (linear / rounded / branched).
**Step 2:** Locate relative to critical, principal, or non-critical zones.
**Step 3:** Compare against acceptance criteria (table above).
**Step 4:** If unclear, perform **surface re-prep** (re-grind, re-clean, re-etch lightly).
**Step 5:** Repeat the LPI cycle.
**Step 6:** Compare with magnetic particle or eddy current results (cross-verify with another NDT method).
**Step 7:** Document and classify as **relevant, non-relevant, or false**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of LPI Indications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LPI Indication Observed Under UV or Visible Light] --> B{Is the mark caused by an actual discontinuity?}
    B -- Yes --> C[RELEVANT INDICATION]
    C --> C1[Linear - Crack Cold Shut Lap]
    C --> C2[Rounded - Porosity Gas Pocket]
    B -- No --> D{Is the mark caused by surface feature?}
    D -- Yes --> E[NON-RELEVANT INDICATION]
    E --> E1[Machining Marks]
    E --> E2[Scribe Stamped Marks]
    E --> E3[Sharp Fillets Laps]
    D -- No --> F[SPURIOUS OR FALSE INDICATION]
    F --> F1[Contamination - Oil Sweat Fingerprints]
```
