```mermaid
    F --> F2[Process Error - Over Application Excess Cleaner]
    F --> F3[Environmental - Dust Lint UV Background]
    F --> F4[Equipment - Aged Penetrant Dirty Tank]

    classDef relevant fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nonrelevant fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef false fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class C,C1,C2 relevant
    class E,E1,E2,E3 nonrelevant
    class F,F1,F2,F3,F4 false
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Where False Indications Are Introduced

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Pre Cleaning] -->|Dirty or Inadequate| FI1[False Indication Risk - Oil Residue]
    P2[Step 2 Penetrant Application] -->|Contaminated Penetrant| FI2[False Indication Risk - Particulate Bloom]
    P3[Step 3 Dwell Time] -->|Too Short| FI3[False Negative Risk]
    P4[Step 4 Excess Removal] -->|Over Cleaning| FI4[False Indication - Halo Bloom]
    P5[Step 5 Developer Application] -->|Over Spray| FI5[False Indication - Masking]
    P6[Step 6 Drying] -->|Insufficient| FI6[False Indication - Diluted Developer]
    P7[Step 7 Inspection] -->|High Background UV| FI7[False Indication - False Brightness]

    classDef process fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef failure fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P6,P7 process
    class FI1,FI2,FI3,FI4,FI5,FI6,FI7 failure
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Safety Architecture in an LPI Booth

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHEMS [Chemical Hazards]
        CH1[Penetrant Hydrocarbons MSDS Sheet]
        CH2[Solvent Cleaner Volatile Flammable]
        CH3[Developer Silica Dust Inhalation]
        CH4[Emulsifier Surfactant Skin Irritant]
    end
    subgraph RAD [Radiation Hazard]
        RA1[UV A Lamp 365 nm Photokeratitis Risk]
    end
    subgraph PPE [Personal Protective Equipment]
        PP1[Nitrile Gloves]
        PP2[UV Blocking Goggles]
        PP3[Respirator for Developer Dust]
        PP4[Lab Coat Apron]
        PP5[Forced Ventilation Exhaust Hood]
    end
    subgraph PROC [Procedural Controls]
        PR1[Material Safety Data Sheet Review]
        PR2[Adequate Booth Ventilation]
        PR3[Fire Extinguisher Within Reach]
        PR4[Spill Containment Kit]
        PR5[Operator Training and Certification]
        PR6[Waste Disposal as per Hazardous Waste Rules]
    end
    CHEMS --> PPE
    RAD --> PPE
    PPE --> PROC
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Distinguishing Relevant vs Non-Relevant Indications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Indication Under UV Light] --> T1{Is it Linear?}
    T1 -- Yes --> T2{Length Greater than 1.5 mm?}
    T2 -- Yes --> T3[REJECT as RELEVANT Crack]
    T2 -- No --> T4{Sharp or Rounded Edges?}
    T4 -- Sharp --> T5[Likely Machining Mark - NON RELEVANT]
    T4 -- Rounded --> T6[REJECT as RELEVANT Defect]
    T1 -- No --> T7{Rounded Spots?}
    T7 -- Yes --> T8{Number per Unit Area?}
    T8 -- Within Limit --> T9[ACCEPT - Non Relevant Porosity]
    T8 -- Above Limit --> T10[REJECT - RELEVANT Porosity]
    T7 -- No --> T11{Branched Irregular?}
    T11 -- Yes --> T12[Investigate - Could be Cold Shut]
    T11 -- No --> T13[Suspicious False Indication - Re clean and Re test]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> Define the term *false indication* as applied to Liquid Penetrant Inspection. Mention any two causes.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **False Indication (LPI):** A visible mark produced during Liquid Penetrant Inspection that **does not correspond to an actual service-relevant discontinuity** in the part being tested. It arises from process errors, contamination, surface features, or environmental conditions rather than from a real flaw.
>
> **Two causes:**
> 1. Inadequate pre-cleaning leaving oil, paint, or coolant residue on the surface that traps penetrant.
> 2. Contamination of the penetrant with dust, lint, or aged material creating extraneous fluorescent specks.

*(2 marks definition + ½ + ½ for two causes = 3 marks)*

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> List the personal protective equipment (PPE) mandatory for operators performing fluorescent LPI.

**Model Answer:**

The mandatory PPE includes:

1. **Nitrile rubber gloves** — protection from hydrocarbon penetrants and solvents.
2. **UV-blocking goggles** (shade 5 or 6) — protection against UV-A photokeratitis.
3. **Respirator / dust mask** — protection when handling dry developer (silica dust).
4. **Lab coat or chemical-resistant apron** — skin protection from splashes.
5. **Closed-toe safety shoes** — general shop-floor safety.

*(½ mark per item = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Examiner Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, students answer **one full question from each module**, with internal choice. The 14 marks are split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Q. A (a)** Classify the different types of indications observed in Liquid Penetrant Inspection. With the help of a neat diagram, explain how each type originates. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. A (b)** Discuss in detail the causes and prevention of **false indications** in LPI. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Indications in LPI are classified into three broad categories:

1. **Relevant Indications** — caused by actual discontinuities such as cracks, porosity, laps, cold shuts, and undercuts in welds. They appear as **linear** or **rounded** marks depending on the flaw geometry. They are the only indications that influence the acceptance/rejection decision.

2. **Non-Relevant Indications** — produced by surface features that are not defects, e.g., machining marks, scribe marks, sharp fillets, weld ripples. Although they look like indications, they are not service-affecting and are documented but disregarded (subject to acceptance criteria).

3. **False (Spurious) Indications** — caused entirely by the inspection process itself. Examples include fingerprints, dust, lint, dirty penetrant, excess developer, over-cleaning halos, or high ambient UV background. They have no correlation with any real flaw.

> **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram above)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Causes of false indications:**

| S.No | Cause Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-cleaning failure | Residual oil, paint, coolant |
| 2 | Penetrant contamination | Aged penetrant, foreign particles |
| 3 | Process error | Over-application, under-rinsing, excess developer |
| 4 | Operator contamination | Fingerprints, sweat, glove residue |
| 5 | Environmental | Dust, lint, ambient UV, temperature drift |
| 6 | Surface roughness | Machining marks, grinding lines |

**Prevention measures:**

1. **Strict pre-cleaning** as per ASTM E 1417 / SSPC-SP standards.
2. **Use of fresh, filtered penetrant** with periodic bath replacement.
3. **Operator training** on dwell time, removal technique, and developer application.
4. **Clean booth environment** with controlled humidity (typically $30$–$60\%$) and temperature ($10$–$40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
5. **Periodic equipment calibration** of UV lamps, black light filters.
6. **Two-stage inspection** with a second qualified examiner for verification.
7. **Re-inspection protocol** when indications are ambiguous — strip coating, re-clean, and re-test.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

> **Q. B (a)** Explain the various **safety hazards** associated with Liquid Penetrant Inspection and the corresponding safety precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q. B (b)** Describe the recommended **first-aid and emergency response** procedures in case of accidental exposure to LPI chemicals. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The safety hazards in LPI fall under three broad heads:

1. **Chemical Hazards**
   - Hydrocarbon-based penetrants and solvent cleaners are **flammable** (flash point $40$–$60\,^{\circ}\text{C}$) and **irritating to skin and eyes**.
   - Halogenated solvents (e.g., trichloroethylene, perchloroethylene) are **suspected carcinogens** with cumulative liver and CNS toxicity.
   - Aerosol sprays may displace oxygen in confined booths → **asphyxiation risk**.

2. **Radiation Hazards**
   - UV-A lamps ($365\,\text{nm}$) can cause **photokeratitis** (welder's flash) and **erythema** on prolonged exposure.
   - Direct viewing of the lamp causes temporary but painful eye damage.

3. **Physical / Environmental Hazards**
   - Silica-based dry developers are respiratory hazards → **silicosis** with chronic exposure.
   - Hot drying ovens ($50$–$60\,^{\circ}\text{C}$) cause **burn risk**.
   - Slip hazards from spilled penetrants on the shop floor.

**Safety precautions:**

- **Ventilation:** Local exhaust ventilation maintaining $4$–$6$ air changes per hour.
- **PPE:** Nitrile gloves, UV goggles (shade 5–6), respirator for dry developer, apron.
- **Fire safety:** No open flames; bonded and grounded containers; fire extinguishers (CO₂ or dry powder) within 3 m.
- **MSDS availability** at the booth.
- **UV lamp shielding** and operator distance $\geq 30\,\text{cm}$.
- **Waste segregation** as per Hazardous Waste Management Rules.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**First-aid procedures:**

| Exposure Type | Immediate Action |
|---|---|
| Skin contact with penetrant | Wash with copious soap and water for $\geq 15$ min |
| Eye contact | Flush with clean water for $15$ min; seek ophthalmologist |
| Inhalation of solvent vapours | Move to fresh air; administer oxygen if needed |
| UV photokeratitis | Rest eyes in dark room; cold compress; medical attention |
| Ingestion of penetrant | Do NOT induce vomiting; seek immediate medical help |
| Solvent fire | Use CO₂ extinguisher; never use water on hydrocarbon fire |
| Spill containment | Use absorbent pads; ventilate area; dispose as hazardous waste |

**Emergency response protocol:**

1. Alert supervisor and evacuate non-essential personnel.
2. Activate booth emergency exhaust.
3. Use spill kit (absorbent pads, neutralisers) — never wash into drains.
4. Provide first aid and call medical assistance.
5. Complete incident report; review MSDS; modify SOP if needed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "false indication" with "non-relevant indication"** — these are NOT the same. False = process error. Non-relevant = surface feature, not a defect.
> 2. **Forgetting MSDS** — many students omit the Material Safety Data Sheet as a statutory requirement. KTU awarders deduct 1 mark for this.
> 3. **Listing hazards without precautions** — examiners expect a paired response: hazard → control measure.
> 4. **No numerical justification** — whenever possible, cite specific values ($365\,\text{nm}$ UV-A, $1000\,\mu\text{W/cm}^{2}$ irradiance, $50$–$60\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ drying). Vague answers lose marks.
> 5. **Skipping re-inspection protocol** — the single most-missed sub-step is "what to do when an indication is ambiguous."

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **False Indication** ≠ **Non-Relevant Indication**. False = spurious (process); Non-relevant = surface feature (still real marks).
- **Three categories** of indications: Relevant, Non-Relevant, False.
- **Six root causes**: cleaning failure, penetrant contamination, process error, operator contamination, environment, surface roughness.
- **Capillary physics**: $h = \dfrac{2\gamma\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ — small $r$ produces long penetration depth, leading to false cracks.
- **Dwell time**: $\sqrt{t}$ relationship — insufficient time = false negative (missed cracks).
- **UV-A wavelength**: **365 nm**; minimum irradiance **1000 μW/cm²**.
- **PPE mandatory**: nitrile gloves, UV-blocking goggles (shade 5–6), respirator, apron.
- **Chemicals used** are flammable (flash point $40$–$60°C$), toxic (CNS/liver), and carcinogenic (halogenated solvents).
- **Ventilation**: $4$–$6$ air changes/hour minimum.
- **Developer dust** = silica → silicosis risk → respirator mandatory.
- **MSDS** must be available at the inspection booth at all times.
- **First aid**: 15-min water flush for skin/eye; fresh air for inhalation; no induced vomiting on ingestion.
- **Re-inspection** is the correct response to any ambiguous indication, not immediate rejection.
- **Acceptance standards**: ASME V Article 6, ASTM E 433, ASTM E 1417, IS 3658.
- **Cross-verification** with another NDT method (MT, ET, UT) reduces false-positive/false-negative risk.
- **Documentation**: every indication must be recorded with location, size, orientation, and classification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Magnetization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methods of Magnetization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Magnetization** in the context of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) is defined as the deliberate process of inducing a controlled magnetic field within a ferromagnetic specimen to render surface and sub-surface discontinuities visible through the application of magnetic particles. According to KTU PEMET636 Module 2 (Magnetic Particle Testing - MPT), the **Methods of Magnetization** refer to the systematic engineering techniques used to generate either **longitudinal** or **circular (circumferential)** magnetic flux within a test specimen, as mandated by ASME Section V Article 7 and ASTM E709 standards.

The two fundamental classifications of magnetization techniques are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Magnetization Techniques:**
> 1. **Direct Magnetization** — Current is passed *directly through* the test specimen, which itself becomes the current-carrying conductor.
> 2. **Indirect Magnetization** — Current is passed through an *external conductor* (coil or yoke) that induces the magnetic field in the specimen without electrical contact.

| Field Type | Direction of Flux | Detects Defects Oriented |
|------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Longitudinal Field** | Parallel to the axis of the part | Transverse (perpendicular to axis) |
| **Circular Field** | Circumferential (rings around current path) | Parallel to the current flow |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to detect a hidden crack inside a steel pipe using a metal detector. The detector creates a magnetic "halo" inside the pipe. When this halo passes over the crack, the magnetic lines bulge out, much like water swirling around a rock in a stream. This disturbance pulls iron filings (magnetic particles) to the surface, creating a visible pattern that pinpoints the crack's location.

**Real-world analogy:**
- **Longitudinal Magnetization** ≈ Sliding a bar magnet *along* the length of a rod — magnetic lines run parallel to the rod's length.
- **Circular Magnetization** ≈ Passing electricity through a wire wrapped around a rod's cross-section — magnetic lines form concentric rings like ripples in a pond.

> [!NOTE]
> The cardinal rule of MPT (The Right Hand Rule): For **any** magnetization method, the magnetic flux must be **perpendicular to the expected defect orientation** to produce a clear, readable indication. This single principle dictates which method must be used for which defect type.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Magnetization

The following physical quantities are the standardized metrics used to define and quantify magnetization effectiveness in KTU-evaluated problems:

- **Magnetizing Force (H)** — measured in **Ampere-turns/meter (At/m)** or **Oersteds (Oe)**. 1 Oe ≈ 79.577 At/m.
- **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** — measured in **Tesla (T)** or **Gauss (G)**. 1 T = 10,000 G.
- **Magnetizing Current (I)** — measured in **Amperes (A)**.
- **Number of Turns (N)** — dimensionless integer.
- **Relative Permeability (μ_r)** — dimensionless, typically **100 to 5000** for ferromagnetic steels.
- **Coercivity (H_c)** and **Retentivity (B_r)** — measured in **A/m** and **T** respectively.

The governing relationship between these quantities is given by:

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$$

where $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m is the **permeability of free space**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theory of Magnetization

To magnetize a specimen effectively, we must understand that **ferromagnetic materials** contain countless microscopic magnetic domains, each acting as a tiny dipole. In the unmagnetized state, these domains are randomly oriented, producing zero net flux. Magnetization aligns these domains along an external field direction, producing a strong measurable flux.

The **ferromagnetic hysteresis loop** defines the relationship between applied magnetizing force **H** and induced flux density **B**:

- **Saturation Point** — All domains aligned; further increase in H produces negligible change in B.
- **Remanence (B_r)** — Residual flux density after H returns to zero.
- **Coercivity (H_c)** — Reverse field required to reduce B back to zero.

For MPT, the standard practice is to magnetize the part to between **2,000 to 4,000 A/m** (≈ 25 to 50 Oe), which corresponds to a point **just below the knee of the B-H curve** — enough to detect defects without saturating the part.

## 2.2 The Right-Hand Rule and Its Critical Importance

The **Right-Hand Rule** is the operational principle used to determine the direction of induced magnetic flux:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Right-Hand Rule Statement:** When a current-carrying conductor is gripped with the right hand such that the thumb points in the direction of conventional current flow, the curled fingers indicate the direction of the magnetic field lines encircling the conductor.

This rule is the foundation for selecting the correct magnetization method and is a guaranteed 2–3 mark question in KTU examinations.

## 2.3 Methods of Magnetization — Detailed Classification

### A. Methods Producing CIRCULAR (Circumferential) Magnetization

These methods generate flux lines that form **concentric circles** around the current path. They detect defects that run **parallel to the current flow**.

#### **A1. Head Shot Technique (Direct Contact Method)**

In this method, current is passed through the part itself using two heavy-duty clamps (called "heads" or "contact bars"). The current enters one end and exits the other, with the part serving as the conductor.

**Operational Principle:**
Current flows axially through the part. By the Right-Hand Rule, magnetic flux lines form circles around the longitudinal axis of the part.

**Required Current Calculation:**
For a solid cylindrical part of diameter *d*, the standard rule is:

$$I = K \cdot D$$

where:
- $I$ = magnetizing current in Amperes (AC, RMS) or Amperes (DC)
- $D$ = diameter of the part in millimetres
- $K$ = constant: **600 A/inch** (≈ 24 A/mm) for AC; **800 to 1000 A/inch** for DC

For longitudinal magnetization using a coil (when head-shot current value is referenced):

$$I_{\text{coil}} = \frac{K \cdot D}{N}$$

where $N$ is the number of coil turns.

#### **A2. Prod Technique (Local Magnetization)**

Two **prods** (pointed copper electrodes with brass tips) are placed on the surface of the part, and current is passed between them through the bulk material. The current creates a circular magnetic field between the prods.

**Applications:**
- Large parts where head-shot current would be impractical.
- Localized inspection of welds, castings, and forgings.

**Critical Limitations:**
> [!WARNING]
> Prod contact produces **arc burns** on the surface if pressure is insufficient. ASTM E709 restricts prod spacing to a minimum of **75 mm (3 inches)** and maximum of **200 mm (8 inches)** to prevent overheating and ensure adequate field coverage.

#### **A3. Central Conductor Technique**

A current-carrying conductor (copper bar or cable) is passed through the **bore** of a hollow cylindrical part. The current does not flow through the part itself but induces a circular field inside the bore that magnetizes the surrounding material.

**Field Strength Formula:**

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

where $r$ is the radial distance from the central conductor axis to the point of interest inside the part wall.

### B. Methods Producing LONGITUDINAL Magnetization

These methods generate flux lines that run **parallel to the long axis** of the part. They detect defects that run **perpendicular (transverse)** to the part axis.

#### **B1. Yoke Method (Indirect Magnetization)**

A **yoke** is essentially a U-shaped or C-shaped ferromagnetic core wrapped with a coil. When energized, it produces a strong magnetic field across its two pole pieces.

**Two Types of Yokes:**

| Yoke Type | Power Source | Field Strength | Use Case |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|----------|
| **Permanent Magnet Yoke** | Alnico or Nd-Fe-B magnet | Limited, ~50 Oe | Field inspections, no power available |
| **Electromagnetic Yoke (AC/DC)** | 115V/230V AC or rectified DC | Adjustable, up to 150 Oe | Workshop inspections, high productivity |

**Key Test — Lift Test:** The electromagnet yoke's strength is verified by its ability to lift a **4.5 kg (10 lb) mild steel block** when fully energized. This is a mandatory calibration check.

**Field Direction:** From **North Pole → through the part → South Pole**, i.e., **parallel to the line connecting the pole faces**.

#### **B2. Coil (Solenoid) Method (Indirect Magnetization)**

The part is placed *inside* or *near* a multi-turn coil carrying magnetizing current. The resulting field magnetizes the part longitudinally.

**Field Strength Inside a Long Solenoid:**

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}$$

where:
- $H$ = magnetizing field strength (At/m)
- $N$ = number of turns in the coil
- $I$ = current through the coil (A)
- $L$ = length of the coil (m)

**For a Coil of Length-to-Diameter Ratio > 10:**

$$H = \frac{NI}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu_r}$$

**For Short Parts (L/D ratio < 10) — Use Demagnetization Coefficient N_d:**

$$H_{\text{inside part}} = \frac{NI}{L} \cdot \left(\frac{\mu_r}{1 + N_d(\mu_r - 1)}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> When the part length is greater than the coil length, the **effective turns ratio** decreases. For parts longer than the coil, the recommended practice is to **rotate the part** so all sections are magnetized, or to **use a longer coil**.

#### **B3. Threading (Cable Wrap) Method**

A flexible cable is wrapped around the part (which acts as the core), and current is passed through the cable. This is functionally equivalent to a single-turn or few-turn coil and produces longitudinal magnetization along the axis of the part.

**Field Strength:**

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{\pi D}$$

where $D$ is the part diameter and $N$ is the number of cable wraps.

**Demagnetization Note:** Threading inherently leaves residual magnetism; therefore, demagnetization is mandatory after inspection.

### C. Permanent Magnetization (Residual Magnetization Method)

For parts with **high retentivity** (e.g., hardened steels, tool steels), the magnetizing current can be removed after the part is brought to saturation. The part **retains** sufficient flux to hold magnetic particles.

**Advantages:**
- No external power needed during inspection.
- Can inspect complex geometries after demounting from the magnetizer.
- Useful for **field inspections** and **batch processing**.

**Disadvantages:**
- Limited to high-retentivity materials.
- Lower sensitivity than continuous magnetization.
- Cannot detect **deep sub-surface defects** as effectively.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Application / Method** | **Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Flux density | All methods | Tesla (T) |
| $H = \dfrac{NI}{L}$ | Field inside solenoid | Coil magnetization | At/m |
| $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$ | Field around conductor | Central conductor, head shot | At/m |
| $H = \dfrac{NI}{\pi D}$ | Field inside threaded part | Cable wrap / threading | At/m |
| $I = K \cdot D$ | Required magnetizing current | Head shot rule | A |
| $I_{\text{coil}} = \dfrac{KD}{N}$ | Current for coil magnetization | Solenoid method | A |
| $\Re = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | Reluctance of magnetic path | Magnetic circuit analysis | At/Wb |
| $\Phi = \dfrac{MMF}{\Re} = \dfrac{NI}{\Re}$ | Total magnetic flux | Magnetic circuits | Weber (Wb) |
| $K = 600 \text{ A/inch (AC)}$ | Empirical constant | Head shot (AC) | A/inch |
| $K = 800\text{–}1000 \text{ A/inch (DC)}$ | Empirical constant | Head shot (DC) | A/inch |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Constants:**
> - $1 \text{ Oersted (Oe)} = 79.577$ At/m
> - $1 \text{ Gauss (G)} = 10^{-4}$ Tesla (T)
> - $1$ inch = 25.4 mm
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Magnetization Methods

The selection of an appropriate magnetization method is fundamental across multiple high-stakes engineering industries:

1. **Aerospace Industry** — Inspection of turbine discs, landing gear, and critical forgings using prod and yoke techniques for surface cracks.
2. **Pressure Vessel Fabrication** — Head-shot and central conductor methods for weld inspection in piping and storage tanks.
3. **Railway Engineering** — Yoke and threading methods for inspection of rails and axles in field environments.
4. **Power Generation** — Inspection of rotor shafts in turbines using coil magnetization to detect transverse fatigue cracks.
5. **Automotive Industry** — Batch residual magnetization of components (e.g., crankshafts, connecting rods) for high-volume production inspection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Magnetic Field Around a Straight Current-Carrying Conductor (Head Shot Foundation)

### Statement of the Physical Law
Consider an infinitely long straight conductor carrying a steady current $I$. By the symmetry of the problem, the magnetic field lines form concentric circles around the conductor, and the magnitude of $\vec{H}$ depends only on the radial distance $r$ from the conductor's axis.

### Application of Ampere's Circuital Law
Ampere's Law in integral form states that the line integral of the magnetic field intensity around any closed loop is equal to the total current enclosed by that loop:

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{enclosed}}$$

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Solution

**Step 1:** Choose a circular Amperian loop of radius $r$ centered on the conductor and lying in a plane perpendicular to the conductor.

**Step 2:** By symmetry, $\vec{H}$ is constant in magnitude on this loop and tangential to it. Therefore:

$$\vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \, dl \cos(0) = H \, dl$$

**Step 3:** Since $H$ is constant along the loop, and the loop circumference is $2\pi r$:

$$\oint_C H \, dl = H \oint_C dl = H \cdot 2\pi r$$

**Step 4:** Equating to the enclosed current $I$:

$$H \cdot 2\pi r = I$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $H$:

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 6:** The corresponding magnetic flux density:

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2\pi r}$$

**Final Result:** For a long straight conductor, the magnetic field varies inversely with the radial distance from the conductor's axis.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the *foundation* of the head-shot, prod, and central conductor techniques. In each of these methods, current flows through a conductor (either the part itself or a central bar), producing circular magnetization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Magnetic Field Inside a Long Solenoid (Coil Magnetization Foundation)

### Physical Setup
Consider a long solenoid with $N$ turns uniformly wound over a length $L$, carrying a current $I$.

### Application of Ampere's Law to a Rectangular Amperian Loop
Choose a rectangular closed path **abcd** with:
- **ab** inside the solenoid, parallel to the axis.
- **bc** perpendicular, going from interior to exterior.
- **cd** outside the solenoid, parallel to the axis.
- **da** perpendicular, returning to start.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Solution

**Step 1:** Write the line integral around the closed loop:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{ab} + \int_{bc} + \int_{cd} + \int_{da}$$

**Step 2:** Outside the solenoid (for an infinitely long solenoid), $H \to 0$, so $\int_{cd} = 0$.

**Step 3:** Along the perpendicular segments **bc** and **da**, $\vec{H}$ is perpendicular to $d\vec{l}$, so:

$$\int_{bc} = 0, \quad \int_{da} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Only segment **ab** contributes. Inside the solenoid, $H$ is parallel to the axis and constant:

$$\int_{ab} H \, dl = H \cdot L_{\text{inside}}$$

**Step 5:** The total current enclosed by the rectangular loop equals the number of turns per unit length times the length enclosed times the current per turn:

$$I_{\text{enclosed}} = \left(\frac{N}{L}\right) \cdot L_{\text{inside}} \cdot I$$

**Step 6:** Equating both sides of Ampere's Law:

$$H \cdot L_{\text{inside}} = \left(\frac{N}{L}\right) \cdot L_{\text{inside}} \cdot I$$

**Step 7:** Solving for $H$:

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}$$

**Final Result:** The magnetic field strength inside a long solenoid depends on the **ampere-turns per unit length**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Increasing either the number of turns or the current proportionally increases the magnetizing force. Doubling $N$ is equivalent to doubling $I$ for magnetizing purposes — this is why coil magnetization is so flexible in field control.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Coil Magnetization Current

### **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A ferromagnetic shaft of diameter 60 mm is to be magnetized using a 5-turn coil. The material requires a minimum magnetizing field of 3,200 At/m. If the coil length is 400 mm, calculate the required current.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- Number of turns, $N = 5$
- Coil length, $L = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.4 \text{ m}$
- Required field, $H = 3{,}200$ At/m

**Step 1:** Use the solenoid field equation:

$$H = \frac{NI}{L}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for current:

$$I = \frac{H \cdot L}{N}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:

$$I = \frac{3{,}200 \times 0.4}{5}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate:

$$I = \frac{1{,}280}{5} = 256 \text{ A}$$

**Answer:** A current of **256 Amperes** is required to achieve the necessary magnetizing field.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Head Shot Current Rule

### **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A solid steel shaft of diameter 100 mm is to be magnetized using the head-shot technique with AC current. Using the standard rule of 600 A/inch, calculate the required magnetizing current.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert diameter from mm to inches:

$$D = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{25.4 \text{ mm/inch}} = 3.937 \text{ inches}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the head-shot rule:

$$I = K \cdot D = 600 \text{ A/inch} \times 3.937 \text{ inches}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate:

$$I = 2{,}362.2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** If a 3-turn coil is to be used instead (to apply the same magnetizing field), calculate the coil current:

$$I_{\text{coil}} = \frac{K \cdot D}{N} = \frac{2{,}362.2}{3} \approx 787.4 \text{ A}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **2,362 A** for head-shot, or **787 A** if a 3-turn coil is used.

> [!NOTE]
> This problem demonstrates a critical engineering principle: **Coil magnetization dramatically reduces the current requirement** by trading current for turns. This is why coil magnetization is preferred for very large parts where head-shot currents would be impractical.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — Central Conductor Field

### **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A hollow steel pipe with inner diameter 200 mm and wall thickness 20 mm is magnetized using a central conductor carrying 1,500 A DC. Calculate the magnetic field strength at the inner surface of the pipe wall.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- Central conductor current, $I = 1{,}500$ A
- Inner radius, $r_{\text{inner}} = 200/2 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$

**Step 1:** Apply the field equation for a straight conductor:

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$H = \frac{1{,}500}{2\pi \times 0.1}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the denominator:

$$2\pi \times 0.1 = 0.2\pi \approx 0.6283$$

**Step 4:** Final calculation:

$$H = \frac{1{,}500}{0.6283} \approx 2{,}387 \text{ At/m}$$

**Answer:** The magnetizing field at the inner pipe wall is approximately **2,387 At/m** (≈ 30 Oe).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[METHODS OF MAGNETIZATION] --> B[DIRECT METHODS]
    A --> C[INDIRECT METHODS]
    A --> D[PERMANENT METHODS]
    
    B --> B1[Head Shot Technique]
    B --> B2[Prod Technique]
    B --> B3[Central Conductor Technique]
    
    C --> C1[Coil Solenoid Method]
    C --> C2[Threading Cable Wrap]
    C --> C3[Yoke Electromagnet or Permanent]
    
    D --> D1[Residual Magnetization]
    D --> D2[Saturate and Remove Current]
    
    B1 --> F1[CIRCULAR FIELD]
    B2 --> F1
    B3 --> F1
    C1 --> F2[LONGITUDINAL FIELD]
    C2 --> F2
    C3 --> F2
```

## 4.2 Field Direction and Defect Detection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Specimen with Defect] --> Q{Defect Orientation}
    Q --> R[Parallel to Axis]
    Q --> S[Transverse to Axis]
    
    R --> T[Need CIRCULAR Magnetization]
    S --> U[Need LONGITUDINAL Magnetization]
    
    T --> M1[Use Head Shot or Prod]
    U --> M2[Use Yoke or Coil]
    
    M1 --> OUT1[Flux lines cut defect perpendicular]
    M2 --> OUT2[Flux lines cut defect perpendicular]
    
    OUT1 --> INSPECT[Clear Magnetic Particle Indication]
    OUT2 --> INSPECT
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Magnetization System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Power Source AC or DC Supply] --> CTRL[Current Control Unit with Ammeter]
    CTRL --> SW[Switching Mechanism FW DC or AC]
    SW --> MG[Magnetizing Apparatus]
    
    MG --> M1[Yoke Head]
    MG --> M2[Prods Head Shot]
    MG --> M3[Coil Central Conductor]
    
    M1 --> SP[Test Specimen Ferromagnetic]
    M2 --> SP
    M3 --> SP
    
    SP --> FLUX[Establishes Controlled Magnetic Flux]
    FLUX --> APPLY[Apply Magnetic Particles Wet or Dry]
    APPLY --> OBS[Observe Indications under UV or White Light]
    OBS --> EVAL[Evaluate against Acceptance Standards]
    EVAL --> DEMAG{Demagnetize Required?}
    DEMAG --> YES[Demagnetize and Clean]
    DEMAG --> NO[Document and Discard]
```

## 4.4 Right-Hand Rule Application Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I[Current I Direction Thumb] --> RH[Apply Right Hand Rule]
    RH --> FLUX[Curled Fingers Show Magnetic Flux Direction]
    
    FLUX --> AXIS{Axis of Specimen}
    AXIS --> PARA[Flux Parallel to Axis equals Longitudinal Magnetization]
    AXIS --> CIRC[Flux Forms Circles around Current Path equals Circular Magnetization]
```

## 4.5 Selection Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Magnetization Method] --> Q1{Part Geometry}
    Q1 --> LONG[Long Cylindrical Part]
    Q1 --> HOLLOW[Hollow Cylinder]
    Q1 --> LOCAL[Local Inspection Needed]
    Q1 --> LARGE[Large Forging or Casting]
    
    LONG --> A1[Use Coil Method for Longitudinal Field]
    HOLLOW --> A2[Use Central Conductor for Circular Field inside Bore]
    LOCAL --> A3[Use Prod or Yoke for Localized Magnetization]
    LARGE --> A4[Use Yoke for Surface or Prod for Deep Inspection]
    
    A1 --> CHECK{Material Retentivity High?}
    A2 --> CHECK
    A3 --> CHECK
    A4 --> CHECK
    
    CHECK --> HIGH[Yes — Residual Method Possible]
    CHECK --> LOW[No — Use Continuous Magnetization with AC or DC]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Magnetization and state the Right-Hand Rule as applied to Magnetic Particle Testing.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Magnetization is the process of inducing a magnetic field within a ferromagnetic material by passing an electric current either directly through the part or through an external conductor (yoke or coil). This produces a controlled magnetic flux that interacts with discontinuities, causing them to attract magnetic particles and form visible indications.

**Right-Hand Rule (1 mark):**
According to the Right-Hand Rule, when the right hand is wrapped around a current-carrying conductor with the thumb pointing in the direction of conventional current flow, the curled fingers indicate the direction of the induced magnetic flux lines encircling the conductor.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Longitudinal and Circular Magnetization.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Longitudinal Magnetization | Circular Magnetization |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Field Direction** | Parallel to the long axis of the part | Circumferential (rings around current) |
| **Produced By** | Yoke, coil, or threading | Head shot, prod, or central conductor |
| **Detects Defects Oriented** | Transverse (perpendicular to axis) | Parallel to current flow |
| **Typical Method** | Solenoid with N turns | Current through part |
| **Formula** | $H = NI / L$ | $H = I / (2\pi r)$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question offers internal choice. Either **Question A** OR **Question B** must be answered. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Question:** Explain the different **Methods of Magnetization** used in Magnetic Particle Testing. Discuss in detail:
- **(a)** Head Shot Technique and Prod Technique for circular magnetization. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** Yoke Method and Coil Method for longitudinal magnetization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Head Shot Technique and Prod Technique (7 Marks):**

**Head Shot Technique (4 marks):**

The Head Shot technique is a **direct magnetization method** where current is passed directly through the test specimen using two heavy-duty clamps called "heads" or "contact bars." The current enters one head and exits the other, with the part acting as the conductor.

- **Flux Pattern:** By the Right-Hand Rule, current flowing axially through the part produces **circular (circumferential) magnetic flux lines** that form concentric rings around the longitudinal axis of the part.
- **Defect Detection:** Detects defects whose **longitudinal axis is parallel to the current flow**, because these defects cut across the flux lines perpendicularly and cause leakage fields.
- **Current Rule:** The required magnetizing current follows the empirical rule $I = K \cdot D$, where $K = 600$ A/inch for AC and $800$–$1000$ A/inch for DC, and $D$ is the part diameter in inches.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires high currents for large parts; arc burn risk at contact points if pressure is insufficient.

**Prod Technique (3 marks):**

The Prod technique is used for **localized magnetization** of large parts where head-shot current would be excessive. Two pointed copper electrodes (prods) are pressed against the part surface, and current flows between them through the bulk material.

- **Flux Pattern:** Circular magnetic field is created in the region *between* the prods.
- **ASTM E709 Constraints:** Prod spacing must be between **75 mm (min)** and **200 mm (max)**.
- **Applications:** Local weld inspection, casting surface defects, and field repairs.
- **Disadvantage:** Leaves arc burn marks; not suitable for finished or precision surfaces.

---

**(b) Yoke Method and Coil Method (7 Marks):**

**Yoke Method (3.5 marks):**

The Yoke is a portable indirect magnetization device comprising a U-shaped ferromagnetic core with a coil wound around it. Two types exist:

- **Permanent Magnet Yoke:** Uses Alnico or Nd-Fe-B magnets; suitable for field use without power.
- **Electromagnetic Yoke:** Powered by AC or DC; provides adjustable field strength.

**Flux Direction:** From the North Pole face, through the part under inspection, into the South Pole face — i.e., **parallel to the line joining the poles** (longitudinal).

**Lift Test:** Calibration requires the yoke to lift a **4.5 kg (10 lb) mild steel block** against gravity when fully energized. This is the standard acceptance criterion per ASTM E709.

**Advantages:** Portable, flexible for any part shape, suitable for welds and field inspections.

**Coil Method (3.5 marks):**

The Coil (Solenoid) method places the test part *inside* or *near* a multi-turn coil carrying current. The part experiences a strong longitudinal magnetic field along its axis.

- **Field Equation:** $H = NI / L$ (for long solenoids).
- **Effective Turns:** For short parts where L/D < 10, a demagnetization coefficient correction is required.
- **Operation Modes:**
  - **Encircling Coil:** Part passed through stationary coil (continuous magnetization on production lines).
  - **Longitudinal Coil:** Part stationary, coil energized.
- **Advantages:** High field strength with relatively low current (because of $N$ turns); uniform magnetization of entire part.
- **Disadvantage:** Part must fit within or near the coil; ineffective for very long parts relative to coil length.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Question:** With suitable diagrams, derive the expressions for:
- **(a)** The magnetic field strength around a straight current-carrying conductor. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The magnetic field strength inside a long solenoid. **(7 marks)**

State how these derivations apply to the choice of magnetization methods in NDT.

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Magnetic Field Around a Straight Conductor (7 Marks):**

**Statement:** Consider a long straight conductor carrying a steady current $I$. By the Right-Hand Rule, the magnetic field forms concentric circles in planes perpendicular to the conductor.

**Ampere's Circuital Law:**

$$\oint_C \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{\text{enclosed}}$$

**Selection of Amperian Loop:** A circular loop of radius $r$ centered on the conductor.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$\oint_C H \, dl = H \cdot (2\pi r) = I$$

Therefore:

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

And:

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2\pi r}$$

**NDT Application (2 marks):** This derivation directly applies to the **head shot, prod, and central conductor techniques**. In all three methods, current passes through a conductor (part or bar), producing circular magnetization. The field strength at any point in the part wall depends on its radial distance from the current path. Higher currents or smaller part cross-sections produce stronger fields.

---

**(b) Magnetic Field Inside a Long Solenoid (7 Marks):**

**Statement:** A long solenoid has $N$ uniformly wound turns over length $L$, carrying current $I$.

**Ampere's Circuital Law applied to a rectangular loop abcd:**

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_{ab} + \int_{bc} + \int_{cd} + \int_{da} = NI_{\text{enc}}$$

**Contributions:**
- Outside solenoid: $\int_{cd} = 0$ (far field negligible for long solenoid).
- Perpendicular segments: $\int_{bc} = \int_{da} = 0$ ($\vec{H} \perp d\vec{l}$).
- Inside segment: $\int_{ab} = H \cdot L_{\text{inside}}$.
- Enclosed current: $I_{\text{enc}} = (N/L) \cdot L_{\text{inside}} \cdot I$.

**Therefore:**

$$H \cdot L_{\text{inside}} = \frac{N}{L} \cdot L_{\text{inside}} \cdot I$$

$$H = \frac{NI}{L}$$

**NDT Application (2 marks):** This derivation underpins the **coil magnetization, yoke, and threading methods**. Increasing the number of turns $N$ linearly increases the magnetizing field for the same current — this is the key engineering advantage that allows small currents to produce strong longitudinal magnetization. For example, with $N = 10$ turns and $L = 0.5$ m, only $I = 160$ A is needed to achieve $H = 3200$ At/m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Field Direction:** Students often state that "head-shot produces longitudinal magnetization" or "yoke produces circular magnetization." This is **incorrect**. Head-shot = circular; Yoke = longitudinal. Memorize this mapping rigorously.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Ampere's Law Derivations:** Always include units (At/m for $H$, m for $L$, A for $I$). Losing units often costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Demagnetization Discussion:** For residual magnetization methods, demagnetization is mandatory after inspection. Forgetting this loses 2 marks easily.
>
> 4. **Not Specifying AC vs DC Constants:** The head-shot rule uses $K = 600$ A/inch for AC and $800$–$1000$ A/inch for DC. Generic "K = 600" without specifying AC is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Misapplying the Right-Hand Rule:** When describing flux direction, always state **"By the Right-Hand Rule, the flux lines form concentric circles in the plane perpendicular to the current direction."** A vague mention without specifying the rule loses marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Magnetization = inducing magnetic flux** in a ferromagnetic specimen to detect defects.
- ✅ **Two fundamental field types:** Longitudinal (parallel to axis) and Circular (circumferential).
- ✅ **Direct magnetization** = current through the part itself (Head Shot, Prod, Central Conductor).
- ✅ **Indirect magnetization** = current through external source (Yoke, Coil, Threading).
- ✅ **Right-Hand Rule** is the operational tool — thumb = current, fingers = flux direction.
- ✅ **Head Shot Rule:** $I = K \cdot D$ where $K = 600$ A/inch (AC) or $800$–$1000$ A/inch (DC).
- ✅ **Coil field:** $H = NI / L$ (solenoid formula).
- ✅ **Conductor field:** $H = I / (2\pi r)$ (Ampere's Law result).
- ✅ **Defect must be perpendicular to flux** for maximum leakage field and indication clarity.
- ✅ **Yoke Lift Test:** Must lift **4.5 kg (10 lb)** mild steel block for acceptance.
- ✅ **Prod spacing:** 75 mm minimum to 200 mm maximum per ASTM E709.
- ✅ **Permanent Magnet Yoke** is for field use; **Electromagnetic Yoke** is for workshop use.
- ✅ **Coil magnetization advantage:** Low current requirement because $N$ turns multiply the field.
- ✅ **Demagnetization** is mandatory after threading and residual field inspections.
- ✅ **Residual magnetization** works only on **high-retentivity** materials (hardened steels).
- ✅ **Magnetize to ~2,000–4,000 At/m** for proper inspection sensitivity.
- ✅ **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- ✅ **Conversion:** 1 Oe ≈ 79.577 At/m; 1 G = 10⁻⁴ T.
- ✅ **Field direction must be perpendicular to expected defect orientation** for clear indications.
- ✅ **Central Conductor** is ideal for hollow cylindrical parts (pipe, tubes, bores).
- ✅ **Yoke** produces surface-sensitive fields; **Coil** produces through-volume fields.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetization Techniques: Head Shot, Cold Shot, Central Conductor, and Yoke Magnetization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Magnetization Techniques: Head Shot, Cold Shot, Central Conductor, and Yoke Magnetization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Magnetization in NDT

Magnetization is the foundational step in **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)**, one of the most widely used Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods for detecting surface and sub-surface (near-surface) discontinuities in **ferromagnetic materials** such as steel, iron, nickel, and cobalt alloys. Without proper magnetization, the formation of leakage fields at defect sites is impossible, and consequently, defect indication cannot occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Magnetization is the process of inducing a magnetic field within a ferromagnetic component such that any discontinuity (crack, inclusion, porosity, laps, seams) will produce a **Leakage Flux (Leakage Field)** perpendicular to the defect plane. This leakage flux attracts ferromagnetic particles (dry powder or wet suspension) to form a visible indication called a **Magnetogram**.

## 1.2 The Four Magnetization Techniques (Module 2 Focus)

### 1.2.1 Head Shot (Contact Shot) Technique

**Formal Definition:** The *Head Shot* technique (also called **Contact Shot** or **Direct Magnetization**) is a magnetization method where **high-amperage electric current is passed directly through the test specimen using prods (contact electrodes/handles)** placed on the surface of the component. The current enters from one prod, flows through the part, and exits from the other prod, creating a **circular (transverse) magnetic field** around the current path.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine holding a water hose and letting water flow through it. The water (current) flows in a straight line, but the *pressure field* around the hose radiates outward in a circular pattern. Similarly, in Head Shot, the electrical current flows in a straight line between the two prods, but the **magnetic field lines wrap around the current path in concentric circles** (governed by the **Right-Hand Thumb Rule**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visual Cue:** In Head Shot, the magnetic field direction is ALWAYS **perpendicular** to the direction of current flow. To detect a longitudinal crack, the prods must be placed on either side of the suspected defect, so that the field crosses the crack transversely.

### 1.2.2 Cold Shot Technique (Residual Magnetization)

**Formal Definition:** The *Cold Shot* technique involves **magnetizing the specimen first, then discontinuing the current** before applying the magnetic particles. The indication is formed by the **Residual Magnetism (Remanent Flux Density, Br)** retained in the part after the magnetizing current is switched off.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a stretched rubber band. When you stretch it (magnetize) and then release it (cut the current), the rubber band doesn't completely return to its original size—it retains some "memory" of being stretched. Similarly, ferromagnetic materials retain a portion of their magnetic field after the magnetizing force is removed. The Cold Shot uses this *lingering memory* to form the indication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Shot Requirement:** This technique is ONLY suitable for materials with **HIGH RETENTIVITY** (high Br), such as hard steels (e.g., AISI 1040, 4140). It is unsuitable for soft, low-carbon steels because their residual magnetism is too weak to attract particles effectively.

### 1.2.3 Central Conductor Technique

**Formal Definition:** The *Central Conductor* technique is a magnetization method used primarily for **tubular/hollow components** (pipes, tubes, pressure vessels, cylindrical shells). A solid bar (or cable) carrying high-amperage current is passed **through the bore (hollow center)** of the component. The current flowing through the central conductor induces a **longitudinal magnetic field** within the walls of the tubular part.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine inserting an electrical heating rod (current-carrying conductor) inside a hollow pipe. The heat (magnetic field) radiates outward from the rod in all directions, passing through the pipe walls. Any crack in the wall will be hit "edge-on" by this radial field, producing a leakage flux and visible indication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Application:** Central conductor magnetization is extensively used in inspecting **welded pipe joints, oil pipelines, boiler tubes, and storage tank shells** where access to internal walls is critical.

### 1.2.4 Yoke Magnetization Technique

**Formal Definition:** The *Yoke Magnetization* technique uses a **permanent magnet or an electromagnet (yoke)** in physical contact with the test specimen. The yoke provides a **closed magnetic circuit** through the part, inducing magnetic flux in the region between the two poles (legs) of the yoke. No direct current passes through the part.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a horseshoe magnet (the classic "U-shape" magnet from school). When you place its two poles on a piece of iron, the iron becomes magnetized. A Yoke in MPT is essentially a portable, powerful version of this horseshoe magnet—either permanent or electromagnetic—designed for field inspection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** Yoke magnetization is the most commonly asked technique in KTU exams due to its simplicity, portability, and relevance to field NDT inspections of welds, castings, and large structural components.

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot of Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Summary for Quick Recall**

| Technique | Current Path | Field Direction | Best For |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|----------|
| **Head Shot** | Through the part (prod-to-prod) | Circular (transverse to current) | Surface cracks in plates, forgings |
| **Cold Shot** | Through the part (residual) | Remanent field | High-retentivity hard steels |
| **Central Conductor** | Through a bar in the bore | Longitudinal (along axis) | Tubes, pipes, hollow cylinders |
| **Yoke** | Through yoke windings | Between pole legs | Welds, large structures, field use |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Magnetization

Every magnetization technique, regardless of the method, relies on a single fundamental principle: **Ampère's Circuital Law** and **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

### 2.1.1 Right-Hand Thumb Rule (Field Direction)

The direction of the magnetic field generated around a current-carrying conductor is determined by the **Right-Hand Thumb Rule**:

> Grasp the conductor with the right hand such that the **thumb points in the direction of conventional current flow**. The **curled fingers indicate the direction of the magnetic flux lines** encircling the conductor.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic of Head Shot (Step-by-Step)

1. The test specimen is cleaned thoroughly to remove dirt, grease, oil, paint, and loose scale.
2. Two **prods (electrode handles)** are firmly pressed onto the surface of the part at the locations flanking the area of interest.
3. A high-amperage, low-voltage **AC or DC magnetizing current** (typically **1000–4000 A**) is passed through the prods.
4. The current flows through the bulk of the part between the contact points, generating a **circular magnetic field** perpendicular to the current path.
5. Where a defect disrupts the surface, the magnetic field lines "leak out" of the material, forming a **Leakage Flux**.
6. **Magnetic particles** (dry powder or wet fluorescent suspension) applied over the surface are attracted to the leakage flux and accumulate to form a visible indication.

### 2.1.3 Operational Logic of Cold Shot (Step-by-Step)

1. The specimen is magnetized using either a Head Shot, Central Conductor, or Coil magnetization technique.
2. **Before applying magnetic particles**, the magnetizing current is switched OFF.
3. The specimen, due to its **magnetic hysteresis**, retains a portion of its magnetization called **Residual Flux Density (Br)**.
4. Magnetic particles are then applied to the surface; they are attracted to leakage fields generated by defects using the residual magnetism.
5. The indication formed is often **sharper and cleaner** because there is no electromagnetic interference or arcing at the prods during particle application.

### 2.1.4 Operational Logic of Central Conductor (Step-by-Step)

1. A solid copper bar or flexible cable is threaded through the **bore (internal diameter)** of the tubular component.
2. The conductor is connected to a high-amperage current source.
3. Current flows axially through the central conductor, generating a **concentric circular magnetic field** in the surrounding space.
4. This field cuts through the walls of the tube in a **circumferential manner**, magnetizing the wall longitudinally along the tube axis.
5. Cracks aligned parallel to the tube axis are detected because the field crosses them transversely, producing leakage flux.
6. For full coverage, the component may be rotated, or multiple shots may be taken along the length.

### 2.1.5 Operational Logic of Yoke Magnetization (Step-by-Step)

1. A **permanent magnet yoke** (using Alnico or NdFeB magnets) OR an **electromagnetic yoke** (AC or DC energized coil around a soft iron core) is selected.
2. The two pole legs (feet) of the yoke are placed in firm contact with the cleaned surface of the test specimen.
3. For **AC Yoke**: The flux alternates at line frequency (50/60 Hz), producing strong particle mobility due to the vibration effect.
4. For **DC Yoke**: The flux is steady and penetrates deeper into the material, suitable for sub-surface defects.
5. Magnetic particles (typically dry powder) are dusted over the surface or sprayed as a wet suspension.
6. Indications form at defect sites between the pole legs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are directly relevant for numerical problems in KTU University Exams (ESE) and Module Internal Assessments.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Ampère's Law** | $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enc}$ | Tesla-meter / Amperes |
| 2 | **Magnetic Field Intensity (H)** around a long straight conductor | $H = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r}$ | A/m (Amperes per meter) |
| 3 | **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** in a medium | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Tesla (T) |
| 4 | **Coercivity** (Hc) | Field needed to reduce B to zero | A/m |
| 5 | **Retentivity / Remanence** (Br) | Residual B after H = 0 | Tesla (T) |
| 6 | **Required Magnetizing Current** (Head Shot) | $I = \dfrac{H \times 2 \pi r}{\mu_0 \mu_r}$ | Amperes |
| 7 | **Lifting Power** (Yoke) | Minimum **4.5 kg (10 lbs)** at 100 mm pole spacing (ASTM E709) | Kilograms / Pounds |
| 8 | **Lift-off Distance** | Maximum **50 mm** between yoke pole and surface during pull test | Millimeters |
| 9 | **Pole Spacing** | Typically **50–200 mm** for AC; **75–150 mm** for DC Yoke | Millimeters |
| 10 | **Demagnetization Factor** | $N_d$ for various geometries (sphere = 1/3, long rod = 0) | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** In KTU exams, do NOT confuse **Magnetic Field Intensity (H)** in A/m with **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** in Tesla. They are related by permeability ($\mu$), but their units and physical meanings are different.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Head Shot** is used in **foundries and forging shops** for detecting transverse surface cracks in shafts, billets, and rough castings.
- **Cold Shot** is critical in **aerospace component inspection** of hard, high-carbon steel parts where residual field provides clean indications.
- **Central Conductor** is indispensable in **oil & gas pipeline inspection**, **boiler tube examination**, and **heat exchanger tube testing**.
- **Yoke magnetization** is the **field-inspection workhorse** for **weld inspection** in bridges, ship hulls, pressure vessels, and storage tanks—anywhere portability matters more than precise field direction control.

## 2.4 Why and How — The Underlying Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Question:** *Why do we need different magnetization techniques?*
> **Answer:** Because defects can occur in **any orientation** on a real component. A single-shot technique (e.g., Head Shot in one direction) will only detect defects perpendicular to the field. To detect defects in **all orientations**, the part must be magnetized in **at least two perpendicular directions** (often **longitudinal + transverse**) — hence the need for multiple techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "How" Question:** *How does the leakage flux attract particles?*
> **Answer:** At a defect site (e.g., a surface crack), the magnetic flux lines cannot pass through the air gap (non-magnetic discontinuity). They bulge outward, creating a **North-South pole pair at the edges of the crack**. This localized dipole field exerts a strong attractive force on the ferromagnetic particles, which align along the flux lines and accumulate at the crack edges, forming the visible **Magnetogram**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Magnetic Field Around a Current-Carrying Conductor (Head Shot Foundation)

This is the core derivation that justifies Head Shot magnetization. Let us derive it rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a long, straight, current-carrying conductor carrying current **I** Amperes. The conductor is surrounded by a non-magnetic medium (air) with permeability $\mu_0$. We wish to find the magnetic field intensity **H** at a perpendicular distance **r** from the center of the conductor.

### 3.1.2 Application of Ampère's Circuital Law

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enc}$$

Choose an **Amperian Loop** (imaginary circular path) of radius $r$ coaxial with the conductor. By symmetry, the magnitude of $H$ is constant at all points on this loop.

**Step 1: Evaluate the left-hand side**

The line integral simplifies because $H$ is parallel to $d\vec{l}$ at every point on the circle and $|H|$ is constant:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \oint dl = H \cdot (2 \pi r)$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the right-hand side**

The enclosed current is simply $I_{enc} = I$ (assuming current flows in one direction).

**Step 3: Equate both sides**

$$H \cdot (2 \pi r) = I$$

**Step 4: Solve for H**

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r}}$$

This is the field intensity at a distance $r$ from a straight conductor.

### 3.1.3 Convert to Flux Density B

In air (or any non-magnetic medium), the magnetic flux density is:

$$B = \mu_0 H$$

$$\boxed{B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}}$$

### 3.1.4 Engineering Application — Head Shot

In a Head Shot, the **specimen itself** acts as the current-carrying conductor. Therefore, at any point inside the specimen at distance $r$ from the central current axis:

$$H_{inside} = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r}$$

The flux density inside the ferromagnetic material becomes:

$$B_{inside} = \mu_0 \mu_r H_{inside} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \mu_r I}{2 \pi r}$$

where $\mu_r$ is the **relative permeability** of the specimen material (typically 100–10000 for steel).

### 3.1.5 Required Current for a Desired Field Strength

For a known specimen material ($\mu_r$ known) and desired magnetizing field $H$, the required current is:

$$\boxed{I_{required} = 2 \pi r H = \dfrac{2 \pi r B_{desired}}{\mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Step-by-Step Numerical Example:**

**Problem:** A steel plate ($\mu_r = 500$) of thickness 25 mm is to be magnetized using the Head Shot technique. The current prods are placed 100 mm apart. If the required magnetizing field intensity inside the part is $H = 3200$ A/m, calculate the magnetizing current needed.

**Step 1: Identify the effective distance**

For a Head Shot on a plate of thickness $t$, the effective $r$ is approximately the half-thickness (since the current flows through the bulk):

$$r = \dfrac{t}{2} = \dfrac{25 \text{ mm}}{2} = 12.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0125 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula**

$$I = 2 \pi r H$$

$$I = 2 \times \pi \times 0.0125 \times 3200$$

$$I = 251.33 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 251 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 3: Verify Flux Density**

$$B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 500 \times 3200$$

$$B = 2.01 \text{ T}$$

This is well above the typical **saturation flux density** of mild steel (~1.6–2.0 T), confirming adequate magnetization.

## 3.2 Derivation: Residual Flux Density for Cold Shot

The Cold Shot technique relies on the **magnetic hysteresis loop** of the specimen material.

### 3.2.1 Hysteresis Loop Concepts

- **Coercivity (Hc):** The reverse magnetizing field required to reduce $B$ to zero after the material has been saturated.
- **Retentivity (Br):** The residual flux density retained by the material after the magnetizing field is removed.
- For **hard magnetic materials** (e.g., high-carbon steel, Alnico): High $H_c$ (>1000 A/m) and high $B_r$ (>0.8 T) → Suitable for Cold Shot.
- For **soft magnetic materials** (e.g., low-carbon steel, pure iron): Low $H_c$ (<100 A/m) and low $B_r$ (<0.4 T) → NOT suitable for Cold Shot.

### 3.2.2 Effective Field for Cold Shot

The effective field that produces leakage flux at a defect is:

$$H_{eff} = \dfrac{B_r}{\mu_0 \mu_r}$$

For Cold Shot to be effective:

$$H_{eff} \geq H_{min}$$

where $H_{min}$ is the minimum field intensity (~800–2400 A/m per ASTM E709) needed to form visible indications.

### 3.2.3 Material Suitability Criterion

For Cold Shot to work:

$$B_r \geq \mu_0 \mu_r H_{min}$$

For a hard steel with $B_r = 1.0$ T, $\mu_r = 200$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$:

$$H_{eff} = \dfrac{1.0}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200} \approx 3979 \text{ A/m}$$

This is more than adequate for indication formation.

## 3.3 Derivation: Central Conductor Field

For a central conductor carrying current $I$ through a tube of inner radius $r_i$ and outer radius $r_o$, the magnetic field inside the tube wall varies with radius:

### 3.3.1 Field at Inner Wall ($r = r_i$)

$$H_{inner} = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r_i}$$

### 3.3.2 Field at Outer Wall ($r = r_o$)

$$H_{outer} = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r_o}$$

### 3.3.3 Average Field in Wall

$$\boxed{H_{avg} = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{r_o - r_i}}$$

### 3.3.4 Numerical Example

**Problem:** A steel tube has inner radius 50 mm and outer radius 60 mm. A central conductor carries 1500 A. Calculate the field at the inner and outer walls.

**Step 1: Inner Wall**

$$H_{inner} = \dfrac{1500}{2 \pi \times 0.050} = \dfrac{1500}{0.3142} = 4775 \text{ A/m}$$

**Step 2: Outer Wall**

$$H_{outer} = \dfrac{1500}{2 \pi \times 0.060} = \dfrac{1500}{0.3770} = 3979 \text{ A/m}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

The inner wall experiences a **stronger field** (4775 A/m) than the outer wall (3979 A/m). This non-uniformity is a known limitation of the Central Conductor technique—surface defects at the inner wall are easier to detect than at the outer wall for a given current.

## 3.4 Derivation: Yoke Magnetization Field Strength

For an electromagnet yoke with $N$ turns of coil carrying current $I_y$, the magnetomotive force (MMF) is:

$$\boxed{F_{mmf} = N \cdot I_y \quad \text{(Ampere-turns, At)}}$$

The field intensity in the specimen (assuming yoke legs are in good contact) is:

$$H_{specimen} = \dfrac{F_{mmf}}{L_{magnetic \, path}}$$

where $L_{magnetic \, path}$ is the total length of the flux path through the yoke + air gaps + specimen.

### 3.4.1 Lifting Power Criterion (ASTM E709)

The **pull-off (lifting) test** verifies the yoke's ability to magnetize a standard steel block. The minimum acceptance criterion is:

$$\boxed{F_{lift} \geq 4.5 \text{ kg} \;\; (10 \text{ lbs}) \;\; \text{at pole spacing} = 100 \text{ mm}}$$

This empirical criterion ensures adequate magnetization without requiring explicit calculation.

### 3.4.2 Pole Spacing Rules

- **AC Yoke:** Pole spacing $50$–$200$ mm (typically **100–150 mm**).
- **DC Yoke:** Pole spacing $50$–$150$ mm (typically **75–100 mm**).
- **Lift-off Distance** during pull test: Maximum **50 mm** before pull-off occurs.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Technique Selection

| Technique | Defect Orientation Detected | Material Suitability | Portability | Typical Current / Field |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Head Shot** | Transverse to current direction (longitudinal cracks) | All ferromagnetic | Low (bench setup) | 1000–4000 A |
| **Cold Shot** | Same as the magnetization method used | High-retentivity steels only | Low (bench) | Residual Br |
| **Central Conductor** | Longitudinal (axial) cracks in tubes | All ferromagnetic tubes | Medium | 1000–3000 A |
| **AC Yoke** | Transverse to pole axis (between legs) | All ferromagnetic | **Very High** | 50–200 A (coil) |
| **DC Yoke** | Transverse to pole axis (deeper penetration) | All ferromagnetic | **Very High** | 50–200 A (coil) |
| **Permanent Yoke** | Transverse to pole axis (surface only) | All ferromagnetic | **Highest** | N/A (fixed Br) |

## 3.6 Demagnetization After Inspection

After MPT inspection, especially with Cold Shot, components may retain unwanted residual magnetism that can interfere with subsequent machining, assembly, or service (e.g., causing attraction of ferrous debris in bearings). **Demagnetization** is performed by:

1. Applying an **alternating current** of progressively decreasing amplitude.
2. Slowly withdrawing the component from the field OR gradually reducing the current to zero.
3. The alternating field repeatedly reverses the magnetic domains with diminishing intensity until $B_r \to 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Criterion for Demagnetization:** Residual field must be **less than 0.003 T (3 mT / 30 Gauss)** at the surface for most engineering applications. This is measured with a **Gaussmeter / Hall-effect probe**.

```mermaid
    q2 -->|Longitudinal Crack| coil1[Use Coil Magnetization]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Head Shot Magnetization Technique
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Head Shot (also called Contact Shot) is a magnetization technique used in Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) in which high-amperage low-voltage electrical current is passed **directly through the test specimen** using two contact electrodes (prods) placed on the component surface. The current flowing through the part generates a **circular magnetic field** perpendicular to the direction of current flow, in accordance with the Right-Hand Thumb Rule. This field detects defects oriented perpendicular to the current direction, producing leakage flux that attracts magnetic particles to form visible indications. The prods must be firmly pressed with adequate contact pressure to avoid arcing, and the surface must be cleaned to remove contaminants that impede current flow.

---

### Question 2: List Any Four Advantages of Yoke Magnetization
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Four advantages of Yoke Magnetization are:

1. **Portability:** Yokes (especially permanent magnet types) are lightweight and battery/electric free, making them ideal for field inspections at remote sites like bridges, ships, and offshore platforms.
2. **No Electrical Contact:** Since no current passes directly through the part, there is no risk of burning, arcing, or overheating at contact points — useful for finished or coated surfaces.
3. **Dual Field Capability:** AC yokes provide good surface sensitivity, while DC yokes provide deeper penetration for sub-surface defects.
4. **Simplicity:** Requires minimal training and setup compared to Head Shot or Central Conductor rigs, reducing operator dependency and inspection time.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper - Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **Head Shot magnetization technique** used in Magnetic Particle Testing. State any **three advantages and three limitations** of this technique. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A steel plate of thickness 20 mm is to be magnetized using the Head Shot technique. The relative permeability of the steel is 600. Calculate the magnetizing current required to produce a magnetic flux density of **1.5 Tesla** at the mid-thickness of the plate. **[7 Marks]**

### MODEL ANSWER (Question A)

#### Part (a) — Head Shot Explanation, Advantages, Limitations

**Explanation with Sketch Description:**

In the Head Shot technique, the test specimen is clamped between two prods (electrode handles) connected to a high-current source. Current passes from one prod, through the bulk of the specimen, and out through the other prod. By the Right-Hand Thumb Rule, a **circular (transverse) magnetic field** is generated around the current path inside the part.

When a crack or discontinuity is oriented perpendicular to the current flow direction, the magnetic flux lines cannot pass through the non-magnetic air gap of the crack. They bulge outward at the surface, creating a **Leakage Flux** (N-S dipole at crack edges). Magnetic particles applied to the surface are attracted to this leakage and form a visible indication called a **Magnetogram**.

**Three Advantages:**

1. Simple to set up; requires only prods and a current source.
2. Generates strong circular field ideal for detecting transverse cracks.
3. Field intensity can be precisely controlled by adjusting current.

**Three Limitations:**

1. Prods cause **burn marks** on the surface if contact is poor; not suitable for finished components.
2. Detects only defects perpendicular to current; longitudinal cracks in current direction are missed.
3. Heat generation and arcing at contact points require careful surface preparation and operator skill.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Plate thickness, $t = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- Relative permeability, $\mu_r = 600$
- Required flux density, $B = 1.5 \text{ T}$
- Vacuum permeability, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T·m/A}$

**Step 1: Find magnetic field intensity H**

$$H = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0 \mu_r} = \dfrac{1.5}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 600}$$

**Step 2: Compute denominator**

$$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 600 = 7.5398 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3: Calculate H**

$$H = \dfrac{1.5}{7.5398 \times 10^{-4}} = 1989.4 \text{ A/m}$$

**Step 4: Determine effective radius**

For a Head Shot through the plate thickness, the effective distance from current axis to mid-thickness is:

$$r = \dfrac{t}{2} = \dfrac{0.020}{2} = 0.010 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Apply Ampère's Law to find current**

$$I = 2 \pi r H$$

$$I = 2 \times \pi \times 0.010 \times 1989.4$$

$$I = 125.0 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{required} \approx 125 \text{ A}}$$

**Conclusion:** A magnetizing current of approximately **125 A** is required to achieve a flux density of 1.5 T at the mid-thickness of the plate.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper - Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the **Central Conductor magnetization technique**. Mention **two typical industrial applications** and explain why the field inside the tube wall is **non-uniform**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Cold Shot technique** in detail. Why is this technique **unsuitable for low-carbon steels**? Justify your answer with reference to the **hysteresis loop** of the material. **[7 Marks]**

### MODEL ANSWER (Question B)

#### Part (a) — Central Conductor Technique

**Diagram Description:** A copper bar is inserted through the bore of a hollow tubular component. The bar is connected at both ends to a high-amperage current source via copper cables. Current flows axially through the bar, generating a concentric magnetic field around it. This field crosses through the tube wall, magnetizing it. When an axial crack is present, leakage flux escapes and attracts particles.

**Two Industrial Applications:**

1. **Inspection of oil and gas pipelines** for longitudinal weld defects and axial cracks.
2. **Inspection of boiler tubes and heat exchanger tubes** in power plants and refineries for service-induced cracking.

**Reason for Non-Uniform Field:**

The magnetic field intensity at any point in the tube wall depends on the radial distance $r$ from the central conductor, as given by:

$$H(r) = \dfrac{I}{2 \pi r}$$

Since $r$ varies across the tube wall (from $r_i$ at inner wall to $r_o$ at outer wall), $H$ is **highest at the inner wall** and **lowest at the outer wall**. This variation makes the inner surface more easily inspected than the outer surface for a given current. To compensate, the operator often rotates the tube or performs multiple passes to ensure uniform coverage.

#### Part (b) — Cold Shot Technique and Material Suitability

**Cold Shot Technique Explanation:**

In the Cold Shot technique, the component is first magnetized to saturation using any standard method (Head Shot, Central Conductor, or Coil). **The magnetizing current is then switched OFF** before the magnetic particles are applied. The particles are attracted to leakage fields formed by defects using the **residual magnetism (Remanent Flux Density, Br)** retained by the part after the external field is removed.

**Why Cold Shot is Unsuitable for Low-Carbon Steels — Justification via Hysteresis Loop:**

Low-carbon steels are classified as **soft magnetic materials**. Their hysteresis loop is characterized by:

- **Low Coercivity (Hc):** Typically less than 100 A/m. The domains realign easily with minimal reverse field.
- **Low Retentivity (Br):** Typically less than 0.4 T. After the magnetizing force is removed, the residual flux density is too weak to produce adequate leakage flux at defect sites.

The hysteresis loop of soft steel is **narrow** (small area enclosed), meaning little energy is stored and most of the magnetism is lost immediately after the field is removed. Consequently, the leakage flux generated at defects is below the **minimum threshold (~800 A/m)** required to attract and hold magnetic particles, resulting in **no visible indication**.

In contrast, **hard steels** (high-carbon, alloy steels) have a **wide hysteresis loop** with high $B_r$ (>0.8 T) and high $H_c$ (>1000 A/m). After magnetization, they retain sufficient residual flux to attract particles effectively, making Cold Shot viable.

**Conclusion:** Cold Shot requires materials with **high retentivity and high coercivity** (hard magnetic materials). Soft low-carbon steels, with their narrow hysteresis loops and low $B_r$, fail to retain adequate residual magnetism for indication formation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing H (A/m) with B (Tesla):** Many students interchange these in numerical problems. Remember: $H$ is the *cause* (magnetizing field), $B$ is the *effect* (flux density). Use the formula $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ correctly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Right-Hand Thumb Rule:** When asked about field direction, students often guess. Always state the rule explicitly.
> 3. **Skipping Units:** Numerical answers without units lose 0.5–1 mark. Always write **A/m, Tesla, mm, kg** etc.
> 4. **Mixing up Cold Shot and Hot Shot:** Cold Shot = particles applied AFTER current is cut off. Hot Shot (or Continuous Method) = particles applied WHILE current is on.
> 5. **Ignoring Demagnetization:** Examiners often award a mark for mentioning post-inspection demagnetization, especially for Cold Shot-applied parts.
> 6. **Not mentioning Retentivity:** When explaining Cold Shot, you MUST mention $B_r$ (residual flux density) and the hysteresis loop. Vague answers like "magnetism remains" are penalized.

---
