## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU Module 2 — Magnetization Techniques**

- ✅ **Head Shot:** Current through part via prods → **circular transverse field** → detects cracks perpendicular to current.
- ✅ **Cold Shot:** Magnetize first → **cut current** → apply particles using **residual flux Br** → only for **hard steels**.
- ✅ **Central Conductor:** Bar through tube bore → current in bar → **radial field through wall** → detects **axial cracks** in tubes.
- ✅ **Yoke Magnetization:** Flux between two pole legs → no current through part → **portable, ideal for field welds**.
- ✅ **Right-Hand Thumb Rule** governs field direction in all current-based techniques.
- ✅ **Ampère's Law:** $H = I / (2 \pi r)$ for a straight conductor (foundational formula).
- ✅ **ASTM E709 Lifting Power:** Minimum **4.5 kg (10 lbs)** at **100 mm pole spacing** for yoke acceptance.
- ✅ **Demagnetization required** after MPT — residual field < **3 mT (30 Gauss)** is typical acceptance.
- ✅ **Two perpendicular magnetization directions** needed to detect defects in **all orientations**.
- ✅ **AC** magnetization = surface defects; **DC** magnetization = sub-surface defects (penetration depth).
- ✅ **Permanent Yoke** = always available, no power needed, surface only.
- ✅ **Electromagnetic Yoke** = adjustable strength, deeper penetration.
- ✅ **Prods cause burn marks** — limitation of Head Shot on finished components.
- ✅ **Central Conductor limitation:** Non-uniform field (stronger at inner wall than outer wall).
- ✅ **Hysteresis loop:** Wide loop → hard steel → Cold Shot viable. Narrow loop → soft steel → Cold Shot NOT viable.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — Magnetization Techniques**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern and Bloom's Taxonomy (K3 / K4 cognitive levels for application and analysis).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct and Indirect Methods of Magnetization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct and Indirect Methods of Magnetization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Magnetization in NDT

In the context of **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)**, **Magnetization** is the process of inducing a magnetic field within a ferromagnetic test specimen so that surface and sub-surface discontinuities (cracks, laps, inclusions, porosity) disturb the uniform magnetic flux and become visible as flux leakage patterns when fine ferromagnetic particles are applied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Magnetization is the act of establishing a magnetic flux within a test object in a predetermined direction and of sufficient magnitude to produce indications of discontinuities.*

A test piece can be magnetized in essentially two ways:
1. **Direct Method** – Electric current is passed **through the test piece itself** (or through a conductor in contact with it), generating the magnetizing field.
2. **Indirect Method** – No current is passed through the part; instead, the magnetic field is generated by an **external source** (coils carrying current or permanent magnets) and coupled into the test object.

> [!NOTE]
> The classification **Direct vs Indirect** is purely about *where the current flows*, not about the type of magnetic field (longitudinal/circular) produced.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to "feel" the texture of a hidden crack in a metal plate using iron filings.

- **Direct Method Analogy:** You hold the metal plate and pass electricity *through* the plate itself (like lighting a bulb using the plate as a wire). The plate becomes magnetized from within — the current creates its own magnetic field internally. *Think: heating a rod by passing current through it.*

- **Indirect Method Analogy:** You don't touch the plate electrically. Instead, you wrap a *separate* coil around the plate (or hold a bar magnet over it). The coil's own magnetic field "leaks" into the plate and magnetizes it. *Think: warming food in a microwave — the heat comes from outside, not from the food's own current.*

## 1.3 Why Magnetization Direction Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A discontinuity will only be detected if it lies **perpendicular (or nearly so) to the direction of the magnetic flux**. Hence, a test piece is normally magnetized in **two mutually perpendicular directions** to ensure defects of all orientations are caught. This is why both *longitudinal* and *circular* magnetization techniques are essential.

## 1.4 Directional Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Longitudinal Magnetization** | Flux lines run along the **length** of the part (parallel to its axis). Best for detecting **transverse** cracks. |
| **Circular Magnetization** | Flux lines form **concentric circles** around the current-carrying axis. Best for detecting **longitudinal** cracks (parallel to length). |

## 1.5 Categories of Magnetization Methods — Overview

| Class | Sub-Methods |
|-------|-------------|
| **Direct** | Prod method, Head-shot (clamp/cable) method, Central conductor method, Direct contact method |
| **Indirect** | Yoke method (permanent & electromagnetic), Encircling coil method, Parallel/Bar conductors method, Threading bar method |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Methods of Magnetization

In all **direct methods**, current is passed through the test piece (or a conductor in electrical contact with it). The magnetic field is therefore produced **by the current inside the part**.

### 2.1.1 Prod Method (Point Electrode Method)

**Operational Concept:**
- Two flexible prods (copper-tipped contact electrodes) are pressed against the **surface** of the test piece at separated points.
- Heavy current (typically **100 A to 1500 A**, AC or DC) is passed between the prods through the bulk of the material.
- The current flows in a **diverging path** between the prods, producing a **diverging (radial) magnetic field** around the current path.
- Defects lying **perpendicular to the current direction** (and hence perpendicular to the diverging flux) will produce flux leakage.

**Why it works:**
By Ampere's Law, current in the part produces circular flux lines around the current path. Where a crack cuts across these flux lines, leakage occurs at the surface.

**Step-by-step application logic:**
1. Clean the contact area on the part surface.
2. Place prods at appropriate spacing (generally **75 mm to 200 mm** for hand prods, ≥ **200 mm** for heavy prods).
3. Apply magnetizing current (pulse or continuous).
4. Spray magnetic particles while current is flowing (for **wet method**) or sprinkle before current (for **residual**).
5. Inspect under suitable lighting (visible light or UV-A for fluorescent).

**Advantages:**
- Portable, simple, low equipment cost.
- Can be used on **large, irregularly shaped** components in situ (welds, ship hulls, bridges).
- Localized inspection possible.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Burn marks / arcing** at prod contact points — can damage the part.
- Localized heating → may burn thin or coated surfaces.
- Only detects cracks **lying roughly perpendicular to the prod–prod line**.
- Depth of magnetization is **shallow** (skin effect with AC).
- Requires good surface contact (paint, oil, dirt must be removed at contact zones).

> [!WARNING]
> **Prods must NEVER be placed on top of a suspected crack or on a non-conductive coating** — the current will arc and create new discontinuities, giving false indications.

---

### 2.1.2 Head-Shot (Clamp / Cable Shot) Method

**Operational Concept:**
- The ends (heads) of the test piece are clamped between two heavy copper contacts (heads) connected to a power source.
- Current flows **axially through the entire length** of the part.
- This produces a **circular magnetic field** (concentric circles around the axis) within the part.

**Resulting Field Type:** **Circular magnetization** — flux circles the part.

**Best For Detecting:** **Longitudinal defects** (cracks running parallel to the axis, e.g., axial cracks in shafts, boiler tubes, welds running lengthwise).

**Why circular flux works on longitudinal cracks:**
The longitudinal crack intersects the circular flux lines, forcing flux to leak out → particle accumulation → indication.

**Practical details:**
- Requires **end access** to the part (both ends must be free for clamping).
- Large current required: roughly **1000 A per inch of diameter** (rule of thumb for DC/AC).
- Head-shot current: I ≈ **20,000 to 45,000 ampere-turns** typically.
- Often used in bench units for production-line testing of shafts, bolts, bars.

**Advantages:**
- Excellent for full-length circular magnetization in one operation.
- Uniform flux density along the part (if cross-section is uniform).
- Better control of current → repeatable indications.

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires clean, flat, conductive end surfaces for head contact.
- **Not suitable for parts with fixed flanges or closed ends** (no clamping access).
- Heavy current requirement → need large power pack.

---

### 2.1.3 Central Conductor Method

**Operational Concept:**
- A heavy copper bar (central conductor) is passed through the **bore** of a hollow cylindrical part (tube, pipe, ring, bushing, valve body).
- Current is passed through this central conductor.
- The magnetic field produced by the conductor is **circular** and surrounds the conductor — i.e., it passes **radially through the wall** of the hollow part.

**Resulting Field Type:** **Circular magnetization** of the **hollow component's wall** (perpendicular to axis).

**Best For Detecting:** **Longitudinal (axial) cracks** in tube walls, bore surfaces, and ring-shaped parts.

**Typical Applications:**
- Tubular welds (long-seam pipes).
- Valve castings.
- Ring forgings.
- Gun barrel bores.

**Advantages:**
- No electrical contact required on the part itself → no burn marks.
- Effective for inspecting internal bores and hollow components.
- Works for parts with no accessible ends.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Conductor sizing** must be matched to part bore — too small a conductor reduces field strength.
- Inspection is limited to the **axial (longitudinal) direction** of the part — transverse defects require an additional method.
- Cannot be used on solid parts.

---

### 2.1.4 Direct Contact Method (Direct Lead)

- Bare copper leads are connected directly to the part using bolted or clamped contacts.
- Effectively a head-shot variant, often used for **odd-shaped or large parts** when the head-shot jig is impractical.

---

## 2.2 Indirect Methods of Magnetization

In **indirect methods**, no current flows through the test piece. The magnetic field is produced by a **separate current-carrying coil or a permanent magnet**, and this field is induced into the part.

### 2.2.1 Yoke Method (Permanent Magnet Yoke / Electromagnetic Yoke)

**Operational Concept:**
- A **horseshoe-shaped (U-shape) yoke** is used.
- The poles of the yoke are placed on the surface of the part, completing a magnetic circuit **through the part**.
- In a **permanent magnet yoke**, the field is provided by hard-magnetic material (Alnico or NdFeB).
- In an **electromagnetic yoke** (AC or DC), a coil wound on the yoke core is energized.

**Resulting Field Type:** **Longitudinal** — flux enters the part at one pole, travels through the part, and exits at the other pole.

**Best For Detecting:** **Transverse cracks** (perpendicular to the line joining the two pole pieces).

**Key Specifications (KTU expected):**
- **Pole spacing:** typically **50 mm to 300 mm**.
- **Lifting power:** For AC yokes, **≥ 4.5 kg (10 lb)** at minimum pole spacing (ASTM E709).
- **DC yokes** are used when subsurface defects (5–6 mm deep) need to be revealed.

**Advantages:**
- **Truly portable** — ideal for field inspection.
- No electrical contact on the part → no risk of arcing or burns.
- Can inspect coated/painted surfaces (coating up to ~0.5 mm with proper technique).
- Permanent magnet yokes need **no power source**.

**Disadvantages:**
- Limited depth of magnetization (shallow with AC).
- Only suitable for **local inspection** between the poles.
- Permanent magnet yokes have fixed strength — may not be sufficient for heavy sections.
- Pole contact must be clean and flat.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The **lifting-power test** is the standard acceptance criterion for an AC yoke. The yoke should lift a 4.5 kg (10 lb) steel block. Failure of this test means the yoke is unfit for service.

---

### 2.2.2 Encircling Coil Method (Solenoid Method)

**Operational Concept:**
- The test piece is placed **inside** a multi-turn coil (solenoid) carrying current.
- The coil's magnetic field runs **along the axis of the coil** — and hence along the **length of the part**.
- Field is essentially **longitudinal** within the part.

**Resulting Field Type:** **Longitudinal magnetization**.

**Best For Detecting:** **Transverse cracks** (circumferential defects) in shafts, bars, billets.

**Key Formula:**

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}$$

Where:
- **H** = magnetizing field strength (A/m)
- **N** = number of turns in the coil
- **I** = current through coil (A)
- **L** = effective magnetic path length of part (m)

For full magnetization, field strength should be:

$$H \ge 8000 \, \text{A/m (for DC)} \quad \text{or} \quad H \ge 2400 \, \text{A/m (for AC)}$$

(These are **rule-of-thumb** values from ASTM E709; actual values depend on material and geometry.)

**Magnetomotive Force (MMF) requirement:**

$$\text{MMF} = N \cdot I = H \cdot L$$

**Advantages:**
- **Uniform longitudinal magnetization** along the part.
- No electrical contact on the part.
- Excellent for long, slender parts.
- Easy to integrate in production lines (pass-through design).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Length-limited**: If the part length exceeds the coil length, the central region is well magnetized but ends may be weak.
- For long parts, a **sliding coil** or **multiple coils** is needed.
- For very short parts (L/D < 2), demagnetization effects at the ends reduce effective H.

---

### 2.2.3 Parallel / Bar Conductors Method

**Operational Concept:**
- Two or more current-carrying conductors are placed **parallel** to the test piece (or above/below the part).
- Each conductor's field is superimposed on the part.
- Used for inspecting **plate, sheet, or large flat surfaces** where prod placement is impractical.

**Best For Detecting:** Cracks running perpendicular to the conductors' field direction.

---

### 2.2.4 Threading Bar Method

- A current-carrying bar is passed through the bore of a hollow part (similar to central conductor), but here the bar is **insulated from the part** (no electrical contact).
- Effectively an **indirect** version of the central conductor method.
- The magnetic field couples into the part wall without current flowing through it.

---

## 2.3 Comparison of Methods — KTU High-Yield Table

| Method | Direct/Indirect | Field Type | Best For (Defect Direction) | Typical Application |
|--------|----------------|------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Prod | Direct | Diverging circular | Cracks perpendicular to prod line | Welds, large structures |
| Head-shot | Direct | Circular (along length) | Longitudinal / axial cracks | Shafts, bars, bolts |
| Central conductor | Direct | Circular (in walls) | Longitudinal cracks in bores | Pipes, tubes, rings |
| Yoke (AC/DC/PM) | Indirect | Longitudinal | Transverse cracks | Field inspection, large parts |
| Encircling coil | Indirect | Longitudinal | Circumferential / transverse cracks | Bars, shafts, billets |
| Parallel conductors | Indirect | Combined | Plate welds | Plates, sheet metal |
| Threading bar | Indirect | Circular | Longitudinal in bores | Hollow shafts, tubes |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| Magnetic field strength (straight current) | $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$ | Field at distance r from current-carrying wire | A/m |
| MMF (solenoid) | $\text{MMF} = N \cdot I$ | Magnetomotive force of a coil | At (ampere-turns) |
| Magnetizing field in coil | $H = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{L}$ | Field inside a long solenoid / part in coil | A/m |
| Required field (DC, steel) | $H \ge 8000$ | Minimum DC field for saturation (ASTM rule) | A/m |
| Required field (AC, steel) | $H \ge 2400$ | Minimum AC field (ASTM rule) | A/m |
| Head-shot current rule | $I \approx 1000 \text{ A per inch of diameter}$ | For solid round bars | A |
| Prod spacing | $S \ge 75$ (hand) ; $S \ge 200$ (heavy) | Minimum prod-to-prod spacing | mm |
| Demagnetization field (AC coil) | $H_d \approx 0.8 \cdot H_m$ | For residual-free demagnetization | A/m |
| Pole lifting power (AC yoke) | $\ge 4.5$ | ASTM minimum lifting test | kg (10 lb) |
| Relative permeability | $\mu_r = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0 H}$ | Dimensionless ratio | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Units:** Always use SI units in derivations. Magnetizing force H has SI unit **A/m** (ampere per metre). Magnetic flux density B has unit **T (Tesla)**. Use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace industry** uses encircling coils and yokes to inspect turbine shafts and landing gear components.
- **Petrochemical/pipeline industry** uses prods and central conductors for in-service weld inspection.
- **Power generation** uses head-shot jigs for bolt and bar inspection.
- **Marine and shipbuilding** uses portable AC yokes for hull-plate crack detection in dry dock.
- **Railway** uses yokes for wheel and axle inspection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field Produced by a Current-Carrying Wire (Basis of Direct Methods)

The foundation of the prod method and head-shot method is the magnetic field around a long straight current-carrying conductor.

**Statement (Biot–Savart Law):**
For an infinitely long straight wire carrying current $I$, the magnetic flux density at a perpendicular distance $r$ from the wire is:

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \, I}{2\pi r}$$

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1 — Apply Ampere's Circuital Law:**
Ampere's law states that the line integral of magnetic field intensity around any closed path equals the total enclosed current:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enclosed}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a Symmetric Amperian Loop:**
Take a circular Amperian loop of radius $r$ centered on the wire, lying in a plane perpendicular to the wire. By symmetry, $\vec{H}$ is everywhere tangent to this loop and has constant magnitude $H$ at radius $r$.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Line Integral:**
The loop has circumference $2\pi r$, so:

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = H \cdot 2\pi r$$

**Step 4 — Equate to Enclosed Current:**
The enclosed current is just $I$ (the current in the wire):

$$H \cdot 2\pi r = I$$

**Step 5 — Solve for H:**

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Flux Density B using $B = \mu_0 H$ for free space (or $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ inside a magnetic material):**

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 \, I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:**
For a defect at depth $r$ below the surface, the field strength is inversely proportional to $r$. Hence **deeper defects require higher current** to be detected. This is the *theoretical basis* for current selection in prod magnetization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Current for Head-Shot Magnetization

We want to magnetize a cylindrical steel bar of diameter $D$ and length $L$ such that the field at the surface of the bar reaches a minimum of $H_{min} = 8000$ A/m (DC, per ASTM).

**Step 1 — Position of Interest:**
The bar's surface is at distance $r = D/2$ from the axis (where the current flows along the bar's axis).

**Step 2 — Apply Field Formula:**

$$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r} = \frac{I}{2\pi (D/2)} = \frac{I}{\pi D}$$

**Step 3 — Set $H = H_{min}$:**

$$\frac{I}{\pi D} = H_{min}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Current I:**

$$I = \pi D \cdot H_{min}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $H_{min} = 8000$ A/m:**

$$I = \pi D \cdot 8000$$

**Step 6 — Convert D from mm to m (or inch to m, depending on unit system).**
For D in **mm**:
$D_{m} = D_{mm} \times 10^{-3}$

So:

$$I = 8000 \cdot \pi \cdot D_{mm} \times 10^{-3} = 8\pi D_{mm} \approx 25.13 \cdot D_{mm}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Example:** For a 50 mm diameter bar:

$$I = 25.13 \times 50 = 1256.6 \, \text{A (DC)}$$

**Step 8 — AC Case (skin effect):** AC is concentrated near the surface; minimum AC field is $H_{min,AC} = 2400$ A/m:

$$I_{AC} = 2400 \cdot \pi \cdot D_{m} = 7.54 \cdot D_{mm}$$

For 50 mm bar: $I_{AC} \approx 377$ A.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Encircling Coil Field Strength

For a long part of length $L$ placed inside a coil of $N$ turns carrying current $I$:

**Step 1 — MMF of the Coil:**

$$\text{MMF} = N \cdot I \quad [\text{ampere-turns (At)}]$$

**Step 2 — Apply MMF = H × L (analogous to EMF = E × L for an electric circuit):**

$$H \cdot L = N \cdot I$$

**Step 3 — Solve for H:**

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}$$

**Step 4 — For Required H, Solve for N · I:**

$$N \cdot I = H \cdot L$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (DC magnetization, ASTM minimum):**
Part length $L = 0.5$ m, required $H = 8000$ A/m, choose $I = 1000$ A:

$$N = \frac{H \cdot L}{I} = \frac{8000 \times 0.5}{1000} = 4 \, \text{turns}$$

This is the **minimum turn count** for full DC magnetization.

**Step 6 — For AC case (H = 2400 A/m, same part and current):**

$$N = \frac{2400 \times 0.5}{1000} = 1.2 \rightarrow 2 \, \text{turns (round up)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **AC magnetization needs far fewer ampere-turns** than DC — AC only magnetizes the near-surface region.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Logic

| Part Characteristic | Recommended Method | Why |
|---------------------|--------------------|-----|
| Long solid shaft, accessible ends | Head-shot | Axial current → circular flux, fast full-length magnetization |
| Hollow pipe/tube, accessible bores | Central conductor | Current through internal bar → flux in walls |
| Field inspection, large welded structure | AC Yoke | Portable, no arcing, longitudinal field for transverse cracks |
| Small part on production line, mass testing | Encircling coil (bench unit) | Uniform longitudinal field, repeatable |
| Local surface crack on irregular surface | Prod method | Localized, high current, no need for full demagnetization |
| Painted/Coated surface | Electromagnetic yoke (AC) | AC can detect through ~0.5 mm coating |
| Part with neither ends nor bore | Yoke or prod | Only these work without contact access |
| Subsurface defect (5 mm deep) | DC magnetization (yoke, coil, or prods) | DC penetrates; AC is skin-effect limited |

## 3.5 Quantitative Comparison of Magnetizing Fields

| Method | Field Type | Typical H (A/m) | Penetration |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|-------------|
| AC Yoke | Longitudinal | 2400–4000 | Surface only (≤ 2 mm) |
| DC Yoke | Longitudinal | 8000–12000 | Subsurface (≤ 6 mm) |
| AC Prod | Diverging circular | 2400–4000 | Surface only |
| DC Prod | Diverging circular | 8000–12000 | Subsurface |
| Encircling coil (AC) | Longitudinal | 2400–4000 | Surface only |
| Encircling coil (DC) | Longitudinal | 8000–12000 | Subsurface |
| Head-shot (AC) | Circular | 2400–4000 | Surface only |
| Head-shot (DC) | Circular | 8000–12000 | Subsurface |
| Central conductor (AC) | Circular | 2400–4000 | Surface only |
| Central conductor (DC) | Circular | 8000–12000 | Subsurface |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Magnetization Methods

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Magnetization Methods in MPT"] --> B["Direct Methods"]
    A --> C["Indirect Methods"]
    B --> B1["Prod Method"]
    B --> B2["Head-Shot Method"]
    B --> B3["Central Conductor Method"]
    B --> B4["Direct Contact Method"]
    C --> C1["Yoke Method"]
    C --> C2["Encircling Coil Method"]
    C --> C3["Parallel Conductors Method"]
    C --> C4["Threading Bar Method"]
    C1 --> C1A["Permanent Magnet Yoke"]
    C1 --> C1B["Electromagnetic Yoke AC"]
    C1 --> C1C["Electromagnetic Yoke DC"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Process Flow of a Typical MPT Inspection

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Step 1: Surface Preparation"] --> S2["Step 2: Apply Magnetizing Current"]
    S2 --> S3{"Current Type?"}
    S3 -- AC --> S3A["Continuous Method: Apply particles during current flow"]
    S3 -- DC --> S3B["Residual Method: Stop current, then apply particles"]
    S3A --> S4["Step 3: Particle Application Wet or Dry"]
    S3B --> S4
    S4 --> S5["Step 4: Interpret Indications under Light UV-A or Visible"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 5: Demagnetize if Required"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 6: Post-cleaning and Documentation"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Decision Logic for Method Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    P["Part to be Inspected"] --> Q1{"Is part solid or hollow?"}
    Q1 -- Hollow --> Q2{"Is bore accessible?"}
    Q1 -- Solid --> Q3{"Are both ends accessible for clamping?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1["Use Central Conductor Method"]
    Q2 -- No --> R4["Use Yoke or Prod Method"]
    Q3 -- Yes --> R2["Use Head-Shot Method"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is defect orientation expected transverse?"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R3["Use Encircling Coil or Yoke"]
    Q4 -- No --> R4
    R1 --> Z["Record Indication and Demagnetize"]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
```

## 4.4 Schematic Block — Setup of a Head-Shot Magnetization

```mermaid
graph LR
    PSU["Power Supply Unit"] -->|Heavy Current Leads| HEAD1["Clamp Head 1"]
    PSU --> HEAD2["Clamp Head 2"]
    HEAD1 --> PART["Cylindrical Test Part"]
    HEAD2 --> PART
    PART -- "Circular Flux Lines Surrounding Axis" --> FL["Flux Density B at Radius r"]
    FL --> DEF["Flux Leakage at Longitudinal Crack"]
    DEF --> IND["Magnetic Particle Indication"]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Block — Setup of Encircling Coil Magnetization

```mermaid
graph LR
    PSU2["Power Supply Unit"] --> COIL["Multi-Turn Solenoid Coil"]
    COIL -->|MMF = N x I| PART2["Test Part Inside Coil"]
    PART2 -- "Longitudinal Flux Along Part Axis" --> FL2["Uniform H Along Length L"]
    FL2 --> DEF2["Flux Leakage at Transverse Crack"]
    DEF2 --> IND2["Magnetic Particle Indication"]
```

## 4.6 Schematic Block — Prod Method on a Welded Plate

```mermaid
graph LR
    PSU3["Power Source"] --> PRODA["Prod A"]
    PSU3 --> PRODB["Prod B"]
    PRODA -- "Heavy Current Through Plate" --> PLATE["Welded Plate"]
    PRODB -- "Current Diverges Between Prods" --> PLATE
    PLATE -- "Diverging Circular Flux" --> FL3["Radial Field Lines"]
    FL3 --> CRACK["Crack Perpendicular to Prod Line"]
    CRACK --> LEAK3["Flux Leakage"]
    LEAK3 --> IND3["Particle Accumulation Visible as Indication"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between direct and indirect methods of magnetization in magnetic particle testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Method | Indirect Method |
|--------|---------------|-----------------|
| Current path | Current flows **through the test part itself** | Current does **not** flow through the test part |
| Source of magnetic field | Current inside the part produces the field | External coil or magnet produces the field |
| Contact required | Yes — electrical contact on the part | No electrical contact needed |
| Risk of arcing/burns | Yes, possible at contact points | No risk |
| Examples | Prod method, Head-shot, Central conductor | Yoke, Encircling coil, Parallel conductors |
| Typical use | Workshop / bench units | Field inspection, coated parts |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples — full 3 marks awarded.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the principle of the yoke method of magnetization and list its merits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Principle (1 mark):** A horseshoe-shaped yoke is placed on the surface of the test part. The magnetic flux produced by the permanent magnet or energised coil in the yoke enters the part at one pole, travels through it, and exits at the other pole, magnetizing the region between the poles longitudinally.
- **Merits (2 marks, any two):**
  1. Portable — ideal for field inspection of large structures.
  2. No electrical contact → no risk of arcing or burning the surface.
  3. Can be used on **painted or coated** surfaces.
  4. Detects **transverse** cracks effectively.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Module 2 — Magnetization Methods)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

(a) **Explain the different methods of magnetization in magnetic particle testing with neat sketches. (7 marks)**

(b) **A solid steel shaft of 60 mm diameter and 800 mm length is to be magnetized using the head-shot method. Compute the minimum DC and AC magnetizing currents required. Use ASTM E709 minimum field values. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Methods of Magnetization (7 marks)**

The methods of magnetization in MPT are classified as **Direct** and **Indirect** based on whether the magnetizing current passes through the part itself.

**1. Prod Method (Direct) — 1.5 marks**
Two copper-tipped prods are pressed on the part surface and current is passed between them. The current diverges through the part, producing a diverging circular magnetic field between the prods. Cracks perpendicular to the prod-prod line will leak flux. The prods must be at least 75 mm apart for hand prods, 200 mm for heavy prods, to avoid local overheating and burn marks. Best for field inspection of welds, large structures.

**2. Head-Shot Method (Direct) — 1.5 marks**
The two ends of the part are clamped between heavy copper heads. Current flows axially through the part, producing a circular magnetic field concentric with the axis. This induces a longitudinal-current → circular-flux condition, ideal for detecting **longitudinal (axial) cracks**. The current required is about **1000 A per inch (25.13 A per mm) of diameter** for DC magnetization.

**3. Central Conductor Method (Direct) — 1 mark**
A heavy copper conductor is passed through the bore of a hollow part. Current in the conductor creates a circular field around the conductor, which threads through the wall of the hollow part radially. Detects axial cracks in pipes, tubes, and ring forgings.

**4. Yoke Method (Indirect) — 1.5 marks**
A horseshoe yoke is placed on the part. Flux enters at one pole, travels through the part, and exits at the other, producing **longitudinal magnetization** between the poles. The AC yoke must lift a 4.5 kg weight (lifting-power test) for acceptance. Best for field inspection of transverse cracks.

**5. Encircling Coil Method (Indirect) — 1.5 marks**
The part is placed inside a solenoid coil. The coil current creates a longitudinal field along the part axis. Field strength is $H = N \cdot I / L$. Best for detecting circumferential (transverse) cracks in bars and shafts.

*(Sketch credit: 1 mark for at least two correct labelled diagrams.)*

---

**(b) Numerical — Minimum DC and AC Currents (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m
- Length $L = 800$ mm $= 0.8$ m
- ASTM E709 minimum: $H_{min,DC} = 8000$ A/m, $H_{min,AC} = 2400$ A/m

**Step 1 — Field at the bar surface (head-shot, current along axis):**

$$H = \frac{I}{\pi D}$$

where $r = D/2$ gives $H = I / (2\pi r) = I / (\pi D)$.

**Step 2 — DC Current:**

$$I_{DC} = H_{min,DC} \cdot \pi \cdot D = 8000 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.060$$

$$I_{DC} = 8000 \times 3.1416 \times 0.060$$

$$I_{DC} = 4800 \times 0.1885$$

$$I_{DC} = 1507.96 \, \text{A} \approx \mathbf{1508 \, A}$$

**Step 3 — AC Current:**

$$I_{AC} = H_{min,AC} \cdot \pi \cdot D = 2400 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.060$$

$$I_{AC} = 2400 \times 3.1416 \times 0.060$$

$$I_{AC} = 1440 \times 0.1885$$

$$I_{AC} = 271.43 \, \text{A} \approx \mathbf{271 \, A}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of L/D ratio:**
$L/D = 800/60 \approx 13.3$. Since $L/D \gg 2$, end effects are negligible and the formula is valid.

**Final Answer:**
- **DC Magnetizing Current = 1508 A (approx. 1.5 kA)**
- **AC Magnetizing Current = 271 A (approx. 0.27 kA)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Module 2 — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

(a) **Compare the direct and indirect methods of magnetization. Discuss the relative advantages, limitations, and typical applications of each. (7 marks)**

(b) **A 1.0 m long steel bar of 40 mm diameter is to be magnetized by an encircling coil of 200 turns. Calculate the coil current required for:**
  **(i) DC magnetization at ASTM E709 minimum field.**
  **(ii) AC magnetization at ASTM E709 minimum field.**
  **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Comparison: Direct vs Indirect Methods (7 marks)**

**Definition contrast (2 marks):**
- **Direct methods** pass the magnetizing current through the test piece itself, using it as part of the electric circuit.
- **Indirect methods** generate the magnetizing field in a separate current-carrying element (coil, bar, or magnet) and couple it into the test piece without any current flowing through it.

**Direct Methods — Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Localized high field is easily achieved (e.g., prods).
2. The internal current automatically produces flux parallel to the part's current path, eliminating geometric matching concerns.
3. Suitable for very large parts in field.

**Direct Methods — Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Risk of arc burns at contact points.
2. Cannot be used on painted or coated surfaces at contact points.
3. Requires good electrical contact and clean surfaces.

**Indirect Methods — Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. No electrical contact — no arcing, no surface damage.
2. Excellent for parts with no accessible ends.
3. Can be used on coated or painted parts.
4. Permanent-magnet yokes need no power source — truly portable.

**Indirect Methods — Limitations (1 mark):**
1. For long parts, end regions may be under-magnetized.
2. Equipment may be bulkier (coil) or limited in strength (PM yoke).

---

**(b) Numerical — Encircling Coil Current (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Length $L = 1.0$ m
- Diameter $D = 40$ mm (not directly needed for coil, but useful to know the part is solid)
- Number of turns $N = 200$
- ASTM E709 minimum: $H_{min,DC} = 8000$ A/m, $H_{min,AC} = 2400$ A/m

**Step 1 — Field inside the coil:**

$$H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for current:**

$$I = \frac{H \cdot L}{N}$$

**Step 3 — DC Current:**

$$I_{DC} = \frac{8000 \times 1.0}{200} = \frac{8000}{200} = 40 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — AC Current:**

$$I_{AC} = \frac{2400 \times 1.0}{200} = \frac{2400}{200} = 12 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — L/D check:**
$L/D = 1000/40 = 25$. Since $L/D \gg 2$, the simple formula $H = NI/L$ is accurate and end demagnetization is negligible.

**Step 6 — Engineering Note:**
A 12 A AC magnetizing current is conveniently produced by a small bench unit, illustrating why AC is widely used for surface defects. The DC case (40 A) is also practical, making encircling coil a versatile technique.

**Final Answer:**
- **DC Coil Current = 40 A**
- **AC Coil Current = 12 A**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in magnetization method questions — and how to avoid losing marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "circular magnetization" with "longitudinal magnetization":** A circular magnetic field is produced *around* a current path; longitudinal field is produced *along the axis* of a coil. Many students write "circular = around the coil" which is wrong — circular means flux circles the current path (which is along the part's axis in head-shot).
> 2. **Wrong unit for H:** Using **A** instead of **A/m** for magnetizing force. KTU evaluators deduct ½ mark for missing unit.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in the formula $I = \pi D H_{min}$. If D is in mm and you substitute directly, you get a current 1000 times too large.
> 4. **Applying the head-shot rule "1000 A per inch" blindly** without stating the assumptions (round bar, DC, ASTM field). Examiners want to see the **derivation** from $H = I / (\pi D)$ first.
> 5. **Not specifying AC or DC** in the answer. KTU expects this distinction because the field threshold values differ.
> 6. **Mixing up the prod method and the head-shot method:** Prods are for *diverging* current between two surface contacts; head-shot is for *axial* current through the whole part.
> 7. **Forgetting the lifting-power criterion** for yokes (≥ 4.5 kg for AC yoke as per ASTM E709) — this is a frequent KTU board exam question.
> 8. **Missing the 75 mm / 200 mm prod spacing rule** — examiners love testing this.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Direct and Indirect Methods of Magnetization**
>
> ✅ Magnetization is the act of inducing a magnetic field in a ferromagnetic part to make discontinuities visible.
>
> ✅ **Direct methods** = current flows through the part (Prod, Head-shot, Central Conductor, Direct Contact).
>
> ✅ **Indirect methods** = current does NOT flow through the part (Yoke, Encircling Coil, Parallel Conductors, Threading Bar).
>
> ✅ **Prod Method:** Localized inspection; **75 mm min spacing** (hand), **200 mm min** (heavy); risk of arc burns.
>
> ✅ **Head-Shot Method:** Clamp both ends; current axial → circular field around axis; detects **longitudinal** cracks.
>
> ✅ **Central Conductor:** Conductor passed through bore of hollow part; flux in walls; detects **longitudinal** cracks in pipes, tubes, rings.
>
> ✅ **Yoke Method:** Horseshoe U-shape; flux enters at one pole, exits at other → longitudinal field; detects **transverse** cracks. **AC yoke lifting power ≥ 4.5 kg.**
>
> ✅ **Encircling Coil Method:** Part inside solenoid; field $H = N I / L$; detects **transverse/circumferential** cracks.
>
> ✅ **Rule-of-thumb fields (ASTM E709):** $H_{min,DC} = 8000$ A/m, $H_{min,AC} = 2400$ A/m.
>
> ✅ **Head-shot current formula:** $I = \pi D H_{min}$; for 50 mm dia bar, $I_{DC} \approx 1257$ A.
>
> ✅ **Encircling coil current formula:** $I = H L / N$; for 1 m bar, 200 turns, $I_{DC} = 40$ A, $I_{AC} = 12$ A.
>
> ✅ **Biot–Savart / Ampere's Law result:** $H = I / (2\pi r)$ — basis of all direct methods.
>
> ✅ **AC magnetizes only the surface (skin effect); DC penetrates deeper** — choose DC for subsurface defects.
>
> ✅ **L/D ratio must be ≥ 2** for encircling coil formula validity; otherwise end demagnetization reduces field.
>
> ✅ **Crack must be perpendicular to flux** to be detected — hence two perpendicular magnetization directions are needed for full coverage.
>
> ✅ **Units to remember:** H → A/m; B → Tesla; MMF → ampere-turns (At); $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous and Residual Techniques of MPI
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI) – Magnetization Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** is a non-destructive testing method that uses magnetic fields and ferrous particles to detect surface and near-surface discontinuities in ferromagnetic materials. The two operational philosophies of magnetization are the **Continuous Technique** and the **Residual Technique**.

The entire science of MPI rests on the principle that when a ferromagnetic component is magnetized, any surface-breaking or near-surface defect **distorts the uniform magnetic flux lines**, causing them to leap across the air gap at the defect (forming a **leakage field** or **fringe field**). Ferrous particles — dry powder or suspended in a liquid carrier — are attracted to and held by this leakage field, creating a visible indication of the defect.

The two techniques differ in **one critical question: WHEN are the magnetic particles applied relative to the magnetizing current?**

| Technique | Magnetizing Current State During Particle Application |
|---|---|
| **Continuous Technique** | **ON** (current is flowing) |
| **Residual Technique** | **OFF** (current has been switched off) |

---

## 1.2 Continuous Technique of MPI

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**
> The **Continuous Technique** is one in which the inspection medium (dry powder or wet suspension of ferromagnetic particles) is applied to the surface of the test object **while the magnetizing current is still flowing** through the part. The particles are attracted to the leakage field generated by the active magnetic flux in real time.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **glowing wire carrying current** — you can see the wire only while current flows. Similarly, in the continuous technique, the magnetic "grip" on the particles exists **only as long as the current is ON**. The instant the current is switched off, the leakage field collapses and any loosely held particles fall off. Hence, the inspector must observe the indications **during the active magnetization phase**.

### Key Characteristics
- Particles applied **simultaneously** with magnetization
- Suitable for materials with **low retentivity** (e.g., soft magnetic materials)
- Detects **surface-breaking defects** with high sensitivity
- Also detects **slightly sub-surface defects** (shallow depth)
- Magnetization must be maintained throughout the application
- Used commonly with **prod contacts, encircling coils, and yokes**

---

## 1.3 Residual Technique of MPI

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition**
> The **Residual Technique** is one in which the part is first magnetized to near saturation, the magnetizing current is **switched OFF**, and **only then** are the magnetic particles applied. The leakage field required to attract the particles is produced by the **residual magnetism** retained in the part after the magnetizing force is removed.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **permanent magnet**. Once you stop applying current to the solenoid winding, the steel core remains magnetized — it is "remembering" the previous field. This memory is the **residual flux density (Br)**. The defect acts as a tiny gap in this frozen magnetic circuit, and leakage flux escapes at that gap, attracting particles.

### Key Characteristics
- Particles applied **after** current is removed
- Requires materials with **high retentivity** (e.g., hard magnetic steels, alloy steels)
- Excellent for detecting **slightly sub-surface defects**
- The part is **demagnetized** after inspection
- The technique is more sensitive to **small, deeply hidden flaws** than the continuous method
- Used widely in production-line testing of components like **castings, forgings, weldments**

---

## 1.4 At a Glance – Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Continuous Technique | Residual Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnetization current state** | ON during particle application | OFF before particle application |
| **Relies on** | Active magnetic field (H) | Residual flux density (Br) |
| **Material requirement** | All ferromagnetic materials | High-retentivity materials only |
| **Defect sensitivity** | Excellent for surface defects | Excellent for sub-surface defects |
| **Observation timing** | During energization | After de-energization |
| **Demagnetization** | Usually not required | Always required |
| **Indications stability** | Indications may fade quickly | Indications are stable and long-lasting |
| **Equipment** | Prod, yoke, coil (with current on) | Coil, central conductor (current off) |
| **Sensitivity level** | High | Can be higher for sub-surface flaws |

---

## 1.5 The Hysteresis Loop – Foundation of MPI Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hysteresis Loop Definition**
> A closed curve showing the relationship between magnetic flux density (**B**) and magnetizing field strength (**H**) in a ferromagnetic material as it is taken through a complete cycle of magnetization and demagnetization.

The four critical points on the hysteresis loop are:
1. **Saturation Flux Density (Bs)** – Maximum B achievable
2. **Remanence / Retentivity (Br)** – Residual B when H = 0 (drives residual technique)
3. **Coercivity (Hc)** – Reverse H required to reduce B to 0
4. **Initial / Maximum Permeability (μ)** – Slope of B–H curve

The **residual technique works only when Br is high enough** to produce a usable leakage field at the defect — this is why low-retentivity materials (like pure annealed iron) cannot be reliably tested by the residual method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of the Continuous Technique

When a magnetizing current is passed through a coil surrounding the part, or through prods clamped onto the part, a **magnetomotive force (MMF)** is generated:

$$
\mathcal{F} = N \cdot I \quad \text{[ampere-turns, At]}
$$

This MMF establishes a magnetizing field H inside the material:

$$
H = \frac{N \cdot I}{L} \quad \text{[A/m]}
$$

where **N** = number of turns, **I** = current in amperes, **L** = mean magnetic path length in meters.

The resulting flux density in the material is:

$$
B = \mu \cdot H = \mu_0 \mu_r H \quad \text{[Tesla, T]}
$$

where:
- $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space = **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$**
- $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of the material

### Why the Technique Works (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. The current passing through the coil creates a **strong, uniform magnetic field H** inside the part.
2. The flux lines prefer to travel **through the steel** (low reluctance path).
3. At a defect (crack, void, inclusion), the flux encounters a **high-reluctance air gap** and is forced to **bulge out** of the surface.
4. This leakage field has both **tangential and normal components** at the surface.
5. The **normal component** attracts and holds the ferromagnetic particles against gravity, forming a visible indication.
6. The current remains ON throughout — keeping the leakage field alive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why particles are applied WHILE current flows:**
> Once the current is removed, the field collapses. If particles were not already held by the leakage field, no indication would form. Hence particle application is concurrent with magnetization.

---

## 2.2 The Physics of the Residual Technique

After the current is removed, the material does **not** return to zero flux density — it retains a residual flux density **Br**, which depends on the material's coercivity and previous magnetic history.

The residual flux in the part can be modeled as:

$$
B_r = \mu_0 (H_m - H_c) \quad \text{(approximately, for linear recoil region)}
$$

More precisely, the **recoil line** on the B-H loop (from the tip at maximum H to the Br point) governs the residual field. The relevant property is the **recoil permeability**:

$$
\mu_{recoil} = \frac{\Delta B}{\Delta H} \quad \text{along the recoil line}
$$

### Why the Technique Works (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. The part is magnetized to a value **above the knee of the B-H curve** (near saturation).
2. The current is switched OFF.
3. The operating point moves from the tip of the loop down the **upper recoil line**.
4. When H = 0, the part still possesses a flux density equal to **Br**.
5. At a defect, the flux is forced out of the part just as in the continuous method, but now the **driving force is Br, not H**.
6. The leakage field is now **frozen** in the part — particles can be applied and viewed at leisure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Material Selection Rule:**
> For the residual technique to work reliably, the material must have a **Br that is high enough to produce a leakage field capable of holding particles**. A rule of thumb used in industry is **Br ≥ 0.8 Tesla** for reliable residual MPI.

---

## 2.3 Total Reluctance Concept

The magnetic circuit of the test part is analogous to an electrical circuit. The total reluctance is:

$$
\mathcal{R} = \frac{L}{\mu A} = \frac{L}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} \quad \text{[At/Wb or A/Wb]}
$$

And the flux through the circuit is:

$$
\Phi = \frac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{NI}{\mathcal{R}} \quad \text{[Weber, Wb]}
$$

At a defect, the air gap introduces an additional reluctance:

$$
\mathcal{R}_{gap} = \frac{L_{gap}}{\mu_0 A_{gap}}
$$

The presence of this gap **reduces total flux** in the part at that location, but the **flux lines are forced around the gap** — some escape into the air as the leakage field.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnetomotive Force (MMF) | $\mathcal{F} = N \cdot I$ | Ampere-turns (At) | Drives the magnetic circuit |
| 2 | Magnetizing Field Strength | $H = N I / L$ | A/m | Depends on coil geometry |
| 3 | Flux Density | $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ | Tesla (T) | B in material under H |
| 4 | Permeability of free space | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m | Universal constant |
| 5 | Relative Permeability | $\mu_r = B / (\mu_0 H)$ | Dimensionless | Material property |
| 6 | Total Flux | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Weber (Wb) | Flux through cross-section |
| 7 | Reluctance | $\mathcal{R} = L / (\mu_0 \mu_r A)$ | A/Wb (or At/Wb) | "Resistance" to flux |
| 8 | Ohm's Law (Magnetic) | $\Phi = \mathcal{F} / \mathcal{R}$ | Wb | Magnetic Ohm's law |
| 9 | Recoil Residual Flux | $B_r \approx \mu_0 (H_m - H_c)$ | Tesla | Approx. for linear recoil |
| 10 | Demagnetizing Factor | $H_d = -N_d M$ | A/m | Depends on geometry |
| 11 | Leakage Field Strength | $H_{leak} \approx 2400 \cdot (B_r)$ | A/m (approx) | Empirical industry rule |
| 12 | Lift-off Force on particle | $F \propto \nabla(H^2)$ | N | Particles move to high gradient |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do **not confuse the B-H curve parameters**. **Br** is on the B-axis (residual B when H=0). **Hc** is on the H-axis (reverse H to reduce B to 0). Marks are commonly lost in B-H curve labeling questions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Component Tested | Technique Used | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Turbine disc bores, landing gear | Both | Critical safety parts |
| Automotive | Crankshafts, gears | Continuous | High-throughput lines |
| Heavy Forgings | Large shaft forgings | Residual | Detects sub-surface H flakes |
| Pressure Vessels | Weld seams | Continuous | Surface-breaking cracks |
| Railway | Axles, rails | Residual | Long parts, subsurface fatigue |
| Castings | Pump housings | Residual | Internal inclusions, porosity |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of technique is **not arbitrary** — it is governed by **material magnetic properties, defect type and location, inspection environment, and required sensitivity**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Field Strength Inside an Encircling Coil

Consider a long solenoid of **N turns** wound uniformly over a length **L**, carrying a current **I**.

**Step 1:** The line integral of H around a closed Amperian loop passing through the coil:

$$
\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = N I
$$

**Step 2:** By symmetry, H is uniform inside the solenoid, parallel to the axis:

$$
H \cdot L = N I
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for H:

$$
\boxed{H = \frac{N I}{L} \quad \text{[A/m]}}
$$

**Step 4:** If the part inside the coil is ferromagnetic with relative permeability $\mu_r$, the flux density is:

$$
B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = \mu_0 \mu_r \frac{N I}{L}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reluctance of the Magnetic Circuit

**Step 1:** Define reluctance — it is the magnetic analogue of electrical resistance:

$$
\mathcal{R} = \frac{\text{MMF}}{\text{Flux}} = \frac{\mathcal{F}}{\Phi} = \frac{NI}{BA}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $B = \mu_0 \mu_r H$ and $H = NI / L$:

$$
\mathcal{R} = \frac{NI}{\mu_0 \mu_r H A} = \frac{NI \cdot L}{\mu_0 \mu_r \cdot NI \cdot A}
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{R} = \frac{L}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} \quad \text{[A/Wb]}}
$$

**Step 4:** For a circuit with a defect (air gap) of length $L_g$ in series with steel of length $L_s$:

$$
\mathcal{R}_{total} = \mathcal{R}_s + \mathcal{R}_g = \frac{L_s}{\mu_0 \mu_r A_s} + \frac{L_g}{\mu_0 A_g}
$$

This is the **magnetic circuit equation** that explains why flux escapes at defects — the gap term dominates because $\mu_r \gg 1$ for steel.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Continuous vs Residual

| Inspection Factor | Prefers Continuous | Prefers Residual |
|---|---|---|
| Material is **low-carbon annealed steel** (low Br) | ✓ | ✗ |
| Material is **hardened alloy steel** (high Br) | ✓ | ✓ (better) |
| Defect is **open to surface** | ✓ | ✗ |
| Defect is **subsurface** (0.5–6 mm deep) | ✗ | ✓ |
| Part is **small and complex** | ✓ | ✗ |
| Part is **large forging/casting** | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Batch production line** (speed priority) | ✓ | ✗ |
| **Field inspection** (portable equipment) | ✓ (yokes, prods) | ✗ |
| Indications must be **photographed later** | ✗ | ✓ |
| Component will be **demagnetized** anyway | ✓ | ✓ |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example – Typical KTU Problem

**Problem:** A steel shaft of length 250 mm and diameter 40 mm is placed inside a 500-turn coil. The residual flux density in the shaft after magnetization is **1.2 T**, and the relative permeability during recoil is approximately **15**. Determine:
(a) The recoil field strength inside the shaft.
(b) The total flux through the shaft cross-section.
(c) Whether the residual leakage field at a 0.5 mm deep sub-surface defect is sufficient to attract dry MPI powder (assume a threshold leakage field of 800 A/m).

**Given:**
- L = 250 mm = 0.25 m
- d = 40 mm → A = π/4 × (0.040)² = 1.257 × 10⁻³ m²
- B_r = 1.2 T
- μ_r (recoil) = 15
- μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m

**Part (a) – Recoil field strength H_recoil:**

$$
H = \frac{B_r}{\mu_0 \mu_r} = \frac{1.2}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 15}
$$

$$
H = \frac{1.2}{1.885 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 6.37 \times 10^4 \text{ A/m}
$$

**Part (b) – Total flux:**

$$
\Phi = B_r \cdot A = 1.2 \times 1.257 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
\Phi \approx 1.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 1.51 \text{ mWb}
$$

**Part (c) – Sufficiency of leakage field:**

For a sub-surface defect at depth d below the surface, the leakage field at the surface decays approximately as:

$$
H_{leak} \approx H_{bulk} \cdot e^{-2\pi d / L_{defect}}
$$

Assuming $L_{defect} \approx 5$ mm and d = 0.5 mm:

$$
H_{leak} \approx 6.37 \times 10^4 \times e^{-2\pi(0.5)/5} = 6.37 \times 10^4 \times e^{-0.628}
$$

$$
H_{leak} \approx 6.37 \times 10^4 \times 0.534 \approx 3.40 \times 10^4 \text{ A/m}
$$

Since $H_{leak} = 34{,}000$ A/m $\gg$ 800 A/m (threshold), the **residual field is amply sufficient** to attract the dry powder. The residual technique is viable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The exponential decay shows why very deep sub-surface defects (>6 mm) become hard to detect even by residual MPI — the leakage field at the surface falls below the threshold needed to hold particles.

---

## 3.5 Comparison of Particle Application Stages

| Stage | Continuous Technique | Residual Technique |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Pre-cleaning** | Remove oil, paint, scale | Remove oil, paint, scale |
| **2. Magnetization** | Energize coil / prod | Energize coil / central conductor |
| **3. Particle application** | **WHILE current is ON** | **AFTER current is OFF** |
| **4. Indication formation** | Live (current still flowing) | Static (current removed) |
| **5. Inspection** | Observe immediately | Observe at leisure |
| **6. Demagnetization** | Usually not required | **Always required** |
| **7. Post-cleaning** | Remove particles | Remove particles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **timing of step 3** is the single most important operational difference between the two techniques. Examiners often test this sequencing in KTU questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow – Continuous Technique of MPI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clean the part surface] --> B[Place part in magnetization setup]
    B --> C[Turn ON magnetizing current]
    C --> D[Apply magnetic particles wet or dry]
    D --> E[Particles attracted to leakage fields at defects]
    E --> F[Inspect surface during magnetization]
    F --> G{Defects detected?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Mark and record indications]
    G -- No --> I[Proceed to next area]
    H --> J[Turn OFF current]
    I --> J[Turn OFF current]
    J --> K[Clean residual particles]
    K --> L[End: Report prepared]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow – Residual Technique of MPI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clean the part surface] --> B[Place part in magnetization coil]
    B --> C[Turn ON magnetizing current ramp to saturation]
    C --> D[Magnetize part to near saturation]
    D --> E[Turn OFF magnetizing current]
    E --> F[Remove current while part retains Br]
    F --> G[Apply magnetic particles to demagnetized circuit]
    G --> H[Particles attracted by residual leakage fields]
    H --> I[Inspect surface at leisure]
    I --> J{Defects detected?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Mark record and photograph indications]
    J -- No --> L[Proceed to next inspection]
    K --> M[Demagnetize the part]
    L --> M[Demagnetize the part]
    M --> N[Clean residual particles]
    N --> O[End: Report prepared]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram – Comparison of Operating Philosophies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SETUP[Magnetization Setup]
        COIL[Encircling Coil or Prod or Yoke]
        SRC[Current Source]
    end

    subgraph CT[Continuous Technique]
        CT1[Turn ON current] --> CT2[Apply particles now]
        CT2 --> CT3[Inspect under active field]
    end

    subgraph RT[Residual Technique]
        RT1[Turn ON current] --> RT2[Reach saturation then turn OFF]
        RT2 --> RT3[Apply particles now using Br]
        RT3 --> RT4[Inspect under residual field]
    end

    COIL --> CT
    COIL --> RT
    SRC --> CT1
    SRC --> RT1
```

## 4.4 Hysteresis Loop – Operating Points for Each Technique

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HYS[B-H Hysteresis Loop]
        A[Origin: B=0, H=0] --> B[Magnetize to Hm above knee]
        B --> C1[Continuous: Operate at peak Hm, B near Bs]
        B --> C2[Residual: Reduce H to zero, B drops to Br]
        C2 --> D[Reverse H to -Hc to fully demagnetize]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The continuous technique operates at the **tip of the magnetization curve** (high B, high H). The residual technique operates at the **Br point** (high B, zero H). Both require the material to be driven past the knee of the curve for maximum effect.

## 4.5 Block Architecture – Selection Logic for Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose MPI Technique] --> Q1{Is material ferromagnetic?}
    Q1 -- No --> STOP1[Not applicable use other NDT method]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Where is the defect suspected?}
    Q2 -- Surface --> CT[Use Continuous Technique]
    Q2 -- Subsurface --> Q3{Does material have high Br?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> RT[Use Residual Technique]
    Q3 -- No --> CT2[Use Continuous Technique alternative]
    Q1 --> Q4{Need photo documentation?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> RT
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Is this a high speed production line?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> CT
    Q5 -- No --> RT2[Use Residual Technique]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between the continuous and residual techniques of Magnetic Particle Inspection. Mention two applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Continuous Technique | Residual Technique |
|---|---|---|
| Particle application timing | During magnetization (current ON) | After magnetization (current OFF) |
| Driving field | Active magnetizing field H | Residual flux density Br |
| Material requirement | Any ferromagnetic | High retentivity (Br ≥ 0.8 T) |
| Demagnetization | Usually not required | Always required |
| Indication stability | Temporary (fades after current off) | Permanent (frozen in part) |
| Best for | Surface defects | Sub-surface defects |

**Applications:**
- **Continuous:** Inspection of welded joints, in-service machinery using yokes, aircraft landing gear surfaces.
- **Residual:** Inspection of large steel castings, forged crankshafts, railway axles for sub-surface fatigue cracks.

**[3 marks: 1.5 for difference table + 1.5 for applications]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is demagnetization mandatory after residual MPI but generally not required after continuous MPI?**

**Model Answer:**

In the **residual technique**, the part retains a significant amount of residual magnetism (Br) even after the magnetizing current is removed. This retained field can:
- Attract iron filings and metal chips during service
- Interfere with downstream machining and measurement
- Affect the performance of electronic components
- Cause the part to behave like a weak permanent magnet, attracting debris

In the **continuous technique**, the magnetizing current is turned off after inspection, and since the part was magnetized in the continuous mode, the field collapses immediately. For low-retentivity materials, the residual Br is too small to be problematic, so demagnetization is not strictly required.

**[3 marks: 1.5 for residual technique reasons + 1.5 for continuous technique explanation]**

---

## PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat B-H hysteresis loop the working principle of the **residual technique** of magnetic particle inspection. Discuss the role of **remanence (Br)** and **coercivity (Hc)** in this technique.

**(b) [7 marks]** A ferromagnetic shaft is magnetized to a peak field strength Hm = 40,000 A/m. The material has Br = 1.1 T and Hc = 1200 A/m. Calculate the **recoil residual field strength** inside the shaft after the magnetizing current is switched off, and comment on whether the residual method can be used.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **continuous technique** of magnetic particle inspection in detail, including the equipment used, sequence of operations, and the physical principle that makes it work. Compare its sensitivity for surface and sub-surface defects with the residual technique.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 300 mm long steel bar of 50 mm diameter is placed inside a 400-turn coil carrying 5 A current. The relative permeability of steel is 1500. Calculate the **magnetizing field strength H, flux density B, total flux Φ, and reluctance R** of the magnetic circuit. State whether the continuous or residual technique would be preferred for inspecting this bar.

---

## DETAILED MODEL SOLUTIONS

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) – Working Principle of Residual Technique

**The B-H Hysteresis Loop** (described in words, since Mermaid cannot draw curves):
- The curve starts at origin (0,0).
- It rises steeply as H increases, bends at the **knee**, and saturates at **Bs**.
- As H is reduced to zero, the curve does not return to origin — it drops to **Br** (remanence) on the B-axis.
- To reduce B to zero, H must be reversed to **−Hc** (coercivity) on the H-axis.
- A symmetric mirror curve continues into the third quadrant.
- The closed curve is the **hysteresis loop**.

**Working Principle:**

1. The ferromagnetic part is placed inside an **encircling coil** (or between prods) and the magnetizing current is increased until the material reaches a point **above the knee** of the B-H curve (near saturation).
2. The current is then **switched OFF**.
3. The operating point on the B-H curve moves along the **upper recoil line** from the peak point down to the Br point on the B-axis.
4. Even when H = 0, the part has a flux density equal to **Br** (retained magnetism).
5. At a defect, the high-reluctance air gap forces the residual flux to leak out of the surface.
6. The **leakage field** (composed of Br's lines of force escaping at the defect) attracts the magnetic particles applied **after** the current is removed.
7. Indications are visible, stable, and can be photographed.

**Role of Br:** Higher Br means stronger residual flux → stronger leakage field → better particle attraction → greater sensitivity to small sub-surface defects.

**Role of Hc:** Hc is the reverse field needed to bring B to zero. A high Hc means the material is "magnetically hard" — it strongly retains its magnetism. This is desirable for residual MPI.

**Key requirement:** For the residual technique to be effective, the material must have **Br ≥ 0.8 T** (industry rule of thumb).

**[7 marks: 2 for hysteresis loop description + 3 for working steps + 2 for role of Br and Hc]**

---

#### Part (b) – Calculation

**Given:** Hm = 40,000 A/m, Br = 1.1 T, Hc = 1200 A/m

**Step 1:** Calculate recoil field using the recoil relation:

$$
H_{recoil} = \frac{B_r}{\mu_0 \mu_r}
$$

We first find the **relative permeability at peak**:

$$
B_{peak} = B_r + \mu_0 (H_m - H_c) \quad \text{(approx.)}
$$

$$
B_{peak} = 1.1 + (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) (40000 - 1200)
$$

$$
B_{peak} = 1.1 + (1.2566 \times 10^{-6})(38800) = 1.1 + 0.0488 \approx 1.149 \text{ T}
$$

**Step 2:** Recoil field strength after demagnetization (using approximate recoil slope):

The recoil permeability can be approximated as:

$$
\mu_r^{recoil} = \frac{B_r}{\mu_0 H_m} = \frac{1.1}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(40000)} = \frac{1.1}{5.027 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 21.9
$$

**Step 3:** Recoil H at the Br point when external H = 0:

When the external H is removed, the residual H inside the part is just zero (the demagnetizing field of the geometry brings H to 0). The **retained flux density is Br = 1.1 T**, and the **magnetic field inside the part is approximately zero by definition** at the Br point. The useful quantity is the **leakage field strength** outside the part at a defect:

$$
H_{leak} \approx \frac{B_r}{\mu_0} = \frac{1.1}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \approx 8.75 \times 10^5 \text{ A/m}
$$

**Step 4:** Comment on usability:

The leakage field of $8.75 \times 10^5$ A/m is **vastly greater than the typical threshold of 800 A/m** required to attract dry MPI powder. The material has Br = 1.1 T > 0.8 T (industry minimum). Therefore, **the residual technique is fully applicable and highly sensitive** for this material.

**[7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for calculation, 1.5 for threshold comparison, 1.5 for conclusion]**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) – Continuous Technique

**Equipment Used:**
- **Encircling coil** for cylindrical parts
- **Prod contacts** for localized magnetization of large parts
- **Permanent or electromagnetic yokes** for portable field inspections
- **Current source** (AC, DC, or half-wave rectified DC magnetizer)
- **Dry powder or wet suspension** of ferromagnetic particles
- **Demagnetizer** (optional for continuous technique)
- **UV lamp** (if fluorescent particles are used in wet method)

**Sequence of Operations:**
1. Clean the part thoroughly.
2. Place the part in the magnetization setup.
3. **Turn ON** the magnetizing current.
4. **Apply** the magnetic particles (by dusting for dry method, or by spraying/bathing for wet method) **while the current remains ON**.
5. Observe the formation of indications (dark lines for dry, fluorescent lines for wet under UV).
6. Record and mark the defects.
7. Turn OFF the current.
8. Clean the part to remove residual particles.

**Physical Principle:**
The current passing through the coil creates a magnetizing field H inside the part. The flux density B = μ₀μᵣH is uniform in the absence of defects. At a defect, the flux is forced to bulge out of the surface as a leakage field. The leakage field is present only **as long as the current is ON** — this is why particles must be applied during energization.

**Comparison of Sensitivity:**

| Defect Type | Continuous | Residual |
|---|---|---|
| Surface-breaking | **Excellent** | Good |
| Sub-surface (≤3 mm) | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Sub-surface (3–6 mm) | Very Poor | **Good** |
| Sub-surface (>6 mm) | Not detectable | Usually not detectable |

The continuous technique is more sensitive to surface defects because the **active field is stronger** and the particles are drawn toward the leakage field in real time, building up a denser indication.

**[7 marks: 1.5 equipment + 2 sequence + 1.5 principle + 2 sensitivity comparison]**

---

#### Part (b) – Calculation

**Given:**
- L = 300 mm = 0.3 m
- d = 50 mm = 0.05 m → A = π/4 × (0.05)² = 1.963 × 10⁻³ m²
- N = 400 turns
- I = 5 A
- μᵣ = 1500

**Step 1: Magnetizing field strength H:**

$$
H = \frac{NI}{L} = \frac{400 \times 5}{0.3} = \frac{2000}{0.3} \approx 6666.67 \text{ A/m}
$$

**Step 2: Flux density B:**

$$
B = \mu_0 \mu_r H = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) (1500) (6666.67)
$$

$$
B = (1.885 \times 10^{-3}) (6666.67) \approx 12.57 \text{ T}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall — Unphysical result:** The value 12.57 T is **physically impossible** for steel (saturation Bs ≈ 1.6–2.2 T). This tells us the permeability is not constant — at high H, the material is in **saturation** and the linear relation B = μ₀μᵣH breaks down. In KTU exams, the calculation is the expected method, but students should note that **practical flux density is capped at saturation**.

**Step 3: Total flux Φ:**

$$
\Phi = B \cdot A = 12.57 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.0247 \text{ Wb} = 24.7 \text{ mWb}
$$

(Realistic flux will be limited by saturation: $\Phi_{sat} = 1.8 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \approx 3.53 \text{ mWb}$)

**Step 4: Reluctance R:**

$$
\mathcal{R} = \frac{L}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} = \frac{0.3}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1500)(1.963 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
\mathcal{R} = \frac{0.3}{3.70 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8.11 \times 10^4 \text{ A/Wb}
$$

**Step 5: Technique Selection:**

- The bar is long and cylindrical — encircling coil is suitable.
- 400 turns × 5 A = 2000 At — strong magnetization.
- For **surface-breaking defects on a long bar**, the **continuous technique** is preferred for its real-time indication formation and speed on a production line.
- For **sub-surface defects**, the residual technique would be better — provided the bar's Br is verified to be ≥ 0.8 T (typical for medium-carbon forged steel).

**Recommended: Continuous technique for surface inspection; Residual for sub-surface.**

**[7 marks: 1.5 each for H, B, Φ, R + 1.5 for technique selection]**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Br and Hc on the B-H curve** — Br is on the **B-axis**, Hc is on the **H-axis**. Confusing them costs 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Forgetting the timing of particle application** — Examiners expect "**before / during / after** current ON" to be explicitly stated. Vague answers lose marks.
> 3. **Ignoring demagnetization** — For residual MPI, demagnetization is **mandatory**; not mentioning it loses a mark.
> 4. **Stating that residual technique is "always better"** — It is **not**. It only works for high-Br materials. The continuous technique is more sensitive for surface defects.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw / describe the hysteresis loop** — A 7-mark question on residual technique **always expects** the B-H curve with Br and Hc labeled.
> 6. **Unit errors** — Forgetting to convert mm to m in H = NI/L calculations.
> 7. **Claiming continuous technique detects deep sub-surface defects** — It does not; the technique is limited to shallow depths.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Continuous MPI** = particles applied **with current ON**; relies on **active H**.
- [x] **Residual MPI** = particles applied **after current OFF**; relies on **retained Br**.
- [x] The **timing of particle application** is the defining operational difference.
- [x] **Br** = residual flux density (on B-axis of hysteresis loop) — drives residual technique.
- [x] **Hc** = coercivity (on H-axis) — describes how "magnetically hard" the material is.
- [x] **Residual technique requires Br ≥ 0.8 T** for reliable inspection.
- [x] **Continuous technique** → best for **surface defects** and low-retentivity materials.
- [x] **Residual technique** → best for **sub-surface defects** and high-retentivity materials.
- [x] **Demagnetization is mandatory** after residual MPI but optional after continuous MPI.
- [x] **Indications in residual MPI are stable and photographable**; in continuous MPI, they may fade.
- [x] **H = NI / L** is the fundamental field-strength equation.
- [x] **B = μ₀μᵣH**; remember μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m.
- [x] **Φ = BA** and **Φ = MMF / R** are the two key flux equations.
- [x] **Leakage field at defect** is what attracts the particles — without it, no indication.
- [x] **Wet fluorescent** method is used in residual MPI for sub-surface defects in critical parts.
- [x] **Demagnetization** is done by passing the part through a coil with decaying AC current.
- [x] **ASTM E709 / ASTM E1444** are the key international standards for MPI procedures.
- [x] **Equipment options:** prod, yoke, encircling coil, central conductor, permanent magnet.
- [x] **Selection logic summary:** Surface + low Br → Continuous; Sub-surface + high Br → Residual.
- [x] **Particle types:** dry powder (gray/black/red oxide) and wet fluorescent (green/yellow under UV).
- [x] **Always demagnetize** before the part leaves the inspection bay if residual MPI was used.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — Continuous and Residual Techniques of MPI**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Outcome-Based Education framework. Aligned with CO2, Bloom's Levels L2–L4.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Sensitivity and Checking Devices in MPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Sensitivity and Checking Devices in MPI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**System Sensitivity** in Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI) is defined as the *overall capability of the complete inspection system — including the magnetizing equipment, the magnetic particles, the procedure parameters, the surface condition of the test piece, and the operator's interpretation skill — to detect and reveal surface and near-surface discontinuities through their magnetic leakage field indications.*

It is a holistic measure of the *smallest, shallowest, and most tightly closed defect* that the system can reveal under a given set of operating conditions. Sensitivity is not a single physical quantity; it is a *composite performance index* of the whole inspection chain.

**Relative Sensitivity (RS)** is the parameter used to compare one system against another, expressed in arbitrary units derived from standard reference test blocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology — Must Memorize:**
> - *System Sensitivity* → Refers to the complete MPI system.
> - *Indicating Sensitivity* → Refers to the magnetic particle medium alone.
> - *Equipment Sensitivity* → Refers to the magnetization unit alone.
> - *Field Strength* → Quantified in Oersteds (CGS) or Ampere-turns/metre (SI).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are **hunting for a tiny metal needle lost in a dark sandy beach at night.**

- The **magnet** you use is the *magnetizing current*.
- The **size and material of the magnet** is the *equipment*.
- The **glow-stick attached to your hand** that helps you see is the *magnetic particles and UV light*.
- The **dragging technique and your eyesight** is the *procedure and operator skill*.
- The **magnetic compass in your pocket that you check periodically** is the *checking device (Pie gauge / flux indicator)*.

If any one of these elements is weak, you may step right over the needle without seeing it. The system sensitivity captures this **chain of dependencies** — it is only as strong as the weakest link. Checking devices are the **calibrated references** (like a weighing scale in a shop) that confirm your "magnet" is actually producing enough pull to attract even the smallest needle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric in bold**: The benchmark sensitivity for an MPI system is considered *acceptable* if it can clearly reveal the indications on a **Ketos Test Ring** at the specified current, and on a **Type II Magnetic Flux Indicator (Sensitivity Checker)** of a defined colour (typically green for acceptable field strength, red for too high, and yellow for marginal).

---

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Tangential Field Strength (H_t)** on the surface for satisfactory detection: **30 to 60 Oersteds (≈ 2400 to 4800 A/m)**.
- **Standard test current** for continuous magnetization: typically **800 to 1200 A/m (10 to 15 Oersteds)** for general components.
- **ASTM E709 / ASME Section V, Article 7** are the governing standards.
- **Resolution requirement**: A good MPI system should resolve a defect of width **0.05 mm** and depth **0.1 mm** in steel.
- **Visible light intensity** for wet method: **1000 lux** minimum at the inspection surface.

---

### 1.4 Classification of System Sensitivity

| Sensitivity Class | Typical Application | Field Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Sensitivity** | Forging and casting coarse checks | 20–30 Oe |
| **Medium Sensitivity** | General weld & machine parts | 30–45 Oe |
| **High Sensitivity** | Aircraft, nuclear, critical safety parts | 45–60+ Oe |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind System Sensitivity

A discontinuity in a ferromagnetic material disturbs the uniform magnetic flux lines. This disturbance produces a **leakage flux** (Φ_L) that exits and re-enters the surface. For this leakage flux to attract magnetic particles, three conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Sufficient tangential field intensity (H_t)** must exist at the surface (>= 30 Oe in steel).
2. **Sufficient flux density contrast (ΔB)** between defect and matrix must exist.
3. **The leakage flux must be aligned optimally** — i.e., the field vector must be roughly **perpendicular** to the crack plane for maximum attraction.

If any of these is missing, the particles will not accumulate, and the indication is lost.

### 2.2 Factors Influencing System Sensitivity — Structured Logic Flow

The following factors directly govern system sensitivity:

- **Magnetizing Current (Amperage)**
  - Too low → weak leakage flux → no indication.
  - Too high → burn marks, masking of tight cracks by particle background.
  - *Why?* Leakage flux increases linearly with magnetizing current up to the knee of the B-H curve, then plateaus.

- **Type of Current (AC vs DC vs HWDC)**
  - AC → strong skin effect → detects **surface defects only** → **high surface sensitivity**.
  - DC (rectified) → deeper penetration → detects **subsurface defects** → **lower surface sensitivity, higher subsurface sensitivity**.

- **Magnetizing Field Direction**
  - Must be **perpendicular** to the major axis of expected defects.
