  - Circular magnetization → finds radial defects; longitudinal magnetization → finds transverse defects.

- **Magnetic Particle Characteristics**
  - Particle size, shape, magnetic permeability, fluorescence, mobility, and contrast.
  - *Why?* Smaller particles = higher mobility = can detect finer leakage fields.

- **Surface Preparation**
  - Surface roughness, paint, oil, scale → reduces particle mobility and visibility.
  - Standards: surface should be free of contaminants, paint removed (unless approved).

- **Demagnetization Remanence**
  - Excessive residual magnetism attracts particles in non-defective areas → false indications / background noise.
  - *Why?* Particles stick wherever stray fields exist.

- **Viewing Conditions**
  - White light intensity (>= 1000 lux) for visible particles.
  - UV-A intensity (>= 1000 μW/cm² at 365 nm) for fluorescent particles.

- **Operator Skill & Eye Acuity**
  - Even with perfect equipment, fatigue and training affect detection probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chain Logic for Examiners:** If the examiner asks *"Why is system sensitivity called a chain?"*, answer: *"Because every parameter (current, particle, light, surface, operator) is a link — failure of any one collapses the system."*

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Markdown Rule:** Vertical bars (absolute value) inside table cells are written using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the table syntax.

| # | Quantity / Relation | Equation | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tangential Field Strength | $H_t = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $N$ = turns, $I$ = current, $D$ = coil diameter | A/m |
| 2 | Field Strength (Coil Method) | $H = \dfrac{K \cdot N \cdot I}{L}$ | $K$ = coil factor (approx 1.7 for short parts), $L$ = part length | A/m |
| 3 | Peak Current for Yoke | $I_{peak} = \dfrac{H \cdot L}{N}$ | — | Amperes |
| 4 | Required Magnetomotive Force | $MMF = H \cdot L$ | — | Ampere-turns |
| 5 | Flux Density in Air Gap (Leakage) | $B_{leak} = \mu_0 \cdot H_{leak}$ | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | Tesla |
| 6 | Detectable Minimum Defect Depth | $d_{min} \propto \dfrac{1}{H_{t}^{2}}$ | Empirical relationship | mm |
| 7 | Current Density for Central Conductor | $I_c = 4\pi \cdot D \cdot H_t$ | $D$ = part diameter | A |
| 8 | Lift-off Sensitivity Coefficient | $k = \dfrac{B_{defect}}{B_{surface}}$ | ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | Permeability Ratio | $\mu_r = \dfrac{B}{\mu_0 H}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 10 | Acceptance Field Limit (Steel) | $H_t = 2.5 \text{ to } 4.0$ kA/m | per EN 1290 / ASTM E709 | kA/m |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry**: High-sensitivity MPI is mandated for turbine discs, landing gear, and rotor shafts to detect fatigue cracks as small as 0.2 mm in length.
- **Nuclear Power**: ASME Section XI in-service inspection requires sensitivity verification using **Berthold (Pie) gauges** before each inspection campaign.
- **Welding Industry**: Post-weld MPI uses medium sensitivity to detect hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC) and lack of fusion.
- **Railways**: Axles and rails inspected using **AC yokes** with pie-gauge verification to maintain accident-prevention safety standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Magnetomotive Force (MMF) for a Cylindrical Part in a Coil

**Problem Setup:** A solid steel shaft of length $L = 200$ mm and diameter $D = 50$ mm is to be magnetized longitudinally inside a coil of $N = 500$ turns. The standard demands a tangential field strength $H_t = 30$ Oe $\approx 2400$ A/m. Find the required current.

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation for longitudinal magnetization:**

$$
H = \frac{K \cdot N \cdot I}{L}
$$

where $K$ is the demagnetization factor (for $L/D \approx 4$, $K \approx 1.6$ to $1.7$ for a long solid bar).

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for current $I$:**

$$
I = \frac{H \cdot L}{K \cdot N}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values in SI units:**

$$
L = 0.200 \text{ m}, \quad H = 2400 \text{ A/m}, \quad K = 1.6, \quad N = 500
$$

$$
I = \frac{2400 \times 0.200}{1.6 \times 500}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify numerator and denominator separately:**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 2400 \times 0.200 = 480 \text{ A·turns}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 1.6 \times 500 = 800 \text{ A·turns per A}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final value:**

$$
I = \frac{480}{800} = 0.60 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

A current of approximately **0.6 A** through a 500-turn coil produces the required 30 Oe tangential field. This is why small currents suffice in high-turn coils — the engineering practice typically rounds up to **0.8–1.0 A** to account for end-effects and coil heating.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Checking Device vs. Magnetization Type

This matrix is **exam-grade** — it is the kind of comparison table examiners expect for full marks.

| Checking Device | Function | Magnetization Mode Verified | Standard Reference | Sensitivity Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pie Gauge (Berthold / Magne-Tech)** | Artificial slot checks tangential field adequacy | Longitudinal (coil) & Yoke | ASTM E709 Sec 7.5 | Medium–High |
| **Ketos Test Ring** | Verify amperage for DC circular magnetization | Circular (head-shot / prod) | ASTM E709, ASME V Art 7 | High |
| **ICL Test Piece (Iowa Corner Lamination)** | Verify detection of **residual** magnetism | Residual / Induced current | EN 1290 | Low–Medium |
| **Type I Flux Indicator (Strip)** | Visualize field direction only (8 dots) | Any | ASTM E709 | Reference only |
| **Type II Flux Indicator (Block with 3 Holes)** | Quantitative field adequacy check (3 hole visibility) | Any | ASTM E709 Sec 6.4 | High |
| **Gaussmeter / Hall-effect probe** | Numerical field measurement in Gauss or Tesla | Any | ASTM E709 | Quantitative |
| **Berthold Test Block (Slotted Bar)** | Pre-calibrated artificial flaws | Yoke magnetization | DIN 54131 | Medium |
| **MTU Test Piece (Magnetic Particle Test Universal)** | Combination residual + induced test | Multi-purpose | EN 1290 | Universal |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Verification Using a Pie Gauge

**Procedure (Examiner Favourite 14-Mark Question):**

1. **Place the Pie Gauge** on the surface of the part at the point of maximum expected field (typically the pole region for a yoke, or the centre of a coil).
2. **Apply magnetizing current** for a short duration (magnetizing shot).
3. **Observe the three radial slots** machined into the gauge — these act as artificial defects of known size.
4. **Interpretation**:
   - If **all three slots** show clear, sharp indications → field is **adequate to high**.
   - If only **two slots** are visible → field is **marginal**, increase current.
   - If **one or none** are visible → field is **insufficient**, **STOP** the inspection and re-calibrate.
5. **Reset the gauge** by demagnetizing before the next shot.

**Why three slots?** The slots are of different depths (typically 0.5 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.5 mm). The visibility of the smallest slot is the **true indicator of system sensitivity** — the larger slots always appear in any reasonable system, so they are not diagnostic.

---

### 3.4 Quantitative Sensitivity Check Using Type II Flux Indicator

A Type II flux indicator is a thin copper plate with **three concentric holes** (typically 1.5 mm, 2.0 mm, 2.5 mm) bonded to a ferromagnetic substrate. When placed under magnetization:

- **Hole 1 (smallest) visible** → field $\geq 20$ Oe → low-end acceptable.
- **Hole 2 visible** → field $\geq 30$ Oe → standard industrial acceptance.
- **Hole 3 visible** → field $\geq 45$ Oe → high sensitivity (aerospace, nuclear).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook for KTU 2024 Board:** *"Three holes — Three sensitivity tiers."*

---

### 3.5 Demagnetization Verification Using ICL Test Piece

**Iowa Corner Lamination (ICL) Test Piece** is a hollow steel cylinder with a tightly fitted **copper lamination block** at one end. Procedure:

1. Magnetize the ICL piece.
2. Apply magnetic particles.
3. A sharp, defined indication should appear at the **corner** where the lamination meets the steel.
4. If the indication is faint or absent → system sensitivity is too low.
5. For **residual field check**, do not re-magnetize; observe whether particles still adhere at the corner.

This is the **gold standard for verifying residual magnetism detection** in accordance with EN 1290.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**  
*Define the term "system sensitivity" as applied to Magnetic Particle Inspection. List any four factors that affect it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*System sensitivity in MPI is defined as the overall ability of the complete inspection system — comprising the magnetizing equipment, the magnetic particle medium, the procedure, the surface condition, and the operator — to detect and display discontinuities of the smallest practical size through their magnetic leakage field indications.* (1.5 marks)

*Four factors affecting system sensitivity:*
1. *Magnetizing current type and magnitude (AC vs DC).* (0.5 mark)
2. *Particle size, fluorescence, and mobility.* (0.5 mark)
3. *Surface preparation and contamination level.* (0.25 mark)
4. *Ambient light intensity and operator skill.* (0.25 mark)

*(Total: 3 marks — examiner expects a clear definition plus four crisp points.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*What is a pie gauge? State its function in MPI.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*A pie gauge (also called a magnetic particle test piece or Magne-Tech gauge) is a small ferromagnetic disc with three radial slots of different depths (typically 0.5 mm, 1.0 mm, and 1.5 mm) machined into its surface.* (1.5 marks)

*Function:*  
*It is placed on the surface of the component being magnetized. After applying a magnetizing shot, the slots act as artificial defects of known size. The number of slots that produce visible indications determines whether the magnetizing field is adequate, marginal, or insufficient.* (1.5 marks)

*(Total: 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Discussion Type*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Examination]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the various factors affecting the sensitivity of a magnetic particle inspection system. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Describe the construction, working principle, and interpretation procedure of a Ketos test ring. How is it used to verify system sensitivity for DC circular magnetization? (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Factors affecting MPI system sensitivity (7 marks):**

The sensitivity of an MPI system depends on a *chain* of interdependent factors. These are presented below in three logical groups:

*Equipment-related factors:* (2 marks)
- Magnetizing current amplitude and type (AC, HWDC, FWDC).
- Yoke vs coil vs prods.
- Calibration drift of current source.

*Material and medium factors:* (2 marks)
- Particle size (finer = higher mobility = better for tight cracks).
- Particle fluorescence (fluorescent = 1000× better contrast under UV).
- Carrier fluid viscosity (wet method) and concentration.

*Procedure and human factors:* (3 marks)
- Surface preparation (paint, oil, scale must be removed).
- Magnetizing current value relative to $L/D$ ratio.
- Viewing light intensity (1000 lux for visible, 1000 μW/cm² UV for fluorescent).
- Demagnetization cycle before and after inspection.
- Operator training, vision acuity, and inspection time per unit area.

A well-drawn block diagram interconnecting these is essential (2 marks for the diagram, 5 marks for the descriptive content).

---

**(b) Ketos test ring — construction, principle, interpretation (7 marks):**

*Construction:* (2 marks)
The Ketos test ring is a circular steel ring (typical outer diameter 100 mm, cross-section 20 × 20 mm) with **eight equispaced radial holes** of two different diameters (commonly 1.0 mm and 2.0 mm) drilled perpendicular to the ring circumference. It is designed to be magnetized by passing a current *through* it (i.e., shot via a central conductor) or by placing it in a coil.

*Working principle:* (2 marks)
When current is passed through the ring (or through a central conductor running through the ring), the ring is **circularly magnetized**. The holes act as artificial surface-breaking discontinuities. Magnetic particles sprayed on the ring will accumulate at the hole edges, producing visible indications.

*Interpretation procedure:* (2 marks)
- All eight holes (1.0 mm and 2.0 mm) produce sharp, clear indications → **system sensitivity is HIGH** → field strength is **adequate** for the most demanding applications.
- Only the 2.0 mm holes are visible → **MEDIUM sensitivity** → sufficient for general industrial work.
- Only some holes are visible or indications are faint → **LOW sensitivity** → current must be increased or the system recalibrated.
- No visible indications → **INSPECTION MUST BE STOPPED** until the cause is found.

*Use for DC circular magnetization:* (1 mark)
The Ketos test ring is specifically designed for **DC (HWDC) circular magnetization** because the artificial holes simulate **sub-surface tight cracks** that are best revealed by DC's deeper penetration. AC magnetization, due to its skin effect, would not adequately test the system's ability to find sub-surface defects.

*(Total: 14 marks — examiners expect 7 + 7 distribution.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular Examination]**

**(a)** *Explain the construction and working of an ICL (Iowa Corner Lamination) test piece. State its specific use in MPI. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With sketches, describe the Type I and Type II magnetic flux indicators. How are they used to verify the adequacy of the magnetizing field? (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) ICL Test Piece (7 marks):**

*Construction:* (3 marks)
The ICL test piece consists of a **hollow steel cylinder** (typical size: 25 mm OD, 15 mm ID, 50 mm long) into which a **copper lamination pack** is tightly inserted at one end. The copper laminations are thin, insulated sheets that mimic the **geometry of a sub-surface lamination-type defect**. The sharp corner where the copper block terminates inside the steel creates a geometric discontinuity that produces a strong, localized leakage field when magnetized.

*Working principle:* (2 marks)
When the ICL piece is magnetized (by passage of current through it or by placing it in a coil), the copper-steel interface acts as an artificial sub-surface defect. Magnetic particles sprayed on the outside will form a sharp, well-defined indication at the **corner location**. The strength and sharpness of this indication serve as a direct measure of system sensitivity.

*Specific use:* (2 marks)
The ICL test piece is the **standard reference for verifying the detection of residual magnetism** in ferromagnetic components. It is also used to confirm that the magnetic particles are correctly formulated (correct concentration, correct fluorescence, correct mobility). It is specified in **EN 1290** as the reference block for low-to-medium sensitivity certification.

---

**(b) Type I and Type II Magnetic Flux Indicators (7 marks):**

*Type I — Flux Indicator Strip:* (3 marks)
Type I is a thin, flexible strip of low-carbon steel (about 50 mm × 20 mm × 0.2 mm) on which **eight small dots of high-permeability material** are printed in a line. When the strip is placed on a magnetized surface, the dots darken to indicate the field direction (the dots are arranged so that only those dots perpendicular to the field show contrast). It is a **qualitative** indicator — it shows field direction but not magnitude.

*Type II — Flux Indicator Block (ASTM E709 Standard):* (2 marks)
Type II is a small ferromagnetic plate (about 50 mm × 25 mm × 5 mm) into which **three holes of different diameters** (typically 1.5 mm, 2.0 mm, 2.5 mm) are drilled. When placed on a magnetized surface, particles accumulate around the holes if the field is adequate. The number of holes showing clear indications is the sensitivity metric.

*Use for verification of field adequacy:* (2 marks)
- All three holes of Type II show clear, sharp indications → field is in the **high-sensitivity range (≥ 45 Oe)**.
- Only two holes visible → **medium sensitivity (30–45 Oe)** — acceptable for most industrial work.
- Only one hole visible → **low sensitivity (20–30 Oe)** — acceptable for rough checks like as-cast or as-forged.
- No holes visible → field is inadequate → recalibrate.

*(Total: 14 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "system sensitivity" with "field strength"** — system sensitivity is a *composite* property. Just quoting "30 Oe" is worth only 1 mark; you need to mention the chain of factors for full marks.
> 2. **Do NOT mix up Ketos test ring with Pie gauge** — Ketos is for **DC circular magnetization** (8 holes in a ring); Pie gauge is for **yoke/coil longitudinal magnetization** (3 radial slots in a disc).
> 3. **Do NOT forget the standard reference** — always cite **ASTM E709** (US) or **EN 1290** (European) for the device, and **EN 1290 for ICL** specifically.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark questions = automatic loss of 2 marks. Always draw a labelled diagram, even if simple.
> 5. **Not mentioning demagnetization** = -1 mark. MPI sensitivity verification is *incomplete* without mentioning that the system is re-demagnetized after each check shot.
> 6. **Mixing "indicating sensitivity" and "equipment sensitivity"** as synonyms of "system sensitivity" = -1 mark. They are *sub-components*, not the whole.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision Notes:**

- **System Sensitivity** = the entire chain's ability to detect the smallest defect, *not* just the equipment.
- **Benchmark field strength** for general steel MPI: **30 to 60 Oersteds** (≈ 2400 to 4800 A/m).
- **Three core checking devices** you MUST know:
  1. **Pie Gauge** — disc with 3 radial slots (0.5/1.0/1.5 mm depth).
  2. **Ketos Test Ring** — 8-hole steel ring for DC circular magnetization.
  3. **ICL Test Piece** — copper-laminated steel cylinder for residual field check.
- **Two flux indicators**:
  - **Type I** → 8 dots → indicates **direction** only (qualitative).
  - **Type II** → 3 holes → indicates **adequacy tier** (quantitative tier).
- **Standards to cite** in any answer: **ASTM E709, EN 1290, ASME V Article 7, ISO 9712**.
- **Current formula** for coil: $I = \dfrac{H \cdot L}{K \cdot N}$ where $K \approx 1.6$ for $L/D \approx 4$.
- **AC magnetization** = best for **surface defects** (skin effect).
- **DC magnetization** = best for **sub-surface defects** (deep penetration).
- **Demagnetization** is a mandatory sensitivity step — never skip it.
- **Light requirements**: visible ≥ 1000 lux, UV-A ≥ 1000 μW/cm² at 365 nm.
- **Three-slot interpretation**: 1 visible = low, 2 visible = medium, 3 visible = high sensitivity.
- **Operator skill** is a sensitivity factor — mention it for full marks in descriptive answers.
- **Chain analogy** is the *signature KTU board phrase* — "Sensitivity is only as strong as the weakest link."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eddy Current Testing: Physical Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eddy Current Testing: Physical Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** is an electromagnetic Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method used to evaluate the surface and sub-surface integrity of electrically conductive materials. It is based on the principles of **electromagnetic induction** as formalized by **Faraday's Law** and **Lenz's Law**. A high-frequency alternating current is passed through a coil (the probe), generating a primary alternating magnetic field. When this probe is brought near a conductive test piece, the time-varying magnetic flux links with the specimen and induces circulating electrical currents—called **eddy currents**—within the material. Any flaw or material property variation alters the eddy current flow pattern, which in turn changes the magnetic field linking back to the coil. This change is monitored as a variation in the coil's **impedance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Eddy current testing is a contact-less, no-couplant method limited to **electrically conductive** materials (metals, alloys, graphite composites). It is exceptionally sensitive to **surface and near-surface defects** but cannot effectively inspect non-conductors (plastics, wood, ceramics) without modification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a calm pond. When you drag your hand quickly across the surface, you create tiny circular swirls (vortices) of water. Now, suppose there is a submerged rock where your hand moves—your swirls bend and distort around that rock. If you could *sense* the distortion in the swirls, you would know the rock is there without seeing or touching it directly.

**Eddy Current Testing works exactly like this:**

- The probe's alternating magnetic field acts like your **moving hand**, disturbing the electrons (the "water") inside the conductor.
- The induced swirling electron flow (the **eddy currents**) behaves like the **circular swirls**.
- A defect, crack, or change in material acts like the **submerged rock**—it distorts the eddy current pattern.
- The probe *senses* the distorted pattern back as a change in electrical impedance.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|-------------------------|
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$** |
| $\sigma$ | Electrical conductivity | **Siemens/metre (S/m)** |
| $\mu_r$ | Relative permeability | Dimensionless (≈ 1 for non-magnetic) |
| $f$ | Test frequency | **Hz** (typically 100 Hz – 10 MHz in ECT) |
| $\delta$ | Standard depth of penetration | **metres (m)** |

> [!NOTE]
> The word **"Eddy"** in eddy currents originates from the German word *Wirbelstrom* (literally "whirlpool current"), coined by the French physicist **Jean-Bernard-Léon Foucault**, who also gave his name to the related concept of **Foucault currents**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics — Step-by-Step

1. **Alternating Excitation:** A sinusoidal alternating current $i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is passed through the test coil. This produces a primary alternating magnetic field $H_p$ with frequency $f = \omega / (2\pi)$.
2. **Flux Linkage with Specimen:** As the probe approaches the conductive specimen, the alternating magnetic flux $\Phi(t) = \Phi_0 \sin(\omega t)$ links with the surface of the material.
3. **Induced EMF (Faraday's Law):** A time-varying flux induces an electromotive force (EMF) in the specimen:
4. **Eddy Current Generation (Lenz's Law):** The induced EMF drives circulating currents—eddy currents—*in closed loops* within the conductor. These currents flow in planes *perpendicular* to the applied magnetic field and in a direction that **opposes** the cause producing them.
5. **Magnetic Field Reaction:** The eddy currents themselves generate a *secondary* magnetic field $H_s$ that opposes the primary field. The net field at the probe coil becomes $H_{net} = H_p - H_s$.
6. **Impedance Change in the Probe Coil:** Because the secondary field alters the total flux linking the probe, the coil's **self-inductance $L$** changes. The coil's **impedance $Z = R + j\omega L$** therefore shifts by an amount dependent on the eddy current strength.
7. **Defect Detection:** Any discontinuity (crack, void, inclusion, corrosion) disturbs the eddy current path, which alters the secondary field, which alters the coil impedance. This change is plotted on the impedance plane and correlated to flaw size/position.

### 2.2 The Probe Coil — Equivalent Circuit

The probe coil can be modelled as a series **RL circuit**:

- $R$ — resistance of the coil winding
- $L$ — self-inductance of the coil in air (and modified when near a specimen)
- $X_L = \omega L$ — inductive reactance

When eddy currents are induced, the secondary reaction field appears to the coil as a *reflected resistance* $\Delta R$ and a *reflected reactance* $\Delta X_L$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula No. | Governing Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|:-----------:|-------------------|-------------|:-------------:|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's Law — Induced EMF | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $\Phi = B \cdot A \cos(\theta)$ | Magnetic flux linkage | Weber (Wb) |
| 3 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | Standard depth of penetration | metres (m) |
| 4 | $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ | Absolute permeability | H/m |
| 5 | $Z_{coil} = R + j \omega L$ | Coil impedance (in air) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | $\Delta Z = Z_{loaded} - Z_{air}$ | Net impedance change due to specimen | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 7 | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$ | Resistivity (inverse of conductivity) | $\Omega \cdot \text{m}$ |
| 8 | $\tan(\phi) = \dfrac{\omega L}{R}$ | Phase angle of coil | radians (rad) |
| 9 | $f_{opt} \approx \dfrac{1}{\sigma \mu d^2}$ | Optimum frequency for a defect of depth $d$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Eddy current testing is the *workhorse* inspection technique in the aerospace industry for surface crack detection on aircraft wings, landing gear, engine turbine blades, and heat-exchanger tubes in nuclear power plants. It is also used for **conductivity sorting** in heat-treat verification and alloy identification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Depth of Penetration ($\delta$)

The depth of penetration defines how deep below the surface the eddy current density is significant (conventionally taken as the depth where the current density has decayed to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its surface value).

#### Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations in a Conductor

For a good conductor with no free surface charge and a time-harmonic field $e^{j\omega t}$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{H} = \mu \sigma \dfrac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}$$

Assuming time-harmonic dependence and a 1-D semi-infinite solid where fields decay with depth $z$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 H}{dz^2} = j \omega \mu \sigma \cdot H$$

#### Solving the Differential Equation

The general solution is:

$$H(z) = H_0 \, e^{\pm \sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}\, z}$$

Only the decaying exponential is physically meaningful (the field must vanish as $z \to \infty$):

$$H(z) = H_0 \, e^{-\sqrt{j\omega\mu\sigma}\, z}$$

We rewrite the complex exponent using $\sqrt{j} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$H(z) = H_0 \, e^{-\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}\, z} \cdot e^{-j\sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}\, z}$$

#### Identifying the Decay Constant

The amplitude of $H$ decays as:

$$ \vert H(z) \vert = H_0 \, e^{-\alpha z}, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} $$

#### Defining Standard Depth of Penetration

By definition, $\delta$ is the depth at which amplitude falls to $1/e$:

$$\alpha \cdot \delta = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} \cdot \delta = 1$$

Solving for $\delta$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$$

#### Final Form in Terms of Frequency

Using $\omega = 2 \pi f$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{2 \pi f \mu \sigma}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

This is the **KTU board-favourite form** of the equation.

### 3.2 Derivation of Optimum Test Frequency

For reliable detection, the depth of the defect $d$ must lie within roughly **one standard depth of penetration** of the surface:

$$d \approx \delta$$

Substituting the formula for $\delta$:

$$d \approx \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$d^2 = \dfrac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

Solving for $f$:

$$f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{\pi d^2 \mu \sigma}$$

This gives the engineer the frequency required to *probe* a defect of known depth.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Material and Frequency on $\delta$

Using $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$, the qualitative behaviour is summarized below.

| Parameter | Action | Effect on $\delta$ | Effect on Defect Detection |
|-----------|:------:|:-----------------:|---------------------------|
| Increase frequency $f$ | raise | $\delta$ **decreases** | Inspect only the surface |
| Decrease frequency $f$ | lower | $\delta$ **increases** | Penetrate deeper; lose resolution |
| Increase conductivity $\sigma$ | raise | $\delta$ **decreases** | Copper/Aluminium: surface only |
| Increase permeability $\mu$ | raise | $\delta$ **decreases** | Ferromagnetic materials: skin effect very strong |
| Defect depth $d$ | larger | $\delta$ must increase | Reduce $f$ to match |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Mnemonic:** "*High frequency hunts the surface; Low frequency finds the depth.*" This single sentence has rescued hundreds of KTU students in viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Eddy Current Testing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Oscillator\nSignal Generator] --> nodeB[Probe Coil\nPrimary Excitation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Test Specimen\nConductive Material]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Induced Eddy Currents\nIn Closed Loops]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Secondary Magnetic Field\nReacts Back]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Probe Coil Impedance\nChanges Detected]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Bridge / Amplifier\nSignal Conditioning]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Phase Sensitive Detector\nImpedance Plane]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Display Crt Or Digital Readout\nLissajous Pattern]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Operator Decision\nAccept Or Reject]
```

### 4.2 Physical Principle — Eddy Current Generation Visualised

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Alternating Current in Coil] --> nodeB[Time Varying Magnetic Field]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Flux Links Conductive Specimen]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Faraday Induces EMF in Material]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Eddy Currents Flow In Loops]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Secondary Opposing Field Generated]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Net Field Modifies Coil Inductance]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Impedance Z Equals R Plus J Omega L]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Defects Disturb Pattern]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Impedance Change Maps To Flaw]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting Test Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Select Test Frequency] --> nodeQ1{Defect Location}
    nodeQ1 -->|Surface| nodeA[High Frequency\n1 To 10 MHz]
    nodeQ1 -->|Subsurface| nodeB[Medium Frequency\n100 To 500 KHz]
    nodeQ1 -->|Deep| nodeC[Low Frequency\n1 To 10 KHz]
    nodeA --> nodeR[Use Small Probe Coil\nHigh Resolution]
    nodeB --> nodeR
    nodeC --> nodeR
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define eddy current testing. State any two of its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

*Eddy Current Testing (ECT) is a non-destructive electromagnetic inspection technique in which circulating electrical currents (eddy currents) are induced in a conductive material by a time-varying magnetic field from a probe coil. Any change in the eddy current path caused by defects or material property variation alters the coil impedance, which is monitored for flaw detection.*

*Limitations (any two):*

1. *Applicable only to electrically conductive materials — plastics, wood, and ceramics cannot be inspected directly.*
2. *Limited to surface and near-surface defects; deep sub-surface flaws may go undetected due to the skin effect.*
3. *Highly sensitive to lift-off variations, which can mask defect signals.*
4. *Ferromagnetic materials require special techniques (e.g., saturation probes) due to high permeability noise.*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is meant by "depth of penetration" in eddy current testing? Write the formula.**

**Model Answer:**

*The depth of penetration ($\delta$) is defined as the depth below the surface of a conductor at which the eddy current density has decreased to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its value at the surface. Beyond this depth, the eddy currents become too weak to be reliably detected.*

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

*where $f$ is the test frequency (Hz), $\mu$ is the absolute permeability (H/m), and $\sigma$ is the electrical conductivity (S/m).*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** *Explain the physical principles of eddy current testing. Derive an expression for the depth of penetration. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *A non-magnetic stainless-steel component ($\mu_r = 1$, $\sigma = 1.4 \times 10^6 \ \text{S/m}$) is tested using an ECT probe operating at 200 kHz. Calculate the standard depth of penetration. Comment on the suitability of this frequency for detecting a sub-surface defect at 2 mm depth. (7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Principle (3 marks):**

1. An AC supply drives the probe coil, producing a time-varying primary magnetic field $H_p$.
2. The flux links the conductive specimen and, by **Faraday's Law**, induces an EMF.
3. This EMF drives closed-loop circulating currents (eddy currents) within the conductor, in a direction given by **Lenz's Law** (opposing the inducing field).
4. These eddy currents generate a secondary magnetic field $H_s$ that opposes $H_p$.
5. The net field changes the coil's impedance $Z = R + j\omega L$.
6. Defects disrupt the eddy current path, altering $Z$, which is measured for inspection.

**Derivation of $\delta$ (4 marks):** *(Refer to derivation in Section 3.1.)*

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}$$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

- $\mu_r = 1 \Rightarrow \mu = \mu_0 \mu_r = (4\pi \times 10^{-7})(1) = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{H/m}$
- $\sigma = 1.4 \times 10^6 \ \text{S/m}$
- $f = 200 \ \text{kHz} = 2 \times 10^5 \ \text{Hz}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the product $f \mu \sigma$:**

$$f \mu \sigma = (2 \times 10^5)(1.2566 \times 10^{-6})(1.4 \times 10^6)$$

$$= (2 \times 10^5)(1.7593)$$

$$= 3.5186 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $\pi$:**

$$\pi f \mu \sigma = 3.1416 \times 3.5186 \times 10^5 = 1.1054 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{1.1054 \times 10^6} = 1051.4$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\delta$:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{1051.4} \approx 9.51 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m} \approx 0.95 \ \text{mm}$$

**Comment (3 marks):** The defect lies at 2 mm depth, which is more than **2 standard depths of penetration** below the surface. At 2 mm, the eddy current density has dropped to $e^{-2} \approx 13.5\%$, which is weak. The defect signal will be poorly resolved. **Recommendation:** Lower the test frequency so that $\delta$ increases to approximately 2 mm. Using $\delta = d$:

$$f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{\pi d^2 \mu \sigma} = \dfrac{1}{\pi (0.002)^2 (1.2566 \times 10^{-6})(1.4 \times 10^6)} \approx 45 \ \text{kHz}$$

Therefore, a frequency around **45 kHz** would be more appropriate for 2 mm defects in this material.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *With neat sketches, describe the generation of eddy currents in a test specimen. List the factors affecting the test. (7 Marks)*

**(b)** *Explain the concept of "lift-off" and "edge effect" in eddy current testing. How do these phenomena complicate interpretation? (7 Marks)*

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Generation of Eddy Currents (4 marks):**

When a probe coil carrying AC is placed near a conductor, the alternating magnetic field penetrates the surface. Inside the conductor, this time-varying flux induces an EMF (Faraday's Law), which drives circulating currents flowing in closed loops perpendicular to the magnetic field direction. By Lenz's Law, these eddy currents flow in such a direction that their magnetic field opposes the change in the inducing field. The depth of penetration is governed by $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$.

**Factors Affecting the Test (3 marks):**

| Factor | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| Electrical conductivity $\sigma$ | Higher conductivity → smaller $\delta$ |
| Magnetic permeability $\mu$ | Higher $\mu$ → smaller $\delta$; affects ferromagnetic materials strongly |
| Test frequency $f$ | Higher $f$ → smaller $\delta$; affects resolution vs depth |
| Geometry of specimen | Curvature, edge effects distort eddy current flow |
| Surface condition | Roughness, coating, paint introduce noise |
| Probe-to-specimen distance | Lift-off changes apparent impedance |
| Defect orientation | Cracks must interrupt eddy current flow to be detected |

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Lift-Off Effect (4 marks):**

Lift-off is the small distance variation between the probe tip and the specimen surface. As the probe moves closer or farther from the surface, the magnetic coupling changes, which alters the coil impedance even when no defect exists. This produces a vertical trace on the impedance plane display. Lift-off must be compensated for by using specialised probe designs (e.g., absolute vs differential probes) or by maintaining constant probe pressure.

**Edge Effect (3 marks):**

When the probe approaches the edge of a specimen, eddy currents cannot flow in closed loops beyond the boundary, causing a sudden drop in effective impedance. This generates an edge signal on the display that can be mistaken for a defect.

**Why These Complicate Interpretation:**

Both effects produce impedance changes *independent of the actual defect*, so they can be confused with flaw signals. Lift-off is the **single biggest source of noise** in manual ECT scanning. Skilled inspectors use phase analysis to separate lift-off signals (which are largely in-phase with the probe axis) from defect signals (which usually have a phase lag).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** the *Standard Depth of Penetration* ($\delta$, defined at $1/e$) with the *Effective Depth of Penetration* (often defined at $2\delta$ or $3\delta$). Many students lose 1–2 marks by mixing these definitions.
> 2. **Do not forget to square and take square roots** properly in numerical problems. Several answer sheets show $\delta = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$ — the *inverse* square root. Memorize: $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$.
> 3. **Always convert units** before substitution (kHz → Hz, mm → m). Errors in $\delta$ magnitudes of $10^3$ to $10^6$ are the most common.
> 4. **Always mention units** with your final numerical answer — examiners frequently deduct half a mark for missing units.
> 5. In derivation questions, *explicitly state* Faraday's Law and Lenz's Law before plugging into Maxwell's equations. Examiners award 1 mark for naming the laws.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ECT Principle:** Time-varying magnetic field → induced EMF → circulating eddy currents → secondary field → impedance change → defect signal.
- **Faraday's Law:** $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
- **Lenz's Law:** Induced current opposes the cause producing it.
- **Depth of Penetration:** $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ (most important formula for KTU).
- **Optimum Frequency:** $f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{\pi d^2 \mu \sigma}$ for detecting a defect of depth $d$.
- **Impedance of Probe Coil:** $Z = R + j\omega L$ — both real and imaginary parts respond to specimen.
- **High $f$** → surface only; **Low $f$** → deeper penetration; this is the cornerstone selection criterion.
- **Lift-off** and **edge effect** are the two biggest sources of false signals — compensation techniques include differential probes, bridge balancing, and phase analysis.
- **ECT is non-contact** (no couplant required), highly sensitive to surface defects, and limited to **electrically conductive** materials.
- **Conductivity Sorting:** ECT can sort alloys by $\sigma$ — used in heat-treat verification of aluminium aerospace components.
- **Probe Types:** Absolute, differential, and reflection (send-receive) — know the differences for viva questions.
- **Units Check:** Always use SI units ($\sigma$ in S/m, $\mu$ in H/m, $f$ in Hz, $\delta$ in m) before plugging into formulas.
- **Physical Constants:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$; remember this by the phrase "*four-pi-ten-to-the-minus-seven*".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Factor, Lift-Off Effect, Edge Effect, and End Effect
Impedance Plane Diagram
Depth of Penetration in ECT and Its Relation with Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Depth of Penetration in ECT and Its Relation with Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Standard Depth of Penetration (δ)** in Eddy Current Testing (ECT) is formally defined as the depth below the surface of a test specimen at which the **eddy current density has decreased to 1/e (approximately 36.8 %)** of its value at the surface, when alternating current (AC) excitation is applied to a probe coil.

Mathematically, if $J_0$ is the eddy current density at the surface ($x = 0$), then at depth $x = \delta$:

$$J(\delta) = J_0 \cdot e^{-1} \approx 0.368 \, J_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternate (and equally valid) Definition (Phase Definition):**
> The standard depth of penetration is also the depth at which the **eddy current lags the surface eddy current by exactly one radian (57.3°)**. This dual definition is critical in KTU valuation — examiners often test both interpretations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Ripple in Muddy Pond"

Imagine dropping a stone into a muddy pond:
- The **surface water moves vigorously** (high current density).
- As you go deeper, the water moves less and less until it becomes still.
- **δ is the "depth of effective stirring"** — beyond this, water is practically motionless.

If you shake the water **faster** (higher frequency), the disturbance stays near the surface — it cannot reach deep. If you shake it **slowly** (lower frequency), the disturbance penetrates deeper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for Beginners:** Frequency is the "depth control knob" of ECT. High frequency = shallow inspection; Low frequency = deep inspection.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range in ECT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency of excitation | $f$ | Hz | **100 Hz – 10 MHz** |
| Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | — |
| Magnetic permeability | $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ | H/m | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| Electrical conductivity | $\sigma$ | S/m (or % IACS) | Copper: **5.8 × 10⁷ S/m** |
| Standard depth of penetration | $\delta$ | mm or m | **0.1 – 10 mm** typical |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Probe Excitation:** A coil carrying AC current $I(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is placed on/near the test piece.
2. **Primary Magnetic Field Generation:** The time-varying current produces a primary alternating magnetic field $H_0$ at the surface of the conductor.
3. **Faraday's Law Activation:** A time-varying magnetic field inside a conductor induces an EMF (per Faraday's Law), which drives circulating currents — called **eddy currents** — in closed loops perpendicular to the field.
4. **Lenz's Law Opposition:** These eddy currents produce their *own* opposing magnetic field (Lenz's Law).
5. **Self-Shielding (Skin Effect):** The induced currents near the surface **oppose** the penetration of the original field into deeper layers. This is the **skin effect**.
6. **Exponential Decay:** The magnetic field intensity and eddy current density **decay exponentially** as a function of depth.
7. **Defining δ:** The depth at which this exponential has dropped to $1/e$ is by definition the standard depth of penetration.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | **Master formula** — standard depth of penetration |
| 2 | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}$ | Equivalent form using $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| 3 | $J(x) = J_0 \, e^{-x/\delta}$ | Eddy current density at depth $x$ |
| 4 | $H(x) = H_0 \, e^{-x/\delta}$ | Magnetic field intensity at depth $x$ |
| 5 | $\theta(x) = \dfrac{x}{\delta} \;\text{radians}$ | Phase lag of eddy current at depth $x$ |
| 6 | $\delta \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}}$ | **Direct proportionality rule** — the central KTU relation |
| 7 | $\delta \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_r}}$ | Lower in ferromagnetic materials |
| 8 | $\delta \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma}}$ | Lower in highly conductive materials |
| 9 | Effective depth $\approx 3\delta$ | 95 % of signal information is contained within $0$ to $3\delta$ |
| 10 | Phase lag at $1\delta$ | $= 1$ radian $\approx 57.3°$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Memorize equations (1), (3), (4), (5), and (6) — these are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE.

### 2.3 Why the Relation $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$ Matters in Engineering

- **Defect Localization:** Surface cracks require high frequency (small δ); sub-surface defects require low frequency (large δ).
- **Material Sorting:** Conductivity differences can be identified by impedance changes at calibrated frequencies.
- **Coating Thickness Measurement:** Non-conductive coatings shift effective frequency-depth response.
- **Heat Exchanger Tube Inspection:** Tube wall thinning at inner surface is detected by selecting frequency for required δ.
- **Aircraft Component Inspection:** Frequencies in the MHz range detect surface fatigue cracks in aluminum alloys.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Depth of Penetration Formula

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations (Differential Form, Phasor Domain):**

For a linear, isotropic, homogeneous conductor with no displacement current at low frequencies (quasi-static approximation), we begin with Faraday's Law and Ampère's Law.

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law in Phasor Form:**

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H}$$

This says the curl of the electric field equals the negative of the time-derivative of the magnetic flux density (with $j$ representing the 90° phase shift inherent in AC).

**Step 2 — Ampère's Law (Conduction Form):**

$$\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

This relates the curl of H-field to the conduction current density (no displacement current in good conductors at ECT frequencies).

**Step 3 — Take the Curl of Faraday's Law:**

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega \mu (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Vector Identity** $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$. For a uniform conductor with no free charge, $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu (\nabla \times \vec{H})$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Ampère's Law** $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \sigma \vec{E}$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega \mu \sigma \, \vec{E}$$

**Step 6 — This is the Helmholtz Equation in 1D.** For a semi-infinite conductor with field varying only with depth $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial x^2} = j\omega \mu \sigma \, E$$

**Step 7 — Assume Exponential Solution** of the form $E(x) = E_0 \, e^{\gamma x}$. Substituting:

$$\gamma^2 = j\omega \mu \sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma = \pm \sqrt{j\omega \mu \sigma}$$

**Step 8 — Apply Physical Boundary Condition:** Field must remain finite as $x \to \infty$, so we discard the positive exponential. The solution is:

$$E(x) = E_0 \, e^{-\gamma x} = E_0 \, e^{-\sqrt{j\omega \mu \sigma} \, x}$$

**Step 9 — Express $\sqrt{j}$ Using Euler's Identity.** Since $j = e^{j\pi/2}$:

$$\sqrt{j} = e^{j\pi/4} = \cos(\pi/4) + j\sin(\pi/4) = \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute Back:**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\omega \mu \sigma} \cdot \frac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}} = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

**Step 11 — Split into Real and Imaginary Parts:**

$$E(x) = E_0 \, \exp\left[-(1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}\, x\right]$$

**Step 12 — Factor the Exponential into Magnitude and Phase:**

$$E(x) = E_0 \, \underbrace{\exp\left[-x\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}\right]}_{\text{Amplitude decay}} \cdot \underbrace{\exp\left[-j\, x\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}\right]}_{\text{Phase lag}}$$

**Step 13 — Define the Standard Depth of Penetration.** The amplitude-decay coefficient is $1/\delta$, where:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\delta} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}} = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Step 14 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu \sigma}}}$$

**Step 15 — Phase Lag Interpretation.** From Step 12, the phase at depth $x$ is $\phi(x) = x\sqrt{\omega \mu \sigma/2} = x/\delta$. Thus **at $x = \delta$, the phase lag is exactly 1 radian (57.3°)** — confirming the alternate definition.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A non-magnetic aluminum test piece has conductivity $\sigma = 3.54 \times 10^7$ S/m and relative permeability $\mu_r = 1$. Find the standard depth of penetration at (a) 1 kHz, (b) 100 kHz, (c) 1 MHz. What frequency is needed for δ = 1 mm?

**Given:** $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m, $\sigma = 3.54 \times 10^7$ S/m.

**Step (a) — At $f = 1$ kHz:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 10^3 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.54 \times 10^7}}$$

**Step-by-step multiplication inside the square root:**

$$\pi f = 3.1416 \times 10^3 = 3141.6$$

$$\pi f \mu = 3141.6 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} = 3.948 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\pi f \mu \sigma = 3.948 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.54 \times 10^7 = 1.398 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\sqrt{1.398 \times 10^{5}} = 373.9 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \delta \approx 2.67 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 2.67\,\text{mm}$$

**Step (b) — At $f = 100$ kHz:** Frequency increased 100×, so δ decreases by $\sqrt{100} = 10$:

$$\delta = 2.67 / 10 = 0.267 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step (c) — At $f = 1$ MHz:** Frequency increased 1000× from (a), so δ decreases by $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62$:

$$\delta = 2.67 / 31.62 \approx 0.084 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step (d) — Find $f$ for $\delta = 1$ mm:**

$$\delta^2 = \frac{1}{\pi f \mu \sigma} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f = \frac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^2}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.54 \times 10^7 \times (10^{-3})^2}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{1.398 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1}{1.398 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7.15 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Hz} \approx 7.15\,\text{kHz}$$

**Verification:** $\delta(7.15\;\text{kHz}) = 2.67 \times \sqrt{1/7.15} = 2.67 \times 0.374 = 0.998 \approx 1$ mm ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Frequency vs. Depth Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Excitation in Probe Coil] --> B[Time-Varying Primary Magnetic Field H0 at Surface]
    B --> C[Faraday Induction: EMF Induced in Conductor]
    C --> D[Eddy Currents Circulate at Surface]
    D --> E{Field Propagates into Depth x}
    E --> F[Exponential Decay: J(x) = J0 exp-x by delta]
    F --> G[Phase Lag: theta x = x by delta radians]
    G --> H[delta = 1 over sqrt of pi f mu sigma]
    H --> I{Frequency Selection}
    I -->|High f| J[Small delta: Surface Defects]
    I -->|Low f| K[Large delta: Sub-surface Defects]
    H --> L{Material Property}
    L -->|High mu_r| M[Shallow Penetration: Ferromagnetic]
    L -->|High sigma| N[Shallow Penetration: Highly Conductive]
    L -->|Low mu_r and Low sigma| O[Deeper Penetration: Austenitic SS Titanium]
    J --> P[Effective Inspection Zone: 0 to 3 delta]
    K --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[95 percent of signal energy captured]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Decay of Eddy Current with Depth

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Surface x equals 0: J equals J0 100 percent] --> B[Depth x equals delta: J equals 0.368 J0]
    B --> C[Depth x equals 2 delta: J equals 0.135 J0]
    C --> D[Depth x equals 3 delta: J equals 0.05 J0]
    D --> E[Depth beyond 3 delta: Negligible Current]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

### 4.3 Parameter Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Frequency f] -->|Inverse square root| DELTA[Standard Depth delta]
    MU[Magnetic Permeability mu] -->|Inverse square root| DELTA
    SIG[Electrical Conductivity sigma] -->|Inverse square root| DELTA
    DELTA --> INS[Inspection Capability]
    F --> INS
    MU --> INS
    SIG --> INS
    INS --> SURF[Surface Crack Detection High f Small delta]
    INS --> SUB[Sub-surface Flaw Detection Low f Large delta]
    INS --> SORT[Material Sorting by Conductivity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

Define the term **"Standard Depth of Penetration"** in Eddy Current Testing. State its formula and explain the significance of the **$1/e$ factor**.

**Model Answer:**

Standard depth of penetration (δ) is the depth below the surface of a conducting material at which the eddy current density has decreased to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value, when the material is subjected to alternating magnetic excitation.

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Significance of 1/e factor:** It provides a *standardized reference* depth against which inspection performance is measured. Beyond 1δ, the eddy current magnitude drops to 36.8 %; by 3δ it is only 5 %. Hence, the practical inspection depth is taken as 0 to 3δ, where nearly 95 % of the useful signal resides.

*(3 marks — [1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark significance])*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

State the relationship between depth of penetration and frequency. How does doubling the frequency affect the depth of penetration in a given material?

**Model Answer:**

The relationship is **inverse square root**:

$$\delta \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{f}}$$

If frequency is doubled ($f \to 2f$), then:

$$\delta_{new} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2f\mu\sigma}} = \frac{\delta_{old}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot \delta_{old}$$

Thus, **doubling the frequency reduces δ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ (about 29.3 % reduction)**. This principle is exploited in selecting probe frequency — higher frequencies for surface defects, lower frequencies for sub-surface inspection.

*(3 marks — [1 mark relation, 1 mark calculation, 1 mark application])*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the standard depth of penetration in eddy current testing. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stainless steel (austenitic) tube has conductivity $\sigma = 1.4 \times 10^6$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$, and is inspected at $f = 50$ kHz. Calculate (i) the standard depth of penetration, (ii) the depth at which eddy current density is 10 % of surface value, (iii) the phase lag at depth 2 mm.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The conductor is **linear, isotropic, and homogeneous**.
2. **Quasi-static approximation** holds (displacement current negligible compared to conduction current at ECT frequencies).
3. The conductor is **semi-infinite** and **non-magnetic** (unless otherwise specified).
4. **No free charge accumulation**, so $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$.

**Derivation (Recap of Section 3.1):**

Starting from Maxwell's equations in the phasor domain, with no displacement current:

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega \mu \vec{H}, \quad \nabla \times \vec{H} = \sigma \vec{E}$$

Taking curl of Faraday's law and applying the vector identity, we obtain the Helmholtz equation:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = j\omega \mu \sigma \, \vec{E}$$

For 1D penetration ($x$-direction), the solution is $E(x) = E_0 \, e^{-\gamma x}$ where:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j\omega \mu \sigma}$$

Expressing $\sqrt{j} = (1+j)/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\gamma = (1+j)\sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}$$

The real part gives the **exponential decay** with characteristic length:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\omega \mu \sigma / 2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}$$

The imaginary part $(1+j)/\sqrt{2}$ shows that the eddy current also **suffers a phase shift** of $x/\delta$ radians relative to the surface.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $\sigma = 1.4 \times 10^6$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$, $f = 50$ kHz, $\mu = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

**Part (i) — Standard Depth of Penetration:**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 5 \times 10^4 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.4 \times 10^6}}$$

**Compute step by step:**

- $\pi f = 3.1416 \times 5 \times 10^4 = 1.5708 \times 10^5$
- $\pi f \mu = 1.5708 \times 10^5 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} = 1.974 \times 10^{-1}$
- $\pi f \mu \sigma = 0.1974 \times 1.4 \times 10^6 = 2.764 \times 10^5$
- $\sqrt{2.764 \times 10^5} = 525.7$

$$\delta = \frac{1}{525.7} = 1.902 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \approx 1.90\;\text{mm}$$

**Part (ii) — Depth at which $J(x) = 0.10 \, J_0$:**

Using $J(x) = J_0 \, e^{-x/\delta}$:

$$0.10 = e^{-x/\delta} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{\delta} = \ln(10) = 2.3026$$

$$x = 2.3026 \times 1.90 = 4.37\;\text{mm}$$

**Part (iii) — Phase lag at $x = 2$ mm:**

$$\theta = \frac{x}{\delta} = \frac{2}{1.90} = 1.053\;\text{radians} = 60.3°$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concept of depth of penetration and its variation with frequency in eddy current testing. Discuss the role of **ferromagnetic vs. non-magnetic** materials on penetration depth.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An inspector wishes to detect sub-surface defects in a brass plate (σ = 1.5 × 10⁷ S/m, μᵣ = 1) at a depth of 4 mm. Recommend a suitable inspection frequency. Justify your answer using the δ formula. Comment on what happens if the plate is replaced by a ferromagnetic steel of the same conductivity but $\mu_r = 100$.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Concept of Depth of Penetration (with sketch description):**

When a probe coil carrying AC is placed on a conductor, eddy currents are induced in closed loops within the conductor. The density of these currents is maximum at the surface ($J_0$) and decreases exponentially with depth. The **standard depth of penetration δ** is defined as the depth at which current density falls to $1/e$ of $J_0$.

*Sketch:*
```
   |  ↑ H (primary field)
   |  ↓
   ▼
  ┌───────────  Conductor Surface (x = 0)
  │  ████ J0 (100 %)
  │  ▓▓▓▓  at x = 1δ  →  36.8 %  (phase lag = 57.3°)
  │  ░░░░  at x = 2δ  →  13.5 %  (phase lag = 114.6°)
  │  .....  at x = 3δ  →  5 %     (phase lag = 171.9°)
  ▼
```

**Variation with Frequency:**

$$\delta \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{f}}$$

- At **low f** (e.g., 100 Hz): δ is large → deep penetration → sub-surface flaws visible.
- At **high f** (e.g., 1 MHz): δ is small → shallow penetration → surface flaws visible.

**Effect of Material Type:**

Since $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{\mu_r \sigma}$:
- **Ferromagnetic materials** (high μᵣ, e.g., steel with μᵣ = 100–500): δ is **drastically reduced**. The high permeability concentrates flux at the surface, limiting penetration.
- **Non-magnetic materials** (μᵣ = 1, e.g., aluminum, copper, austenitic SS): δ is **larger** for the same frequency and conductivity, allowing deeper inspection.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $\sigma = 1.5 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu_r = 1$, desired inspection depth ≈ 4 mm.

**Step 1 — Recall that practical inspection depth ≈ 3δ.** To detect a flaw at 4 mm, we need 3δ ≥ 4 mm, i.e., δ ≥ 1.33 mm. Choose δ = 1.5 mm (a small safety margin).

**Step 2 — Solve for frequency from the δ formula:**

$$f = \frac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^2}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.5 \times 10^7 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

**Compute numerator:** 1 (constant)

**Compute denominator step by step:**
- $3.1416 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 3.948 \times 10^{-6}$
- $3.948 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5 \times 10^7 = 59.22$
- $(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}$
- $59.22 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.332 \times 10^{-4}$

$$f = \frac{1}{1.332 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7.51 \times 10^3\;\text{Hz} \approx 7.5\;\text{kHz}$$

**Recommended Frequency:** Approximately **7.5 kHz** (a practical choice might be 8 kHz, a standard test frequency).

**Step 3 — Effect of Replacing Brass with Ferromagnetic Steel (μᵣ = 100):**

The new $\mu = 100 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$. The depth of penetration becomes:

$$\delta_{steel} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu_r \mu_0 \sigma}} = \frac{\delta_{brass}}{\sqrt{\mu_r}} = \frac{1.5\;\text{mm}}{\sqrt{100}} = \frac{1.5}{10} = 0.15\;\text{mm}$$

**Result:** Penetration depth **drops by a factor of 10** (from 1.5 mm to 0.15 mm). The same 7.5 kHz frequency would now be **totally inadequate** for inspecting 4 mm depth. To compensate, frequency must be **reduced by a factor of 100** (to ~75 Hz), which may pose other practical challenges such as low signal-to-noise ratio.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/e:** Many students write "depth at which current becomes zero" — this is **wrong**. The current never becomes zero; it decays asymptotically. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error.
> 2. **Confusing $\delta$ with effective depth:** Examiners often ask "up to what depth can you inspect?" The correct answer is **3δ** (≈ 95 % energy), not δ.
> 3. **Linear proportionality trap:** A common mistake is writing $\delta \propto 1/f$. It is $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$. Loss of 1 mark.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in numerical problems:** Always express δ in mm when dealing with engineering thicknesses. Mixing SI units (m) and practical units (mm) leads to calculation errors.
> 5. **Forgetting the phase-lag definition:** Examiners often give 2 marks for stating that at depth δ, the phase lag is 1 radian. Don't skip this in derivations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Standard Depth of Penetration (δ):** Depth at which eddy current density drops to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value. *(1 mark favourite question)*
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$
- ✅ **Key Relation:** $\delta \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}}$ — this is the most-tested KTU relation in this module.
- ✅ **Doubling f → δ reduces by $\sqrt{2}$; Quadrupling f → δ reduces by 2.**
- ✅ **Practical inspection depth = 3δ** (covers ~95 % of signal).
- ✅ **Phase lag at depth δ = 1 radian (57.3°)** — dual definition of δ.
- ✅ **Ferromagnetic materials (high μᵣ) drastically reduce δ** — limit ECT applicability.
- ✅ **Assumptions in derivation:** Linear, isotropic, homogeneous conductor; quasi-static (no displacement current); semi-infinite body; no free charge.
- ✅ **Frequency selection rule of thumb:**
  - **Surface defects** → **high f** (small δ)
  - **Sub-surface defects** → **low f** (large δ)
- ✅ **Typical inspection frequencies:** 100 Hz to 10 MHz; tube inspection often 5 kHz – 500 kHz.
- ✅ **Conductivity effect:** Higher σ → smaller δ → shallower penetration (e.g., copper vs. titanium).
- ✅ **Lift-off effect:** This is a separate phenomenon from δ — caused by probe-to-specimen gap, not depth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment and Accessories for ECT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equipment and Accessories for ECT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** is an electromagnetic Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) method that employs the principle of **electromagnetic induction** to detect surface and sub-surface defects, measure material properties (conductivity, permeability, hardness), and determine coating thickness on electrically conductive materials.

The **equipment and accessories for ECT** collectively refer to the complete instrumentation chain required to generate, inject, sense, condition, and display the eddy current response from a test specimen. The major functional blocks are:

| Functional Block | Primary Role |
|---|---|
| Eddy Current Instrument | Generates AC excitation, processes coil impedance, displays response |
| Probe / Test Coil | Produces primary magnetic field & senses secondary field |
| Reference Standards | Calibrates the instrument & validates flaw response |
| Cables & Connectors | Transmit signals with minimum noise & impedance mismatch |
| Auxiliary Accessories | Aid in handling, scanning, de-magnetizing, cleaning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the ESE (End Semester Examination), questions on equipment are frequently asked with sub-parts requesting **diagrammatic explanation of probe construction** and **functional description of each block**. Memorize the role of every accessory, not just the instrument.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Metal Detector at the Beach"**
> Imagine swinging a metal detector over sand. When the coil (loop) approaches a buried coin, the detector **beeps** because the coin alters the magnetic field generated by the coil. The detector cannot "see" the coin; instead, it senses the **change in electrical behavior** of its own coil caused by the coin's presence.
> - The **detector head** → Probe / Test Coil
> - The **control box with knob & display** → ECT Instrument
> - The **coin itself** → Flaw or material property change
> - The **calibration piece (a known sample kept in your pocket to "tune" the device)** → Reference Standard
>
> ECT equipment works on the *exact same principle* — it indirectly "feels" defects by monitoring how the coil's impedance shifts when eddy currents are disturbed.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- Permeability of free space: **μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- Typical test frequency range: **100 Hz to 10 MHz**
- Standard depth of penetration (δ) for aluminum at 100 kHz: **≈ 0.08 mm**
- ASTM standards governing ECT equipment: **E127, E566, E709, E1004, E1033, E1312**

---

## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the Equipment Chain Works

**Step 1 — Excitation (Signal Generation)**
The instrument's **oscillator/signal generator** produces a sinusoidal alternating current (typically 1 kHz – 10 MHz). This current drives the **test coil**, generating a time-varying primary magnetic field around it (governed by Faraday's Law).

**Step 2 — Field Interaction (Probe ↔ Specimen)**
When the probe approaches a conductive specimen, the changing primary field induces **eddy currents** (closed loops of induced current) inside the test piece. The eddy currents flow in closed paths and generate their own **secondary magnetic field** that opposes the primary (per Lenz's Law).

**Step 3 — Impedance Sensing (Coil Pickup)**
The secondary field reacts back on the test coil, changing its **impedance**:

$$Z_{coil} = R + j\omega L$$

where the change in resistance (R) and reactance (ωL) carry information about:
- Material conductivity & permeability
- Flaw presence (cracks, voids, inclusions)
- Probe lift-off
- Edge effects (boundaries)

**Step 4 — Signal Conditioning**
The instrument amplifies, demodulates, filters, and phase-shifts the impedance signal. The **phase-sensitive detector** separates the real (resistive) and imaginary (reactive) components.

**Step 5 — Display**
The result is plotted on the **Impedance Plane Display** (Lissajous pattern):
- Horizontal axis → Resistive component (R)
- Vertical axis → Reactive component (jωL)

Different defects, lift-off variations, and material changes trace different characteristic loops on this plane, enabling discrimination.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why" Behind It)

| Step | Reason It Matters |
|---|---|
| Excitation | Frequency choice controls **depth of penetration** — higher freq → surface, lower freq → subsurface |
| Field Interaction | The **coupling** between coil & specimen is the source of all useful information |
| Impedance Sensing | Without precise impedance measurement, defect signals are indistinguishable from noise |
| Signal Conditioning | Removes drift, lift-off noise, temperature variation |
| Display | Provides a **visual signature** for operator interpretation |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All symbols must be memorized for Part A & Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Description / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | Standard depth of penetration (m) — fundamental for frequency selection |
| 2 | $J_x = J_0 \, e^{-x/\delta}$ | Eddy current density decay with depth |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{x}{\delta}$ | Phase lag of eddy current at depth x (radians) |
| 4 | $Z_{coil} = R + j\omega L$ | Coil impedance (essential for impedance-plane analysis) |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{d^2}{D^2}$ | Fill factor — d = specimen dia, D = coil ID |
| 6 | $f_{opt} \approx \dfrac{1}{\mu \sigma d^2}$ | Optimal frequency for tubular inspection (Hz) |
| 7 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\omega L}{R}$ | Phase angle θ of coil impedance |
| 8 | $\mu_r = \dfrac{B}{B_0}$ | Relative permeability (used for ferrous inspection) |

Where:
- $f$ = test frequency (Hz)
- $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$ = absolute permeability (H/m)
- $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity (S/m)
- $d$ = specimen diameter
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ECT equipment is used in:
- **Aerospace industry** — inspection of aircraft fuselage, wing skins, turbine blades (for fatigue cracks)
- **Nuclear power plants** — in-service inspection of steam generator tubes (bobbin + rotating probes)
- **Petrochemical industry** — inspection of heat-exchanger tubes (down to ID)
- **Manufacturing** — sorting of alloys based on conductivity (Al vs Cu vs Brass)
- **Coating thickness** — non-conductive coatings on conductive substrates (paint thickness on aluminum)
- **Hardness testing** — correlation between conductivity and hardness in heat-treated alloys

---

## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Standard Depth of Penetration (δ)

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations**
For a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in a good conductor, the magnetic field intensity decays exponentially:

$$H(x) = H_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$$

where $\alpha$ is the attenuation constant. For a conductor:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

**Defining the Standard Depth of Penetration**
By definition, the depth at which the eddy current density drops to $\dfrac{1}{e}$ (≈ 36.8%) of its surface value is called the **standard depth of penetration**, δ.

Setting:

$$\alpha \cdot \delta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

**Final Compact Form (Standard Reference):**

$$
\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}
$$

**Explanation:**
- When $f$ increases, $\delta$ decreases → surface defects only
- When $\sigma$ increases (better conductor), $\delta$ decreases → skin effect is stronger
- When $\mu$ increases (ferromagnetic), $\delta$ decreases significantly

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Frequency Selection for Aluminum

**Problem:** Determine the test frequency required to inspect aluminum to a depth of **2 mm** below the surface.

**Given Data:**
- Conductivity of aluminum: $\sigma_{Al} = 3.45 \times 10^7$ S/m
- Relative permeability: $\mu_r = 1$ (non-magnetic)
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- Required depth: x = 2 mm = $2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Rule: choose frequency such that δ ≈ x (or δ ≈ 2x for practical inspection)

**Step 1 — Compute Absolute Permeability**

$$\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 1 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H/m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into δ Formula**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

Solving for f:

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug in Numerical Values**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{\pi \times (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.45 \times 10^{7}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}
$$

Compute denominator step by step:

- $\pi \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} = 3.948 \times 10^{-6}$
- $3.948 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.45 \times 10^{7} = 136.21$
- $\delta^{2} = (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$
- Denominator $= 136.21 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 5.448 \times 10^{-4}$

Therefore:

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{5.448 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1835 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Recommendation**

> [!NOTE]
> Practical ECT uses a frequency such that δ is approximately **2 to 3 times** the required inspection depth to allow signal return from the flaw. Using δ = 2x → $f \approx 460$ Hz; using δ = x → f ≈ 1.8 kHz.
> **Engineering compromise:** Use a frequency in the range **1 kHz – 2 kHz** for this case.

**Answer:** For 2 mm deep inspection in aluminum, a test frequency of approximately **1.8 kHz** is theoretically required (standard depth equal to required depth).

### 3.3 Impedance Plane Diagram — Numerical Interpretation

**Problem:** A surface probe is scanned over an aluminum plate containing a 5 mm long, 1 mm deep surface crack. The impedance plane shows the response loop. Determine the approximate phase angle between the lift-off signal and the crack signal.

**Given:** Crack signal appears rotated by **75°** counter-clockwise from the lift-off vector (which is conventionally along the horizontal axis).

**Solution:**
Phase angle between signals:

$$
\Delta\phi = 75^{o}
$$

Since lift-off produces a predominantly resistive change, while cracks produce both resistive and reactive components, the angular separation between them indicates **good discrimination**.

---

## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Complete ECT Equipment Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[Oscillator Signal Generator] --> AMP1[Power Amplifier]
    AMP1 --> COIL[Test Probe Coil]
    COIL -->|<Induced Eddy Currents>| SPEC[Test Specimen]
    SPEC -->|<Secondary Field Back-reaction| COIL
    COIL --> BRG[Bridge Balancing Circuit]
    BRG --> PSD[Phase Sensitive Detector]
    PSD --> FILT[Filter and Amplifier Stage]
    FILT --> CRT[Impedance Plane Display CRT or LCD]
    FILT --> MTR[Strip Chart Recorder]
    FILT --> ALARM[Threshold Alarm]
    OSC --> REF[Reference Standard for Calibration]
    REF --> CAL[Calibration of Phase Angle and Gain]
    CAL --> BRG
```

**Interpretation:**
1. Oscillator generates sinusoidal excitation (100 Hz – 10 MHz)
2. Power amplifier drives the test coil
3. Test coil induces eddy currents in specimen
4. Specimen's secondary field alters coil impedance
5. Bridge circuit converts impedance change to voltage
6. Phase-sensitive detector separates real & imaginary parts
7. Filtered signal drives display, recorder, alarm
8. Reference standards calibrate phase & amplitude

### 4.2 Probe Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Specimen Geometry and Defect Type] --> Q1{Location of Defect}
    Q1 -->|Surface on Flat Plate| P1[Pancake Surface Probe]
    Q1 -->|Surface on Curved Tube| P2[Encircling Probe]
    Q1 -->|Inner Surface of Tube| P3[Bobbin Internal Probe]
    Q1 -->|ID Bore of Tube| P4[Rotating Eddy Current Probe]
    Q1 -->|Inside Small Opening| P5[Pencil Probe]
    Q1 -->|Spot Inspection| P6[Spring Loaded Probe]

    P1 --> Q2{Flaw Type}
    Q2 -->|Crack Differentiation Needed| D1[Differential Probe]
    Q2 -->|Absolute Measurement Needed| D2[Absolute Probe]
    Q2 -->|Through Transmission| D3[Reflection Send Receive Probe]
```

### 4.3 Reference Standard Construction (ASTM E127 Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BLK[Aluminum Reference Block 25 mm thick] --> HB1[Flat Bottom Hole 0.4 mm dia at 0.4 mm depth]
    BLK --> HB2[Flat Bottom Hole 0.8 mm dia at 0.8 mm depth]
    BLK --> HB3[Flat Bottom Hole 1.6 mm dia at 1.6 mm depth]
    BLK --> HB4[Flat Bottom Hole 3.2 mm dia at 3.2 mm depth]
    BLK --> SLOT[EDM Notch 0.2 mm wide x 5 mm long x various depths]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **ASTM E127** reference blocks contain flat-bottomed holes (FBH) of graded diameters at graded depths. They are used to **calibrate sensitivity, depth, and phase** of an ECT system on non-ferrous alloys (typically aluminum).

---

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any six essential accessories used in eddy current testing and state the purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (6 Points — 3 Marks):**

| # | Accessory | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reference Standard / Calibration Block | Calibrates instrument sensitivity, phase, and depth response using known artificial flaws |
| 2 | Probe Cables (Coaxial) | Transmits high-frequency signals from probe to instrument with minimum noise pickup |
| 3 | Demagnetizer | Removes residual magnetism after inspection of ferromagnetic parts |
| 4 | Probe Positioner / Scanner | Maintains consistent lift-off and traverses probe at constant speed |
| 5 | Cleaning Materials (Acetone, lint-free cloth) | Removes oil, dirt, oxides that interfere with eddy current coupling |
| 6 | Conductivity Meter | Verifies the conductivity of reference specimens for material sorting |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between absolute and differential probes in ECT.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form — 3 Marks):**

| Feature | Absolute Probe | Differential Probe |
|---|---|---|
| Coils | Single coil | Two identical coils side-by-side |
| Output | Measures total impedance change | Measures **difference** between two coils |
| Lift-off response | High — sensitive to lift-off | Minimal — common-mode lift-off cancelled |
| Use | Conductivity, coating thickness, lift-off | Crack detection, edge detection |
| Display trace | Single point / curve | Butterfly loop signature |
| Sensitivity to small flaws | Moderate | Very high |

---
