### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional operation of a complete eddy current testing instrument. Label each block and state its role. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Derive the standard depth of penetration formula and explain how frequency selection is carried out for inspecting aluminum to a depth of 5 mm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

The complete ECT instrument consists of the following functional blocks:

**Block 1 — Oscillator (1 mark)**
Generates a stable sinusoidal AC signal in the range 100 Hz to 10 MHz. The frequency is selectable to control depth of penetration. A crystal-controlled oscillator provides high stability.

**Block 2 — Power Amplifier (1 mark)**
Boosts the oscillator signal to a level sufficient to drive the test coil, providing adequate magnetic field strength for inducing eddy currents in the specimen.

**Block 3 — Bridge Balancing Circuit (1 mark)**
The test coil forms one arm of an AC bridge. When impedance changes (due to flaw or material variation), the bridge becomes unbalanced and produces an error voltage proportional to the change.

**Block 4 — Phase-Sensitive Detector (1 mark)**
Demodulates the bridge output using a reference signal from the oscillator. It separates the signal into **in-phase (resistive, R)** and **quadrature (reactive, jωL)** components.

**Block 5 — Filter and Amplifier Stage (1 mark)**
Removes high-frequency noise, drift, and unwanted harmonics. Amplifies the demodulated signal to a level suitable for display.

**Block 6 — Display Devices (1 mark)**
Includes:
- **Impedance Plane Display** (X-Y plot of R vs jωL)
- **Strip Chart Recorder** (time-based trace)
- **Digital Meter** (numerical amplitude readout)
- **Threshold Alarm** (audio/visual indication when defect exceeds set limit)

**Block 7 — Reference Standard (1 mark)**
ASTM E127-type aluminum block with flat-bottomed holes is used to calibrate phase angle, sensitivity, and to verify instrument performance before testing.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Starting from the electromagnetic wave equation in a conducting medium, the magnetic field intensity decays as:

$$H(x) = H_0 e^{-\alpha x}$$

For a good conductor:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

By definition, the standard depth of penetration δ is the depth at which H drops to 1/e of its surface value:

$$\alpha \delta = 1$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}
$$

**Numerical — Frequency for 5 mm depth in Aluminum (3 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Required inspection depth: x = 5 mm = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Rule of thumb: choose δ ≈ 2x (to allow signal return) → δ = 10 mm = $10 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Aluminum: $\sigma = 3.45 \times 10^7$ S/m
- $\mu_r = 1$ → $\mu = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m

**Solving for f:**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^{2}}
$$

Compute numerator and denominator:

$$
\pi \mu \sigma = 3.1416 \times (1.2566 \times 10^{-6}) \times (3.45 \times 10^7}) = 136.21
$$

$$
\delta^{2} = (10 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{136.21 \times 1 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.01362} \approx 73.4 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Conclusion:** For practical inspection of aluminum to 5 mm depth, a frequency of approximately **70–200 Hz** is appropriate (depending on sensitivity required).

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Classify eddy current probes based on construction and application. Describe the construction and working of any three types in detail. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** What are reference standards? Describe the construction of an ASTM E127 reference block and explain the procedure for calibrating an ECT instrument using it. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification (2 Marks):**

Based on **construction:**
1. Absolute Probe (single coil)
2. Differential Probe (two coils)
3. Reflection / Send-Receive Probe (two coils, one transmit, one receive)

Based on **application / geometry:**
1. Surface Probe (Pancake)
2. Encircling Probe
3. Internal / Bobbin Probe
4. Rotating Probe
5. Pencil Probe
6. Spring-loaded Probe

**Detailed Description of Three Types (5 Marks — distribute as 2 + 2 + 1):**

**(i) Absolute Probe — 2 Marks**

Construction:
- A single coil wound on a non-conductive former (typically plastic or ceramic)
- Coil is energized directly by the instrument
- Connected via coaxial cable to the bridge circuit

Working:
- When the probe approaches a specimen, eddy currents are induced.
- These eddy currents produce a secondary field that alters the coil's impedance.
- The change is measured as a single absolute value.
- Best for: conductivity measurement, coating thickness, material sorting, lift-off detection.

**(ii) Differential Probe — 2 Marks**

Construction:
- Two identical coils wound on the same former, side-by-side (a few mm apart).
- Connected in a bridge circuit in opposite arms.

Working:
- When the probe is over a uniform region, both coils see the same impedance → balanced output → no signal.
- When a flaw passes under one coil but not the other, the bridge becomes unbalanced → produces a sharp signal.
- Common-mode signals (lift-off, temperature drift) are cancelled.
- Best for: detecting small cracks, edges, discontinuities.

**(iii) Encircling Probe — 1 Mark**

Construction:
- A coil wound on a circular former, through which the test piece (rod, tube, wire) passes axially.
- Diameter of the coil is slightly larger than the specimen diameter.

Working:
- Specimen acts as the secondary "single-turn coil" coupled to the encircling primary.
- Any defect, variation in diameter, hardness, or conductivity is detected.
- Used for in-line inspection of rods and tubes during manufacturing.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Reference Standards — Definition (1 Mark):**
Reference standards are test specimens with **known, reproducible artificial flaws** (or known material properties) used to calibrate, verify, and document the performance of an ECT instrument.

**ASTM E127 Reference Block Construction (3 Marks):**

- **Material:** Aluminum alloy (typically 2024 or 7075) — chosen for stable conductivity
- **Dimensions:** Approximately 25 mm thick, 75 × 100 mm or similar
- **Artificial Flaws:** Contains **flat-bottomed holes (FBH)** of graded diameters and depths:
  - Diameters: 0.4 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.6 mm, 3.2 mm
  - Depths: 0.4 mm, 0.8 mm, 1.6 mm, 3.2 mm, 6.4 mm
- **Optional:** EDM notches of varying depths for crack detection calibration

The block is engraved with identification numbers for traceability.

**Calibration Procedure (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Clean the reference block thoroughly to remove oils and oxides.

**Step 2:** Select the test frequency appropriate for the material and depth of interest.

**Step 3:** Place the probe on a defect-free region of the block. Adjust the **phase** (rotates the impedance loop) and **balance** (centers the trace on the screen).

**Step 4:** Scan the probe over each flat-bottomed hole. Note the **amplitude** and **phase angle** of the response on the impedance plane.

**Step 5:** Plot a calibration curve: **amplitude vs. hole diameter** at a fixed depth, and **phase angle vs. hole depth** at a fixed diameter.

**Step 6:** Use this calibration to interpret unknown flaw responses in the actual test specimen.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing absolute & differential probes** — A common error is describing both as if they had only one coil. Always clarify coil count.
> 2. **Wrong units in penetration formula** — Students often write $\mu$ in F/m instead of H/m. Use **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** strictly.
> 3. **Forgetting fill factor** in encircling coil problems — Without $\eta = d^2/D^2$, full coupling questions cannot be solved.
> 4. **ASTM E127 = aluminum only** — Do not claim it works for steel; for ferrous materials, separate standards (E709, E1004) apply.
> 5. **Skipping diagram** — A block diagram in 7-mark questions is almost mandatory. Lose 1–2 marks if absent.
> 6. **Not stating practical frequency rule** — Examiners expect mention of "frequency such that δ ≈ 2x to 3x" for real inspections.

---

### TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **ECT works on electromagnetic induction** — primary field induces eddy currents, which produce a secondary field opposing the primary.
- **Standard depth of penetration formula** (memorize): $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$
- **Higher frequency → shallower penetration, greater surface sensitivity.**
- **Lower frequency → deeper penetration, lower sensitivity.**
- **Major Equipment Chain:** Oscillator → Amplifier → Probe → Bridge → Phase Detector → Filter → Display
- **Probe Types (Must Memorize):** Absolute, Differential, Reflection, Pancake, Encircling, Bobbin, Rotating, Pencil
- **Differential probes** cancel lift-off, ideal for cracks.
- **Absolute probes** measure total impedance, ideal for conductivity & coating thickness.
- **Encircling probes** are used for tubes, rods, wires in-line.
- **Bobbin probes** are inserted into tubes (ID inspection).
- **Rotating probes** scan tube circumferentially for high-speed inspection.
- **Reference Standards (ASTM E127)** are aluminum blocks with graded flat-bottomed holes (0.4 to 3.2 mm diameter, 0.4 to 6.4 mm depth).
- **Fill factor** for encircling coils: $\eta = d^{2}/D^{2}$ — coupling efficiency.
- **Optimal frequency** for tubular inspection: $f_{opt} \approx \dfrac{1}{\mu \sigma d^{2}}$
- **Impedance plane display:** X-axis = R (resistive), Y-axis = jωL (reactive).
- **Lift-off signal** lies along one direction on the impedance plane; **crack signal** is rotated typically 60°–90° from lift-off.
- **Phase separation** allows distinguishing lift-off, cracks, conductivity changes, and thickness changes.
- **Key Standards:** ASTM E127 (Al reference), ASTM E566 (non-ferrous tubes), ASTM E709 (ferrous), ASTM E1004 (bolts, studs), ASTM E1033 (flat products).
- **Typical frequency range:** 100 Hz to 10 MHz.
- **Conductivity of aluminum** = $3.45 \times 10^{7}$ S/m (memorize for numericals).
- **Conductivity of copper** = $5.80 \times 10^{7}$ S/m.
- **Permeability of free space** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- **Real-world use:** Aerospace, nuclear, petrochemical tubes, alloy sorting, coating thickness.
- **Accessories to remember:** Reference standards, cables, demagnetizer, scanner, cleaning kit, conductivity meter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- (Content already delivered in Section 2 above) -->

topics.title: Applications of ECT: Conductivity Measurement, Hardness Measurement, Defect Detection, and Coating Thickness Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of ECT: Conductivity Measurement, Hardness Measurement, Defect Detection, and Coating Thickness Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Eddy Current Testing (ECT)?

**Eddy Current Testing (ECT)** is an electromagnetic Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) technique that uses the principle of electromagnetic induction to inspect electrically conductive materials. When an alternating current (AC) is passed through a coil (probe), it generates a primary alternating magnetic field. When this probe is brought near a conductive test piece, eddy currents are induced in the material. Any change in the test piece—its conductivity, permeability, geometry, or the presence of flaws—alters the eddy current flow, which in turn changes the probe's impedance. This impedance change is monitored to extract material properties or defect information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET636 Module 2):**
> ECT is classified as an *electromagnetic induction–based* NDT method. It is restricted to **electrically conductive materials** (metals and alloys) and is primarily sensitive to **surface and near-surface** conditions (typically up to a few millimeters depth, governed by the *standard depth of penetration*).

### 1.2 The Four Major Applications of ECT

The topic covers **four** prime industrial applications of ECT:

| # | Application | What it Measures | Typical Use |
|---|-------------|------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Conductivity Measurement** | Electrical conductivity (% IACS) | Material sorting, heat treatment verification |
| 2 | **Hardness Measurement** | Hardness (indirect, via conductivity) | QC of aluminum alloy heat treatment |
| 3 | **Defect Detection** | Cracks, voids, inclusions, corrosion | Surface/near-surface flaw detection |
| 4 | **Coating Thickness Measurement** | Thickness of non-conductive/conductive coatings | Paint, anodize, plating thickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Metal Detector on Steroids" Idea:**
> Imagine walking along a beach with a metal detector. The detector sends out an invisible magnetic "buzz." When you sweep over a buried coin, the buzz changes pitch. Eddy current testing works exactly the same way, but with a much more sophisticated "buzz" (an alternating electromagnetic field). Instead of just hearing a beep, the instrument measures the *exact* change in electrical "resistance and reactance" of its sensing coil. That change is decoded into one of four things: **how well the metal conducts electricity, how hard it is, whether a crack is hiding inside, or how thick a paint layer is covering it**. The eddy currents are like tiny invisible whirlpools swirling just under the metal surface—anything that disturbs those whirlpools (a crack, a soft spot, a paint film) gets noticed.

### 1.3 The Standard Depth of Penetration (δ)

The **standard depth of penetration** $\delta$ is the single most important parameter in ECT. It defines the depth at which the eddy current density has decreased to **1/e (≈ 36.8 %)** of its surface value.

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \, f \, \mu \, \sigma}}
$$

Where:
- $\delta$ = standard depth of penetration in **meters (m)**
- $f$ = test frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**
- $\mu$ = magnetic permeability of the test material in **H/m**
- $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity of the test material in **S/m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Memory Hook:** Doubling the frequency cuts the depth of penetration by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. Quadrupling the frequency cuts the depth by a factor of **2**. Higher frequency = shallower inspection.

### 1.4 Units of Conductivity — % IACS

The international benchmark for conductivity is **% IACS** (International Annealed Copper Standard), where **100 % IACS = 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m** at 20 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **% IACS Quick Reference:**
> - Pure Copper (annealed): 100 % IACS
> - Pure Aluminum (annealed): ≈ 61 % IACS
> - Titanium: ≈ 1.8 % IACS
> - Stainless Steel (austenitic, 304): ≈ 1.4 % IACS
> - Brass (typical): ≈ 28 % IACS
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The Four-Step Logic

ECT always follows this four-step logical sequence:

1. **Excite** the probe coil with an AC signal → creates primary magnetic field.
2. **Induce** eddy currents in the test piece (Lenz's law) → creates secondary magnetic field opposing the primary.
3. **Interact** the secondary field with the primary → modulates probe coil impedance $Z = R + j\omega L$.
4. **Display / Decode** the impedance change on a meter, impedance plane, or digital readout.

### 2.2 Application 1 — Conductivity Measurement

#### 2.2.1 The "Why" Behind Conductivity Measurement

Every metallic alloy has a unique conductivity signature based on its **chemical composition, heat treatment, cold work, and aging condition**. ECT can read this signature non-destructively. Conductivity measurement is so sensitive that it can detect:
- Wrong alloy grade (e.g., 2024 vs 2014 aluminum)
- Inadequate heat treatment (over-aged, under-aged T6 condition)
- Heat damage from fire or improper thermal exposure
- Cold work percentage and residual stress

#### 2.2.2 The Theory

For a probe held at constant lift-off, the coil impedance $Z$ is a function of $\sigma$, $\mu$, $f$, and the coil geometry. When $\mu$ is held constant (non-ferromagnetic material) and $f$ is fixed:

$$
Z = f(\sigma) \quad \text{(single-valued function for a calibrated probe)}
$$

The instrument is calibrated using **known conductivity reference standards** (e.g., 8.0, 14.0, 30.0, 62.0 % IACS blocks). A linear or polynomial fit gives:

$$
\sigma_{\text{meas}} = a + b \cdot V_{\text{out}}
$$

Where $V_{\text{out}}$ is the instrument's analog/digital output and $a, b$ are calibration constants.

#### 2.2.3 Frequency Selection for Conductivity

To maximize conductivity sensitivity while minimizing lift-off and edge effects, a **frequency-to-conductivity ratio rule** is used:

$$
f \approx 4 \times \sigma \quad \text{(for absolute surface probes)}
$$

For example, for an aluminum alloy with $\sigma = 30 \,\%$ IACS, an optimum test frequency is around **f ≈ 120 kHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration Discipline:** Conductivity measurements require a **two-point** (minimum) or **three-point** calibration on certified reference blocks of known % IACS. The calibration must be redone if test frequency is changed or if probe temperature drifts significantly.

### 2.3 Application 2 — Hardness Measurement (Indirect Method)

#### 2.3.1 The "Why" — Why Does Conductivity Relate to Hardness?

For **non-ferromagnetic alloys** (especially aluminum alloys, copper alloys, brasses, and some stainless grades), there is a well-established **empirical correlation** between hardness and electrical conductivity. This is because:

- Solution heat treatment followed by aging changes the **precipitate distribution** in the alloy matrix.
- Fine, coherent precipitates (GP zones, θ″) increase hardness by impeding dislocation motion.
- These same precipitates perturb the free electron flow, modifying conductivity.
- Thus: **Hardness ↑ ↔ Conductivity ↓** (in most age-hardenable Al alloys).

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** ECT hardness correlation works **only** for non-ferromagnetic materials where hardness changes are primarily driven by precipitation hardening or microstructural changes that also affect conductivity. It is **not** a direct, universal method. The calibration curve must be developed for the **specific alloy, temper, and geometry** being tested.

#### 2.3.2 The Calibration Relationship

The ECT-measured conductivity is correlated to hardness through an empirical curve:

$$
H = c_0 + c_1 \sigma + c_2 \sigma^2
$$

Where:
- $H$ = hardness (e.g., Rockwell B, Brinell HB, or Vickers HV)
- $\sigma$ = conductivity in % IACS
- $c_0, c_1, c_2$ = experimentally determined coefficients (alloy-specific)

### 2.4 Application 3 — Defect Detection

#### 2.4.1 The "Why" — How Does a Crack Disturb Eddy Currents?

A surface or near-surface defect (crack, void, inclusion, lap, delamination) acts as a **barrier** to the flow of eddy currents. The eddy currents must detour around the defect, which:
- **Reduces the effective cross-sectional area** available for current flow
- **Alters the local magnetic field** sensed by the probe
- Produces a **characteristic impedance change** that is a function of defect size, orientation, and location

The **best detection sensitivity** is achieved when the defect is oriented **perpendicular to the eddy current flow direction**. Defects parallel to the current flow are nearly invisible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defect Orientation Rule:** Eddy currents flow in loops parallel to the surface beneath the probe. Cracks running perpendicular to these loops (i.e., transverse to the probe's scan direction for a circular probe) produce maximum signal.

#### 2.4.2 The Impedance Plane (Lissajous Pattern)

A **two-axis impedance display** plots coil resistance $R$ on the horizontal axis and inductive reactance $\omega L$ on the vertical axis. As the probe is scanned:
- **Lift-off** (vertical probe motion) traces a curved line
- **Frequency** change traces another curve
- **Defects** cause point deflections on this plane
- **Conductivity changes** shift the position on the plane

This is the foundation of modern ECT defect analysis.

#### 2.4.3 Key Defect Parameters Detected

| Defect Type | Detectability | Notes |
|-------------|---------------|-------|
| Surface cracks | Excellent | Best with high-frequency probes |
| Near-surface cracks | Good | Frequency-tuned to defect depth |
| Sub-surface defects | Limited | Penetration depth is the limit |
| Corrosion thinning | Fair–Good | Requires calibration on known thickness |
| Lap, fold, delamination | Good | Causes strong conductivity discontinuity |
| Welds (surface) | Good | Weld profile + defect detection |

### 2.5 Application 4 — Coating Thickness Measurement

#### 2.5.1 The "Why" — Two Distinct Modes

ECT can measure coating thickness in two fundamentally different ways:

**Mode A: Non-conductive Coating on Conductive Substrate** (most common)
- Example: paint, anodize, plastic, ceramic coating on aluminum or steel
- The non-conductive coating acts as a **lift-off spacer** between probe and substrate.
- As coating thickness increases, probe lift-off increases.
- Lift-off is a strong, monotonic function of the gap.

**Mode B: Conductive Coating on Less-Conductive Substrate**
- Example: copper or aluminum coating on plastic, ceramic, or non-conductive composite
- The conductive coating carries the eddy currents.
- Coating thickness affects effective conductivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Mode A:** The substrate must be **electrically conductive**. Mode A is widely used in the aerospace industry to verify anodized aluminum coating thickness on aircraft skins (typical 5–50 μm).

#### 2.5.2 The Lift-Off Curve and Its Use

For a given probe and frequency, the coil impedance varies with lift-off in a characteristic curve called the **lift-off curve**. The slope of this curve is very steep at small lift-offs, providing excellent sensitivity for thin coatings (a few micrometers). The slope decreases with thickness, so sensitivity falls off for thicker coatings.

#### 2.5.3 Mathematical Model

For a non-conductive coating of thickness $t_c$ on a conductive substrate, the effective lift-off is:

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = L_0 + t_c
$$

Where $L_0$ is the baseline lift-off (probe body clearance). The impedance change $\Delta Z$ is empirically:

$$
\Delta Z \approx k \cdot t_c \quad \text{(for thin coatings, linear region)}
$$

For thicker coatings, the relationship becomes non-linear and is best handled by a multi-point calibration curve.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Must-Memorize Formula Bank**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\delta = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}$ | Standard depth of penetration | m |
| 2 | $e = \dfrac{V_t}{E_t} \quad (\text{or } e = \dfrac{V_{tc} - V_a}{V_{tc} + V_a})$ | Fill factor (ratio of coil to test piece area) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $f_{\text{opt}} \approx 4 \times \sigma_{\%IACS}$ | Optimum test frequency for conductivity | kHz, % IACS |
| 4 | $Z = R + jX = R + j\omega L$ | Coil complex impedance | ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | Conductivity–resistivity reciprocal | S/m |
| 6 | $100 \% IACS = 5.8 \times 10^7 \,\text{S/m}$ | Copper conductivity benchmark | S/m |
| 7 | $\% IACS = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{sample}}}{\sigma_{\text{copper}}} \times 100$ | % IACS definition | % |
| 8 | $H = c_0 + c_1 \sigma + c_2 \sigma^2$ | Hardness–conductivity empirical fit | HRC/HB, % IACS |
| 9 | $\Delta Z \approx k \cdot t_c$ | Linear lift-off model for thin coatings | $\Omega$, m |
| 10 | $V_{\text{induced}} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's law of induction | V |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Conductivity sorting of aluminum wing skins; FPI replacement for crack detection on wheel hubs; anodized coating thickness on aircraft structures.
- **Nuclear Industry:** In-service inspection (ISI) of steam generator tubes in PWRs (e.g., Alloy 600/690) using **bobbin probes and rotating pancake probes**.
- **Petrochemical:** Heat-exchanger tube inspection; corrosion monitoring of clad surfaces.
- **Heat Treatment QC:** Sorting under-aged vs. over-aged aluminum alloy components (e.g., 7075-T6 vs. 7075-T73) by conductivity signature.
- **Automotive:** Hardness sorting of rocker arms, bolts, and valve springs.
- **Coin and Bullion:** Verifying purity of precious-metal coins and bars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Depth of Penetration (δ)

The eddy current density $J(z)$ at depth $z$ below the surface of a semi-infinite conductor, due to a surface magnetic field $H_0$, follows an exponentially decaying profile. The derivation begins from **Maxwell's equations** for a plane conductor.

**Step 1 — Starting Maxwell equation (Faraday's law in differential form):**

$$
\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 2 — Using $\vec{B} = \mu \vec{H}$ and substituting $\vec{J} = \sigma \vec{E}$ (Ohm's law in vector form):**

$$
\nabla \times \vec{E} = \nabla \times \left(\frac{\vec{J}}{\sigma}\right) = -\mu \frac{\partial \vec{H}}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 3 — Take curl of Ampère's law $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}$:**

$$
\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla \times \vec{J}
$$

Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{H}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{H}) - \nabla^2 \vec{H}$ and noting $\nabla \cdot \vec{H} = 0$ for no free magnetic poles:

$$
-\nabla^2 \vec{H} = \nabla \times \vec{J}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with Step 2 to eliminate $\vec{J}$:**

For sinusoidal excitation at angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$, phasor notation gives $\partial/\partial t \rightarrow j\omega$:

$$
-\nabla^2 \vec{H} = -\mu \sigma (j\omega) \vec{H} = j \omega \mu \sigma \vec{H}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{H} = j \omega \mu \sigma \vec{H}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the one-dimensional form (depth z only):**

$$
\frac{d^2 H_z}{dz^2} = j \omega \mu \sigma \, H_z
$$

Let $k^2 = j \omega \mu \sigma$. The general solution is:

$$
H_z(z) = H_0 \, e^{-kz}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the square root of $j$:**

Since $j = e^{j\pi/2}$, the principal square root is $\sqrt{j} = e^{j\pi/4} = \dfrac{1+j}{\sqrt{2}}$.

$$
k = \sqrt{j \omega \mu \sigma} = (1+j) \sqrt{\frac{\omega \mu \sigma}{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$:**

$$
k = (1+j) \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}
$$

**Step 8 — Write the full exponential decay of magnetic field intensity:**

$$
H_z(z) = H_0 \, e^{-z\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}} \, e^{-j z \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

The real part of $k$ is the attenuation constant $\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$. The eddy current density $J \propto H$ and follows the same exponential.

**Step 9 — Define the standard depth of penetration as the depth where $|J| = |J_0|/e$:**

$$
e^{-\alpha \delta} = \frac{1}{e} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha \delta = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}}
$$

This is the foundational result. Units: $[\delta] = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(\text{Hz})(\text{H/m})(\text{S/m})}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{m}^2}{\text{S} \cdot \text{H} \cdot \text{S}^{-1}}} = \text{m}$ ✓

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples

#### 3.2.1 Example A — Depth of Penetration in Aluminum

**Given:** $f = 100\,\text{kHz}$, aluminum: $\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7\,\text{S/m}$, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\,\text{H/m}$.

**Find:** Standard depth of penetration $\delta$.

**Solution:**

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times (100 \times 10^3) \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (3.5 \times 10^7)}}
$$

Step-by-step multiplication inside the square root:

$$
\pi \times 100 \times 10^3 = 3.1416 \times 10^5
$$

$$
3.1416 \times 10^5 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 3.1416 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-2} = 4\pi^2 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Numerically, $4\pi^2 \approx 39.478$:

$$
39.478 \times 10^{-2} \times 3.5 \times 10^7 = 39.478 \times 3.5 \times 10^5
$$

$$
39.478 \times 3.5 = 138.17 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 138.17 \times 10^5 = 1.3817 \times 10^7
$$

$$
\sqrt{1.3817 \times 10^7} = \sqrt{13.817 \times 10^6} = 3.717 \times 10^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{3717} = 2.69 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.269\,\text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At 100 kHz in aluminum, eddy currents penetrate only about **0.27 mm** deep. To inspect deeper, the frequency must be **lowered**.

#### 3.2.2 Example B — Frequency Selection for a Given Depth

**Given:** Inspection target depth = 2 mm in titanium alloy. $\sigma_{\text{Ti}} = 0.6 \times 10^7\,\text{S/m}$ (≈ 1.03 % IACS, $\mu \approx \mu_0$).

**Find:** Required frequency (rule of thumb: $\delta_{\text{desired}} = 3 \times \text{depth of deepest defect}$).

**Step 1 — Determine required $\delta$:**

$$
\delta_{\text{req}} = 3 \times 2\,\text{mm} = 6\,\text{mm} = 0.006\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the depth equation for $f$:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{\pi \mu \sigma \delta^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{\pi \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (0.6 \times 10^7) \times (0.006)^2}
$$

Compute denominator piece by piece:

$$
\pi \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 4\pi^2 \times 10^{-7} \approx 3.948 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
3.948 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.6 \times 10^7 = 3.948 \times 0.6 \times 10 = 23.69
$$

$$
23.69 \times (0.006)^2 = 23.69 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-5} = 8.528 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
f = \frac{1}{8.528 \times 10^{-4}} = 1172\,\text{Hz} \approx 1.17\,\text{kHz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Takeaway:** Deep inspection of low-conductivity titanium requires **low kHz frequencies**. A 100 kHz probe would not see a 2 mm deep defect in titanium at all.

#### 3.2.3 Example C — Conductivity in % IACS

**Given:** An aluminum alloy coupon has $\sigma = 2.9 \times 10^7\,\text{S/m}$.

**Find:** Conductivity in % IACS.

**Solution:**

$$
\% IACS = \frac{\sigma_{\text{sample}}}{\sigma_{\text{Cu}}} \times 100 = \frac{2.9 \times 10^7}{5.8 \times 10^7} \times 100
$$

$$
\% IACS = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \,\% IACS
$$

This is consistent with a typical 2xxx-series aluminum alloy (e.g., 2024 in T3 temper).

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing ECT Parameters

> [!TIP]
> **The Three Knobs of ECT:** Frequency, Probe Diameter, Lift-off. Adjusting these selects *what* you see.

| Inspection Goal | Frequency | Probe Type | Lift-off | Sensitivity Priority |
|-----------------|-----------|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| Surface crack detection (Al) | 100 kHz – 2 MHz | Small, sharp tip | As close as possible | Crack depth & length |
| Conductivity measurement | 60 – 500 kHz | Absolute, large dia. | Controlled constant | Conductivity ± 0.5 % IACS |
| Coating thickness (thin) | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | High-resolution absolute | Variable (the signal) | Sub-micron thickness |
| Coating thickness (thick, > 1 mm) | 1 – 10 kHz | Larger diameter | Variable | mm-range thickness |
| Tube inspection (steam gen.) | 10 – 500 kHz | Bobbin / rotating pancake | Internal (fixed) | Tube wall defects |
| Hardness correlation (Al alloy) | 60 – 300 kHz | Absolute conductivity | Controlled | Indirect hardness |

### 3.4 Lift-Off and Edge Effect — The Two Main Confounders

**Lift-off effect:** Variation in the gap between probe and test surface. Produces a signal that can mask defect signals. Compensation:
- Use **differential probes** (cancel common lift-off)
- Use **absolute probes with reference compensation** (dual coil)
- Use **probes at fixed mechanical stand-off**

**Edge effect:** Distortion in signal when the probe is near the edge of the test piece. Eddy current loops are abruptly cut off by the edge, producing a strong false signal. Compensation:
- Maintain probe **2 × probe diameter** away from edges
- Use **shielded probes** for close-edge inspection
- Calibrate the lift-off curve to include edge effect zones

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Lift-off and edge effects are **not defects**. A student who misinterprets them as flaw signals during inspection scores zero on practical analysis questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — ECT System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Osc[Oscillator<br/>AC signal source] --> PowerAmp[Power Amplifier<br/>sets drive amplitude]
    PowerAmp --> Bridge[Bridge or Driver Circuit]
    Bridge --> Probe[Probe Coil<br/>absolute / differential]
    Probe --> TestPiece[Test Piece<br/>conductive material]
    TestPiece --> ECField[Eddy Current Field<br/>in material]
    ECField -.induces impedance change.-> Probe
    Probe --> Demod[Demodulator<br/>rectifier + filter]
    Demod --> Amp[Signal Conditioner<br/>gain + filtering]
    Amp --> Display[Display<br/>impedance plane / meter / digital %]
    Display --> Calib[Calibration Engine<br/>references % IACS / lift-off curve]
    Calib --> Display
    Calib -.feedback to operator.-> Operator[Operator Decision<br/>accept / reject / sort]
```

### 4.2 Application-Flow Diagram — The Four Uses of ECT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([ECT Probe<br/>with AC excitation]) --> EField[Alternating Magnetic Field<br/>in test piece]
    EField --> EC[Eddy Currents Induced<br/>in surface layer]
    EC --> Q1{What property<br/>is being assessed?}
    Q1 -- Conductivity --> A1[Material Identification<br/>and Sorting]
    Q1 -- Hardness Correlation --> A2[Indirect Hardness<br/>via Conductivity Signature]
    Q1 -- Discontinuity --> A3[Defect Detection<br/>cracks, voids, laps]
    Q1 -- Lift-off Variation --> A4[Coating Thickness<br/>non-conductive layer]
    A1 --> A1out[Output: % IACS<br/>e.g., 31.5 %]
    A2 --> A2out[Output: HRB / HB<br/>via calibrated curve]
    A3 --> A3out[Output: Alarm / signal<br/>impedance jump]
    A4 --> A4out[Output: microns<br/>e.g., 25 μm anodize]
```

### 4.3 Impedance Plane Diagram — Interpreting Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Zplane[Impedance Plane Display]
        R[Horizontal Axis: R, coil resistance]
        XL[Vertical Axis: XL, inductive reactance]
        A[Point A: empty probe in air]
        B[Point B: probe on low conductivity material]
        C[Point C: probe on high conductivity material]
        D[Point D: probe near defect]
    end
    A -.lift-off curve.-> B
    B -.increasing conductivity.-> C
    C -.defect signal vector.-> D
```

### 4.4 Conductivity Reference Standards — Calibration Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ref1[Ref Block 1<br/>e.g., 8.0 % IACS] --> Cal[Two-Point Calibration]
    Ref2[Ref Block 2<br/>e.g., 30.0 % IACS] --> Cal
    Ref3[Ref Block 3<br/>e.g., 62.0 % IACS] --> Cal
    Cal --> Linear[Linear Fit<br/>slope + intercept stored]
    Linear --> Probe[Apply to Probe]
    Probe --> Unknown[Measure Unknown Sample]
    Unknown --> Result[Read % IACS on Display]
```

### 4.5 Coating Thickness Measurement — Mode A Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Probe[ECT Probe] --> Gap[Non-Conductive Coating<br/>thickness t sub c]
    Gap --> Substrate[Conductive Substrate<br/>Al, Cu, Steel]
    Substrate --> EC[Eddy Currents in Substrate]
    EC -.impedance.-> Probe
    Probe --> Proc[Instrument Processor]
    Proc --> Cal[Apply Lift-off Curve<br/>t sub c vs delta Z]
    Cal --> Out[Thickness Reading<br/>microns]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is meant by % IACS? List any two reference conductivity values for common metals.**

**Model Answer:**
% IACS stands for *percent International Annealed Copper Standard*. It is a normalized unit for expressing the electrical conductivity of a non-ferromagnetic metal relative to annealed pure copper, whose conductivity is defined as 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m at 20 °C. Mathematically:

$$
\% IACS = \frac{\sigma_{\text{sample}}}{\sigma_{\text{Cu}}} \times 100
$$

Two reference values:
- **Pure copper (annealed) = 100 % IACS** (5.8 × 10⁷ S/m)
- **Pure aluminum (annealed) ≈ 61 % IACS** (3.54 × 10⁷ S/m)
- *(Optional: Brass ≈ 28 % IACS, Titanium ≈ 1.8 % IACS, Austenitic SS 304 ≈ 1.4 % IACS)*

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Coverage:** Definition (1 M) + formula (1 M) + two values (1 M).

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define 'standard depth of penetration' in ECT. How does it change with doubling of test frequency?**

**Model Answer:**
The standard depth of penetration $\delta$ is the depth below the surface of a conductor at which the eddy current density falls to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8 %) of its value at the surface.

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

**Effect of doubling frequency:** Since $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$, doubling $f$ reduces $\delta$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$, i.e., the depth of penetration becomes **1/√2 (≈ 70.7 %)** of its original value.

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Mark Coverage:** Definition (1 M) + formula (1 M) + frequency-doubling logic (1 M).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (Choice I) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of an Eddy Current Testing (ECT) system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An ECT system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Oscillator / Frequency Generator** — Produces a stable AC signal at the chosen test frequency (typically 1 kHz to 5 MHz for industrial ECT). Frequency selection depends on the application and required depth of inspection.

2. **Power Amplifier / Driver Circuit** — Boosts the oscillator signal to a level sufficient to drive the probe coil and overcome the impedance of cables.

3. **Probe (Test Coil)** — The sensing element, which is one of the following types:
   - **Absolute probe** — single coil; measures total impedance; sensitive to lift-off but gives absolute readings.
   - **Differential probe** — two coils; outputs the *difference*; suppresses lift-off, enhances defect sensitivity.
   - **Reflection (pancake) probe** — driver and pickup coils co-axial; suitable for surface scanning.

4. **Bridge / Impedance Comparison Circuit** — Compares the probe's impedance with a reference impedance. Any imbalance produces an error signal.

5. **Demodulator / Phase-Sensitive Detector** — Extracts the in-phase (resistive) and quadrature (reactive) components of the signal, allowing separation of conductivity, permeability, and lift-off effects.

6. **Signal Conditioner & Display** — Amplifies, filters, and displays the signal — typically as a digital readout (% IACS, thickness, defect alarm) or as a 2-D impedance plane (Lissajous) trace.

7. **Calibration Engine** — Stores calibration curves for conductivity, thickness, or hardness conversions.

> A simple block diagram is:

```
Oscillator → Amplifier → Probe (over Test Piece) → Bridge → Demod → Display
                                                              ↑
                                              Reference Impedance
```

The probe generates a primary alternating magnetic field. Eddy currents are induced in the test piece. The eddy currents' secondary field opposes the primary, changing the probe impedance. The bridge detects the imbalance, the demodulator splits the signal into R and ωL components, and the display reveals either material property or defect presence.

**(b)** With the help of a labelled impedance plane diagram, explain how conductivity, lift-off, and defects are distinguished in ECT. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **impedance plane** plots the coil's complex impedance $Z = R + j\omega L$ on a 2-D Cartesian plot, with $R$ (resistance) on the horizontal axis and $\omega L$ (reactance) on the vertical axis.

- **Effect of lift-off:** As the probe is moved vertically away from the test piece (increasing lift-off), the impedance moves along a characteristic curve — generally a curved line sloping downward and to the right. This is the **lift-off curve**, which must be distinguished from defect signals.

- **Effect of conductivity change:** Increasing conductivity (e.g., from 30 % IACS to 60 % IACS aluminum) shifts the operating point in a direction along a different curve (the conductivity curve). Higher conductivity tends to increase $R$ and decrease $\omega L$ (because stronger eddy currents load the coil more).

- **Effect of defect (e.g., crack):** A defect typically causes a **sudden, localized deflection** of the impedance point. The orientation of this deflection vector indicates the type of discontinuity: surface cracks tend to produce horizontal (resistive) shifts; sub-surface defects produce different angle signatures.

- **Distinguishing them:** By varying test **frequency**, the operator moves the operating point on the plane. At optimum frequency (e.g., $f \approx 4 \sigma_{\%IACS}$ for absolute probes), the conductivity curve is steepest and lift-off curve is least steep — maximizing the **signal-to-confound ratio**. Phase-sensitive detection allows the operator to position a **phase window** that excludes the lift-off vector and accepts the defect vector.

- **Practical implementation:** Modern digital ECT instruments display the impedance plane in real time and use **alarm gates** (rectangular or elliptical zones) to auto-flag defect indications while rejecting lift-off transients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Sketch Required:** A hand-drawn impedance plane with R on x-axis, ωL on y-axis, three labelled vectors: "L → lift-off direction", "C → conductivity shift", "D → defect signal".

#### Question B (Choice II) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the standard depth of penetration of eddy currents in a conductor. A non-destructive test is to be performed on an aluminum alloy ($\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0$) at a frequency of 60 kHz. Calculate the depth of penetration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation** (as in Section 3.1 above):

Starting from Maxwell's equations in phasor form for a semi-infinite conductor:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{H} = j\omega \mu \sigma \vec{H}
$$

Solving in 1-D for depth $z$:

$$
H_z(z) = H_0 \, e^{-(1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} \, z}
$$

The amplitude decays as $e^{-\alpha z}$ with $\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$. Defining $\delta$ as the depth at which amplitude falls to $1/e$:

$$
\alpha \delta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $f = 60\,\text{kHz} = 60 \times 10^3$ Hz, $\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7$ S/m, $\mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

$$
\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi \times 60 \times 10^3 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5 \times 10^7}}
$$

Simplify inside the square root step by step:

Step 1: $\pi \times 4\pi = 4\pi^2 \approx 39.478$.

Step 2: $39.478 \times 60 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-7} = 39.478 \times 60 \times 10^{-4} = 2368.7 \times 10^{-4} = 0.2369$.

Step 3: $0.2369 \times 3.5 \times 10^7 = 0.8290 \times 10^7 = 8.290 \times 10^6$.

Step 4: $\sqrt{8.290 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{82.90 \times 10^5} = 9.105 \times 10^{2.5} \approx 2879$.

$$
\boxed{\delta = \frac{1}{2879} = 3.47 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} \approx 0.347\,\text{mm}}
$$

**(b)** Describe the principle of coating thickness measurement using ECT for non-conductive coatings on a conductive substrate. List the typical industrial applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
When a non-conductive coating (paint, plastic, anodize, ceramic) is applied to a conductive substrate (Al, Cu, steel), the coating does not carry eddy currents. It acts purely as a **lift-off spacer** between the ECT probe and the substrate. The probe's magnetic field must traverse the coating thickness before reaching the conductive substrate where eddy currents can be induced.

The coil impedance $Z$ varies monotonically with the gap between the probe tip and the conductive substrate. As the coating thickness $t_c$ increases:
- Effective lift-off $L_{\text{eff}} = L_0 + t_c$ increases.
- Probe coupling to the substrate weakens.
- Coil inductance rises and resistance changes in a characteristic manner.

For thin coatings, the relationship is approximately linear:

$$
\Delta Z \approx k \cdot t_c
$$

where $k$ is a probe-specific sensitivity (ohms per micrometer). The instrument is calibrated using **coated reference standards** of known thickness, typically at three or more points, and a calibration curve is fitted.

**Procedure:**
1. Calibrate on a **bare substrate** (zero thickness baseline) and on one or more coated reference standards of known thickness.
2. Place the probe on the coated surface — the reading is converted to thickness via the calibration curve.
3. Take multiple readings (typically 5–10) per inspection zone for averaging.

**Industrial Applications:**
- **Aerospace:** Anodized coating thickness on aluminum aircraft skins (typical 5–50 μm). Critical for corrosion protection compliance.
- **Automotive:** Paint and primer thickness on body panels (50–200 μm).
- **Appliance manufacturing:** Enamel coating on steel (50–500 μm).
- **Pipeline industry:** Epoxy/FBE coating thickness on line pipe (300–1000 μm).
- **Electronics:** Solder mask thickness on PCBs; conformal coating thickness.
- **Heat treatment verification:** Diffusion coating (e.g., aluminide) thickness on turbine blades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration Discipline:** The substrate alloy, surface roughness, and probe temperature must match the actual test conditions. Even a few tenths of a degree of temperature change can shift conductivity by 1 % IACS and corrupt the thickness reading.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in ECT Application Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing conductivity and resistivity units.** Use $\sigma$ in S/m, **not** Ω·m. 1 S/m = 1/(Ω·m).
> 2. **Forgetting the 20 °C temperature reference** for % IACS. Always state "at 20 °C" when quoting conductivities.
> 3. **Using $\mu = \mu_0$ for steel.** Steel is ferromagnetic; $\mu_r$ can be 100–500. Don't use $\mu_0$ in calculations involving carbon steel.
> 4. **Treating ECT hardness measurement as a direct method.** It is **indirect** and alloy-specific. The examiner will deduct marks if you don't emphasize calibration.
> 5. **Ignoring lift-off in coating thickness.** The lift-off curve slope determines sensitivity. A student who says "the reading is direct" loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Failing to mention defect orientation in defect detection.** Cracks parallel to eddy current flow are nearly invisible. Always state the orientation rule.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ ECT works **only on electrically conductive materials** (metals & alloys). Not for plastics, wood, ceramics.
- ✅ **Standard depth of penetration:** $\delta = 1/\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$. Doubling $f$ reduces $\delta$ by $\sqrt{2}$.
- ✅ **% IACS** = sample conductivity relative to annealed pure copper (= 5.8 × 10⁷ S/m at 20 °C).
- ✅ **Conductivity** is the most fundamental ECT measurement. Other applications (hardness, thickness) are derived from it.
- ✅ **Hardness via ECT is INDIRECT** — based on empirical correlation between conductivity and hardness for a specific alloy/temper.
- ✅ **Defect detection** is best for **surface and near-surface** flaws. Sensitivity depends on defect orientation (perpendicular to current flow is best).
- ✅ **Coating thickness (Mode A)** uses the coating as a lift-off spacer — works only if substrate is conductive.
- ✅ **Lift-off and edge effects** are the two main confounders — must be controlled or compensated.
- ✅ **Differential probes** reject lift-off, enhance defect signals. **Absolute probes** give absolute readings.
- ✅ **Optimum test frequency** for absolute conductivity probes: $f \approx 4 \times \sigma_{\%IACS}$ (kHz).
- ✅ **Calibration standards** are mandatory — always state which reference blocks are used.
- ✅ ECT is a **contact or near-contact** method — probe must be close to surface (within a few mm).
- ✅ **Frequency range** for industrial ECT: 100 Hz to 10 MHz typically; low kHz for deep inspection, high MHz for surface micro-cracks.
- ✅ **Major industries** using ECT: aerospace (crack + conductivity), nuclear (steam generator tubes), petrochemical (heat exchangers), automotive (sorting), heat treatment QC.
- ✅ **Limitations of ECT:** conductive materials only, surface-biased, sensitive to lift-off, requires reference standards, limited depth penetration.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Applications of ECT**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UT Testing Methods: Contact and Immersion Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** UT Testing Methods: Contact and Immersion Testing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Ultrasonic (UT) Testing – A Brief Recap Ultrasonic Testing is a volumetric Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) technique that uses high-frequency sound waves (typically **1 MHz to 25 MHz**) to detect internal flaws, measure thickness, and characterize material properties. A piezoelectric transducer emits an ultrasonic pulse, which propagates through the test material; reflections from flaws or back-walls are analyzed to determine defect location and size. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** The two principal methods of coupling the ultrasonic beam from the transducer into the test specimen are **(i) Contact Testing** and **(ii) Immersion Testing**. This module forms the foundation for all advanced UT techniques (TOFD, Phased Array, C-scan imaging). --- ## 1.2 Contact Testing – Formal Definition **Contact Testing** is an ultrasonic inspection method in which the search unit (probe) is placed **directly on the surface of the test specimen**, with a thin film of liquid **couplant** (gel, oil, or paste) interposed between the transducer face and the specimen to facilitate acoustic energy transfer. $$f_{coupling} = \frac{\text{Acoustic energy transmitted}}{\text{Acoustic energy emitted by probe}}$$ The couplant eliminates the air gap (which would otherwise reflect **99.9 %** of the ultrasonic energy due to the massive acoustic impedance mismatch between air, $Z_{air} \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$, and steel, $Z_{steel} \approx 46 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$). --- ## 1.3 Immersion Testing – Formal Definition **Immersion Testing** is an ultrasonic inspection method in which **both the search unit and the test specimen are completely submerged in a liquid medium** (most commonly water, occasionally glycerin, oil, or alcohol). The ultrasonic beam travels from the probe through the liquid couplant, reflects from the test piece, and is received back through the same or a second transducer. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference:** ASTM E1004 governs practice for UT immersion contact testing. ASTM E214 covers immersion shear-wave testing of steel. --- ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – The "Whispering Through Walls" Effect Imagine you are trying to listen to your friend whisper through a thick concrete wall: - If you **press your ear directly to the wall**, you hear almost nothing because dry contact between your ear and the rough wall creates countless tiny air gaps that scatter the sound waves. But if you **smear a little gel/oil on the wall first** (mimicking couplant), sound conducts beautifully. This is exactly **Contact Testing** — couplant bridges the microscopic air gaps. - Now imagine you and your friend are both **fully underwater in a swimming pool**, with a waterproof speaker. Sound travels from the speaker, through the water, into the wall, back into the water, and into your ear — without ever "leaving" the liquid medium. This is **Immersion Testing** — the entire system is acoustically coupled by the liquid. > [!TIP] > **The single most important practical takeaway:** Couplant selection (gel, oil, or water) is *not* an optional extra — it is the *physical enabler* of UT. No couplant = no UT. --- ## 1.5 The Role of Acoustic Impedance The transmission coefficient $T$ across an interface is governed by: $$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$ | Couplant | Density (kg/m³) | Velocity (m/s) | Acoustic Impedance $Z$ (×10⁶ kg/m²·s) | |----------|----------------|----------------|----------------------------------------| | Air (no couplant) | 1.2 | 330 | 0.0004 | | Water (25 °C) | 1000 | 1490 | 1.49 | | Glycerin | 1260 | 1900 | 2.40 | | SAE 30 Oil | 920 | 1430 | 1.32 | | Steel (specimen) | 7850 | 5900 | 46.0 | | Plexiglas (delay line) | 1180 | 2700 | 3.20 | > [!WARNING] > Without couplant, $T \approx 0.0001$ — virtually **no signal** enters the specimen. With water couplant, $T \approx 0.12$ — a **120,000×** improvement. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Contact Testing – Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Surface Preparation:** The test surface is cleaned (free of paint, scale, rust, oil films > 0.1 mm). 2. **Couplant Application:** A thin, continuous film of couplant (cellulose gel, glycerin, or light oil) is applied to bridge the transducer-to-surface air gap. 3. **Probe Placement:** The probe is pressed firmly on the surface, ensuring the couplant film thickness remains between **0.025 mm and 0.05 mm** (ideal). 4. **Pulse Emission:** The pulser/receiver fires a high-voltage spike (typically **100–400 V**) across the piezo crystal, generating an ultrasonic pulse. 5. **Pulse Propagation:** The pulse enters the specimen, encountering flaws or the back-wall. 6. **Echo Reception:** Reflected pulses return to the same (Pulse-Echo) or a second (Through-Transmission) probe, producing A-scan peaks. 7. **Interpretation:** The time-base (gated range) is calibrated to thickness/distance, and the operator analyzes echo amplitude, position, and shape. ### Why Contact Testing Works - **Pro:** Simple, portable, fast, low-cost; ideal for **field inspection** of welds, pressure vessels, pipes, and storage tanks. - **Con:** Couplant film thickness variability produces signal amplitude fluctuations of up to **±6 dB**; surface roughness > **3.2 μm Ra** degrades performance; manual couplant replenishment needed. --- ## 2.2 Immersion Testing – Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Tank Filling:** The immersion tank is filled with de-aerated, degassed water (degas < 5 ppm O₂ to prevent cavitation bubbles). 2. **Specimen Mounting:** The test piece is clamped on a motorized scanner (X-Y bridge or rotational turntable) fully submerged below the water surface by **≥ 25 mm**. 3. **Probe Immersion:** The search unit is mounted on the scanner, with its active face submerged at a controlled **water-path distance** (typically **25–150 mm**) from the specimen. 4. **Water-Path Calibration:** The water path delays the initial pulse, creating a clear time window before the entry-surface (IE) echo — this is used for precise gating. 5. **Automated Scanning:** The scanner moves the probe over the specimen at speeds of **50–300 mm/s**, generating a 2-D C-scan image. 6. **Data Acquisition:** At each grid point, the gated echo amplitude (or time-of-flight) is recorded and mapped to a color/greyscale image. 7. **Post-Processing:** The C-scan, B-scan, and A-scan records are analyzed for defect location, size, and depth. ### Why Immersion Testing Works - **Pro:** Highly reproducible signal amplitude (±1 dB); enables **automated C-scan imaging**; superior for small/thin/complex parts; no couplant replenishment; ideal for composite laminates, aerospace honeycomb, and electronic components. - **Con:** Requires immersion tank, degassed water, scanner, and trained operator; specimen must be **waterproof** and not affected by corrosion. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | kg/(m²·s) | $\rho$ = density, $c$ = longitudinal velocity | | 2 | Reflection Coefficient (R) | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Energy ratio | | 3 | Transmission Coefficient (T) | $T = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | dimensionless | Energy ratio | | 4 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m | $c$ = velocity, $f$ = frequency | | 5 | Near-Field Length (N) | $N = \dfrac{D^2 - \lambda^2}{4\lambda} \approx \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 c}$ | m | $D$ = transducer diameter | | 6 | Beam Spread Half-Angle | $\sin \theta = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | rad | Far-field divergence | | 7 | Beam Diameter at Depth $z$ | $D_z = 1.22 \lambda \dfrac{z}{D}$ | m | For $z > N$ | | 8 | Time of Flight (2-way) | $t = \dfrac{2x}{c}$ | s | $x$ = depth of reflector | | 9 | Acoustic Pressure at Depth | $P(z) = P_0 \cdot \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 + 4 \lambda z}$ | Pa | For $z \le N$ | | 10 | SNR Improvement (Water vs Air) | $\dfrac{T_{water}}{T_{air}} = \dfrac{4 Z_w Z_s / (Z_w + Z_s)^2}{4 Z_{air} Z_s / (Z_{air} + Z_s)^2}$ | ratio | $\approx 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Important units used in KTU papers:** Distance is in **mm**, time in **μs**, frequency in **MHz**, velocity in **m/s**. Always convert before substituting. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility – Where Each Method is Used | Industry Sector | Preferred Method | Why | |-----------------|------------------|-----| | Pipeline girth weld inspection (in-service) | **Contact** | Portable, no tank needed | | Aerospace composite laminates (CFRP) | **Immersion** | Precise, automated, detects delaminations < 1 mm | | Pressure vessel in-service (ASME) | **Contact** | Field-deployable | | Nuclear fuel rod end-cap welds | **Immersion** | Clean-room, repeatable | | Boiler tube thickness mapping | **Contact** (TOFD) | Long-range, high-temperature delay lines | | Electronic component lead inspection | **Immersion** | Miniature parts, fine resolution | | Railway axle in-service | **Contact** | Mobile rail-yard inspection | | Turbine blade crack detection | **Immersion** | Complex geometry, automated scanning | > [!TIP] > **Rule of thumb for KTU problems:** Use **Contact** when $f < 10$ MHz, $D > 10$ mm, specimen is large and stationary. Use **Immersion** when high frequency ($f > 10$ MHz), small flaws, complex geometry, or when **C-scan imaging** is required. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Near-Field Length (Fresnel Zone) for a Circular Piston Transducer A circular piezo disc of diameter $D$ radiates a wave-front that is initially collimated (cylindrical) up to a distance $N$, after which it diverges spherically. This transition occurs at the *last axial maximum* of the pressure field. ### Step 1: Huygens' Integral for a Piston Source The on-axis pressure amplitude $P(z)$ is given by: $$P(z) = 2 P_0 \left[ 1 - \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{\lambda}\left[\sqrt{D^2/4 + z^2} - z\right]\right) \right]$$ where $P_0$ is the pressure at the transducer face. ### Step 2: Identify the Last Maximum The last maximum occurs when the argument of the cosine is exactly $-\pi$ (i.e., the cosine function hits its minimum and flips), giving: $$\frac{\pi}{\lambda}\left[\sqrt{D^2/4 + N^2} - N\right] = \pi$$ ### Step 3: Solve for $N$ $$\sqrt{D^2/4 + N^2} - N = \lambda$$ $$\sqrt{D^2/4 + N^2} = N + \lambda$$ Squaring both sides: $$\frac{D^2}{4} + N^2 = N^2 + 2N\lambda + \lambda^2$$ Cancelling $N^2$: $$\frac{D^2}{4} = 2N\lambda + \lambda^2$$ Solving for $N$: $$N = \frac{D^2 - \lambda^2}{4\lambda}$$ ### Step 4: Practical Approximation (since $D \gg \lambda$ in UT) $$\boxed{N \approx \frac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \frac{D^2 f}{4 c}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical example for KTU practice:** A 20-mm diameter, 4-MHz probe in steel ($c = 5900$ m/s, $\lambda = 1.475$ mm) gives $N = (0.02)^2 / (4 \times 0.001475) = 67.8$ mm. Anything within 67.8 mm of the probe is in the near field — defects here are **difficult to size accurately** because of pressure oscillations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Beam Divergence (Far-Field Half-Angle) and Beam Diameter at Depth The far-field pattern of a piston source is analogous to a single-slit diffraction pattern. The first null of the on-axis pressure field in the far field occurs at an angle $\theta$ given by the Rayleigh criterion. ### Step 1: Path Difference for Edge Waves Consider two diametrically opposite points on the rim of the disc separated by distance $D$. The path difference for waves reaching a far-field point at angle $\theta$ is $D \sin \theta$. ### Step 2: Destructive Interference Condition The first minimum (null) occurs when this path difference equals one wavelength: $$D \sin \theta = \lambda$$ $$\boxed{\sin \theta = \frac{\lambda}{D}}$$ For a uniformly-illuminated circular aperture, the **1.22** factor arises from the Bessel-function analysis of the circular piston: $$\sin \theta = \frac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$$ ### Step 3: Beam Diameter at Distance $z$ For a cone of half-angle $\theta$, the beam diameter at depth $z$ is: $$D_z = D + 2 z \tan \theta \approx D + \frac{2 \cdot 1.22 \lambda z}{D} \quad (z > N)$$ If we define the beam diameter as the central lobe (between first nulls), it can also be written as: $$D_z = 2.44 \lambda \frac{z}{D} \quad \text{(full beam diameter at depth } z, z \gg N\text{)}$$ > [!NOTE] > This divergence is why **immersion testing with focused transducers** is used for small flaw detection: a spherical lens or curved piezo focuses the beam to a **focal spot of 0.5–2 mm** at the focal point, dramatically improving lateral resolution. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Time of Flight in Pulse-Echo Mode (Inspection Range) A flaw is located at depth $x$ below the entry surface. The pulse travels *down* and *back up* — total path $= 2x$. ### Step 1: Basic Relation $$\text{Time} = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Velocity}}$$ $$t = \frac{2x}{c}$$ ### Step 2: In Immersion Testing (with water path $L$) The pulse first travels through water (velocity $c_w$), then the specimen (velocity $c_s$), reflects, and returns: $$t_{IE} = \frac{2L}{c_w} \quad \text{(time to entry-surface echo)}$$ $$t_{flaw} = \frac{2L}{c_w} + \frac{2x}{c_s} \quad \text{(time to flaw at depth } x\text{)}$$ The flaw position is then: $$\Delta t = t_{flaw} - t_{IE} = \frac{2x}{c_s}$$ $$\boxed{x = \frac{c_s \cdot \Delta t}{2}}$$ ### Step 3: Worked Example (KTU-typical numerical) **Given:** Steel plate, $c_s = 5900$ m/s, contact probe on top surface. The flaw echo appears at 6.5 μs, and the back-wall echo at 13.0 μs on the A-scan. Find the flaw depth and plate thickness. **Solution:** $$\text{Back-wall echo time} = t_{BW} = \frac{2T}{c_s}$$ where $T$ is plate thickness. Thus: $$T = \frac{c_s \cdot t_{BW}}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 13.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.03835 \text{ m} \approx 38.35 \text{ mm}$$ $$x_{flaw} = \frac{c_s \cdot t_{flaw}}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.01918 \text{ m} \approx 19.18 \text{ mm}$$ The flaw is at **19.18 mm depth** (essentially the mid-thickness of a 38.35 mm plate). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix – Contact vs Immersion | Parameter | Contact Testing | Immersion Testing | |-----------|-----------------|-------------------| | Couplant | Gel / paste / oil (thin film) | Water (bulk liquid) | | Couplant film thickness | 0.025–0.05 mm | Water path 25–150 mm | | Signal amplitude reproducibility | ±6 dB | ±1 dB | | Maximum practical frequency | 1–10 MHz | 1–100 MHz | | Near-field problems | Common, must use delay line | Avoidable — focus at any depth | | Surface roughness tolerance | ≤ 3.2 μm Ra | ≤ 25 μm Ra (smooth surface) | | Specimen size limit | Unlimited (portable) | Limited by tank size | | Automation capability | Limited (manual scan) | Fully automated C-scan | | Capital cost | Low (₹ 50 K – 5 L) | High (₹ 5 L – 50 L) | | Operating cost | Low | Water treatment, electricity | | Best for | Welds, pressure vessels, in-service | Composites, electronics, small parts | | KTU 2024 expected CO | CO3 — Apply UT to engineering cases | CO3 — Apply UT to engineering cases | | Bloom's Level (KTU) | Apply / Analyze | Apply / Analyze / Evaluate | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram – Contact Testing Setup (Pulse-Echo Mode) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pulser/Receiver
100-400V spike] --> B[Piezoelectric

Steel Plate or Weld] D --> E[Flaw / Back-Wall

Time-Base] F --> G[A-Scan Display
on CRO or LCD] F --> H[Alarm / Gate
