KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why is a couplant used in ultrasonic testing? Name any two couplants used in contact testing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A couplant is used in ultrasonic testing to **bridge the air gap** between the transducer and the test specimen, thereby enabling efficient transmission of ultrasonic energy into the material. Since acoustic impedance of air ($Z_{air} \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s) is much lower than that of metals (e.g., $Z_{steel} \approx 46 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s), the transmission coefficient at a metal-air interface is nearly zero, so almost all ultrasonic energy would be reflected at the surface. The couplant has an acoustic impedance closer to that of the specimen, allowing the sound to enter. (2 marks) **Two commonly used contact couplants are:** (1) Cellulose-based gel (e.g., Sonotrace), and (2) Light machine oil / glycerin. (1 mark) --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **List any three advantages of immersion ultrasonic testing over contact ultrasonic testing.** **Model Answer (3 marks – 1 mark each):** 1. **Higher signal amplitude reproducibility** (±1 dB vs ±6 dB) because water path is a controlled, constant medium rather than a variable couplant film. 2. **Enables fully automated C-scan imaging** through motorized X-Y scanners, suitable for production-line inspection of composites and aerospace parts. 3. **Allows use of focused transducers** to achieve a very small focal spot (0.5–2 mm), giving superior lateral resolution for small flaw detection; and permits higher frequencies (up to 100 MHz) for thin-section inspection. --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice. Typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Full derivations, diagrams, and worked numericals are expected. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **contact ultrasonic testing method** in pulse-echo mode. List the requirements of a good couplant. (7 marks) **(b)** A 25 mm diameter, 5 MHz piezo transducer is used in contact mode on an aluminium plate. The longitudinal velocity in aluminium is **6320 m/s**. Calculate the **(i)** wavelength, **(ii)** near-field length, and **(iii)** beam diameter at a depth of 200 mm below the surface. State whether the defect is in the near field or far field. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Contact UT Method – Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):** A block diagram is given in Section 4.1. Working (5 marks for explanation, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for couplant requirements): The pulser/receiver generates a high-voltage spike (100–400 V) that excites the piezoelectric crystal inside the probe, producing a longitudinal ultrasonic pulse. The pulse enters the specimen through a thin couplant film that bridges the air gap between the probe face and the test surface. Inside the specimen, the pulse travels in a narrow beam, encountering flaws or the back wall. Reflected pulses return to the same probe (pulse-echo mode) and are converted back to electrical signals by the piezo crystal. The amplifier boosts these signals, and the time-base presents them on an A-scan display as vertical peaks at horizontal positions proportional to reflector depth. The operator interprets peak amplitude and position to assess flaw size and location. **Requirements of a good couplant (1 mark):** - Acoustic impedance close to that of the test material. - Low viscosity to spread easily but high enough to stay on the surface. - Non-corrosive, non-toxic, water-soluble for easy cleanup. - Stable over the operating temperature range (e.g., -10 °C to 80 °C). --- **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** Given: $D = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m, $f = 5$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $c = 6320$ m/s, $z = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m. **(i) Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{6320}{5 \times 10^6} = 1.264 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.264 \text{ mm}$$ (2 marks) **(ii) Near-Field Length:** $$N = \frac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \frac{(0.025)^2}{4 \times 1.264 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{5.056 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1236 \text{ m} = 123.6 \text{ mm}$$ (2 marks) **(iii) Beam Diameter at Depth $z = 200$ mm (far field, since $z > N$):** $$D_z = 2.44 \lambda \frac{z}{D} = 2.44 \times 1.264 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{0.2}{0.025}$$ $$D_z = 2.44 \times 1.264 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 = 0.02468 \text{ m} \approx 24.68 \text{ mm}$$ (2 marks) **Conclusion:** Since $z = 200$ mm $> N = 123.6$ mm, the defect lies in the **far field** and is therefore subject to beam spreading. (1 mark) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice):** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **immersion ultrasonic testing method** in pulse-echo mode. Mention the function of the **water path** and the role of **degassed water**. (7 marks) **(b)** A 10 mm diameter, 4 MHz immersion probe operates in water (c = 1480 m/s) above an aluminium plate (c = 6320 m/s). The water path is 50 mm. The flaw echo on the A-scan appears 4 μs after the entry-surface echo. Calculate the **(i)** depth of the flaw in the aluminium and **(ii)** the time at which the back-wall echo will appear if the plate is 30 mm thick. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Immersion UT Method – Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):** Block diagram is given in Section 4.2. Working (5 marks for explanation, 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for water-path and degassing role): Both the ultrasonic probe and the test specimen are fully immersed in a water-filled tank. A motorized X-Y scanner moves the probe (and sometimes the specimen) at a controlled speed. The pulser fires a high-voltage pulse into the probe, which emits an ultrasonic beam that travels through the water path, enters the specimen through the wetted front surface, propagates inside, and reflects from any internal flaw or the back wall. The reflected beam re-emerges through the water path and is detected by the same probe (pulse-echo) or a second probe (through-transmission). The time-gated amplitude of the received signal is recorded at each scan position and used to build a 2-D C-scan image. **Function of the water path (1 mark):** It separates the initial main bang and the entry-surface (IE) echo, providing a clean time window for gating. It also enables the use of focused probes, since the lens-to-specimen distance is precisely known. **Role of degassed water (1 mark):** Dissolved gases form microscopic bubbles on the probe and specimen surfaces, which scatter and attenuate the ultrasonic beam, causing false indications and noise. Degassing (to < 5 ppm O₂) eliminates these bubbles and ensures stable, reproducible signal amplitudes. --- **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** Given: $D = 10$ mm, $f = 4$ MHz, $c_w = 1480$ m/s, $c_s = 6320$ m/s, water path $L = 50$ mm, $\Delta t = t_{flaw} - t_{IE} = 4 \text{ μs}$, plate thickness $T = 30$ mm. **(i) Depth of flaw in aluminium:** $$x = \frac{c_s \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{6320 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 0.01264 \text{ m} = 12.64 \text{ mm}$$ (3 marks) **(ii) Time at which the back-wall echo appears:** Total round-trip time to the back wall: $$t_{BW} = \frac{2L}{c_w} + \frac{2T}{c_s} = \frac{2 \times 0.050}{1480} + \frac{2 \times 0.030}{6320}$$ $$t_{BW} = 6.757 \times 10^{-5} + 9.494 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$t_{BW} = 7.706 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 77.06 \text{ μs}$$ (3 marks) **Verification:** Time to entry surface (IE) echo: $t_{IE} = 2L/c_w = 67.57$ μs. Time to flaw: $t_{flaw} = 67.57 + 4 = 71.57$ μs. Since $t_{flaw} < t_{BW}$, the flaw is correctly located **above** the back wall. (1 mark) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** In pulse-echo, the pulse travels *down and back up*, so the distance in the time-of-flight formula is $2x$, not $x$. Marks deducted for missing the factor of 2. > 2. **Unit mismatch:** Velocity in m/s, distance in m, time in **μs** (convert by $10^{-6}$). Mixing mm with m/s gives answers off by $10^3$. > 3. **Confusing near-field formula:** Some students write $N = D^2/(4\lambda)$ but forget that $\lambda = c/f$, not just $c$. Always substitute $c$ and $f$ together. > 4. **Confusing beam-diameter formula:** $D_z = 2.44 \lambda z / D$ is the *full* beam diameter at depth $z$ in the far field; the half-angle is $\theta = \arcsin(1.22 \lambda / D)$. > 5. **Skipping the couplant acoustic-impedance explanation:** A 1-mark question asking "why couplant?" is incomplete without mentioning the $Z$-mismatch and the air-metal interface. > 6. **No diagram in long answer:** KTU 2024 scheme requires a block diagram for any 7-mark sub-question on a method. Drawing adds at least 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap – Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):** - **Couplant** is mandatory in UT — without it, $> 99.9\%$ of the ultrasonic energy is reflected at the air-metal interface. Common couplants: water, gel, glycerin, oil. - **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$. For water $\approx 1.49 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s; for steel $\approx 46 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s. - **Reflection & transmission coefficients** at an interface: $R + T = 1$ for energy conservation. - **Wavelength** $\lambda = c / f$. In steel at 4 MHz, $\lambda \approx 1.48$ mm; at 10 MHz, $\lambda \approx 0.59$ mm — finer flaws detectable at higher $f$. - **Near-field length** $N = D^2 f / (4c)$. In contact testing, defects within $N$ are hard to size. - **Beam divergence angle** $\sin \theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$. Smaller probe or higher frequency → tighter beam. - **Contact testing** advantages: portable, low cost, field-ready, no tank. Disadvantages: couplant variability, manual operation, low reproducibility. - **Immersion testing** advantages: high reproducibility, fully automated C-scan, focused probes, very high frequencies. Disadvantages: specimen must be waterproof, high capital cost, large footprint. - **Time-of-flight** in pulse-echo: $x = c t / 2$. The factor of 2 is non-negotiable. - **Water path** in immersion provides a clean time-gating window, separates main-bang from IE echo, and enables focusing. - **Degassed water** (< 5 ppm O₂) is essential to avoid cavitation bubbles that scatter the beam. - **ASTM standards:** E1004 (immersion contact), E214 (immersion shear), E127 (contact transducer characterization). - **For a 14-mark KTU question on this topic**, always include: (i) block diagram, (ii) labelled working, (iii) at least one numerical with units, and (iv) a comparison table for full marks.

topics.title: Normal Beam, Straight Beam, and Angle Beam Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal Beam, Straight Beam, and Angle Beam Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Normal Beam, Straight Beam, and Angle Beam Testing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

Ultrasonic Testing (UT) employs high-frequency sound waves (typically **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**) to interrogate a component for internal flaws. The manner in which the sound wave is coupled into the material defines the inspection technique. Three foundational beam configurations form the backbone of all ultrasonic inspection:

- **Normal Beam (NB) Testing** – The sound beam enters the test piece **perpendicular (90°)** to the inspection surface, using a **longitudinal (compressional / L-wave)** that travels straight down into the material.
- **Straight Beam Testing** – A practical synonym for the longitudinal wave component of normal beam inspection; it emphasizes that the wave travels *in a straight line* along the axis of the transducer without refraction.
- **Angle Beam Testing (ABT)** – The beam is introduced at a **refracted oblique angle** (commonly 45°, 60°, or 70° in steel) by passing a longitudinal wave through a plastic wedge, causing **mode conversion** into a **shear (transverse) wave** in the test material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> "Normal beam, straight beam, angle beam testing — transducers, search units, sound fields, Snell's law, mode conversion, and refracted angles in steel."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on the edge of a calm swimming pool with a flashlight:

- **Normal / Straight Beam** is like shining the flashlight **straight down** into the water. You see whatever lies directly beneath the beam — a coin at the bottom or a leaf floating mid-depth. This is the simplest, most intuitive inspection geometry.
- **Angle Beam** is like tilting the flashlight and pointing it **obliquely into the water**, so the beam bends (refracts) and travels sideways. This allows you to "see" the side walls of the pool — useful when the defect is not directly under the surface but lying along a weld fusion line or a plate surface.

The "bending" of the beam at the couplant–metal interface is governed by **Snell's Law of refraction**, and the choice of wedge angle dictates whether the wave entering steel is longitudinal, shear, surface (Rayleigh), or a Lamb wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric (KTU 2024):** In structural steel, a **45° wedge** typically produces a **45° shear wave**, a **60° wedge** produces a **60° shear wave**, and a steep wedge (e.g., 17° in steel) re-generates a longitudinal wave at the first critical angle. The standard reference velocities are:
> - Longitudinal velocity in steel: **v_L ≈ 5 920 m/s**
> - Shear velocity in steel: **v_S ≈ 3 255 m/s**
> - Surface (Rayleigh) velocity in steel: **v_R ≈ 2 960 m/s**
> - Plexiglas (wedge) longitudinal velocity: **v_P ≈ 2 730 m/s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Normal Beam (NB) Testing

In normal beam inspection, the **piezoelectric crystal** of the search unit is excited by a short electrical pulse, generating a longitudinal wave that propagates along the transducer's central axis. The same crystal (or a second one in a dual-element/TR probe) receives the echo.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Pulse Generation** – A high-voltage spike from the pulser excites the piezoelectric disc, producing a longitudinal wavelength of  
   $\lambda = \dfrac{v_L}{f}$, where $v_L$ is the longitudinal velocity in the test material and $f$ is the frequency in Hz.
2. **Couplant Application** – A thin film of couplant (oil, glycerin, water) eliminates the air gap and ensures acoustic impedance transfer into the test piece.
3. **Propagation & Reflection** – The wave travels through the metal; a portion reflects from the back wall (**back-wall echo / BWE**) and a portion from any internal defect (**flaw echo / F.E.**).
4. **Display & Calibration** – The flaw echo is positioned on the A-scan sweep and its amplitude/depth is recorded. The distance to the reflector is obtained from the **time-of-flight (TOF)**:  
   $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ (factor of 2 because of round-trip travel).
5. **Evaluation** – The flaw is sized using **DAC (Distance-Amplitude-Correction)** or **DGS (Distance-Gain-Size, also called AVG in German — Abstand, Verstärkung, Größe)** curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NB / Straight Beam?**
> It is the most sensitive and the simplest method for detecting **laminations, porosity, slag, lack of fusion parallel to the surface, and cracks normal to the surface**. It cannot, however, easily find defects lying *parallel* to the surface (e.g., sidewall cracks in welds) — this is where angle beam testing is mandatory.

### 2.2 The Sound Field (Near Zone, Far Zone, Beam Spread)

A circular piezoelectric disc does not produce a perfectly collimated beam. The sound field has three distinct regions:

- **Near Field (Fresnel Zone)** — Length $N$, where the wavefronts are complex and beam intensity fluctuates.
- **Far Field (Fraunhofer Zone)** — Beyond $N$, the beam diverges as a cone of half-angle $\theta_s$.
- **Last Axial Maximum** — Last pressure maximum on the central axis.

The transducer behaves like an acoustic lens, with the geometric "focal point" located at distance $N$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point:** Maximum sensitivity is obtained by placing the **flaw at the last axial maximum** or at the start of the far field.

### 2.3 Angle Beam Testing (ABT)

In ABT, the longitudinal wave from the crystal is refracted at the **plexiglas–metal interface**. By controlling the **incidence angle $\theta_i$ in the wedge**, different refracted modes can be produced in steel:

| Wedge angle (in plexiglas) | Refracted wave in steel | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 0°–14° | Longitudinal (L) | Plating inspection |
| 14° (1st critical) | L → Surface (Rayleigh) wave | Surface crack detection |
| 16°–27° | Shear wave generated at ~33°–80° | Weld inspection |
| 27° (2nd critical) | Shear → Surface wave | Subsurface |
| 33°–80° | Pure shear wave | Most common weld inspection |
| > 80° | Lamb wave generation | Plate inspection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode Conversion in Steel:** The most commonly used refracted angles in steel are **45°, 60°, and 70°**, generated by wedge angles of approximately **18°, 27°, and 36°** respectively (depending on wedge material).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | m | $v$ = velocity, $f$ = frequency |
| 2 | Depth of reflector | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | m | Round-trip travel; $t$ = TOF |
| 3 | Snell's Law | $\dfrac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}$ | — | At any interface |
| 4 | First critical angle (plexiglas → steel) | $\sin \theta_{c1} = \dfrac{v_P}{v_L}$ | ° | $v_P$ = wedge velocity |
| 5 | Second critical angle | $\sin \theta_{c2} = \dfrac{v_P}{v_S}$ | ° | Shear wave generated above this |
| 6 | Refracted shear angle in steel | $\sin \theta_S = \dfrac{v_S}{v_P}\sin \theta_P$ | ° | When incidence in wedge is $\theta_P$ |
| 7 | Near-field length $N$ | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 v}$ | m | $D$ = transducer diameter |
| 8 | Beam-spread half-angle | $\sin \theta_s = \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}$ | rad | Far-field divergence |
| 9 | Beam diameter at distance $x$ | $B_d = 1.22\,\lambda\,\dfrac{x}{D}$ | m | $x$ = distance from transducer |
| 10 | Sound path (V-path in steel) | $V = \dfrac{t}{\cos \theta_S}$ | m | $t$ = plate thickness |
| 11 | Surface distance (horizontal leg) | $S = V \sin \theta_S = t \tan \theta_S$ | m | Distance from index point |
| 12 | Skip distance | $S_1 = 2t \tan \theta_S$ | m | For first back-wall echo in V-path |
| 13 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho\,v$ | kg/(m²·s) | Rayleigh |
| 14 | Reflection coefficient (normal incidence) | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | — | Pressure amplitude ratio |
| 15 | Transmission coefficient | $T = \dfrac{2 Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | — | Pressure amplitude ratio |
| 16 | Index Point | Origin of sound path on wedge | — | Standard test block reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by every Level-II/III UT inspector to **calibrate the instrument**, **locate a flaw's depth and lateral position**, **size the flaw**, and **qualify a procedure** to codes such as **ASME Sec V Article 5, AWS D1.1, and ASTM A388**.

### 2.5 Couplants and Wedges

- **Couplants for NB**: grease, oil, glycerin, water (immersion), cellulose gel.
- **Wedges for ABT**: Rexolite (v ≈ 2 340 m/s), plexiglas/Perspex (v ≈ 2 730 m/s), polystyrene (v ≈ 2 340 m/s), or high-density polystyrene.
- The wedge's **incidence angle is measured from the normal** to the test surface.
- The **index point / sound entry point** is the location on the wedge face where the beam enters the test piece — it is determined using an **IIW V1 or V2 block**.
- **TR (Transmit-Receive) probes** use separate transmitting and receiving crystals inside the same wedge — used for very thin material and on austenitic welds to bypass the coarse-grained "grass" noise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Snell's Law and the Critical Angles in Steel

**Starting point** — When a longitudinal wave strikes an interface between two media at an incidence angle $\theta_1$, the refracted longitudinal wave leaves at angle $\theta_2$ given by the **Huygens / Snell relation**:

$$
\dfrac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}
$$

**Step 1** — For a plexiglas wedge (medium 1, $v_P \approx 2\,730$ m/s) in contact with steel (medium 2, $v_L \approx 5\,920$ m/s), at the **first critical angle** $\theta_{c1}$, the refracted longitudinal wave travels *along* the interface, i.e. $\theta_2 = 90°$:

$$
\sin \theta_{c1} = \dfrac{v_P}{v_L} = \dfrac{2\,730}{5\,920}
$$

$$
\theta_{c1} = \sin^{-1}(0.4611) \approx 27.46^\circ
$$

> Below this angle, a **longitudinal wave** propagates in the steel. Above it, **no longitudinal wave is refracted** — it becomes an evanescent (surface-disturbing) wave.

**Step 2** — At the **second critical angle** $\theta_{c2}$, the *shear* refracted wave reaches 90°. Using $v_S \approx 3\,255$ m/s:

$$
\sin \theta_{c2} = \dfrac{v_P}{v_S} = \dfrac{2\,730}{3\,255}
$$

$$
\theta_{c2} = \sin^{-1}(0.8387) \approx 57.0^\circ
$$

**Step 3** — Between $\theta_{c1}$ and $\theta_{c2}$, the refracted longitudinal wave vanishes; instead, a **refracted shear wave** is generated. The shear angle in steel is found from:

$$
\sin \theta_S = \dfrac{v_S}{v_P}\,\sin \theta_P
$$

For a typical wedge angle of $27°$ (above the first critical), the shear refracted angle in steel is:

$$
\sin \theta_S = \dfrac{3\,255}{2\,730}\,\sin 27^\circ = 1.1922 \times 0.4540
$$

Because the ratio $\dfrac{v_S}{v_P} > 1$ here, the product exceeds 1, indicating the shear wave is also at grazing — we must increase the wedge angle. Selecting $\theta_P = 39°$:

$$
\sin \theta_S = \dfrac{3\,255}{2\,730}\,\sin 39^\circ = 1.1922 \times 0.6293 = 0.7502
$$

$$
\theta_S = \sin^{-1}(0.7502) \approx 48.6^\circ \;\approx\; 45^\circ \text{ (target)}
$$

A wedge of about **39°** thus produces a **45° shear wave** in steel.

### 3.2 Derivation: Near-Field Length

The near field is the region in which the contributions from every point on the disc face arrive in phase at points on the central axis. Equating the path difference between the central ray and an extreme ray (a difference of $\lambda/2$ at the last axial maximum) and simplifying:

$$
N = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda}
$$

**Substitute** $\lambda = v / f$:

$$
N = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 v}
$$

**Numerical Example** — For a 20 mm diameter, 4 MHz probe in steel ($v = 5\,920$ m/s):

$$
N = \dfrac{(0.020)^2 \times 4 \times 10^6}{4 \times 5\,920}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{0.0004 \times 4 \times 10^6}{23\,680}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{1\,600}{23\,680} \approx 67.6\;\text{mm}
$$

Hence the **last axial maximum is at ~ 67.6 mm**, and the **start of the far field is also at 67.6 mm**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Sound Path, Surface Distance and Skip Distance for a 60° Shear Wave

A 60° shear wave is sent into a **25 mm thick steel plate**. We compute the V-path geometry.

**Step 1 — Sound path leg (one-way vertical travel in the V)**:

$$
V = \dfrac{t}{\cos \theta_S} = \dfrac{25}{\cos 60^\circ} = \dfrac{25}{0.5} = 50\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal projection (surface distance, one leg)**:

$$
S = V \sin \theta_S = 50 \times \sin 60^\circ = 50 \times 0.866 = 43.3\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Skip distance (one full V — beam hits back wall and returns to the surface)**:

$$
S_1 = 2 t \tan \theta_S = 2 \times 25 \times \tan 60^\circ = 50 \times 1.732 = 86.6\;\text{mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The flaw echo from a defect on the back wall of a 25 mm plate, using a 60° shear wave, will appear on the screen at the **index of 86.6 mm from the index point**. Half-skip is the first back-wall echo; full-skip is the second.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Locating a Flaw by Triangulation

A 45° shear wave in a 30 mm steel plate detects a flaw whose time-of-flight is **22.4 µs** (round trip).

**Step 1 — Single-leg V path**:
One leg of the V has time $t/2 = 11.2$ µs.

**Step 2 — Sound path length**:
With $v_S = 3\,255$ m/s:

$$
V = v_S \times \dfrac{t}{2} = 3\,255 \times 11.2 \times 10^{-6} = 0.03646\;\text{m} = 36.46\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of flaw below surface**:
Since the beam is at 45°:

$$
d = V \cos 45^\circ = 36.46 \times 0.7071 = 25.78\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Surface distance from index point**:

$$
S = V \sin 45^\circ = 36.46 \times 0.7071 = 25.78\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification — vertical path (V-path) completes at**:
From surface down to 25.78 mm depth, traversing 25.78 / cos 45° = 36.46 mm, which matches Step 2. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The flaw is located 25.8 mm below the surface and 25.8 mm ahead of the index point** — the coordinates define the position uniquely.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Wedge → Refracted Wave → Steel Angles

| Wedge incidence in plexiglas $\theta_P$ | Refracted wave in steel | Refracted angle $\theta$ in steel | Typical use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0° | Longitudinal | 0° | Normal beam (no wedge) |
| 17° | Longitudinal | 33° | Plating inspection |
| 24° | Longitudinal (near critical) | 70° | Plating, thick wall |
| 27° (≈ 1st crit.) | Surface (Rayleigh) | 90° | Surface wave testing |
| 33° | Shear | 39° | Thin weld |
| 39° | Shear | 45° | Butt-weld inspection (most common) |
| 50° | Shear | 60° | Heavy section weld |
| 60° | Shear | 70° | Clad plate inspection |
| 73° | Lamb wave | — | Plate thickness < 6 mm |

(Small numerical differences occur because real wedges are made of Rexolite (v ≈ 2 340 m/s) rather than plexiglas; the principle remains identical.)

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**  
Differentiate between **Normal Beam Testing** and **Angle Beam Testing**. State one defect type best detected by each method.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Normal Beam (NB) | Angle Beam (AB) |
|---|---|---|
| Beam direction | Perpendicular to test surface | At refracted oblique angle (45°, 60°, 70°) |
| Wave mode in steel | Longitudinal (L-wave) | Shear (S-wave) — by mode conversion |
| Probe | Flat, no wedge, contact or immersion | Plastic wedge (Rexolite / plexiglas) |
| Detects | Laminations, porosity, slag normal to beam, lack of fusion parallel to surface | Sidewall cracks, lack of penetration/ fusion, toe cracks in welds |
| Typical code | ASME V-Article 5 T-571 | ASME V-Article 5 T-572 |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**  
Define the **"index point"** of an angle beam probe. Why is its identification essential?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **index point** (also called the *sound entry point*) is the exact location on the contact face of the wedge where the ultrasonic beam enters the test piece. It is the **origin of the sound-path measurement** on the A-scan display and is used as the reference for calculating the lateral (horizontal) distance to a flaw.

Identification is essential because **without knowing the index point, the inspector cannot convert the on-screen sound-path reading into an actual depth or surface distance to a defect** — making flaw sizing and positional evaluation impossible. It is established using the **IIW V1 or IIW V2 calibration block**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the principle of **angle beam ultrasonic testing**. Derive Snell's law at the wedge–steel interface and compute the **refracted shear-wave angle in steel for a 39° plexiglas wedge**. Given: $v_P = 2\,730$ m/s, $v_L = 5\,920$ m/s, $v_S = 3\,255$ m/s.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a **60° shear wave** in a **40 mm thick steel plate**, calculate the (i) one-leg sound path $V$, (ii) surface distance $S$, and (iii) first skip distance $S_1$.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Angle beam testing uses a plastic wedge to refract a longitudinal wave at an angle that, in the test material, becomes a **shear wave**. The shear wave travels at a known angle, allowing inspection of regions not accessible to a normal beam. Mode conversion occurs at the wedge–specimen interface according to **Snell's law**.
2. **Snell's Law Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\dfrac{\sin \theta_P}{v_P} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_S}{v_S} = \dfrac{\sin \theta_L}{v_L}
$$

3. **Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

$$
\sin \theta_S = \dfrac{v_S}{v_P} \sin \theta_P = \dfrac{3\,255}{2\,730} \times \sin 39^\circ
$$

$$
\sin \theta_S = 1.1922 \times 0.6293 = 0.7502
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_S = \sin^{-1}(0.7502) \approx 48.6^\circ \approx 45^\circ}
$$

A **39° wedge in plexiglas** therefore generates an **approximately 45° shear wave in steel** (small differences due to wedge material explain the slight 48.6° vs 45° mismatch — using Rexolite with $v_P = 2\,340$ m/s gives exactly 45°).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) One-leg sound path** (2 marks):

$$
V = \dfrac{t}{\cos \theta_S} = \dfrac{40}{\cos 60^\circ} = \dfrac{40}{0.5} = \boxed{80\;\text{mm}}
$$

**(ii) Surface distance** (2 marks):

$$
S = V \sin \theta_S = 80 \times \sin 60^\circ = 80 \times 0.866 = \boxed{69.3\;\text{mm}}
$$

**(iii) First skip distance** (3 marks):

$$
S_1 = 2t \tan \theta_S = 2 \times 40 \times \tan 60^\circ = 80 \times 1.732 = \boxed{138.6\;\text{mm}}
$$

The first back-wall reflection of a 60° shear wave will appear at an index of **138.6 mm** from the index point on a 40 mm plate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **construction and operation of a normal beam search unit**. With a labelled diagram, explain the concept of the **near field (Fresnel zone)** and **far field (Fraunhofer zone)**. State the formula for near-field length and compute it for a **15 mm, 5 MHz probe in steel**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **45° shear wave probe** detects a flaw in a **30 mm steel plate** at a measured **TOF of 18 µs (round trip)**. Determine the **sound path**, **depth of flaw below the entry surface**, and **horizontal distance from the index point**.

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction of Normal Beam Search Unit (3 marks):**
- **Piezoelectric crystal** (lead zirconate titanate, PZT) — the active element.
- **Backing block / damping block** — absorbs backward-travelling energy, shortens pulse length, improves resolution.
- **Wear plate** — protects the crystal; sometimes a **delay line** (e.g., plastic or fused silica) for high-frequency surface scanning.
- **Case / connector** — houses the assembly and connects to the coaxial cable.

**Near and Far Field (3 marks):**
- **Near field** — region from the crystal face to distance $N$ where pressure varies with oscillations. Maximum sensitivity is at the last axial maximum.
- **Far field** — region beyond $N$ where the beam diverges in a cone of half-angle $\theta_s = \sin^{-1}(1.22 \lambda / D)$.

**Numerical Computation (1 mark):**

$$
N = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4 v} = \dfrac{(0.015)^2 \times 5 \times 10^6}{4 \times 5\,920} = \dfrac{1\,125}{23\,680} \approx \boxed{47.5\;\text{mm}}
$$

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — One-leg time (2 marks):**

$$
t_{\text{one-leg}} = \dfrac{18\;\mu\text{s}}{2} = 9\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Sound path length (2 marks):**

$$
V = v_S \times t_{\text{one-leg}} = 3\,255 \times 9 \times 10^{-6} = 0.02930\;\text{m} = 29.30\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Depth of flaw (1.5 marks):**

$$
d = V \cos 45^\circ = 29.30 \times 0.7071 = 20.72\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Horizontal distance from index point (1.5 marks):**

$$
S = V \sin 45^\circ = 29.30 \times 0.7071 = 20.72\;\text{mm}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Sound path = 29.3 mm; Depth = 20.7 mm; Horizontal distance = 20.7 mm. The flaw is located 20.7 mm below the surface and 20.7 mm forward of the index point.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in TOF** — depth must use round-trip time divided by 2.
> 2. **Mixing up $v_L$ and $v_S$** — Normal beam uses longitudinal velocity; angle beam (shear) uses shear velocity. Mismatch yields 50 % error.
> 3. **Confusing "angle in wedge" with "angle in steel"** — Snell's law requires the *refracted* angle in steel, not the wedge incidence.
> 4. **Sign error in critical-angle calculation** — write the equation as $\sin \theta_c = v_1 / v_2$ (slower to faster medium), not the inverse.
> 5. **Neglecting units** — angles in degrees, velocities in m/s, distances in mm — keep consistency.
> 6. **Skipping diagram labels** — KTU examiners award at least 2 marks in part (a) for a clean, labelled schematic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Normal Beam = Longitudinal wave, perpendicular to surface.** Detects defects whose reflective face is **normal to the beam axis**.
- **Straight Beam = Synonym for the longitudinal component** of normal beam inspection; emphasises the *in-line* propagation.
- **Angle Beam = Longitudinal wave from crystal → refracted into shear wave in steel** via a plastic wedge. Used for **weld inspection** (45°, 60°, 70° standard).
- **Snell's Law:** $\sin\theta_1 / v_1 = \sin\theta_2 / v_2$ — governs all refraction and mode conversion.
- **Critical angles in steel (using plexiglas wedge):**  
  First critical ≈ 27.5° (L → surface); Second critical ≈ 57° (S → surface).
- **Shear wave velocity in steel** ≈ 3 255 m/s, **longitudinal** ≈ 5 920 m/s, **Rayleigh** ≈ 2 960 m/s.
- **Near field length** $N = D^2/(4\lambda)$ — place flaws at or just beyond $N$ for best sensitivity.
- **Beam spread** at distance $x$: $B_d = 1.22 \lambda (x/D)$ — important for flaw sizing in the far field.
- **Index point** is the **origin of the sound-path scale** — it is *not* the centre of the crystal; the IIW V1 block is used to locate it.
- **V-path sound path** = thickness / cos θ; **Skip distance** = 2 t tan θ.
- **Couplant is mandatory** — air impedance (~ 0.0004 × 10⁶ Rayl) is negligible compared to steel (~ 46 × 10⁶ Rayl); without couplant, > 99.9 % of energy is reflected.
- **Calibration blocks** — IIW V1 / V2 (international) and ASTM E127 series; reference reflectors: FBH (flat-bottom hole), SDH (side-drilled hole), EDMH (edge-drilled metal hole).
- **DAC / DGS** is used to compensate amplitude for distance, allowing equal-amplitude reporting at varying depths — KTU expects familiarity with the *concept* even if numerical curves are not asked.
- **TR (Transmit-Receive) probes** — separate Tx and Rx crystals; excellent for thin materials and noisy austenitic welds; their pulse-echo response is fundamentally different from a standard probe.
- **Beam identification table for KTU:**  
  *Normal/Straight → L-wave, contact.*  
  *Angle → S-wave (most common), wedge.*  
  *Surface → wedge at critical.*  
  *Lamb → thin plate, special wedge or immersion.*

> **Final Study Tip:** Re-draw the Snell's-law diagram, the V-path geometry, and the near-field/beam-spread diagram from memory before every KTU ESE — they are the most-frequently-asked, highest-marks-yielding figures in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual Crystal Probe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dual Crystal Probe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Dual Crystal Probe** (also known as a **TR Probe**, **Transmit-Receive Probe**, or **Double Crystal Probe**) is a specialized ultrasonic testing transducer that incorporates **two independent piezoelectric crystals** housed within a **single, acoustically-isolated casing**. One crystal acts as the **transmitter (T)** that converts electrical pulses into mechanical ultrasonic waves, while the second crystal functions as the **receiver (R)** that converts the returning ultrasonic echoes back into electrical signals. The two elements are physically separated by an acoustic barrier (typically a sound-absorbing material or acoustic isolator/wedge) to prevent **electromagnetic cross-talk** and **acoustic direct coupling** between them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 PEMET636 syllabus (Module 3: Ultrasonic Testing), Dual Crystal Probes are classified under the family of **special-purpose ultrasonic probes** used to overcome the inherent limitations (dead zone / receiver recovery time) of conventional single-crystal straight-beam or angle-beam probes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long hallway and want to detect how far a wall is from you. Instead of clapping your hands and listening to the echo yourself (**single-crystal pulse-echo probe**), you use **two different friends** — one who claps (transmitter) and another standing next to you who listens (receiver). 

- If your clapping friend (T) is too close to your listening friend (R), the "clap" sound will deafen the listener for a moment, and he will miss nearby echoes — this is the **"dead zone"** problem.
- Solution: **Place a soundproof barrier** between the two friends (the acoustic isolator), and tilt them slightly so their beams cross at a desired depth below the test surface — this is exactly how a Dual Crystal Probe works!

A practical real-world analogy: A Dual Crystal Probe behaves like the **sophisticated sonar on a submarine** — it uses **separate projectors and hydrophones** rather than a single transducer that must switch between modes. The separation eliminates the recovery time gap.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Parameters

The key standard metrics (per **ASTM E1065** and **EN 12668**) used to characterize Dual Crystal Probes are:

- **Nominal Frequency:** Typically **1 MHz – 10 MHz** (most common: **2 MHz, 4 MHz, 5 MHz**)
- **Crystal Size (TX / RX):** Often **different** (e.g., 10 mm × 10 mm TX, 20 mm × 20 mm RX)
- **Index Point / Beam Angle:** Usually defined by the geometry of the delay lines/wedges
- **Depth of Focal Zone:** Typically **15 mm – 25 mm** below the test surface
- **Delay Line Material:** Perspex, Rexolite, or epoxy resin (velocity ≈ **2730 m/s** for Perspex)
- **Acoustic Isolator Material:** Cork, rubber, or air gap (impedance ≈ very low)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** The dead-zone measurement for a dual-element contact probe is governed by **ASTM E127**, and the overall transducer performance is governed by **ASTM E1065**.

### 1.4 Classification of Dual Crystal Probes

| Type | Construction | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Contact Type** | Both crystals on a single delay line block | Surface inspection of plates, bars |
| **Immersion Type** | Crystals immersed in water couplant | Automated scanning of components |
| **Angle-Beam Dual** | Crystals on angled wedges with sound crossing path | Weld inspection, corrosion mapping |
| **Normal-Beam Dual** | Coaxial crystals with acoustic barrier | Thickness gauging of thin materials |

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Structured Logic Breakdown

The operational sequence of a Dual Crystal Probe can be broken down into the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Electrical Excitation (TX Side):**
The pulser/receiver unit sends a high-voltage, short-duration electrical spike (typically **100 V – 400 V**, **< 1 μs** rise time) to the **transmitting crystal**. The piezoelectric material (commonly PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) converts this electrical energy into a mechanical vibration via the **inverse piezoelectric effect**.

- **Step 2 — Ultrasonic Pulse Generation:**
The TX crystal vibrates at its **resonant frequency** $f_0$ and launches a longitudinal ultrasonic wave packet into the **delay line** (typically Perspex, $V_L \approx 2730$ m/s). The wavelength inside the delay line is:
$$\lambda_d = \frac{V_d}{f_0}$$

- **Step 3 — Refraction at Delay Line / Test Material Interface:**
Because the delay line and the test material (e.g., steel) have different acoustic velocities, the beam **refracts** at the interface. By **Snell's Law of Acoustics**:
$$\frac{\sin \theta_d}{V_d} = \frac{\sin \theta_m}{V_m}$$
For a typical Dual Crystal Probe, the wedge angle is chosen so the sound in the test material propagates at $\theta_m \approx \mathbf{0^\circ}$ (normal incidence) but the transmit and receive beams **cross at a specific focal depth**.

- **Step 4 — Beam Cross-Over (Focal Zone):**
The TX beam converges and crosses the RX beam at a precisely engineered **focal depth** $D_f$ below the test surface. This is the region of **maximum sensitivity**. The acoustic isolator between the two crystals physically prevents the TX beam from directly impinging on the RX crystal (avoiding saturation/ringing).

- **Step 5 — Reflection from Flaw or Back Surface:**
When the ultrasonic wave encounters an acoustic-impedance mismatch (a flaw, inclusion, crack, or the back wall), part of the energy is reflected. For a flaw at depth $d$, the total round-trip path is longer for the dual probe due to the V-shaped path.

- **Step 6 — Reception (RX Side):**
The reflected echo enters the **receiver crystal** through its delay line. The mechanical vibration is converted back into an electrical signal via the **direct piezoelectric effect**. The receiver is electrically decoupled from the TX pulser to prevent the transmit pulse from saturating the receiver amplifier.

- **Step 7 — Display and Interpretation:**
The received signal is amplified, displayed on the A-scan CRT/LCD, and interpreted by the operator. The horizontal time base, calibrated using the **velocity of sound** in the test material, gives the **depth location** of the reflector.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| Acoustic Isolator | Without it, the TX pulse would couple directly into RX, blinding the receiver for the **first few microseconds** — eliminating any chance to detect near-surface flaws. |
| Separate TX/RX Crystals | Allows **independent optimization** of TX power and RX sensitivity (RX can be made larger and more sensitive than TX). |
| Delay Line | Provides a known **time delay** so the receiver can recover from the initial transmit pulse before any echoes arrive. This is the key trick that **minimizes the dead zone**. |
| Angled Geometry | Allows the TX and RX beams to cross at a controlled depth, ensuring the **focal zone** lies within the material being inspected (not on the surface). |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield — they appear repeatedly in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \times V$ | $\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ or **Rayl** ($\times 10^6$) | $\rho$ = density, $V$ = sound velocity |
| 2 | Reflection Coefficient (pressure) | $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Dimensionless | At normal incidence |
| 3 | Transmission Coefficient | $T = \dfrac{2Z_2}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ | Dimensionless | At normal incidence |
| 4 | Snell's Law (Acoustic) | $\dfrac{\sin\theta_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{\sin\theta_2}{V_2}$ | Degrees | Defines beam refraction |
| 5 | Wavelength in Medium | $\lambda = \dfrac{V}{f}$ | metres | $f$ = frequency |
| 6 | Near-Field Length (N) | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda} = \dfrac{D^2 f}{4V}$ | metres | $D$ = crystal diameter |
| 7 | Beam Divergence Angle (half-angle) | $\sin \alpha = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ | radians | Far-field divergence |
| 8 | Depth from Time-of-Flight | $d = \dfrac{V \cdot t}{2}$ | metres | For **straight-beam pulse-echo** |
| 9 | **Dual Probe Path Length** | $L = \dfrac{V_m \cdot t}{2 \cos\theta_m}$ | metres | For **angled dual probe** |
| 10 | **Beam Cross-Over Depth (focal)** | $D_f \approx \dfrac{r}{\tan\theta}$ | metres | $r$ = crystal separation, $\theta$ = half-beam angle |
| 11 | Dead-Zone (single crystal) | $DZ = \dfrac{V \cdot t_r}{2}$ | metres | $t_r$ = receiver recovery time |
| 12 | Dead-Zone (dual crystal) | $DZ \approx \dfrac{V_m \cdot t_{iso}}{2}$ | metres | $t_{iso}$ = isolator transit time (very small) |
| 13 | Resolution (axial) | $\delta_{ax} = \dfrac{n \lambda}{2}$ | metres | $n$ = number of cycles in pulse |
| 14 | Index Point Offset | $I = V_d \cdot \Delta t$ | metres | $\Delta t$ = acoustic delay difference |

> [!WARNING]
> In the **markdown table above**, all absolute-value-style notation has been rendered using `\vert` / `\mid` to comply with markdown pipe-escaping rules.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dual Crystal Probes are the **workhorses** of the following NDT applications:

- **Corrosion Mapping:** Locating wall-thickness loss in pipelines, storage tanks, and pressure vessels (e.g., oil & gas industry).
- **Thin-Section Thickness Gauging:** Measuring sheets as thin as **0.5 mm** — impossible with conventional contact probes due to dead zone.
- **Weld Inspection (Cap and Root):** Both top-side and bottom-side surface flaws can be detected due to the suppressed dead zone.
- **Composite Material Testing:** Detecting delamination, porosity, and inclusions in CFRP / GFRP laminates used in aerospace.
- **In-Service Inspection:** Critical for **fitness-for-service** assessments per **API 570** and **API 510**.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Reflection and Transmission Coefficients at a Normal-Incidence Interface

Consider a plane longitudinal wave traveling in **Medium 1** (acoustic impedance $Z_1$) and striking a flat interface with **Medium 2** (acoustic impedance $Z_2$) at **normal incidence**. We wish to derive the fraction of pressure amplitude reflected back into Medium 1 and transmitted into Medium 2.

**Boundary Conditions** (continuity of pressure and particle velocity at the interface):

$$p_i + p_r = p_t \quad \text{(pressure continuity)}$$

$$\frac{p_i}{Z_1} - \frac{p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2} \quad \text{(particle velocity continuity)}$$

where $p_i$ = incident pressure amplitude, $p_r$ = reflected pressure amplitude, $p_t$ = transmitted pressure amplitude.

**Step 1:** From the velocity equation, multiply throughout by $Z_1 Z_2$:

$$p_i Z_2 - p_r Z_2 = p_t Z_1$$

**Step 2:** From the pressure equation, multiply throughout by $Z_2$:

$$p_i Z_2 + p_r Z_2 = p_t Z_2$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the velocity-derived equation from the pressure-derived equation:

$$(p_i Z_2 + p_r Z_2) - (p_i Z_2 - p_r Z_2) = p_t Z_2 - p_t Z_1$$

$$2 p_r Z_2 = p_t (Z_2 - Z_1)$$

**Step 4:** Add the two multiplied equations instead, to eliminate $p_r$:

$$(p_i Z_2 + p_r Z_2) + (p_i Z_2 - p_r Z_2) = p_t Z_2 + p_t Z_1$$

$$2 p_i Z_2 = p_t (Z_1 + Z_2)$$

Therefore:

$$p_t = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}\, p_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{p_t}{p_i} = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $p_t$ back into the pressure boundary equation:

$$p_i + p_r = p_t = \frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} p_i$$

$$p_r = p_i \left(\frac{2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} - 1\right) = p_i \left(\frac{2 Z_2 - Z_1 - Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{p_r}{p_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}}$$

**Numerical Verification:** For a steel–air interface ($Z_{\text{steel}} \approx 46 \times 10^6$ Rayl, $Z_{\text{air}} \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6$ Rayl):

$$R = \frac{0.0004 - 46}{0.0004 + 46} \approx -0.99998$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** on reflection, and $|R| \approx 1$ confirms that **nearly 100% of the acoustic energy is reflected** at a steel–air boundary — which is why **air gaps in a couplant are devastating** to ultrasonic inspection.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Depth Calculation in an Angled Dual Crystal Probe

Unlike a straight-beam probe, the sound path in a Dual Crystal Probe is **V-shaped**. The TX beam travels at angle $\theta_m$ to the normal, reflects from a flaw, and returns to the RX crystal at angle $\theta_m$ on the other side.

Let:
- $V_m$ = longitudinal wave velocity in the test material
- $t$ = measured time-of-flight on the A-scan (between TX pulse and flaw echo)
- $\theta_m$ = refraction angle in the test material (relative to surface normal)

**Step 1 — One-Way Path Length:**

The acoustic path from TX crystal to the flaw is a straight line inclined at angle $\theta_m$ to the normal. If the **slant distance** from the test surface to the flaw is $L_s$, then:

$$L_s = V_m \cdot \frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Vertical Depth:**

The true vertical depth (measured perpendicular to the test surface) is:

$$\boxed{d = L_s \cos\theta_m = \frac{V_m \cdot t}{2 \cos\theta_m}}$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal Surface Distance (Sound Path Entry Point Offset):**

The horizontal offset from the probe's index point to the flaw location on the surface is:

$$x = L_s \sin\theta_m = \frac{V_m \cdot t}{2} \tan\theta_m$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A Dual Crystal Probe operating at **4 MHz** is used to inspect a **steel plate** ($V_L = 5920$ m/s, $V_S = 3250$ m/s). The probe's wedges are designed such that in steel, the longitudinal wave travels at a refraction angle of **$\theta_m = 12^\circ$**. The flaw echo arrives at **$t = 6.8 \,\mu s$** on the A-scan. Calculate:
> (a) The one-way slant distance to the flaw.
> (b) The vertical depth of the flaw below the test surface.
> (c) The wavelength of the ultrasonic wave in the steel.
> (d) Whether this flaw is **resolvable** given an axial resolution criterion of $\delta_{ax} = 2\lambda$.

---

**(a) One-Way Slant Distance:**

$$L_s = \frac{V_L \cdot t}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 6.8 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$L_s = \frac{0.040256}{2} = 0.020128 \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{20.13 \text{ mm}}$$

**(b) Vertical Depth:**

$$d = L_s \cos\theta_m = 0.020128 \times \cos(12^\circ)$$

$$d = 0.020128 \times 0.97815 = \mathbf{0.01969 \text{ m} \approx 19.69 \text{ mm}}$$

**(c) Wavelength in Steel (Longitudinal):**

$$\lambda_L = \frac{V_L}{f} = \frac{5920}{4 \times 10^6} = 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = \mathbf{1.48 \text{ mm}}$$

**(d) Axial Resolution:**

$$\delta_{ax} = 2 \lambda_L = 2 \times 1.48 = 2.96 \text{ mm}$$

Since the vertical depth difference between two adjacent flaws must exceed **2.96 mm** for them to be resolved, and our single flaw is at **19.69 mm**, the probe **can resolve** any flaw whose neighbouring reflector is at least 2.96 mm further or shallower. **The flaw is well within the resolvable range.**

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Dead-Zone Comparison (Single Crystal vs. Dual Crystal)

**Single-Crystal Probe — Dead Zone:**

After a transmit pulse, the **same crystal** must wait for mechanical ringing to decay before it can detect an echo. This ringing time is called the **recovery time** $t_r$. The corresponding "blind" distance is:

$$DZ_{\text{single}} = \frac{V \cdot t_r}{2}$$

For a typical $t_r \approx 5 \,\mu s$ in steel:

$$DZ_{\text{single}} = \frac{5920 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \approx 14.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Dual-Crystal Probe — Dead Zone:**

In a dual probe, the **TX and RX crystals are decoupled** by the acoustic isolator. The receiver does not experience the full mechanical ringing of the transmitter. The residual dead zone is governed mainly by the **transit time through the isolator**:

$$DZ_{\text{dual}} = \frac{V_m \cdot t_{\text{iso}}}{2}$$

For a cork isolator ($V \approx 500$ m/s, thickness $d_{\text{iso}} = 3$ mm):

$$t_{\text{iso}} = \frac{d_{\text{iso}}}{V_{\text{iso}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{500} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 6 \,\mu s$$

Wait — this gives $DZ_{\text{dual}} \approx \frac{5920 \times 6 \times 10^{-6}}{2} \approx 17.8$ mm, which is *larger* than the single-crystal case. The reason: the **sound must still travel from the test surface through the focal zone back to the RX crystal**. The actual *near-surface resolution* improvement is because the TX pulse **does not saturate** the RX amplifier, allowing the RX to be receptive immediately. The effective dead zone is **reduced to ~1–2 mm**, sufficient for thin-sheet inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> The improvement in **near-surface resolution** in a Dual Crystal Probe is achieved primarily through **electrical isolation of TX and RX circuits**, not through acoustic isolation alone. This is a frequently tested conceptual point in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dual Crystal Probe

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pulser["PULSER / RECEIVER UNIT"]
        P["High-Voltage Pulser"]
        RCV["Receiver Amplifier"]
        DSP["Time-Base / A-Scan Display"]
    end

    subgraph probe["DUAL CRYSTAL PROBE HOUSING"]
        TXC["Transmit Crystal PZT"]
        WDG1["TX Delay Line Wedge"]
        ISOL["Acoustic Barrier / Isolator (Cork/Rubber)"]
        WDG2["RX Delay Line Wedge"]
        RXC["Receive Crystal PZT"]
    end

    subgraph mat["TEST MATERIAL STEEL PLATE"]
        TXB["TX Ultrasonic Beam angled path"]
        FOC["Beam Cross-Over Focal Zone Depth Df"]
        RXB["RX Echo Beam"]
        FLW["Reflector Flaw / Back Wall"]
    end

    P -->|electrical spike| TXC
    TXC -->|mech vibration| WDG1
    WDG1 -->|refracted wave| TXB
    TXB -->|travels in steel| FOC
    FOC -->|encounters| FLW
    FLW -->|reflected echo| RXB
    RXB -->|returns through wedge| RXC
    RXC -->|electrical signal| RCV
    RCV -->|amplified| DSP
    ISOL -.blocks direct coupling.-> WDG1
    ISOL -.blocks direct coupling.-> WDG2
```

### 4.2 Beam Path Geometry — V-Path in Test Material

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["TX Crystal Surface"] -->|theta_m| B["Flaw Location Depth d"]
    B -->|theta_m| C["RX Crystal Surface"]
    subgraph geometry["PATH GEOMETRY"]
        G1["Slant Distance Ls = Vm x t / 2"]
        G2["Vertical Depth d = Ls cos theta_m"]
        G3["Horizontal Offset x = Ls sin theta_m"]
        G4["Focal Depth Df where TX and RX beams cross"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pulser Trigger"] --> B["TX Crystal Mechanical Vibration"]
    B --> C["Delay Line Wedge TX"]
    C --> D["Test Material Propagation"]
    D --> E{"Reflector Present"}
    E -->|Yes| F["Echo Returns"]
    E -->|No| G["No Echo"]
    F --> H["Delay Line Wedge RX"]
    H --> I["RX Crystal Mechanical Vibration"]
    I --> J["Electrical Signal Output"]
    J --> K["Receiver Amplifier"]
    K --> L["A-Scan Display"]
    G --> L
```

### 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Single vs. Dual Crystal Probe

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph single["SINGLE CRYSTAL PROBE"]
        S1["Single PZT Crystal"]
        S2["Switches between TX and RX"]
        S3["Dead Zone typically 10 to 20 mm"]
    end
    subgraph dual["DUAL CRYSTAL PROBE"]
        D1["Separate TX Crystal"]
        D2["Separate RX Crystal"]
        D3["Acoustic Isolator Between"]
        D4["Dead Zone reduced to 1 to 3 mm"]
    end
    single -.limitations.- LIM["Cannot detect near-surface flaws"]
    dual -.advantage.- ADV["Excellent near-surface resolution"]
    LIM --> ADV
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define a **Dual Crystal Probe**. Mention **any two advantages** it has over a conventional single-crystal pulse-echo probe.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Dual Crystal Probe is an ultrasonic testing transducer that contains **two independent piezoelectric elements** within a single housing — one for transmitting (T) and one for receiving (R) the ultrasonic signal — separated by an acoustic barrier.

**Two advantages (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Reduced Dead Zone:** The receiver does not experience the full ringing of the transmitter, allowing detection of flaws located **very close to the test surface** (within 1–3 mm), which a single-crystal probe would miss.
2. **Independent Optimization:** The TX and RX crystals can be **independently sized and optimized** — for instance, a larger RX crystal gives higher sensitivity, while a smaller TX crystal gives finer resolution. This is not possible in a single-crystal probe.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

What is the role of the **acoustic isolator** in a Dual Crystal Probe? Why is it essential?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **acoustic isolator** (typically cork, rubber, or an air gap) is a low-impedance barrier placed between the TX and RX crystals. **(1 mark)** Its role is to **prevent the ultrasonic energy transmitted by the TX crystal from coupling directly into the RX crystal** through the housing. **(1 mark)** Without the isolator, the receiver would be saturated by the transmitted pulse and unable to detect faint returning echoes for several microseconds — eliminating the probe's ability to detect **near-surface flaws**. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **construction and working principle** of a Dual Crystal Probe with the help of a **neat labelled sketch**. Discuss the function of each major component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **4 MHz** Dual Crystal Probe with a refraction angle of **$\theta_m = 10^\circ$** in steel ($V_L = 5900$ m/s) is used to inspect a weld. A flaw echo is observed at a time-of-flight of **$t = 8.4 \,\mu s$**.
Calculate:
1. The **one-way slant distance** to the flaw.
2. The **vertical depth** of the flaw below the inspection surface.
3. The **horizontal offset** from the probe index point.
4. The **wavelength** of the ultrasonic wave in steel.
5. Comment on the **resolution capability** of the probe.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

**Construction:** A Dual Crystal Probe consists of the following major components (describe each — 4 marks):

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Transmit Crystal (TX)** | Piezoelectric element (usually PZT) that converts electrical pulses into ultrasonic waves via the **inverse piezoelectric effect**. |
| **Receive Crystal (RX)** | Separate piezoelectric element that converts returning ultrasonic echoes back into electrical signals via the **direct piezoelectric effect**. |
| **Acoustic Isolator / Barrier** | A wedge of cork, rubber, or air gap that prevents direct acoustic coupling between TX and RX, eliminating receiver saturation. |
| **Delay Line Wedges (TX and RX)** | Made of Perspex or Rexolite; provide a known acoustic delay, refract the beam to a specific angle, and protect the crystals from wear. |
| **Probe Housing (Case)** | Metallic enclosure that holds components in precise alignment, provides electrical shielding, and mechanical protection. |
| **Connector / Cable** | Coaxial cable connection to the pulser-receiver unit. |
| **Wear Plate** (optional) | Replaceable plastic face to extend probe life. |

**Working Principle (3 marks):** The pulser sends an electrical spike to the TX crystal, which generates an ultrasonic pulse that travels through the TX delay line, refracts into the test material at angle $\theta_m$, propagates through the test piece, and reflects from any flaw or back wall. The reflected echo re-enters the probe through the RX delay line and strikes the RX crystal, which converts it back into an electrical signal displayed on the A-scan.

**[Sketch – 2 marks (to be drawn by student on answer paper):** A neat diagram showing the housing, TX and RX crystals at angle, acoustic isolator in the middle, delay lines, and the V-shaped beam path crossing at the focal depth inside the test material.**]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $f = 4 \text{ MHz}$, $\theta_m = 10^\circ$, $V_L = 5900$ m/s, $t = 8.4 \,\mu s$.

**Step 1 — One-way slant distance:** (1.5 marks)

$$L_s = \frac{V_L \cdot t}{2} = \frac{5900 \times 8.4 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

$$L_s = \frac{0.04956}{2} = 0.02478 \text{ m} = \mathbf{24.78 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 2 — Vertical depth:** (1.5 marks)

$$d = L_s \cos\theta_m = 0.02478 \times \cos(10^\circ) = 0.02478 \times 0.98481$$

$$d = \mathbf{0.02440 \text{ m} \approx 24.40 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Horizontal offset:** (1.5 marks)

$$x = L_s \sin\theta_m = 0.02478 \times \sin(10^\circ) = 0.02478 \times 0.17365$$

$$x = \mathbf{0.004303 \text{ m} \approx 4.30 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Wavelength in steel:** (1 mark)

$$\lambda_L = \frac{V_L}{f} = \frac{5900}{4 \times 10^6} = 1.475 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = \mathbf{1.475 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Resolution comment:** (1.5 marks)

The axial resolution is approximately $2\lambda_L = 2.95$ mm. The flaw is at depth 24.40 mm, well within the probe's resolvable range. **Two flaws separated by more than ~2.95 mm vertically can be distinguished by this probe.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Single Crystal** and **Dual Crystal** ultrasonic probes with respect to:
(i) Construction
(ii) Dead zone
(iii) Application suitability
(iv) Cost
(v) Sensitivity
(vi) Beam path geometry
(vii) Near-surface flaw detection capability

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **2.25 MHz** Dual Crystal Probe is used to inspect an **aluminium plate** ($V_L = 6320$ m/s, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³). The wedge material is Perspex ($V_d = 2730$ m/s). The wedge angle is **$\theta_d = 30^\circ$**.
1. Find the **refraction angle** in aluminium using Snell's Law.
2. Calculate the **acoustic impedance** of aluminium.
3. Calculate the **reflection coefficient** at the Perspex–Aluminium interface (from Perspex to Aluminium).
4. A flaw echo is received at **$t = 5.6 \,\mu s$**. Calculate the **slant distance** and **vertical depth** of the flaw.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Parameter | Single Crystal Probe | Dual Crystal Probe |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | One crystal acts as both TX and RX (with a TR switch). | Two separate crystals; one TX, one RX, with acoustic isolator. |
| **Dead Zone** | Large (~10–20 mm in steel). | Very small (~1–3 mm in steel). |
| **Application Suitability** | General-purpose inspection, deep flaws. | Thin sections, near-surface flaws, thickness gauging. |
| **Cost** | Lower (simpler construction). | Higher (two crystals + precision alignment). |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate; constrained by trade-off between TX power and RX sensitivity. | High; TX and RX can be independently optimized (RX often larger). |
| **Beam Path Geometry** | Straight coaxial beam (or angled via single wedge). | V-shaped beam; TX and RX beams cross at focal depth. |
| **Near-Surface Flaw Detection** | Poor due to dead zone. | Excellent due to electrical isolation of TX and RX. |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $f = 2.25$ MHz, $V_L^{\text{Al}} = 6320$ m/s, $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2700$ kg/m³, $V_d^{\text{Perspex}} = 2730$ m/s, $\theta_d = 30^\circ$, $t = 5.6 \,\mu s$.

**Step 1 — Refraction angle in aluminium (Snell's Law):** (2 marks)

$$\frac{\sin\theta_d}{V_d} = \frac{\sin\theta_m}{V_m}$$

$$\sin\theta_m = \frac{V_m \sin\theta_d}{V_d} = \frac{6320 \times \sin(30^\circ)}{2730} = \frac{6320 \times 0.5}{2730} = \frac{3160}{2730}$$

$$\sin\theta_m = 1.1575$$

Since $\sin\theta_m > 1$, the result is **physically impossible**. This indicates that **Perspex to Aluminium produces a longitudinal wave at an angle that exceeds the critical angle** — there is **total internal reflection** of the longitudinal wave at the Perspex-Aluminium interface, and instead a **shear (transverse) wave** is generated in aluminium. (1 mark)

For shear wave generation, we use $V_S^{\text{Al}} = 3130$ m/s (standard):

$$\sin\theta_m^{(S)} = \frac{3130 \times \sin(30^\circ)}{2730} = \frac{1565}{2730} = 0.5733$$

$$\theta_m^{(S)} = \arcsin(0.5733) = \mathbf{34.96^\circ}$$

**Step 2 — Acoustic impedance of aluminium:** (1.5 marks)

$$Z_{\text{Al}} = \rho_{\text{Al}} \times V_L^{\text{Al}} = 2700 \times 6320 = 1.7064 \times 10^7 \text{ Rayl} = \mathbf{17.06 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}}$$

**Step 3 — Reflection coefficient at Perspex–Aluminium interface:** (1.5 marks)

First, impedance of Perspex ($\rho_d \approx 1180$ kg/m³):

$$Z_d = 1180 \times 2730 = 3.221 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$

$$R = \frac{Z_{\text{Al}} - Z_d}{Z_{\text{Al}} + Z_d} = \frac{17.06 - 3.22}{17.06 + 3.22} \times 10^6$$

$$R = \frac{13.84}{20.28} = \mathbf{0.6825 \text{ (positive — no phase inversion)}}$$

**Step 4 — Slant distance and vertical depth:** (2 marks)

Using shear wave velocity $V_S = 3130$ m/s:

$$L_s = \frac{V_S \cdot t}{2} = \frac{3130 \times 5.6 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.017528}{2} = \mathbf{8.764 \text{ mm}}$$

$$d = L_s \cos\theta_m^{(S)} = 8.764 \times \cos(34.96^\circ) = 8.764 \times 0.8192 = \mathbf{7.180 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Dual Crystal Probe Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in depth calculation** — students often write $d = V \cdot t$ instead of $d = V \cdot t / 2$. This loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Not dividing by $\cos\theta_m$ for angled dual probes** — the V-shaped path requires correction; the simple straight-beam formula gives the *slant distance*, not the *vertical depth*.
> 3. **Confusing longitudinal and shear wave velocities** — if Snell's Law gives $\sin\theta > 1$ for longitudinal, you **must switch to shear wave velocity**. Marks are lost when this is missed.
> 4. **Calling the acoustic isolator "soundproofing"** — examiners expect the term **"acoustic barrier"** or **"acoustic isolator"**. Avoid casual terminology.
> 5. **Omitting the sketch in Part B (a)** — a **neat labelled diagram is worth 2 marks**. Examiners explicitly state in the valuation key that a missing sketch incurs a **2-mark penalty**.
> 6. **Not mentioning the delay line's role** — the delay line provides both **mechanical protection** *and* **time delay** for receiver recovery. Many students mention only one.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Dual Crystal Probe** has **two independent piezoelectric crystals** in one housing, separated by an **acoustic isolator**.
- ✅ The **acoustic isolator** prevents direct coupling of TX energy into the RX crystal, eliminating receiver saturation.
- ✅ The **dead zone** of a dual probe is **much smaller (1–3 mm)** than a single-crystal probe (10–20 mm).
- ✅ Dual probes excel at **near-surface flaw detection** and **thin-section thickness gauging**.
- ✅ The **delay line** (Perspex/Rexolite) provides **mechanical protection** + **acoustic time delay** + **beam refraction**.
- ✅ The beam path in the test material is **V-shaped**, crossing at a **focal depth** $D_f$.
- ✅ Key formulas: $d = \dfrac{V_m t}{2 \cos\theta_m}$, $\lambda = \dfrac{V}{f}$, $R = \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$.
- ✅ Snell's Law: $\dfrac{\sin\theta_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{\sin\theta_2}{V_2}$.
- ✅ If $\sin\theta_m > 1$ for longitudinal, switch to **shear wave** velocity.
- ✅ Standard test frequency range: **1 MHz to 10 MHz**.
- ✅ Standards governing dual probes: **ASTM E1065**, **ASTM E127**, **EN 12668**.
- ✅ Applications: corrosion mapping, weld inspection, composite testing, thin-sheet thickness gauging.
- ✅ Acoustic impedance unit: **Rayl** (= kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹); often quoted in **Mega-Rayl (×10⁶)**.
- ✅ Remember the **factor of 2** in every round-trip distance/time calculation — examiner's favourite trick.
- ✅ Common TX / RX crystal materials: **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)**.
- ✅ Always draw a **neat labelled sketch** in Part B questions — it's worth **2 marks** minimum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Testing Techniques: Resonance, Through Transmission, and Pulse Echo
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Testing Techniques: Resonance, Through Transmission, and Pulse Echo

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Ultrasonic Testing (UT) Techniques

**Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** is a family of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods that use high-frequency mechanical waves (typically **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**, well above the human audible limit of ~20 kHz) propagating through a test material to detect surface and subsurface defects, measure thickness, and evaluate material properties.

Within Module 3 of PEMET636, three classical UT techniques are prescribed:

1. **Resonance Testing (Resonance Method)**
2. **Through Transmission Testing (Transmission Method)**
3. **Pulse-Echo Testing (Reflection / Pulse-Echo Method)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Verbatim):** *"Ultrasonic testing utilizes the propagation of high-frequency sound waves in materials to detect surface and sub-surface flaws and to measure thickness. The three principal methods used are the Resonance method, Through-Transmission method, and Pulse-Echo method."*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Bats, Doctors, and Drummers"**
> Imagine you are blindfolded in a large empty hall. You clap once (**Pulse-Echo**), and you listen for the echo to estimate how far the wall is. Alternatively, you could ask a friend at the opposite end to clap and you both listen simultaneously (**Through Transmission**). Finally, you could hum a continuous note and walk towards the wall — when your humming synchronizes with the echo, you feel a vibration amplify (**Resonance**). Each technique tells you something different about the space (or material) and any obstacle (flaw) inside it.

| Technique | Analogy | Key Variable Measured |
|---|---|---|
| Resonance | Drummer finding room resonance | Thickness (whole-number half-wavelengths) |
| Through Transmission | Two friends shouting across a field | Amplitude drop across material |
| Pulse-Echo | Sonar / bat / shouting in a canyon | Time-of-flight of reflected wave |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- Speed of longitudinal sound in **steel**: **5920 m/s**
- Speed of shear sound in **steel**: **3255 m/s**
- Speed of longitudinal sound in **aluminum**: **6320 m/s**
- Speed of longitudinal sound in **water (couplant)**: **1480 m/s**
- Typical inspection frequency: **1 MHz – 10 MHz**
- Acoustic Impedance of steel: **Z = 46 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)**
- Acoustic Impedance formula: **Z = ρ · c** (kg/m²·s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter:** In the KTU board exam, you are often given the material and asked to compute either the wavelength, the depth of a flaw, or the resonant thickness. Memorising the speed-of-sound values for **steel** and **aluminum** is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Resonance Testing — Theory of Operation

The **Resonance Method** employs a **continuous-wave (CW)** ultrasonic signal whose frequency is swept (varied). When the wavelength of the sound inside the test piece becomes exactly equal to a rational fraction of the part thickness, **standing waves** form, producing a sharp peak in the transducer's electrical impedance or response.

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

1. A piezoelectric transducer coupled to the test piece emits continuous ultrasonic waves.
2. The frequency of the generator is **swept** (typically 0.5 – 10 MHz) by an oscillator.
3. Each time the part thickness $t$ becomes an integer multiple of half-wavelengths, resonance occurs.
4. The operator observes resonance peaks on the meter/screen and counts them.
5. Knowing the resonant frequency and number of peaks, thickness is computed.

### 2.1.2 Resonance Condition — The Core Equation

For a part of thickness $t$, resonance occurs when:

$$
t = n \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}, \quad \text{where } n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

where $\lambda$ is the ultrasonic wavelength inside the material. Combining with $\lambda = c / f$, we obtain:

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{n \cdot c}{2 f_n}}
$$

where:
- $t$ = thickness of the part (m)
- $c$ = velocity of sound in the material (m/s)
- $f_n$ = frequency of the $n^{th}$ resonance peak (Hz)
- $n$ = peak number (positive integer)

### 2.1.3 Practical Use — Single-Peak vs. Multi-Peak Method

- **Single-peak method**: Operate at a known frequency $f_1$; thickness is $t = c / (2 f_1)$ when the *first* resonance is observed.
- **Multi-peak method**: Identify two successive resonances $f_n$ and $f_{n+1}$; thickness is computed as:

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{c}{2 (f_{n+1} - f_n)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **multi-peak method is more accurate** because errors in identifying the exact peak number cancel out. KTU examiners frequently test this formula.

---

## 2.2 Through-Transmission Testing — Theory of Operation

**Through-Transmission (TT)** uses **two transducers**: a **transmitter** on one face and a **receiver** on the opposite face. A defect anywhere along the path reduces, attenuates, or completely blocks the received signal.

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

1. The transmitter injects a continuous or pulsed ultrasonic beam into the test piece.
2. The beam travels through the bulk of the material.
3. The receiver, mounted on the opposite face, picks up the transmitted energy.
4. The receiver's output amplitude is monitored on a CRT/LCD or meter.
5. **Any drop in received amplitude** indicates a defect (since sound is reflected, scattered, or absorbed by the flaw).

### 2.2.2 Defect-Size Estimation

If a flaw of projected area $A_f$ blocks the beam, and the beam has cross-section $A_b$, the transmitted intensity falls roughly to:

$$
I_{received} = I_0 \left(1 - \frac{A_f}{A_b}\right)
$$

In practice, the **receiver output voltage** is what is measured, and it is calibrated against reference blocks.

### 2.2.3 Advantages and Limitations

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| Coupling | One side only needs couplant on each face; dual transducer arrangement |
| Sensitivity | High — defects anywhere in the volume are detected |
| Sizing | Cannot easily determine flaw depth; only detects presence and gross size |
| Use case | Billets, plates, sheets, simple geometry |

---

## 2.3 Pulse-Echo Testing — Theory of Operation

**Pulse-Echo (PE)** is the most widely used UT method. A short-duration **pulse** is emitted into the material; reflections from the back surface (and any internal flaws) return as **echoes** to the same transducer (acting as both transmitter and receiver via a duplexer).

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle — Step by Step

1. The pulser sends a short, high-voltage pulse to the piezoelectric transducer.
2. The transducer converts the electrical pulse into an ultrasonic pulse (~ 1 µs wide).
3. The pulse travels into the material; part of it reflects at the back wall (back-echo).
4. Any flaw along the path reflects part of the energy as an **flaw echo** that arrives *earlier* than the back-echo.
5. The transducer (now in receive mode) detects these echoes and converts them back into voltage.
6. The horizontal axis of the display represents **time**, calibrated to **distance** using $d = c \cdot t / 2$.

### 2.3.2 The Master Distance Equation

Because the sound travels from the transducer to the reflector and back, the time-of-flight gives the **round-trip distance**:

$$
\boxed{d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}}
$$

where:
- $d$ = distance from transducer face to reflector (m)
- $c$ = velocity of sound in the material (m/s)
- $\Delta t$ = measured time-of-flight (s)

### 2.3.3 Dead Zone, Near Field & Resolution

- **Dead zone** = the region immediately below the transducer in which echoes cannot be resolved (because the transducer is still "ringing" after transmission).
- **Near field (Fresnel zone)** distance $N$:

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{D^2 - \lambda^2}{4 \lambda} \approx \frac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \frac{D^2 f}{4 c}}
$$

where $D$ = transducer diameter, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $f$ = frequency.

- **Beam spread angle (divergence half-angle)** $\sin \theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Single-Page Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU 2024 Module 3. Memorise the units, not just the symbols.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c$: m/s, $f$: Hz | All three methods |
| 2 | Resonance condition | $t = n\lambda/2$ | $t$: m, $n$: integer | Resonance |
| 3 | Single-peak resonance | $t = c/(2 f_1)$ | $f_1$: Hz | Resonance |
| 4 | Multi-peak resonance | $t = c / [2(f_{n+1} - f_n)]$ | $f$: Hz | Resonance |
| 5 | Acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | $\rho$: kg/m³, $c$: m/s | All three |
| 6 | Pulse-Echo distance | $d = c \Delta t / 2$ | $\Delta t$: s | Pulse-Echo |
| 7 | Near field length | $N \approx D^2 f / (4 c)$ | $D$: m, $f$: Hz, $c$: m/s | Pulse-Echo |
| 8 | Beam divergence | $\sin \theta = 1.22 \lambda / D$ | $\theta$: radians | Pulse-Echo |
| 9 | Intensity through defect | $I = I_0(1 - A_f/A_b)$ | $A$: m² | Through-Transmission |
| 10 | Snell's law (refraction) | $\sin \theta_1 / c_1 = \sin \theta_2 / c_2$ | Angles in rad | Pulse-Echo (angle beam) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Pulse-Echo is the backbone of flaw detectors used in aerospace (wing-skin inspections), petrochemical (pipeline weld scanning), and railway (axle/rail testing). Through-Transmission is the workhorse for plates and billets. Resonance remains the standard for **rapid thickness gauging of thin sheets and tubes** where the entire wall thickness can be made to resonate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Resonance Condition

We start from the physics of a standing wave in a bounded medium of thickness $t$. The wave equation for a longitudinal wave in a 1-D rod is:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}
$$

A standing-wave solution is of the form:

$$
u(x, t) = A \sin(kx) \cos(\omega t)
$$

Applying the **boundary conditions** that the two faces of the part are free surfaces (stress-free), the particle velocity must be maximum at $x = 0$ and $x = t$. This means:

$$
k \cdot t = n \pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

Substituting $k = 2 \pi / \lambda$:

$$
\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot t = n \pi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad t = \frac{n \lambda}{2}
$$

Now using $\lambda = c / f_n$:

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{n c}{2 f_n}}
$$

This is the **fundamental resonance equation** for a free-free plate of thickness $t$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Multi-Peak Thickness Formula

Consider two successive resonance peaks, $n$ and $n+1$, at frequencies $f_n$ and $f_{n+1}$ respectively.

$$
t = \frac{n c}{2 f_n} = \frac{(n+1) c}{2 f_{n+1}}
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
\frac{n}{f_n} = \frac{n+1}{f_{n+1}}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
n f_{n+1} = (n+1) f_n
$$

Solving for $n$:

$$
n = \frac{f_n}{f_{n+1} - f_n}
$$

Substituting back into the single-peak formula:

$$
t = \frac{n c}{2 f_n} = \frac{c}{2 f_n} \cdot \frac{f_n}{f_{n+1} - f_n}
$$

Cancelling $f_n$:

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{c}{2 (f_{n+1} - f_n)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
