> **Critical insight:** The peak number $n$ has disappeared from the formula — we do *not* need to know which harmonic we are observing. This is why the multi-peak method is more reliable and is the preferred KTU answer in numericals.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Pulse-Echo Distance Formula

A pulse is emitted at time $t = 0$ from the transducer face. It travels to a flaw (or back surface) located at distance $d$ inside the material, reflects, and returns. The transducer detects the echo at time $\Delta t$.

The total path length traversed is $2d$ (down and back). With uniform velocity $c$:

$$
2 d = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{d = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical):**

> **Problem:** In a steel plate (c = 5920 m/s), a flaw echo is detected 6.0 µs after the initial pulse. A back-wall echo is detected 18.0 µs after the pulse. Find the depth of the flaw.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Use the back-wall echo to verify calibration:
The plate thickness $T$ is:

$$
T = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t_{BW}}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 18.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
T = \frac{0.10656}{2} = 0.05328 \text{ m} = 53.28 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 2 — Compute flaw depth $d$:

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t_{flaw}}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d = \frac{0.03552}{2} = 0.01776 \text{ m} = 17.76 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 3 — Express result:

$$
\boxed{d \approx 17.76 \text{ mm from the entry surface}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Near-Field (Fresnel) Distance

The on-axis pressure amplitude from a circular piston transducer of radius $a = D/2$ at distance $x$ is:

$$
P(x) = 2 P_0 \sin\!\left[ \frac{\pi}{\lambda}\!\left(\sqrt{a^2 + x^2} - x\right) \right]
$$

The **last axial maximum** of $P(x)$ occurs when the argument of the sine equals $\pi / 2$. Setting:

$$
\frac{\pi}{\lambda}\!\left(\sqrt{a^2 + N^2} - N\right) = \frac{\pi}{2}
$$

Solving:

$$
\sqrt{a^2 + N^2} - N = \frac{\lambda}{2}
$$

Rearranging and squaring (with $a = D/2$):

$$
\boxed{N = \frac{D^2 - \lambda^2}{4 \lambda} \approx \frac{D^2 f}{4 c}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Within $N$, the beam is roughly cylindrical (good for inspection); beyond $N$, the beam diverges (use angled shear-wave probes to bypass this region).

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Three Methods

| Feature | Resonance | Through-Transmission | Pulse-Echo |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transducer count | 1 (CW) | 2 (Tx + Rx) | 1 (with duplexer) |
| Wave type | Continuous | CW or pulsed | Pulsed |
| Primary measurement | Thickness (mode count) | Received amplitude | Time-of-flight |
| Best for | Thickness gauging | Volume defect scan | Depth & location |
| Flaw depth info | No | No | **Yes** |
| Sizing accuracy | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Couplant needed | Yes | Yes (both sides) | Yes |
| Equipment cost | Low | Medium | Higher |
| Display | Frequency vs. amplitude | Amplitude vs. time | A-scan / B-scan / C-scan |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Resonance Testing — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    osc[Variable Frequency Oscillator] --> amp[RF Amplifier]
    amp --> tx[PZT Transmitter Coupled to Part]
    tx --> part[Test Part with Standing Waves]
    part --> fb[Returning Acoustic Signal]
    fb --> det[Detector / Impedance Meter]
    det --> disp[Frequency Counter and Peak Indicator]
    osc -.frequency feedback.-> det
```

**Interpretation:** The oscillator sweeps frequency. When the part's natural modes are excited, the transducer's electrical impedance changes and the detector flags a resonance peak. The frequency at each peak is recorded, and thickness is computed using $t = c / [2 (f_{n+1} - f_n)]$.

---

## 4.2 Through-Transmission Testing — Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pulser[Pulsed or CW Signal Generator] --> tx[Transmitter Transducer T1]
    tx --> part[Test Material with Internal Flaw]
    part --> rx[Receiver Transducer T2]
    rx --> amp[Receiver Amplifier]
    amp --> disp[Display Oscilloscope or Meter]
    flaw[Internal Defect attenuates beam] -.reflects and scatters.-> part
```

**Interpretation:** Any drop in receiver amplitude vs. a reference indicates a flaw. Two-sided access to the part is mandatory.

---

## 4.3 Pulse-Echo Testing — Architecture & A-Scan Display

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    syn[Sync Timer and Clock] --> pulser[Pulsed High Voltage Pulser]
    syn --> sweep[Sweep Generator Time Base]
    pulser --> dup[Duplexer Switch]
    dup --> probe[PZT Probe Transducer]
    probe --> mat[Test Material]
    mat --> echo1[Back Wall Echo]
    mat --> echo2[Flaw Echo]
    echo1 --> probe
    echo2 --> probe
    probe --> dup
    dup --> rxamp[Receiver Amplifier]
    rxamp --> det[Detector and Video Amplifier]
    det --> crt[A-Scan Display]
    sweep --> crt
    crt[CRT Display showing T F B] --> user[Operator reads Time t1 and t2]
```

**A-Scan Display Layout (Textual Schematic):**

```
    Amplitude
       |
       |         .---.              .---.
       |         | T |              | B |
       |         '---'              '---'
       |   .---.
       |   | F |
       |   '---'
       |___|___|_________|__________|__________|________> Time
          0    t1        t2         t3
```

**Legend:**
- **T** = Initial transmit pulse (transducer ring-down)
- **F** = Flaw echo at time $t_F$
- **B** = Back-wall echo at time $t_B$
- Flaw depth: $d_F = c \, t_F / 2$
- Plate thickness: $T = c \, t_B / 2$

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right UT Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Need to Inspect Material] --> q1{Access to both sides of part?}
    q1 -- Yes --> tt[Use Through-Transmission]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Continuous thickness only?}
    q2 -- Yes --> rt[Use Resonance Method]
    q2 -- No --> pe[Use Pulse-Echo Method]
    tt --> out1[Detect presence of internal flaws]
    rt --> out2[Compute thickness via standing waves]
    pe --> out3[Compute flaw depth via time-of-flight]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between Through-Transmission and Pulse-Echo methods of ultrasonic testing.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Through-Transmission | Pulse-Echo |
|---|---|---|
| Transducers | Two (Tx and Rx) | One (with duplexer) |
| Access | Required on both sides | One side only |
| Measurement | Received amplitude drop | Time-of-flight of echoes |
| Flaw location | Cannot be determined | Determined from echo timing |
| Sensitivity | High (entire volume) | High (near and far surface) |
| Typical use | Plate, billet inspection | Weld, forging, casting inspection |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**State the condition for resonance in ultrasonic testing and write the expression for thickness using the multi-peak method.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

**Resonance condition:** The thickness of the test piece must equal an integer multiple of half-wavelengths:

$$
t = \frac{n \lambda}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

**Multi-peak thickness formula:**

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{c}{2 (f_{n+1} - f_n)}}
$$

where $c$ is the velocity of ultrasound in the material and $f_n$, $f_{n+1}$ are two successive resonant frequencies.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Resonance + Through-Transmission Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Resonance Method** of ultrasonic testing. Derive the expression for the thickness of a test piece using the multi-peak resonance technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Through-Transmission Method** of ultrasonic testing with a schematic. List its advantages, limitations, and applications. Compare it with the Pulse-Echo method. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) — Resonance Method [7 marks]

**Block diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the **Resonance Testing block diagram** in Section 4.1. Key blocks: Variable-frequency oscillator → RF amplifier → PZT transducer coupled to test piece → returning acoustic signal → detector → frequency counter.

**Principle (2 marks):**
When continuous-wave ultrasound of frequency $f$ is fed into a test piece of thickness $t$, **standing waves** form whenever $t$ equals an integer multiple of $\lambda / 2$. At these frequencies, the transducer's electrical impedance changes sharply, producing peaks in the detector response.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
For the $n^{th}$ harmonic:

$$
t = \frac{n c}{2 f_n}
$$

For two successive resonances $(n+1)$ and $n$:

$$
t = \frac{n c}{2 f_n} = \frac{(n+1) c}{2 f_{n+1}}
$$

Eliminating $n$ (as derived in Section 3.2):

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{c}{2 (f_{n+1} - f_n)}}
$$

**Result:** Thickness can be obtained without identifying the harmonic number, giving higher accuracy than the single-peak method.

---

##### Part (b) — Through-Transmission Method [7 marks]

**Schematic (2 marks):**
Refer to the **Through-Transmission schematic** in Section 4.2. Two transducers face each other across the test piece.

**Working (2 marks):**
The transmitter injects ultrasound; the receiver monitors the transmitted amplitude. A **defect** along the beam path reflects/scatters/absorbs energy, causing a drop in the received signal. By scanning, a defect map is built.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High sensitivity to volumetric flaws
- No dead-zone issue on the receiving side
- Simple electronics

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Requires two-sided access
- Cannot determine flaw depth (only presence)
- Requires precise alignment of Tx and Rx

**Comparison with Pulse-Echo (1 mark):**
Pulse-Echo uses a single transducer, can locate flaw depth via time-of-flight, and is more versatile for one-sided inspection. Through-Transmission is preferred for plates, billets, and online production testing.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Pulse-Echo Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled A-scan display, explain the **Pulse-Echo Method** of ultrasonic testing. Derive the depth-location formula and explain the concept of dead zone and near field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel plate is inspected using a 4 MHz longitudinal-wave probe. The flaw echo appears at 8 µs and the back-wall echo at 24 µs after the initial pulse. Given $c_L = 5920$ m/s for steel, find: (i) the thickness of the plate, (ii) the depth of the flaw, (iii) the near-field length of the probe if its diameter is 20 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — Pulse-Echo Method [7 marks]

**A-scan display description (2 marks):**
Refer to the **A-scan textual schematic** in Section 4.3. The horizontal axis represents time (calibrated to distance); the vertical axis represents echo amplitude. Three features appear:
- **T**: Initial transmit pulse (transducer ring-down)
- **F**: Flaw echo (appears between T and B)
- **B**: Back-wall echo (last echo)

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The pulse travels from the probe to the reflector (distance $d$) and back (distance $d$ again). Total path = $2d$. With velocity $c$:

$$
2 d = c \cdot \Delta t \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{d = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}}
$$

**Dead zone and near field (2 marks):**
- **Dead zone:** The minimum distance from the probe face within which a flaw cannot be detected because the transducer is still vibrating (ringing) after firing. Length ≈ pulse duration × $c$ / 2.
- **Near field (Fresnel zone):** The region $0 \le x \le N$ where the beam is roughly cylindrical. Length:

$$
N \approx \frac{D^2 f}{4 c}
$$

where $D$ = transducer diameter. Beyond $N$, the beam diverges.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $f = 4 \text{ MHz} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $c = 5920 \text{ m/s}$
- $\Delta t_F = 8 \, \mu\text{s} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$
- $\Delta t_B = 24 \, \mu\text{s} = 24 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$
- $D = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$

**(i) Thickness of the plate (3 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t_B}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 24 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
T = \frac{0.14208}{2} = 0.07104 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 71.04 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(ii) Depth of the flaw (2 marks):**

$$
d_F = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t_F}{2} = \frac{5920 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{2}
$$

$$
d_F = \frac{0.04736}{2} = 0.02368 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_F \approx 23.68 \text{ mm}}
$$

**(iii) Near-field length (2 marks):**

$$
N \approx \frac{D^2 f}{4 c} = \frac{(0.020)^2 \times 4 \times 10^6}{4 \times 5920}
$$

$$
N = \frac{0.0004 \times 4 \times 10^6}{23680} = \frac{1600}{23680}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 0.0676 \text{ m} = 67.6 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Comment:** The flaw (23.68 mm) lies inside the near field (67.6 mm), so detection is favourable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Module 3 UT Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Pulse-Echo distance formula $d = c \Delta t / 2$. Always remember it is a *round trip*.
> 2. **Writing $t = n \lambda / 2$ but plugging the wrong $n$** in numericals. Use the multi-peak formula $t = c / [2(f_{n+1} - f_n)]$ when successive peaks are given.
> 3. **Mixing up longitudinal and shear velocities.** For steel: $c_L = 5920$ m/s, $c_S = 3255$ m/s. Use the one given in the problem.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** in the resonance formula — frequencies must be in **Hz**, thickness in **m**, velocity in **m/s**.
> 5. **Writing `|` (vertical bar) inside LaTeX tables** breaks the markdown table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 6. **Ignoring the near field** when interpreting flaw-echo positions — examiners will deduct marks if a numerical answer does not comment on whether the flaw lies inside or beyond $N$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 UT Techniques**

- [ ] **Resonance Method** uses continuous-wave ultrasound and identifies standing-wave peaks at $t = n \lambda / 2$.
- [ ] **Multi-peak thickness formula** $t = c / [2 (f_{n+1} - f_n)]$ is harmonic-independent and more accurate.
- [ ] **Through-Transmission** uses two transducers on opposite faces; detects flaws by amplitude loss; cannot locate depth.
- [ ] **Pulse-Echo** uses a single transducer with a duplexer; measures **time-of-flight**; depth from $d = c \Delta t / 2$.
- [ ] **A-scan display** shows initial pulse T, flaw echo F, and back-wall echo B along a time axis.
- [ ] **Near field (Fresnel zone)** length: $N \approx D^2 f / (4 c)$. Inspection inside $N$ is favourable.
- [ ] **Dead zone** is the unresolved region immediately below the probe due to transducer ring-down.
- [ ] **Standard velocities to memorise:** Steel — $c_L = 5920$ m/s, $c_S = 3255$ m/s; Aluminum — $c_L = 6320$ m/s.
- [ ] **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho c$ governs transmission/reflection at interfaces.
- [ ] **Pulse-Echo is the most versatile and widely used** UT method in industry for weld, forging, and casting inspections.
- [ ] **Through-Transmission is preferred for plates, sheets, and billets** with two-sided access.
- [ ] **Resonance is best for rapid thickness gauging** of thin sheets, tubes, and coatings.
- [ ] Always use **absolute brackets `\vert` or `\mid`** in markdown tables, never the pipe symbol `|` inside equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruments Used in Ultrasonic Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Instruments Used in Ultrasonic Testing # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Ultrasonic Testing (UT) Instruments** refer to the complete set of electromechanical devices employed to generate, transmit, receive, process, and display high-frequency mechanical waves (typically **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz**) for the purpose of detecting, characterizing, and evaluating internal flaws, measuring thickness, and determining material properties without causing damage to the component under test. The primary instrument ensemble in UT comprises: - **Ultrasonic Flaw Detector (UFD)** — the central processing unit. - **Ultrasonic Transducers / Probes** — the sensing elements. - **Pulse Generators and Receivers** — for excitation and signal conditioning. - **Display Systems** — CRT, LCD, or digital readouts. - **Couplants** — the acoustic transmission medium. - **Reference / Calibration Blocks** — for instrument standardization. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** As per Module 3 of PEMET636, students must be able to identify, classify, and explain the working of all major UT instruments, including their constructional features, operating principles, and selection criteria for specific industrial applications. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Echo in a Dark Room" Intuition Imagine you are standing in a large, pitch-dark warehouse and you shout loudly. Based on the **time delay** between your shout and the **echo** that returns, plus the **clarity/strength** of that echo, you can deduce: - **How far** the walls or any obstacle are from you (distance = time × speed of sound). - **Whether something is in the room** at all (presence of an echo). - **Roughly the size and shape** of the obstacle (echo strength and pattern). **Ultrasonic testing works on exactly this principle** — but instead of audible sound (which travels through air and is low frequency), it uses **ultrasonic waves** (>20 kHz) traveling through solid/liquid materials. The "shout" is replaced by an **electrical pulse** sent to a **piezoelectric transducer**, and the "echo" is detected by the same (or a separate) transducer, processed, and shown on a screen. | Real-World Analogy | Ultrasonic Testing Equivalent | |---|---| | Voice / Shout | Electrical pulse applied to transducer | | Sound waves through air | Ultrasonic waves through solid material | | Time of echo return | Time-of-Flight measurement | | Strength of echo | Amplitude on the screen | | Darkness (you can't see) | Inability to see inside the metal | | Walls / objects in the room | Flaws, cracks, voids, inclusions, back wall | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Since sound travels much faster and with less attenuation in metals than in air, ultrasonic testing can detect sub-millimeter flaws deep inside thick components — a capability no visual or surface NDT method (like dye-penetrant or magnetic-particle testing) can match. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis) - **Speed of sound in steel ≈ 5920 m/s** - **Speed of sound in aluminum ≈ 6320 m/s** - **Speed of sound in water (couplant) ≈ 1480 m/s** - **Standard test frequencies: 1 MHz, 2.25 MHz, 5 MHz, 10 MHz** - **Acoustic Impedance (Z)**: a critical property, measured in **Rayleighs (kg/m²·s)** or **MRayl (×10⁶ kg/m²·s)** - **Wavelength (λ) for 2.25 MHz in steel ≈ 2.63 mm** --- ## 1.4 Block Overview — The UT Instrument Family ``` ULTRASONIC TESTING INSTRUMENTS │ ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐ │ │ │ TRANSMITTERS RECEIVERS DISPLAYS & PULSERS & SIGNAL & INDICATORS │ PROCESSING │ │ │ │ ┌────┴────┐ ┌─────┴─────┐ ┌────┴────┐ │Pulse │ │Amplifier │ │CRT/LCD │ │Generat.│ │Filter │ │Thickness│ │Damping │ │Gating │ │Meter │ │Circuit │ │Detector │ │Recorder │ └────────┘ └────────────┘ └─────────┘ ``` This classification will be expanded with full Mermaid-based block diagrams in **Section 4**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — The Pulse-Echo Method The **Pulse-Echo technique** is the cornerstone of modern ultrasonic flaw detection. Its operation can be broken down into five sequential stages: ### Step 1: Pulse Generation A high-voltage (typically **100 V to 400 V**) narrow electrical pulse (rise time ≈ 10 ns, width ≈ 50–200 ns) is generated by the pulser/receiver unit at a **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** of typically **100 Hz to 5 kHz**. ### Step 2: Piezoelectric Conversion The pulse is fed to the piezoelectric crystal inside the transducer, causing it to **vibrate mechanically at its resonant frequency**, launching an ultrasonic wave into the test material via a couplant. ### Step 3: Wave Propagation & Interaction The ultrasonic wave travels through the material. Upon encountering an **acoustic impedance discontinuity** (flaw, crack, void, back surface), part of the wave is **reflected**, part is **refracted**, and part is **mode-converted**. ### Step 4: Echo Reception The reflected wave returns to the transducer (or a second transducer in pitch-catch mode), where the mechanical vibrations are converted back into electrical signals. ### Step 5: Signal Processing & Display The receiver amplifies the weak return signal (often in the **microvolt to millivolt range**), gates it, and routes it to a display where it appears as a vertical deflection (A-scan) or a colored pixel map (B-scan/C-scan). --- ## 2.2 Critical Components — Explained ### 2.2.1 Pulser-Receiver Unit - **Function:** Generates the excitation pulse and conditions the received echo. - **Key Parameters:** - **Pulse Voltage:** Determines energy transmitted into the material. - **Damping:** Controls pulse width — high damping = short pulse = better resolution, low damping = long pulse = more sensitivity. - **PRF:** Controls inspection speed. - **Receiver Gain:** Typically **0 to 110 dB**, adjustable. - **Frequency Bandwidth:** Matched to the transducer (narrowband or broadband). ### 2.2.2 Transducers / Probes (Detailed in Section 3) - **Straight Beam (Normal) Probe:** For detecting flaws parallel to the surface. - **Angle Beam Probe:** For detecting flaws inclined to the surface (typically 45°, 60°, 70°). - **Dual Element Probe:** Separate transmit and receive elements — for near-surface and rough surfaces. - **Contact Probe:** Used with couplant gel. - **Immersion Probe:** Used in water bath. - **Phased Array Probe:** Multiple elements electronically steered. ### 2.2.3 Couplants - **Purpose:** To displace air (which has very poor acoustic transmission) between the probe and the test surface. - **Common Couplants:** - **Water** (immersion testing). - **Glycerine** (high viscosity, good wetting). - **SAE 30 motor oil** (general purpose). - **Cellulose gel** (industrial). - **Honey** (high-impedance materials). - **Commercial products** (e.g., Sono-gel, Krautkramer ultrasonic couplant). ### 2.2.4 Reference / Calibration Blocks - **IIW Block (International Institute of Welding)**: V1, V2 blocks for angle beam calibration. - **ASTM E428 Reference Blocks**: For sensitivity and distance calibration. - **Area-Amplitude Blocks** (e.g., 5-10-15-20-25-30 mm flat-bottom holes). - **Distance-Amplitude Blocks**: Curved surfaces with side-drilled holes. - **DAC (Distance-Amplitude Correction) Block**: Used for plotting time-corrected gain curves. ### 2.2.5 Display & Recording Systems - **A-scan:** Amplitude vs Time — the classical CRT/LCD display showing peaks at flaw and back-wall locations. - **B-scan:** Cross-sectional view (depth vs position). - **C-scan:** Plan-view map (top view showing flaw distribution). - **TOFD Display:** Time-of-Flight Diffraction — shows tip-diffracted signals. - **Thickness Gauge:** Simplified A-scan showing only back-wall echo; digitally reads thickness. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `\vert` or `\mid` is used in place of the bare pipe symbol `|` inside table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. | # | Formula | Description | Symbol Definitions | Units / Typical Values | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave velocity = frequency × wavelength | $V$ = velocity, $f$ = frequency, $\lambda$ = wavelength | $V$ in m/s; $f$ in Hz; $\lambda$ in m | | 2 | $Z = \rho \cdot V$ | Acoustic Impedance | $Z$ = acoustic impedance, $\rho$ = density, $V$ = velocity | $Z$ in **Rayleigh (kg/m²·s)** or **MRayl**; Steel Z ≈ 46 MRayl; Aluminum Z ≈ 17 MRayl; Water Z ≈ 1.5 MRayl; Air Z ≈ 0.0004 MRayl | | 3 | $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Reflection Coefficient (intensity basis) | $R$ = fraction reflected, $Z_1$ = medium 1 impedance, $Z_2$ = medium 2 impedance | Dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ | | 4 | $T = \dfrac{4 \cdot Z_1 \cdot Z_2}{\left(Z_2 + Z_1\right)^2}$ | Transmission Coefficient (intensity basis) | $T$ = fraction transmitted | Dimensionless, $0 \le T \le 1$ | | 5 | $\dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | Snell's Law (refraction) | $\theta_i$ = incident angle, $\theta_r$ = refracted angle, $V_1$, $V_2$ = velocities | Angles in degrees | | 6 | $\theta_{cr} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ | Critical angle (when $\theta_r = 90°$) | $\theta_{cr}$ = critical angle | Degrees | | 7 | $d = \dfrac{V \cdot t}{2}$ | Flaw depth (pulse-echo) | $d$ = depth, $V$ = velocity, $t$ = time-of-flight | $d$ in m, $t$ in s; divided by 2 because wave travels to flaw and back | | 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{V}{f}$ | Wavelength | $\lambda$ = wavelength | m or mm | | 9 | $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda} = \dfrac{D^2 \cdot f}{4 V}$ | Near-field length (Fresnel zone) | $N$ = near-field distance, $D$ = transducer diameter | m or mm | | 10 | $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \dfrac{1.22 \cdot \lambda}{D}$ | Beam divergence (half-angle) | $\theta$ = beam spread angle | Degrees or radians | | 11 | $\alpha_{tot} = \alpha_a + \alpha_s$ | Total attenuation | $\alpha_a$ = absorption, $\alpha_s$ = scattering | dB/m | | 12 | $A(x) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$ | Amplitude vs distance (exponential attenuation) | $A_0$ = initial amplitude, $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient | $x$ in m | | 13 | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A}\right)$ | Attenuation in decibels | $A_{dB}$ = attenuation | dB | | 14 | $\text{Resolution (axial)} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ | Minimum resolvable flaw separation (axial) | — | m or mm | | 15 | $\text{Resolution (lateral)} = D$ | Minimum resolvable flaw separation (lateral) | $D$ = transducer diameter | m or mm | --- ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility The instruments described here are deployed across: - **Aerospace:** Inspection of turbine blades, composite panels, riveted joints. - **Power Generation:** Welded joints in pressure vessels, steam pipes, rotor shafts. - **Oil & Gas:** Pipeline girth weld inspection, corrosion mapping. - **Automotive:** Crank-shaft, gear, casting quality control. - **Nuclear:** Heavy forging and castings, in-service inspection. - **Railways:** Axle, wheel disc, and rail inspection. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Question setters frequently ask students to relate the chosen instrument/specification to the **type of flaw** being detected. Always answer with the triad: **(Instrument type) + (Flaw orientation) + (Material being tested)**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pulse-Echo Depth Calculation ### Problem Statement A flaw is detected in a steel block using a 2.25 MHz straight-beam probe in pulse-echo mode. The echo from the flaw appears on the A-scan at a screen position corresponding to a time delay of **8 microseconds (8 μs)** after the initial pulse. The longitudinal velocity in steel is **5920 m/s**. Calculate the depth of the flaw. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.** The fundamental pulse-echo depth equation is: $$ d = \dfrac{V \cdot t}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Convert all quantities to SI base units.** $$ V = 5920 \; \text{m/s} $$ $$ t = 8 \; \mu\text{s} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{s} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the equation.** $$ d = \dfrac{5920 \; \text{m/s} \times 8 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{s}}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Perform numerator multiplication.** $$ 5920 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 47360 \times 10^{-6} = 0.04736 \; \text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Divide by 2 (factor-of-two because of round-trip travel).** $$ d = \dfrac{0.04736}{2} = 0.02368 \; \text{m} $$ **Step 6 — Convert to a more readable engineering unit (millimeters).** $$ d = 0.02368 \times 1000 = 23.68 \; \text{mm} $$ ### Final Answer $$ \boxed{d \approx 23.68 \; \text{mm}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Near-Field Length of a Circular Transducer ### Problem Statement A **20 mm diameter** circular piezoelectric transducer operates at **4 MHz** in a steel test piece (longitudinal velocity **5920 m/s**). Calculate the near-field length $N$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Recall the governing formula.** $$ N = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda} $$ where $\lambda = V / f$. **Step 2 — Compute the wavelength first.** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{V}{f} = \dfrac{5920 \; \text{m/s}}{4 \times 10^6 \; \text{Hz}} = 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} = 1.48 \; \text{mm} $$ **Step 3 — Convert transducer diameter to meters.** $$ D = 20 \; \text{mm} = 0.020 \; \text{m} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the near-field formula.** $$ N = \dfrac{(0.020)^2}{4 \times 1.48 \times 10^{-3}} $$ **Step 5 — Square the diameter.** $$ D^2 = (0.020)^2 = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Compute the denominator.** $$ 4 \lambda = 4 \times 1.48 \times 10^{-3} = 5.92 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} $$ **Step 7 — Final division.** $$ N = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-4}}{5.92 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0676 \; \text{m} $$ ### Final Answer $$ \boxed{N \approx 67.6 \; \text{mm}} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** Flaws located closer than ~67.6 mm from the probe surface fall within the **near field (Fresnel zone)**, where complex interference makes sizing difficult. Flaw detection is best performed in the **far field**, beyond this distance. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Reflection and Transmission at a Steel–Air Interface ### Problem Statement A longitudinal ultrasonic wave travelling in steel strikes a **flat crack filled with air**. Given: - $Z_{steel} = 46 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$ - $Z_{air} = 0.0004 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$ Calculate the reflection and transmission coefficients. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply the reflection coefficient equation.** $$ R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2 $$ With $Z_1 = Z_{steel}$ and $Z_2 = Z_{air}$: $$ R = \left(\dfrac{0.0004 \times 10^6 - 46 \times 10^6}{0.0004 \times 10^6 + 46 \times 10^6}\right)^2 $$ **Step 2 — Factor out $10^6$ and simplify.** $$ R = \left(\dfrac{0.0004 - 46}{0.0004 + 46}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{-45.9996}{46.0004}\right)^2 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the fraction.** $$ \dfrac{-45.9996}{46.0004} \approx -0.9999826 $$ **Step 4 — Square to get the intensity ratio.** $$ R \approx (-0.9999826)^2 \approx 0.99997 \approx 0.999 $$ **Step 5 — Transmission coefficient (by conservation: $R + T = 1$).** $$ T = 1 - R = 1 - 0.99997 \approx 0.00003 = 3 \times 10^{-5} $$ ### Final Answer $$ \boxed{R \approx 99.997\% \quad\text{and}\quad T \approx 0.003\%} $$ **Engineering insight:** This is why UT is **extraordinarily sensitive** to air-filled cracks — virtually **100% of the wave energy reflects back** at even a hairline air gap, producing a strong echo. This is the fundamental reason why UT can detect micro-cracks that other methods might miss. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Ultrasonic Instruments | Inspection Requirement | Recommended Instrument | Reason for Selection | |---|---|---| | Detect sub-surface cracks parallel to surface | **Straight-beam normal probe (2.25 MHz, 20 mm dia)** | Maximizes reflection from planar flaws parallel to wavefront | | Detect inclined cracks in welds | **Angle probe (45° or 60° in steel)** | Refracted shear wave hits the inclined flaw at near-normal incidence | | Inspect very rough or corroded surfaces | **Dual-element probe or low-frequency (1 MHz) probe** | Reduces surface noise and accommodates rough contact | | Inspect thin sheets (<6 mm) | **High-frequency probe (5–10 MHz) or delay-line probe** | Short wavelength avoids multiple back-wall echoes | | Inspect complex geometry / small components | **Immersion testing + focused probe** | Water couplant conforms to shape; focusing improves resolution | | Map corrosion in pipes | **TOFD (Time-of-Flight Diffraction)** | Sizing accuracy is independent of probe angle | | Full volumetric map of large areas | **Phased Array + C-scan** | Electronic scanning replaces mechanical scanning | | Quick thickness reading on-site | **Digital thickness gauge** | Portable, single-purpose, automatic | | Detect near-surface flaws (<2 mm deep) | **Delay-line probe or dual-element probe** | Avoids dead-zone of standard contact probe | --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of a Piezoelectric Crystal ### Problem Statement A PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) crystal used in an ultrasonic probe has a thickness of **1 mm**. The longitudinal wave velocity inside the crystal is **4350 m/s**. Calculate the fundamental resonant frequency of the crystal. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Recall that for a piezoelectric plate, the fundamental resonance occurs when the plate thickness equals half a wavelength.** $$ t = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = 2t $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the thickness.** $$ \lambda = 2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the wave equation $V = f \lambda$ to solve for $f$.** $$ f = \dfrac{V}{\lambda} = \dfrac{4350}{2 \times 10^{-3}} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate.** $$ f = 4350 \times \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 2175 \times 10^{3} \; \text{Hz} $$ ### Final Answer $$ \boxed{f \approx 2.175 \; \text{MHz}} $$ **Engineering interpretation:** This is precisely why ultrasonic probe manufacturers grind the piezoelectric element to a precise thickness — each desired frequency requires a different crystal thickness. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Flaw Detector (Pulse-Echo Mode) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the signal path inside a modern digital ultrasonic flaw detector. This is the **most important diagram** for KTU Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart LR %% ===== Nodes ===== syncsrc((SYNC
CLOCK)) pulser[PULSER
UNIT] txsw[TRANSMIT
RECEIVE
SWITCH] probe((PIEZO
PROBE)) testpiece[TEST
PIECE
WITH FLAW] recvr[RECEIVER
AMPLIFIER] detector[DETECTOR
RECTIFIER] gate[GATING
CIRCUIT] alarm[ALARM
UNIT] display((A-SCAN
DISPLAY
LCD OR CRT)) timebase[TIME BASE
GENERATOR] hvdcc[(HIGH
VOLTAGE
SUPPLY)] powerdc[(LOW
VOLTAGE
SUPPLY)] refblock[REFERENCE
CALIBRATION
BLOCK] %% ===== Connections ===== syncsrc --> pulser pulser --> txsw txsw --> probe probe -- "COUPLANT" --> testpiece testpiece -- "REFLECTED
ECHO" --> probe probe --> txsw txsw --> recvr recvr --> detector detector --> gate gate --> alarm gate --> display timebase --> display hvdcc --> pulser powerdc --> recvr powerdc --> detector powerdc --> timebase powerdc --> alarm refblock -. "CALIBRATION
BEFORE TEST" .-> probe %% ===== Styling ===== classDef power fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef signal fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class hvdcc,powerdc power class pulser,txsw,recvr,detector,gate,timebase signal class display,alarm,probe output ``` ### Description of the Signal Path 1. **Sync Clock** triggers the system. 2. **Pulser Unit** generates a high-voltage spike. 3. **T/R Switch** routes the pulse to the probe and protects the receiver from overload. 4. **Piezo Probe** converts electrical → mechanical → sound. 5. **Test Piece** reflects the wave from flaw and back wall. 6. **Receiver Amplifier** boosts the weak echo. 7. **Detector/Rectifier** demodulates the RF echo. 8. **Gating Circuit** selects the depth zone of interest. 9. **Alarm** triggers if echo amplitude exceeds threshold. 10. **A-Scan Display** shows amplitude vs time. 11. **Reference Block** is used to calibrate before the test. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Components Inside a Piezoelectric Transducer ```mermaid flowchart TB conn[CONNECTOR] housing[STAINLESS STEEL
HOUSING] blockback[BACKING
DAMPER
TUNGSTEN EPOXY] electrodeR[REAR
ELECTRODE] piezo[PIEZOELECTRIC
CRYSTAL
PZT or QUARTZ] electrodeF[FRONT
ELECTRODE] wearplate[WEAR PLATE
ALUMINA CERAMIC] lens[ACOUSTIC LENS
FOR FOCUSED PROBES] cable[COAXIAL CABLE
TO FLAW DETECTOR] conn --> cable cable --> electrodeR electrodeR --> piezo piezo --> electrodeF electrodeF --> wearplate wearplate --> lens blockback --> piezo housing --- conn housing --- blockback housing --- lens classDef mech fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef elec fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class conn,cable,electrodeR,electrodeF elec class housing,blockback,piezo,wearplate,lens mech ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Immersion UT System ```mermaid flowchart LR tank[IMMERSION
WATER TANK] probe[IMMERSION
PROBE] scanner[MECHANICAL
X-Y SCANNER] part[PART
UNDER TEST] pusher[PULSER RECEIVER] ufd[ULTRASONIC
FLAW DETECTOR] computer[(PC FOR
C-SCAN
IMAGING)] motorctrl[MOTOR
CONTROLLER] motorctrl --> scanner scanner --> probe tank --- probe tank --- part probe -- "WATER COUPLANT" --> part pusher --> probe probe --> pusher pusher --> ufd ufd --> computer motorctrl --> computer classDef ctrl fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef sig fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef disp fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class motorctrl,scanner ctrl class probe,pusher,ufd sig class computer,tank,part disp ``` --- ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Typical Inspection Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD start([START]) prep[STEP A SURFACE
PREPARATION] cal[STEP B CALIBRATION
USING REFERENCE BLOCK] select[STEP C SELECT PROBE
ANGLE FREQUENCY SIZE] scan[STEP D PERFORM
MANUAL OR AUTO SCAN] obs[STEP E OBSERVE
A SCAN OR B SCAN] eval[STEP F EVALUATE
ECHOES FLAW DEPTH] mark[STEP G MARK
ACCEPT REJECT] doc[STEP H DOCUMENT
RECORD RESULTS] stop([END]) start --> prep --> cal --> select --> scan --> obs --> eval --> mark --> doc --> stop classDef step fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class prep,cal,select,scan,obs,eval,mark,doc step ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked to draw a UT inspection setup, examiners expect students to show **at least** the test piece, the probe with couplant, and the flaw detector. Adding calibration block, T/R switch labelling, and the time-base display component earns extra credit. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define piezoelectric effect. Name two piezoelectric materials used in ultrasonic transducers.** **Model Answer:** The **piezoelectric effect** is the property of certain crystalline materials to generate an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress (**direct effect**), and conversely, to undergo mechanical deformation when an electric field is applied across them (**converse/inverse effect**). Ultrasonic transducers exploit the converse effect for transmission and the direct effect for reception of ultrasonic waves. Two commonly used piezoelectric materials are: 1. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — most widely used due to high electromechanical coupling and wide frequency range. 2. **Quartz (SiO₂)** — used in high-temperature and high-frequency applications due to its stability. *(Other acceptable answers: Lithium Niobate, Barium Titanate, Polyvinylidene Fluoride PVDF.)* **Marks distribution (as per KTU valuation):** - Definition of piezoelectric effect — **1.5 marks** - Naming two materials with brief relevance — **1.5 marks** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: List any six types of ultrasonic probes used in NDT and state one specific application of each.** **Model Answer:** | # | Probe Type | Specific Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | Straight beam (normal) contact probe | Detection of laminations in forged billets | | 2 | Angle beam probe (45°) | Detection of transverse cracks in pipe girth welds | | 3 | Dual-element probe | Thickness measurement on corroded, rough surfaces | | 4 | Immersion probe | Inspection of small turbine blades in a water tank | | 5 | Delay-line probe | Inspection of thin sheets and near-surface flaw detection | | 6 | Phased array probe | Full volumetric inspection of complex geometry welds | *(Acceptable alternatives: Surface-wave probe, Through-transmission probe, Contact vs non-contact, Focused probe, Normal-incidence shear-wave probe.)* **Marks distribution:** - Six correct types — **2 marks (≈ 0.33 each)** - Six correct applications — **1 mark (inferred from quality of answers)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Module 3 contains a 14-mark question with internal choice. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (not both). Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an ultrasonic flaw detector in pulse-echo mode.** **Model Answer:** A **pulse-echo ultrasonic flaw detector** is the central instrument in any UT setup. Its operation involves the following stages: **1. Synchronization (1 mark)** A master **synchronizing clock (sync generator)** initiates the entire cycle at a fixed Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF), typically between 100 Hz and 5 kHz. **2. Pulse Generation (1 mark)** The **pulser unit**, powered by a high-voltage DC supply (100–400 V), generates a narrow, sharply rising electrical spike of width 50–200 ns. This pulse is the excitation energy for the probe. **3. Transmit-Receive (T/R) Switching (1 mark)** The high-voltage pulse is routed to the probe via a **T/R switch**. The same switch also protects the sensitive receiver from the damaging high-voltage transmit pulse by shorting the receiver input during transmission. **4. Probe Action and Echo Reception (1 mark)** The **piezoelectric probe** converts the electrical pulse into ultrasonic waves that travel into the test piece through a couplant. Reflected echoes from flaws and the back wall are received by the same probe, which converts them back into weak electrical signals. **5. Signal Conditioning and Display (2 marks)** The receiver **amplifies** (0–110 dB gain) the echo, a **detector/rectifier** demodulates the high-frequency carrier, and the resulting envelope is sent to a **time-base display**. The horizontal axis represents time (proportional to depth), and the vertical axis represents echo amplitude. The flaw appears as a peak between the initial pulse and the back-wall echo. **6. Calibration and Alarm (1 mark)** Before the test, the system is calibrated using a **reference block**. A **gating circuit** with adjustable threshold and depth window can be used to trigger an audible alarm when an echo exceeds a preset amplitude within a chosen depth range. **Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** (1 mark for correct labelled diagram) --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain the construction and working of different types of ultrasonic transducers used in NDT.** **Model Answer:** The **ultrasonic transducer** is the heart of any UT system. Its basic construction consists of: - A **piezoelectric crystal** (PZT most common) sandwiched between two electrodes. - A **backing/damping block** (tungsten-epoxy) behind the crystal — absorbs backward-travelling energy and shortens the pulse for better resolution. - A **wear plate** (alumina or sapphire) in front — protects the crystal from wear. - A **metal housing** with electrical connector. The **types** of transducers commonly used in NDT are: **1. Straight-Beam (Normal) Contact Probe (1 mark)** - Emits longitudinal waves perpendicular to the test surface. - Used for detecting flaws parallel to the surface (e.g., laminations, delaminations) and for thickness measurement. **2. Angle-Beam Probe (1 mark)** - The crystal is mounted on a plastic wedge (e.g., Rexolite) at an angle, typically 45°, 60°, or 70° in steel. - Converts longitudinal waves in the wedge into **shear (transverse) waves** in the test piece via mode conversion at the wedge–test-piece interface. - Used to detect flaws inclined to the surface, such as transverse cracks in welds. **3. Dual-Element Probe (1 mark)** - Contains two separate crystals — one for transmit and one for receive — separated by an acoustic barrier. - Crossed-beam configuration focuses the ultrasound at a specific depth, eliminating the **dead zone** of single-element probes. - Ideal for thickness gauging on rough, corroded, hot, or curved surfaces. **4. Immersion Probe (1 mark)** - Operates submerged in a water bath; no contact couplant needed. - Allows precise mechanical scanning with motorized X-Y-Z stages. - Used for inspecting complex geometry and small precision components. **5. Delay-Line Probe (1 mark)** - Contains a plastic or quartz delay line between the crystal and the test surface. - Pushes the initial pulse off-screen, enabling **near-surface flaw detection** without the dead-zone problem. - Used for inspecting thin components, surface coatings, and near-surface defects. **6. Phased Array Probe (1 mark)** - Contains multiple (typically 16, 32, 64, or 128) small piezoelectric elements, each individually controlled. - By applying time delays to each element, the beam can be **electronically steered, focused, and scanned** without moving the probe. - Enables **S-scan, P-scan, and dynamic depth focusing** for advanced weld inspection. --- ### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Describe in detail the various display modes (A-scan, B-scan, C-scan, TOFD) used in ultrasonic testing with neat sketches.** **Model Answer:** **1. A-Scan Display (2 marks)** The **Amplitude Scan** is the classical display of ultrasonic testing. - **Horizontal axis:** Time of flight (proportional to depth in the material). - **Vertical axis:** Amplitude of the received echo. - **Interpretation:** The initial pulse (transmit marker) appears on the left, followed by echoes at positions corresponding to reflectors (flaws, back wall). - **Use:** Most common in manual flaw detection; allows sizing by comparing echo amplitude with reference-block echoes. **2. B-Scan Display (2 marks)** The **Brightness Scan** (or cross-sectional view). - **Horizontal axis:** Transducer position along the surface. - **Vertical axis:** Depth. - **Brightness of pixel** at each (x, depth) point indicates echo amplitude. - **Use:** Gives a cross-sectional image of the test piece; useful for visualizing the depth and lateral position of flaws. **3. C-Scan Display (2 marks)** The **Plan-View Scan** (top-view map). - **Both horizontal axes:** Spatial position (X-Y) over the test surface. - **Brightness or colour** at each pixel indicates echo amplitude at a chosen depth gate. - **Result:** A 2-D plan map showing the distribution and shape of flaws. - **Use:** Common in aerospace composite inspection and corrosion mapping. **4. TOFD — Time-of-Flight Diffraction (1 mark)** - Uses two probes — one transmitter and one receiver on either side of a weld. - Detects **diffracted signals from flaw tips**, not direct reflections. - Provides highly accurate sizing and is largely independent of probe orientation. *(Sketches: A simple coordinate system showing the axes for each mode earns 1 mark.)* --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain the construction and working of an ultrasonic thickness gauge with a block diagram. List its industrial applications.** **Model Answer:** **Construction (2 marks)** An **ultrasonic thickness gauge** is a compact, portable, single-purpose instrument used to measure the wall thickness of materials (especially metals) from one side. Its major components are: - A **dual-element probe** (most common) — one element transmits, the other receives. - A **pulser** generating low-voltage (≈ 50 V) pulses. - A **microprocessor** for time measurement. - A **digital display** (LCD) showing the thickness in mm or inches. - Battery power and keypad for calibration. - Optional **B-scan or A-scan** mini-display. **Working Principle (3 marks)** 1. The pulser excites the transmit crystal; an ultrasonic pulse travels through a delay line and into the test piece. 2. The pulse reflects from the **back wall** of the test piece. 3. The receive crystal detects the echo and converts it back to an electrical signal. 4. The microprocessor measures the **time-of-flight $t$** between transmit and receive. 5. The thickness $d$ is computed using: $$ d = \dfrac{V \cdot t}{2} $$ where $V$ is the calibrated longitudinal velocity of the material. 6. The computed value is displayed digitally. The instrument must be **zero-calibrated** on a known thickness (often a 5 mm or 10 mm step block) and **velocity-calibrated** before each measurement. **Industrial Applications (2 marks)** 1. **Corrosion mapping** of pipelines, storage tanks, and pressure vessels in oil refineries and chemical plants. 2. **In-service inspection** of boiler tubes, ship hulls, and rail axles without dismantling. 3. **Quality control** of castings and rolled plates during production. 4. **Thickness monitoring** of worn machine parts (e.g., brake discs, crane rails). 5. **Inspection of materials inaccessible from both sides** — only one-sided access is needed. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones in UT Instrument Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "transmit" and "receive" modes of piezoelectric effect.** Many students lose 1–2 marks here. Remember: **Converse effect** = electrical → mechanical (transmit); **Direct effect** = mechanical → electrical (receive). > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the pulse-echo equation.** The wave travels **to the flaw AND back**, so $d = Vt/2$, not $Vt$. > > 3. **Mixing up Snell's Law with the law of reflection.** The equation $\dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ must be remembered precisely. Critical angle $\theta_{cr} = \sin^{-1}(V_1/V_2)$ for the case $\theta_r = 90°$. > > 4. **Labelling diagrams incorrectly.** In the T/R switch block, students often forget to label the protection of the receiver. Always mention "T/R switch protects the receiver from the high-voltage transmit pulse" — it carries 1 mark. > > 5. **Writing "crystal" without specifying piezoelectric.** Always say "piezoelectric crystal" or "PZT crystal" — generic "crystal" loses marks. > > 6. **Confusing A-scan, B-scan, and C-scan axes.** Practice drawing these correctly with labelled axes — KTU examiners award 1 mark just for the axes. > > 7. **Not stating units.** Every numerical answer must carry units (mm, m/s, dB). A correct number without a unit loses 0.5–1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — UT Instruments):** - ✅ **Ultrasonic frequency range used in NDT:** **0.5 to 25 MHz** (most common 1–10 MHz). - ✅ **Velocity of longitudinal wave in steel ≈ 5920 m/s; in aluminum ≈ 6320 m/s.** - ✅ **Pulse-echo depth formula:** $d = Vt/2$ — *always divide by 2 for round-trip.* - ✅ **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho V$ — mismatch causes reflection. - ✅ **Reflection coefficient (intensity):** $R = \left(\dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$. - ✅ **Snell's Law:** $\dfrac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$; critical angle when $\theta_r = 90°$. - ✅ **Near-field length:** $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4\lambda}$ — avoid this zone for accurate sizing. - ✅ **Piezoelectric effect:** Direct (mechanical → electrical, for receive); Converse (electrical → mechanical, for transmit). - ✅ **Common piezoelectric materials:** PZT (most common), Quartz, Barium Titanate, Lithium Niobate. - ✅ **Probe types to remember:** Straight beam, Angle beam, Dual element, Immersion, Delay-line, Phased array. - ✅ **Couplant purpose:** Displaces air; common couplants: water, oil, glycerine, gel. - ✅ **Calibration standards:** IIW V1/V2 blocks, ASTM E428, DAC blocks. - ✅ **Display modes:** A-scan (amplitude vs time), B-scan (depth vs position), C-scan (plan view), TOFD (tip diffraction). - ✅ **Major flaw-detector components:** Sync clock → Pulser → T/R switch → Probe → Receiver → Detector → Gating → Display. - ✅ **Resolution:** Axial resolution $\approx \lambda/2$; Lateral resolution $\approx D$ (probe diameter). - ✅ **Higher frequency = better resolution but more attenuation** — choose frequency based on material thickness and grain size. - ✅ **Attenuation increases with:** Increasing frequency, increasing grain size, increasing travel distance. **End of Module 3 Notes — Instruments Used in Ultrasonic Testing**

topics.title: Transducers: Types, Frequencies, and Sizes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Transducers: Types, Frequencies, and Sizes ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the context of Ultrasonic Non-Destructive Testing (UT-NDT), a **Transducer** is defined as a device that converts one form of energy into another. Specifically, an ultrasonic transducer converts **electrical energy into mechanical (acoustic) energy** and vice-versa, enabling the generation, transmission, reception, and interpretation of high-frequency sound waves used to inspect materials for internal flaws. The most widely used transducer in modern ultrasonic NDT is the **Piezoelectric Transducer**, which exploits the **Piezoelectric Effect** discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880. The active element is typically a polarized ferroelectric ceramic such as **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Snapshot:** A transducer is a **frequency-agile energy converter** that operates in two modes: > - **Transmitter Mode:** Electrical pulse → Mechanical (ultrasonic) wave > - **Receiver Mode:** Returning ultrasonic echo → Electrical signal > > Both modes occur in the same physical element during pulse-echo operation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sonar Ping" Analogy Imagine you are standing at the edge of a deep well with a flashlight and a stopwatch: - **Flashlight beam = Ultrasonic wave** sent by the transducer into the material. - **Stopwatch = Time-base** on your flaw detector screen that measures how long the wave takes to return. - **Water surface at bottom = Flaw (crack, void, inclusion)** that reflects the wave back. - **Your eye (catching the reflected light) = Transducer in receive mode.** Just as a tighter, more focused flashlight beam reveals sharper details at greater depth, a transducer with **higher frequency** and **larger element diameter** produces a more collimated ultrasonic beam capable of resolving smaller flaws at greater distances. The transducer is therefore the **"mouth and ear"** of every ultrasonic flaw detector — without it, there is no inspection. ### 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (Standard NDT Reference Values) | Metric | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | Inspection Frequency Range | **0.5 MHz to 25 MHz** (common: 1–10 MHz) | | Velocity of sound in **Steel** | **5920 m/s** (longitudinal) | | Velocity of sound in **Aluminium** | **6320 m/s** (longitudinal) | | Velocity of sound in **Water** | **1480 m/s** (couplant medium) | | Typical element diameter | **6 mm – 25 mm** (¼″ – 1″) | | Damping material | Tungsten-epoxy, metallic powders | > [!NOTE] > **Frequency selection rule of thumb (KTU high-yield):** > - **Lower frequency (1–2 MHz):** Coarse grain, thick sections, high attenuation materials (e.g., cast iron, composites). > - **Higher frequency (5–15 MHz):** Fine resolution, thin sections, smooth-grained metals. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Ultrasonic Transducers Ultrasonic transducers are classified along **four orthogonal axes**, each carrying distinct engineering trade-offs: **Axis A — By Construction Type** 1. **Contact (Straight-Beam) Transducer** — pressed directly against the test surface through a thin couplant film. 2. **Angle-Beam (Wedge) Transducer** — generates refracted shear or surface waves using an angled polymer wedge (typically 45°, 60°, 70°). 3. **Immersion Transducer** — submerged in water column; the test piece is also immersed. Eliminates couplant variability. 4. **Dual-Element Transducer** — separate transmitter and receiver elements in one housing; ideal for near-surface and corrosion inspection. 5. **Phased Array Transducer (PAUT)** — multi-element assembly with electronically controlled time delays enabling beam steering, focusing, and sectorial scanning. **Axis B — By Active Element Material** - **Piezoelectric crystals** — natural (quartz) or polarized ceramic (PZT, lead metaniobate). - **Piezoelectric polymers** — PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) for broadband receivers. - **Magnetostrictive** — nickel, ferrites — used in EMAT (Electromagnetic Acoustic Transducer). **Axis C — By Frequency Band** - **Low frequency (0.5–2 MHz):** long wavelength, deep penetration. - **Medium frequency (2–7 MHz):** general-purpose inspection. - **High frequency (7–25 MHz):** thin materials, precision thickness gauging. **Axis D — By Element Diameter** - **Small diameter (6–13 mm):** higher natural focus, better near-surface resolution. - **Large diameter (19–25 mm):** longer near-field, better beam collimation at depth. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Transducer Design Trade-offs The transducer designer must balance **four competing physics constraints**: 1. **Penetration depth** ∝ 1/f (higher frequency attenuates faster in attenuative media). 2. **Beam collimation** ∝ D/λ (larger diameter and shorter wavelength yield a tighter beam). 3. **Near-field length N** ∝ D²/λ (larger elements create longer near-fields but smaller focal spot). 4. **Sensitivity to small flaws** ∝ 1/λ (shorter wavelengths detect smaller reflectors). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Transducer Selection Triangle (KTU board favorite):** > > $$ \text{Penetration} \leftrightarrow \text{Sensitivity} \leftrightarrow \text{Beam Shape} $$ > > No single transducer optimizes all three simultaneously — the choice is governed by the **inspection standard** (e.g., ASME Sec. V, AWS D1.1, ASTM E164). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols / Units | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Wavelength** | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | v = velocity (m/s); f = frequency (Hz); λ = wavelength (m) | Relate frequency to flaw resolution | | 2 | **Period** | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | T = period (s); f = frequency (Hz) | Pulse repetition calculations | | 3 | **Near-Field Length (Fresnel zone)** | $N = \dfrac{D^{2}}{4\lambda} = \dfrac{D^{2}f}{4v}$ | D = element diameter (m); f (Hz); v (m/s) | Locate focal point; maximum amplitude | | 4 | **Beam Spread Half-Angle** | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}$ | θ = half-angle to first null (rad) | Compute beam divergence | | 5 | **Beam Diameter at distance s (in far field)** | $d(s) = 2.44\,\dfrac{\lambda\,s}{D}$ | s = axial distance (m); d(s) = beam dia. (m) | Determine coverage at depth | | 6 | **Refraction (Snell's Law in wedges)** | $\dfrac{\sin\theta_{1}}{v_{1}} = \dfrac{\sin\theta_{2}}{v_{2}}$ | θ = angle; v = velocity in medium | Compute critical angles for 45°, 60°, 70° wedges | | 7 | **Piezoelectric constitutive relation** | $D = d\,T + \varepsilon^{T}E$ | D = electric displacement; d = piezo strain constant; T = stress; εᵀ = permittivity at constant stress; E = electric field | Relate mechanical input to electrical output | | 8 | **Attenuation (material loss)** | $I(x) = I_{0}\,e^{-\alpha x}$ | I = intensity; α = attenuation coefficient (Np/m); x = distance | Estimate usable inspection range | | 9 | **Critical angle for L → S mode** | $\sin\theta_{c} = \dfrac{v_{L\text{-couplant}}}{v_{S\text{-specimen}}}$ | First critical angle at which mode converts | Wedge design | | 10 | **Second critical angle** | $\sin\theta_{c2} = \dfrac{v_{L\text{-couplant}}}{v_{L\text{-specimen}}}$ | L → L → S transition angle | Surface wave generation | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Transducer selection governs inspection outcomes across industries: - **Aerospace:** Phased-array PAUT inspects turbine disc cooling holes (high resolution, complex geometry). - **Oil & Gas pipelines:** Low-frequency (2 MHz) dual-element transducers measure wall thickness through heavy coatings. - **Nuclear reactor components:** Immersion transducers at 5–10 MHz scan zircaloy cladding tubes. - **Railway axle inspection:** 4 MHz contact transducers with 60° shear wedges detect fatigue cracks. - **Additive manufactured parts:** High-frequency (10–15 MHz) immersion transducers resolve layer-by-layer porosity. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Near-Field Length Equation The near-field length **N** is the axial distance from the transducer face to the last on-axis pressure maximum. It is derived from the principle that waves radiated from all points on the transducer surface arrive at the axial point **N** with phase differences not exceeding **π radians** (i.e., half a wavelength). **Step 1 — Geometry Setup.** Consider a circular piston source of diameter **D** radiating into a homogeneous medium with wavelength **λ**. Pick two extreme points on the piston: the centre **C** and an edge point **E**. The path difference between waves from these two points arriving at the on-axis point **P** at distance **x** from the transducer is: $$ \Delta = \sqrt{x^{2} + \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2}} - x $$ **Step 2 — Apply the small-difference limit.** For $x \gg D/2$, we use the binomial approximation $\sqrt{x^{2} + a^{2}} \approx x + \dfrac{a^{2}}{2x}$: $$ \Delta \approx \sqrt{x^{2} + \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^{2}} - x \approx \dfrac{(D/2)^{2}}{2x} = \dfrac{D^{2}}{8x} $$ **Step 3 — Impose the phase condition for near-field edge.** The boundary of the near field is reached when the path difference equals **λ/2** (i.e., waves from C and E are exactly out of phase by π): $$ \dfrac{D^{2}}{8N} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for N.** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{D^{2}}{8N} &= \dfrac{\lambda}{2} \\[4pt] D^{2} &= 8N \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{2} \\[4pt] D^{2} &= 4N\lambda \\[4pt] N &= \dfrac{D^{2}}{4\lambda} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute the wavelength identity** $\lambda = v/f$: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{D^{2}}{4\,(v/f)} \\[4pt] N &= \dfrac{D^{2}\,f}{4\,v} \end{aligned} $$ This is the canonical near-field length equation used in every KTU numerical. ### 3.2 Derivation of Beam Spread Half-Angle By analogy with optical diffraction from a circular aperture (Airy pattern), the first minimum of the radiated pressure field occurs at angle **θ** given by: $$ \begin{aligned} \sin\theta &= \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D} \\[4pt] \theta &= \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}\right) \approx \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D} \quad \text{(small-angle)} \end{aligned} $$ The constant **1.22** is the first zero of the Bessel function $J_{1}(x)$, evaluated at $x = 3.83 \rightarrow 1.22\,\pi$. The total included beam-spread angle (between first nulls) is therefore: $$ 2\theta = 2\,\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}\right) $$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Beam Diameter at Axial Distance s (Far Field) In the far field ($s \gg N$), the beam diverges linearly. Geometrically, the beam diameter at axial distance **s** beyond the transducer face is: $$ \begin{aligned} d(s) &= D + 2\,s\,\tan\theta \\[4pt] &\approx D + 2\,s\,\sin\theta \quad \text{(small angle)} \\[4pt] d(s) &= D + 2\,s \cdot \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D} \end{aligned} $$ For $s \gg N$, the term $D$ becomes negligible, yielding the commonly tabulated: $$ d(s) \approx \dfrac{2.44\,\lambda\,s}{D} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A 19 mm diameter contact transducer operating at 4 MHz inspects a steel plate. Calculate (a) wavelength in steel, (b) near-field length, (c) beam-spread half-angle, and (d) beam diameter at 250 mm depth. **Given:** - $D = 19 \text{ mm} = 0.019 \text{ m}$ - $f = 4 \text{ MHz} = 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$ - $v_{\text{steel}} = 5920 \text{ m/s}$ **(a) Wavelength:** $$ \begin{aligned} \lambda &= \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{5920}{4 \times 10^{6}} \\[4pt] \lambda &= 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.48 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Near-field length:** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{D^{2}\,f}{4\,v} = \dfrac{(0.019)^{2} \times 4 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 5920} \\[4pt] N &= \dfrac{3.61 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 \times 10^{6}}{23680} \\[4pt] N &= \dfrac{1444}{23680} = 0.0610 \text{ m} \approx 61 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Beam-spread half-angle:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sin\theta &= \dfrac{1.22 \times 1.48 \times 10^{-3}}{0.019} = \dfrac{1.8056 \times 10^{-3}}{0.019} = 0.0950 \\[4pt] \theta &= \sin^{-1}(0.0950) \approx 5.45^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$ **(d) Beam diameter at s = 250 mm = 0.25 m:** $$ \begin{aligned} d(s) &= D + 2\,s\,\tan\theta = 0.019 + 2 \times 0.25 \times \tan(5.45^{\circ}) \\[4pt] &= 0.019 + 0.5 \times 0.0954 = 0.019 + 0.0477 \\[4pt] &= 0.0667 \text{ m} \approx 66.7 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always state the **boundary condition** (e.g., "assuming far-field approximation valid since s ≫ N"). Examiners award bonus marks for **explicit assumption statements**. ### 3.5 Piezoelectric Constitutive Equation — Step Expansion The linear piezoelectric constitutive relations in **stress-charge form** are: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{i} &= d_{ijk}\,T_{jk} + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_{i} \quad \text{(direct piezoelectric effect)} \\[4pt] S_{ij} &= s^{E}_{ijkl}\,T_{kl} + d^{\prime}_{ijk}\,E_{k} \quad \text{(converse piezoelectric effect)} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $D_{i}$ = electric displacement vector (C/m²) - $T_{jk}$ = mechanical stress tensor (N/m²) - $E_{i}$ = electric field vector (V/m) - $d_{ijk}$ = **piezoelectric strain-charge coefficient** (m/V or C/N) - $\varepsilon^{T}_{ij}$ = permittivity at constant stress (F/m) - $S_{ij}$ = mechanical strain tensor - $s^{E}_{ijkl}$ = elastic compliance at constant E-field (m²/N) For a **1-D simplified model** used at undergraduate KTU level: $$ D = d\,T + \varepsilon^{T}\,E $$ This equation demonstrates that the electric displacement **D** has two contributions: one from mechanical stress via the piezoelectric coefficient **d**, and one from the electric field via the dielectric permittivity **εᵀ**. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Transducer Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TX[Transmitter Mode] A[Pulser / Excitation
Voltage Spike 100-400V] --> B[Piezoelectric
Crystal Element] B --> C[Matching Layer
Lambda-quarter impedance match] C --> D[Wedge / Couplant
Acoustic Coupling] D --> E[Test Specimen
Steel / Aluminum] end subgraph RX[Receiver Mode] E --> F[Reflected Echo
From Flaw or Backwall] F --> D2[Couplant Returns Echo] D2 --> G[Piezo Crystal
Mechanical to Electrical] G --> H[Damping Block
Absorbs Back Vibration] H --> I[Receiver Amplifier
Signal Conditioning] I --> J[Display Screen
A-scan / B-scan] end B -.same element.-> G ``` ### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD T[Ultrasonic Transducer] --> T1[Contact Type] T --> T2[Immersion Type] T --> T3[Angle Beam Wedge] T --> T4[Dual Element] T --> T5[Phased Array] T --> T6[EMAT Type] T1 --> F1[Frequency 1 to 25 MHz] T1 --> S1[Size 6 to 25 mm diameter] T2 --> F2[Frequency 5 to 25 MHz] T2 --> S2[Size 6 to 19 mm] T3 --> W1[Wedge Angle 45 degree] T3 --> W2[Wedge Angle 60 degree] T3 --> W3[Wedge Angle 70 degree] T4 --> D1[Separate Tx and Rx] T4 --> D2[Crossed Delay Line] T5 --> P1[Linear Array 16 to 128 elements] T5 --> P2[Matrix Array 2D] T6 --> E1[Lorentz Force Mechanism] T6 --> E2[Magnetostriction Mechanism] ``` ### 4.3 Frequency-Size Selection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Inspection Target?} -->|Thin wall under 5 mm| A1[High Frequency
10 to 15 MHz] Q1 -->|Medium Section 5 to 50 mm| A2[Medium Frequency
2 to 5 MHz] Q1 -->|Thick Section over 50 mm| A3[Low Frequency
0.5 to 2 MHz] Q1 -->|Coarse Grain Cast Iron| A4[Very Low Frequency
0.5 to 1 MHz] Q2{Surface Condition?} -->|Rough Curved| B1[Small Diameter 6 to 13 mm] Q2 -->|Smooth Flat| B2[Large Diameter 19 to 25 mm] Q3{Near Surface Flaws?} -->|Yes| C1[Dual Element or
Delay Line] Q3 -->|No| C2[Standard Contact] ``` ### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Contact Transducer ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CT[Contact Transducer Assembly] direction TB L1[Top Connector
BNC / Microdot] --> L2[Outer Casing
Stainless Steel] L2 --> L3[Internal Electrode
Top Face of Crystal] L3 --> L4[Piezoelectric Crystal
PZT Disc] L4 --> L5[Backing Block
Tungsten Epoxy Damper] L4 --> L6[Matching Layer
Lambda Quarter Epoxy] L6 --> L7[Wear Plate
Alumina or Sapphire] L7 --> L8[Test Surface
Couplant Film] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering rationale of each layer (frequently asked in KTU viva):** > - **Backing Block (Damping):** Absorbs rearward radiation → short pulse → better axial resolution. > - **Matching Layer:** Matches acoustic impedance of crystal (~30 MRayl) to specimen (~45 MRayl for steel) → improved energy transfer. > - **Wear Plate:** Protects crystal from mechanical wear during scanning. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define the term "transducer" as used in ultrasonic NDT. List any two common piezoelectric materials used in their construction.* **Model Answer:** A transducer in ultrasonic NDT is a device that converts electrical energy into high-frequency mechanical (acoustic) energy and vice-versa, enabling the transmission and reception of ultrasonic waves for flaw detection. Two common piezoelectric materials are: 1. **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)** — most widely used ferroelectric ceramic with high coupling coefficient. 2. **Lead Metaniobate (PbNb₂O₆)** — used for high-temperature applications. *(Acceptable alternatives: Quartz, PVDF, Lithium Niobate, Barium Titanate.)* **(3 Marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *State the relationship between wavelength, frequency, and velocity of an ultrasonic wave. If a 4 MHz transducer is used in steel, calculate the wavelength.* **Model Answer:** **Relationship:** $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$, where $v$ is the longitudinal velocity in the medium and $f$ is the frequency. **Calculation** (with $v_{steel} = 5920 \text{ m/s}$): $$ \lambda = \dfrac{5920}{4 \times 10^{6}} = 1.48 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.48 \text{ mm} $$ **Wavelength = 1.48 mm.** **(3 Marks)** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **piezoelectric contact transducer**. Discuss the function of the backing block and matching layer in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 25 mm diameter, 2 MHz contact transducer is used to inspect an aluminium plate (longitudinal velocity = 6320 m/s). Calculate the **(i)** wavelength, **(ii)** near-field length, and **(iii)** beam-spread half-angle. Comment on the inspection implications. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-A(a):** **Construction (3 Marks):** A piezoelectric contact transducer consists of the following layers (from top to bottom): 1. **Connector** — BNC or microdot coaxial output. 2. **Outer casing** — stainless steel housing providing EMI shielding. 3. **Piezoelectric crystal** — PZT disc, typically 6–25 mm diameter and 0.5–2 mm thick, with silver electrodes vacuum-deposited on both faces. 4. **Backing block (damping block)** — tungsten-epoxy or metallic powder composite, acoustically matched to crystal to absorb rearward energy. 5. **Matching layer** — quarter-wavelength-thick epoxy or composite film between crystal and wear plate. 6. **Wear plate** — alumina, sapphire, or polycarbonate protective face. **Working (2 Marks):** - In **transmit mode**, a high-voltage spike (~100–400 V) from the pulser is applied across the crystal electrodes, causing it to mechanically vibrate at its natural resonant frequency, launching an ultrasonic wave into the test piece through the couplant. - In **receive mode**, returning echoes deform the crystal, generating a voltage that is amplified and displayed on the flaw detector. **Function of Backing Block (1 Mark):** The backing block absorbs rearward-radiated acoustic energy, **damping** the crystal vibration. This produces a **short pulse** with broadband frequency content, improving **axial resolution** (ability to resolve two reflectors along the beam axis). **Function of Matching Layer (1 Mark):** The quarter-wave matching layer provides an **intermediate acoustic impedance** between the high-impedance crystal (~30 MRayl) and the low-impedance couplant/test piece. This **reduces reflection losses at the crystal face**, improving energy transfer efficiency and transducer sensitivity. --- **Model Solution for Q-A(b):** **Given:** $D = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$; $f = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$; $v_{Al} = 6320 \text{ m/s}$. **(i) Wavelength:** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} = \dfrac{6320}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 3.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.16 \text{ mm} $$ **(ii) Near-field length:** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \dfrac{D^{2}\,f}{4\,v} = \dfrac{(0.025)^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 6320} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{6}}{25280} = \dfrac{1250}{25280} \\[4pt] N &= 0.0494 \text{ m} \approx 49.4 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Beam-spread half-angle:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sin\theta &= \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D} = \dfrac{1.22 \times 3.16 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025} = \dfrac{3.855 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025} \\[4pt] \sin\theta &= 0.1542 \\[4pt] \theta &= \sin^{-1}(0.1542) \approx 8.87^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$ **Inspection Implications (1 Mark):** - The beam is well-collimated but diverges appreciably (~8.87° half-angle). - Maximum amplitude occurs at the near-field boundary (49.4 mm). - For thicknesses exceeding 49.4 mm, inspection should be performed in the far field where sensitivity drops with distance. - A 25 mm element at 2 MHz is suited for medium-to-thick aluminium sections (e.g., aerospace wing skins). --- #### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary 2024]** **(a)** Compare **contact**, **immersion**, and **dual-element** transducers in terms of construction, application, and advantages. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define **near-field length** and **far-field region**. A 10 mm diameter, 5 MHz transducer is used to inspect a steel component ($v_L = 5920 \text{ m/s}$). Determine the near-field length and the beam diameter at 200 mm depth. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-B(a):** | Parameter | Contact Transducer | Immersion Transducer | Dual-Element Transducer | |---|---|---|---| | **Couplant** | Thin gel film (grease, glycerin) | Water bath | Gel or delay line | | **Construction** | Single piezo element with wear plate | Single element with waterproof housing | Two separate elements (Tx + Rx) in one housing with crossed delay lines | | **Frequency Range** | 1–15 MHz | 5–25 MHz | 2–10 MHz | | **Best Application** | Field inspection, weld testing | Laboratory high-precision scanning | Near-surface flaw detection, thickness gauging of corroded surfaces | | **Key Advantage** | Portable, simple, fast | Reproducible coupling, scans complex geometries | Eliminates dead-zone near transducer face | | **Limitation** | Couplant variability | Requires water tank setup | Limited sensitivity for deep flaws | **(7 Marks — 2 for table, 2 for construction details, 2 for applications, 1 for comparison conclusion.)** --- **Model Solution for Q-B(b):** **Definitions (2 Marks):** - **Near-field length (N):** The axial distance from the transducer face to the last on-axis pressure maximum, within which the ultrasonic beam remains approximately collimated and exhibits complex interference patterns. - **Far-field region:** The region beyond the near-field length where the beam diverges as a smooth cone and pressure amplitude decreases monotonically with distance. **Calculation (4 Marks):** Given: $D = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}$; $f = 5 \text{ MHz} = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$; $v = 5920 \text{ m/s}$. **(i) Wavelength:** $$ \lambda = \dfrac{5920}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 1.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.184 \text{ mm} $$ **(ii) Near-field length:** $$ N = \dfrac{D^{2}\,f}{4\,v} = \dfrac{(0.010)^{2} \times 5 \times 10^{6}}{4 \times 5920} = \dfrac{500}{23680} \approx 0.0211 \text{ m} \approx 21.1 \text{ mm} $$ **(iii) Beam diameter at s = 200 mm = 0.20 m:** Since $s = 200 \text{ mm} \gg N = 21.1 \text{ mm}$, we are in the **far-field** and may use: $$ \begin{aligned} d(s) &\approx D + 2\,s\,\tan\theta \\[4pt] \tan\theta &\approx \sin\theta = \dfrac{1.22\,\lambda}{D} = \dfrac{1.22 \times 1.184 \times 10^{-3}}{0.010} = 0.1444 \\[4pt] d(0.20) &= 0.010 + 2 \times 0.20 \times 0.1444 \\[4pt] &= 0.010 + 0.0578 = 0.0678 \text{ m} \approx 67.8 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Inspection Interpretation (1 Mark):** The 10 mm / 5 MHz combination yields a relatively short near-field but the beam has already expanded to ~68 mm diameter at 200 mm depth, indicating that **flaw sizing accuracy degrades significantly beyond the focal zone**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — *cost: 0.5 mark per missing unit.* > 2. **Mixing up "near field" and "focal point":** Focal point = last axial maximum; Near field = axial distance to that maximum. Both occur at the **same location** but are conceptually distinct. > 3. **Using $v = 5920 \text{ m/s}$ without explicitly stating it** as the longitudinal velocity in steel — *cost: 0.5 mark.* > 4. **Wrong formula for beam spread:** Use $\sin\theta = 1.22\lambda/D$, **not** $\theta = \lambda/D$.
