The 1.22 factor is mandatory. > 5. **Not stating assumptions** (far-field, small-angle) — examiners penalize incomplete solutions. > 6. **Confusing shear-wave velocity ($v_S \approx 3250 \text{ m/s}$ in steel) with longitudinal** when using angle-beam wedges. ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Transducer** = energy converter; pulse-echo operation uses the same piezo element for Tx and Rx. - [x] **Most common material:** Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT) — high coupling coefficient (~0.5). - [x] **Three core layer functions:** - **Backing block** → damps crystal → short pulse → better resolution. - **Matching layer** → reduces acoustic impedance mismatch → better sensitivity. - **Wear plate** → mechanical protection. - [x] **Frequency selection rule:** Higher f = better resolution but less penetration. - [x] **Standard longitudinal velocity in steel:** $v_L = 5920 \text{ m/s}$. - [x] **Near-field length:** $N = \dfrac{D^{2}f}{4v} = \dfrac{D^{2}}{4\lambda}$. - [x] **Beam-spread half-angle:** $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1.22\lambda}{D}$. - [x] **Beam diameter at distance s (far field):** $d(s) \approx 1.22\lambda s / D$ (sometimes written $2.44\lambda s / D$ when defined as full-width first-null). - [x] **Five transducer types to memorize:** Contact, Immersion, Angle-beam, Dual-element, Phased Array. - [x] **EMAT** = no couplant needed; works through air gaps and coatings; uses Lorentz force or magnetostriction. - [x] **Phased Array (PAUT):** Uses time-delayed excitation of multiple elements → electronic beam steering and focusing. - [x] **Standard element diameters:** 6 mm (¼″), 10 mm (⅜″), 13 mm (½″), 19 mm (¾″), 25 mm (1″). - [x] **Standard test frequencies:** 1, 2.25, 4, 5, 10 MHz (per ASME Sec. V, Article 4). - [x] **Acoustic impedance** $Z = \rho v$ governs reflection coefficient: $R = \dfrac{(Z_2 - Z_1)^2}{(Z_2 + Z_1)^2}$.

topics.title: Reference Blocks with Artificially Created Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reference Blocks with Artificially Created Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Blocks (Calibration Test Blocks):** Standardised specimens of known geometry, material, and artificially introduced flaw characteristics (size, shape, orientation, location) used to **calibrate**, **verify sensitivity**, **standardise performance**, and **qualify** non-destructive testing (NDT) equipment, procedures, and personnel against internationally recognised standards such as **ASTM E428**, **BS 2704**, **ISO 2400**, **ASTM E127**, and **ASME V Article 7**.

An **artificially created defect** (also termed a *manufactured flaw*, *deliberate discontinuity*, or *reference reflector*) is a discontinuity intentionally introduced into the reference block through controlled machining, welding, or material-removal processes. These defects are geometrically simple, dimensionally accurate, and reproducible — providing a **known acoustic, radiographic, magnetic, or electrical contrast** against which actual indications in service components are compared.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the term *Reference Block with Artificial Defects* is considered synonymous with the terms *Calibration Block*, *Test Block*, *Reference Standard*, and *Standard Reference Piece* across literature. The student should treat them interchangeably for examination purposes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

Imagine a carpenter who needs to verify whether a weighing scale is reading correctly. Before weighing a bag of rice, the carpenter places a **standard 1 kg iron weight** (a "reference") on the scale. If the scale reads exactly 1 kg, it is *calibrated*. If it reads 0.9 kg or 1.1 kg, the carpenter knows the error and can compensate.

In exactly the same way, an NDT inspector cannot reliably inspect a pressure vessel, weld, or aerospace component without first checking their instrument against a **standard specimen of known behaviour**. That specimen is the **Reference Block**.

- The **1 kg iron weight** ↔ The Reference Block
- The **defect drilled/EDM-machined inside it** ↔ The Artificially Created Defect
- The **weighing scale** ↔ The NDT Instrument (UT flaw detector, X-ray unit, MPI bench, etc.)

Without this "weighing-scale check", every inspection result would be guesswork.

---

## 1.3 Why Reference Blocks Are Indispensable in NDT

| Purpose | Engineering Function |
|---|---|
| **Equipment Calibration** | Establishes the **known input-output relationship** of the NDT instrument. |
| **Sensitivity Setting** | Determines the **smallest detectable flaw size** under defined conditions. |
| **Procedure Qualification** | Validates that an NDT procedure can reliably detect flaws of a stated size. |
| **Personnel Qualification** | Provides **objective evidence** of an operator's ability to detect, interpret, and size flaws (per **ASNT SNT-TC-1A**, **ISO 9712**, **PCN**). |
| **Acceptance Benchmark** | Supplies the **physical standard** against which production indications are compared for accept/reject decisions. |
| **Performance Verification** | Confirms that the equipment continues to function correctly after repair, calibration, or shift change. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Reference blocks are *mandatory* under ASME Section V Article 7 for ultrasonic and radiographic examinations of welds. Their use is non-negotiable for certified inspection work.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Reference Blocks

Reference blocks may be classified by **NDT method**, **design philosophy**, or **defect type**:

### A. By NDT Method
1. **Ultrasonic Test Blocks** — e.g., IIW V1/V2 blocks, ASTM E127 reference blocks, DSC blocks, Area-Amplitude blocks, Distance-Amplitude blocks, JIS Z 2345 blocks.
2. **Radiographic IQIs (Image Quality Indicators)** — Wire-type (ASTM E747), Hole-type (ASTM E1025), Duplex-wire (EN 462-5).
3. **Liquid Penetrant Reference Blocks** — Cracked-block comparators (e.g., ASTM E433, TAM panel).
4. **Magnetic Particle Reference Blocks** — Magnetic comparator (Berthold type), natural-defect specimens.
5. **Eddy Current Reference Blocks** — Conductivity blocks, Notch standards, EDM-notched standards.

### B. By Design Philosophy
- **Standard Design** — Conform to a published national/international code (ASTM, BS, ISO, JIS).
- **User-Designed (Special) Blocks** — Custom-made when no standard block is available; must be qualified by the user.
- **Couplant-Compensating Blocks** — Embedded to offset surface roughness or temperature effects.

### C. By Artificial Defect Type
- **Flat-Bottomed Hole (FBH)** — Drilled and bottomed; diameter usually expressed as a fraction of an inch (e.g., **5/64 inch FBH ≈ 2 mm**).
- **Side-Drilled Hole (SDH)** — Drilled transverse to the test surface; preferred in modern codes.
- **Notches** — EDM (Electro-Discharge Machined) rectangular or U-notches; surface or sub-surface.
- **Saw Cuts** — Of defined depth, width, and length.
- **Artificial Slag Inclusions** — Introduced during welding of a test-coupon plate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Considerations for a Reference Block

A well-engineered reference block must satisfy several rigorous criteria. The KTU examiner often asks students to enumerate these criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> The block's material must be **acoustically, magnetically, and metallurgically identical** to the component being inspected — otherwise the calibration is *invalid*.

1. **Material Match:** Same alloy, heat-treatment condition, grain structure, and surface condition.
2. **Sound Base Material:** The block must itself be free of natural flaws that would confound the response.
3. **Defect Geometry:** Each artificial defect must be of **known size**, **known location**, **known orientation**, and **reproducible** within tight tolerances.
4. **Defect Reflector Dimensional Tolerance:** Typically **± 0.05 mm** for small notches, **± 0.1 mm** for FBHs.
5. **Surface Finish:** Comparable to the component under test (roughness Ra typically **0.8 µm – 3.2 µm**).
6. **Identification Marking:** Permanently engraved with a unique serial number, material grade, and defect type.
7. **Documentation:** Traceable certificate stating manufacturing method, defect dimensions, and calibration date.

---

## 2.2 The DGS / AVG Diagram Family — A Central UT Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DGS** (German: **D**istanz – **G**röße – **S**tärke; English equivalent: **AVG** — **A**bstand – **V**ergleichsgröße – **G**röße; French: **DAC** — **D**istance – **A**mplitude – **C**orrection) is a theoretical/nomogram-based reference block system that mathematically relates the **distance (D)** between probe and reflector, the **reflector size (G)**, and the **echo amplitude (S)**.

### Concept Logic (Step-by-Step Bullets)

- The DGS/AVG diagram assumes an **equivalent flat-bottomed hole** as the universal reference reflector.
- Any real flaw (SDH, slag, crack) is characterised by its **equivalent reflector size** — i.e., the diameter of the FBH that would produce the same echo height.
- The diagram is plotted for a specific probe frequency, probe diameter, and test material velocity.
- It allows the inspector to **size flaws directly from the A-scan screen** without needing a physical block for every flaw size.

### Why It Matters
- Eliminates the need to manufacture a separate block for each flaw size.
- Particularly powerful for **manual and automated UT inspection of thick-walled components** (boilers, nuclear piping, pressure vessels).
- Adopted in **EN 10160**, **DIN 54120**, and increasingly referenced in **ASME V**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{v}{2 \cdot f}$ | Minimum measurable thickness, where $v$ = velocity (m/s), $f$ = frequency (Hz) | UT wave physics |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{v}$ | Time-of-flight to a reflector at depth $d$ (m), $v$ in m/s, $T$ in seconds | UT pulse-echo |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Ultrasonic wavelength, $v$ in m/s, $f$ in Hz | UT wave physics |
| 4 | $D_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{\pi}}$ | Equivalent circular reflector diameter for an irregular flaw of area $A$ | DGS/AVG theory |
| 5 | $R(d) = R_{ref} \cdot f(d)$ | Distance-amplitude relationship, where $R(d)$ is echo amplitude at distance $d$, $R_{ref}$ is reference echo, $f(d)$ is the material/beam attenuation correction | DAC/DGS calibration |
| 6 | $\Delta_{DAC} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)$ | dB difference between two reference points on the Distance-Amplitude Curve | DAC construction |
| 7 | $S_{c} = S_{ref} + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{ref}}{d_{c}}\right) - \alpha (d_{ref} - d_{c})$ | Sensitivity compensation for distance and attenuation, where $\alpha$ is attenuation coefficient (dB/mm) | Calibration equation |
| 8 | $IQI\,Sensitivity = \dfrac{\text{Visible wire diameter}}{\text{Test thickness}} \times 100\%$ | Radiographic Image Quality Indicator sensitivity percentage | ASTM E747 / EN 462-5 |
| 9 | $FBH_{eq} = 1.03 \cdot \sqrt{A_{SDH}}$ | Empirical relationship converting Side-Drilled Hole area to equivalent FBH diameter (mm) | Empirical, valid for Ø 10–25 mm probes |
| 10 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{v}{2 \cdot d_{min}}$ | Maximum usable UT frequency given minimum reflector $d_{min}$, for far-field reflection | Diffraction-limited UT |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When substituting values into formula 6 or 7, students often forget that $d_2 > d_1$ (i.e., the *far* reflector is usually the larger distance). Sign convention errors cost marks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Inspection of turbine discs, rotor shafts, composite panels. Reference blocks traceable to **NADCAP** standards. |
| **Nuclear** | In-service inspection of reactor pressure vessels, steam generator tubes (ASME XI). |
| **Oil & Gas** | Welds in cross-country pipelines, offshore platforms, LNG tanks. |
| **Manufacturing** | Production-line UT of billets, rolled plates, seamless tubes. |
| **Power Generation** | Boiler tubes, headers, turbine blades, rotor forgings. |
| **Railways** | Wheel-set ultrasonic inspection (wheel flats, rim cracks). |

> [!NOTE]
> The economic value of proper reference-block calibration is dramatic. False-positive indications can lead to unnecessary part-rejection costing **hundreds of thousands of rupees**; false-negatives can lead to catastrophic failure. A ₹ 5,000 reference block saves crores in liability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Distance-Amplitude Correction (DAC) Curve Equation

### Context
The DAC curve is the graphical plot of echo amplitude (typically in **% Full Screen Height**, FSH) versus reflector distance. It is constructed from three or more reference reflectors (usually **FBHs of equal diameter**) at different depths in the reference block.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Statement of the Physics.**
When an ultrasonic wave travels through a medium, three independent amplitude-modulating effects occur:

1. **Geometric Beam Spread (Near/Far Field Effects):** amplitude falls off with distance due to Huygens' principle.
2. **Material Attenuation:** amplitude decays exponentially with distance due to scattering and absorption.
3. **Reference Reflector Reflectivity:** larger reflectors return more energy.

**Step 2 — Defining the Geometric-Attenuation Function.**
For a circular piston probe of diameter $2a$ in a medium of wavelength $\lambda$, the far-field on-axis pressure amplitude at distance $d$ is:

$$
p(d) = p_0 \cdot \dfrac{a^2}{d \cdot \lambda}
$$

where $p_0$ is the reference pressure amplitude at the transducer face.

**Step 3 — Including Attenuation.**
For a material with attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ (in nepers per metre), the pressure amplitude after traversing a round-trip distance $2d$ becomes:

$$
p(d) = p_0 \cdot \dfrac{a^2}{d \cdot \lambda} \cdot e^{-2 \alpha d}
$$

**Step 4 — Converting to dB.**
Converting to decibels (relative to some reference distance $d_{ref}$):

$$
\Delta A = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p(d)}{p(d_{ref})}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{ref}}{d}\right) + 8.686 \cdot 2 \alpha (d_{ref} - d)
$$

Note that $2 \alpha d \cdot 20 \log_{10}(e) = 8.686 \cdot 2 \alpha d$ since $20 \log_{10}(e) \approx 8.686$.

**Step 5 — Final DAC Equation.**
Combining both effects:

$$
\boxed{\; A(d) = A_{ref} + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{ref}}{d}\right) + 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot 2 \cdot (d_{ref} - d) \;}
$$

where:
- $A(d)$ = echo amplitude (in dB or % FSH) at distance $d$,
- $A_{ref}$ = echo amplitude at the reference distance $d_{ref}$,
- $\alpha$ = material attenuation (Nepers/m).

This is the equation used to *construct the DAC curve* on the flaw detector's screen.

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**
- The term $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{ref}}{d}\right)$ accounts for **geometric beam spread**.
- The term $8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot 2 \cdot (d_{ref} - d)$ accounts for **material attenuation**.
- A larger $d$ (deeper reflector) gives a *smaller* echo — hence a **downward-sloping** DAC curve.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):**
A 25 mm diameter, 4 MHz normal probe is used on a steel reference block. A **2 mm FBH at 25 mm depth** produces **80 % FSH**. A **2 mm FBH at 100 mm depth** produces **40 % FSH**. The material attenuation $\alpha$ is **0.04 Np/m**. Verify the geometric-spread term and confirm the dB difference.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Geometric spread from 25 mm to 100 mm:**

$$
\Delta A_{geom} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{25}{100}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.25) = 20 \times (-0.60206) = -12.04 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step B — Material attenuation contribution over $\Delta d = 75$ mm:**

$$
\Delta A_{att} = 8.686 \times 2 \alpha \times (d_{ref} - d) = 8.686 \times 2 \times 0.04 \times 0.075 = 0.0521 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step C — Net dB drop (80 % → 40 %):**

Converting %FSH to dB:
$$
\Delta A_{FSH} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{40}{80}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) = -6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step D — Compare:**
The geometric drop is **−12.04 dB**, but the actual drop observed is **−6.02 dB**. The discrepancy indicates that the flaw detector's **Time-Corrected Gain (TCG)** or **Distance-Amplitude Compensation (DAC)** function must be activated to compensate for beam spread — this is precisely why reference blocks with multiple FBHs at varying depths are essential!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The KTU examiner loves questions where you have to *recognise* that the reference block is the means by which you **measure** the beam-spread/attenuation profile of your UT system. This profile is then *programmed into the flaw detector* for accurate production inspection.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Artificial Defect Types

| Defect Type | Manufacturing Method | UT Response | RT Visibility | MT/PT Visibility | Typical Size | Cost | Reproducibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flat-Bottomed Hole (FBH)** | Flat-bottomed drill + bottom-face grind | Strong, predictable | Good | Not visible | 0.4 – 5 mm Ø | Low | High |
| **Side-Drilled Hole (SDH)** | Twist-drill transverse to surface | Strong, predictable | Poor (often invisible) | Not visible | 1 – 3 mm Ø | Low | High |
| **EDM Notch (Rectangular)** | Wire EDM | Sharp, well-defined | Good (thin slot) | Visible on surface | 0.2 – 5 mm depth | High | Very High |
| **EDM Notch (U-shaped)** | Sinker EDM | Predictable | Good | Visible on surface | 0.5 – 10 mm depth | High | Very High |
| **Saw Cut** | Slitting saw or abrasive cut | Variable | Visible | Visible | 0.5 – 3 mm wide | Very Low | Low |
| **Artificial Slag** | Controlled weld deposition | Irregular, realistic | Visible | Variable | 1 – 10 mm | High | Medium |
| **Tungsten Inclusion** | Arc melt with W electrode | Strong | Variable | Variable | 0.5 – 2 mm | Medium | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Role of a Reference Block in the NDT Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component to be Inspected] --> B[NDT Method Selection]
    B --> C[Reference Block Selection]
    C --> D[Calibration & Sensitivity Setting]
    D --> E[Verification on Reference Block]
    E --> F{Equipment OK?}
    F -- No --> G[Re-calibrate / Reject Equipment]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Production Inspection of Component]
    H --> I[Indication Captured]
    I --> J[Compare with Reference Block Response]
    J --> K{Accept / Reject Decision}
    K -- Accept --> L[Component Cleared for Service]
    K -- Reject --> M[Repair / Scrap]
```


```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pulser / Transmitter] -->|Electrical pulse| T[Transducer]

    RB -->|Beam strikes artificial defect| AD[Artificial Defect - e.g. FBH]
    AD -->|Reflected beam| T2[Same Transducer]
    T2 -->|Electrical echo| R[Receiver / Amplifier]
    R -->|Processed signal| S[A-Scan Display]
    S -->|Calibrated echo height| CMP[Compared to Production Indication]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Define a Reference Block. List any four design considerations that must be satisfied when manufacturing an ultrasonic reference block.**

**Model Answer:**

A Reference Block (or Calibration Test Block) is a standardised specimen containing one or more artificially created defects of known geometry, location, and orientation, used to calibrate and verify the performance of ultrasonic, radiographic, magnetic particle, liquid penetrant, or eddy current testing equipment.

Four essential design considerations are:

1. **Material match** with the component under test (same alloy, heat-treatment, grain size).
2. **Known and reproducible defect geometry** (typically within ± 0.05 mm tolerance).
3. **Sound base material** — free from natural flaws that could confound results.
4. **Traceable identification marking** with permanent serial number and certified dimensional report.

*(Any four valid points – 3 marks; definition 1 mark, listing 2 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**Distinguish between a Flat-Bottomed Hole (FBH) and a Side-Drilled Hole (SDH) as artificial defects in ultrasonic reference blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | FBH (Flat-Bottomed Hole) | SDH (Side-Drilled Hole) |
|---|---|---|
| Orientation | Drilled perpendicular to test surface | Drilled parallel to test surface |
| Manufacturing | Drill + bottom face flat grind | Twist-drill to required depth |
| Echo prediction | Highly accurate, used as primary reference | Slightly variable, but easier to manufacture |
| Modern preference | Traditional standard | Preferred in ASME V (Article 4) |
| Visibility on radiograph | Visible | Usually invisible |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the constructional features and applications of the **IIW V2 Reference Block**. Discuss its role in calibrating a normal-beam ultrasonic flaw detector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **DGS (Distance-Gain-Size) / AVG Diagram** concept. How does it eliminate the need for a physical reference block of every size? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — (a)

**Construction of the IIW V2 Block:**

The IIW V2 block is a low-carbon steel rectangular block of nominal dimensions **300 mm × 100 mm × 50 mm**. It contains the following features:

1. **Three concentric arcs** of radii **25 mm, 50 mm, and 100 mm** on the top surface, used to verify the **sound-path distance** for a 25 mm-diameter, 4 MHz probe.
2. A **25 mm thick reference step** on the upper edge for **thickness calibration**.
3. A **25 mm-diameter side-drilled hole** at the centre of the 100 mm arc (i.e., 100 mm from the test surface) — used for **sensitivity setting**.
4. A **5 mm diameter FBH** at the **91 mm depth** position — used as the **sensitivity reference reflector**.
5. An **index mark** at the 25 mm thickness step.
6. **Material:** Low-carbon steel with longitudinal wave velocity **5920 m/s**, density **7.85 g/cm³**.

**Role in Calibration (Procedure):**

1. **Probe index point verification:** Slide the probe along the 100 mm arc — the index point should align with the scale at **91 mm**.
2. **Sound path calibration:** Adjust the flaw detector's range so that the back-wall echo of the 91 mm depth returns at exactly **91 mm**.
3. **Sensitivity setting:** Maximise the echo from the 5 mm FBH at 91 mm and set it to a known reference height (e.g., 80 % FSH).
4. **Resolution check:** The 25 mm SDH provides a known resolution benchmark.

*(Construction description 3 marks; sketch 2 marks; calibration procedure 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

### Model Answer — (b)

**The DGS/AVG Concept:**

DGS stands for **D**istance – **G**ain (or **G**röße/Size) – **S**ignal amplitude (or **S**tärke). It is a theoretical reference system in which:

- The **equivalent flat-bottomed hole (eFBH)** diameter is the universal "currency" of flaw size.
- The **distance** between the probe and the reflector is normalised to the near-field length $N = \dfrac{D^2}{4 \lambda}$.
- The **signal amplitude** is normalised to the maximum echo from an infinitely large reflector at the focus.

**How It Eliminates Multiple Physical Blocks:**

Using a **single** reference reflector (e.g., one FBH at one depth), the inspector can:

1. Calibrate the probe/material combination.
2. Read off a **DGS nomogram** to determine what echo height a **2 mm eFBH** would produce at any other depth.
3. Compare this predicted curve against indications from the actual component to **size flaws** without needing a block with a 2 mm hole, a 3 mm hole, etc.

The mathematical form of the DGS relationship is:

$$
\Delta G = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{actual}}{d_{ref}}\right) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\phi_{flaw}}{\phi_{ref}}\right)
$$

where $\phi$ represents equivalent reflector diameters.

**Engineering Value:** Drastically reduces the inventory of physical blocks; provides a *universal sizing* method; the basis of **EN 10160** for full-body ultrasonic testing of plates.

*(Concept 2 marks; mathematical form 2 marks; practical utility 3 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the purpose and types of **Image Quality Indicators (IQIs)** used in radiographic testing. Discuss the **wire-type IQI** as per ASTM E747 with a typical sensitivity calculation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and use of **Berthold Comparator** for magnetic particle testing. How does it provide a known indication for sensitivity verification? **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — (a)

**Purpose of IQIs:**

IQIs (also called **Penetrameters**) are reference devices placed on the source side of a radiographed component to **indicate the quality** (sensitivity, definition) of the resulting radiograph. They verify that the radiographic technique has sufficient resolution and contrast to detect flaws of a stated size.

**Types of IQIs:**

1. **Wire-Type IQI** (ASTM E747) — A set of six parallel wires of progressively increasing diameter, mounted in a transparent plastic envelope. Available in sets labelled **1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6** corresponding to wire diameters from **0.063 mm to 1.6 mm**.
2. **Hole-Type IQI** (ASTM E1025) — A small plate with three holes of different diameters.
3. **Duplex-Wire IQI** (EN 462-5) — Two wires of different diameters crossed, for geometric unsharpness measurement.
4. **Step-Wedge / Hole-Type Combination IQIs** — For specialised applications.

**ASTM E747 Wire-IQI Sensitivity Calculation:**

For a **steel component** of thickness $t$ = 30 mm inspected using IQI set **3**, the thinnest visible wire on the radiograph corresponds to **0.20 mm** diameter. The sensitivity is:

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \dfrac{\text{Smallest visible wire diameter}}{\text{Test thickness}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.20}{30} \times 100\% = 0.67\%
$$

ASTM E747 acceptance for **Class 1** radiography of 30 mm steel requires sensitivity of **2 % or better**, so this radiograph qualifies.

*(Purpose 2 marks; types 2 marks; calculation 3 marks = 7 marks.)*

### Model Answer — (b)

**Berthold Comparator (Magnetic Particle Testing):**

The **Berthold Magnetic Comparator** (also known as the **Berthold Bar** or **Koehler Bar**) is a precision device used to verify the strength and uniformity of the magnetising field in magnetic particle inspection.

**Construction:**

- A **massive steel bar** with a central bore containing a **magnetic particle suspension reservoir** and a calibrated **spring-loaded magnetic slug**.
- Two **pole pieces** at the ends establish a known magnetic field when current is applied.
- A **graduated scale** indicates the magnetic flux density in Gauss or Tesla.

**Working Principle:**

1. The bar is magnetised using the same yoke/equipment as the component.
2. The calibrated slug lifts against the spring force, indicating field strength.
3. The **lift-off height** of the slug corresponds to a **known magnetic flux density**.
4. The operator adjusts the magnetising current until the comparator indicates the **desired flux density** (e.g., 10 Gauss for steel).

**How It Provides a Known Indication:**

The comparator creates a **controlled, repeatable magnetic leakage field** at a known location. When magnetic particles are applied, they form a **standard pattern** (a pile of particles at the slug's edge). The inspector compares the **sharpness and density** of this standard pattern with indications on the component.

**Engineering Value:** Provides a **quantitative** measure of magnetising current — older qualitative methods (lift-weight tests, snap-tests) cannot do this.

*(Construction 3 marks; working 2 marks; standard-indication concept 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Mixing FBH and SDH roles:** Students often assume any drilled hole will do. The examiner expects you to know that **FBH = primary reference**, **SDH = practical alternative** with different echo-prediction formulae.
> 2. **Ignoring the material identity:** A "reference block" of aluminium cannot be used to inspect a steel weld — the velocity, attenuation, and acoustic impedance mismatch will invalidate the calibration.
> 3. **Skipping the calibration procedure:** KTU students often describe *what* a reference block is but forget to describe *how it is used* in the actual UT/RT/MT workflow. Always describe **procedure** alongside **principle**.
> 4. **Forgetting the formula for IQI sensitivity:** The simple $\dfrac{d}{t} \times 100\%$ formula is tested almost every exam cycle — learn it cold.
> 5. **Confusing DGS with DAC:** DGS uses **equivalent reflector size**, DAC uses **distance-only compensation**. They are related but not identical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A reference block is a calibrated standard containing artificial defects of known geometry, used to verify NDT equipment sensitivity and performance.
- **Artificial Defect Types:** FBH, SDH, EDM notch (rectangular/U), saw cut, artificial slag, tungsten inclusion.
- **Most-used UT Block:** IIW V1 and V2 blocks (BS 2704 / ISO 2400).
- **Modern UT Standard Block:** ASTM E127 (FBH at 5 depths: 3/64, 5/64, 8/64, 10/64, 12/64 inch).
- **DGS / AVG:** Distance-Gain-Size diagram; eliminates need for multiple physical blocks by mathematical extrapolation.
- **DAC:** Distance-Amplitude Curve; plots %FSH vs depth using equal-diameter reflectors at multiple depths.
- **IQI Sensitivity Formula:** $\dfrac{d_{wire}}{t_{component}} \times 100\%$ — ASTM E747 wire-type; ASTM E1025 hole-type.
- **MT Reference:** Berthold Comparator — measures magnetising field strength quantitatively.
- **PT Reference:** Cracked-block comparator (e.g., TAM panel per ASTM E433); half is clean, half contains a known tight crack.
- **EDT Reference:** Conductivity blocks (e.g., TM-1, TM-2, TM-3 standards) and notch standards.
- **Material Rule:** Block material **must** match component material (alloy + heat treatment).
- **Tolerance:** ± 0.05 mm for notches, ± 0.1 mm for FBHs.
- **Documentation:** Every block carries a traceable certificate — serial number, defect dimensions, material grade.
- **Codes:** ASME V (Articles 4 & 7), ASTM E428, ASTM E127, ASTM E747, ASTM E1025, ASTM E433, BS 2704, ISO 2400, EN 462-5.
- **Traceability:** Reference blocks must be traceable to a national/international standards body (NPL, NIST, NABL).
- **Beam-Spread Formula:** $\Delta A_{geom} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_{ref}}{d}\right)$ dB.
- **Attenuation Formula:** $\Delta A_{att} = 8.686 \cdot 2 \alpha \cdot \Delta d$ dB.
- **Golden Rule of NDT Calibration:** "No block → No inspection."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Calibration of Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**, **Calibration of Equipment** is defined as the documented, traceable process of comparing the response of an NDT inspection instrument against a **known physical reference standard** of established dimensions, material properties, or signal characteristics, in order to verify, adjust, and certify the instrument's **accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, and reproducibility** within defined acceptance tolerances.

Mathematically expressed, the calibration operation seeks to determine and minimize the **measurement uncertainty (U)** such that:

$$ U = \sqrt{u_{cal}^2 + u_{std}^2 + u_{drift}^2 + u_{env}^2} $$

where:
- $u_{cal}$ = uncertainty due to the calibration procedure
- $u_{std}$ = uncertainty of the reference standard
- $u_{drift}$ = instrument drift since last calibration
- $u_{env}$ = environmental influence (temperature, humidity, electromagnetic noise)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Calibration is the **foundation of validity** for any NDT inspection report. An uncalibrated instrument renders an inspection legally, technically, and contractually invalid under codes such as **ASME Section V**, **ASTM E543**, and **ISO 9712**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of calibration like **weighing yourself on a bathroom scale before buying fish at the market**:
- If the scale reads **500 g** when nothing is on it, it has a **zero error** (needs *zero/balance adjustment*).
- If a standard **1 kg weight** reads as **950 g**, it has a **gain/sensitivity error** (needs *sensitivity adjustment*).
- You perform this check **before every transaction** (analogous to *pre-inspection calibration*).

In NDT, the "fish" is a defect in a critical component (e.g., a turbine blade, a pressure vessel weld). The "scale" is your ultrasonic flaw detector, eddy current probe, or X-ray exposure system. The "market regulation" is the international material testing code that mandates traceability to **National Metrology Institutes** such as **NIST (USA)**, **NPL (UK)**, or **NABL (India)**.

### 1.3 Critical Importance in NDT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Calibration is Non-Negotiable in NDT:**
> 1. **Defect Sizing Accuracy** — Without calibration, an indicated defect of 5 mm may actually be 12 mm.
> 2. **Acceptance/Rejection Decisions** — Codes specify precise accept/reject thresholds based on calibrated amplitude (e.g., DAC curve).
> 3. **Legal Forensics** — A failure analysis testimony is only admissible if instrument calibration records exist.
> 4. **Repeatability Across Shifts/Sites** — Ensures Inspector A in Plant 1 and Inspector B in Plant 2 produce identical readings.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Highlights)

The following **calibration-defining metrics** are universally accepted in NDT metrology:

- **Accuracy** — Closeness of measured value to true value. Expressed as **$\pm$ % of full scale**.
- **Precision / Repeatability** — Consistency of repeated measurements under identical conditions. Expressed as **$\sigma$ (standard deviation)**.
- **Sensitivity** — Smallest detectable change in input. Quantified in UT as **dB**.
- **Resolution** — Smallest distinguishable difference in measurement. Units depend on method (mm, $\mu$m, dB).
- **Linearity** — Maximum deviation of the instrument response from a best-fit straight line. Expressed as **$\pm$ %**.
- **Drift** — Slow change of output with time, independent of input change. Units: **$\mu$V/hour** or **%/day**.
- **Traceability** — Unbroken chain of comparisons linking the instrument to a recognized primary standard.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Tier Calibration Architecture in NDT

NDT equipment calibration follows a rigorous **two-tier hierarchy**:

#### Tier 1: Primary (Master) Calibration
- Performed by **equipment manufacturers** or **accredited calibration laboratories** (e.g., NABL-accredited in India).
- Uses **primary reference standards** traceable to **NIST, PTB, or NPL**.
- Performed **annually** or after major repair.
- Yields a formal **Calibration Certificate** with uncertainty budget.

#### Tier 2: Secondary (Operational / Working) Calibration
- Performed **on-site** by certified NDT Level II/III personnel **before each inspection shift**.
- Uses **working/reference blocks** (e.g., IIW V1/V2 blocks, ASTM E127 reference blocks).
- Verifies that the instrument still operates within the **baseline established in Tier 1**.
- Compensates for **drift, environmental, and handling effects**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Point:** Most exam questions (especially 14-mark questions) focus on **Tier 2 calibration** because it directly governs inspection validity on the shop floor.

### 2.2 Method-Wise Calibration Standards — KTU High-Yield Table

| **NDT Method** | **Reference Standard** | **Standard Code** | **Parameter Calibrated** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | IIW V1/V2 Block, ASTM E127 Block | ASME Sec V Art 4, ASTM E317 | Time-base (velocity), Sensitivity (dB), Beam angle |
| Phased Array UT (PAUT) | IIW Block with side-drilled holes | ASTM E2700, ISO 13588 | Focal laws, S-scan geometry, TCG |
| Radiographic Testing (RT) | Image Quality Indicator (IQI / Penetrameter) | ASTM E747, ISO 19232 | Geometric unsharpness, Contrast sensitivity |
| Magnetic Particle (MT) | Pie Gauge, Berthold Test Bar, MT Yoke Field Indicator | ASTM E709, ISO 9934 | Lifting power (kg), Field strength |
| Liquid Penetrant (PT) | ASTM PSM-5 Sensitivity Panel | ASTM E165, ISO 3452 | Penetrant sensitivity, removal efficiency |
| Eddy Current (ET) | Reference Standards with EDM notches | ASTM E215, ISO 15549 | Frequency, phase, amplitude baseline |
| Visual Testing (VT) | Calibration Ruler, Borescope Reticles | ASTM E1417, AWS QC1 | Resolution, illumination, magnification |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Calibration Mathematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM INSTRUCTION (CRITICAL):** All formulas below are testable. Memorize the **variables**, **units**, and **physical meaning**. The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ is used for absolute value to preserve table syntax integrity.

| # | **Formula** | **Quantity Calculated** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \frac{2d}{t}$ | Material Sound Velocity (cross-check) | m/s | UT calibration verification |
| 2 | $\Delta t = \frac{2d}{V}$ | Time-of-Flight for given thickness | $\mu$s | UT time-base calibration |
| 3 | $A_dB = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_m}{A_{ref}}\right)$ | Amplitude ratio in decibels | dB | UT DAC/TCG calibration |
| 4 | $dB_{\mu V} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V}{V_{ref}}\right)$ | Voltage ratio in decibels (1 $\mu$V ref) | dB $\mu$V | ET absolute calibration |
| 5 | $P_{lift} = \frac{F}{mg}$ | MT Yoke Lifting Power | kgf or N | MT field-strength verification |
| 6 | $U_g = \frac{d \cdot m}{M}$ | Geometric Unsharpness (RT) | mm | RT source-to-film geometry |
| 7 | $D_{eff} = \sqrt{D_s^2 + d^2}$ | Effective source size | mm | RT sharpness assessment |
| 8 | $S_{norm} = S_{obs} \cdot \frac{D_{std}}{D_{test}}$ | Normalized Sensitivity | mm or % | IQI sensitivity normalization |
| 9 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard Deviation (Precision) | (same as $x$) | Repeatability check |
| 10 | $\% Error = \left(\frac{x_{meas} - x_{true}}{x_{true}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage Accuracy Error | % | Calibration verification |

### 2.4 Variables Glossary

- $d$ = distance/thickness (mm)
- $t$ = measured time-of-flight ($\mu$s)
- $V$ = longitudinal wave velocity in the material (m/s)
- $A_m$ = measured amplitude (V or % screen height)
- $A_{ref}$ = reference amplitude from standard block
- $F$ = measured magnetic force (N)
- $m$ = mass of test piece (kg)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $D_s$ = actual focal spot size (mm)
- $M$ = geometric magnification
- $\bar{x}$ = arithmetic mean of measurements
- $x_i$ = $i^{th}$ measurement value
- $n$ = number of measurements

### 2.5 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

1. **Aerospace (AS9100 compliant):** UT calibration on titanium and nickel superalloy reference blocks is mandatory before every engine disk inspection.
2. **Nuclear (ASME Section XI):** Inservice inspection of reactor pressure vessels demands full Tier 1 + Tier 2 calibration with documented uncertainty < 5%.
3. **Petrochemical (API 570/653):** Tank and piping weld inspection relies on calibrated RT penetrameters for flaw visibility confirmation.
4. **Railways (RDSO Specifications):** Ultrasonic rail testing uses calibrated 70° shear wave probes against a **RAILTEST block** to detect transverse defects.

### 2.6 Common Sources of Calibration Error

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Examiners frequently award or deduct 2–3 marks based on whether the student identifies **multiple** error sources, not just one.

- **Couplant variation** in UT (water vs. gel vs. oil) → alters amplitude.
- **Temperature drift** in ET probes (resistance change with temperature).
- **Source decay** in radioactive isotopes (Ir-192 decays; effective focal spot changes).
- **Surface roughness** of the reference block (worn standard).
- **Operator technique** (probe pressure, alignment angle).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: UT Amplitude Calibration (DAC Curve)

The **Distance-Amplitude Correction (DAC) curve** compensates for the natural decrease in ultrasonic echo amplitude as the reflector moves deeper into the material due to **beam spreading** and **material attenuation**.

**Step 1 — Attenuation Law of an Ultrasonic Beam:**

For a circular disc transducer of radius $a$, radiating into a medium with attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ (Np/m), the on-axis pressure amplitude $P(r)$ at distance $r$ is given by:

$$ P(r) = \frac{P_0 \cdot a^2}{r} \cdot e^{-\alpha r} $$

Here, $P_0$ is the source pressure amplitude at $r = a$ (the near-field distance).

**Step 2 — Convert Pressure Ratio to Decibels:**

The decibel equivalent $A_{dB}(r)$ referenced to a near-field amplitude $P_{ref}$ is:

$$ A_{dB}(r) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P(r)}{P_{ref}}\right) $$

Substituting the attenuation law from Step 1:

$$ A_{dB}(r) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_0 \cdot a^2}{r \cdot P_{ref}}\right) - 20 \cdot \alpha \cdot r \cdot \log_{10}(e) $$

**Step 3 — Simplification Using Natural Log Conversion:**

Since $20 \cdot \log_{10}(e) = 8.686$, we get:

$$ A_{dB}(r) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_0 \cdot a^2}{P_{ref}}\right) - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8.686 \cdot \alpha \cdot r $$

**Step 4 — Identification of the DAC Curve Slope:**

The DAC curve is plotted as $A_{dB}$ versus $r$. The slope in dB/mm is therefore:

$$ \frac{dA_{dB}}{dr} = -\frac{8.686}{r} - 8.686 \cdot \alpha $$

This shows that attenuation has **two contributions**: geometric beam spreading (proportional to $1/r$) and material absorption (linear in $r$).

**Step 5 — Practical Calibration Procedure (KTU Lab Standard):**

Using an **ASTM E127 reference block** (or IIW V1/V2 block) with multiple flat-bottom holes (FBH) at known depths $d_1, d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n$ of identical diameter:

- Set the FBH at depth $d_1$ to **80% Full Screen Height (FSH)**.
- Hold the dB level constant.
- Record echo amplitudes from FBHs at $d_2, d_3, \ldots, d_n$.
- Plot amplitude (dB) versus depth (mm) → this is the **DAC curve**.
- During inspection, any reflector whose echo peak crosses or touches the DAC is reported as an equivalent FBH.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (Exam-Style):**

> Given: An ultrasonic pulse-echo test on a steel block (V = 5900 m/s). A 5 mm diameter FBH at depth 25 mm produces an echo of amplitude 60% FSH. The same FBH at depth 100 mm produces 18% FSH. Determine the attenuation coefficient.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

Convert amplitudes to dB (reference = 100% FSH):

$$ A_1 = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{60}{100}\right) = -4.437 \text{ dB} $$

$$ A_2 = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{18}{100}\right) = -14.894 \text{ dB} $$

Difference in amplitude:

$$ \Delta A = A_2 - A_1 = -14.894 - (-4.437) = -10.457 \text{ dB} $$

Difference in depth:

$$ \Delta d = 100 - 25 = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m} $$

Attenuation slope (combined beam-spreading + absorption):

$$ \frac{\Delta A}{\Delta d} = \frac{-10.457}{75} = -0.1394 \text{ dB/mm} $$

For practical UT calibration in the **far field** (where beam-spreading contribution is small compared to attenuation), we approximate:

$$ \alpha \approx \frac{\vert \Delta A / \Delta d \vert}{8.686} = \frac{0.1394}{8.686} = 0.01605 \text{ Np/mm} $$

Converting to dB/mm:

$$ \alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \times 0.01605 = 0.1394 \text{ dB/mm} $$

> This matches the directly calculated slope, confirming consistency. **Final answer:** $\alpha \approx 0.016 \text{ Np/mm}$ or **0.139 dB/mm** in steel.

### 3.2 Derivation: Geometric Unsharpness in Radiography

**Step 1 — Definition of Geometric Unsharpness:**

For a point source of finite size, the penumbra (blurred edge) on a radiographic film has width:

$$ U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D - t} $$

where $d$ = source size, $t$ = object-to-film distance, $D$ = source-to-film distance.

**Step 2 — Special Case (object in contact with film, $t \to 0$):**

$$ U_g \to 0 $$

This is why contact radiography gives sharpest images for surface flaws.

**Step 3 — Practical Limit (object at infinity, $D \gg t$):**

$$ U_g \approx \frac{d \cdot t}{D} $$

**Step 4 — Calibration Use:**

ASTM E747 IQI sensitivity (e.g., 2% of specimen thickness) is verified only when $U_g$ is below the IQI wire diameter. This gives:

$$ \frac{d \cdot t}{D} \leq 0.02 \cdot T_{specimen} $$

Solving for maximum allowed source size:

$$ d_{max} = \frac{0.02 \cdot T_{specimen} \cdot D}{t} $$

This is a routine check done during RT calibration before inspection.

### 3.3 Derivation: Calibration Uncertainty Budget (Simplified)

**Step 1 — Define combined standard uncertainty $u_c$:**

$$ u_c = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i^2 \cdot u_i^2} $$

where $c_i$ = sensitivity coefficient, $u_i$ = standard uncertainty of input $i$.

**Step 2 — Expanded uncertainty $U$ (95% confidence, coverage factor $k=2$):**

$$ U = k \cdot u_c = 2 \cdot u_c $$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**

A UT thickness gauge calibration has:
- Reference block uncertainty $u_1 = 0.01$ mm, $c_1 = 1$
- Instrument resolution $u_2 = 0.005$ mm, $c_2 = 1$
- Temperature drift $u_3 = 0.008$ mm, $c_3 = 1$

Combined uncertainty:

$$ u_c = \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + (0.005)^2 + (0.008)^2} $$

$$ u_c = \sqrt{0.0001 + 0.000025 + 0.000064} $$

$$ u_c = \sqrt{0.000189} = 0.01375 \text{ mm} $$

Expanded uncertainty:

$$ U = 2 \times 0.01375 = 0.0275 \text{ mm} \approx \mathbf{0.03 \text{ mm}} $$

> **Reporting format:** "Measured thickness = $T \pm 0.03$ mm at 95% confidence level."

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Calibration Standard by NDT Method

| **Inspection Parameter** | **UT (Pulse-Echo)** | **RT (Film)** | **ET (Surface Scan)** | **MT (Surface)** | **PT (Surface)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference Standard Type** | FBH / SDH in metal block | IQI wires on film | EDM notch on plate | Pie gauge, MT Yoke block | Cracked panel (PSM-5) |
| **Calibration Frequency** | Pre-shift + every 4 hrs | Per exposure setup | Pre-shift | Pre-shift | Pre-shift |
| **Acceptance Tolerance** | $\pm 2$ dB from baseline | All IQI wires visible | $\pm 5\%$ amplitude | 4.5 kg lift min. | All 5 cracks visible |
| **Key Parameter Tracked** | Sensitivity, Velocity | Density, Sensitivity | Frequency, Phase | Field strength | Brightness contrast |
| **Re-calibration Trigger** | Probe change, battery low | New film batch | Temperature $\Delta > 5°C$ | Yoke demagnetized | Penetrant batch change |

