## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Define calibration of NDT equipment. List any four parameters that are verified during ultrasonic flaw detector calibration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Calibration of NDT equipment is the documented process of comparing the response of an inspection instrument against a **known physical reference standard** under controlled conditions to verify accuracy, sensitivity, and traceability, and to adjust the instrument if necessary so that measurements conform to specified tolerances.

**Four Parameters Verified in UT Calibration (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
1. **Time-base / Velocity** — using known thickness steps in a reference block.
2. **Sensitivity** — measured in dB, using flat-bottom or side-drilled holes.
3. **Beam Angle / Probe Index Point** — using IIW V1/V2 block.
4. **Linearity / Vertical Linearity** — verified across 10%–100% screen height.
5. **Dead Zone / Near-surface Resolution** — verified using small-thickness steps.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**What is the role of an Image Quality Indicator (IQI) in radiographic calibration? State the standard codes governing IQI use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Role of IQI (2 marks):**
The IQI (also called a penetrameter) is a **geometric standard** placed on the source side of the specimen during radiographic exposure. It verifies that the radiographic image possesses sufficient **sensitivity** to detect flaws of a defined size. The IQI enables verification of:
- Spatial resolution (wire visibility).
- Contrast sensitivity (hole visibility).
- Geometric unsharpness of the image.
- Overall image quality for the specific exposure parameters used.

**Standard Codes (1 mark):**
- **ASTM E747** — Standard practice for design, manufacture, and material grouping of IQIs.
- **ASTM E1025** — Standard practice for hole-type and wire-type IQI.
- **ISO 19232** — Non-destructive testing — Image quality of radiographs.
- **ASME Section V, Article 2** — Radiographic examination.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the procedure for ultrasonic flaw detector calibration using an IIW V2 block. List the parameters verified and mention the relevant standard code. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. IIW V2 Block Description (1 mark):**
The IIW V2 block is a **reference standard** made of carbon steel (or material matching the test piece) with:
- A **25 mm radius** for transducer placement.
- A **100 mm radius** curved surface for angle-beam calibration.
- Multiple **steps, slots, and side-drilled holes** at known positions.
- A **200 mm scale** engraved on the side for time-base calibration.

**2. Parameters Verified (4 marks):**
| Parameter | Procedure on IIW V2 | Acceptance |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-base / Velocity** | Couple probe on 25 mm step; adjust range to place back-wall echo at correct distance | $\pm 1\%$ of full range |
| **Pulse Repetition Frequency** | Set per code (typically 100–1000 Hz) | Per instrument spec |
| **Probe Index Point & Beam Angle** | Couple 60° shear wave probe; locate 100 mm arc echoes from R25 and R100 slots | Beam angle within $\pm 1°$ of nominal |
| **Sensitivity / dB Calibration** | Set 100 mm radius arc echo to 80% FSH; record dB value | Used as reference dB |
| **Resolution** | Resolve 5 mm and 10 mm diameter holes at known depths | Both echoes distinct |
| **Dead Zone** | Use thin steps (e.g., 5 mm, 10 mm) | First back-wall echo visible at $\geq$ 10 mm |

**3. Relevant Standard Code (1 mark):**
**ASTM E317** — Standard practice for evaluating performance of ultrasonic pulse-echo testing instruments and systems without using electronic measurement instruments. Supported by **ASME Section V, Article 4** for UT.

**4. Documentation (1 mark):**
- Date, time, instrument serial number, probe serial number, couplant batch, ambient temperature.
- dB reference value for the shift.
- Operator signature (Level II minimum).
- Filed in Calibration Log Book and linked to inspection report.

---

**(b) Define DAC curve. Derive the relationship between echo amplitude and distance for an ultrasonic pulse-echo inspection, assuming linear attenuation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. DAC Definition (1 mark):**
The **Distance-Amplitude Correction (DAC) curve** is a graph of echo amplitude (in dB or %FSH) versus reflector depth (in mm), constructed from multiple identical reflectors (e.g., FBHs of the same diameter) at different depths in a reference block. It compensates for amplitude loss due to beam divergence and material attenuation.

**2. Derivation (5 marks):**

The on-axis pressure amplitude from a disc transducer of radius $a$ at distance $r$ in a medium with attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ is:

$$ P(r) = \frac{P_0 \cdot a^2}{r} \cdot e^{-\alpha r} $$

Converting to dB relative to a reference amplitude $P_{ref}$ at reference distance $r_{ref}$:

$$ A_{dB}(r) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P(r)}{P(r_{ref})}\right) $$

$$ A_{dB}(r) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_0 \cdot a^2 \cdot e^{-\alpha r}}{r \cdot P(r_{ref})}\right) $$

Splitting the logarithm:

$$ A_{dB}(r) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_0 \cdot a^2}{P(r_{ref})}\right) - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 20 \alpha r \log_{10}(e) $$

Using $20 \log_{10}(e) = 8.686$:

$$ A_{dB}(r) = C - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8.686 \alpha r $$

where $C$ is the constant determined by the initial reference calibration. The slope of the DAC at any depth $r$ is:

$$ \frac{dA_{dB}}{dr} = -\frac{8.686}{r} - 8.686 \alpha $$

**3. Practical Implication (1 mark):**
The first term $-\frac{8.686}{r}$ dominates near the transducer (beam-spreading); the second term $-8.686\alpha$ dominates in the far field (true attenuation). A linear DAC approximation assumes the latter dominates in the inspection range, simplifying field use.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Explain the calibration procedure for eddy current testing (ET) equipment. What reference standards are used and what parameters are verified? Mention the governing standards. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. ET Calibration Overview (1 mark):**
ET calibration establishes the baseline **frequency, phase, gain, and amplitude response** of the eddy current instrument against reference standards containing artificial defects (EDM notches, through-holes, or conductivity variations).

**2. Reference Standards Used (2 marks):**
- **ASTM E215 Reference Standard** — flat plate with EDM notches of defined width, length, and depth.
- **Conductivity Reference Blocks** — calibrated to **% IACS** (International Annealed Copper Standard) covering 1–110% IACS.
- **Coil-specific reference standards** — supplied by probe manufacturer for a given probe frequency and diameter.
- **Ferrous and non-ferrous reference plates** with known thickness variations.

**3. Calibration Procedure (3 marks):**

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | Warm up instrument for at least 15 minutes |
| 2 | Verify lift-off response on a non-defect area (signal should be a single horizontal trace) |
| 3 | Adjust frequency to specification (typically 100 kHz – 5 MHz) |
| 4 | Adjust phase to place lift-off lobe vertically |
| 5 | Place probe on reference notch; adjust gain so defect signal reaches 50% FSH |
| 6 | Record reference dB and phase angle |
| 7 | Verify on a second notch at different depth → confirm linearity |

**4. Parameters Verified (1 mark):**
- Operating frequency
- Phase rotation accuracy
- Amplitude / Gain linearity
- Conductivity measurement accuracy
- Lift-off compensation
- Filter settings (high-pass / low-pass)

**5. Standards Governing ET Calibration (0 marks — bonus):**
**ASTM E215, ASTM E566, ISO 15549, ASME Section V Article 8.**

---

**(b) Write a detailed note on the calibration of MT equipment. Explain the role of the pie gauge and the Berthold test bar. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Purpose of MT Calibration (1 mark):**
MT calibration verifies that the **magnetic field strength** applied to the component is sufficient to detect surface and near-surface defects, while ensuring no **burn-through** or excessive field occurs.

**2. Pie Gauge (Magnetic Field Indicator) (3 marks):**
- **Construction:** Eight low-carbon steel segments joined at a center; when magnetized, the segments attract each other and hold a weight (typically 4.5 kg or 18 kg).
- **Use:** Placed on the component surface after magnetization. If the magnetic field is adequate, the segments hold the rated weight against gravity.
- **Standards:** Conforms to **ASTM E709** and **ISO 9934-3**.
- **What it verifies:** Adequate tangential field strength to produce **sharp, visible indications** of surface-breaking defects.

**3. Berthold Test Bar (Magnetic Particle Test Bar) (2 marks):**
- **Construction:** A bar with three **artificial defects** (e.g., drilled holes or slots) at known positions — one in the center, one at one end, and one off-center.
- **Use:** Used to verify **adequate field direction and strength** in bar magnetization (current flow technique). All three defects should produce visible indications when MT is properly applied.
- **Standards:** Referenced in **ASTM E709, Section 7**.

**4. Calibration Frequency and Documentation (1 mark):**
- **Before each shift** and whenever equipment parameters are changed.
- Recorded in MT Calibration Log: yoke serial number, lift-off weight, ambient lighting, MT medium batch.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Tier 1 and Tier 2 calibration** — Students often mix up factory/master calibration with on-site working calibration. Clearly distinguish both.
> 2. **Forgetting units in attenuation calculation** — Always specify dB/mm or Np/mm explicitly; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Naming standards without year** — Mention the standard number and (if memory permits) the year of latest revision.
> 4. **Skipping traceability** — Any calibration answer that does NOT mention **traceability to a National Metrology Institute (NMI)** is considered incomplete by KTU 2024 examiners.
> 5. **Mixing DAC with DGS** — DAC is amplitude-based using FBHs; DGS is a distance-gain-size analytical method using theoretical curves. They are not interchangeable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet Snapshot:**

- **Calibration = Comparison with Known Standard** + Documentation + Traceability to NMI.
- **Two Tiers:** Primary (annual, NABL lab) vs Operational (pre-shift, on-site).
- **UT Block:** IIW V1 (basic), IIW V2 (advanced), ASTM E127 (FBH series).
- **RT Standard:** IQI / Penetrameter (wire-type per ASTM E747, hole-type per ASTM E1025).
- **MT Standard:** Pie Gauge (lift-off test) + Berthold Test Bar (current flow verification).
- **PT Standard:** ASTM PSM-5 cracked sensitivity panel — 5 cracks of graded size.
- **ET Standard:** ASTM E215 plate with EDM notches + conductivity reference (1–110% IACS).
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $V = 2d/t$
  - $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_m/A_{ref})$
  - $U_g = dt / (D-t)$
  - $U = k \sqrt{\sum c_i^2 u_i^2}$ (uncertainty, $k=2$ for 95%)
- **DAC Curve:** Compensates for beam-spreading ($1/r$) + attenuation ($\alpha r$). Slope = $-8.686/r - 8.686\alpha$.
- **Calibration Frequency:** Pre-shift + every 4 hours + after probe/battery/couplant change.
- **Acceptance Tolerances:** UT $\pm 2$ dB from baseline; MT $\geq 4.5$ kg lift; RT — all IQI wires visible; ET $\pm 5\%$ amplitude; PT — all 5 PSM-5 cracks visible.
- **Documentation is Mandatory:** Calibration without records is **not a valid calibration** under KTU/ASME/ASTM norms.
- **Traceability Chain:** Component → Probe → Calibrated Block → NABL Lab → NMI (NIST/NPL).
- **Examiner Triggers:** Always mention **uncertainty, traceability, frequency, and acceptance criteria** — these four keywords cover 70% of valuation marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiography Testing: Electromagnetic Radiation Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiography Testing: Electromagnetic Radiation Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Radiographic Testing (RT)** is a volumetric, non-destructive examination method that uses penetrating **electromagnetic radiation** (high-energy photons) — either **X-rays** or **gamma (γ) rays** — to produce a shadow image of the internal structure of a test object on a radiation-sensitive film or digital detector. The image reveals volumetric defects such as voids, cracks, inclusions, porosity, and corrosion.

An **electromagnetic radiation source** in radiography is any device or radioactive isotope that emits high-frequency, short-wavelength photons capable of penetrating engineering materials. The two principal families of sources are:

| Source Class | Origin | Typical Energy Range |
|---|---|---|
| **X-ray sources** | Man-made, machine generated (X-ray tube/linear accelerator) | 50 keV to 25 MeV |
| **Gamma-ray sources** | Naturally emitted by radioactive isotopes (Co-60, Ir-192, Cs-137) | 0.06 MeV to 1.33 MeV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET636 Module 3):** The focus of this topic is on understanding *how* these high-energy photon sources are produced, *what governs their energy*, and *which source is selected for a given material thickness and density*. The fundamental physics — Bremsstrahlung, characteristic radiation, and radioactive decay — must be mastered before moving to exposure techniques and IQI sensitivity.

## 1.2 The Electromagnetic Spectrum in NDT

Radiographic sources occupy the **high-frequency** end of the electromagnetic spectrum. Only a narrow band of wavelengths (≈ 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻¹² m) possesses sufficient energy to penetrate metallic and composite engineering components.

$$\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} \quad ; \quad E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where:
- $\nu$ = frequency of radiation (**Hz**)
- $c$ = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (**m**)
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $E$ = photon energy (**J**, often expressed in **keV** or **MeV**)

> [!NOTE]
> **1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**. In radiography, photon energies are typically expressed in **kilo-electron volts (keV)** or **mega-electron volts (MeV)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Photon Flashlight"

Imagine you are trying to see the bones inside a closed suitcase without opening it. A normal torch (visible light) cannot pass through the leather. Now imagine a "super flashlight" whose beam is so energetic that it passes straight through the suitcase, but gets dimmer where the bones are dense. If you place a photographic plate on the far side, you get a **shadow picture** of the bones.

- The **torchlight** is your X-ray or γ-ray photon beam.
- The **suitcase** is the test specimen (weld, casting, forging).
- The **photographic plate** is the radiographic film / digital detector.
- **Denser regions** (bones / defects / thicker metal) absorb more photons and cast a **darker (less exposed)** region on the film because fewer photons reach it.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** The radiation source is the "engine" of the radiographic system. Its **energy, intensity, and stability** directly determine penetration, exposure time, and the quality (sensitivity) of the resulting radiograph.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of X-ray Generation

X-rays are produced when **high-velocity electrons** are suddenly decelerated upon striking a metallic target inside a **Coolidge-type X-ray tube** (the standard industrial source). Two distinct mechanisms generate the X-ray spectrum:

### 2.1.1 Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation) — Continuous Spectrum

When a bombarding electron passes close to the nucleus of a target atom, the strong Coulomb field **decelerates** the electron. By conservation of energy, the kinetic energy lost is emitted as an X-ray photon.

- The electron may lose **all** of its kinetic energy in a single encounter → produces a photon of **maximum energy** (the **Duane–Hunt limit**).
- The electron may lose only a **fraction** of its energy → produces a photon of **lower energy**.
- The cumulative effect of millions of such encounters produces a **continuous (white) spectrum**.

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V}\ \text{(V in volts, λ in metres)}$$

- $\lambda_{\min}$ = minimum wavelength (the quantum limit, set by tube voltage only)
- $V$ = accelerating voltage across the X-ray tube
- $e$ = electron charge = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C

### 2.1.2 Characteristic Radiation — Discrete Spectrum

When a bombarding electron **ejects an inner-shell (K or L) electron** from a target atom, an outer-shell electron drops down to fill the vacancy. The energy difference between shells is emitted as a **characteristic X-ray photon** with a sharply defined wavelength unique to the target material.

- **K-series** (Kα, Kβ): Transitions from L→K and M→K shells — most energetic, most used.
- **L-series** (Lα, Lβ): Transitions from M→L and N→L shells — lower energy.
- Characteristic peaks appear **superimposed** on the Bremsstrahlung continuum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moseley's Law** relates the frequency of characteristic X-rays to the atomic number (Z) of the target:
> $$\sqrt{\nu} \propto (Z - \sigma)$$
> where $\sigma$ is the **screening constant**. This is why **tungsten (Z = 74)** and **molybdenum (Z = 42)** are preferred targets — higher Z yields more efficient X-ray production.

## 2.2 The Physics of Gamma-ray Sources

Unlike X-rays, gamma rays are **not produced by machines**. They originate from the **nuclear decay** of radioactive isotopes. The parent isotope transitions from an **excited (metastable) nuclear state** to a lower energy state, releasing the energy difference as a γ-ray photon.

### 2.2.1 Radioactive Decay Law

$$N(t) = N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$$

- $N(t)$ = number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$
- $N_0$ = initial number of nuclei
- $\lambda$ = decay constant (**s⁻¹**)
- $t$ = elapsed time

### 2.2.2 Half-Life Relation

$$T_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

Equivalently:
$$N(t) = N_{0} \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{t / T_{1/2}}$$

### 2.2.3 Activity (Source Strength)

$$A(t) = \lambda N(t) = A_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$$

- $A(t)$ = activity in **Becquerels (Bq)** — 1 Bq = 1 disintegration per second
- The **older unit Curie (Ci)** is still used in industry: **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**
- For radiography, source strengths are typically **10–100 Ci** (Ir-192) or **5–100 Ci** (Co-60).

## 2.3 The X-ray Tube (Coolidge Tube) — Functional Anatomy

A modern industrial X-ray tube consists of:

1. **Cathode**: Tungsten filament heated to ≈ 2400 °C → thermionic emission of electrons.
2. **Anode (Target)**: High-Z material (W, Mo) embedded in a copper block for heat dissipation.
3. **Glass/ceramic envelope**: Maintains vacuum (≈ 10⁻⁶ torr).
4. **High-voltage supply**: 50 kV – 450 kV (industrial) or up to 25 MeV (linear accelerator).
5. **Cooling system**: Water or oil circulation to remove ≈ 99 % of input energy as heat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Distribution of an X-ray Tube:** Approximately **99 %** of the electron kinetic energy is converted to **heat** at the anode; only **≈ 1 %** is converted to useful X-radiation. This is the key engineering reason anode cooling is critical.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = h\nu$ | Photon energy from frequency | J, eV |
| 2 | $\lambda = c/\nu$ | Wavelength–frequency relation | m, Hz |
| 3 | $E = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy from wavelength | J, eV |
| 4 | $\lambda_{\min} = 1.24 \times 10^{-6} / V$ | Duane–Hunt limit (minimum wavelength) | m (V in volts) |
| 5 | $E_{\max} = eV$ | Maximum photon energy | keV (V in kV) |
| 6 | $N(t) = N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay law | dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$ | Half-life | s |
| 8 | $A = \lambda N$ | Source activity | Bq, Ci |
| 9 | $\sqrt{\nu} \propto (Z - \sigma)$ | Moseley's law (characteristic X-rays) | Hz |
| 10 | $1\ \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq}$ | Activity unit conversion | — |

## 2.5 Comparative Selection — When to Use X-ray vs Gamma-ray Source

| Selection Criterion | X-ray Source | Gamma-ray Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy adjustability** | Yes — continuous, via kV/mA | No — fixed by isotope |
| **Output intensity** | High, controllable (mA) | Decreases with time (half-life) |
| **Field portability** | Limited (requires power) | Excellent (isotope capsule) |
| **Cost of operation** | Power + maintenance | Source replacement |
| **Best for thin/low-density** | X-ray (low kV) | — |
| **Best for thick steel (>100 mm)** | Linear accelerator (≥ 9 MeV) | Ir-192, Co-60 |
| **On/off capability** | Yes (instant shutoff) | No (always emitting) |
| **Safety infrastructure** | Standard shielding | Special licence, storage, transport |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In a Kerala-based boiler / pressure-vessel fabrication shop, **X-ray units (200–400 kV)** are used for routine weld inspection. For **field radiography of cross-country pipelines** (e.g., Kochi–Mangaluru pipeline), **Ir-192 crawlers** are preferred for portability. **Co-60** is reserved for very thick castings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Duane–Hunt Law (Minimum Wavelength)

**Statement:** When an electron is accelerated through a potential difference $V$ and strikes the target, the *maximum* photon energy that can be produced is numerically equal to the kinetic energy of the electron, and the corresponding minimum wavelength is governed solely by the tube voltage.

### Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of the Incident Electron

A single electron of charge $e$ accelerated through a potential $V$ acquires kinetic energy:
$$E_{k} = eV$$

### Step 2 — Maximum Photon Energy

In the limiting case of a **head-on (central) collision** with the nucleus, the electron loses *all* of its kinetic energy to a single photon. By conservation of energy:
$$E_{\text{photon,max}} = E_{k} = eV$$

### Step 3 — Convert Energy to Wavelength

Using the Planck–Einstein relation $E = hc/\lambda$:
$$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\min}} = eV$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda_{\min}$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Constants

Using $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$, and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(V)}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot V}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V}\ \text{metres}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's shortcut:** If $V$ is expressed in **kV**, then $\lambda_{\min}$ in **angstroms (Å)** is:
> $$\lambda_{\min}(\text{Å}) \approx \dfrac{12.4}{V(\text{kV})}$$
> At 100 kV, $\lambda_{\min} \approx 0.124\ \text{Å}$ — well inside the hard X-ray region.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Half-Life from Decay Constant

**Statement:** The half-life $T_{1/2}$ is the time required for the activity of a source to fall to one-half of its initial value.

### Step 1 — Apply the Decay Law

$$N(t) = N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 2 — Set the Boundary Condition

By definition, at $t = T_{1/2}$, the number of undecayed nuclei is $N_0/2$:

$$\dfrac{N_0}{2} = N_{0} e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

### Step 3 — Cancel $N_0$ and Take Natural Logarithm

$$\dfrac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$-\ln 2 = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $T_{1/2}$

$$\boxed{T_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Activity Decay of an Ir-192 Source

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 50 Ci Ir-192 source is used for field radiography. Given the half-life of Ir-192 is **73.83 days**, calculate:
1. The decay constant $\lambda$.
2. The activity remaining after **180 days** of use.
3. The activity in **Becquerels** at the start.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Decay Constant**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.693}{73.83\ \text{days}} = 9.386 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{per day}$$

Converting to SI (per second):

$$\lambda = \dfrac{9.386 \times 10^{-3}}{86400} = 1.086 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Activity After 180 Days**

$$A(180) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} = 50 \times e^{-(9.386 \times 10^{-3})(180)}$$

$$A(180) = 50 \times e^{-1.6895} = 50 \times 0.1846$$

$$A(180) \approx 9.23\ \text{Ci}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Initial Activity to Bq**

$$A_0 = 50 \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} = 1.85 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Bq} = 1.85\ \text{TBq}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Ir-192 source has dropped to ≈ 18.5 % of its original strength in 180 days (≈ 2.5 half-lives). At this point, exposure times must be **increased by ≈ 5.4×** to maintain the same film density, or the source must be **replenished**. This is the key practical reason why radiographic source management is critical in NDT.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Isotopes Used in Industrial Radiography

| Property | **Cobalt-60 (⁶⁰Co)** | **Iridium-192 (¹⁹²Ir)** | **Caesium-137 (¹³⁷Cs)** | **Thulium-170 (¹⁷⁰Tm)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Principal γ-energy | 1.17 & 1.33 MeV (avg 1.25 MeV) | 0.20 – 0.61 MeV (avg 0.35 MeV) | 0.662 MeV (mono-energetic) | 0.084 MeV |
| Half-life | **5.27 years** | **73.83 days** | **30.17 years** | **128.6 days** |
| Steel penetration | Up to **≈ 230 mm** | Up to **≈ 70 mm** | Up to **≈ 80 mm** | Up to **≈ 12 mm** |
| Specific activity | High | High | Moderate | High |
| Typical use | Heavy castings, thick welds | Pipeline, field work | Calibration, medium sections | Thin sections, light alloys |
| Decay product | ⁶⁰Ni (stable) | ¹⁹²Pt (stable) | ¹³⁷Ba (stable) | ¹⁷⁰Yb (stable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-energy advantage of Cs-137:** It emits a *mono-energetic* γ-ray, making it ideal for exposure-time calculations and densitometric studies. Co-60 emits **two close energies** (1.17 + 1.33 MeV) — for practical radiography they are treated as a single "average" 1.25 MeV peak.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coolidge X-ray Tube Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HV Power Supply 50 to 400 kV] --> B[Cathode Filament Tungsten Heated to 2400 C]
    B --> C[Thermionic Electron Emission]
    A --> D[Accelerating Potential V Applied Between Cathode and Anode]
    C --> E[High Velocity Electron Beam]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Vacuum Envelope Glass or Ceramic 10 to power minus 6 torr]
    F --> G[Focused Beam Strikes Tungsten Anode Target Z equals 74]
    G --> H1[Bremsstrahlung Continuous Spectrum 1 percent of energy]
    G --> H2[Characteristic K and L Lines 1 percent of energy]
    G --> I[Heat Dissipation 99 percent of energy]
    I --> J[Copper Anode Block + Water or Oil Cooling]
    H1 --> K[Useful X ray Beam Exits Through Beryllium Window]
    H2 --> K
    K --> L[Collimator and Filter]
    L --> M[Radiographic Exposure on Test Specimen]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the electron path from cathode → vacuum envelope → anode. Note the 99:1 energy split between heat and X-rays. The beryllium window is used because Be (Z = 4) is highly transparent to X-rays while maintaining vacuum integrity.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Decision Tree for Source Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start Material Thickness and Density to be Inspected] --> Q1{Is power available on site?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Steel thickness less than 100 mm?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is field portability critical?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Thickness less than 70 mm?}
    Q3 -- No --> Q5[Use Fixed X ray Installation]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Ir 192 source 0.35 MeV avg]
    Q4 -- No --> Q7[Use Co 60 source 1.25 MeV avg]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q8{Is variable kV needed for multiple jobs?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9[Use 200 to 400 kV Industrial X ray Unit]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10[Use Constant Potential X ray Unit 160 kV]
    Q5 --> DONE[Apply ALARA Shielding and Safety Protocols]
    Q9 --> DONE
    Q10 --> DONE
    Q6 --> DONE
    Q7 --> DONE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — X-ray Spectrum Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Incident Electrons at Energy eV]
        E0[Electron Beam Energy equals eV keV]
    end
    INPUT --> M1{Mechanism of Photon Production}
    M1 -- Nuclear Coulomb field Deceleration --> B[Bremsstrahlung Continuous Spectrum]
    M1 -- Inner Shell Electron Ejection --> C[Characteristic Peaks K alpha K beta L alpha]
    B --> OUT[Combined X ray Spectrum Intensity vs Wavelength]
    C --> OUT
    OUT --> LAMBDA[Lambda min equals 1.24 divided by V kV angstrom]
    OUT --> PEAK[Discrete K alpha K beta peaks superimposed on continuum]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Radioactive Decay Chain for Co-60

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CO60[Co 60 Excited Nucleus Half life 5.27 years] -- beta decay --> NI60m[Ni 60 Metastable]
    NI60m -- gamma 1.17 MeV --> NI60a[Ni 60 Intermediate State]
    NI60a -- gamma 1.33 MeV --> NI60s[Ni 60 Stable]
    CO60 -. original gamma emission .-> CO60_out[Useful Radiography Photons 1.17 and 1.33 MeV]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Bremsstrahlung and characteristic X-radiation. State the factors that control the minimum wavelength of the emitted X-ray spectrum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic X-rays |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Deceleration of bombarding electrons in the Coulomb field of the target nucleus | Electron transition between atomic shells (e.g., L→K) after inner-shell ionisation |
| **Spectrum** | Continuous (broad wavelength range) | Discrete (sharp, well-defined peaks Kα, Kβ) |
| **Energy control** | Depends on tube voltage $V$ | Depends on target material atomic number $Z$ |

The **minimum wavelength** is controlled *only* by the **accelerating voltage** $V$, given by the **Duane–Hunt law**:
$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V}\ \text{metres}$$

**(Marking key:** 1 mark for Bremsstrahlung explanation, 1 mark for characteristic explanation, 1 mark for Duane–Hunt formula with units.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define radioactive half-life. A Cobalt-60 source has a half-life of 5.27 years. If the initial activity is 30 Ci, what is the activity after 10.54 years?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Half-life $T_{1/2}$ is the time required for the activity of a radioactive source to decay to **half** of its initial value.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

Number of half-lives elapsed:
$$n = \dfrac{10.54}{5.27} = 2$$

Final activity:
$$A = A_0 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = 30 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2} = 30 \times \dfrac{1}{4}$$

$$\boxed{A = 7.5\ \text{Ci}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — X-ray Production & Spectrum Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEMET636]**

**a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Coolidge X-ray tube**. Discuss the role of the cathode, anode, vacuum envelope, and high-voltage supply. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An X-ray tube operates at 200 kV and 5 mA. Calculate:
   1. The **minimum wavelength** of the emitted X-rays.
   2. The **maximum photon energy** in keV.
   3. The **number of photons per second** striking the target (assume 1 % conversion efficiency).
   4. Comment on the **percentage of input power** that becomes X-rays vs heat. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working of Coolidge Tube (7 marks):**

1. **Cathode (1 mark):** A tungsten filament heated to ≈ 2400 °C by a separate low-voltage/current source. Thermionic emission releases a stream of electrons. The filament is housed in a focusing cup that directs the electron beam onto a small area of the anode (the focal spot).

2. **Anode / Target (1.5 marks):** Made of a high-Z refractory metal — typically **tungsten (Z = 74)** — embedded in a copper anode block. Tungsten is chosen for its high melting point (3422 °C) and high atomic number, which maximises X-ray production efficiency. The copper block conducts heat away from the focal spot to the cooling system.

3. **Vacuum Envelope (1 mark):** A sealed glass or ceramic tube evacuated to ≈ 10⁻⁶ torr. The vacuum prevents the electrons from colliding with gas molecules (which would scatter the beam and damage the filament).

4. **High-Voltage Supply (1.5 marks):** A high-voltage transformer (50–450 kV for industrial units) accelerates the electrons from cathode to anode. The applied voltage $V$ controls the **maximum photon energy** ($E_{\max} = eV$) and the **minimum wavelength** (Duane–Hunt law).

5. **Cooling System (1 mark):** Water-cooled or oil-cooled anode removes the ≈ 99 % of input energy that is converted to heat at the focal spot. Without cooling, the anode would melt within seconds.

6. **Beryllium Window (1 mark):** X-rays exit the tube through a thin beryllium window, chosen because Be (Z = 4) is highly transparent to X-rays while maintaining vacuum integrity.

> Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** for the functional architecture.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**1) Minimum wavelength (2 marks):**
$$\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$\lambda_{\min} = 6.2 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{m} = 0.062\ \text{Å}$$

**2) Maximum photon energy (2 marks):**
$$E_{\max} = eV = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(200 \times 10^{3})$$

$$E_{\max} = 3.204 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{J} = 200\ \text{keV}$$

**3) Number of photons per second (2 marks):**

Input electrical power:
$$P_{\text{in}} = V \times I = (200 \times 10^{3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) = 1000\ \text{W}$$

Power converted to X-rays (1 % efficiency):
$$P_{X} = 0.01 \times 1000 = 10\ \text{W}$$

Number of photons per second (each at maximum energy, conservatively):
$$n = \dfrac{P_{X}}{E_{\max}} = \dfrac{10}{3.204 \times 10^{-14}}$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 3.12 \times 10^{14}\ \text{photons/second}}$$

*(Note: In practice, photons have a spectrum of energies, so the actual photon count is higher — but the calculation gives a lower bound.)*

**4) Heat vs X-ray comment (1 mark):** Approximately **99 % of the 1000 W input power is dissipated as heat** (≈ 990 W) at the anode, while only **10 W (1 %)** is converted to useful X-radiation. This is why anode cooling is a critical design constraint in industrial X-ray tubes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Gamma-ray Sources & Radioactive Decay**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEMET636]**

**a)** Explain the principle of gamma-ray emission. Compare the properties of **Cobalt-60, Iridium-192, and Caesium-137** as industrial radiography sources. State one typical application for each. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A radiography company receives a fresh **30 Ci Iridium-192 source**. The half-life of Ir-192 is **73.83 days**.
   1. Calculate the **decay constant** in s⁻¹.
   2. Determine the **activity** of the source after **6 months** (≈ 180 days).
   3. If the source is used continuously for 1 year, what is the **total number of disintegrations** that occur in that year? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principle of Gamma-ray Emission & Isotope Comparison (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** Gamma rays are emitted by **radioactive nuclei** that are in an excited (metastable) state. The nucleus transitions to a lower energy state by releasing the excess energy as a high-energy photon. Unlike X-rays (which originate from electron-shell transitions), γ-rays originate from **transitions within the atomic nucleus**. The energy of γ-rays is **characteristic of the specific isotope** and is **not adjustable** by the operator.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | ⁶⁰Co | ¹⁹²Ir | ¹³⁷Cs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **γ-energy** | 1.17 & 1.33 MeV | 0.20 – 0.61 MeV (avg 0.35 MeV) | 0.662 MeV (mono) |
| **Half-life** | 5.27 years | 73.83 days | 30.17 years |
| **Steel range** | 50 – 230 mm | 10 – 70 mm | 30 – 80 mm |
| **Application** | Heavy castings, reactor pressure vessels | Field pipeline crawlers, circumferential welds | Medium-thickness castings, calibration |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- **Co-60:** Radiography of thick pressure-vessel forgings in fabrication shops.
- **Ir-192:** Field radiography of cross-country oil & gas pipeline girth welds.
- **Cs-137:** Calibration of radiation monitors, inspection of medium-thickness components where long source life is critical.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**1) Decay constant (2 marks):**

$$T_{1/2} = 73.83\ \text{days} = 73.83 \times 86400\ \text{s} = 6.379 \times 10^{6}\ \text{s}$$

$$\lambda = \dfrac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.693}{6.379 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 1.086 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s}^{-1}}$$

**2) Activity after 180 days (2 marks):**

$$A(180) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} = 30 \times e^{-(1.086 \times 10^{-7})(180 \times 86400)}$$

$$A(180) = 30 \times e^{-1.6895} = 30 \times 0.1846$$

$$\boxed{A(180) \approx 5.54\ \text{Ci}}$$

**3) Total disintegrations in 1 year (3 marks):**

Number of atoms in 30 Ci:
$$A_0 = 30 \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} = 1.11 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Bq}$$

Initial number of atoms:
$$N_0 = \dfrac{A_0}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1.11 \times 10^{12}}{1.086 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.022 \times 10^{19}\ \text{atoms}$$

Activity after 1 year (365 days):
$$A(365) = 30 \times e^{-(1.086 \times 10^{-7})(365 \times 86400)} = 30 \times e^{-3.426}$$

$$A(365) = 30 \times 0.0325 = 0.975\ \text{Ci} = 3.61 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq}$$

Total disintegrations in 1 year (integral of activity from 0 to T):
$$\text{Total} = \int_{0}^{T} A(t)\, dt = \dfrac{A_0 - A(T)}{\lambda}$$

$$\text{Total} = \dfrac{(1.11 \times 10^{12}) - (3.61 \times 10^{10})}{1.086 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\text{Total} = \dfrac{1.074 \times 10^{12}}{1.086 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total} \approx 9.89 \times 10^{18}\ \text{disintegrations in 1 year}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PEMET636 Module 3 — Electromagnetic Radiation Sources:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the role of target material (Z):** Many students state that the minimum wavelength depends on target material. **It does not** — $\lambda_{\min}$ depends *only* on the tube voltage $V$. The target material $Z$ controls the *intensity* and the *characteristic peaks*, not $\lambda_{\min}$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Bremsstrahlung with characteristic radiation:** Bremsstrahlung gives a *continuous* spectrum governed by $V$; characteristic X-rays give *discrete* peaks governed by $Z$. Marks are lost when students interchange these.
>
> 3. **Unit confusion in decay problems:** Always state $A$ in **Ci or Bq consistently**. Mixing units inside one calculation (e.g., $A$ in Ci and $\lambda$ in s⁻¹) is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark penalty.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the 99 % heat loss:** In Coolidge tube questions, examiners expect explicit mention of the **≈ 99 % thermal dissipation** and the need for **anode cooling**. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Half-life of Ir-192:** A common confusion is between **Ir-192 (73.83 days)** and **Co-60 (5.27 years)**. Mixing these half-lives in numerical problems leads to wild errors. Memorise both.
>
> 6. **Missing Moseley's Law:** When asked about characteristic radiation, always cite **Moseley's law** ($\sqrt{\nu} \propto Z - \sigma$) for a 1-mark bonus.
>
> 7. **Calculation of $N$ from $A$:** When $A = \lambda N$, students often forget to convert half-life to seconds before computing $\lambda$. **Always convert $T_{1/2}$ to the same time unit as the elapsed time $t$.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Electromagnetic radiation** is energy propagated as coupled electric and magnetic waves; in radiography, only high-frequency, short-wavelength photons (X-rays and γ-rays) are used.
- [ ] **X-rays** are produced in a **Coolidge tube** by accelerating electrons into a tungsten target.
- [ ] **Bremsstrahlung** = continuous spectrum (depends on $V$). **Characteristic X-rays** = discrete peaks (depend on $Z$).
- [ ] **Duane–Hunt law:** $\lambda_{\min} = \dfrac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V}$ metres (with $V$ in volts).
- [ ] **Moseley's law:** $\sqrt{\nu} \propto (Z - \sigma)$ — higher $Z$ → higher characteristic frequency.
- [ ] **Gamma rays** originate from **nuclear transitions**; they are mono-energetic (or discrete multi-peak) for a given isotope.
- [ ] **Decay law:** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$; **Half-life:** $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Activity units:** $1\ \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq}$.
- [ ] **60-Co:** $E_\gamma \approx 1.25$ MeV, $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ yr, used for **thick sections (>100 mm steel)**.
- [ ] **192-Ir:** $E_\gamma \approx 0.35$ MeV avg, $T_{1/2} = 73.83$ days, used for **field pipeline radiography**.
- [ ] **137-Cs:** $E_\gamma = 0.662$ MeV, $T_{1/2} = 30.17$ yr, used for **calibration and medium sections**.
- [ ] **170-Tm:** $E_\gamma = 0.084$ MeV, used for **thin sections and light alloys**.
- [ ] **X-ray tube efficiency:** ≈ 1 % X-ray, 99 % heat → **cooling is critical**.
- [ ] **X-ray source is adjustable** in energy (kV) and intensity (mA); **gamma source is not**.
- [ ] **X-ray can be switched off**; **gamma source always emits** — requires licensed storage, transport, and ALARA shielding.
- [ ] **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) governs all source-handling decisions.
- [ ] **Constant-potential X-ray units** produce more uniform spectra than pulsed units — preferred for critical sensitivity work.
- [ ] **Linear accelerators (LINACs)** are used for energies ≥ 9 MeV (very thick steel, > 250 mm) and concrete radiography.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU Exam Strategy:** For numerical problems, always write the **formula first**, then the **substitution of numerical values with units**, and finally the **boxed answer with units**. Examiners give partial credit for the correct formula even if the arithmetic fails.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection Techniques: SWSI, DWSI, DWDI, Panoramic Exposure, and Real-Time Radiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspection Techniques: SWSI, DWSI, DWDI, Panoramic Exposure, and Real-Time Radiography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Overview of Radiographic Inspection Techniques

Radiographic Testing (RT) is one of the most widely used NDT methods for detecting volumetric defects (porosity, inclusions, cracks, lack of fusion) in welds and castings. The **inspection technique** refers to the geometric configuration of the radiation source, the test object, and the recording medium (film/detector). Choosing the right technique is critical because it determines defect sensitivity, geometric unsharpness, and coverage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** This module specifically deals with **specialized exposure techniques** used when the geometry of the component (pipes, nozzles, vessels) prevents the use of a standard planar setup.

---

## 1.2 The Four Special Radiographic Techniques

### (a) SWSI — Single Wall Single Image

**Formal Definition:** In the *Single Wall Single Image* technique, the radiation source is placed on one side of the test object and the film (or detector) on the other side, such that the X-ray/gamma beam penetrates **only one wall** of the object. A single image is recorded on the film.

- **Geometry:** Source → Wall 1 → Film
- **Best suited for:** Thick plates, large diameter pipes, pressure vessel shells.
- **ASTM Reference:** ASME Section V, Article 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (SWSI):** Imagine shining a flashlight through a single glass window. You see the shadow of the object pressed against that one pane of glass — only one wall is illuminated and recorded.

---

### (b) DWSI — Double Wall Single Image

**Formal Definition:** In the *Double Wall Single Image* technique, the radiation source and film are placed on **opposite sides** of a hollow cylindrical object (pipe/tube), and the beam must pass through **both walls** to reach the film. Only **one radiographic image** is produced.

- **Geometry:** Source → Wall 1 → Cavity → Wall 2 → Film
- **Best suited for:** Small-to-medium diameter pipes where placing source inside is impossible.
- **Limitation:** The far wall produces an enlarged, blurred image due to geometric unsharpness ($U_g$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (DWSI):** Think of taking an X-ray of your own hand by placing the film behind your palm. The beam crosses two "walls" (back of hand, then palm), and the bones closer to the source appear sharper than those farther away.

---

### (c) DWDI — Double Wall Double Image

**Formal Definition:** In the *Double Wall Double Image* technique, the radiation beam is **offset at an angle** (typically 15°–30° from the pipe axis) so that **two distinct images** of the far and near walls appear on the same film.

- **Geometry:** Source offset → Beam passes through both walls at two regions → Two images on one film.
- **Best suited for:** Small diameter pipe welds (where both walls must be inspected and inside access is impossible).
- **Key insight:** The angle separates the two wall images spatially on the film.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (DWDI):** Imagine taking a photograph of a long hallway mirror with two candles in it. The mirror reflects both candles, and the offset angle causes the two candle images to appear separated in the photo — this is exactly what DWDI does with pipe walls.

---

### (d) Panoramic Exposure

**Formal Definition:** A *Panoramic Exposure* is a technique in which the radiation source is placed **inside** a hollow cylindrical component (or at the center of a circular object), and the film is wrapped around the **outer circumference** of the component, providing a complete 360° image in a single exposure.

- **Geometry:** Source at center → Film wrapped externally → Continuous annular image.
- **Best suited for:** Circumferential welds on pipes, nozzles, turbine casings, small pressure vessels.
- **Key constraint:** Source size must be small; the SFD varies along the wrap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Panoramic):** Place a candle in the center of a transparent cylindrical glass, and put a strip of photographic paper around the outside. The paper will record a 360° "shadow" of any internal defect — this is the essence of panoramic exposure.

---

### (e) Real-Time Radiography (RTR)

**Formal Definition:** *Real-Time Radiography* is a modern digital inspection technique where the conventional X-ray film is replaced by an **electronic image receptor** (Flat Panel Detector, Image Intensifier, or Linear Diode Array), and the image is displayed on a monitor **within seconds** of exposure — no chemical processing is required.

- **Output:** Live digital image on monitor, stored electronically.
- **Dynamic capability:** Can capture moving objects (e.g., flowing fluids, rotating components).
- **Standards:** ASTM E 2597, ASME V Article 2 (mandatory Appendix III).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (RTR):** Think of an X-ray machine in a hospital that shows your bones on a TV screen instantly, instead of printing them on a film that takes time to develop. That's RTR in industrial inspection.

---

## 1.3 Summary Definition Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize KTU ready-reckoner** for the examination:

| Technique | Source Position | Wall(s) Penetrated | No. of Images | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SWSI** | Outside | One | One | Plates, large vessels |
| **DWSI** | Outside | Two | One | Medium pipes (no internal access) |
| **DWDI** | Outside (offset) | Two | Two | Small pipe welds |
| **Panoramic** | Inside (center) | One wall only | Continuous 360° | Circumferential welds |
| **RTR** | Outside / Inside | One or Two | Digital (live) | Dynamic inspection, automation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Unsharpness — The Underlying Theory

All radiographic techniques are governed by one fundamental principle: **the size of the radiation source (focal spot) creates a "penumbra" (geometric blur)** at the edges of any defect or object boundary. This blur is called **Geometric Unsharpness ($U_g$)**.

### Derivation Logic — Step by Step:

1. Consider a point defect in the test object at distance $t$ from the film.
2. The source is a disc of diameter $d$ located at Source-to-Film Distance $D$.
3. Rays from the **edges** of the source disc project two different shadow images of the defect.
4. The displacement between these two shadows is the geometric unsharpness $U_g$.

**Standard Formula (by similar triangles):**

$$
U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D - t}
$$

Where:
- $U_g$ = Geometric unsharpness (mm)
- $d$ = Effective source/focal spot size (mm)
- $D$ = Source-to-Film Distance, SFD (mm)
- $t$ = Source-side object thickness or object-to-film distance (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For SWSI:** $t$ = thickness of the wall being radiographed.
> **For DWSI:** $t$ = thickness of the **far wall** (the wall nearest the film) plus the source-side wall — geometric unsharpness is **larger**, so sensitivity is reduced.
> **For Panoramic:** $D$ varies along the wrap, so $U_g$ is **not constant** — it is maximum at the position diametrically opposite the source.

---

## 2.2 Magnification in Radiography

The projected size of a defect on the film is magnified due to the divergent beam. The **magnification factor $M$** is:

$$
M = \frac{D}{D - t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For DWSI, the **far wall** appears enlarged by $M_{far} = D/(D - t_{far})$ and is also blurred by $U_{g,far} = d \cdot t_{far} / (D - t_{far})$.
> The **near wall** has $t_{near} = 0$ (it is adjacent to the source), so $M_{near} \approx 1$ and $U_{g,near} \approx 0$ — the near wall image is **sharper and at near-actual size**.

This is the key reason why **DWSI and DWDI prefer to inspect the far wall** — the near wall creates a confusing sharp image overlay.

---

## 2.3 Inverse Square Law (Intensity)

The X-ray intensity at the film follows the inverse square law:

$$
I = \frac{I_0 \cdot D_0^2}{D^2}
$$

Where $I_0$ is the intensity at reference distance $D_0$. For **panoramic exposure**, since $D$ varies around the circumference, the intensity variation must be compensated by using **sensitive films** or **Lead Screens of varying thickness** (a technique called "intensity compensation shims").

---

## 2.4 Minimum Source-to-Film Distance (ASTM E 1000)

To limit geometric unsharpness, codes specify a **minimum SFD**:

$$
D_{min} = \frac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}} + t
$$

**Common Code Values of $U_{g,\max}$:**
- ASME Section V: $U_{g,\max} \le 0.020$ in (0.51 mm) for Class welds.
- ISO 17636-1: $U_{g,\max}$ typically $\le 0.3 \cdot t$ or per acceptance class.

For **DWDI**, an additional constraint is the **angle of offset** $\theta$:

$$
\sin(\theta) \approx \frac{d_w + S}{D}
$$

where $d_w$ is the wall-image separation on the film and $S$ is the source offset from the pipe centerline.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exam — Memorize with Units:**

| Formula | Expression | Application | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Geometric Unsharpness | $U_g = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{D - t}$ | All RT techniques | mm |
| Magnification Factor | $M = \dfrac{D}{D - t}$ | Defect image size | Dimensionless |
| Min. SFD | $D_{min} = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}} + t$ | Code compliance | mm |
| Inverse Square Law | $I = \dfrac{I_0 D_0^2}{D^2}$ | Exposure calculation | mR, Gy |
| Exposure (mAs) | $E = mA \cdot s$ | X-ray exposure | mA·s |
| Panoramic SFD variation | $D_{max} = 2R$ (R = pipe radius) | Panoramic | mm |
| DWDI offset angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{d_w}{D}\right)$ | DWDI geometry | radians/degrees |
| RTR Frame rate | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{int}}$ | Real-time display | fps |

> **Constants / Standard Values (Bolded):**
> - **ASTM E 1000** $U_{g,\max} = 0.020$ **in (= 0.51 mm)** for ASME.
> - **ISO 17636-1 Class B:** $U_{g,\max} \le 0.3 \cdot t$ (for $t \ge 10$ mm).
> - **Typical RTR matrix size:** $1024 \times 1024$ **pixels to $4096 \times 4096$ pixels**.
> - **Typical RTR pixel pitch:** $100\ \mu m$ **to $400\ \mu m$**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SWSI**: Used in fabrication of pressure vessel shells, boiler drums, nuclear containment — high sensitivity critical.
- **DWSI**: Used in field welds of piping spools where in-line crawlers are too expensive.
- **DWDI**: Standard technique for **small-bore instrument tubing** in petrochemical and aerospace hydraulic lines.
- **Panoramic**: Used for inspection of **nuclear fuel rod bundles** and **gun barrel liners** — internal source required.
- **RTR**: Used in **airport security scanning**, **castings production lines** (e.g., aluminum wheels), and **weld in-service monitoring** (e.g., creep damage studies).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Geometric Unsharpness ($U_g$) from First Principles

**Statement to Prove:** For a disc source of diameter $d$ at distance $D$ from the film, and an object of thickness $t$ placed in contact with the film, the geometric unsharpness of a point feature is given by $U_g = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{D - t}$.

**Setup:** Consider a point $P$ inside the test object, located at depth $t$ from the film surface. The radiation source is a circular disc of diameter $d$ centered at distance $D$ from the film.

**Step 1 — Identify similar triangles.**

The two extreme points of the source (top and bottom of the disc) project two shadow images of point $P$ onto the film. The geometry forms two similar triangles.

**Step 2 — Triangle from the source edge to the film.**

The half-source radius is $d/2$, and the source-to-film distance is $D$. From the source edge, the ray travels to the bottom of the projected shadow. The vertical span of the projected blur is $U_g$.

**Step 3 — Similar triangle ratio.**

The small triangle (source half-diameter to point $P$) has height $(D - t)$ and base $(d/2)$. The large triangle (source half-diameter to shadow) has height $D$ and base $(U_g/2 + d/2)$.

$$
\frac{d/2}{D - t} = \frac{d/2 + U_g/2}{D}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply.**

$$
\frac{d}{2(D - t)} = \frac{d + U_g}{2D}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the 2's and cross-multiply both sides by $2D(D-t)$.**

$$
d \cdot D = (d + U_g)(D - t)
$$

**Step 6 — Expand the right side.**

$$
d \cdot D = d \cdot D - d \cdot t + U_g \cdot D - U_g \cdot t
$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $d \cdot D$ from both sides.**

$$
0 = - d \cdot t + U_g \cdot D - U_g \cdot t
$$

**Step 8 — Rearrange for $U_g$.**

$$
U_g \cdot (D - t) = d \cdot t
$$

**Step 9 — Final Result.**

$$
\boxed{U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D - t}}
$$

This is the **fundamental unsharpness equation** used in every RT setup.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Source-to-Film Distance ($D_{min}$)

**Statement to Prove:** Given maximum allowable geometric unsharpness $U_{g,\max}$ and source size $d$, the minimum SFD to keep unsharpness within limits is $D_{min} = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}} + t$.

**Step 1 — Start with the unsharpness equation.**

$$
U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D - t}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $U_g$ equal to its maximum allowed value (worst-case design).**

$$
U_{g,\max} = \frac{d \cdot t}{D_{min} - t}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $D_{min} - t$.**

$$
D_{min} - t = \frac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}}
$$

**Step 4 — Add $t$ to both sides.**

$$
D_{min} = \frac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}} + t
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{min} = \frac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}} + t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (Numerical Example):** Let $d = 2$ mm, $t = 20$ mm, $U_{g,\max} = 0.5$ mm.
> $D_{min} = (2 \cdot 20)/0.5 + 20 = 80 + 20 = 100$ mm. This is sensible — the SFD must be much larger than the object thickness to limit blur.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Magnification of Far Wall in DWSI

**Statement to Prove:** The image size of a flaw on the far wall (wall adjacent to film) is $M = D/(D - t_{far})$ times the actual flaw size.

**Step 1 — Define geometry.** A point flaw of size $a$ is located at the **far wall** (at distance $t_{far}$ from the film, where $t_{far}$ is the sum of both wall thicknesses + any internal bore).

**Step 2 — Projected image size $a'$.** By similar triangles, the projection of the flaw onto the film is scaled by the ratio of the source-to-film distance to the source-to-flaw distance:

$$
\frac{a'}{D} = \frac{a}{D - t_{far}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for magnification $M = a'/a$.**

$$
M = \frac{D}{D - t_{far}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final.**

$$
\boxed{M = \frac{D}{D - t_{far}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Consequence:** As $t_{far} \to D$, $M \to \infty$ (the flaw image becomes infinitely large and infinitely blurred). This is why **DWSI on thick double walls becomes unworkable** — the image degrades severely.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Panoramic SFD Distribution

**Statement to Prove:** In a panoramic setup with a source of diameter $d$ at the center of a pipe of inner radius $R$, the unsharpness at angular position $\alpha$ from the source direction is $U_g(\alpha) = \dfrac{d \cdot (R - R\cos\alpha)}{2R - (R - R\cos\alpha)}$.

**Step 1 — Set up geometry.** Let the source lie on the pipe axis. The film lies on the inner wall of the pipe at distance $D(\alpha) = 2R$ (diameter) along the line through the source. At angle $\alpha$ from this line, the distance to the film is $D(\alpha) = R + R\cos\alpha = R(1 + \cos\alpha)$.

**Step 2 — Object-to-film distance at angle $\alpha$.** A point on the inner pipe surface at angle $\alpha$ has its defect at the pipe wall, so the defect-to-film distance is zero there (in contact). For a defect in the bulk wall at depth $t$:

$$
t(\alpha) = t_{\text{pipe wall}} \quad (\text{constant})
$$

**Step 3 — Geometric unsharpness at angle $\alpha$:**

$$
U_g(\alpha) = \frac{d \cdot t(\alpha)}{D(\alpha) - t(\alpha)} = \frac{d \cdot t}{R(1 + \cos\alpha) - t}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum and minimum.**

- At $\alpha = 0$ (directly opposite source): $D = 2R$, so $U_g$ is **minimum** (best sharpness).
- At $\alpha = 180°$ (adjacent to source): $D = 0$, $U_g \to \infty$ (unworkable — the source is on the film!).

**Step 5 — Practical range.** Panoramic exposure is only useful over $\alpha \in [30°, 150°]$, beyond which $U_g$ exceeds 0.5 mm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume panoramic has uniform SFD. **It does not.** The SFD varies as $D(\alpha) = R(1 + \cos\alpha)$. The film density also varies — must use **compensating filters (intensity shims)** made of lead or copper.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (for KTU Answers)

| Parameter | SWSI | DWSI | DWDI | Panoramic | RTR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wall penetrated | 1 | 2 | 2 (offset) | 1 (radial) | 1 or 2 |
| Source position | External | External | External | **Internal** | Either |
| $U_g$ control | Easy | Difficult | Difficult | Variable | Detector-limited |
| Inspection time | Long | Long | Long | Long | **Short** |
| Film required? | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No (digital)** |
| Cost | Low | Low | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Defect sensitivity | Best | Lower | Moderate | Variable | **Best (with image processing)** |
| Dynamic inspection | No | No | No | No | **Yes** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define SWSI and DWSI radiographic techniques. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**SWSI (Single Wall Single Image):** In this technique, the radiation source is placed on one side of the test object and the film is placed on the opposite side, such that the beam passes through **only one wall** of the object. A single image of the weld/region is recorded on the film.

**DWSI (Double Wall Single Image):** In this technique, the source and film are placed on **opposite sides** of a hollow cylindrical component (e.g., pipe), and the radiation beam must penetrate **both walls** to reach the film. Only **one image** is recorded on the film, showing the projection of both walls superimposed.

**Applications:**
- SWSI: Inspection of **boiler drum shell circumferential welds**, large pressure vessel seams.
- DWSI: Inspection of **piping welds in chemical plants** where internal crawlers are not used.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Real-Time Radiography (RTR)? List any two advantages over conventional film radiography.**

**Model Answer:**

**Real-Time Radiography (RTR)** is a digital NDT technique in which the conventional radiographic film is replaced by an **electronic image receptor** such as a Flat Panel Detector (FPD), Image Intensifier, or Linear Diode Array (LDA). The attenuated radiation beam is converted into a **digital image that is displayed on a monitor within seconds** of exposure. No chemical film processing is required.

**Two Advantages over Conventional Film Radiography:**
1. **Instant Image Availability:** The image is available in **less than 30 seconds** versus 10–30 minutes for film development, drastically reducing inspection cycle time.
2. **Dynamic Inspection Capability:** RTR can capture **moving objects** (e.g., fluid flow, rotating turbine blades), enabling inspection of dynamic processes impossible with film.
3. **Image Enhancement:** Digital post-processing (contrast, edge enhancement, noise filtering) improves defect visibility without re-exposure.
4. **No chemical waste** — environmentally friendly, no darkroom required.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Double Wall Double Image (DWDI)** radiographic technique. Derive the relationship between the source offset angle and the wall-image separation on the film. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A circumferential weld in a pipe of **outer diameter 200 mm and wall thickness 12 mm** is to be inspected using the **DWDI technique**. If the source size is **2.0 mm** and the geometric unsharpness must not exceed **0.6 mm**, calculate the **minimum Source-to-Film Distance**. Also determine the **offset angle** if the two wall images must be separated by **4 mm** on the film. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) DWDI Technique Explanation:**

In the **Double Wall Double Image (DWDI)** technique, the radiation source is placed **outside** the pipe and is **offset from the pipe centerline** by an angle $\theta$ (typically 15° to 30°). The beam passes through **both walls** of the pipe at two different angular positions, producing **two distinct images** of the weld on the same film.

- **Top image:** Corresponds to the **upper wall** of the pipe (the wall closer to the source).
- **Bottom image:** Corresponds to the **lower wall** (the wall farther from the source).
- The two images are **separated vertically** on the film due to the offset angle.

**Derivation of source offset angle vs. image separation:**

Consider the source offset from the centerline by a distance $h$. The two wall images on the film are separated by:

$$
S = h \cdot \left( \frac{D}{D - t_{far}} \right) - h \cdot \left( \frac{D}{D - t_{near}} \right)
$$

For a thin wall approximation ($t_{near} \approx 0$ and $t_{far} \approx $ pipe diameter), the separation simplifies to:

$$
S \approx h \cdot \frac{D}{D - D_{pipe}} = h \cdot \frac{1}{1 - D_{pipe}/D}
$$

For $D \gg D_{pipe}$:

$$
S \approx h = D \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

Thus:

$$
\sin(\theta) = \frac{S}{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{S}{D}\right)
$$

**Schematic** (described): Source offset by angle θ from pipe centerline; two divergent beams pass through near wall and far wall; two images appear vertically separated on the film placed below the pipe.

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Pipe outer diameter $D_o = 200$ mm
- Wall thickness $t_w = 12$ mm
- Source size $d = 2.0$ mm
- Maximum geometric unsharpness $U_{g,\max} = 0.6$ mm
- Wall-image separation required on film $S = 4$ mm

**Step 1 — Total thickness of far wall (in DWSI/DWDI).** For DWDI on a pipe, the relevant object-to-film distance $t$ for the far wall is the full pipe outer diameter (beam must cross both walls and the internal bore).

$$
t = D_o = 200 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate minimum SFD using:**

$$
D_{min} = \frac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}} + t
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
D_{min} = \frac{2.0 \times 200}{0.6} + 200
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
D_{min} = \frac{400}{0.6} + 200 = 666.67 + 200 = 866.67 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute offset angle:**

$$
\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{S}{D}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{866.67}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.004615)
$$

**Step 6 — Final angle:**

$$
\theta \approx 0.264° \approx 16 \text{ arc-minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A very small angle is sufficient at this large SFD. In practice, the offset is achieved by simply shifting the source **a few millimeters** laterally, not by precise angle measurement.

---

### Question B: **[KTU University Exam – Model Alternative]**

**(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the **Panoramic Exposure** technique. Discuss why compensating filters are needed and how the geometric unsharpness varies along the film. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of **Real-Time Radiography (RTR)**. Compare RTR with conventional film radiography in terms of sensitivity, speed, and inspection of dynamic processes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Panoramic Exposure Technique:**

In **panoramic exposure**, a small radioactive source (typically Ir-192 or a micro-focus X-ray tube) is positioned **at the center** of a hollow cylindrical component such as a pipe, nozzle, or pressure vessel. A **flexible film cassette** (or a series of curved cassettes) is wrapped around the **outer surface** of the component, covering the full 360° circumference.

**Diagram** (described): Cross-section showing a point source at the center of a circular pipe. Radial lines extend outward in all directions. The film lies on the inner wall of an outer concentric casing. A weld at any angular position is projected radially outward onto the film.

**Why compensating filters are needed:**

The Source-to-Film Distance (SFD) varies along the circumference:

$$
D(\alpha) = R_{pipe} \cdot (1 + \cos \alpha)
$$

- At $\alpha = 0°$ (directly opposite source): $D = 2R_{pipe}$ (maximum SFD, lowest intensity).
- At $\alpha = 180°$ (behind source): $D = 0$ (the source touches the film, intensity infinite — this region is **not exposed**).

By the **inverse square law**, the intensity at $\alpha = 0°$ is **four times lower** than at $\alpha = 90°$. Without compensation, the film density would be highly non-uniform — overexposed near the source, underexposed far from it.

**Compensating filters** are stepped lead or copper wedges placed between the source and the film. They are **thickest where the SFD is largest** (to attenuate the over-bright regions) and **thinnest where the SFD is small** (to let more radiation through). This produces a **uniform optical density** across the developed film.

**Geometric unsharpness variation:**

$$
U_g(\alpha) = \frac{d \cdot t}{D(\alpha) - t} = \frac{d \cdot t}{R(1 + \cos\alpha) - t}
$$

- At $\alpha = 0°$: $U_g$ is **minimum** (best sharpness).
- As $\alpha \to 90°$: $U_g$ increases (acceptable range).
- As $\alpha \to 180°$: $U_g \to \infty$ (region unusable).

**Practical range:** Inspection is reliable over $\alpha \in [30°, 150°]$ where $U_g \le 0.5$ mm.

---

**(b) Real-Time Radiography (RTR) — Block Diagram and Comparison:**

**Block Diagram (described):**

X-ray source → test object → Flat Panel Detector (FPD) → Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) → Image Processor → Monitor display & Digital archive (DICONDE).

**Working Principle:**
1. The X-ray beam passes through the test object.
2. The attenuated beam strikes a **digital detector** — typically an **amorphous silicon Flat Panel Detector (FPD)** coated with a scintillator (e.g., CsI or Gd2O2S).
3. The scintillator converts X-rays to **visible light**.
4. The a-Si photodiodes convert the light into **electrical charge**.
5. The charge is read out row-by-row by thin-film transistors (TFT) and digitized by the ADC.
6. The image processor applies **flat-field correction, noise reduction, and gain calibration**.
7. The **live image** is displayed on a monitor within 1–30 seconds.
8. The image is **stored digitally** in DICONDE (Digital Imaging and Communication in NDE) format, compliant with ASTM E 2597.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Conventional Film RT | Real-Time Radiography (RTR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensitivity** | High (with fine-grain film) | Comparable, can be higher with image processing |
| **Speed** | Slow (10–30 min development) | **Fast (1–30 seconds)** |
| **Dynamic inspection** | Not possible | **Possible (video rate up to 30 fps)** |
| **Re-exposure cost** | Re-shoot required | **Digital re-look possible** |
| **Environmental** | Chemical waste (developer/fixer) | **No chemical waste — green** |
| **Storage** | Physical film (degrades) | **Digital archive, permanent** |
| **Capital cost** | Low | **High (detector + software)** |
| **Standards** | ASME V, ASTM E 142 | ASME V Appendix III, ASTM E 2597 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing DWDI with DWSI:** Many students write "DWDI" when the question asks for "DWSI" — both penetrate two walls, but DWDI produces **two images**, DWSI produces **one image**. (Lose 2 marks easily.)
> 2. **Forgetting compensating filters in Panoramic:** If a panoramic question is asked and you do not mention **intensity variation and the need for compensating filters**, you will lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong formula for $D_{min}$:** Some students write $D_{min} = d/U_g$ instead of $D_{min} = d \cdot t / U_g + t$. Always include the "+ t" term.
> 4. **Neglecting units in calculations:** Always write "mm" or "cm" — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **RTR vs Digital Radiography (DR):** DR is **still a static digital image** (one frame). **RTR is dynamic / live video.** This distinction is frequently tested.
> 6. **DWDI offset angle range:** If you write "the offset is 45°", you are wrong. Standard offset is **15°–30°** for proper image separation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SWSI = 1 wall, 1 image** — best sensitivity, easiest setup, used for thick plates and large vessels.
- ✅ **DWSI = 2 walls, 1 image** — used for medium pipes without internal access; far wall is enlarged and blurred.
- ✅ **DWDI = 2 walls, 2 images** — used for small-bore pipe welds; source is **offset by 15°–30°**.
- ✅ **Panoramic = source inside, film outside** — gives 360° image; **intensity variation requires compensating filters**; SFD varies as $D(\alpha) = R(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
- ✅ **RTR = digital, real-time** — uses **Flat Panel Detector (FPD) or Image Intensifier**; ASTM E 2597 standard; output is **DICONDE format**.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $U_g = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{D - t}$ — applicable to **all** radiographic techniques.
- ✅ **Min. SFD formula:** $D_{min} = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{U_{g,\max}} + t$ — used in **code-compliant procedure qualification**.
- ✅ **Magnification formula:** $M = \dfrac{D}{D - t}$ — image is always larger than object for divergent beam.
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law:** Doubling SFD reduces intensity to **¼** — drives exposure time and source activity selection.
- ✅ **ASTM E 1000** sets $U_{g,\max} = 0.020$ **in (0.51 mm)** for ASME Section V.
- ✅ **DWDI typical offset:** 15° to 30° from pipe centerline.
- ✅ **RTR advantages:** Real-time display, no film, dynamic inspection, digital archiving, no chemicals.
- ✅ **RTR disadvantage:** High capital cost; sensitivity dependent on detector pixel pitch and scintillator.
- ✅ **Panoramic limitation:** Cannot inspect region directly behind the source (α ≈ 180°); requires multiple exposures for full 360° coverage in very large pipes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
