<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Films in Industrial Radiography: Types, Speed, and Quality

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Industrial Radiographic Film?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**  
> An **industrial radiographic film** is a specially manufactured photographic recording medium consisting of a flexible transparent **base** coated with a light-sensitive **silver halide emulsion**, designed to permanently record the latent image produced by penetrating ionising radiation (X-rays or gamma rays) after chemical development.

Unlike ordinary photographic film, industrial X-ray film is designed to:
* Respond to **ionising radiation** as well as visible light.
* Provide extremely **fine grain** for resolving small defects.
* Offer high **contrast** between thin and thick regions of the test piece.
* Be processed under controlled, repeatable conditions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Bucket" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a radiographic film as a special "rain gauge" used in a thunderstorm.**  
> Each silver halide grain is like a tiny bucket. When radiation (the "rain") strikes the emulsion, each bucket collects some energy. Where radiation passes through a thin section of the workpiece, many buckets fill up (producing dark regions). Where radiation is absorbed by a thick section or a defect, few buckets fill up (producing light regions). After **development**, the size of each bucket is fixed — and we "read" the rainfall pattern to find hidden cracks, porosity, or inclusions.

---

### 1.3 Film Construction — The Layered Sandwich

A modern industrial X-ray film is a multi-layer composite:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Functional Layers of Industrial X-ray Film (Top to Bottom):**
>
> 1. **Protective Overcoat** — A thin, tough gelatin layer (1–2 µm) that shields the emulsion from abrasion and fingerprints.
> 2. **Emulsion Layer** — The active heart of the film. Contains microscopic **silver bromide (AgBr)** crystals (often with a small percentage of AgI) suspended in gelatin. Typical thickness: 10–25 µm.
> 3. **Subbing Layer** — An adhesion-promoting layer that bonds the emulsion firmly to the base.
> 4. **Base** — A polyester (PET) or cellulose triacetate sheet, typically **0.18 mm thick**, providing dimensional stability and mechanical strength.
> 5. **Anti-halation Backing** — A dye layer on the back that absorbs any light reflected back from the base, preventing unsharp halo images.

---

### 1.4 How Does a Film "Capture" an Image?

When X-rays or gamma photons pass through the test piece and strike the emulsion:
1. Photons interact with **silver halide crystals** via the **photoelectric effect** and **Compton scattering**.
2. These interactions liberate **photoelectrons** within the crystal lattice.
3. The photoelectrons migrate to **sensitivity specks** (defects in the crystal lattice, usually silver sulfide).
4. At the specks, neutral **silver atoms (Ag⁰)** form a **latent image**.
5. During **chemical development**, the latent image catalyzes the reduction of the entire crystal to metallic silver, producing visible blackening.
6. The film is then **fixed**, washed, and dried, yielding a permanent radiograph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Density (D)** is defined as the negative logarithm of the ratio of transmitted light ($I$) to incident light ($I_0$) on the developed film:  
> $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$

---

### 1.5 The Two Major Film Families

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A. Non-Screen (Direct-Exposure) Films** — Used WITHOUT intensifying screens. The film responds directly to ionising radiation. Provides the finest detail but requires longer exposure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **B. Screen-Type Films** — Used WITH metallic intensifying screens (typically lead foil). The screen emits secondary electrons upon irradiation, amplifying the exposure effect on the film. Faster exposure, but slightly less sharpness.

| Feature | Non-Screen Film | Screen-Type Film |
|---|---|---|
| Intensifying Screen | Not used | Lead foil screens used |
| Relative Exposure | Very long | Short (5–10× faster) |
| Image Sharpness | Excellent | Good (slightly reduced) |
| Typical Use | Thin sections, critical components | Thick castings, welds, routine work |
| Common ASTM Class | Class I & II | Class III & IV |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ASTM Classification of Industrial X-ray Films (CRITICAL FOR EXAMS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM E1815) classifies industrial X-ray films into **four classes** based on speed (sensitivity) and image quality. **This classification is the most asked classification in KTU exams.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ASTM Film Classification Table:**
>
> | Class | Trade Name Examples | Relative Speed | Grain Size | Contrast | Typical Application |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Class 1 | Kodak AA, Fuji IX50 | Very Slow | Very Fine | Very High | Extremely critical components, thin alloys, low-energy radiography |
> | Class 2 | Kodak T, Fuji IX80 | Slow | Fine | High | Castings, weld inspection, general high-quality radiography |
> | Class 3 | Kodak MX, Fuji IX100 | Medium | Medium | Medium | General purpose work, thicker sections |
> | Class 4 | Kodak CX, Fuji IX150 | Fast | Coarse | Low | Very thick sections, high-energy radiography where exposure time must be minimised |

**Memory Trick:** "**1 is choosy, 4 is greedy**" — Class 1 needs lots of exposure but gives excellent detail; Class 4 needs little exposure but sacrifices detail.

---

### 2.2 Film Speed (Sensitivity) — Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Film speed is the **reciprocal of the exposure (in roentgens or mR) required to produce a given optical density** (commonly D = 2.0 above base + fog).

Mathematically:

$$S = \frac{k}{E}$$

where $S$ is the speed, $E$ is the exposure required to achieve density 2.0, and $k$ is a constant.

**Properties:**
* **High speed** = less exposure required = larger silver halide crystals.
* **Low speed** = more exposure required = smaller, finer crystals.
* Speed is determined primarily by:
  1. **Grain size** of silver halide crystals.
  2. **Emulsion thickness**.
  3. **Concentration of silver halide** per unit area.
  4. **Use of intensifying screens** (amplification factor).

---

### 2.3 The Characteristic (Hurter & Driffield) Curve — The Heart of Film Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **H&D Curve** (or **Characteristic Curve**) is the fundamental diagnostic tool that relates the **logarithm of exposure** to the resulting **film density**. It reveals speed, contrast, and latitude all in one graph.

```
Density (D)
   |              ___________  <- Shoulder (max density)
   |            /
   |          /  <- Slope = Gamma (Contrast)
   |        /
   |      /
   |    /   <- Toe (under-exposure region)
   |___/___________________________
      Log Exposure (log E)
      
   Fog |  Toe  | Linear | Shoulder
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Critical Parameters Read from the H&D Curve:**
>
> 1. **Base + Fog Density** — Density of unexposed, fully processed film.
> 2. **Speed Point** — The exposure required to produce a density of 1.0 above base + fog.
> 3. **Gamma ($\gamma$)** — The slope of the straight-line portion. Represents contrast.
> 4. **Latitude** — The range of log exposure over which the film responds usefully (approximately from the toe to the shoulder).
> 5. **Maximum Density ($D_{max}$)** — Density at the shoulder, beyond which the film saturates.

---

### 2.4 Film Contrast, Latitude, and Their Inverse Relationship

$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta (\log E)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Inverse Relationship (Frequently Asked):**  
> A film with **high contrast** (high $\gamma$, steep slope) has a **narrow latitude** (short exposure range). A film with **low contrast** (shallow slope) has a **wide latitude** (long exposure range).

This is why Class 1 films are great for thin-section work with limited thickness variation (narrow latitude is acceptable), while Class 4 films are preferred for variable-thickness castings.

---

### 2.5 Film Quality — The Triangle of Attributes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Film "quality" is NOT a single quantity.** It is composed of four interrelated attributes, often pulling in opposite directions:
>
> 1. **Speed (Sensitivity)** — How fast the film responds.
> 2. **Contrast ($\gamma$)** — How sharply the film distinguishes thickness differences.
> 3. **Granularity / Graininess** — The visual "noisiness" of the developed image (RMS granularity, measured on a microdensitometer).
> 4. **Sharpness / Resolution** — The ability to record fine geometric detail (measured in line-pairs per mm, lp/mm).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet for Radiographic Films

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Bank (Memorize for KTU ESE):**

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Optical Density | $D = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$ | Dimensionless |
| Film Speed | $S = k / E$ | $E$ in R or mR |
| Gamma (Contrast) | $\gamma = \Delta D / \Delta(\log E)$ | Slope of linear region |
| Exposure Reciprocity | $I_1 \cdot t_1 = I_2 \cdot t_2$ | For same density |
| Intensification Factor | $IF = E_{\text{no screen}} / E_{\text{with screen}}$ | Always > 1 |
| Radiographic Contrast | $\Delta D = \gamma \cdot \log(I_1 / I_2)$ | Function of subject & film |
| Exposure for Thickness Change | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | Lambert–Beer law |
| Granularity (RMS) | $\sigma_D = \sqrt{(1/N) \sum (D_i - \bar{D})^2}$ | Measured over 48 µm aperture |

---

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace weld inspection** demands Class 1 or 2 films — defects as small as 0.5% of the wall thickness must be detected.
* **Pipeline girth weld inspection** (oil & gas) routinely uses Class 3 films with lead screens for productivity.
* **Nuclear reactor pressure vessel** inspection requires the highest-quality, finest-grain films due to safety-critical nature.
* **Foundry inspection** of large castings uses Class 4 films + fluorescent screens to keep exposure times practical.

---

### 2.8 Storage, Handling & Processing Notes

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU frequently tests processing-related marks. Remember:
>
> * Films must be stored at **10–24 °C** and **30–60% RH**.
> * Exposed films should be processed **as soon as possible** (latent image decay begins within hours).
> * Manual development: **5 minutes at 20 °C** in standard developer is typical.
> * Fixer must clear unexposed silver halide completely (~5–10 min) to prevent browning over time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Subject Contrast, Film Contrast, and Radiographic Contrast

The total radiographic contrast (the visual difference in density between two regions on the developed film) is the product of two independent factors:
1. **Subject Contrast** (depends on the test piece and the radiation used).
2. **Film Contrast** (depends only on the film's characteristic curve).

**Step 1: Subject contrast** arises from differential attenuation:

$$I_1 = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x_1}, \quad I_2 = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x_2}$$

**Step 2: Ratio of intensities** arriving at the film from two regions of thickness $x_1$ and $x_2$:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_2} = \frac{I_0 \, e^{-\mu x_1}}{I_0 \, e^{-\mu x_2}} = e^{-\mu (x_1 - x_2)}$$

**Step 3: Take the logarithm** to obtain the exposure ratio in log form:

$$\log\left(\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right) = -\mu (x_1 - x_2) \cdot \log_{10} e = -0.4343 \, \mu \Delta x$$

**Step 4: Apply the definition of film gamma**. The change in density on the film is gamma times the log exposure difference:

$$\Delta D = \gamma \cdot \left[\log(I_1) - \log(I_2)\right] = \gamma \cdot \log\left(\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Combine** the two results:

$$\Delta D = -0.4343 \, \gamma \, \mu \, \Delta x$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation (Frequently Tested):**  
> To maximise visible contrast on the radiograph:
> 1. **Increase $\mu$** — use lower-energy radiation (softer X-rays), BUT only if the part is thin enough to be penetrated.
> 2. **Increase $\gamma$** — use a higher-contrast film (Class 1 or 2).
> 3. **Increase $\Delta x$** — this is the defect dimension itself; we cannot control it.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Selecting the Correct Exposure

**Problem (KTU-style):**  
A 25 mm thick aluminium casting is to be radiographed using an Iridium-192 source (effective energy $\approx$ 0.4 MeV). For aluminium, the linear attenuation coefficient at this energy is $\mu \approx 0.28$ cm$^{-1}$. Class 2 film has $\gamma = 4.0$. The density range for acceptable radiography is 1.5 to 3.0.

**Find:** The minimum detectable thickness change ($\Delta x_{min}$) if the film can register a minimum density difference of $\Delta D_{min} = 0.10$.

**Step 1: Write the contrast equation:**

$$\Delta D = 0.4343 \, \gamma \, \mu \, \Delta x$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for $\Delta x$:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{\Delta D}{0.4343 \, \gamma \, \mu}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{0.10}{0.4343 \times 4.0 \times 0.28}$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{0.10}{0.4343 \times 1.12} = \frac{0.10}{0.4864}$$

**Step 5: Final result:**

$$\Delta x \approx 0.2056 \text{ cm} \approx 2.06 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Using Class 2 film at 0.4 MeV, the radiographer can theoretically detect a thickness change of about **2 mm** in a 25 mm aluminium casting. To improve detectability (smaller $\Delta x$), the examiner should switch to **lower-energy radiation** (e.g., X-ray tube at 100–150 kV) or use a **higher-gamma film** (Class 1).

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Speed vs Quality Trade-Off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision Matrix (Likely 14-Mark Question Topic):**

| Parameter | Class 1 Film | Class 2 Film | Class 3 Film | Class 4 Film |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Relative Speed (1/fastest) | 0.10 | 0.25 | 1.00 | 4.00 |
| Graininess (RMS × 1000) | 8 | 13 | 22 | 35 |
| Resolution (lp/mm) | 100+ | 80–100 | 50–80 | 30–50 |
| Typical Gamma | 4.5–5.5 | 3.5–4.5 | 2.5–3.5 | 1.5–2.5 |
| Latitude | Very narrow | Narrow | Medium | Wide |
| Section Thickness (steel at 200 kV) | Up to 12 mm | 12–30 mm | 30–60 mm | 60+ mm |
| Cost per Sheet | Highest | High | Medium | Low |

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Exposure Reciprocity (Bunsen–Roscoe Law)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a given film and processing, the same density is produced if the product of intensity and time is constant:

$$I_1 \cdot t_1 = I_2 \cdot t_2$$

**Proof (qualitative):**  
Each silver halide crystal requires a minimum cumulative energy to form a developable latent image. If the rate (intensity) halves, doubling the time delivers the same total energy. **Validity Range:** This holds between roughly $10^{-3}$ to $10^{3}$ seconds. Outside this range, **reciprocity law failure** occurs — long exposures become less efficient because some latent image centres fade before development begins.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Intensification Factor (IF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The intensification factor quantifies the exposure reduction achieved by adding lead intensifying screens:

$$IF = \frac{E_{\text{direct exposure}}}{E_{\text{with screen}}}$$

Typical values:
* **Lead foil screen (front and back):** IF = 3 to 5
* **Fluorescent screen with rare-earth phosphors:** IF = 30 to 60 (but lower sharpness)

This is why production radiography is dominated by screen-type films — exposure time reductions of an order of magnitude directly improve economics.

---

### 3.6 Mathematical Definition of Granularity

Granularity is the spatial noise of the developed image:

$$\sigma_D(x,y) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ D(x_i,y_i) - \bar{D} \right]^2}$$

where $\sigma_D$ is the standard deviation of density measured through a scanning aperture of standard diameter (typically 48 µm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Rule:** Class 1 films have $\sigma_D \approx 0.008$, while Class 4 films have $\sigma_D \approx 0.035$. This 4× increase in noise is what limits the smallest defect that can be reliably detected on Class 4 films.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Radiographic Film Workflow

The diagram below captures the entire film lifecycle, from raw stock to finished radiograph, including all interactions with radiation, screens, and chemistry.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. List the four ASTM classes of industrial X-ray films and give one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (Memorise This Layout):**

The ASTM E1815 standard classifies industrial X-ray films into four classes:

1. **Class 1** – Very slow speed, finest grain, highest contrast. *Application:* inspection of thin superalloy turbine blades in aerospace.

2. **Class 2** – Slow speed, fine grain, high contrast. *Application:* general-purpose weld and casting inspection.

3. **Class 3** – Medium speed, medium grain, medium contrast. *Application:* medium-thickness castings and weldments.

4. **Class 4** – Fast speed, coarse grain, low contrast. *Application:* very thick castings where short exposure times are essential.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. Define (a) film speed and (b) film contrast. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

* **(a) Film Speed:** The reciprocal of the exposure (in roentgens) required to produce a specified optical density (usually D = 2.0 above base + fog). High-speed films require less exposure.

* **(b) Film Contrast ($\gamma$):** The slope of the straight-line portion of the characteristic H&D curve, defined as the change in density per unit change in log exposure: $\gamma = \Delta D / \Delta (\log E)$.

* **Relationship:** Speed and contrast are inversely related in most film systems — a film with very fine grain (high contrast) is generally slow, while a fast film with large grains has lower contrast. However, this is a *general trade-off*, not a strict mathematical inverse.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the characteristic (H&D) curve of an industrial X-ray film. Explain how the curve is used to determine (i) film speed, (ii) film contrast, and (iii) film latitude. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the ASTM classification of industrial X-ray films. Discuss in detail the speed–quality trade-off between Class 1 and Class 4 films, with at least two examples of engineering applications for each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

**Introduction:** The characteristic curve, introduced by Hurter and Driffield (1890), plots the **optical density (D)** of the processed film against the **logarithm of exposure (log E)**. It is the most important diagnostic graph in radiographic film technology.

**Sketch (reproduce during exam):**

```
   D |
     |             ___________ Dmax (Shoulder)
     |           /
   2 |----------/------- <- Density 2.0 used for Speed measurement
     |        / 
     |      /  <- Straight-line portion: Slope = gamma
     |    /
     |   /
     |  /  <- Toe (curved under-exposure region)
   Fg+________________________________ Log E
     |        |
     |  Lat   | Speed 
     |  itude | Point
```

**Determination of Parameters:**

**(i) Film Speed:** Locate the exposure value (log E) at which the curve crosses density 1.0 above base + fog. The reciprocal of this exposure is the film speed. A film whose speed-point log E is lower is **faster**.

**(ii) Film Contrast ($\gamma$):** Draw a straight line tangent to the linear portion of the curve between densities ~0.5 and ~2.5. The slope of this line is $\gamma = \Delta D / \Delta (\log E)$. Higher $\gamma$ = higher contrast.

**(iii) Film Latitude:** Measure the log-exposure range between the upper toe (where $\gamma$ falls to ~0.4 of max) and the lower shoulder (similar criterion). Latitude is the inverse of contrast — a steep curve has narrow latitude.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **latitude** with **speed**. Remember — latitude is the *horizontal width of useful exposure range*, not the speed itself.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**ASTM Classification (ASTM E1815):** Films are grouped into four classes based on **speed** (sensitivity to radiation) and **image quality** (grain, contrast, resolution).

**Speed–Quality Trade-Off Table:**

| Property | Class 1 (e.g., Kodak AA) | Class 4 (e.g., Kodak CX) |
|---|---|---|
| Relative Speed | Very slow (~10% of Class 4) | Very fast (10× faster than Class 1) |
| Grain | Extremely fine | Coarse |
| Contrast | Very high ($\gamma$ > 4) | Low ($\gamma$ < 2) |
| Resolution | Excellent (>100 lp/mm) | Moderate (~40 lp/mm) |
| Latitude | Very narrow | Wide |

**Engineering Applications of Class 1:**
1. **Aerospace turbine blade inspection** — defects as small as 0.2 mm must be reliably detected.
2. **Electronic component inspection** — fine gold-plated leads and solder joints.
3. **Nuclear fuel pellet inspection.**

**Engineering Applications of Class 4:**
1. **Heavy-section steel casting inspection** (e.g., hydro-turbine runners) — exposure time is the limiting factor.
2. **Pipeline field radiography** with Ir-192 where productivity matters.
3. **Ship hull plate welds** in shipyards.

**Conclusion:** The engineer must balance inspection time, defect detection requirements, and cost when choosing between these extremes. Most production work uses Class 2 or 3 as a compromise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the construction of an industrial radiographic film in detail with a labelled diagram. Explain the role of each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define (i) granularity, (ii) resolution, (iii) intensification factor. Explain how intensifying screens reduce exposure time and state two disadvantages of using them. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**Introduction:** Industrial X-ray film is a multi-layered composite engineered for high resolution and stable performance under varied storage and processing conditions.

**Construction (Top to Bottom):**

1. **Protective Overcoat (1–2 µm):** A thin gelatin layer that protects the soft emulsion from mechanical damage, fingerprints, and scratches during handling.

2. **Emulsion Layer (10–25 µm):** The light- and radiation-sensitive layer containing silver bromide (sometimes with 1–5% silver iodide for finer grain) crystals dispersed in gelatin. This is where the latent image forms.

3. **Subbing Layer:** An adhesion layer, typically a thin gelatin-resin blend, ensuring the emulsion firmly sticks to the base and does not peel during processing.

4. **Base (~0.18 mm polyester):** Provides dimensional stability, mechanical strength, and flexibility. Modern films use polyethylene terephthalate (PET), which is tear-resistant and dimensionally stable.

5. **Anti-halation Backing:** A dye or pigment layer on the back that absorbs stray light or radiation reflected from the base, preventing a "halo" of unsharpness around dense images.

**Diagram:** (See Section 4.2 Block Diagram above — draw a clear horizontal layered sketch in the answer script.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**(i) Granularity:** The statistical fluctuation in optical density observed when a uniformly exposed film is viewed at high magnification. Quantified as **RMS granularity** — the standard deviation of density measured through a 48 µm scanning aperture. **Lower granularity = smoother image = better detectability of small low-contrast defects.**

**(ii) Resolution:** The ability of the film to record fine geometric detail separately. Measured in **line-pairs per millimetre (lp/mm)** using a resolution test chart. Class 1 films: >100 lp/mm; Class 4 films: ~40 lp/mm.

**(iii) Intensification Factor (IF):** The ratio of direct exposure (without screens) to screen-aided exposure required to produce the same density. $IF = E_{\text{direct}} / E_{\text{with screen}}$. Typical lead-screen IF = 3–5; fluorescent-screen IF = 30–60.

**How Screens Reduce Exposure Time:** When X-rays or gamma rays strike a lead foil screen, they eject **secondary electrons** by photoelectric and Compton interactions. These low-energy secondary electrons are absorbed in the film's emulsion much more efficiently than the primary photons. The screen thus acts as a **"radiation-to-electron converter"** that boosts the effective exposure on the film.

**Two Disadvantages of Intensifying Screens:**
1. **Loss of sharpness** — secondary electrons scatter laterally in the screen and film before being absorbed, causing a slight penumbra (unsharpness), particularly with fluorescent screens.
2. **Moiré patterns** — when two screens of slightly different thickness are used (front and back), interference patterns can appear on fine periodic structures.
3. (Optional third) **Reduced contrast** — the additional electrons may slightly flatten the H&D curve.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Triggers:**
> 1. Confusing **film speed** with **latitude** (very common error).
> 2. Writing "film speed = exposure" instead of **reciprocal of exposure**.
> 3. Forgetting that the **Class number increases as speed increases** (i.e., Class 4 is the fastest, not Class 1).
> 4. Omitting the units in the formula $\gamma = \Delta D / \Delta (\log E)$ — mention "dimensionless".
> 5. Not labeling the H&D curve axes correctly during sketches — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unlabeled axes.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Films in Industrial Radiography:**

- ☐ Industrial X-ray film = **base + emulsion + protective layers**, not a single sheet.
- ☐ The **emulsion** contains **silver bromide (AgBr)** crystals suspended in gelatin.
- ☐ ASTM classifies films into **four classes** — speed rises from Class 1 → Class 4, image quality declines.
- ☐ **Class 1** = slowest, finest grain, highest contrast, best for thin critical components.
- ☐ **Class 4** = fastest, coarsest grain, lowest contrast, used for very thick sections.
- ☐ **Film speed** = **reciprocal of exposure** required to reach a given density (commonly D = 2.0).
- ☐ **Film contrast ($\gamma$)** = slope of linear region of H&D curve = $\Delta D / \Delta (\log E)$.
- ☐ **Latitude** is **inversely related** to contrast.
- ☐ The **H&D curve** has **toe, linear (working) region, and shoulder**.
- ☐ **Subject contrast × film contrast = radiographic contrast**.
- ☐ **Radiographic contrast equation:** $\Delta D = 0.4343 \, \gamma \, \mu \, \Delta x$.
- ☐ **Intensifying screens** reduce exposure time by emitting **secondary electrons** that boost film response.
- ☐ **Lead foil screens** give IF = 3–5; **fluorescent rare-earth screens** give IF = 30–60.
- ☐ **Granularity** measures film noise (RMS density fluctuation); **resolution** measures ability to record fine detail (lp/mm).
- ☐ **Speed–quality trade-off** is fundamental: improving one degrades the other.
- ☐ Standard development: **5 minutes at 20 °C**; standard fixing: **5–10 minutes** in sodium thiosulfate.
- ☐ Films must be stored at **10–24 °C** and **30–60% RH**; process **immediately** after exposure.
- ☐ **Screen-type films** require screens; **non-screen films** do not and offer maximum sharpness.
- ☐ The **base** is typically **0.18 mm polyester (PET)**; the **anti-halation backing** prevents unsharp halos.
- ☐ Always mention **units and dimensionless nature** of $\gamma$ in derivations to earn full marks.
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark answers, **always include a labelled diagram** — examiners reward visual clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screens Used in Radiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Screens Used in Radiography # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Radiographic Screens** are auxiliary thin sheets placed in direct contact with the X-ray or gamma-ray film to **convert the invisible, high-energy ionizing radiation into visible light photons** (fluorescent screens) or **secondary electrons** (metal screens) that expose the film emulsion. Their primary engineering function is to **reduce exposure time, improve image contrast, and enhance the overall radiographic sensitivity** of the inspection process. According to the **ASTM E94** and **ASME Section V, Article 2** standards (the governing codes for industrial radiography), intensifying screens are classified as an *integral part of the film-screen system*, and their selection directly influences the **Image Quality Indicator (IQI) sensitivity** achievable during inspection. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The "Bioluminescence Diving Analogy"** 🐠 > > Imagine you are trying to photograph deep-sea fish in pitch-black ocean water using only a flashlight. The film in your camera is slow to react to the dim flashlight reflection. Now suppose you coat the fish with a *bioluminescent paint* — the moment the flashlight hits them, they glow brightly in the dark, and your camera captures them instantly and clearly. > > - The **flashlight** = X-ray or gamma radiation (hard to "see" directly on film) > - The **bioluminescent paint** = **Intensifying Screen** (converts invisible rays to visible light) > - The **fast camera capture** = Drastically reduced exposure time > - The **bright glow** = Enhanced image density and contrast This is exactly the role of screens in industrial radiography — they make the "invisible" radiographic image rapidly and efficiently recordable on film. ## 1.3 Why Are Screens Necessary? (The Engineering Justification) Direct exposure of industrial X-ray film to radiation requires **extremely long exposure times**, which is impractical for production environments. Screens provide three critical advantages: - **Speed Amplification Factor (SAF)**: Exposure times can be reduced by a factor of **10× to 60×** depending on the screen type. - **Improved Image Uniformity**: Screens provide a more uniform exposure across the film area. - **Reduced Geometric Unsharpness**: Faster exposure allows shorter source-to-film distances with acceptable exposure. ## 1.4 Classification Overview (The Big Picture) Radiographic screens are broadly classified into **three families**: | Family | Operating Principle | Typical Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Metal Screens** (Lead Foils) | Secondary electron emission | Gamma radiography, High-kV X-ray (≥ 200 kV) | | **Fluorescent Screens** (Salt Screens) | Photon conversion via phosphors | Low to medium kV X-ray (≤ 200 kV) | | **Fluorometallic Screens** | Combined electron + fluorescence | Wide range of energies, modern preferred choice | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip**: A frequent question framing is *"Differentiate between metal and fluorescent screens"* or *"Which screen is used with Ir-192 / Co-60?"*. Always memorize the **energy threshold of 200 kV** as the dividing line between typical fluorescent and metal screen usage. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Metal Screens (Lead Intensifying Screens) ### 2.1.1 Construction & Operating Principle Metal screens are thin, high-purity **lead foil sheets** placed on **both sides** of the film in a light-tight cassette. They operate on the principle of **secondary electron emission and characteristic X-ray fluorescence**. **Front Screen (Tube Side):** - Thickness: typically **0.005 to 0.0127 inch (0.13 mm to 0.32 mm)** - Always thinner to avoid excessive image unsharpness from electron scattering. **Back Screen (Film Side):** - Thickness: typically **0.010 to 0.025 inch (0.25 mm to 0.64 mm)** - Thicker because its sole purpose is to expose the back emulsion of the **double-coated film**. ### 2.1.2 The Physics of Metal Screen Action — Step-by-Step 1. **Primary radiation** (gamma or high-kV X-ray) strikes the front lead screen. 2. The lead atoms absorb the radiation and emit **secondary electrons** (photoelectric + Compton interactions). 3. These low-energy electrons have **short, straight paths** in the film emulsion — this is the key advantage: they produce **less scatter** than the original high-energy photons. 4. The **back screen** reflects any radiation that passed through the film back into the rear emulsion. 5. Net result: **Higher photographic density with sharper edges** (improved radiographic contrast). ### 2.1.3 Advantages of Metal Screens - ✅ Sharper images (less geometric scatter blur) — best image quality. - ✅ Reduced backscatter fog (when combined with a lead-backing plate ≥ 0.25 inch). - ✅ Suitable for **high-energy radiation** (Co-60, Ir-192, > 400 kV X-ray). - ✅ Minimal screen-contact artifacts because they are physically robust. - ✅ Impervious to humidity, dirt, and aging (very long shelf life). ### 2.1.4 Disadvantages of Metal Screens - ❌ **Lower intensification factor** (typically 2× to 4× speed gain). - ❌ Lead screens become *brittle and crack* with rough handling. - ❌ Cannot be folded — must be stored flat. - ❌ Dirty screens cause white spots on the radiograph. --- ## 2.2 Fluorescent (Salt) Screens ### 2.2.1 Construction & Operating Principle Fluorescent screens consist of a **cardboard or plastic base** coated with a layer of fine **phosphor crystals** (typically **calcium tungstate, CaWO₄**) suspended in a binder. When struck by X-rays, the phosphor emits **visible blue light** that exposes the film. ### 2.2.2 The Physics of Fluorescent Screen Action 1. X-ray photon strikes the **phosphor crystal**. 2. The crystal lattice absorbs the energy and re-emits it as **multiple lower-energy visible-light photons** (wavelength ≈ **430 nm blue light** for CaWO₄). 3. Each X-ray photon → typically **1000 to 3000 visible photons**, causing massive amplification. 4. Visible light exposes the silver halide emulsion efficiently. ### 2.2.3 Types of Fluorescent Screens (Speed Classes) | Screen Class | Relative Speed | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Detail / Slow** | 1× (baseline) | Maximum sharpness, fine defect detection | | **Medium / Par Speed** | 4× to 8× | General-purpose industrial work | | **High Speed / Fast** | 8× to 16× | Thick sections, low-power sources | | **Ultra-Fast (Rare Earth)** | 16× to 60× | Field radiography with portable equipment | > [!NOTE] > **Rare Earth Screens** (e.g., **Gadolinium Oxysulfide — Gd₂O₂S:Tb**, **Lanthanum Oxybromide — LaOBr:Tm**) emit **green light** (550 nm) and require **orthochromatic film**. They offer dramatically higher intensification factors (up to 60×) than calcium tungstate. ### 2.2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages **Advantages:** - ✅ Very high intensification (shorter exposure → higher productivity). - ✅ Lower radiation dose to personnel (important for field work). - ✅ Ideal for **low-energy, low-output** X-ray machines. **Disadvantages:** - ❌ Screen unsharpness due to **lateral light diffusion** in the phosphor layer. - ❌ Phosphor grain size limits resolution (coarser grain = faster but blurrier). - ❌ Sensitive to **screen-film contact**: poor contact = blurred radiograph. - ❌ Susceptible to **static marks, dust, and scratches** that appear as artifacts. --- ## 2.3 Fluorometallic Screens (Hybrid Screens) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fluorometallic screens** are the **modern industrial standard** (per ASME V). They consist of a **lead foil backing** with a **calcium tungstate or rare-earth phosphor layer** on the front. They combine the **speed of fluorescent screens** with the **sharpness of metal screens**, and are specified for **Class I and Class II welds** under **ASME Section V**. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Formula Reference** — Memorize these for KTU ESE | # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units / Boundary Conditions | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Exposure Time Reduction Factor (Speed Gain)** | $G = \dfrac{t_{direct}}{t_{screen}}$ | Dimensionless; $t_{direct}$ = time without screen | | 2 | **Screen Intensification Factor (SIF)** | $SIF = \dfrac{\text{Density with screen at } t_1}{\text{Density without screen at } t_1}$ | Ratio ≥ 1; typical fluorescent = 4–60 | | 3 | **Total Image Unsharpness (Combined)** | $U_{total} = \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_s^2 + U_f^2}$ | mm; $U_g$ geometric, $U_s$ screen, $U_f$ film | | 4 | **Geometric Unsharpness** | $U_g = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{D - t}$ | mm; $d$ = source size, $t$ = object depth, $D$ = SFD | | 5 | **Screen Unsharpness (Fluorescent)** | $U_s \approx 0.10 \text{ to } 0.30 \text{ mm}$ | Depends on phosphor layer thickness | | 6 | **Light Conversion Efficiency (CaWO₄)** | $\eta \approx 4\%$ to $6\%$ | of X-ray energy converted to visible light | | 7 | **Lead Screen Thickness Rule** | Front: 0.005–0.0127 in; Back: 0.010–0.025 in | ASTM E94 standard | | 8 | **Energy Threshold for Screen Selection** | $< 200$ kV → Fluorescent; $\geq 200$ kV → Metal | General industrial guideline | **Worked Example Using Formula 4 (Geometric Unsharpness):** A Co-60 source of diameter **$d = 4$ mm**, SFD **$D = 600$ mm**, object-to-film distance **$t = 50$ mm**: $$U_g = \frac{d \cdot t}{D - t} = \frac{4 \times 50}{600 - 50}$$ $$U_g = \frac{200}{550} \approx 0.364 \text{ mm}$$ For acceptable sharpness, **$U_g < 0.5$ mm** is generally required — this configuration passes. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace Industry**: Fluorescent + fluorometallic screens are mandatory for **turbine blade and honeycomb structure inspections** where micro-cracks must be resolved. - **Pipeline Girth Welds (Ir-192)**: Lead metal screens dominate because Ir-192 emits high-energy gamma rays (~ 0.4 MeV). - **Nuclear Reactor Vessel Inspection**: Co-60 (1.25 MeV) requires **0.25 mm to 0.64 mm lead screens**; fluorescent screens are ineffective at such energies. - **Pressure Vessel Fabrication (ASME VIII)**: Fluorometallic screens specified in **Section V, T-274.3** for Class I and II welds. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why Metal Screens Improve Image Sharpness at High kV > [!NOTE] > This derivation explains the **theoretical foundation** of why lead metal screens are *preferable* to fluorescent screens above 200 kV — a classic KTU 14-mark question framework. ### Step 1 — State the Problem At high photon energies (Co-60, > 1 MeV), a single primary photon can **scatter multiple times** within the film emulsion, generating scattered electrons over a wide lateral area. This produces **radiographic unsharpness**. ### Step 2 — Define the Mean Free Path of a High-Energy Photon in Film The probability of interaction per unit length is governed by the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu_{film}$: $$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{film} \cdot x}$$ The **mean free path** $\lambda$ is the inverse of the attenuation coefficient: $$\lambda = \frac{1}{\mu_{film}}$$ ### Step 3 — Lead Screen Acts as a Secondary Source When the high-energy photon passes through the **front lead screen**, the interaction cross-section of lead at this energy is *much higher* than that of film emulsion (lead has high Z = 82). The photon undergoes **photoelectric absorption** and releases: $$E_{electron} = h\nu - W_{Pb}$$ where $h\nu$ is the photon energy and $W_{Pb}$ is the lead K-shell binding energy (≈ 88 keV). ### Step 4 — Energy Comparison For **Co-60 gamma ray** ($E_\gamma = 1.25$ MeV): $$E_{electron} = 1.25 \text{ MeV} - 0.088 \text{ MeV} = 1.162 \text{ MeV}$$ This electron has **very short range** in film emulsion (a few tens of microns), much shorter than the lateral spread of scattered primary photons. Hence, the **lateral scatter zone shrinks dramatically**. ### Step 5 — Formulate the Total Unsharpness Comparison **Without lead screen (high-kV direct exposure):** $$U_{direct} = \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_{scatter}^2}$$ **With lead screen:** $$U_{screen} = \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_{electron}^2}$$ Since $U_{electron} \ll U_{scatter}$, we conclude: $$U_{screen} < U_{direct} \implies \text{Sharper image}$$ ### Step 6 — Engineering Consequence This is the **fundamental reason** lead metal screens are mandated by **ASTM E94 and ASME V** for high-energy gamma radiography (Ir-192, Co-60) and high-kV X-radiography (≥ 400 kV). --- ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Correct Screen | Inspection Parameter | Recommended Screen | Justification | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **Energy < 120 kV** (thin steel, aluminum) | Fine-detail fluorescent or no screen | Best contrast, sharpest image | | **Energy 120–250 kV** | Medium fluorescent or fluorometallic | Balanced speed & sharpness | | **Energy 250 kV – 1 MeV** (Ir-192) | Fluorometallic or thin lead (0.005 in front) | Speed gain + sharpness | | **Energy > 1 MeV** (Co-60, Linac) | Lead metal screens (0.010–0.025 in) | Mandatory — fluorescent fails | | **Field radiography, low output** | Rare-earth fast screen (Gd, LaOBr) | Maximum 60× speed gain | | **Class I welds, critical aerospace** | Fluorometallic per ASME V | Best compromise for code compliance | | **Real-time imaging** | Fluoroscopic screen + image intensifier | Continuous display | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Calculation: Exposure Time Savings **Problem (KTU-style numerical):** A 25 mm thick steel plate is radiographed with a 200 kV X-ray machine. Direct exposure requires **8 minutes**. Using a medium-speed fluorescent screen, the density obtained in 1 minute with the screen equals the density obtained in 8 minutes without the screen. Find: - (a) The Screen Intensification Factor (SIF). - (b) The percentage time saving. ### Solution **Part (a):** Screen Intensification Factor: $$SIF = \frac{t_{direct}}{t_{screen}} = \frac{8 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ min}} = 8$$ **Part (b):** Percentage time saving: $$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{t_{direct} - t_{screen}}{t_{direct}} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{8 - 1}{8} \times 100 = \frac{7}{8} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ Saving} = 87.5\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examination Insight**: The question deliberately leaves the SIF definition ambiguous. Always state your assumed definition explicitly at the start of your answer — KTU examiners award 1 mark for clear problem definition. --- ## 3.4 Detailed Comparison Table: Metal vs Fluorescent Screens | Property | Metal Screen (Lead) | Fluorescent Screen (CaWO₄ / Rare Earth) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Operating Principle** | Secondary electron emission | Visible light fluorescence | | **Energy Range** | ≥ 200 kV (high) | ≤ 200 kV (low–medium) | | **Intensification Factor** | 2× to 4× | 4× to 60× | | **Image Sharpness** | Excellent (no lateral diffusion) | Limited by light scatter | | **Sensitivity to Contact** | Low | High — critical concern | | **Durability** | Robust but cracks if bent | Fragile, scratches easily | | **Cost** | Moderate (reusable for years) | Low–moderate, shorter life | | **Recommended For** | Gamma (Co-60, Ir-192), High-kV X-ray | Low-kV X-ray, portable X-ray sets | | **Cleaning Method** | Soft brush + lead-safe wipes | Anti-static solution, soft cloth | | **Common Failure Mode** | Cracking, abrasion, dirt spots | Phosphor peeling, static marks | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Film-Screen Cassette Architecture (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["PRIMARY RADIATION
X-RAY OR GAMMA SOURCE"] node2["FRONT METAL SCREEN
0.005 INCH LEAD FOIL"] node3["FILM EMULSION LAYER
DOUBLE COATED"] node4["BACK METAL SCREEN
0.010 INCH LEAD FOIL"] node5["BACKING PLATE
0.25 INCH LEAD"] node6["CASSETTE FRAME
LIGHT TIGHT"] node7["DEVELOPED RADIOGRAPH
FINAL IMAGE"] node1 -->|"1. PRIMARY BEAM"| node2 node2 -->|"2. SECONDARY ELECTRONS"| node3 node3 -->|"3. EXPOSURE TO BOTH EMULSIONS"| node3 node3 -->|"4. UNEXPOSED RADIATION"| node4 node4 -->|"5. REFLECTED ELECTRONS"| node3 node4 -->|"6. RESIDUAL RADIATION"| node5 node5 -->|"7. ABSORPTION OF BACKSCATTER"| node5 node3 -.->|"DEVELOPMENT PROCESS"| node7 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Screen Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["START
SELECT SCREEN TYPE"] nodeB{"RADIATION
ENERGY?"} nodeC["FLUORESCENT SCREEN
CALCIUM TUNGSTATE"] nodeD["FLUOROMETALLIC SCREEN
HYBRID"] nodeE["METAL SCREEN
LEAD FOIL"] nodeF{"OUTPUT POWER
AVAILABLE?"} nodeG["RARE EARTH SCREEN
FAST SPEED"] nodeH["MEDIUM FLUORESCENT
BALANCED"] nodeI{"CLASS OF
WELD?"} nodeJ["FLUOROMETALLIC
ASME V"] nodeK["METAL SCREEN
STANDARD"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB -->|"LESS THAN 120 KV"| nodeC nodeB -->|"120 TO 250 KV"| nodeD nodeB -->|"GREATER THAN 250 KV"| nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeF -->|"LOW OUTPUT"| nodeG nodeF -->|"ADEQUATE OUTPUT"| nodeH nodeD --> nodeI nodeI -->|"CLASS I OR II"| nodeJ nodeI -->|"CLASS III"| nodeK ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Energy-to-Light Conversion in Fluorescent Screens ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP["X-RAY PHOTON
HIGH ENERGY"] nodeQ["PHOSPHOR CRYSTAL
CAWO4 OR GD2O2S"] nodeR["VISIBLE PHOTON 1
430 NM BLUE"] nodeS["VISIBLE PHOTON 2
430 NM BLUE"] nodeT["VISIBLE PHOTON N
UP TO 3000 PHOTONS"] nodeU["FILM EMULSION
EXPOSED"] nodeP -->|"ABSORPTION"| nodeQ nodeQ -->|"EMISSION 1"| nodeR nodeQ -->|"EMISSION 2"| nodeS nodeQ -->|"EMISSION N"| nodeT nodeR --> nodeU nodeS --> nodeU nodeT --> nodeU ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Block-Level Screen Care Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeS1["NEW SCREEN
RECEIVED"] nodeS2["INSPECT FOR
DEFECTS"] nodeS3["CLEAN WITH
ANTISTATIC SOLUTION"] nodeS4["STORE FLAT IN
DRY CABINET"] nodeS5["LOAD FILM IN
DARKROOM"] nodeS6["ENSURE TIGHT
FILM SCREEN CONTACT"] nodeS7["AFTER EXPOSURE
INSPECT SCREEN"] nodeS8["CLEAN OR
REPLACE SCREEN"] nodeS9["RETURN TO
STORAGE"] nodeS1 --> nodeS2 nodeS2 -->|"DEFECTS FOUND"| nodeS8 nodeS2 -->|"OK"| nodeS3 nodeS3 --> nodeS4 nodeS4 --> nodeS5 nodeS5 --> nodeS6 nodeS6 --> nodeS7 nodeS7 -->|"DIRTY"| nodeS3 nodeS7 -->|"DAMAGED"| nodeS8 nodeS7 -->|"OK"| nodeS9 nodeS8 --> nodeS9 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What are intensifying screens in radiography? Why are they used?** **Model Answer:** Intensifying screens are thin sheets of **lead foil (metal)** or **phosphor-coated (fluorescent)** material placed in close contact with the radiographic film. They are used to: 1. **Reduce exposure time** by 2× to 60×, increasing productivity. 2. **Improve radiographic contrast** by producing secondary electrons (metal) or visible light (fluorescent). 3. **Reduce scatter radiation effect**, especially at high kV. 4. **Lower radiation dose** to operators, which is critical in field radiography. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Key**: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any two reasons correctly explained. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between front and back lead screens in a radiographic cassette.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Front Screen (Tube Side) | Back Screen (Film Side) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Thickness** | Thin (0.005–0.0127 in / 0.13–0.32 mm) | Thick (0.010–0.025 in / 0.25–0.64 mm) | | **Purpose** | Initial interaction with primary beam | Backscatter protection + exposure of rear emulsion | | **Reason for Thinness** | Avoids image unsharpness from electron scatter | No unsharpness concern; maximum intensification | The front screen is thin because a thicker screen would scatter secondary electrons laterally, blurring the image. The back screen can be thicker because its only role is to expose the rear emulsion layer of the double-coated film. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a) Explain in detail the construction, working principle, and applications of metal intensifying screens in industrial radiography. (7 marks)** **(b) Compare and contrast metal screens with fluorescent screens. Under what conditions is each type preferred? (7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Metal Intensifying Screens — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):** **Construction (2 marks):** Metal screens are made of **pure lead foils (99.9% Pb)**, manufactured in standard thicknesses per **ASTM E94**: - **Front screen**: 0.005 to 0.0127 inch (0.13 to 0.32 mm) - **Back screen**: 0.010 to 0.025 inch (0.25 to 0.64 mm) - Optional **backing plate**: 0.25 inch (6 mm) lead to absorb backscatter. The screens are mounted inside a **light-tight cassette** with the film sandwiched between them. **Working Principle (3 marks):** 1. Primary X-ray or gamma photon strikes the **front lead screen**. 2. Due to lead's high atomic number (Z = 82), the photon undergoes **photoelectric absorption**. 3. The absorbed energy releases a **secondary electron** of energy $E_e = h\nu - W_{Pb}$. 4. This electron has a **short, straight path** in the film emulsion, exposing the silver halide grains locally. 5. The **back screen** reflects residual radiation back into the rear emulsion of the double-coated film. 6. Total density is achieved with **far less exposure time** than direct exposure. **Applications (2 marks):** - High-energy gamma radiography (Ir-192, Co-60). - High-kV X-radiography (≥ 400 kV). - Critical weld inspections per ASME Section V. - Nuclear and aerospace industries. --- **(b) Comparison Table — Metal vs Fluorescent Screens (7 marks):** | Property | Metal Screen | Fluorescent Screen | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Principle | Electron emission | Light fluorescence | | Energy Range | High (≥ 200 kV) | Low to Medium (≤ 200 kV) | | Intensification | Low (2–4×) | High (4–60×) | | Sharpness | Excellent | Moderate (light scatter) | | Contact Sensitivity | Low | High | | Cost | Moderate (long life) | Low (replace frequently) | | Best For | Co-60, Ir-192, ≥ 400 kV X-ray | ≤ 200 kV X-ray, field work | **Preferred Conditions (3 marks):** - **Prefer metal screens when**: High-energy radiation, maximum sharpness required, long screen life needed, robust field conditions. - **Prefer fluorescent screens when**: Low-energy radiation, exposure time must be minimized, low-output X-ray equipment, dose reduction for operator safety is critical. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing thickness units**: Students often state 0.5 mm or 5 mm instead of the correct 0.13–0.32 mm. Wrong by 10×! > 2. **Forgetting the backing plate role**: The 0.25 inch backing plate is what *eliminates backscatter fog*, not the back screen. Examiners deduct 1 mark here. > 3. **Energy threshold confusion**: Saying "fluorescent screens are used with Co-60" is a critical error costing up to 2 marks. > 4. **Missing rare-earth category**: Not mentioning rare-earth screens (Gd, LaOBr) loses 1 mark in the comparison. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Internal Choice]` **Q: (a) Describe the working of fluorescent intensifying screens. Explain why calcium tungstate is widely used as a phosphor material. (7 marks)** **(b) What are fluorometallic screens? Discuss their advantages over pure metal or pure fluorescent screens, with reference to ASME Section V requirements. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Fluorescent Screens — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):** **Working Principle (3 marks):** 1. X-ray photon strikes the **phosphor layer** (CaWO₄ crystals bonded on a cardboard/plastic base). 2. The crystal lattice absorbs the photon energy and re-emits it as **multiple visible-light photons** (wavelength ≈ 430 nm, blue light). 3. One X-ray photon produces approximately **1000–3000 visible photons** — this is the source of massive intensification. 4. The blue light exposes the silver halide emulsion of the film. **Why CaWO₄ is Preferred (4 marks):** 1. **High X-ray absorption coefficient** due to tungsten's high atomic number (Z = 74). 2. **Emission wavelength (≈ 430 nm)** is in the **maximum sensitivity range** of standard blue-sensitive industrial film. 3. **Chemical stability** — does not degrade with exposure to humidity or radiation. 4. **High conversion efficiency** (≈ 4–6%) — competitive with rare-earth phosphors. 5. **Low cost** and **abundant availability** compared to rare-earth materials like gadolinium. 6. **Physical robustness** — phosphor crystals adhere well to the base. --- **(b) Fluorometallic Screens — Explanation (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** A **fluorometallic screen** is a hybrid construction consisting of a **fluorescent phosphor layer (CaWO₄ or rare-earth)** laminated onto a **lead foil backing**. It combines the speed gain of fluorescent screens with the sharpness and backscatter protection of metal screens. **Construction (2 marks):** - Outer layer: Phosphor coating (≈ 50–100 µm thick). - Inner layer: Lead foil (≈ 0.005–0.010 inch). - Backing plate: 0.25 inch lead (as per ASME V). **Advantages (3 marks):** | Advantage | Explanation | | :--- | :--- | | Higher speed than metal alone | Phosphor layer amplifies exposure (4–8×) | | Better sharpness than fluorescent alone | Lead backing prevents light scatter back to film | | Backscatter protection | Built-in lead backing reduces fog | | Wider energy range usable | Suitable from 120 kV to 1 MeV | | **ASME V compliance** | Specifically referenced for Class I & II welds in Article T-274 | **ASME Section V Reference:** Per **ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2, T-274.3**, fluorometallic screens are explicitly recommended for **Class I and Class II welds** where both high productivity (fast exposure) and code-mandated sharpness are required. The screens must conform to **ASTM E94** for dimensional and purity standards. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing CaWO₄ emission wavelength**: It emits **blue (~ 430 nm)**, not green. Green emission is rare-earth (Gd, LaOBr). > 2. **Forgetting intensifying mechanism**: "CaWO₄ absorbs X-rays" is incomplete — must say "absorbs and re-emits as VISIBLE LIGHT". > 3. **ASME mis-citation**: Writing "ASME Section XI" instead of "Section V" is wrong (XI is in-service inspection, V is examination methods). > 4. **Missing the conversion efficiency**: Typical CaWO₄ converts only 4–6% of absorbed X-ray energy to visible light — this number impresses examiners. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before ESE** ### Core Concepts (Must-Know) - ☐ Definition of **Intensifying Screen** as per ASTM E94. - ☐ Three types: **Metal (Lead), Fluorescent (CaWO₄/Rare-Earth), Fluorometallic (Hybrid)**. - ☐ Lead screen thicknesses: **Front = 0.005–0.0127 in; Back = 0.010–0.025 in**. - ☐ **Energy threshold rule**: < 200 kV → Fluorescent; ≥ 200 kV → Metal. - ☐ CaWO₄ emits **blue light (≈ 430 nm)**; Rare-earth emits **green light (≈ 550 nm)**. - ☐ SIF for CaWO₄ = **4–8×**; Rare-earth = **16–60×**; Lead = **2–4×**. - ☐ Fluorometallic = ASME V preferred for Class I & II welds. ### Critical Numerical Values - ☐ $\lambda = 1 / \mu_{film}$ — mean free path formula. - ☐ $U_g = d \cdot t / (D - t)$ — geometric unsharpness. - ☐ $U_{total} = \sqrt{U_g^2 + U_s^2 + U_f^2}$ — combined unsharpness. - ☐ $E_e = h\nu - W_{Pb}$ — secondary electron energy. ### Engineering Code References - ☐ **ASTM E94** — Standard guide for radiographic examination using industrial film. - ☐ **ASME Section V, Article 2** — Examination methods for welds. - ☐ **ASME Section V, T-274.3** — Fluorometallic screen specification. ### Common Examiner Traps - ☐ Confusing screen thickness units (inches vs mm). - ☐ Confusing CaWO₄ emission color (blue, not green). - ☐ Stating "fluorescent screens for Co-60" — INCORRECT. - ☐ Forgetting to mention **backing plate** for backscatter protection. - ☐ Missing **screen-film contact** as a critical sharpness factor for fluorescent screens. ### Real-World Applications to Mention in Answers - ☐ **Aerospace turbine blades** → Fine-detail fluorescent. - ☐ **Pipeline girth welds (Ir-192)** → Fluorometallic. - ☐ **Nuclear reactor vessels (Co-60)** → Thick lead metal screens. - ☐ **Pressure vessels (ASME VIII)** → Fluorometallic per ASME V.

topics.title: Quality of a Good Radiograph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality of a Good Radiograph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **good radiograph** is one that possesses sufficient **radiographic sensitivity** to reveal the smallest flaw of engineering significance while presenting the image with optimum **contrast**, adequate **density**, sharp **definition**, and minimum **distortion**. The quality is quantified using an **Image Quality Indicator (IQI)**, commonly the wire-type or hole-type penetrameter, in accordance with standards such as **ASTM E 747**, **ASTM E 1025**, and **IS 3658**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiographic Quality** is *not* the same as radiographic density. A dark, dense radiograph can still be of *poor* quality if it lacks contrast, has geometric blur, or fails to resolve the IQI wires.

The four pillars of radiographic quality, as prescribed by the KTU NDT syllabus, are:

1. **Radiographic Density (D)** – the degree of blackening of the processed film.
2. **Radiographic Contrast (ΔD)** – the difference in density between two adjacent regions.
3. **Radiographic Definition / Sharpness** – the distinctness of the boundaries of the image.
4. **Radiographic Sensitivity (S)** – the smallest percentage change in thickness that can be detected.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are taking a **portrait photograph** of a person standing in front of a window. If the photo is:
- *Too dark* → you cannot see the face (low **density** problem).
- *Washed out* with no black–white separation → face blends with background (low **contrast** problem).
- *Blurry around the edges* of the nose and eyes → you cannot tell where the nose ends and the cheek begins (low **definition / unsharpness** problem).
- *Cannot reveal a small pimple on the cheek* → the camera lacks the **sensitivity / resolution** to capture fine detail.

A **good industrial radiograph** must satisfy *all four* of these photographic virtues simultaneously, but with X-rays instead of visible light and with metal rather than skin. Just as a photographer controls **aperture, shutter speed, ISO, focal length, and lighting**, a radiographer controls **kV, mA, exposure time, focal spot size, source-to-film distance (SFD), screen type, and processing chemistry**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the End-Semester Examination, questions on *Quality of a Good Radiograph* typically test the **definition**, the **IQI sensitivity concept**, the **geometric unsharpness formula**, and the **influence of kV, mA, SFD, and film type** on the four quality parameters.

---

### 1.3 Engineering Standards & Constants to Remember

- **ASTM E 1025** – Wire-type IQI (penetrameter) standard.
- **ASTM E 747** – Hole-type plaque IQI standard.
- **IS 3658 / 12813** – Indian Standard equivalents for radiograph sensitivity.
- **Target film density** for general weld inspection: **2.0 to 3.0** (transmission density).
- **Acceptable geometric unsharpness** for critical work: **Ug ≤ 0.020 inch (≈ 0.5 mm)**; for general work: **Ug ≤ 0.040 inch (≈ 1.0 mm)**.

---

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Image Quality Parameters

| Parameter | What it Measures | Acceptable Range (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (D)** | Overall blackening of film | 1.5 – 4.0 |
| **Contrast (ΔD)** | Density difference per unit thickness | 2.0 – 4.0 |
| **Geometric Unsharpness (Ug)** | Edge blur due to finite focal spot | 0.2 – 1.0 mm |
| **Sensitivity (S %)** | Smallest detectable thickness step | 1 % – 2 % |
| **IQI Visible Wires** | Image quality indicator rating | 2-2T (typical) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars – Step-by-Step Logic

#### 2.1.1 Radiographic Density (D)

- **Definition:** The logarithm of the ratio of incident light intensity (I₀) to transmitted light intensity (I) through the developed film.
- **Equation:**
$$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$
- **How it is controlled:** Primarily by the **exposure (mA·s)** and the **kV**.
- **Why it matters:** If density is too low (< 1.0), the eye cannot distinguish small density differences. If too high (> 4.0), the film becomes saturated and contrast vanishes in the dense regions.

#### 2.1.2 Radiographic Contrast (ΔD)

- **Definition:** The difference in density between two adjacent areas of the radiograph, arising from a difference in thickness (Δx), composition (ΔZ), or both.
- **Equation (Subject Contrast):**
$$\Delta D = -0.434 \cdot \mu \cdot G \cdot \Delta x$$
where $\mu$ = linear absorption coefficient, $G$ = film gradient (slope of characteristic curve), $\Delta x$ = thickness change.
- **Film Contrast (G):** Slope of the Hurter & Driffield (H & D) characteristic curve at a given density. Higher G → sharper visual contrast.
- **Real-world lever:** **Lowering kV** dramatically increases subject contrast (sharper differences in absorption) but reduces penetration. **Higher kV** gives long wavelength latitude but lower contrast.

#### 2.1.3 Radiographic Definition (Sharpness)

Two types of unsharpness dominate:
1. **Geometric Unsharpness (Ug)** – caused by the finite size of the X-ray focal spot.
2. **Film-Screen Unsharpness (Ufs)** – caused by the finite thickness of the intensifying screen and film emulsion.

**Geometric Unsharpness Derivation Outline:**
- The focal spot is a *point-like* disc of finite diameter $F$.
- An edge of the object at the source-to-object distance $a$ projects a *penumbra* on the film.
- By similar triangles, the penumbra width is the geometric unsharpness.

**Screen Unsharpness:**
$$U_{fs} \approx 0.5 \cdot \text{screen thickness} \times \tan(\text{light spread angle})$$

#### 2.1.4 Radiographic Sensitivity (S)

- **Definition:** The smallest percentage change in material thickness that is detectable on the radiograph.
- **Equation:**
$$S(\%) = \frac{\Delta x_{min}}{x} \times 100$$
where $x$ = total thickness, $\Delta x_{min}$ = smallest detectable thickness step (often the wire or hole of the IQI).
- **KTU typical target:** **2 % sensitivity** for general weld inspection; **1 %** for aerospace / critical components.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Film Density | $D = \log_{10}(I_0/I)$ | $I_0$ = incident light, $I$ = transmitted light | dimensionless |
| 2 | Subject Contrast | $\Delta D = 0.434 \cdot \mu \cdot G \cdot \Delta x$ | $\mu$ = linear absorption coeff., $G$ = film gradient, $\Delta x$ = thickness difference | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Geometric Unsharpness** | $U_g = \dfrac{F \cdot b}{a - b}$ | $F$ = focal spot size, $a$ = SFD, $b$ = object-to-film distance | mm |
| 4 | Equivalent form | $U_g = \dfrac{F \cdot t}{D - t}$ | $D$ = source-to-film, $t$ = object-to-film | mm |
| 5 | Inverse Square Intensity | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{D^2}$ | $D$ = source-to-film distance | — |
| 6 | Exposure Rule | $E = \dfrac{mA \cdot s}{D^2}$ | $mA$ = tube current, $s$ = time, $D$ = SFD | mA·s/m² |
| 7 | Radiographic Sensitivity | $S(\%) = \dfrac{\Delta x_{min}}{x} \times 100$ | $\Delta x_{min}$ = minimum detectable thickness, $x$ = total thickness | % |
| 8 | Exposure Compensation for Distance | $E_1 D_1^2 = E_2 D_2^2$ | $E$ = exposure, $D$ = SFD | mA·s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In derivations, *never* confuse **source-to-object distance (a)** with **source-to-film distance (D)**. A common KTU error is to write $U_g = F \cdot b / a$ instead of $F \cdot b / (a-b)$. Watch this in the exam hall.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Turbine blade inspection demands 1 % sensitivity and Ug ≤ 0.1 mm; hence micro-focus X-ray tubes (focal spot < 0.1 mm) are mandatory.
- **Nuclear pressure vessels**: ASME Section V mandates IQI visibility of 2-2T and density 2.0–3.5.
- **Pipeline girth welds**: Double-wall / double-image techniques use small SFD to fit elliptical projections; geometric unsharpness becomes the limiting factor.
- **Castings vs. welds**: Castings use higher kV (longer wavelength latitude) because the inherent structure produces spurious contrast that must be flattened.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Geometric Unsharpness (Ug)

Consider a point source *P* of radiation emitting a divergent cone that strikes an opaque object of thickness $t$ located at distance $a$ from the source. The film is located at distance $D$ from the source, so the object-to-film distance is:
$$b = D - a$$

Now, the focal spot is not a mathematical point but has a finite diameter $F$. Hence the radiation actually emerges from any point within a disc of diameter $F$. The disc can be modeled as two extreme point sources:
- A source at the **top** of the focal spot.
- A source at the **bottom** of the focal spot, separated by distance $F$.

**Step 1 – Set up similar triangles**

The geometric penumbra of the image of the top edge of the object is determined by similar triangles formed between:
- The focal spot diameter $F$ (triangle at source).
- The penumbra width $U_g$ (triangle at the film).

The **smaller triangle** at the source has:
- Base = $F$ (full focal spot diameter)
- Height = $a$ (source-to-object distance)

The **larger triangle** at the film has:
- Base = $F + U_g$
- Height = $D$ (source-to-film distance)

By the property of similar triangles (light propagates in straight lines):

$$\frac{F}{a} = \frac{F + U_g}{D}$$

**Step 2 – Solve for Ug**

Cross-multiplying:

$$F \cdot D = a \cdot (F + U_g)$$

Expanding the right side:

$$F \cdot D = a \cdot F + a \cdot U_g$$

Rearranging to isolate the $U_g$ term:

$$a \cdot U_g = F \cdot D - a \cdot F$$

$$a \cdot U_g = F(D - a)$$

Since $b = D - a$ (the object-to-film distance), we substitute:

$$a \cdot U_g = F \cdot b$$

**Step 3 – Final form**

$$\boxed{U_g = \frac{F \cdot b}{a} = \frac{F \cdot b}{D - b} = \frac{F \cdot (D-a)}{a}}$$

> The two common equivalent forms are:
> $$U_g = \frac{F \cdot b}{D - b} \quad \text{or} \quad U_g = \frac{F \cdot t}{D - t}$$
> where $t = b$ = object-to-film distance and $D$ = source-to-film distance.

**Interpretation:**
- $U_g$ is **directly proportional** to the focal spot size $F$ → use small focal spots for fine work.
- $U_g$ is **directly proportional** to the object-to-film distance $b$ → keep the object as close to the film as possible.
- $U_g$ is **inversely proportional** to the source-to-object distance $a$ → increase the SFD to reduce unsharpness (at the cost of intensity, by inverse square law).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example – Calculation of Geometric Unsharpness

**Problem (KTU pattern):**
A weld in a steel plate of thickness 20 mm is being radiographed. The X-ray tube has a focal spot of 2.0 mm, the SFD is 1000 mm, and the object-to-film distance is 50 mm. Determine the geometric unsharpness.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $F = 2.0$ mm
- $D = 1000$ mm (SFD)
- $b = 50$ mm (object-to-film)

$$U_g = \frac{F \cdot b}{D - b} = \frac{2.0 \times 50}{1000 - 50} = \frac{100}{950}$$

$$\boxed{U_g \approx 0.105 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The geometric unsharpness is well below the ASME limit of 1.0 mm, so the radiograph quality criterion is satisfied.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example – Effect of SFD on Exposure (Inverse Square Law)

**Problem:**
An exposure of 4 minutes (240 s) at 5 mA gives the correct density at an SFD of 1000 mm. What exposure time is required at the same mA if the SFD is changed to 1500 mm?

**Solution:**

Using the exposure-distance relation $E_1 D_1^2 = E_2 D_2^2$, with $E = mA \times t$:

$$mA \cdot t_1 \cdot D_1^2 = mA \cdot t_2 \cdot D_2^2$$

The mA cancels (since the same tube current is used):

$$t_1 \cdot D_1^2 = t_2 \cdot D_2^2$$

$$240 \times 1000^2 = t_2 \times 1500^2$$

$$t_2 = \frac{240 \times 10^6}{2.25 \times 10^6} = 106.67 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_2 \approx 107 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.78 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** Increasing SFD from 1 m to 1.5 m (a 50 % increase) multiplies the required exposure time by a factor of $(1.5)^2 = 2.25$. The **radiation intensity falls off as the square of the distance**, hence exposure time must increase as $D^2$ to maintain the same film density.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example – Radiographic Sensitivity Calculation

**Problem:**
The smallest visible wire on the IQI corresponds to a thickness of 0.4 mm. The total specimen thickness is 20 mm. Calculate the radiographic sensitivity.

**Solution:**

$$S(\%) = \frac{\Delta x_{min}}{x} \times 100 = \frac{0.4}{20} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{S = 2.0\%}$$

This 2 % sensitivity satisfies the typical ASME / IS requirement for general weld inspection.

---

### 3.5 Summary Comparison Table – Effect of Variables on Quality

| Variable ↑ Increased | Density (D) | Contrast (ΔD) | Unsharpness (Ug) | Sensitivity (S) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **kV** | ↑ (more penetration → less density unless exposure reduced) | ↓ (lower contrast) | ↑ (more scattering) | ↓ (worse) |
| **mA** | ↑ (linear) | no direct effect | no direct effect | marginal |
| **Exposure time (s)** | ↑ (linear) | no direct effect | no direct effect | marginal |
| **SFD (D)** | ↓ (inverse square) | no direct effect | ↓ (Ug decreases) | ↑ (better) |
| **Object-to-film (b)** | no direct effect | no direct effect | ↑ (Ug increases) | ↓ (worse) |
| **Focal spot (F)** | no direct effect | no direct effect | ↑ (Ug increases) | ↓ (worse) |
| **Film gradient (G)** | no direct effect | ↑ (proportional) | no direct effect | ↑ (better) |
| **Lower kV** | ↓ (less penetration) | ↑ (higher contrast) | ↓ (less scatter) | ↑ (better) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram – Radiographic Image Formation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[XRayTube] --> B[PrimaryBeam]
    B --> C[ObjectWithFlaw]
    C --> D[AttenuatedBeam]
    D --> E[IntensifyingScreen]
    E --> F[FilmEmulsion]
    F --> G[LatentImage]
    G --> H[ChemicalProcessing]
    H --> I[VisibleRadiograph]
    I --> J[QualityEvaluation]
    J --> K{IQIVisible?}

The attenuated beam (modulated by flaws) reaches the intensifying screen, which converts X-rays to light, exposing the film emulsion. After chemical processing, a visible radiograph is produced. The image is then evaluated for IQI visibility and density/contrast values against the relevant acceptance standard (e.g., ASME Section V).
