# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the three cardinal principles of radiation protection. Explain the inverse square law with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three cardinal principles of radiation protection are:

1. **TIME** – Minimize the duration of exposure. Total dose $D = \dot{D} \times t$, so reducing time reduces dose.
2. **DISTANCE** – Maximize distance from the source. Dose rate follows the **Inverse Square Law**:
   $$\dot{D} \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$$
3. **SHIELDING** – Place absorbing material (lead, concrete) between the source and personnel.

**Example:** If a radiographer receives 100 µSv/hr at 1 m from a source, at 2 m he receives 25 µSv/hr, and at 4 m only 6.25 µSv/hr. Thus, simply **stepping back** dramatically reduces dose.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define ALARA. What is the annual dose limit for a radiation worker as per AERB?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ALARA** stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Achievable."** It is the fundamental philosophy of radiation protection which states that all radiation doses must be kept as low as practically possible, considering economic and social factors — not just below the regulatory limit, but reduced to the minimum achievable.

**AERB Annual Dose Limit for Occupational Workers: 20 mSv/year** (averaged over 5 years, maximum 50 mSv in any single year).

For members of the public, the limit is **1 mSv/year**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the different types of personal monitoring devices used in industrial radiography. Discuss the working principle of a **TLD badge** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ¹⁹²Ir source has an activity of 100 Ci. Calculate the dose rate at 5 m distance, given the gamma ray constant of Ir-192 is **0.5 R·cm²/(mCi·hr)**. What is the minimum safe distance to keep the dose rate below 25 µSv/hr (assume 1 R = 10 mSv)? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Personal Monitoring Devices (7 marks)**

The three main types of personal monitoring devices are:

1. **Film Badge Dosimeter**:
   - Uses photographic film inside a plastic holder with multiple filters (plastic, aluminum, copper, lead).
   - On exposure to radiation, the film gets exposed and darkens.
   - After processing, the optical density is measured using a **densitometer**.
   - Range: **0.1 mSv to 10 Sv**. Permanent record, but limited accuracy at high doses.

2. **TLD Badge (Thermoluminescent Dosimeter)**:
   - Uses crystals of **LiF (Lithium Fluoride)** or **CaF₂** doped with Mn or Mg.
   - When irradiated, electrons get trapped in lattice defects.
   - On heating (~300°C), the trapped electrons fall back, emitting light (**thermoluminescence**).
   - The emitted light is measured by a **PMT (Photomultiplier Tube)**.
   - The intensity of light is proportional to the dose received.
   - **Advantages:** Reusable (after annealing), high accuracy, wide range (µSv to Sv), can be read repeatedly.

3. **Pocket Dosimeter (Direct Reading Dosimeter, DRD)**:
   - A small pen-sized ionization chamber with a quartz fiber electrometer.
   - On exposure, the fiber deflects; this deflection is viewed through a built-in eyepiece and gives an **instant direct reading**.
   - Range: typically 0–200 mSv. **Used for daily spot checks.**

4. **Electronic Personal Dosimeter (EPD)**:
   - Modern digital device using a **semiconductor detector (Si-diode)**.
   - Gives real-time digital readout with audible alarm if preset dose/dose rate is exceeded.
   - Data-logging capability for record-keeping.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Dose Rate Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Activity $A = 100 \, Ci = 100 \times 10^3 \, mCi = 10^5 \, mCi$
- Gamma ray constant $\Gamma = 0.5 \, R \cdot cm^2 / (mCi \cdot hr)$
- Distance $d = 5 \, m = 500 \, cm$
- Required dose rate $\leq 25 \, \mu Sv/hr = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, R/hr$ (since 1 R = 10 mSv = $10^4$ µSv)

**Step 1:** Calculate dose rate at 5 m:

$$\dot{D} = \frac{\Gamma \times A}{d^2}$$

$$\dot{D} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^5}{(500)^2} = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{2.5 \times 10^5}$$

$$\dot{D} = 0.2 \, R/hr$$

**Step 2:** Convert to mSv/hr:

$$\dot{D} = 0.2 \times 10 = 2 \, mSv/hr = 2000 \, \mu Sv/hr$$

**Step 3:** Find minimum safe distance $d_{min}$:

$$\frac{0.2}{d_{min}^2 / (500)^2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, R/hr$$

$$d_{min}^2 = \frac{0.2 \times (500)^2}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.0025} = 2 \times 10^7 \, cm^2$$

$$d_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^7} = 4472 \, cm = 44.72 \, m$$

> **Final Answers:**
> - Dose rate at 5 m = **2 mSv/hr** (2000 µSv/hr)
> - Minimum safe distance = **~44.72 m**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the different **biological effects of ionizing radiation**. Differentiate between **stochastic** and **deterministic** effects with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **emergency procedure** to be followed when a radioactive source fails to retract into its container during field radiography. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Biological Effects (7 marks)**

**Categories of Biological Effects:**

**1. Somatic Effects (affect the individual exposed):**
- **Acute Effects (High dose > 1 Sv, short time):**
  - 0.5–1 Sv: Nausea, fatigue, blood count changes.
  - 1–2 Sv: Mild radiation sickness, 10% mortality.
  - 4–6 Sv: 50% mortality (LD-50), severe bone marrow damage.
  - > 10 Sv: Death within days (CNS syndrome, GI syndrome).
- **Chronic Effects (low dose, long time):**
  - Cancer (leukemia, thyroid, breast, lung, skin).
  - Cataract formation (lens opacification).
  - Sterility, genetic mutation.
  - Shortened lifespan.

**2. Genetic Effects (hereditary, affect offspring):**
- Damage to DNA in reproductive cells (gonads).
- Mutations passed to next generations.

**Stochastic vs Deterministic:**

| Feature | Stochastic Effects | Deterministic Effects |
|---|---|---|
| **Threshold** | No threshold (any dose has some probability) | Has a threshold dose |
| **Severity** | Independent of dose (cancer yes/no) | Increases with dose |
| **Probability** | Increases with dose | Above threshold, certain to occur |
| **Examples** | Cancer, leukemia, genetic mutation | Skin burns, hair loss, cataract, sterility, radiation sickness |
| **Onset** | Years to decades | Days to weeks |

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Emergency Procedure (7 marks)**

**Scenario:** A radioactive source (e.g., ¹⁹²Ir) fails to retract into its shielded camera (source stuck in exposed position).

**Emergency Procedure:**

1. **STOP** the exposure immediately. Turn off the X-ray generator OR retract the source crank manually (if possible).

2. **EVACUATE** all personnel from the area to a safe distance. Establish a **10-meter exclusion zone** (more for high-activity Co-60 sources).

3. **POST** warning signs and barriers around the area. Cordon off the site with rope/cones.

4. **NOTIFY** the **Radiation Safety Officer (RSO)** and site supervisor immediately. Inform the **AERB** (regulatory body) within 24 hours.

5. **MONITOR** the area continuously with a **survey meter** (Geiger counter or ionization chamber) to map the dose rate field.

6. **ATTEMPT** remote retrieval using **long-handled tongs** (minimum 1.5 m) to manually push the source back into the camera. **NEVER use bare hands.**

7. If unsuccessful, use the **emergency source recovery kit** (lead shield, tongs, spare container) to place the source in an **emergency shielded container**.

8. After retrieval, **survey** the entire area to confirm zero residual radiation.

9. **DECONTAMINATE** if needed, and decontaminate personnel if exposed.

10. **DOCUMENT** the incident in the safety logbook, conduct a **root cause analysis**, and implement corrective actions.

11. **INFORM AERB** in writing and conduct retraining if required.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where students lose marks):**
> 1. **Confusing Gy and Sv** — Gy is *absorbed dose*; Sv is *equivalent dose* (incorporates radiation quality factor). For X-rays and gamma rays, Q = 1, so numerically Gy = Sv, but the conceptual difference matters.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Many students lose marks in numerical problems by mixing up Bq/Ci or Sv/rem. Always state conversions clearly.
> 3. **Misapplying inverse square law** — It applies to a **point source** only. For large/collimated sources, the falloff is less steep.
> 4. **Ignoring ALARA** — Even if the calculated dose is below the limit, examiners expect mention of ALARA — the goal is **minimization**, not just compliance.
> 5. **Confusing HVL and TVL** — HVL = halves intensity (50%); TVL = reduces to 10%. **TVL = 3.32 × HVL**.
> 6. **Not sketching diagrams** — In "neat sketch" questions, a proper labelled diagram is worth 2-3 marks. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss.
> 7. **Mixing up dose limits** — Worker = 20 mSv/yr; Public = 1 mSv/yr. Trainee (16-18 yr) = 6 mSv/yr. Don't confuse these.
> 8. **Not stating units** in final numerical answers — Always write "5 m" not just "5", and "µSv/hr" not just "µSv".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Safety Aspects in Radiography**

- ✅ **ALARA** = As Low As Reasonably Achievable — the **golden rule**.
- ✅ **Three Principles** = TIME (↓), DISTANCE (↑), SHIELDING (use it).
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law**: $\dot{D} \propto 1/d^2$ — **most powerful practical tool**.
- ✅ **Exponential Attenuation**: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
- ✅ **HVL = 0.693/μ**; **TVL = 3.32 × HVL**.
- ✅ **Dose Limits**: Worker = **20 mSv/yr**; Public = **1 mSv/yr**; Trainee = **6 mSv/yr**.
- ✅ **Units**: Sievert (Sv) = dose equivalent; Gray (Gy) = absorbed dose; Becquerel (Bq) = activity.
- ✅ **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**; **1 Sv = 100 rem**.
- ✅ **Common Sources**: ⁶⁰Co (high energy, 1.17 & 1.33 MeV), ¹⁹²Ir (medium, 0.3–0.6 MeV), ⁷⁵Se (low energy), ¹⁶⁹Yb (very low energy).
- ✅ **Shielding materials**: Lead (Pb) > Steel > Concrete > Water (for neutrons).
- ✅ **Personal Monitors**: TLD (most accurate), Film Badge (permanent record), Pocket Dosimeter (instant reading), EPD (digital).
- ✅ **Stochastic effects** = cancer, genetic damage (no threshold).
- ✅ **Deterministic effects** = burns, cataracts, radiation sickness (threshold-based).
- ✅ **Controlled Area** = dose rate > 100 µSv/hr; **Supervised Area** = > 7.5 µSv/hr (or as per AERB).
- ✅ **Warning signs**: Trefoil symbol (☢) — yellow & magenta.
- ✅ **Emergency plan**: STOP → EVACUATE → CORDON → NOTIFY RSO → REMOTE RETRIEVAL → CONTAIN → SURVEY → REPORT to AERB.
- ✅ **RSO (Radiation Safety Officer)** = mandatory at every radiography site.
- ✅ **AERB** (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, India) = national regulatory body.
- ✅ **ICRP** (International Commission on Radiological Protection) = sets international dose limits.
- ✅ **IAEA** (International Atomic Energy Agency) = sets safety standards globally.
- ✅ **ALARA must always be quoted** in any safety-related answer — it carries marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle and Procedure of Digital Signal and Image Processing and Digital Image Correlation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle and Procedure of Digital Signal and Image Processing and Digital Image Correlation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Digital Signal Processing (DSP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** *Digital Signal Processing (DSP) is the mathematical manipulation of discretely sampled, quantized signals to extract, enhance, or transform information carried by analog waveforms originating from NDT transducers (ultrasonic, eddy current, acoustic emission).*

In the context of Non-Destructive Testing, DSP takes the raw electrical voltage signal from a probe (e.g., a piezoelectric UT transducer or an eddy current coil) and converts it into a sequence of numbers that can be filtered, analyzed, and displayed.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine listening to a heart monitor (the analog signal — a continuous wavy line). If you want a computer to "understand" that wave, you must first "freeze" it into a series of still photographs taken at very precise time intervals, and then measure the height of the wave in each photograph. This process of freezing and measuring is exactly what DSP does — it **samples** (freezes) and **quantizes** (measures) the signal so a digital computer can perform surgery on it (filter noise, find echoes, measure defect depth).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core NDT Constant:** The minimum sampling rate required is **twice the highest frequency** present in the signal, known as the **Nyquist Rate** ($f_s \geq 2f_{max}$).

---

### 1.2 Digital Image Processing (DIP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Digital Image Processing (DIP) is the use of computer algorithms to process a two-dimensional pixel matrix (image) for the purpose of enhancement, segmentation, feature extraction, and automated defect recognition in NDT imagery such as radiographs, thermograms, and ultrasonic C-scans.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an X-ray photograph of a weld. To the naked eye, it looks like a foggy grayscale picture. DIP is like giving the radiographer a "super-powered magnifying glass and chemistry set." It sharpens edges, removes fog (noise filtering), highlights cracks (contrast stretching), and can even automatically circle defects (segmentation). The image is nothing but a grid of numbers (0–255 brightness values) that the algorithm manipulates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 4, DIP forms the backbone of automated inspection of **radiographs (RT-DICONDE)**, **thermograms**, and **shearography/fringe patterns**.

---

### 1.3 Digital Image Correlation (DIC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Digital Image Correlation (DIC) is a non-contact, full-field optical metrology technique that tracks the displacement of a random speckle pattern on a specimen surface between a reference (undeformed) image and a deformed image to compute displacement and strain fields — used in NDT for residual stress mapping, fatigue crack opening, and deformation analysis.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you paint a wall with thousands of random black-and-white dots (a *speckle pattern*). You photograph it before applying load, then again after applying load. A computer compares the two photos dot-by-dot, figuring out exactly *where each dot moved* and *how the dots stretched apart*. This way, you can measure strain over the entire surface (not just at one point like a strain gauge). This is DIC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric in DIC:** The quality of the speckle pattern is quantified by its **mean intensity gradient** $\partial f / \partial x$. A good speckle pattern has a gradient magnitude between **5 and 20 gray levels per pixel** for optimum correlation accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-by-Block Operational Analysis

#### A. Digital Signal Processing Pipeline in NDT

The standard DSP chain applied to ultrasonic / eddy current signals involves five sequential stages:

1. **Anti-Aliasing Filtering (Low-Pass Analog Filter)**
   - *Why:* Removes frequencies above $f_s/2$ to prevent *aliasing* (where high frequencies masquerade as low frequencies after sampling).
   - *How:* A hardware Butterworth or Bessel analog filter is placed *before* the ADC.

2. **Sampling (Time Discretization)**
   - *Why:* Converts continuous time $t \to$ discrete index $n$.
   - *How:* The Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) reads the signal voltage at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$.

3. **Quantization (Amplitude Discretization)**
   - *Why:* Maps continuous voltage to a finite set of integer levels (e.g., 8-bit $\to$ 256 levels, 12-bit $\to$ 4096 levels).
   - *How:* The ADC rounds each sample to the nearest quantization step $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^{B}$, where $B$ = bit depth.

4. **Digital Filtering / Transformation**
   - *Why:* Extract defect echoes, suppress noise, identify flaw signatures.
   - *How:* Apply Finite Impulse Response (FIR) / Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) filters, or compute the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) to move into the frequency domain.

5. **Display / Decision**
   - The processed signal is plotted as A-scan, B-scan, or C-scan, and an automated threshold algorithm flags defects.

---

#### B. Digital Image Processing Pipeline in NDT

The standard DIP chain for radiographic / thermographic inspection:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Radiographic film is scanned, or digital detector array (DDA) captures directly.
2. **Preprocessing** — Geometric correction, intensity normalization, flat-field correction.
3. **Enhancement** — Contrast stretching, histogram equalization, unsharp masking, edge detection (Sobel, Canny, Laplacian).
4. **Image Restoration** — Wiener / inverse filtering to remove blur.
5. **Segmentation** — Thresholding (Otsu), region growing, watershed algorithms to isolate defect regions.
6. **Feature Extraction & Classification** — Measure defect area, perimeter, shape factor; classify using neural networks or rule-based AI.

---

#### C. Digital Image Correlation Procedure (DIC)

The DIC operational sequence:

1. **Specimen Preparation** — Apply high-contrast stochastic speckle pattern (spray paint, airbrush, toner).
2. **Reference Image Capture ($f$)** — Photograph undeformed surface at $t = 0$.
3. **Load Application** — Apply mechanical, thermal, or fatigue load.
4. **Deformed Image Capture ($g$)** — Photograph at $t = t_1$.
5. **Subset Definition** — Divide reference image into small square subsets (typically $21\times 21$ to $51\times 51$ pixels).
6. **Correlation Search** — For each subset, find the location $(u, v)$ in the deformed image that *best matches* via cross-correlation.
7. **Displacement Field Output** — Produce contour plots of $u(x,y)$ and $v(x,y)$.
8. **Strain Computation** — Numerically differentiate displacement field using finite-difference or least-squares smoothing.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | Time between two successive samples | s |
| 3 | Quantization Step Size | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{B}}$ | Resolution per bit | Volts |
| 4 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Quantization) | $\text{SNR}_q = 6.02\,B + 1.76$ | Ideal ADC SNR in dB | dB |
| 5 | Discrete Fourier Transform | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$ | Converts discrete signal to frequency domain | — |
| 6 | Inverse DFT | $x[n] = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\, e^{j 2\pi kn / N}$ | Reconstructs time-domain signal | — |
| 7 | Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) Coefficient | $C = \dfrac{\sum (f-\bar f)(g-\bar g)}{\sqrt{\sum (f-\bar f)^2 \sum (g-\bar g)^2}}$ | Match metric for DIC (-1 to +1) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Zero-Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC) | $C_{ZN} = \dfrac{\sum [f(x,y)-\bar f][g(x',y')-\bar g]}{\sqrt{\sum [f-\bar f]^2 \sum [g-\bar g]^2}}$ | Illumination-invariant DIC metric | dimensionless |
| 9 | Strain from Displacement | $\varepsilon_{xx} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}$ | Spatial gradient of DIC field | strain (unitless) |
| 10 | Image Resolution | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{\text{Field of View}}{\text{Number of Pixels}}$ | Spatial resolution per pixel | mm/pixel |
| 11 | Otsu Threshold | $\sigma_B^2(k) = \omega_0 \omega_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2 = \text{max}$ | Optimal image threshold (DIP segmentation) | — |
| 12 | Mean Intensity Gradient (Speckle Quality) | $\delta_f = \sum \left\vert \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right\vert$ | Quality indicator of speckle for DIC | gray levels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World NDT Utility:** The above formulas power ultrasonic flaw detection (5), automated weld radiographic inspection (11), residual-stress measurement in aerospace (9), and full-field strain mapping in fatigue-tested components (7, 8).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)

We begin with the continuous Fourier Transform pair and discretize it.

**Step 1:** Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT).

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

*Logic:* Decompose $x(t)$ into complex exponentials $e^{-j\omega t}$.

**Step 2:** Sample $x(t)$ at $t = nT_s$. The continuous integral becomes a discrete sum.

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$

*Logic:* Replace $\int dt \to T_s \sum$, and $\omega = 2\pi k / (N T_s)$ since we observe $N$ samples over the window $N T_s$.

**Step 3:** Inverse operation (IDFT) reconstructs $x[n]$ from frequency bins.

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k]\, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

*Logic:* Orthogonality of complex exponentials over $N$ samples: $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k(m-n)/N} = N \delta_{mn}$.

**Step 4:** In NDT ultrasonic signal processing, this transform converts an A-scan (time-domain echo train) into a frequency spectrum. Defect reflections, material attenuation, and transducer resonance each produce characteristic spectral peaks that the inspector identifies.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Normalized Cross-Correlation Coefficient (NCC) for DIC

**Step 1:** Define two image intensity functions: $f(x,y)$ for the reference subset and $g(x',y')$ for the deformed subset, where $x' = x+u$ and $y' = y+v$.

**Step 2:** The sum of squared differences (SSD) metric is:

$$C_{SSD}(u,v) = \sum \sum \bigl[ f(x,y) - g(x+u,\, y+v) \bigr]^2$$

*Logic:* Smaller SSD means better match. We seek $(u,v)$ that *minimizes* SSD.

**Step 3:** Expand the SSD algebraically:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{SSD}(u,v) &= \sum \sum f^2 + \sum \sum g^2 - 2 \sum \sum f \cdot g \\
&= \sum f^2 + \sum g^2 - 2 \sum f g
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Since $\sum f^2$ and $\sum g^2$ do not depend on $(u,v)$, **minimizing SSD is equivalent to maximizing the cross-correlation term** $\sum f g$.

**Step 4:** Define the **Cross-Correlation (CC)**:

$$C_{CC}(u,v) = \sum_{x,y} f(x,y)\, g(x+u,\, y+v)$$

**Step 5:** Normalize by intensity means to remove bias from uniform brightness changes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar f &= \frac{1}{N}\sum f(x,y), \qquad \bar g = \frac{1}{N}\sum g(x',y') \\
C_{NCC}(u,v) &= \frac{\sum [f(x,y)-\bar f]\,[g(x',y')-\bar g]}{\sqrt{\sum [f-\bar f]^2 \cdot \sum [g-\bar g]^2}}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Subtracting the means makes the metric invariant to additive illumination shifts. Dividing by the standard deviations makes it invariant to multiplicative scaling (gain changes).

**Step 6:** Range. By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$-1 \leq C_{NCC}(u,v) \leq +1$$

A value of $+1$ indicates perfect positive correlation (exact match), and $-1$ indicates perfect negative correlation (inverse match). DIC solvers typically accept the displacement $(u,v)$ at the sub-pixel peak of $C_{NCC}$ as the local displacement vector.

**Step 7:** Sub-pixel refinement. A parabolic or Gaussian fit is performed around the integer-pixel peak of $C_{NCC}(u,v)$ to achieve sub-pixel displacement accuracy (often $0.01$ pixel).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Strain from the DIC Displacement Field

**Step 1:** Once the displacement field $u(x,y)$ and $v(x,y)$ is known across the surface, the **normal strain** components are obtained by spatial differentiation:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \qquad \varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}$$

**Step 2:** The **shear strain** is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3:** In a discrete DIC grid, derivatives are approximated by **finite differences** using neighboring point displacements:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{xx}(x_i, y_j) &\approx \frac{u(x_{i+1}, y_j) - u(x_{i-1}, y_j)}{2\,\Delta x} \\
\varepsilon_{yy}(x_i, y_j) &\approx \frac{v(x_i, y_{j+1}) - v(x_i, y_{j-1})}{2\,\Delta y}
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Central-difference scheme — second-order accurate, less sensitive to noise than forward differences.

**Step 4:** Von Mises equivalent strain (useful for comparing with yield criteria in NDT fatigue analysis):

$$\varepsilon_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}\left(\varepsilon_{xx}^2 + \varepsilon_{yy}^2 + \varepsilon_{xy}^2\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering utility — In fatigue-tested aluminum coupons, DIC strain maps reveal crack-tip plastic zones invisible to ordinary strain gauges, providing quantitative validation for fracture-mechanics models.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Stage | Input | Operation | Output | Governing Equation |
|-------|-------|-----------|--------|--------------------|
| Sampling | $x(t)$ | ADC at $f_s$ | $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ |
| Quantization | $x[n]$ | Round to nearest | $x_Q[n]$ | $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^B$ |
| DFT | $x[n]$ | Frequency decomposition | $X[k]$ | DFT formula (above) |
| Filtering | $X[k]$ | Multiply by $H[k]$ | $Y[k] = H[k]X[k]$ | Convolution theorem |
| Display | $y[n]$ | D/A or plot | A/B/C-scan | — |
| DIC Match | $f,g$ subsets | Search $(u,v)$ | $u(x,y),\, v(x,y)$ | NCC formula |
| Strain Map | $u,v$ fields | Spatial derivative | $\varepsilon_{xx}, \varepsilon_{yy}$ | $\partial u/\partial x$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
Define the Nyquist sampling theorem. Why is anti-aliasing filtering required before sampling an NDT signal?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Nyquist sampling theorem states that a band-limited analog signal containing no frequency components above $f_{max}$ must be sampled at a rate **at least twice** its highest frequency, i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, to allow perfect reconstruction.
Anti-aliasing filtering is required because any frequency component above $f_s/2$ in the input signal would "fold back" into the lower-frequency band after sampling, producing false low-frequency artifacts (aliasing) that mimic genuine defect echoes. A low-pass analog filter placed before the ADC removes these out-of-band components, ensuring that the digitized signal faithfully represents the original waveform. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
What is meant by a "speckle pattern" in Digital Image Correlation, and why is its quality important?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A speckle pattern in DIC is a high-contrast, random, stochastic distribution of dots (typically black speckles on a white background) applied to the specimen surface so that every small neighborhood has a unique grayscale "fingerprint."
Its quality is critical because the DIC algorithm tracks subsets from the reference to the deformed image by matching grayscale patterns via cross-correlation. If the speckles are too large, too sparse, or too low-contrast, neighboring subsets become indistinguishable and the correlation search fails, producing noisy or ambiguous displacement fields. Quality is quantified by the **mean intensity gradient**, ideally between **5 and 20 gray levels per pixel**. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake in DSP questions:** Students forget the factor of $1/N$ in the IDFT equation or omit the anti-aliasing filter stage, losing 2 marks.
> - **Common mistake in DIC questions:** Writing the SSD formula but failing to explain *why* normalization to NCC is necessary (illumination invariance) costs 3 marks.
> - **Common mistake in DIP questions:** Confusing *image enhancement* (contrast stretching) with *image restoration* (deblurring via Wiener filter) — examiners allocate marks separately.
> - **Always** draw a labeled block diagram; a missing block diagram forfeits up to 2 marks even if the textual answer is correct.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Digital Signal Processing system as applied to ultrasonic NDT signal analysis. Discuss the role of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) equation from the continuous Fourier transform. Explain how the magnitude spectrum of the DFT can be used to identify defect types in ultrasonic testing. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of DSP system in ultrasonic NDT (7 Marks):**

The DSP system in ultrasonic NDT processes the radio-frequency (RF) echo train received from a piezoelectric transducer to extract flaw information. The functional block architecture is:

1. **Receiving Transducer (1 mark):** A piezoelectric probe receives the ultrasonic echo returning from a flaw. The output is a weak analog RF voltage signal in the MHz range.

2. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter (1 mark):** A hardware Butterworth analog filter limits the signal bandwidth to $f_{max}$. This ensures that no frequency component above $f_s/2$ enters the ADC, preventing aliasing.

3. **Sample-and-Hold and ADC (1 mark):** The sampler reads the signal voltage at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$. The quantizer then maps each sample to a digital code with $B$-bit resolution, producing a sequence $x[n]$.

4. **Digital Filter (1 mark):** A digital FIR or IIR band-pass filter removes electronic noise and isolates the frequency band of interest (typically 1–10 MHz for flaw detection). The output is a clean digital A-scan.

5. **DFT / FFT Processor (1 mark):** The processor computes the Discrete Fourier Transform of the filtered signal, producing a magnitude spectrum $|X[k]|$ versus frequency.

6. **Decision and Display (1 mark):** The A-scan and its spectrum are displayed, and an automated threshold detector flags echoes that exceed the background noise level, indicating flaws.

7. **Block diagram (1 mark):** As shown in Figure 4.1 above.

**(b) Derivation of DFT and defect identification (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
The continuous-time Fourier Transform is:

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

Sampling at $t = nT_s$ gives:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n]\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$

(The detailed steps are shown in Section 3.1 above.)

**Identification of defects (3 marks):**
The magnitude spectrum $|X[k]|$ of an ultrasonic echo train contains characteristic peaks at frequencies related to the transducer center frequency and the geometry of the reflector:
- A **flat-bottom hole** produces strong peaks at the resonance frequency of the transducer, with minimal harmonic distortion.
- A **delamination** (planar defect) reflects strongly and produces a spectrum dominated by a narrow band around the transducer center frequency.
- A **porosity cluster** scatters energy, spreading the spectrum and lowering peak amplitude.
- A **crack tip** produces diffracted waves that introduce lower-frequency components below the transducer resonance, shifting the spectrum centroid toward lower frequencies.

By comparing the spectral signature of a measured echo with these signatures, an inspector can classify the defect type non-destructively.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Describe the principle and procedure of Digital Image Correlation (DIC). Derive the Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) coefficient used for subset matching. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the steps involved in Digital Image Processing of a radiographic film. Discuss any two image enhancement techniques used in radiographic NDT in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle and procedure of DIC + NCC derivation (7 Marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** DIC is an optical, non-contact metrology technique that tracks the movement of a stochastic speckle pattern on a specimen surface between a reference and a deformed state. By matching small pixel subsets from the two images, it yields full-field displacement vectors $(u,v)$ from which strain is computed by spatial differentiation.

**Procedure (2 marks):** (i) Apply speckle, (ii) capture reference image $f$, (iii) load specimen, (iv) capture deformed image $g$, (v) divide $f$ into subsets, (vi) search each subset's best match in $g$ via NCC, (vii) refine to sub-pixel, (viii) compute displacement and strain fields.

**NCC Derivation (3 marks):** (As derived in Section 3.2 above.)

$$C_{NCC}(u,v) = \frac{\sum [f(x,y)-\bar f]\,[g(x',y')-\bar g]}{\sqrt{\sum [f-\bar f]^2 \cdot \sum [g-\bar g]^2}}$$

Subtracting the mean intensities gives illumination invariance; dividing by the standard deviations gives gain invariance. The range is $-1 \leq C_{NCC} \leq +1$.

---

**(b) Steps in DIP of a radiographic film + two enhancement techniques (7 Marks):**

**Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Acquisition:** The exposed radiographic film is scanned by a high-resolution digitizer (typically 50–100 µm pixel size), producing a digital image matrix $I[m,n]$ of grayscale intensities (0–255 for 8-bit).
2. **Preprocessing:** Flat-field correction (dividing by a blank film scan) removes non-uniform illumination; geometric correction accounts for film distortion.
3. **Enhancement:** Contrast stretching and histogram equalization make subtle defects visible.
4. **Segmentation:** Otsu thresholding or adaptive thresholding isolates defects from the background.
5. **Feature extraction:** Defect area, perimeter, circularity, and orientation are computed.
6. **Classification:** An AI or rule-based system decides accept/reject per ISO 5817.

**Two enhancement techniques (4 marks — 2 marks each):**

**(i) Histogram Equalization:** The original histogram $H(r)$ of the radiograph is remapped using the cumulative distribution function $s = T(r) = \sum_{j=0}^{r} H(j)/N$. This spreads the intensity values across the full 0–255 range, dramatically improving the visibility of low-contrast weld defects such as undercuts or sidewall fusion.

**(ii) Unsharp Masking:** A blurred copy of the image is subtracted from the original to extract high-frequency edge details:

$$I_{sharp} = I_{original} - I_{blurred}$$

This sharpened image is added back to the original with a weighting factor $k$:

$$I_{enhanced} = I_{original} + k \cdot I_{sharp}$$

This technique highlights fine cracks and porosity edges in welds without amplifying low-frequency background noise.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **DSP** converts 1-D analog NDT waveforms into digital form via **sampling** (governed by Nyquist theorem, $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$) and **quantization** (resolution $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^B$).
- **Anti-aliasing filter** is mandatory before the ADC to prevent high-frequency components from masquerading as low-frequency noise.
- **DFT** transforms time-domain A-scans into frequency spectra for defect classification; FFT is the fast algorithm to compute it.
- **DIP** treats every NDT image (radiograph, thermogram, C-scan) as a 2-D pixel matrix of grayscale intensities, processed through **acquisition → preprocessing → enhancement → segmentation → feature extraction → classification**.
- **Histogram equalization** and **unsharp masking** are the two most common DIP enhancement techniques used in radiographic NDT.
- **Otsu's method** automatically finds the optimal threshold for separating defect from background by maximizing inter-class variance $\sigma_B^2$.
- **DIC** tracks a stochastic **speckle pattern** between reference and deformed images to compute full-field displacement and strain.
- **NCC / ZNCC** is the standard correlation metric in DIC because it is invariant to uniform brightness changes (additive shifts) and gain variations (multiplicative scaling).
- **Speckle quality** is measured by the **mean intensity gradient**, ideally 5–20 gray levels/pixel.
- **Sub-pixel refinement** via parabolic/Gaussian fitting achieves displacement accuracy of ~0.01 pixel.
- **Strain** in DIC is computed by spatial differentiation of the displacement field: $\varepsilon_{xx} = \partial u/\partial x$, $\varepsilon_{yy} = \partial v/\partial y$.
- All three paradigms — DSP, DIP, DIC — share the common NDT goal: **automated, quantitative, reliable defect detection and characterization** to replace subjective human judgment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acoustic Emission Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Acoustic Emission Inspection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Acoustic Emission (AE)** is the class of phenomena whereby **transient elastic waves** are generated by the **rapid release of energy** within a material. This energy release originates from sources such as crack initiation and growth, plastic deformation, phase transformations, corrosion, friction, and delamination. AE Inspection is a passive, real-time, non-destructive technique that detects, locates, and characterizes these emissions using sensitive piezoelectric transducers coupled to the surface of the test piece. In KTU terminology, the process is often defined through its core measurable descriptors: the **AE signal**, the **AE event**, and the **AE source**. The technique is standardized under **ASTM E1106**, **ASTM E2191**, and **ASTM E569** (the latter for structural monitoring). ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Material Stethoscope" Analogy:** Imagine placing a highly sensitive stethoscope on a wall while someone taps, cracks, or breaks a pencil on the other side. You cannot see the action, but you *hear* the elastic energy traveling through the wall. Acoustic Emission testing does exactly this — but for engineering materials. When a crack grows by a microscopic amount (perhaps 1 µm), it suddenly releases stored elastic energy, sending out a tiny but detectable sound wave that races through the structure at the speed of sound. By capturing these "whispers" with surface-mounted sensors, engineers can hear cracks growing *in real time*, even inside massive pressure vessels or aircraft wings that would be impossible to inspect with X-rays or ultrasounds from the outside. ### Core Terminology (Standardized) | Term | Definition | Physical Significance | |------|------------|----------------------| | **AE Signal** | The electrical output of an AE sensor in response to an elastic wave | Raw voltage vs. time waveform | | **AE Event** | A single, discrete release of energy producing one or more hits | Represents one physical occurrence (e.g., a crack jump) | | **Hit** | The detection of an AE wave that exceeds a threshold | One sensor's response to an event | | **Source** | The physical origin of the AE (crack, corrosion pit, etc.) | The "thing that whispers" | | **Felicity Effect** | Re-emission of AE at loads *below* a previously applied load | Indicates permanent damage (Kaiser Effect violation) | ### Key Physical Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis) The following four standard metrics are tested repeatedly in KTU examinations and must be memorized in **bold**: - **Threshold (dB):** Typically **20–100 dB** referenced to **1 µV at the sensor**; used to filter out electrical and mechanical noise. - **Amplitude:** Peak voltage of the largest excursion in a hit, measured in **dB** or **volts**. - **Rise Time (µs):** Time from first threshold crossing to the peak of the signal. - **Duration (µs):** Time from first to last threshold crossing of a single hit. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — The AE Inspection Chain The AE process is a closed-loop physical and electronic chain. For KTU 2024 Scheme questions, students must understand the **why** behind every stage: 1. **Source Activation (Mechanical Step):** A sudden micro-mechanical event (crack growth, fiber breakage in a composite) releases strain energy stored in the material lattice. 2. **Wave Propagation (Physical Step):** The released energy radiates as elastic (stress) waves through the bulk of the material and along surfaces. In a plate, two wave modes dominate: **longitudinal (P-wave)** at speeds of ~**5,000–6,000 m/s** in steel, and **shear (S-wave)** at ~**3,000–3,200 m/s**. 3. **Sensing (Transduction Step):** A **piezoelectric sensor** (usually PZT — lead zirconate titanate, or PVDF polymer) mechanically coupled to the surface converts the arriving mechanical wave into an electrical signal. 4. **Conditioning (Electronic Step):** The signal is amplified (typically **20–60 dB pre-amplifier gain**), filtered (often **20 kHz – 1 MHz** bandpass to suppress mechanical and electrical noise), and thresholded. 5. **Analysis (Computational Step):** Specialized AE processors extract parameters (amplitude, counts, energy, rise time, duration) and may perform **source location** by triangulation. ### 2.2 Two Foundational Phenomena — Kaiser & Felicity > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kaiser Effect:** A material, when reloaded to a level **below** its previous maximum stress, produces **little or no AE**. The "memory" of the highest previously applied stress is retained. This is *useful* for proof testing and stress history determination. > > **Felicity Effect:** When significant damage has accumulated, AE begins at loads **below** the previous maximum — a direct indication of structural degradation. The **Felicity Ratio** = (load at which AE re-appears) ÷ (previous maximum load). A ratio **< 1.0** indicates damage; values **< 0.95** are warning signs in fiber-reinforced polymers (FRP). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | Application / Note | |---|-------------------|-----------------|---------------------| | 1 | $c = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | $c$ = wave speed (m/s), $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) | Longitudinal wave speed in a thin rod | | 2 | $c_{L,\text{bulk}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E(1-\nu)}{\rho(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}}$ | $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | Longitudinal wave speed in infinite medium | | 3 | $c_{S} = \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2\rho(1+\nu)}}$ | $G$ = shear modulus (Pa) | Shear wave speed | | 4 | $A_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | $A_{\text{dB}}$ = amplitude in decibels, $V_{\text{ref}} = 1\ \mu\text{V}$ | Standard AE amplitude expression | | 5 | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ | $d$ = distance from sensor to source (m), $\Delta t$ = one-way travel time (s) | Single-sensor time-of-flight distance | | 6 | $x_{s} = \dfrac{c \cdot (t_{B} - t_{A})}{2} + \dfrac{x_{B} - x_{A}}{2}$ | $x_{s}$ = source location (m), $t_{A},t_{B}$ = arrival times at sensors A and B, $x_{A},x_{B}$ = sensor coordinates | **2D linear source location** (two sensors) | | 7 | $(x - x_{i})^{2} + (y - y_{i})^{2} = c^{2}(t_{i} - t_{0})^{2}$ | $(x_{i},y_{i})$ = i-th sensor position, $t_{i}$ = arrival time, $t_{0}$ = source time | **Triangulation for point source** (≥ 3 sensors) | | 8 | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{x}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_{0}}{A_{x}}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = attenuation coefficient (Np/m), $A_{0},A_{x}$ = amplitudes at 0 and $x$ | Material attenuation (Beer–Lambert type) | | 9 | $\text{Felicity Ratio} = \dfrac{P_{\text{AE onset}}}{P_{\text{previous max}}}$ | $P$ = applied load or stress | Damage assessment | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Aid for KTU:** In formula (6), the factor **2** appears because in **Pulse-Echo** mode the wave travels to the source and *back*, but in **AE (Through-Transmission Listening)** mode the wave travels only *one way* between source and sensor — so the formula is divided by 2. This is a classic KTU trick question. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pressure vessels & storage tanks:** Continuous in-service integrity monitoring on ammonia spheres, LPG bullets. - **Aerospace:** Monitoring of fatigue cracks in aircraft fuselage and composite wing skins during ground tests. - **Civil infrastructure:** Bridge cable monitoring, dam concrete crack detection, pipeline leak detection. - **Manufacturing:** Tool wear monitoring, weld monitoring, and detection of grinding burn. - **Geophysics (analogous principle):** Microseismic monitoring of mines and reservoirs uses the exact same physics. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: 2D Linear Source Location from Two Sensors **Problem Setup:** Two AE sensors A and B are mounted on a long, straight structural member (e.g., a pipe or pressure vessel wall). Sensor A is at position $x_{A} = 0$ and sensor B is at $x_{B} = L$. A crack at unknown position $x_{s}$ emits an AE wave that propagates at speed $c$. **Step 1 — Express arrival times at each sensor:** $$ t_{A} = t_{0} + \dfrac{x_{s} - 0}{c} = t_{0} + \dfrac{x_{s}}{c} $$ **Step 2:** For sensor B (which lies to the right of A by distance $L$): $$ t_{B} = t_{0} + \dfrac{\vert x_{B} - x_{s}\vert}{c} $$ **Step 3 — Form the time difference $\Delta t = t_{B} - t_{A}$:** $$ \Delta t = \dfrac{\vert L - x_{s}\vert - x_{s}}{c} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for the case where source is between A and B ($0 \le x_{s} \le L$):** In this region $\vert L - x_{s}\vert = L - x_{s}$, so: $$ \Delta t = \dfrac{L - 2x_{s}}{c} $$ **Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the source location:** $$ x_{s} = \dfrac{L - c\,\Delta t}{2} $$ This is the **standard KTU-board form** (equivalent to the formula in the cheat sheet, just rearranged). For source outside the sensor pair, take the absolute value of the difference. ### 3.2 Derivation: 3-Sensor Triangulation (Point Source) **Setup:** Three sensors A, B, C at known coordinates $(x_{A},y_{A})$, $(x_{B},y_{B})$, $(x_{C},y_{C})$ detect a single event at unknown $(x,y)$ and unknown emission time $t_{0}$. The wave speed $c$ is known. **Step 1 — Write the range equation for each sensor:** $$ r_{i} = c\,(t_{i} - t_{0}) \qquad \text{for } i = A, B, C $$ **Step 2 — Square the geometric identity $r_{i}^{2} = (x - x_{i})^{2} + (y - y_{i})^{2}$:** $$ (x - x_{i})^{2} + (y - y_{i})^{2} = c^{2}(t_{i} - t_{0})^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Subtract the equation for sensor A from those of B and C to eliminate the quadratic terms $x^{2}$ and $y^{2}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} 2x(x_{A} - x_{B}) + 2y(y_{A} - y_{B}) &= c^{2}(t_{A} - t_{B})(t_{A} + t_{B} - 2t_{0}) + x_{A}^{2} - x_{B}^{2} + y_{A}^{2} - y_{B}^{2} \\ 2x(x_{A} - x_{C}) + 2y(y_{A} - y_{C}) &= c^{2}(t_{A} - t_{C})(t_{A} + t_{C} - 2t_{0}) + x_{A}^{2} - x_{C}^{2} + y_{A}^{2} - y_{C}^{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — These two linear equations in $x$ and $y$ (with $t_{0}$ known or eliminated) can be solved by Cramer's rule, yielding the 2D source location.** A 4-sensor array adds the third spatial dimension. ### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Question (KTU Style):** In a steel pipeline ($c = 5{,}900$ m/s), two AE sensors are placed **8 m apart**. A leak-induced emission is detected **3 µs earlier** at sensor A than at sensor B. Locate the source on the line of sensors. **Solution:** Using the formula $x_{s} = \dfrac{L - c\,\Delta t}{2}$ where the sign convention: if A detects first, the source is closer to A. $$ x_{s} = \dfrac{8 - (5900)(3 \times 10^{-6})}{2} = \dfrac{8 - 0.0177}{2} = \dfrac{7.9823}{2} = 3.991\ \text{m from A} $$ **Result:** The source is **≈ 3.99 m from sensor A**, or equivalently **4.01 m from sensor B**. (Sum = 8 m ✓ — a useful KTU sanity check.) ### 3.4 Signal Parameter Relationships The energy content of a single AE hit is approximated from the rectified voltage envelope: $$ E_{\text{AE}} = \int_{t_{1}}^{t_{2}} V^{2}(t)\,dt \quad \big[\text{V}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{s}\big] $$ and the **counts** (number of threshold crossings) relate loosely to amplitude via a **log-linear empirical law** (varies by material): $$ \log_{10}(N) = a - b \cdot A_{\text{dB}} $$ where $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants fitted for the material under test. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 AE Inspection System — Block Architecture Flow The complete AE inspection chain integrates mechanical, acoustic, electronic, and computational subsystems. A KTU-board block diagram must always show signal flow direction with arrows and label the key physical quantity at each boundary. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source
Crack or Defect] --> B[Material Medium
Plate or Vessel Wall] B --> C[AE Sensor
Piezoelectric PZT] C --> D[Pre-Amplifier
20 to 60 dB Gain] D --> E[Bandpass Filter
20 kHz to 1 MHz] E --> F[Threshold Discriminator
Fixed dB Level] F --> G[AE Processor
Parameter Extraction] G --> H[Display and Alarm
Real Time Output] G --> I[Source Location
Triangulation] I --> J[Data Storage
Historical Trend] H --> K[Operator Decision
Accept or Reject] J --> K ``` ### 4.2 Sensor Coupling & Wave-Mode Partitioning ```mermaid flowchart TB SRC[AE Source Inside Wall] --> P[Longitudinal P Wave] SRC --> S[Shear S Wave] SRC --> R[Rayleigh Surface Wave] P --> SENS[Surface Mounted Sensor] S --> SENS R --> SENS SENS --> COUP[Couplant Gel or Wax] COUP --> PZT[PZT Piezoelectric Disc] PZT --> AMP[Charge to Voltage Converter] AMP --> OUT[Output Signal to Processor] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Logic for Accept/Reject (Kaiser vs. Felicity) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Begin Proof Test] --> Q1{Has Prior Load History?} Q1 -->|No| K[Kaiser Effect Valid Baseline] Q1 -->|Yes| K2[Apply Increment Loads] K2 --> Q2{AE Reappears Below Previous Max?} Q2 -->|No| K3[Structure Sound] Q2 -->|Yes| F[Calculate Felicity Ratio] F --> Q3{Ratio less than 0.95?} Q3 -->|Yes| FAIL[Reject or Repair] Q3 -->|No| WARN[Warning Schedule Reinspection] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Drawing Tip:** Always include arrows on signal-flow lines and label the *physical quantity* at each interface (mechanical wave → electrical voltage → digital counts). This is the most common reason students lose marks on block-diagram questions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Define Acoustic Emission. List any four sources of acoustic emission in engineering materials.** **Model Answer:** Acoustic Emission is the phenomenon of generation of transient elastic waves due to the rapid release of energy within a material. It is detected and recorded for non-destructive evaluation. Four sources of AE: 1. Crack initiation and growth (ductile or brittle). 2. Plastic deformation such as dislocation movement and slip in metals. 3. Phase transformations (e.g., martensitic transformation in steel). 4. Corrosion (e.g., pitting, stress-corrosion cracking) and fiber breakage in composites. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for sources — ½ mark each.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Explain the Kaiser Effect. Mention its significance in proof testing of pressure vessels.** **Model Answer:** The Kaiser Effect is the phenomenon in which a material, upon reloading, produces little or no acoustic emission until the previously applied maximum stress level is exceeded. The material effectively "remembers" its highest prior stress. **Significance in proof testing:** - A pressure vessel that has been hydrostatically proof-tested to a known pressure can be re-pressurized in service. - If AE re-appears *below* the proof-test pressure, it indicates new damage (Felicity Effect). - This forms the basis of the **Felicity Ratio** acceptance criterion (typically reject if ratio < 0.95). *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for proof-test significance.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to 2024 Scheme]* **(a) Describe with a neat block diagram the working principle of an Acoustic Emission testing system. Explain each block in detail. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block diagram:** (Refer Section 4.1 of these notes — the same flow chart serves as the answer figure.) **Detailed explanation of each block:** 1. **AE Source (1 mark):** A defect such as a growing crack releases stored strain energy suddenly. The released energy is of the order of **10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁶ J per event**. 2. **Material Medium (0.5 mark):** The elastic wave propagates through the structure. Wave speed in steel ≈ **5,900 m/s** longitudinal and **3,200 m/s** shear. 3. **AE Sensor (1.5 marks):** A piezoelectric PZT disc resonating typically in the **50 kHz to 1 MHz** range converts the mechanical wave to an electrical voltage. Couplant (grease, wax) is used to ensure acoustic impedance matching. 4. **Pre-Amplifier (1 mark):** Boosts the microvolt-level sensor output by **20 to 60 dB** to overcome noise in the long cable run. 5. **Filter (0.5 mark):** Band-pass filter (e.g., **100–300 kHz**) rejects mechanical vibration noise and electrical interference. 6. **Threshold Discriminator (0.5 mark):** Only signals exceeding a set dB threshold (typically **20–40 dB above background**) are recorded as hits. 7. **AE Processor (1 mark):** Extracts parameters: amplitude, rise time, duration, counts, energy, and performs source location. 8. **Display/Alarm (1 mark):** Real-time display of AE activity and triggering of alarm for operator action. --- **(b) Two AE sensors placed 10 m apart on a steel pipe detect a continuous emission. Sensor A records the wave 2.5 µs earlier than sensor B. The longitudinal wave speed in steel is 5,900 m/s. Determine the source location and discuss the practical limitations of this technique. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Location Calculation (4 marks):** Using $x_{s} = \dfrac{L - c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$ where the earlier detection at A implies source is closer to A. $$ x_{s} = \dfrac{10 - (5900)(2.5 \times 10^{-6})}{2} = \dfrac{10 - 0.01475}{2} = \dfrac{9.98525}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{x_{s} \approx 4.993\ \text{m from sensor A}} $$ **Practical Limitations (3 marks):** 1. **Background noise rejection:** Ambient mechanical noise (machinery, rain) and electrical interference can mask real AE events. Requires careful thresholding and filtering. 2. **Material attenuation:** High-frequency components attenuate quickly in highly attenuative materials (e.g., polymers, cast iron), limiting the sensor spacing to typically **1–3 m**. 3. **Active vs. passive defect detection:** AE cannot detect *inactive* defects (cracks not growing). A defect that does not emit under the test load will be missed — a critical limitation compared to ultrasonic testing. 4. **Wave-mode complexity and velocity dispersion:** Multiple wave modes (P, S, Rayleigh) with different speeds complicate arrival-time picking and source location. 5. **Couplant dependency:** Poor sensor coupling introduces time delays and amplitude loss, causing location errors of tens of millimetres. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a) Explain the Felicity Effect. How is the Felicity Ratio used as a damage indicator in fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP) components? (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Felicity Effect Definition (3 marks):** The Felicity Effect is the phenomenon in which a previously loaded material re-emits acoustic emission at a stress level **lower** than its prior maximum stress. It is a direct violation of the Kaiser Effect and indicates the presence of permanent, cumulative damage such as matrix cracking, fibre-matrix debonding, or fibre breakage in composites. **Felicity Ratio (2 marks):** The Felicity Ratio (FR) is defined as: $$ \text{FR} = \dfrac{\text{Load at which AE re-appears on reloading}}{\text{Previous maximum applied load}} $$ **Use as a Damage Indicator in FRP (2 marks):** In FRP laminates, the following acceptance criteria (per **ASTM E2191 / E1106**) are standard: - **FR ≥ 1.0:** No significant damage (Kaiser Effect intact). - **0.95 ≤ FR < 1.0:** Minor damage — schedule follow-up inspection. - **FR < 0.95:** Significant damage — reject or repair. - **FR < 0.90 (with high AE counts):** Severe degradation — immediate removal from service. This is widely used in aerospace and wind-turbine blade certification tests. --- **(b) Compare Acoustic Emission Testing with Ultrasonic Testing. List at least four points of comparison. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | # | Parameter | Acoustic Emission (AE) | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) | |---|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------| | 1 | Nature of test | **Passive** — listens to energy released by the defect | **Active** — sends in energy and listens for reflection | | 2 | Defect state detected | Only **active / growing** defects (dynamic) | Both **active and dormant** defects | | 3 | Real-time capability | Continuous real-time monitoring of entire structure | Typically point-by-point scanning | | 4 | Couplant requirement | Permanent couplant (grease/wax) at sensor location | Wet gel or water immersion during scanning | | 5 | Coverage | Large area monitored by a few sensors | Requires scanning each point — slow for large areas | | 6 | Source location capability | Inherent (by triangulation) | Manual estimation or phased-array imaging | | 7 | Sensitivity to noise | High (mechanical noise is major interference) | Moderate | | 8 | Typical application | Pressure vessel in-service monitoring | Weld inspection, thickness gauging, flaw sizing | *(Award 1 mark per well-explained comparison, 7 marks for 7 points.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in AE Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in distance formulas:** In AE (one-way travel), students incorrectly use the pulse-echo formula $d = c \cdot t / 2$. This yields answers that are off by a factor of 2. Always re-read: *is the wave reflecting back, or just traveling from source to sensor?* > 2. **Confusing Kaiser and Felicity Effects:** Kaiser = no AE below prior load. Felicity = AE *does* occur below prior load. Mixing them up in proof-test scenarios will cost 2–3 marks instantly. > 3. **Wrong decibel reference:** AE amplitude is referenced to **1 µV at the sensor output**, NOT 1 V. Writing $A_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}(V/1\text{V})$ will lose full marks. > 4. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners strictly deduct ½ mark per missing unit. Always write **m/s, µs, dB, MPa** explicitly. > 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows or labels:** A diagram without signal flow arrows is treated as incomplete — 1 mark is reserved specifically for the labelled, arrowed diagram. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Acoustic Emission Inspection** - **Core idea:** AE is *passive* — it listens to the material's own released energy during dynamic events like crack growth. - **Equation of life:** $c = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ (longitudinal) and $c_{S} = \sqrt{G/\rho}$ (shear) — both must be memorized. - **One-way vs. two-way travel:** AE = one-way (no factor of 2 in numerator); UT pulse-echo = two-way (factor of 2 in denominator). - **Standard source location formula (2D):** $x_{s} = (L - c \cdot \Delta t)/2$. - **Kaiser Effect:** No AE below previous max load → structurally sound memory. - **Felicity Effect:** AE reappears below previous max → damage present. - **Felicity Ratio = $P_{\text{onset}} / P_{\text{previous max}}$**; threshold = **0.95** for FRP. - **AE signal parameters to memorize:** Amplitude (dB), Rise time, Duration, Counts, Energy, Frequency. - **Sensors:** Piezoelectric PZT, frequency range **20 kHz – 1 MHz**, pre-amplifier gain **20–60 dB**. - **Threshold:** Set in **dB re 1 µV** at sensor — typically 20–40 dB above background. - **Cannot detect dormant defects** — major limitation compared to UT, RT, MPT. - **Major standards:** ASTM E1106 (monitoring), ASTM E2191 (FRP), ASTM E569 (structural). - **Applications:** Pressure vessels, pipelines, aerospace, bridges, dam concrete, FRP wind blades. - **Remember the 5-block chain:** Source → Medium → Sensor → Pre-amp + Filter → Processor → Display. - **For diagrams, always include arrows and label the physical quantity at each interface.**

topics.title: Thermography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermography** is a non-contact, non-destructive testing (NDT) technique that employs the detection and quantification of **infrared radiation (IR)** emitted by a material's surface to map its temperature distribution and reveal subsurface anomalies such as delaminations, cracks, voids, inclusions, corrosion, and moisture intrusion. It is governed by the principles of **blackbody radiation**, heat conduction, and the **Stefan–Boltzmann law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Thermography is classified as a thermal/infrared-based NDT method under Module 4 (Advanced NDT Techniques). It relies on the fact that every object above absolute zero (**0 K / -273.15 °C**) emits electromagnetic radiation, the intensity and spectral distribution of which depend on its temperature and surface emissivity.

The two principal classifications recognized in KTU syllabus are:

| Classification | Stimulus Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Thermography** | Natural heat from the component under test | Electrical hot spots, insulation breakdown, furnace linings |
| **Active Thermography** | External energy input (flash lamp, halogen, ultrasonic, eddy current heating, microwave) | Composite inspection, weld inspection, coating evaluation |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Night Vision Through Heat Signatures":**
> Imagine you walk into a pitch-dark room wearing night-vision goggles. You do not "see" the objects — instead, you perceive the **infrared heat they radiate**. A person who just exercised glows brighter than the cold chair they sat on. A leaking hot water pipe inside a wall is invisible to the naked eye, but a thermal camera clearly shows a hot streak. **Thermography works exactly this way**: instead of visible light, it captures the *invisible thermal fingerprint* of a component. Where heat flow is uniform, the surface appears isothermal (uniform color). Where a defect blocks, diverts, or traps heat, the surface temperature changes — and that anomaly is our **defect signature**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-4}$
- **Speed of light** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s}$
- **Planck's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J·s}$
- **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
- **Wien's displacement constant** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$
- **Thermal diffusivity** $\alpha$ — units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ — represents how fast heat propagates through a medium.
- **Thermal effusivity** $e = \sqrt{k \rho c_p}$ — units $\text{J m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1/2}$ — governs surface temperature response.

## 1.4 Electromagnetic Spectrum Position of IR

Infrared radiation used in thermography spans wavelengths from approximately **0.7 µm to 1000 µm**, subdivided into:

| Band | Wavelength (µm) | Detector Material |
|---|---|---|
| Near Infrared (NIR) | 0.7 – 1.5 | InGaAs, Si |
| Short-Wave IR (SWIR) | 1.5 – 3 | InSb |
| Mid-Wave IR (MWIR) | 3 – 5 | InSb, HgCdTe (MCT) |
| Long-Wave IR (LWIR) | 8 – 14 | MCT, Microbolometer |
| Far Infrared (FIR) | 15 – 1000 | Not commonly used in NDT |

> [!TIP]
> Most commercial thermographic cameras for NDT operate in the **LWIR (8–14 µm)** atmospheric window because terrestrial objects near room temperature (~300 K) emit their peak radiation in this band, per Wien's displacement law.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Physics of Thermal Radiation

### 2.1.1 Blackbody Radiation Concept

A **blackbody** is an idealized object that absorbs **all** incident electromagnetic radiation and re-emits energy at the maximum theoretical rate for its temperature. Real engineering materials are **gray bodies** — they emit a fraction of blackbody radiation characterized by a property called **emissivity (ε)**.

$$\boxed{Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = radiated power (W)
- $\varepsilon$ = surface emissivity ($0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan–Boltzmann constant ($5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$)
- $A$ = radiating surface area ($\text{m}^2$)
- $T$ = absolute surface temperature (K)

### 2.1.2 Why and How Thermography Detects Defects — The Core Logic

The entire principle of thermographic NDT reduces to **three logical steps**:

1. **Heat Application / Observation** — Either the component self-heats (passive) or an external heat pulse is applied (active). A steady-state or transient thermal field is established.
2. **Heat Conduction & Disturbance** — Heat propagates through the solid via conduction. A defect (void, delamination, inclusion of different conductivity) locally alters the **thermal diffusivity path**, causing heat to accumulate above the defect and create a thermal contrast.
3. **Infrared Emission & Capture** — The IR camera measures surface radiance, which is converted to an apparent temperature map. Variations from baseline indicate defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **thermal contrast (ΔT)** between a sound area and a defective area is what the thermographer interprets. A high-contrast defect (e.g., air-filled delamination in a composite) blocks conductive heat flow, producing a **hot spot** on the surface if heated from the front, or a **cold spot** if heated from behind.

## 2.2 Governing Equations

### 2.2.1 Planck's Law (Spectral Radiance)

$$B_\lambda(T) = \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{\,hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1}$$

- $B_\lambda(T)$ = spectral radiance ($\text{W sr}^{-1} \text{m}^{-3}$)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)

### 2.2.2 Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength)

$$\lambda_{\max} T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$$

At $T = 300 \, \text{K}$ (room temperature), $\lambda_{\max} \approx 9.66 \, \mu\text{m}$ → confirms why **LWIR cameras** dominate NDT thermography.

### 2.2.3 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction

$$\boxed{q = -k \nabla T}$$

Where:
- $q$ = heat flux vector ($\text{W/m}^2$)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity ($\text{W/m·K}$)

### 2.2.4 Heat Diffusion Equation (1-D Transient)

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

Where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$).

### 2.2.5 Thermal Diffusivity Depth Penetration (Active Thermography)

For pulsed thermography, the maximum inspection depth $d_{\max}$ scales with diffusion length:

$$d_{\max} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha \, t}$$

- $t$ = observation time after heat pulse
- For steel: $\alpha \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- For aluminum: $\alpha \approx 9.7 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — memorize for ESE**

| Formula / Law | Equation | Application in NDT |
|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann Law | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$ | Total emitted power from a surface |
| Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Choosing detector band |
| Planck's Spectral Radiance | $B_\lambda = \frac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}(e^{hc/\lambda k_B T}-1)}$ | Theoretical radiance |
| Fourier Conduction | $q = -k \nabla T$ | Heat flow through material |
| 1-D Heat Diffusion | $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \partial^{2} T/\partial x^{2}$ | Transient analysis |
| Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$ | Material selection for inspection |
| Penetration Depth | $d = 2\sqrt{\alpha t}$ | Active thermography depth |
| Radiant Exitance | $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Surface heat loss |
| NETD (Noise) | $\text{NETD} = \dfrac{4 F^{2} \sqrt{\Delta f}}{\tau_D D^{*}\sqrt{A_d}}$ | Camera sensitivity benchmark |
| Lock-in Frequency | $\mu = \sqrt{\pi f / \alpha}$ | Thermal diffusion length in lock-in |
| Emissivity Ratio | $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \varepsilon_s + (1-\varepsilon_s)(1-\tau_a)\tau_a^{-1}$ | Atmospheric correction |

## 2.4 Detector Classifications

### 2.4.1 Thermal Detectors (Uncooled)

Operate by absorbing IR and measuring the resultant temperature rise. **No cryogenic cooling required.**

- **Microbolometers** — most common; amorphous silicon or VOₓ resistor array. NETD ≈ 20–50 mK.
- **Thermopiles** — series of thermocouples; slow but inexpensive.
- **Pyroelectric** — require chopped/modulated input.

### 2.4.2 Photon (Quantum) Detectors (Cooled)

Operate on direct photon-electron interactions in semiconductors. **Require cryogenic cooling** (typically 77 K liquid nitrogen or Stirling cooler).

- **InSb** — sensitive 1–5.5 µm
- **HgCdTe (MCT)** — tunable bandgap, sensitive 3–14 µm
- **InGaAs** — NIR and SWIR

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Applications

| Industry | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Composite delamination detection (CFRP wings) |
| Power Generation | Electrical switchgear hot joints, transformer bushings |
| Petrochemical | Refractory lining wear, storage tank floor corrosion |
| Civil Engineering | Building envelope heat loss, bridge delamination |
| Electronics | PCB hot-spot analysis, microelectronic failure |
| Manufacturing | Weld inspection, forging crack detection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stefan–Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

### Step 1: Start with Planck's spectral radiance

$$B_\lambda(T) = \frac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$$

### Step 2: Integrate over all wavelengths to obtain total hemispheric radiance

$$B(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} B_\lambda(T) \, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1} \, d\lambda$$

### Step 3: Substitute $x = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$, so $d\lambda = -\dfrac{hc}{k_B T x^{2}} dx$

When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$. Flipping limits removes the negative sign:

$$B(T) = \frac{2 k_B^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} dx$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the integral (standard Boltzmann integral)

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

### Step 5: Substitute back and group constants

$$B(T) = \frac{2 \pi^{4} k_B^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} \, T^{4} = \sigma T^{4}$$

where the Stefan–Boltzmann constant is:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^{4} k_B^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W m}^{-2} \text{K}^{-4}}$$

### Step 6: Multiply by surface area and emissivity for real (gray) body

$$Q = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$$

**Interpretation:** This shows the radiated power scales with the **fourth power of absolute temperature** — a small temperature rise produces a large radiance increase, which is precisely why thermography is so sensitive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength)

### Step 1: Maximize Planck's spectral radiance

To find $\lambda_{\max}$, take $\dfrac{d B_\lambda}{d \lambda} = 0$. It is easier to maximize the equivalent function:

$$f(x) = \frac{x^{5}}{e^{x} - 1}, \quad x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$$

### Step 2: Take derivative and set to zero

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{5 x^{4} (e^{x} - 1) - x^{5} e^{x}}{(e^{x} - 1)^{2}} = 0$$

Numerator simplification:

$$5 (e^{x} - 1) - x e^{x} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 5 e^{x} - 5 - x e^{x} = 0$$

$$e^{x} (5 - x) = 5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; (5 - x) = 5 e^{-x}$$

### Step 3: Transcendental equation → numerical solution

$$x_{\max} \approx 4.9651$$

### Step 4: Convert back to wavelength

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{hc}{x_{\max} k_B T} = \frac{b}{T}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} T = b = \frac{hc}{x_{\max} k_B} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}}$$

### Step 5: Numerical verification for room temperature

At $T = 300 \, \text{K}$:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{300} = 9.66 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 9.66 \, \mu\text{m}$$

This lies inside the **LWIR (8–14 µm)** atmospheric window — explaining camera selection.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diffusion Length and Penetration Depth in Active Thermography

### Step 1: Assume a Dirac-delta heat pulse $Q_0$ applied at $x = 0$ at $t = 0$

The 1-D heat diffusion equation:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

### Step 2: Solution by Green's function (semi-infinite solid)

$$T(x,t) - T_0 = \frac{Q_0}{\rho c_p \sqrt{4 \pi \alpha t}} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^{2}}{4 \alpha t}\right)$$

### Step 3: Identify the diffusion length scale

The exponential factor $\exp(-x^{2}/4\alpha t)$ shows temperature decays over the characteristic length:

$$\boxed{\mu = 2\sqrt{\alpha t}}$$

This is the **diffusion length** — the maximum depth from which thermal information can be retrieved within time $t$.

### Step 4: Penetration depth estimation

A defect at depth $d$ is detectable if $\mu \geq d$. For practical SNR ≥ 3:

$$d_{\max} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha t}$$

### Step 5: Worked numerical example

A steel plate with $\alpha = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ is pulsed for $t = 2 \, \text{s}$:

$$d_{\max} = 2\sqrt{(2.3 \times 10^{-5})(2)} = 2\sqrt{4.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 2 \times 6.78 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$d_{\max} \approx 1.36 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m} = 13.6 \, \text{mm}$$

→ A 2-second flash can detect delaminations up to **~13.6 mm deep** in steel.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Active Thermography Techniques

| Technique | Heat Source | Modulation | Best For | Typical Frequency / Energy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pulsed Thermography** | Xenon flash lamp | Single pulse (~ms) | Shallow defects, rapid scanning | 1–10 kJ per flash |
| **Lock-in Thermography** | Halogen or LED array | Sinusoidal modulated | Depth-resolved imaging | 0.01 Hz – 1 kHz |
| **Pulse Phase Thermography (PPT)** | Flash lamp + FFT processing | Phase-based | Noise rejection, mid-depth | — |
| **Ultrasonic Thermography** | Contact transducer | High-frequency sonic | Tight cracks, delaminations | 20–40 kHz (UT), MHz (phased array) |
| **Eddy Current Thermography** | Induced eddy currents | Pulsed or modulated | Surface cracks in metals | kHz range |
| **Microwave Thermography** | Microwave applicator | Continuous / pulsed | Non-conductive materials | 1–10 GHz |

---

## 3.5 Lock-in Thermography — Thermal Diffusion Length

In lock-in thermography, a periodic heat source of frequency $f$ creates thermal waves. The diffusion length is:

$$\boxed{\mu = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{\pi f}}}$$

This means **low frequency → deep penetration**, **high frequency → shallow penetration**. By sweeping frequency, a depth profile is obtained.

---

## 3.6 Emissivity Correction Equation

For a real surface viewed through atmosphere:

$$T_{\text{surface}}^{n} = \frac{T_{\text{apparent}}^{n} - (1-\varepsilon)T_{\text{reflected}}^{n}}{\varepsilon}$$

where $n \approx 4$ in the LWIR band for moderate temperature ranges.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermographic Inspection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Under Test] --> B[Heat Source Selection]
    B --> B1[Passive: Self Heat]
    B --> B2[Active: External Stimulation]
    B2 --> B2a[Flash Lamp]
    B2 --> B2b[Halogen Modulated]
    B2 --> B2c[Ultrasonic Transducer]

    B2b --> C
    B2c --> C
    B2d --> C
    B2e --> C
    C --> D[Atmospheric Transmission]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Heat Flow Distortion at a Subsurface Defect

```mermaid
        A1[External Heat Source] --> A2[Component Surface Heated]
        A2 --> A3[Heat Diffuses Internally]
```

```mermaid
    C1 --> D1[Use LWIR Camera, capture 0 to 10 second transient]
```
