# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Define thermography. Distinguish clearly between passive and active thermography with one industrial example of each.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Definition (1 mark):** Thermography is a non-destructive testing technique that detects and maps surface temperature distribution by measuring the infrared radiation emitted by a material.
- **Passive (1 mark):** The component under test is the source of heat itself — e.g., detection of overheating joints in a high-voltage electrical busbar using an LWIR camera.
- **Active (1 mark):** An external heat source (flash lamp, halogen, ultrasonic) is applied to stimulate the component — e.g., flash thermography used to detect delamination in a CFRP aircraft wing skin.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**State and explain Stefan–Boltzmann law. Why is it fundamental to thermographic NDT?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
- **Statement (1.5 marks):** The total radiant exitance $M$ of a gray body is $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, $\varepsilon$ is emissivity, and $T$ is absolute temperature.
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** Because radiance scales with the **fourth power of temperature**, even minute temperature differences (mK level) produce measurable radiance differences. This high sensitivity forms the physical basis for thermography's ability to detect subtle subsurface defects.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:
Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions (a + b = 14 marks).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**a) (7 marks)** Explain the physical principle of infrared thermography. With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a thermographic inspection system. Discuss the role of emissivity in measurement accuracy.

**b) (7 marks)** A steel component at 350 K emits radiation that is captured by a thermographic camera operating in the 8–14 µm band. Calculate: (i) the peak emission wavelength, and (ii) the total radiant exitance assuming the surface has emissivity 0.85. Given $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ and $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Principle and System:**

1. **Physical principle (2 marks):** Every object above 0 K emits IR radiation governed by Stefan–Boltzmann law. Heat flow inside the material follows Fourier's law and the diffusion equation. Defects alter local thermal diffusivity, producing surface temperature anomalies captured by the IR detector array.

2. **Block diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 — Heat Source → Component → IR Emission → Atmosphere → Optics → Detector (Microbolometer/MCT) → Signal Processing → Image Reconstruction → Display → Interpretation.

3. **Emissivity role (2 marks):** Emissivity $\varepsilon$ (0–1) determines how much of the blackbody radiance a real surface emits. Low $\varepsilon$ (e.g., polished aluminum ≈ 0.05) reflects ambient radiation and produces severe measurement errors unless corrected. Standard practice: apply high-emissivity tape or paint, or use the dual-band ratio method.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

(i) Peak wavelength using Wien's law:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{350}$$

$$\lambda_{\max} = 8.28 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 8.28 \, \mu\text{m}$$

This lies within the LWIR (8–14 µm) band — confirming proper camera selection. **(3 marks)**

(ii) Radiant exitance using Stefan–Boltzmann:

$$M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4} = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (350)^{4}$$

Calculate $350^{4}$:

$$350^{2} = 122{,}500$$
$$350^{4} = (122{,}500)^{2} = 1.5006 \times 10^{10}$$

$$M = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.5006 \times 10^{10}$$

$$M = 0.85 \times 850.8 = 723.2 \, \text{W/m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{M \approx 723 \, \text{W/m}^{2}}$$

**(4 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**a) (7 marks)** With a clear classification diagram, discuss the various active thermography techniques. Compare pulsed thermography and lock-in thermography with respect to inspection depth, speed, and signal-to-noise ratio.

**b) (7 marks)** A flash thermography test is conducted on an aluminum plate ($\alpha = 9.7 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$). If the observation window is 3 seconds after the flash, determine: (i) the maximum detectable defect depth, and (ii) how the depth changes if lock-in thermography at 0.1 Hz is used instead.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Classification and Comparison:**

1. **Classification (3 marks):** Active thermography is classified by heat source: optical (flash lamp, halogen), mechanical (ultrasonic), electromagnetic (eddy current, microwave), and hybrid. Each couples differently into the material.

2. **Pulsed Thermography (2 marks):** Uses a single high-energy flash (~kJ, ~ms duration). The entire thermal transient is recorded. **Inspection depth:** shallow to moderate, dependent on $\alpha$ and frame rate. **Speed:** very fast capture. **SNR:** moderate; vulnerable to non-uniform heating and surface emissivity variations.

3. **Lock-in Thermography (2 marks):** Uses a sinusoidally modulated heat source. Only the amplitude and phase images are recorded. **Inspection depth:** tunable via frequency — lower $f$ → deeper. **Speed:** slower (requires multiple cycles). **SNR:** excellent due to narrowband demodulation; phase image rejects emissivity artifacts.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

(i) Pulsed thermography penetration depth:

$$d_{\max} = 2\sqrt{\alpha t} = 2\sqrt{(9.7 \times 10^{-5})(3)}$$

$$d_{\max} = 2\sqrt{2.91 \times 10^{-4}} = 2 \times 1.706 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\max} \approx 3.41 \, \text{cm} = 34.1 \, \text{mm}}$$

**(3.5 marks)**

(ii) Lock-in thermography diffusion length at $f = 0.1 \, \text{Hz}$:

$$\mu = \sqrt{\frac{\alpha}{\pi f}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.7 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.1}}$$

$$\mu = \sqrt{\frac{9.7 \times 10^{-5}}{0.3142}} = \sqrt{3.087 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\mu \approx 1.757 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m} = 17.6 \, \text{mm}}$$

**(3.5 marks)**

**Comparison:** Pulsed at 3 s gives ~34 mm; lock-in at 0.1 Hz gives ~17.6 mm. Pulsed is deeper here because it integrates over 3 s. To get equivalent depth in lock-in, use $f \approx 0.025 \, \text{Hz}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Thermography Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $T$** — Stefan–Boltzmann requires **absolute Kelvin**, not Celsius. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Confusing radiant exitance $M$ with radiance $L$** — $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ is per unit area (W/m²), while $L$ involves solid angle (W/m²/sr).
> 3. **Mixing up active vs passive examples** — KTU strictly requires the example to match the classification. Writing "flash lamp passive" will fetch 0 marks.
> 4. **Using wrong Stefan–Boltzmann constant** — Always use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$. Do not confuse with Planck or Boltzmann constants.
> 5. **Ignoring emissivity in numerical problems** — If the problem specifies a non-black surface, the factor $\varepsilon$ must appear.
> 6. **Block diagrams without arrows or labels** — KTU awards marks for *labeled* and *logically sequential* blocks. Arrows showing signal/data flow are mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Thermography**

- **Core Definition:** NDT technique using IR radiation (8–14 µm) emitted by all bodies above 0 K.
- **Two classes:** Passive (self-heat) vs Active (external stimulus).
- **Six active modalities:** Pulsed, Lock-in, Pulse-Phase, Ultrasonic, Eddy Current, Microwave.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ — the foundation law.
- **Wien's law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$ — explains LWIR dominance.
- **Thermal diffusivity:** $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ — material-specific, governs penetration.
- **Pulsed depth:** $d = 2\sqrt{\alpha t}$.
- **Lock-in diffusion length:** $\mu = \sqrt{\alpha/(\pi f)}$.
- **Detector types:** Microbolometer (uncooled, LWIR) vs MCT/InSb (cooled, MWIR/LWIR).
- **Emissivity correction:** mandatory for low-$\varepsilon$ surfaces — apply paint/tape or use dual-band ratio.
- **Atmospheric windows:** 3–5 µm (MWIR) and 8–14 µm (LWIR) — camera band selection criterion.
- **Sensitivity:** Radiance ∝ $T^{4}$ → very high temperature sensitivity (mK-class NETD detectors).
- **Typical defects detected:** Delamination, voids, inclusions, corrosion under insulation, hot joints, weld cracks, water ingress.
- **For ESE numericals:** Always convert °C → K; always include emissivity if given.
- **Examiner keyword triggers:** "non-contact," "real-time," "full-field," "safe," "remote inspection."
- **Limitation:** Line-of-sight required; surface emissivity dependency; depth resolution decreases with depth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computed Tomography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET636 - NON – DESTRUCTIVE TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computed Tomography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computed Tomography (CT)** is an advanced volumetric Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) technique that uses penetrating radiation (typically **X-rays** or gamma rays) to produce two-dimensional (2D) cross-sectional slices and three-dimensional (3D) volumetric reconstructions of the internal structure of an object without causing any physical damage. Unlike conventional radiographic testing, which produces a single superimposed shadow image, CT acquires a series of projections at multiple angular positions around the specimen and mathematically reconstructs them using algorithms such as the **Radon Transform** and **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In NDT, CT is classified under *Radiographic Methods* (Module 4) and represents the most information-rich radiographic technique. It is used when planar radiography cannot resolve overlapping features or when internal geometry must be quantified.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are looking at a **loaf of sliced bread** sealed inside an opaque plastic bag.

- **Conventional Radiography (X-ray film):** You take one photograph with a flashlight. You see the *combined shadow* of all slices stacked together — you cannot tell whether a seed is in slice #3 or slice #12.
- **Computed Tomography (CT):** You take hundreds of photographs while slowly *rotating* the loaf in front of the flashlight. Each photo is taken at a different angle. A powerful computer then mathematically "un-stacks" these shadows to give you a clean, individual picture of **slice #1, slice #2, slice #3 …** — and you can even reassemble all slices into a 3D digital loaf you can rotate and slice virtually on a screen.

In engineering terms: the **bread loaf** = the test specimen (casting, composite, electronic assembly); the **flashlight** = the X-ray source; the **camera** = the digital detector; the **computer un-stacking** = the reconstruction algorithm.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Linear Attenuation Coefficient ($\mu$):** measured in **cm⁻¹** (or mm⁻¹), quantifies how strongly a material absorbs X-rays per unit thickness.
- **Mass Attenuation Coefficient ($\mu/\rho$):** measured in **cm²/g**, the material property independent of physical density.
- **CT Number / Hounsfield Unit (HU):** dimensionless normalized scale, where **air = −1000 HU** and **water = 0 HU**.
- **Voxel:** the 3D equivalent of a pixel; the smallest volume element in a CT volume (typical industrial CT voxel size: **5 µm to 200 µm**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** Always express $\mu$ in consistent units with the path length. Mixing cm path length with mm⁻¹ coefficient is a common KTU valuation error.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The CT process is governed by the physics of radiation-matter interaction and the mathematics of image reconstruction.

**Step 1 — Radiation Attenuation (Physics Layer)**
When a monochromatic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a homogeneous material of thickness $x$, the emerging intensity $I$ follows the **Beer-Lambert Law of exponential attenuation**:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 2 — Multi-Material Path (Line Integral)**
For a real specimen composed of different materials along the beam path, attenuation becomes the *integral* of $\mu$ along the ray. The detector therefore measures a *projection*:

$$P = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \int_L \mu(x, y) \, ds$$

This is known as the **Radon Transform** of the attenuation field $\mu(x,y)$.

**Step 3 — Multi-Angle Projection Acquisition (Geometry Layer)**
The source-detector pair rotates around the specimen in small angular increments (e.g., **0.5° steps over 360°** = 720 projections). Each projection is a 1-D intensity profile.

**Step 4 — Reconstruction (Mathematics Layer)**
The complete set of projections (stored as a **sinogram**) is inverted to recover the 2-D attenuation map $\mu(x,y)$. Two main algorithms exist:
- **Analytical:** Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) — fast, dominant in industry.
- **Iterative:** Algebraic Reconstruction Technique (ART), Simultaneous Iterative Reconstruction Technique (SIRT) — better for noisy/limited-angle data.

**Step 5 — Slice Stacking (Visualization Layer)**
Adjacent slices are stacked to form a **3-D voxel volume**, which can be rendered, sectioned virtually, or used for metrology.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer-Lambert exponential attenuation (monochromatic) | $I$ in counts/s, $x$ in cm, $\mu$ in cm⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\mu/\rho$ | Mass attenuation coefficient | cm²/g |
| 3 | $P = \int_L \mu(x,y)\, ds$ | Projection (Radon transform) — measured quantity | dimensionless (after log) |
| 4 | $\mathrm{SNR} \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{photons}}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio of CT image | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | CT Number (Hounsfield Unit) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\Delta x_{\min} \approx \dfrac{1}{\mathrm{Nyquist}} \cdot \dfrac{d}{N_p}$ | Minimum resolvable feature (geometric unsharpness) | mm |
| 7 | $D = \sum \mu_i \cdot \Delta s_i$ | Discrete projection for polychromatic beams | dimensionless |
| 8 | $f(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} [P_\theta(s) * h(s)] \, d\theta$ | Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) formula | arbitrary (image intensity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse *linear attenuation coefficient* $\mu$ (depends on density) with *mass attenuation coefficient* $\mu/\rho$ (depends only on atomic number and photon energy). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

Industrial CT is the gold-standard NDT method in:

- **Aerospace:** inspection of turbine blades, composite honeycomb, additive-manufactured (3-D printed) metal parts for internal porosity.
- **Electronics:** failure analysis of BGA solder joints, through-silicon vias (TSVs).
- **Automotive & Castings:** detecting shrinkage porosity, inclusions, and cracks in aluminum and magnesium die castings.
- **Medical / Pharmaceutical:** non-destructive verification of tablets, capsules.
- **Cultural Heritage & Geology:** scanning fossils, cores, and artworks.

The CT-derived voxel data enables **dimensional metrology** with sub-50 µm accuracy, **CAD comparison** (nominal vs actual), and **reverse engineering** of legacy parts without drawings.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law from First Principles

Consider a thin slab of material of thickness $dx$. The fractional reduction in intensity $dI/I$ is proportional to the slab thickness:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

Integrate from $0$ to $x$ (intensity from $I_0$ to $I$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 = -\mu x$$

$$\boxed{\,I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}\,}$$

This is the **monochromatic Beer-Lambert Law** — the foundational equation for all quantitative CT.

### 3.2 Derivation of Projection Equation for a Multi-Layer Specimen

For a specimen with $n$ distinct layers along the X-ray path, each layer $i$ has thickness $x_i$ and linear attenuation coefficient $\mu_i$:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-(\mu_1 x_1 + \mu_2 x_2 + \cdots + \mu_n x_n)}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i x_i$$

For the continuous case (CT reconstruction scenario), the sum becomes an integral:

$$\boxed{\,P = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \int_L \mu(s) \, ds\,}$$

This **projection $P$** is what the detector measures at every angular position. Reconstruction is the *inverse problem* of finding $\mu(s)$ from $P$.

### 3.3 Derivation of CT Number (Hounsfield Unit)

The CT number normalizes the linear attenuation coefficient of any voxel against that of **water** (the international reference medium).

By definition, water is assigned $\mathrm{HU} = 0$:

$$\mathrm{HU} = K \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{voxel}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

The constant $K = 1000$ is chosen so that air (with $\mu \approx 0$) maps to $-1000$ HU. Substituting $K$:

$$\boxed{\,\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{voxel}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}\,}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**

Given: For a voxel of an aluminum alloy, $\mu_{\text{Al-alloy}} = 1.50 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. For water at the same X-ray energy, $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.20 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.

Step 1 — Substitute values:

$$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{1.50 - 0.20}{0.20}$$

Step 2 — Numerator:

$$1.50 - 0.20 = 1.30 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

Step 3 — Division:

$$\frac{1.30}{0.20} = 6.5$$

Step 4 — Multiply by 1000:

$$\boxed{\,\mathrm{HU} = +6500\,}$$

This high positive value is typical for metals and explains why *industrial CT* rarely uses the medical HU scale (which would saturate); instead, industrial scanners report $\mu$ directly or use extended greyscale ranges.

### 3.4 Discrete Sinogram Acquisition Model

For a CT scanner with $N_p$ angular projections and $N_d$ detector elements per projection, the measured dataset $R(\theta_m, s_n)$ (the **sinogram**) is:

$$R(\theta_m, s_n) = \int_0^{\infty} \mu(s_n \cos\theta_m - t \sin\theta_m, \, s_n \sin\theta_m + t \cos\theta_m) \, dt$$

where:
- $\theta_m$ = projection angle, $m = 1, 2, \dots, N_p$
- $s_n$ = detector element position, $n = 1, 2, \dots, N_d$
- $t$ = coordinate along the ray direction

The reconstruction algorithm inverts this discrete Radon transform.

### 3.5 Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) Reconstruction — Conceptual Derivation

The basic *back-projection* of all projection angles smears each profile back across the image plane. Without filtering, this produces blurred images (the **1/r blur artifact**).

Mathematically, back-projection alone gives:

$$f_{\text{BP}}(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} P_\theta(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta) \, d\theta$$

To remove the 1/r blur, we apply the **Ramp filter** $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ in the frequency domain to each projection before back-projecting:

$$\boxed{\,f(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} \bigl[ P_\theta(s) * h(s) \bigr] \, d\theta\,}$$

where $h(s)$ is the filter kernel (inverse Fourier transform of $\vert \omega \vert$, often realized via Shepp-Logan, Hamming, or Hann windows to suppress high-frequency noise).

This is the **Central Slice Theorem** implementation that underlies virtually every modern industrial CT scanner.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Industrial CT Scanner — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional architecture and signal flow of a typical industrial cone-beam CT system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    XRAY[X-ray Source Tube] --> COLL[Collimator Assembly]
    COLL --> SPEC[Rotating Specimen Stage]
    SPEC --> OBJ[Object Under Inspection]
    OBJ --> DET[Flat Panel Detector FPD]
    DET --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System]
    DAQ --> SINO[Sinogram Storage Matrix]
    SINO --> FBP[Filtered Back Projection Algorithm]
    FBP --> SLICE[2D Cross Sectional Slice]
    SLICE --> STK[3D Voxel Volume Stack]
    STK --> METRO[Dimensional Metrology Module]
    STK --> CAD[CAD Comparison Module]
    STK --> DEF[Defect Detection Analysis]
    STK --> DISP[3D Visualization Workstation]
    CTRL[Motion Controller and Safety Interlock] -.-> XRAY
    CTRL -.-> SPEC
    CTRL -.-> DET
```

### 4.2 CT Acquisition Workflow — Process Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: Specimen Mounting on Rotary Chuck] --> B[Step 2: Set X-ray Tube Voltage kV and Current mu A]
    B --> C[Step 3: Define Number of Projections Np and Detector Resolution]
    C --> D[Step 4: Acquire Projection 1 at Angle theta 1]
    D --> E[Step 5: Rotate by Delta theta and Acquire Projection 2]
    E --> F{Step 6: All Np Projections Acquired}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Step 7: Correct Beam Hardening and Scatter Artifacts]
    G --> H[Step 8: Apply Filtered Back Projection to Reconstruct Slices]
    H --> I[Step 9: Stack Slices to Build 3D Volume]
    I --> J[Step 10: Perform NDT Evaluation and Metrology]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CT Reconstruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Data Layer]
        PR[Raw Projection Data Set]
    end
    subgraph PreProc[Pre Processing Stage]
        PR --> CL[Logarithmic Conversion]
        CL --> BH[Beam Hardening Correction]
        BH --> SC[Scatter Correction]
        SC --> RC[Ring Artifact Removal]
    end
    subgraph Recon[Reconstruction Engine]
        RC --> FLT[Apply Ramp or Shepp Logan Filter]
        FLT --> BP[Back Projection across 0 to 180 degrees]
        BP --> SLICE[Generate Reconstructed Slice]
    end
    subgraph PostProc[Post Processing and Analysis]
        SLICE --> VOL[3D Volume Rendering]
        SLICE --> SEG[Segmentation of Defects]
        VOL --> METRO[Metrology Report]
        SEG --> NDTRPT[NDT Inspection Report]
    end
```

### 4.4 Schematic Explanation of Cone-Beam Geometry

```
                    X-ray Source (Focal Spot)
                            ●
                            |\
                            | \
                            |  \  (Cone Beam)
                            |   \
                            |    \
                            |     \
                            |      ●─────────►  Detector Plane
                            |     /            (Flat Panel, e.g. 200 mm × 200 mm)
                            |    /

                            | /

                   ┌────────●────────┐
                   │   Object        │  ← Rotates about vertical axis
                   │   (Specimen)    │

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Computed Tomography. List any two advantages of CT over conventional film radiography.**

**Model Answer:**
Computed Tomography (CT) is a non-destructive radiographic technique that produces cross-sectional (2-D) and volumetric (3-D) images of an internal structure of an object by acquiring multiple X-ray projections at different angular positions and reconstructing them using mathematical algorithms such as Filtered Back-Projection.

Two advantages over conventional film radiography:
1. **Eliminates superimposition** of features: Overlapping defects in different depth planes appear separated in individual CT slices.
2. **Provides quantitative 3-D geometry** suitable for dimensional metrology, CAD comparison, and porosity quantification (vol %, size distribution).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Hounsfield Unit (HU)? What is its value for water and air?**

**Model Answer:**
The Hounsfield Unit (HU), also called the CT Number, is a dimensionless normalized measure of the linear attenuation coefficient of a voxel relative to that of water.

$$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{voxel}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

- **Water:** $\mathrm{HU} = 0$
- **Air:** $\mathrm{HU} = -1000$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an industrial Computed Tomography system. List the major hardware and software components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Beer-Lambert law of attenuation. A monoenergetic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0 = 5000$ counts/s passes through a steel plate of thickness **8 mm**. If the linear attenuation coefficient of steel at this energy is **$\mu = 1.2$ mm⁻¹**, calculate the transmitted intensity $I$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle of Industrial CT:**

The system consists of an X-ray source (typically a micro-focus tube with focal spot 1–200 µm), a motorized rotary stage holding the specimen, a flat-panel digital detector (e.g. CMOS or a-Si), a gantry or manipulator providing translation/rotation, a data acquisition system, and a reconstruction workstation running FBP or iterative algorithms.

**Hardware Components:**
- X-ray tube (high voltage generator 20–450 kV)
- Collimator and beam-shaping filters
- Precision rotation stage (resolution ~0.001°)
- Flat-panel detector (active area 200×200 mm² typical, pixel pitch 50–200 µm)
- Radiation shielding cabinet and interlocks

**Software Components:**
- Acquisition control software
- Reconstruction engine (FBP / iterative)
- 3-D visualization (volume rendering, MPR — multi-planar reformat)
- Analysis tools (metrology, CAD overlay, defect segmentation)

**Working:** The specimen is rotated step-by-step. At each angular step, a 2-D projection image is captured. After 360° (or 180° + fan angle), all projections are mathematically inverted to yield a stack of 1-D cross-sectional slices, which combine to form a 3-D voxel volume.

**(b) Beer-Lambert Derivation:**

For a thin slab $dx$, fractional attenuation $dI/I = -\mu dx$. Integrating:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_0^{x} dx \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Step 1 — Identify given values:

$$I_0 = 5000 \text{ counts/s}, \quad x = 8 \text{ mm}, \quad \mu = 1.2 \text{ mm}^{-1}$$

Step 2 — Compute the exponent:

$$\mu x = 1.2 \times 8 = 9.6$$

Step 3 — Evaluate exponential:

$$e^{-9.6} = 6.74 \times 10^{-5}$$

Step 4 — Compute transmitted intensity:

$$\boxed{\,I = 5000 \times 6.74 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.337 \text{ counts/s}\,}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Such an extremely low transmitted intensity indicates that 8 mm of steel is essentially opaque at this energy. In practice, the X-ray energy (kV) would have to be increased substantially to obtain measurable transmission.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) reconstruction in CT. Why is filtering necessary before back-projection? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CT voxel is composed of **40% cortical bone** ($\mu = 2.4$ cm⁻¹) and **60% soft tissue** ($\mu = 0.20$ cm⁻¹) by volume along the beam path. Calculate (i) the effective linear attenuation coefficient of the voxel, and (ii) the corresponding Hounsfield Unit. Take $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.20$ cm⁻¹. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Filtered Back-Projection:**

FBP reconstructs the 2-D attenuation map $\mu(x,y)$ from a set of 1-D projections $P_\theta(s)$ acquired at angles $\theta = 0, \Delta\theta, 2\Delta\theta, \ldots, 180°$.

**Steps:**
1. Each projection $P_\theta(s)$ is Fourier transformed: $S_\theta(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{P_\theta(s)\}$.
2. Apply the **ramp filter** $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ in frequency domain to compensate for the $1/r$ blurring introduced by simple back-projection.
3. Inverse Fourier transform to obtain the filtered projection: $P_\theta^*(s) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{S_\theta(\omega) \cdot \vert \omega \vert\}$.
4. Back-project the filtered projection across the image plane for all $\theta$:

$$f(x,y) = \int_0^{\pi} P_\theta^*(x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta) \, d\theta$$

**Why filtering is necessary:**
Without filtering, simple back-projection smears each projection back across the image, producing a blurred reconstruction with a characteristic $1/r$ point spread function. The ramp filter boosts high spatial frequencies to counteract this blur, restoring sharp edges and accurate feature representation.

**(b) Effective Attenuation & Hounsfield Unit:**

Step 1 — Effective $\mu$ (volume-weighted):

$$\mu_{\text{eff}} = 0.40 \times 2.4 + 0.60 \times 0.20$$

Step 2 — Numerator for cortical bone:

$$0.40 \times 2.4 = 0.96 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

Step 3 — Soft tissue contribution:

$$0.60 \times 0.20 = 0.12 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

Step 4 — Sum:

$$\boxed{\,\mu_{\text{eff}} = 1.08 \text{ cm}^{-1}\,}$$

Step 5 — Hounsfield Unit:

$$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{1.08 - 0.20}{0.20} = 1000 \times \frac{0.88}{0.20} = 1000 \times 4.4$$

$$\boxed{\,\mathrm{HU} = +4400\,}$$

**Interpretation:** This value is consistent with mixed bone/tissue voxels and demonstrates that HU is highly sensitive to density differences — a key reason CT can resolve small soft-tissue variations in industrial composites and biomedical samples.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** When $\mu$ is in cm⁻¹, the thickness $x$ *must* be in cm. Mixing mm and cm⁻¹ yields wrong exponents and wrong intensities — a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **HU saturation in metals:** Industrial CT of dense metals like steel or tungsten produces extremely high HU values (>10000). Do **not** apply the medical HU scale directly to metals — use extended scales or raw $\mu$ reporting.
> 3. **Forgetting the logarithm:** When stating the projection equation $P = \int \mu \, ds$, students often forget the step $P = \ln(I_0/I)$. Both forms are acceptable, but the logarithmic conversion is the *definition* of a CT projection and must be shown explicitly.
> 4. **Back-projection without filter = blurred image:** Always mention the **ramp filter** $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ when describing FBP; omitting it costs marks.
> 5. **Confusing projection angle coverage:** Fan-beam CT typically needs **180° + fan angle**, while cone-beam CT requires a **full 360°** rotation for complete data (or 180° + 2·cone angle in special geometry). Writing "180° is enough" without qualifier is imprecise.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CT definition:** Volumetric NDT using X-ray projections + mathematical reconstruction (Radon Transform inversion).
- **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — fundamental equation.
- **Projection equation:** $P = \ln(I_0/I) = \int_L \mu(s) \, ds$ — the line integral of attenuation.
- **CT Number / HU:** $\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot (\mu_{\text{voxel}} - \mu_{\text{water}})/\mu_{\text{water}}$; water = 0, air = −1000.
- **Reconstruction algorithms:** Filtered Back-Projection (FBP, analytical, fast) and Iterative methods (ART/SIRT, better for noisy data).
- **Sinogram:** 2-D matrix where rows are detector elements and columns are projection angles; the raw data of a CT scan.
- **Voxel:** smallest 3-D volume element — determines spatial resolution (5 µm to 200 µm typical in industrial CT).
- **Beam hardening & scatter artifacts** must be corrected *before* reconstruction for accurate results.
- **Advantages over film radiography:** no superimposition, true 3-D geometry, quantitative metrology, void/defect sizing.
- **Limitations:** expensive equipment, limited specimen size (cabinet geometry), long scan/reconstruction times, polychromatic beam artifacts, radiation safety requirements.
- **Common applications:** aerospace composites, additive-manufactured parts, castings (porosity, inclusions), electronics (BGA solder voids), geological cores, heritage artefacts.
